title: Capital, Risk, and the Sin of Usury
subtitle: The Forgotten History of the Extrinsic Titles

chapters.title: Why the Extrinsic Titles Matter Again
chapters.label: Preface

sections.title: 0.1 The Modern Problem Hidden Inside an Ancient Doctrine
paragraphs: At first glance, the Christian doctrine of usury seems to belong to another world — an age of grain loans and moneychangers, canon lawyers and confessors, monasteries and market squares, when theologians argued over whether money was "sterile" and whether it was sinful to receive back more from a loan than one had given. To a modern reader the whole discussion can sound archaic, even quaint: surely a civilization equipped with interest rates, derivatives, venture capital, sovereign debt, risk premiums, and discount models has left the medieval question of usury far behind.
But the old question has not disappeared. It has only changed its clothing.

The medieval theologians were not merely asking whether "interest" was allowed. That is the shallow version of the question. They were asking something far more precise, and far more dangerous to modern finance: when may capital receive more than it gives?

No percentage can answer that by itself. A low interest rate may still be unjust if it extracts from a borrower's need, traps a household in dependency, or shifts all the risk onto the vulnerable; a higher return may be morally defensible if it reflects genuine risk-sharing, real labor, actual loss, or participation in an enterprise whose gains and losses are held in common. The moral issue is not simply how much is paid back but what the surplus payment means: compensation, restoration, payment for labor, reimbursement of real cost, a share in genuine risk — or a claim on another person's future, secured by superior bargaining power and dressed up in the language of contract.

This is why the old doctrine of usury deserves to be recovered — not because every medieval conclusion can be imported unchanged into modern finance, and not because all lending for gain can be treated as morally identical. The tradition itself was subtler than that. It distinguished loans from partnerships, selling from lending, actual loss from imagined profit, risk genuinely borne from risk rhetorically invoked, and it developed a careful language for asking whether a payment beyond principal was truly external to the loan itself or simply usury under another name.
That language centered on what the scholastics called extrinsic titles.

An extrinsic title was a reason, standing outside the mere fact of the loan, that could justify receiving something more than the principal. The lender might suffer an actual loss. The borrower might fail to repay on time and cause damage. A merchant might forego a concrete and licit profit because his capital had been diverted. An investor might genuinely expose the principal itself to loss. In such cases the surplus payment was not supposed to be a reward for the bare passage of time; it was supposed to correspond to something real — loss, delay, labor, risk, or foregone use.

Four such titles stand at the center of this book: poena conventionalis, or morae poena, the penalty for late payment, which repairs or penalizes a borrower's culpable delay — unless the penalty has been engineered in advance, so that "late payment" becomes merely disguised interest; damnum emergens, actual loss incurred, where compensation restores equality rather than creating profit; lucrum cessans, profit foregone, the most dangerous of the four because it stands so close to the modern concept of opportunity cost — read too broadly, it threatens to justify almost any return on money simply because money could have been used elsewhere; and periculum sortis, the risk of the principal, the title most relevant to the modern distinction between guaranteed debt and risk-bearing investment, because it asks whether capital is genuinely vulnerable with the enterprise or has protected itself while others absorb the downside.

These titles were not loopholes — not, at least, in their original moral purpose. They were tests: attempts to distinguish compensation from extraction, restitution from profit-taking, risk-sharing from domination, partnership from disguised debt; ways of asking whether a financial arrangement restored justice or violated it.

That distinction matters again because modern finance is full of arrangements that are legally valid but morally opaque. A venture capital fund may claim to be "risk capital" while using liquidation preferences, control rights, and exit pressure to shift much of the real burden onto founders, workers, customers, or mission. A private equity firm may speak the language of efficiency while loading an acquired company with debt, extracting fees, and leaving employees and creditors exposed. A lender may justify default interest as compensation while quietly benefiting from the borrower's distress. A fund may claim an opportunity cost because its capital could have earned a market return elsewhere, even if that return is abstract, speculative, or dependent on forms of extraction the investor has never morally examined. A platform company may raise capital to serve a public need, only to find its governance structure gradually bending the mission toward investor exit. The question in every case is not merely whether these structures are legal but whether they are just.

Medieval moral theology gives us tools for seeing what modern financial language often obscures. It forces us to ask who owns, who governs, who bears risk, who receives surplus, who can walk away, and who is trapped — whether capital has become a servant of life, work, and community, or has turned another person's need, labor, mission, or vulnerability into a source of guaranteed growth.

This is also why the doctrine of usury cannot be reduced to a simple condemnation of all finance. The tradition knew that economic life requires credit, trade, investment, partnership, labor, administration, risk, and trust, and it knew that not every surplus payment is unjust. A merchant may earn profit by serving a real need. A partner may share in gain because he also shares in loss. A lender may be compensated for actual damage. An institution may recover the real costs of administering charitable credit. Capital may serve.

But the tradition also knew that money has a peculiar power: it can detach claim from relationship, turn time into revenue, convert another person's need into a creditor's asset, hide domination beneath clean legal form, and make extraction appear neutral, mathematical, inevitable. That is why usury was treated not merely as an economic problem but as a moral and spiritual one. It concerned justice between persons, the meaning of property, the relation between law and conscience — whether economic exchange could remain ordered toward the common good, or would become a mechanism by which the strong extract from the weak.

The old language is foreign to us now: mutuum, interesse, damnum emergens, lucrum cessans, periculum sortis, societas, foenus nauticum. The terms can sound technical and remote. But behind them lies a surprisingly contemporary question: what kind of surplus can be received without turning finance into extraction?
This book is an attempt to recover that question.

It begins before scholastic theology, in the ancient world of debt, grain, silver, pledges, servitude, and release. Ancient Near Eastern law already knew that debt could destabilize households and concentrate power. Scripture placed those concerns under covenantal judgment: one must not profit from the desperation of the poor, or keep a neighbor's cloak overnight, or harden one's heart before the Sabbath release, or allow debt to recreate slavery among God's people. Aristotle added a philosophical suspicion of money breeding money. Roman law supplied the technical category of the mutuum, the loan for consumption in which ownership passes to the borrower and only an equivalent must be returned.

Christian moral theology inherited all of these streams, and the result was not a simplistic hatred of commerce but a demanding moral anatomy of exchange. Aquinas asked what was being sold, who owned the money, whether the "use" of a consumable thing could be separated from the thing itself, and whether equality in commutative justice had been preserved. Later canonists, scholastics, Franciscans, Dominicans, Salamancans, Jesuits, and manualists extended, narrowed, stretched, and contested his conclusions as commerce became more complex.

The history is not tidy. Some theologians were stricter, others more permissive; some distinctions preserved the heart of the usury prohibition, others came close to dissolving it; some titles began as careful safeguards and later became tools that could justify what earlier thinkers had feared. The development of the extrinsic titles is therefore not a story of progress from primitive suspicion to modern sophistication but a story of moral pressure: the constant struggle to adapt justice to economic complexity without allowing complexity to become a hiding place for domination.

That is why this history matters now. We live in a world of extraordinary financial abstraction. Capital moves faster than communities can respond. Ownership is layered through funds, holding companies, preference stacks, debt instruments, derivatives, and governance rights. The people who bear the risk are often not the people who receive the upside; the people who build the enterprise may not control it; the people affected by the technology may not govern it; and the people whose labor and communities create the value may be paid last, while capital protects itself first.

In such a world, the old scholastic questions become newly sharp. Is a preferred return really compensation for risk, or is it priority extraction? Is a late fee truly a penalty for culpable delay, or a revenue model? Is a management fee reimbursement for labor, or a way to extract regardless of outcome? Is opportunity cost concrete and morally serious, or merely a theological-sounding justification for target returns? Is capital actually at risk, or has it structured the downside away from itself? Does the contract create partnership, or does it preserve creditor domination under the language of investment? These are not antiquarian questions. They belong to anyone trying to build financial structures that are patient, mission-locked, steward-owned, non-extractive, and ordered toward life.

The purpose of this book is therefore not to romanticize the Middle Ages but to retrieve a moral vocabulary that modern finance badly needs. The scholastic tradition does not give us a ready-made spreadsheet; it gives us something more important — a disciplined way of asking what a financial claim is really claiming, and whether that claim can be justified before God, neighbor, and justice.
The ancient doctrine of usury was never only about interest. It was about the moral form of capital. That is the problem hidden inside the old doctrine, and it is the problem this book will follow.

sections.title: 0.2 The Four Extrinsic Titles as a Moral Diagnostic System
paragraphs: The scholastic tradition did not ask only whether a lender received "more." It asked why the lender received more, what the extra payment corresponded to, and whether that extra payment arose from the loan itself or from some external circumstance. That distinction is the key to the entire doctrine.

In a strict mutuum, the borrower receives ownership of a consumable or fungible thing — money, grain, wine, oil, seed, or some other good that is used by being consumed, spent, or alienated — and is obligated to return not the identical thing but an equivalent: the same quantity and the same quality. In the bare loan itself, the lender's claim is to the principal. If the lender demands additional payment simply because time has passed, or simply because the borrower had the use of the money, the tradition identifies the surplus as usury.

But real economic life is rarely so simple. Lending can cause loss; repayment can be delayed; capital can be diverted from a concrete use. A funder can bear genuine risk. A charitable lending institution can incur labor and administrative expense. A merchant may hold not merely idle coin but capital already ordered toward trade. A partner may expose wealth to loss in a common enterprise. A creditor may be harmed by a borrower's failure to repay when promised.

The scholastics therefore developed the language of extrinsic titles: reasons external to the mutuum itself that might justify receiving something above the principal. These titles were not supposed to make money fertile by magic, or to turn every loan into an income-producing asset, or to serve as pious labels pasted onto ordinary extraction. Properly understood, they were tests of justice — in their strict form, not exceptions that swallow the rule — and they asked whether the surplus payment corresponded to something real.

The four titles at the center of this book are poena conventionalis, damnum emergens, lucrum cessans, and periculum sortis. Each names a different kind of burden or disturbance in justice. Each can be legitimate under strict conditions. Each can also be abused.

subsections.title: Poena conventionalis / morae poena: The Penalty for Delay
paragraphs: The first title is poena conventionalis, also called morae poena when it concerns delay: an agreed penalty imposed when the borrower fails to repay on time.

The moral logic is initially straightforward. The loan itself remains gratuitous, and the borrower owes only the principal. But a borrower who fails to repay when promised has done something new. The delay may injure the lender, disrupt the lender's own obligations, impose costs, or violate the agreement, and a penalty may therefore be attached — not to the loan itself, but to the borrower's default. In its strict form the title does not say, "You used my money for six months, therefore you owe me more." It says, "You promised to repay on this date. You culpably failed to do so. That failure caused harm or violated justice. Therefore a proportionate penalty may be owed." The distinction is crucial: the penalty is a response to wrongful delay, not a price for time.

Yet this is also why the title was so easy to abuse. A contract could be written with an unrealistically short repayment period. The lender could expect the borrower to miss the deadline. The penalty could be set so high that default became profitable. The lender might secretly prefer late repayment, because late repayment produced the penalty — at which point the "penalty" is no longer truly penal at all; it is disguised interest.

The scholastic phrase poena nec in fraudem captures the issue beautifully: a penalty is acceptable only if it is not in fraud of the usury prohibition. The problem is never merely the legal wording of the clause but the moral structure of the transaction. Does the penalty deter breach, restore equality, and compensate real harm — or does it create a revenue stream from the borrower's weakness?

In modern terms, this title asks hard questions about late fees, default interest, penalty clauses, cure periods, grace periods, and enforcement charges. Does the lender truly want repayment on time? Is the borrower realistically able to avoid the penalty? Is the amount proportionate to actual harm? Are hardship and impossibility considered? Is the penalty capped? Is it booked and understood as compensation, or as yield? The title is morally valid only when the lender would prefer not to receive it.

subsections.title: Damnum emergens: Actual Loss Incurred
paragraphs: The second title is damnum emergens, actual loss arising — the cleanest of the four, because its logic is fundamentally restorative. If the lender suffers a real loss because of the loan, the lender may be compensated for that loss. The payment is not profit but repair; it restores equality in commutative justice.

Aquinas gives the classic formulation when he says that a lender may, without sin, agree with the borrower on compensation for the loss he incurs, because this is not selling the use of money but avoiding a loss. The lender may be restored to the position he should have occupied; he may not enrich himself merely because he lent. The claim is never "my money should grow because I lent it," but "because I lent, I suffered a real and specific loss, and justice permits that loss to be repaired." Examples might include actual administrative costs, legal costs, collection costs, emergency losses caused by the unavailability of funds, or damage caused by delayed repayment. The title becomes morally suspect the moment such losses are fictionalized, inflated, generalized, or converted into a standing return.

And the danger is subtle. Once a category of "actual loss" exists, people can begin to redefine almost anything as loss. The inability to invest elsewhere becomes loss; the absence of liquidity becomes loss; a desired return becomes loss; a fund's target IRR becomes loss. At that point damnum emergens begins to slide into lucrum cessans, and then into a generalized claim that capital is entitled to be compensated for not growing.

The strict scholastic test is therefore demanding. The loss must be actual. It must be caused by the loan or by the borrower's conduct. It must be documented, or at least reasonably provable. It must be proportionate. It must not be double-counted under some other title. And it must restore equality rather than create gain.

In modern non-extractive finance, damnum emergens is indispensable. Real costs should be covered, and stewardship work should not be made impossible: administration, accounting, legal structure, due diligence, and monitoring all require labor, and a moral financial institution may need to be sustainable. But sustainability is not the same as extraction, and cost recovery must remain cost recovery — it must not quietly become a disguised return to capital. The title stays honest as long as the governing question is "What did this actually cost?" — and turns dangerous the moment the question becomes "What return did we want?"

subsections.title: Lucrum cessans: Foregone Profit
paragraphs: The third title is lucrum cessans, the gain that ceases — the most controversial of the four, because it stands close to the modern idea of opportunity cost. The basic claim runs: the lender was using, or preparing to use, the money in a concrete and licit profit-making activity; by lending the money instead, that profit ceased; the lender therefore asks to be compensated for the gain that was lost.

In its strict and defensible form, the title is not abstract. The claim it makes is not that money can always earn something somewhere, so that every loan deserves a return; the claim is that this particular capital was already ordered toward a concrete, licit, reasonably probable use, that lending interrupted the use, and that the foregone gain was real enough to be morally considered.

That is why Aquinas is cautious. He accepts compensation for actual loss, but he resists compensation merely for profit not made, because the lender would be selling what he does not yet have. The profit might never have materialized. It might have been prevented by many causes. To charge for it in advance risks turning possibility into property. Later scholastics gradually softened this position in concrete commercial cases: a merchant's money is not necessarily idle — it may be already committed to trade; a stock of grain may have a predictable future value; a sum of money may be part of a real business plan. If lending interrupts that plan, the lost gain begins to look more like a real detriment than a mere fantasy.

But this is precisely where the danger lies. Once one admits foregone profit, the title can expand without limit. Every investor can claim that capital could have been deployed elsewhere; every lender can point to a benchmark; every fund can invoke its hurdle rate; every financial actor can say, "We gave up return, therefore we are owed return." The scholastic tradition never fully resolves this tension. Instead, it disciplines the title with conditions. The alternative use must be concrete. The gain must be licit. The lender must really have intended, or been positioned, to pursue it. The loan must truly cause the gain to cease. The amount must be proportionate. The compensation must not be deducted upfront in a way that disguises the true principal. And the title must not become universal.

For modern finance, this title is perhaps the most tempting and the most dangerous. Much of contemporary investment theory is built around opportunity cost: capital is assumed to deserve a return because it could always be doing something else. The scholastic question is sharper. What else, exactly? Was the alternative real? Was it moral? Was it available? Was it intended? Was the expected gain reasonably certain? Or is "opportunity cost" merely a sophisticated way of saying that capital must never serve without being paid? A non-extractive financial system cannot ignore opportunity cost, but it cannot absolutize it either; the mere fact that money could have been used elsewhere does not prove that every borrower must pay tribute to that possibility. The title is morally valid when capital gives up a real, concrete, licit path — and extractive when capital treats every imaginable alternative as a debt owed by the borrower.

subsections.title: Periculum sortis: Risk of Capital
paragraphs: The fourth title is periculum sortis, the risk of the principal — the most structurally important of the four, because it asks not only what happened around the loan but what kind of financial relationship exists in the first place.

In a strict mutuum, the lender gives ownership of the money to the borrower and receives a claim to repayment. The borrower bears the ordinary risk of using the money: if he makes a profit, the profit belongs to him; if his use goes badly, he still owes repayment. The lender's capital is legally protected by the claim. In a partnership or genuinely risk-bearing investment, the situation is different. The capital is exposed. The investor may lose. The return is not simply demanded from the borrower regardless of outcome; it is connected to participation in the enterprise. The funder shares in the upside because the funder also shares in the downside.

This is the moral intuition behind periculum sortis. The claim is not that all lending is risky and all interest therefore justified, but that where capital is genuinely exposed to loss, compensation for bearing that risk may be morally different from usury.

The difference matters. If the principal is guaranteed, collateralized, senior, insured, personally guaranteed, or otherwise protected — so that the borrower, worker, founder, or community bears the real downside — then the title is weak or false. The lender may speak of risk, but the structure says otherwise: the capital is not truly vulnerable with the enterprise. If, however, the capital can actually be lost — if it is subordinated, patient, contingent, loss-absorbing, or otherwise exposed to the same fate as the project — then surplus may correspond to genuine participation rather than extraction. This gives the scholastic tradition a surprisingly modern lens. It asks about downside allocation: who is protected and who is exposed, whether capital shares the vulnerability of the enterprise or merely purchases a claim against it, whether the investor is a partner or a creditor.

Yet periculum sortis also carries a serious danger. Every loan has some risk of nonpayment, and if ordinary default risk were enough to justify a premium, the usury prohibition would collapse. The mere possibility that a borrower might fail cannot automatically sanctify interest. The risk must be real, substantial, borne by the lender, and proportionately priced — and it must not be double-counted through fees, penalties, control rights, liquidation preferences, and senior claims.

For modern non-extractive finance, this title is essential. It asks whether capital is genuinely at risk with the people and the mission it finances; whether capital is patient enough to absorb loss, or demands protection while others carry the fragility; whether the legal structure of an investment corresponds to the moral language of partnership. The title is morally valid when capital is truly vulnerable — and extractive when capital invokes risk while shifting the real downside onto others.

subsections.title: The Titles as a System
paragraphs: Taken together, the four titles form a moral diagnostic system. They do not merely ask whether there was interest. They ask whether there was a real loss, a wrongful delay, a concrete foregone gain, a genuine risk to principal, labor or expense. They ask whether the amount was proportionate and the title documented; whether the borrower was vulnerable; whether the lender was restored or enriched; whether risk was shared or shifted; whether the surplus was external to the loan or arose from the loan itself; and whether the title was real, or merely a disguise. Beneath them all runs the same question: is the surplus payment tied to a real external burden, or merely to bargaining power?

Each title can serve justice, and each can become a mask. A late-payment penalty can restore discipline, or become a revenue model. Actual loss can be repaired, or inflated. Foregone profit can be real, or dissolve into abstract opportunity cost. Risk can be genuinely borne, or invoked by capital that has carefully protected itself.

This is why the history of the extrinsic titles is not a story of loopholes but a story of moral discernment under economic pressure. The scholastics were trying to preserve the prohibition against usury while recognizing that economic life includes real costs, real losses, real risks, and real obligations. They were trying to prevent charity from becoming financially impossible without allowing finance to become predatory — to distinguish the lender who is harmed from the lender who extracts, the partner who shares risk from the creditor who dominates, the institution that recovers costs from the institution that monetizes dependency.

That distinction remains urgent. Modern finance often treats surplus as self-justifying: capital has a cost, risk has a price, time has value, liquidity has value, opportunity has value. These claims are not always false. But they are morally incomplete, and the scholastic tradition asks the next questions: whose time, whose risk, whose opportunity, whose loss, whose vulnerability, whose life?

The four extrinsic titles force capital to explain itself. They do not permit capital simply to say, "I could have earned more elsewhere." They require capital to say: "Here is the real loss, the real risk, the real labor, the real foregone use — and here is why this payment restores justice rather than extracts from need." That is why they matter now. They offer a grammar for distinguishing capital that serves life from capital that feeds on life.

sections.title: 0.3 How This Book Is Structured
paragraphs: This book follows the doctrine of usury backward and forward at the same time.

It moves backward because the scholastic doctrine cannot be understood if we begin with medieval technical vocabulary. The words mutuum, interesse, damnum emergens, lucrum cessans, and periculum sortis appear late in the story; the human problem behind them is much older. Before there were scholastic titles, there were debtors and creditors. Before there were canonists classifying contracts, there were grain loans, silver loans, pledges, harvest failures, family land, debt bondage, and royal proclamations of release. Before theologians asked whether the lender could sell the use of money, Scripture had already asked whether a poor neighbor's cloak could be kept overnight.

And it moves forward because those ancient and medieval questions have not disappeared. They reappear wherever finance claims a right to surplus — wherever a lender charges penalties, an investor claims opportunity cost, capital calls itself "risk-bearing" while protecting itself from actual loss, or mission, labor, founders, communities, and the poor bear the downside while capital captures the upside.

The purpose of the structure is therefore diagnostic, not merely historical. Each part of the book adds another layer to the same central question: when does finance restore justice, and when does it extract from life?

Part I begins in the ancient world, with debt before the Christian doctrine of usury and even before the Roman legal category of the mutuum. Ancient Near Eastern law already knew the power of loans to support households or destroy them. Grain and silver could be lent, interest charged, pledges taken; children, land, labor, and freedom could become entangled in repayment, and debt could become a path into dependency. This is why ancient rulers sometimes proclaimed debt release. Those proclamations were not sentimental gestures but acts of social repair: they recognized that, left unchecked, creditor claims could destabilize households, concentrate land, and produce forms of servitude incompatible with political order.

From there, the book turns to Scripture. The Hebrew Bible does not speak in the language of Roman contract law; it does not define the mutuum, and it does not distinguish formally between damnum emergens and lucrum cessans. But it gives the moral grammar without which later Christian theology cannot be understood: lending to the poor must not become profit from distress; the cloak taken in pledge must be returned; no increase may be taken from the vulnerable brother; Sabbath release and Jubilee are instituted; and debt is treated not merely as a private contract but as a spiritual and social relation ordered before God. This opening part establishes the deepest moral context of the book. Debt is never merely technical. It always concerns persons, households, land, vulnerability, power, and the possibility of release.

Part II introduces the legal form that will become central to Christian usury doctrine: the mutuum. Here the story becomes more precise. In Roman law, a mutuum is a loan for consumption: one person transfers ownership of a fungible good — money, grain, wine, oil, silver, gold — to another, and the borrower must return not the identical thing but an equivalent amount of the same kind and quality. The thing lent becomes his; the lender receives a claim to repayment. This legal structure changes the moral question. If ownership passes to the borrower, the lender no longer owns the money. If the money is consumable in use, its "use" cannot be separated from the thing itself. And if the lender nevertheless charges for the use of the money while also demanding the return of the principal, he appears to charge twice: once for the thing, and again for the use of the thing. This is the Roman legal anatomy that Aquinas later transforms into moral theology. Part II also distinguishes the mutuum from related forms — commodatum, depositum, locatio-conductio, societas, the commenda, and the foenus nauticum or sea loan — because not every financial arrangement is a loan. A partnership is not a mutuum. A risk-bearing maritime investment is not the same as a guaranteed repayment claim. An administrative charge is not necessarily profit. A contract's legal form may reveal its moral form, or conceal it.

Part III turns to Thomas Aquinas — not as the inventor of the whole doctrine, but as the thinker who gives it its most influential theological architecture. His treatment of usury belongs inside his broader account of justice: the relevant texts form a cluster in the Secunda Secundae — justice in general, commutative justice, restitution, buying and selling, just price, and usury — and the book reads them in sequence.

It begins with commutative justice in Summa Theologiae II-II, q.61, where Aquinas distinguishes distributive justice, which concerns proportional allocation within a community, from commutative justice, which concerns equality between persons in exchange, injury, repayment, and restitution. Usury is a problem of commutative justice because it concerns whether one person takes more than justice permits from another. It then examines restitution in q.62 — important because the extrinsic titles are all, in different ways, claims about restoring equality: if the lender suffers a loss, if a borrower delays payment, if a future gain is interrupted, if capital is exposed to loss, the moral question becomes whether a surplus payment repairs a disturbance in justice or creates a new one. Next comes just price in q.77, where Aquinas asks whether one may sell a thing for more than it is worth, whether defects must be revealed, and whether merchants may sell for more than they paid — a discussion that shows him thinking about exchange, value, owner-specific loss, and morally permissible gain. Finally the book examines usury in q.78, where Aquinas gives the classic account: in a mutuum, the lender may not demand payment for the use of money, because the use of consumable money is not separable from the money itself. Yet he also recognizes that compensation may be owed for actual loss, and that partnership differs from lending because the investor may share risk. These small openings become decisive for later development. Part III therefore establishes Aquinas as both strict and subtle: he rejects usury but not every surplus payment; he condemns selling the use of money in a mutuum but allows compensation for real loss and profit-sharing in genuine partnership. Later theologians will build, stretch, and contest these distinctions.

Part IV presents the extrinsic titles directly, treated as this preface has treated them: not as loopholes but as moral tests, each asking whether a surplus payment corresponds to a real circumstance external to the bare loan. This part introduces the four central titles — poena conventionalis / morae poena, the penalty for late payment; damnum emergens, actual loss incurred; lucrum cessans, profit foregone; periculum sortis, risk of the principal — together with the adjacent categories: stipendium laboris, compensation for labor; expense reimbursement; carentia pecuniae, the lack or absence of money; and insurance-like compensation for assuming risk. It is the conceptual hinge of the book: everything before it prepares the moral and legal grammar, and everything after it traces the titles in detail.

Part V follows poena conventionalis, the penalty for delay. The title begins not as a theological invention but as a legal penalty clause: a borrower promises to repay on a certain date, and if he fails, a penalty may be imposed — the loan remains formally gratuitous while the penalty arises from default. But the title is morally unstable from the beginning. A lender can manipulate the due date, expect default, or set the penalty so high that late payment becomes profitable; a penalty can be called a penalty while functioning as interest. This part follows the title through Robert of Courçon, Raymond of Peñafort, Hostiensis, Aquinas, Duns Scotus, Bernardino of Siena, Sant'Antonino of Florence, Salamanca, Molina, Lessius, and Alphonsus Liguori, and shows how the tradition gradually develops its anti-abuse logic: a penalty is legitimate only if it is truly penal, proportionate, avoidable, and not in fraud of the usury prohibition. The modern question becomes: when is a late fee actually compensation, and when is it a revenue model?

Part VI follows damnum emergens, actual loss incurred — the cleanest title, because its logic is restorative. If the lender suffers a real loss because of the loan, the lender may be compensated; the payment does not reward capital but repairs damage and restores equality. This part begins with interesse, the older legal concept of compensation for loss, and follows the title through canon law, Hostiensis, Aquinas, late medieval development, Cajetan, Salamanca, Molina, Lessius, Lugo, Benedict XIV, and Alphonsus Liguori. Aquinas's formula is central: compensation for loss is not selling the use of money, but avoiding loss. Yet the title becomes more difficult as commercial life grows more complex. What counts as actual loss? Legal costs? Emergency liquidity harm? Administrative expense? Lost ability to meet one's own obligations? The title is safe only when the loss is real, causal, documented, and proportionate. The modern question becomes: when is cost recovery genuine, and when does "cost" become a disguised target return?

Part VII follows lucrum cessans, foregone profit — the most controversial title. Aquinas resists it because the lender would be selling what he does not yet have: a future profit may never materialize, and to charge for it risks turning possibility into property. Yet later theologians recognize that not all foregone gain is imaginary. A merchant's capital may already be ordered toward trade; a good may have a concrete future value; lending may interrupt a real and licit profit-making activity. In such cases, foregone gain begins to look like a compensable detriment. This part follows the title through Gregory IX's Naviganti, Raymond of Peñafort, Hostiensis, Aquinas, Peter John Olivi, Sant'Antonino, Bernardino, Cajetan, Conrad Summenhart, Domingo de Soto, Martín de Azpilcueta / Navarrus, Molina, Lessius, Lugo, and Alphonsus. The title's danger is that it can expand into universal opportunity cost: if every lender can say that capital could have earned elsewhere, then every borrower owes tribute to hypothetical alternatives. The scholastic tradition tries to prevent this by requiring a concrete alternative use, licit gain, real intention, causality, and proportionate valuation. The modern question becomes: when is opportunity cost morally real, and when is it simply capital demanding payment for not being somewhere else?

Part VIII follows periculum sortis, risk of the principal — the title that concerns the structure of the transaction itself. It asks whether the capital is genuinely exposed to loss. If the lender's principal is protected, guaranteed, collateralized, senior, or enforceable regardless of outcome, the lender is not truly sharing the enterprise's downside; but if the funder can actually lose the principal, then compensation for risk may be morally different from usury. This part begins with the prehistory of risk-bearing contracts — societas, commenda, foenus nauticum, insurance-like arrangements, and exchange contracts — then turns to Aquinas's distinction between mutuum and partnership, Alexander of Alexandria's early formulation in the Tractatus de Usuris, late medieval hesitation, Medina, Juan de la Peña, Salamanca, Molina, Lessius, Lugo, and the later rigorist backlash represented by Daniele Concina. The title's danger is that every loan has some risk; if ordinary default risk were enough, the doctrine would collapse, and the tradition therefore struggles to distinguish genuine capital exposure from generic lending risk. The modern question becomes: when does capital truly bear downside, and when does it use the language of risk while shifting loss onto others?

Part IX turns to practical institutions and adjacent titles. The Monti di Pietà are central here: Franciscan-inspired charitable lending institutions created to protect the poor from predatory lenders, which nevertheless required buildings, staff, records, administration, and enforcement. Could they charge enough to cover costs without becoming usurious? The debates around the Monti show that the tradition was not opposed to institutional sustainability — it insisted only that sustainability remain ordered toward service rather than profit. This part also examines stipendium laboris, compensation for labor; administrative expense; the Fifth Lateran Council's approval of the Monti; and the role of Bernardino and Antonino in practical moral finance. It then turns to carentia pecuniae, the absence or lack of money, especially in Lessius — a title that approaches the modern concept of a liquidity premium or term compensation, and that drew Lugo's important criticism: if absence of money alone justifies payment, then almost no contract remains usurious. Finally, this part looks at exchange, bills, public debt, and disguised loans, asking how theologians classified contracts when finance became more complex than a simple loan.

Part X gathers the historical figures and modern scholars who help reconstruct the story. It follows the movement from early canonists and confessors — Robert of Courçon, Raymond of Peñafort, Hostiensis — through Aquinas, Duns Scotus, Peter John Olivi, Alexander of Alexandria, Bernardino, Antonino, Summenhart, Cajetan, Soto, Navarrus, Medina, Juan de la Peña, Molina, Lessius, Lugo, Benedict XIV, Alphonsus, and Concina. It then turns to modern scholarship: David Graeber, Michael Hudson, John T. Noonan, Odd Langholm, Giacomo Todeschini, Raymond de Roover, Fabio Monsalve, Wim Decock, André Lapidus, John Munro, and Pierre Januard. The purpose is not merely bibliographic; each scholar illuminates a different layer: Graeber and Hudson on ancient debt and release, Noonan and Langholm on scholastic usury, Todeschini on Franciscan economic thought, de Roover on exchange and late medieval commerce, Monsalve on the late Spanish scholastics and the extrinsic titles, Decock on Lessius and contract law, Lapidus on information and risk, Munro on economic history, and Januard on Aquinas and risk. This part shows that the book is not built on a single narrative source but is a synthesis across legal history, theology, economic history, biblical studies, and moral philosophy.

Part XI steps back from the chronology and asks what the doctrine means as a system. The four titles are compared directly, each placed on a spectrum from concrete to abstract, from past loss to future possibility, from event-based compensation to structural risk-sharing — and each shown to have a legitimate form and an abusive form. A late-payment penalty can be discipline or revenue; actual loss can be compensation or fiction; foregone profit can be concrete or imaginary; risk can be borne or merely invoked. The moral difference lies not in the label but in the structure. This part then distinguishes compensation from extraction: compensation restores equality, remains proportionate to real burden, and shares risk honestly; extraction uses another's need as opportunity, multiplies claims through complexity, and protects capital while exposing others. It also translates the old categories into modern capital structures. Debt often resembles a mutuum, because repayment is fixed and enforceable. Equity sometimes resembles a societas, but not always: venture capital may be formally risk-bearing while practically extractive through preferences, control rights, and exit pressure, and private equity may use debt to shift downside while extracting fees. Opportunity cost may become a universalized lucrum cessans; a risk premium may become a hollow periculum sortis if capital is protected from actual loss. This part supplies the conceptual bridge from scholastic doctrine to non-extractive finance.

Part XII applies the historical theology to modern financial design. It begins by asking how to audit a modern contract scholastically. What is the legal form? What is the moral form? Who owns the capital? Who governs? Who bears risk? What justifies surplus? Which title is invoked? Is it documented? Is it proportionate? Is it double-counted? Is the borrower vulnerable? Does the structure create partnership or domination? It then translates each title into a modern diagnostic tool: poena conventionalis becomes a test for late fees and default interest; damnum emergens a test for cost recovery and documented loss; lucrum cessans a test for opportunity cost and foregone alternatives; periculum sortis a test for risk-bearing, subordination, and loss absorption; stipendium laboris a test for management fees and stewardship labor; carentia pecuniae a warning about liquidity premiums and the monetization of time. This part also introduces the idea of a moral-finance simulator: a way of making assumptions explicit, classifying surplus claims, testing risk allocation, modeling downside, and identifying extraction geometries. It connects scholastic moral theology to modern accounting, dashboard design, Exact Online exports, auditability, related-party payments, mission drift, and governance countermeasures. The point is not to reduce theology to code but to show that moral reasoning can become operational: if finance has dashboards for yield, risk, and liquidity, it can also have dashboards for justice, vulnerability, loss allocation, and extraction.

Part XIII returns to the book's central claim. The old doctrine does not teach that every surplus is unjust; it teaches that surplus must explain itself — must show whether it arises from restoration, participation, labor, cost, or genuine risk, and that it is not merely a claim on another's need. Nor does the doctrine teach that legal permission is enough. A contract may be valid and still morally disordered. A return may be market-standard and still extractive. A risk premium may be mathematically sophisticated and still shift real loss onto the vulnerable. A mission-driven fund may still drift toward the logic of capital unless its governance, accounting, and return structures are disciplined. The conclusion therefore argues for a renewed moral economy: one in which capital is structured to serve work, community, mission, technology, stewardship, and life. The old scholastic question becomes the modern one: Who owns? Who governs? Who bears risk? Who receives surplus? Who can lose everything? Who is protected? Who is served?

This book is an attempt to recover the discipline of asking those questions carefully. It is a call not to abandon finance but to convert it. Capital can serve: it can build, sustain, repair, steward, and protect. It can also dominate, extract, and hide its claims behind necessity. The tradition of usury, rightly understood, helps us tell the difference. That is why the history matters. The past rarely gives us answers we can use as they stand; what it gives us is sharper questions, and moral economy begins there.

chapters.title: Debt Before Money: The Ancient Near Eastern Background
chapters.label: Chapter 1

sections.title: 1.1 Loans before modern markets
paragraphs: Before there were banks, bonds, venture funds, interest-rate models, collateral packages, credit scores, or central banks, there was debt. It is one of the oldest recorded social facts of civilization, appearing wherever human beings live with harvests, households, obligations, scarcity, surplus, memory, and time. Someone has grain now; someone else needs grain before the next harvest. Someone has silver; someone else has an obligation to settle. Someone has seed; someone else needs to plant. Someone has livestock, tools, oil, or food; someone else has a family to keep alive, a field to cultivate, a tax to pay, a ritual obligation to fulfill, or a debt already coming due. Long before debt became a matter of financial markets, it was a matter of survival.

The modern imagination tends to begin elsewhere — with the market: individuals meeting as free and equal exchangers, each bringing property to the transaction, bargaining by preference, accepting or rejecting a contract. In that world, debt is one financial instrument among others, a voluntary exchange of present money for future money priced by time, risk, inflation, and opportunity cost. But the story does not begin there. The oldest world of debt is not autonomous investors seeking yield; it is households trying to survive the distance between need and provision — seedtime and harvest, hunger and storage, drought and tax, obligation and dependence. A loan there is not yet an abstract claim on discounted cash flows. It is a social relation stretched across time.

That does not make ancient lending innocent or purely charitable. Far from it. Ancient societies knew interest, collateral, pledges, merchants, contracts, and creditor enforcement. They knew loans could be profitable, that debt could create hierarchy, and that one person's emergency could become another person's opportunity. But they also knew something modern finance often forgets: debt is never merely mathematical; it binds people.

The simplest ancient loans were loans of consumables — grain, silver, oil, seed, food. Borrowing a cloak, a plow, or an animal means returning the identical thing; these were loans of things consumed, planted, spent, mixed, eaten, or exchanged, so the borrower returned not the same grain or the same pieces of silver but an equivalent amount later. The structure should already sound familiar. It is not yet the Roman mutuum, but materially it is moving in the same world: a fungible thing is transferred, used by being consumed or alienated, and an equivalent is owed later. The legal word comes later; the human pattern comes first.

So a farmer borrowed seed grain before planting and repaid grain after harvest; a household borrowed food during scarcity and repaid when the stores were full; a merchant received silver and returned silver after trade; a tenant or smallholder borrowed to meet obligations to palace, temple, landlord, or tax collector; a family pledged land, tools, animals, or even the labor of household members as security.

These were not marginal practices; they belong to the structure of early agrarian civilization. Agriculture itself creates temporal vulnerability. The need to eat is daily, but the harvest comes once a season; taxes fall due at fixed times whether the crop was good or bad; and a flood, a drought, an illness, a pest, a war, or a failed harvest can turn a self-sufficient household into a dependent one very quickly. The distance between today's need and tomorrow's possible repayment is the space in which debt arises.

That is why ancient debt was so closely tied to time — not time as an abstract financial input but time as waiting: for rain, for harvest, for a caravan to return, for a debtor to recover, for a household to regain its balance. A loan bridged time. The bridge could become a trap.

If the harvest succeeded, the loan could be repaid. If it failed, the debt rolled forward. Interest accumulated; collateral could be seized; land could pass out of the household. A child could be pledged. A wife, a son, a daughter, or the debtor himself could enter service. What began as help across a season could become dependency across generations. This is the recurring danger at the root of the entire history of usury: the conversion of temporary need into permanent subordination.

The ancient world saw the danger with remarkable clarity. Its laws often accepted interest-bearing debt but limited creditor power — regulating pledges, addressing crop failure, specifying what could and could not be taken, sometimes punishing abusive creditors. Most dramatically, rulers sometimes proclaimed debt cancellations, wiping away certain debts and restoring persons or property. These releases were not simply private mercy; they were political and social maintenance. Where too many households lost land, labor, and freedom to creditors, society grew unstable; concentrated claims could fracture the social body; and if the obligations of debt overwhelmed the obligations of kinship, worship, land tenure, and subsistence, the whole order could begin to break.

This is one reason the story of usury must begin before the scholastics. Medieval theologians inherited not only biblical prohibitions and Roman legal forms but an ancient social memory: debt can become domination — swallowing households, turning neighbors into masters and dependents, converting need into a machine for accumulation.

The modern financial world often hides all this beneath abstraction. We speak of credit allocation, yield, risk-adjusted return, liquidity, leverage, and debt service — terms that describe real things but can conceal the older reality: someone owes, someone can demand, someone can enforce; someone can wait and someone cannot; someone can walk away and someone cannot. Ancient debt made the asymmetry visible. It was written on land, harvests, bodies, and households, and the creditor's claim was not merely a line in a spreadsheet. It could become a claim on the debtor's future, labor, family, and freedom.

None of this means every creditor was cruel, every debtor innocent, or every loan exploitative. Ancient economies needed credit: households needed ways to survive bad seasons, merchants needed capital, temples and palaces needed accounting, communities needed ways of sharing resources across time. Debt could be useful, even necessary. And precisely because it was necessary, it was dangerous. A society cannot abolish dependence, need, risk, seasonality, or time — but neither can it allow those realities to become unlimited instruments of extraction.

The whole later doctrine of usury grows inside this tension. Lending can serve life: it can bridge hardship, allow planting, trade, repair, recovery, and survival; it can be an act of solidarity across time. And lending can feed on life: it can exploit hunger, bad harvests, social obligation, and desperation; it can make the poor pay for being poor, and turn the uncertainty of the vulnerable into the security of the creditor.

The scholastics will eventually build technical distinctions for navigating that tension. They will ask whether a contract is a mutuum or a partnership, and whether payment above principal comes from the loan itself or from an extrinsic title; they will distinguish actual loss from foregone gain, penalty from interest, risk-sharing from guaranteed repayment, using words like damnum emergens, lucrum cessans, and periculum sortis. But before all of that there is the simple ancient scene: a person in need receives something consumable from someone who has enough to lend, and promises to return an equivalent later. Everything depends on what happens next. Does the loan help restore the borrower to life, or does it give the lender a claim that grows stronger as the borrower grows weaker? That is the first moral question of debt.

sections.title: 1.2 Hammurabi and the regulation of creditor power
paragraphs: The ancient world did not wait for philosophers or theologians to discover that debt could become dangerous. Long before the scholastics debated compensation for loss, foregone profit, delay, or risk, lawgivers were regulating the relation between debtor and creditor.

One of the most famous witnesses is the Code of Hammurabi, from the eighteenth century before Christ. Hammurabi ruled Babylon from about 1792 to 1750 BC, and his laws survive on a pillar of polished black stone just over seven feet tall — diorite or basalt, depending on which specialist you ask; the argument has now run for more than a century. The stone has had an eventful afterlife: an Elamite king, Shutruk-Nahhunte, hauled it to Susa as war plunder in the twelfth century BC; French archaeologists dug it up there in the winter of 1901–02; today it stands in the Louvre, the clearest surviving example of an ancient society ordering economic life through public rules. The Code is not a modern statute book, nor a systematic civil code in the contemporary sense. But it reveals a world in which lending, borrowing, pledging, repayment, interest, crop failure, and creditor misconduct were already central enough to require legal attention.

For the history of usury, the lesson is that the basic problem is not medieval, and not specifically Christian, but civilizational. A household needs grain; a cultivator needs seed; a merchant needs silver. A borrower promises repayment, and a lender wants assurance. A crop may fail, a debtor may default, a creditor may overreach, a pledge may be abused — and the lender's claim may become a claim not only on goods but on labor, children, land, and freedom. Hammurabi's world knows all of this.

The Code regulates loans of silver and grain, the two basic media of ancient credit — silver as unit of value and medium for obligations, grain as part of the agricultural cycle of subsistence, seed, harvest, and tax. Both could be lent, repaid with increase, and turned into instruments of dependence.

The very presence of such rules tells us something simple but profound: ancient societies did not regard credit as morally self-regulating. A creditor's claim was not automatically just because it had been contracted, nor a bargain sacred simply because it had been made; lending creates power, and power must be limited. The Code even set ceilings on increase — a fifth on silver, a third on barley — and a lender who took more than the law allowed forfeited what he had lent.

Beyond rates, the Code addresses the practical fabric of the credit relation: a borrower who cannot repay in one form but can repay in another; the taking and handling of pledges; disputes over repayment; consequences for creditors who manipulate terms or extract more than is permitted. It also faces agricultural risk directly. The forty-eighth law provides that if a storm flooded a man's field, or the harvest failed, or the grain did not grow for want of water, he owed his creditor nothing that year: he "washed" his debt-tablet — the record was clay, and could be dissolved — and no interest ran while the fields were bare.

That provision is already a primitive version of a question that will later become central to Christian moral theology: who should bear the risk when reality does not unfold as expected? If a man borrowed seed and the harvest failed through no fault of his own, should the lender's claim stand unchanged and the interest go on accumulating? Should the creditor be entitled to treat every failure as the borrower's responsibility? Should a household lose land, labor, or children because rain did not fall? Ancient law did not always answer in ways a modern reader would find merciful. But it saw the questions, and that alone matters.

The crop-failure provisions show that debt was already understood as embedded in contingency. Repayment was never merely a matter of moral will; it depended on weather, harvest, animals, labor, household health, political demands, and the larger agricultural economy. A debt contract might be fixed, but the world into which it was written was not. Here is one of the oldest cracks in the idea of absolute creditor right: a claim enforced without regard to reality can become unjust. Law must ask not only what was promised, but what happened, who bore the risk, and whether enforcement would destroy the very household whose productivity made repayment possible in the first place.

Pledges carry the same moral weight. A pledge is not repayment but security — something the creditor can hold, seize, or use if the debtor fails — and it is charged territory because pledges come out of the debtor's sphere of life: tools, animals, land rights, household goods, and sometimes family members or their labor. A pledge can reassure the lender. It can also intensify domination. The Code's one hundred seventeenth law governed the hardest case in plain terms: a man overwhelmed by debt might give his wife, his son, or his daughter into service in the creditor's house — but for three years only. In the fourth year they went free.

The later biblical law about returning a poor man's cloak before nightfall belongs to this same moral universe. The cloak may be collateral, but it is also the poor man's covering — not merely an asset but part of his survival. Seen only as security, collateral yields a simple creditor's logic: take whatever secures repayment. Seen as bound up with life, it forces a different question: what may a creditor hold without violating the debtor's dignity? Hammurabi's world does not yet give the biblical answer. But it shows that ancient law already understood the need to regulate the creditor's hand.

Then there is interest itself, which the ancient world had in abundance. Its existence did not make all increase morally unproblematic. Rates could be customary, capped, regulated, or contested, and a lender who exceeded agreed or lawful terms could be punished; ancient economic life already distinguished permitted from abusive increase. The later Christian tradition will draw the line differently — asking whether any gain from a mutuum is licit at all, then developing extrinsic titles to distinguish compensation from usury. Hammurabi's law does not do this; it permits forms of interest that later Christian theologians would question. But it shares one important assumption: creditor power cannot be unlimited. Debt creates a legal relation, and law must judge the relation.

This is why Hammurabi belongs in the prehistory of the usury doctrine — not because Babylonian law contains Christian theology in embryo, and not because it anticipates the scholastic distinction between intrinsic and extrinsic titles. It does not; its moral universe, theology, politics, and social order are not those of Israel, the Church, or the medieval universities. What it shows is the ancient architecture of the problem: fungible loans and repayment with increase, pledges and defaults, crop failure and creditor abuse, legal intervention, and, underneath everything, anxiety about households falling under the weight of debt. These are the raw materials out of which later moral theology will build.

The Code is also a reminder that the problem of usury is never only about money. It is about the relation between fixed claims and fragile life. A creditor's claim is written as a certainty — repay this amount by this date — but the borrower's world is not certain. Harvests fail, children fall ill, animals die, floods come, trade routes close, taxes rise, labor disappears, need arrives before income. Finance turns the future into an obligation; life turns it into a risk. When the two meet, law must decide whether obligation will bend to life or life will be broken by obligation. Hammurabi's laws are one early attempt to keep the creditor's claim from becoming socially destructive — partial, hierarchical, bound to the assumptions of its time, but real. The regulation of debt belongs to the foundations of civilization.

For this book, Hammurabi matters because he stands at one end of a very long arc: at this end, ancient rules about grain, silver, pledges, and crop failure; at the other, scholastic debates about the mutuum, just price, risk, loss, and foregone profit; between them, Scripture's insistence that debt must not recreate slavery among God's people. The line is not direct, but the concern is continuous: debt must be governed because creditor power can exceed justice, limited because human need can be monetized, and judged because a contract can be formally valid and socially destructive. The later Christian doctrine will sharpen that judgment, asking not only whether the creditor obeyed the rules but whether the surplus itself is justified — the lender restored or enriched, risk shared or shifted, a penalty repairing injustice or disguising yield, a foregone gain real or imaginary, capital serving life or feeding on distress. But Hammurabi shows that the first question is older still: how do we stop debt from turning need into domination?

sections.title: 1.3 Debt cancellation and the "clean slate"
paragraphs: Regulation was not the ancient world's only answer to creditor power. Sometimes the problem grew too large for ordinary rules — the accumulated claims of creditors threatened households, villages, temples, palaces, fields, and labor itself — and then ancient societies turned to a more dramatic remedy: debt cancellation.

Modern people tend to hear "debt cancellation" as a radical invention, an ideological interruption of the normal workings of economic life. In the ancient Near East, proclamations of release belonged to the ordinary political and moral vocabulary of kingship. A ruler could proclaim a restoration — a release, a return to order. Certain debts were wiped away; persons held in debt-servitude were released; land or family rights were restored; the social field was cleared so that the community did not collapse under the accumulated weight of claims.

The practice was already old when Babylon took it up. The earliest cancellation on record was proclaimed by Enmetena, ruler of the Sumerian city of Lagash, around 2400 BC; his inscription says that he "restored the child to its mother, and the mother to her child," and canceled the interest owed. The Sumerian word for such an act was ama-gi — literally "return to the mother," because it restored people to their families. The Akkadian-speaking world called the release andurārum, and the kings of Babylon proclaimed it in edicts of mīšarum, "justice" or "equity." Scholars have counted roughly thirty general cancellations in Mesopotamia between about 2400 and 1400 BC. Whatever else these proclamations were, they were not improvisations.

The idea is sometimes described as a clean slate. The phrase is modern, but it names an ancient recognition: debt, allowed to compound indefinitely, can become socially destructive — at some point, enforcing every claim no longer preserves order but destroys the very households and productive relationships on which order depends.

A debt system can become self-consuming. Creditors accumulate claims; debtors lose land, labor, and freedom; families slide into dependency. Productive smallholders become tenants, servants, or runaways; fields are abandoned; military obligations weaken; tax capacity declines. The palace itself may suffer if too much of the population falls under private creditor control. What begins as private contract becomes public danger, and debt cancellation was one way ancient rulers reasserted the primacy of the social order over accumulated private claims.

None of this requires romanticizing ancient kings. The proclamations were not necessarily democratic, egalitarian, or even compassionate: a new king might proclaim release to secure loyalty, stabilize his reign, restore soldiers and cultivators to their households, or break the power of local creditors, and release could serve the palace as much as the poor. It is morally significant all the same, because it reveals that ancient societies understood something modern finance often resists: creditor claims are not absolute. They exist inside a social order that can judge them.

The best-preserved example is the Edict of Ammisaduqa, from the second millennium before Christ. Ammisaduqa was Hammurabi's great-great-grandson, and on taking the throne of Babylon around 1646 BC he proclaimed a mīšarum — the only such edict whose text survives nearly complete. Like other Mesopotamian release edicts, it did not abolish every obligation. Commercial debts between merchants might be treated differently from subsistence debts; certain obligations remained. The point was not to erase economic life but to restore social balance where debt had become destructive.

Ancient cancellation, in other words, was not a blanket refusal of repayment but a selective intervention into debt relations that threatened the social body, differentiating between kinds of debt, creditor, debtor, and social harm. The clean slate was already a moral classification system. It asked what kind of debt this was: commercial credit between parties able to bear the risk, or a subsistence loan to a household in distress? An obligation to the palace, or a private claim that had swallowed a family's land or labor? Debt that preserved exchange, or debt that was dissolving the community? These questions will reappear later in a very different language — whether a contract is a mutuum or a societas, whether surplus arises from the loan itself or from an extrinsic title, whether the lender is compensated for loss or enriched by need. But the deeper pattern is already visible: debt must be morally classified, because not all claims are equal.

The clean slate also reveals a profound tension between memory and mercy. Debt depends on memory: someone must remember what was given, when, to whom, under what terms, and what must be returned. Accounting is structured memory; a debt record says that this obligation persists across time, that the past has a claim on the future. Release interrupts that memory — not denying that the debt existed, not pretending nothing was owed, but declaring that the claim's continuation now threatens a greater good. The memory remains, but its enforceability is ended; the debtor is no longer bound; the future is reopened.

This is why debt cancellation has always had spiritual resonance: it is not only an economic act but a liberation from a binding past, a declaration that a person, a family, a community is not reducible to the obligation that has come to define them — that life must be able to begin again. And this is why the biblical Jubilee tradition will later carry such enormous theological power. Jubilee is not simply an economic policy. It is a vision of time redeemed from accumulation, a refusal to let debt become destiny, a proclamation that land, labor, family, and worship ultimately belong to God, not to the creditor's ledger.

But before Jubilee appears in Israel's Scripture, something like the problem is already present in Mesopotamia. The ancient world already knows that debts can become too heavy to carry, that households can be lost, that servitude can arise through obligation rather than conquest, and that the law's ordinary enforcement may not be enough. Sometimes justice requires not only payment, but release.

The clean slate therefore belongs to the deep prehistory of moral economy: the question of debt is not only what was promised, but what happens if every promise is enforced without mercy. Here lies one of the great differences between a merely contractual view of debt and a moral-theological one. The contractual view begins and ends with consent and enforcement: Did the borrower agree? Was the contract valid? Has payment come due? What remedies are available? The moral-theological view asks more. What was the borrower's condition, and was the agreement made under distress? What kind of life does enforcement leave behind? Does the debt preserve order or destroy it — does repayment restore justice or deepen domination? Is the creditor's claim still ordered toward the common good, or has it become an idol?

Ancient cancellation also forces us to see that credit is political. A creditor class can accumulate not only money but power over land, labor, and governance; if too many families fall into dependency, the political community itself changes, and debt becomes a mechanism of social reorganization. We like to imagine debt and investment today as private arrangements between consenting parties; in fact, large-scale debt structures determine who owns housing, who controls companies, who governs infrastructure, who captures technological value, who bears ecological risk, and who can survive a downturn. Private claims reorder public life, and societies have always had to ask whether creditor claims serve the whole or gradually consume it.

Which is not to say that cancellation is always just. A society cannot function if every obligation is meaningless: trust requires memory, credit requires some expectation of repayment, and promises matter. The lender's position matters too — especially when the lender is not a predatory elite but another household, a community institution, or a steward of entrusted resources. Debt release must itself be governed by justice. But the opposite error is the more common one in financial societies: treating repayment as absolute while treating the borrower's life as secondary. The clean slate is a refusal of that absolutism. It says that debt is real but not ultimate, that claims are binding but not divine, and that the ledger — necessary as it is — must answer, in the end, to life.

This insight will become central to the Christian tradition, whose very language of redemption is entangled with debt, bondage, ransom, and release. To be redeemed is to be freed from a binding claim. To forgive a debt is not merely to adjust a balance sheet; it is to release a person from a future controlled by the past. This is why Jesus can teach his disciples to pray for the forgiveness of debts. The metaphor works because debt is not merely financial. It is existential: the condition of being bound, answerable, exposed, unable to free oneself by one's own resources.

The scholastic doctrine of usury will not simply repeat the clean slate. Medieval theologians will not argue that all debts should be canceled; they will develop a far more precise analysis of contracts, ownership, use, risk, loss, price, and restitution, asking when surplus is unjust, when compensation is permitted, when risk-sharing is real, and when legal form disguises moral disorder. But the ancient clean slate remains in the background as a warning: a debt system that cannot release becomes a system that cannot forgive, a system that cannot forgive eventually forgets what human beings are for, and a financial system that has forgotten what human beings are for will turn every future into collateral. The clean slate was the ancient world's way of declaring that it must not be so — its answer to the danger that creditor claims might become stronger than households, than land, than kinship, than worship, than the community's ability to begin again.

In the long history of moral economy, then, debt cancellation is not an anomaly. It is one of the first signs that debt belongs under judgment. Not every debt should be canceled, not every creditor is unjust, and not every borrower is oppressed — but no creditor's claim is beyond moral examination. That is the lesson that passes from ancient Mesopotamia into biblical Jubilee, and from biblical Jubilee into the Christian suspicion of usury. Debt may be necessary; credit may serve life. But when debt becomes a mechanism for permanent domination, justice must find a way to say: enough. The slate must sometimes be cleared so that life can continue.

sections.title: 1.4 Michael Hudson and the lost tradition of debt release
paragraphs: The modern recovery of ancient debt cancellation owes much to the economist Michael Hudson, who has argued for decades — most fully in ...and forgive them their debts (2018) — that the earliest economies cannot be understood through the lens of modern market individualism. Ancient debt, in Hudson's telling, was not simply a private arrangement between two autonomous parties. It was embedded in the structure of land tenure, labor obligations, temple and palace administration, taxation, military service, and household survival: to understand the debt, you must understand the whole social order around it.

Hudson's central claim is that ancient Near Eastern societies developed a tradition of periodic debt release because they recognized the danger of allowing creditor claims to accumulate without limit. If debts were enforced indefinitely, cultivators could lose their land, families could lose members to debt service, and local communities could become dependent on private creditors. This was not only a moral problem but a political and economic one. A king needed free cultivators, functioning households, taxable production, and men for his army; if too much of society fell into debt bondage or dependency, the ruler's own base of power was weakened.

Debt release, in Hudson's account, was therefore not an accidental kindness but a structural feature of early statecraft. The ruler periodically restored balance by annulling certain personal debts, liberating debt-servants, and returning families to the land or status they needed in order to function. These acts were not usually directed at all debts without distinction: they focused on the agrarian, personal, and subsistence debts that threatened household viability, while commercial obligations among merchants might be treated differently. The aim was not to abolish credit but to prevent credit from devouring the social order.

Debt release, Hudson shows, is therefore not peripheral to moral economy; it is one of its oldest institutions. Where the modern assumption treats cancellation as an exceptional interruption of normal economic life, Hudson reverses the picture: in the ancient Near East, the unlimited enforcement of creditor claims was the danger, and release was how rulers restored a viable order. To modern ears, cancellation sounds like disorder; in the ancient context, it could be precisely the restoration of order.

Hence the phrase "lost tradition." Modern societies, Hudson argues, have largely forgotten that early civilizations treated debt as something requiring periodic political and moral reset. We have inherited the ledger, but not the release. We have kept the idea that obligations continue across time, and lost the idea that time itself may need to be redeemed from accumulated claims.

This is why Hudson matters so much for the biblical Jubilee. The Jubilee tradition in Leviticus, he argues, does not emerge from nowhere. It stands within a broader ancient Near Eastern world of debt release, restoration, and liberation from servitude — indeed, the Hebrew word for the release Leviticus proclaims, derôr, is cognate with the Akkadian andurārum. But the biblical tradition transforms that world theologically. What was sometimes a royal act of political restoration becomes, in Israel, a covenantal command under the sovereignty of God. The land is not ultimately the creditor's; the people are not ultimately the creditor's; time itself is not ultimately the creditor's. The land belongs to God, and the people are not to be permanently enslaved by one another. That transformation is decisive: in Mesopotamia the king proclaims release, while in Leviticus the rhythm of release is rooted in divine ownership and covenantal order. The point is no longer only that society needs periodic reset; it is that no human creditor has an ultimate claim over what belongs to God.

Here is one of the deep roots of the later Christian suspicion of usury: the problem is not only that the rate may be too high, but that a creditor's claim can become totalizing — extending across time, gathering increase, absorbing labor, seizing pledges, transforming need into dependence. If no higher law interrupts it, the debt becomes a kind of false sovereignty, saying to the debtor: your future belongs to this claim. Debt release says otherwise.

Hudson also restores the material and institutional background of that claim. Debt is not merely a metaphor; before it becomes a spiritual image, it is a social reality. People actually lost land and liberty; families surrendered labor; fields passed from one household to another; the poor became dependent on the wealthy. The language of release is rooted in these concrete realities, and that keeps us from spiritualizing the tradition too quickly. When Scripture speaks of release, redemption, slavery, debt, and forgiveness, it speaks from a world where debt could bind bodies, households, fields, and futures.
