A lender who demands repayment of the equivalent and also charges for the use sells the same thing twice — the return of the thing's equivalent plus the price of a use that is not separable from the thing. The mutuum thus exposes the hidden contradiction in usury. The lender wants the benefits of having transferred ownership and the benefits of having retained it: he wants the borrower to bear the obligation of an owner, since the equivalent is owed regardless of what the borrower did with the money, and he wants the payment due to an owner, since he charges for use as if the money remained his. That is the moral anatomy of usury in its strict form.

The second cut concerns risk. In a mutuum the borrower generally bears the ordinary risk of what happens to the money once ownership has passed. If he spends it badly, he still owes the equivalent; if he profits greatly, the profit is his; if the coins are lost, the obligation does not disappear with them, because the obligation is to return an equivalent. The lender's claim is fixed by the debt, not by the fortunes of the borrower's use. So the lender does not ordinarily share the enterprise risk — he is owed repayment whether or not the borrower succeeds — and that is exactly why he cannot claim a share of the borrower's profit. He did not share the downside; he stood outside with a claim.

In societas, partnership, everything is otherwise. Capital is placed into a common enterprise; the investor may lose and may gain; profit-sharing is morally intelligible because risk-sharing is real. The participant does not stand outside the venture with a guaranteed claim — he stands inside its uncertainty. The distinction between mutuum and societas gives the tradition its way of telling creditor from partner: the creditor asks to be repaid; the partner shares the venture's fate. And the difference is not merely legal but moral and spiritual. The creditor may be helpful, but he remains outside the borrower's risk; the partner becomes vulnerable with the enterprise. If capital wants the moral legitimacy of participation, it must accept the vulnerability of participation. This is why the later title periculum sortis matters: if the lender truly bears risk of principal, the relation may no longer be morally equivalent to a pure mutuum. But the risk must be real. A creditor cannot claim partnership-like surplus while preserving creditor-like protection. He cannot stand outside the storm and claim the wages of those who sailed.

The third cut concerns time. Modern finance often assumes that time itself justifies payment: money now is worth more than money later, so a borrower who holds money over time owes the lender for the duration. The scholastic tradition does not ignore time; it refuses to treat time as automatically saleable by the lender. In the mutuum the lender is not selling time and is not renting a durable thing across time. He has transferred ownership of a consumable and holds a claim to equivalent repayment, and the mere passage of time creates no title to more. Something must happen in time that justice recognizes: damage, delay, lost concrete use, genuine risk, labor, expense. Hence the importance of poena conventionalis, damnum emergens, lucrum cessans, and periculum sortis — they identify the moral events or structures that can arise around time. Delay may be culpable; loss may occur; gain may be interrupted; risk may be borne. But time as such is not enough. This is one of the sharpest challenges the tradition poses to modern finance, which treats the time value of money as axiomatic. The scholastics ask: whose time is being priced, and by what right? If the lender has suffered no actual loss, borne no genuine risk, provided no ongoing labor, and foregone no concrete use, what exactly does the passage of time justify?

The fourth cut concerns surplus. The mutuum reveals that surplus is not native to the contract: the bare loan for consumption creates an obligation to return the equivalent, and anything beyond that must explain itself. This is the foundation of the extrinsic-title doctrine. The lender may say: I suffered actual loss; the borrower delayed culpably; I gave up a concrete and licit gain; my principal was genuinely exposed to loss; I performed labor; I incurred expense; I insured risk. Each claim can be examined — some may be valid, some exaggerated, some fraudulent — but the mutuum requires the lender to name the basis of the surplus. It prevents surplus from appearing natural. This may be its most important moral function: it denaturalizes return. Modern finance treats return as the natural property of capital — capital expects yield, capital has cost, capital requires compensation. The mutuum says: not so fast. If this is a loan for consumption, the principal is owed, the surplus is not self-evident, and the title must be named.

The fifth cut concerns the borrower's benefit. A borrower may use the money well — avoid a greater loss, build a business, earn profit, survive a crisis. Does this benefit justify the lender's surplus? The strict analysis says no, not by itself. If ownership has passed, the borrower's gain is the borrower's. The lender may rejoice that the borrower benefited, but he cannot claim the benefit simply because his former money helped make it possible. The point is morally radical, because it resists a common intuition: "You benefited from my money, therefore I deserve part of the benefit." The tradition replies: only if the relation was structured as participation — partnership, risk-sharing, or another title-bearing form. In a mutuum you gave ownership and retained a claim to equivalent repayment; you did not retain a claim on all the fruits of the borrower's use. The modern application is direct, because capital often claims the right to participate in upside while avoiding full participation in downside — you built with our money, therefore we deserve a share. The scholastic question cuts through: what relation did you actually create? Were you a partner? Did you bear risk? Did you share governance responsibly? Did you expose your principal? Or did you retain a protected claim, and now seek additional upside anyway?

The sixth cut concerns the lender's deprivation. During the term of the loan the lender does not have the money, and the deprivation feels real. Does it automatically create a title? The stricter tradition says no. Being without the money is not, by itself, sufficient — otherwise every mutuum would bear return, and the usury prohibition would collapse. The deprivation must be connected to an actual loss, a concrete foregone gain, a real risk, or another recognized title. This is why carentia pecuniae, the lack of money, becomes so controversial in later scholastic thought: if the mere absence of money is compensable, all lending can be priced. The mutuum therefore asks the lender to distinguish feeling deprived from being unjustly damaged — a spiritually important distinction. A lender may experience the absence of money as loss because he desires control, liquidity, or gain, and not every desire creates a claim in justice. Christian moral theology disciplines desire by asking whether what is missed was actually owed, actually lost, or actually at risk.

The seventh cut concerns charity and justice. The mutuum can be gratuitous. In its morally clean form the loan for consumption is an act of assistance — the lender hands over fungible goods for a time and receives the equivalent back — and it can be an act of neighborly help, especially when the borrower is in need. Scripture's commands around lending to the poor presuppose exactly that possibility: lending can be a form of mercy. The usurious mutuum corrupts this mercy by making need productive for the lender. Not every loan must be charitable in the same way; commercial life is complex. But the gratuitous structure of the mutuum remains morally significant. Not every act of providing money should become an income source. Sometimes the proper return is simply repayment, and sometimes the surplus belongs not to justice but to greed.

The eighth cut concerns restoration. If usury has been taken, restitution may be owed — it follows from the anatomy of the contract. A lender who received more than the contract and justice permitted has taken what was not his, and the remedy is not only regret but restoration: the unjust surplus must be returned where possible. The doctrine is not merely theoretical. It reaches the ledger. It changes accounts. It demands repair. For modern non-extractive finance this is crucial. If a financing structure is found to be extractive, rebranding is not enough. The structure may need to be changed, terms rewritten, excess payments returned or redirected, control rights relinquished, repayment restructured. The moral analysis must become operational.

Together the eight cuts give us a method. Begin with the contract. Identify ownership, use, risk, obligation, and surplus. Ask whether the surplus arises from the loan itself: if it does, it is usurious; if it does not, identify the title, test the title, require proportionality, consider the borrower's condition, and demand restitution where injustice has occurred. The method is old but not obsolete. It can be applied to modern loans, revenue-based finance, convertible instruments, preferred equity, foundation recoverable grants, mission loans, venture term sheets, private equity structures, supplier finance, platform credit, and fund repayment waterfalls. The forms are new; the moral anatomy remains recognizable. What is the transaction really? Who owns what, and who owes what? Who uses what, who bears loss, and who receives gain? Who can enforce, and who can walk away? Who is bound — and who is served?

The mutuum teaches that financial morality begins with the structure of relation: it refuses to let money hide behind abstraction, asking what has passed, what remains, and what is claimed. This is why it became central to Christian moral theology — it made the old biblical concern precise. The poor borrower, the brother, the cloak, the release, and the God who hears all remain essential; the mutuum adds the legal clarity that in this kind of loan ownership passes and the equivalent is owed, so the lender may not charge for use as if ownership remained.

The whole moral anatomy of the mutuum can be summarized in one sentence. If mine has become yours, I may not charge you as though it remained mine. Everything else in the usury doctrine unfolds from that sentence.

chapters.part: Part II — Roman Law and the Legal Anatomy of Lending

chapters.title: Commutative Justice in the Summa
chapters.label: Chapter 6

sections.title: 6.1 Where Aquinas treats commutative justice
paragraphs: Aquinas does not begin his treatment of usury with money. He begins a long way back, with justice — and the distance is the point. Usury, for Aquinas, is not primarily a violation of financial efficiency, commercial custom, or economic productivity. It is a violation of justice, and specifically of commutative justice: the justice that governs exchanges, obligations, injuries, repayments, and restitution between persons.

Read only Aquinas's question on usury and the argument can seem narrow, a technical claim about money as a consumable good. But in the architecture of the Summa Theologiae, the question on usury comes near the end of a much larger treatment of justice, and the placement is deliberate. Aquinas wants the reader to understand lending, selling, price, fraud, repayment, and usury inside a broader account of what is due to another.

The relevant section is the Secunda Secundae, the Second Part of the Second Part of the Summa, where Aquinas treats the theological and cardinal virtues, justice among them. His discussion of justice begins with the concept of right, or ius, then moves through justice itself, injustice, judgment, the parts of justice, and restitution before reaching the various sins and acts related to justice. For the purposes of moral economy, several questions matter especially:

• II-II, q.57 - On right.
  • II-II, q.58 - On justice.
  • II-II, q.59 - On injustice.
  • II-II, q.60 - On judgment.
  • II-II, q.61 - On the parts of justice, including distributive and commutative justice.
  • II-II, q.62 - On restitution.
  • II-II, q.77 - On cheating in buying and selling, including just price.
  • II-II, q.78 - On the sin of usury.

The sequence is not accidental. Aquinas first establishes what justice is, then how it divides, then how injustice is repaired, and only afterward how particular economic acts can violate it. Buying and selling, just price, and usury are not isolated economic curiosities; they are applications of a larger moral structure — the last rooms in a building you reach only by walking through the rooms that explain them.

The most important question for commutative justice is II-II, q.61, "Of the Parts of Justice." In Article 1, Aquinas asks whether there are two species of justice, distributive and commutative, and follows Aristotle in answering that justice orders relations in two ways. One concerns the relation of the community to persons, above all in the distribution of common goods. The other concerns the relation of one person to another in exchanges and dealings. In Article 2 he explains that the two forms use different kinds of equality. Distributive justice follows a proportional or geometrical equality: goods are distributed according to some relevant proportion — office, need, merit, role, contribution, or another ordering principle of the community. Commutative justice follows arithmetic equality: it concerns what one person gives, takes, owes, damages, restores, buys, sells, borrows, lends, or pays in relation to another.

The distinction is essential. Commutative justice is the justice of one-to-one relations, and its question is always whether equality between the parties has been preserved. If one person takes too much, injures another, pays too little, charges too much, withholds what is owed, or receives what is not due, equality is disturbed, and justice requires that it be restored.

This is why usury belongs here. The usurer receives more than is due under the moral structure of the mutuum: he disturbs equality in exchange by demanding a surplus the loan itself does not justify. The problem is not only greed, though greed may be present. The problem is that the lender receives what is not owed.

Aquinas's treatment of restitution in q.62 follows naturally. If commutative justice concerns equality between persons, then injustice creates an inequality that must be repaired, and restitution restores the balance: whoever has taken what belongs to another, or received more than justice permits, must give it back. This is why usury requires restitution. The unjust surplus is not merely spiritually unfortunate, a symptom of a disordered heart. It is a taking. The lender has received what he had no right to receive, and insofar as possible he must restore it.

The placement matters for the extrinsic titles too, because each title is an attempt to show that the lender has not received more than justice permits. If there is actual loss, compensation may restore equality. If there is culpable delay, a penalty may answer a new injustice. If there is concrete foregone gain, later theologians will ask whether that lost gain has become a compensable detriment. If capital is genuinely at risk, profit may correspond to participation in uncertainty rather than payment for the mere use of money. The titles are not random exceptions carved out of the prohibition; they are attempts to show that equality in commutative justice has not been violated.

The place of q.77, on buying and selling, is also crucial. Aquinas treats just price immediately before usury, and this too is no accident. In q.77 he asks whether it is lawful to sell a thing for more than it is worth, whether defects must be disclosed, and whether merchants may sell at a profit — questions about equality in exchange. A seller who charges more than the thing is worth, or hides a defect, injures the buyer and receives more than justice permits. In q.78, Aquinas applies the same concern to lending: a lender who charges for the use of money in a mutuum receives more than justice permits, because the use of consumable money cannot be sold separately from the money itself. Just price and usury belong to the same moral field, the preservation of equality in exchange.

Modern readers need this pressed, because we habitually separate pricing from justice. A price is whatever the market bears; a loan term is whatever the parties agree to. Aquinas does not think this way. For him, exchange is morally charged because one person can injure another by taking more than is due. Consent matters, but consent does not automatically make an exchange just. A person can agree to an unjust price. A borrower can agree to usury. A desperate party can consent to terms that exploit his need. The moral question remains — and this refusal to reduce justice to procedure is one of the great strengths of the framework. That a transaction occurred voluntarily does not settle whether equality was preserved; that a price was accepted does not prove the price was just; that a borrower promised repayment with surplus does not prove the lender has a right to the surplus. The transaction must be judged according to what is due.

The same refusal explains why Aquinas remains relevant in a world so different from his own. Modern finance often assumes that sophistication, consent, disclosure, and legality are enough: if the parties are informed, the contract enforceable, the rate disclosed, the risks allocated, the transaction is treated as legitimate. Aquinas would ask a deeper question — does the transaction preserve justice between the parties? — and sophistication does not eliminate that question. It makes it more necessary, because the more complex a contract becomes, the easier it is to hide inequality inside form. A price can include hidden coercion. A fee can disguise yield. A penalty can monetize distress. A preferred return can shift risk, a covenant can transfer control, and a liquidation preference can let capital claim value ahead of mission, labor, and community. Legal complexity can make injustice harder to see. Commutative justice asks us to see it anyway.

Locating economic questions inside justice also blocks a purely utilitarian defense of finance. One might argue that interest-bearing lending produces economic growth, allocates capital, rewards saving, or enables investment. These may be real considerations, and Aquinas is not indifferent to usefulness. But usefulness does not eliminate justice. A practice can be productive and still unjust if it gives one person more than is due from another. The point lands squarely on non-extractive finance. A financing structure may help a company grow and still be extractive; provide needed capital and still demand unjust priority; enable innovation and still capture governance; fund a mission and still bend the mission toward investor exit. The fact that capital does something useful does not automatically justify every claim capital makes.

The question commutative justice asks instead — the question that will guide all of Aquinas's economic reasoning — is: what is due? In sale, what price is due? In restitution, what repair? In lending, what repayment? In partnership, what share? In injury, what compensation? In delay, what penalty or restoration? In risk, what return, if any? The language of "due" is the moral heart of justice.

It is also what distinguishes justice from generosity. A person may freely give more than is due: a borrower may offer a gift out of gratitude, a lender may forgive a debt out of mercy, a partner may accept less than his share for the sake of the enterprise. Charity may go beyond justice. But coercing, requiring, or contracting for what is not due violates justice, even if the other party agrees. This distinction will matter repeatedly in the usury tradition. A voluntary gift freely given after repayment may be permissible; a "gift" expected from the beginning is not truly free. A borrower's gratitude may be real; but if the lender structures the loan so that gratitude is effectively required, the gift becomes hidden interest. Aquinas and the later canonists are alert to this because justice concerns not the outward form of obligation but its reality.

For the same reason, the extrinsic titles must be genuine. Each identifies something that might be due beyond principal — and if the title is false, the surplus is not due. A fake loss does not create justice; neither does an engineered delay, a hypothetical opportunity cost, or a risk premium without real risk. Naming a title does not make the surplus due.

Aquinas's account of commutative justice therefore supplies the moral grammar for the entire book. Before asking whether a late penalty is legitimate, we must ask whether it restores equality or creates inequality; before asking whether actual loss can be compensated, whether the loss is real and caused by the transaction; before asking whether foregone profit can be claimed, whether the gain was concrete enough to be treated as due; before asking whether risk justifies return, whether the risk is truly borne by the one claiming the return; and before asking whether a modern fund is non-extractive, whether its surplus claims correspond to what is actually due. That is why this treatment belongs ahead of the chapters on just price and usury. It gives us the deeper norm: equality in relations between persons.

The gap between that norm and the modern idiom is profound. Modern finance speaks of optimization; Aquinas speaks of justice. Modern finance asks what return compensates capital; Aquinas asks what is due between persons. Modern finance asks how risk is priced; Aquinas asks who bears the risk and whether the claim is just. Modern finance asks whether terms clear the market; Aquinas asks whether the exchange preserves equality. He does not give us a full modern theory of finance — he does not know our institutions, instruments, data, or markets — but he gives us the moral starting point modern finance often lacks: economic exchange is not morally neutral. It is a place where justice is either preserved or violated.

That is why the usury question appears where it does in the Summa — not as a footnote to economic history, but as part of the virtue of justice. And once that is clear, the question "May the lender receive more?" becomes a specific form of a larger question:
Is this surplus truly due, or has one person taken more than justice permits from another?

sections.title: 6.2 Distributive justice versus commutative justice
paragraphs: Aquinas follows Aristotle in distinguishing distributive justice from commutative justice. The distinction may sound technical, and it is essential: without it, you cannot see why usury is a matter of justice rather than merely a matter of personal greed. Both forms of justice concern equality. They do not concern it in the same way.

Distributive justice concerns the way goods, burdens, offices, honors, resources, responsibilities, and common benefits are distributed within a community. It asks how the whole relates to its parts: what does the community owe to this person, this household, this office, this role, this need, this contribution? The equality here is not simple sameness but proportion — Aquinas, following Aristotle, calls it geometrical. Persons may receive different shares because their relations to the common good differ. A community does not distribute every good identically to every person. A public office goes to someone qualified for it; a burden is assigned according to capacity; aid is given according to need, honor according to merit, responsibility according to role. The point of distributive justice is not that everyone receives the same thing, but that each receives according to a just proportion within the whole.

Commutative justice, by contrast, concerns the relation of one person to another in exchange, injury, repayment, contract, sale, lending, restitution, and compensation. It asks how one party stands toward another when something is given, taken, owed, damaged, bought, sold, borrowed, lent, or restored — and here the equality is arithmetical. If one person takes too much, the excess must be returned. If one person damages another, the loss must be repaired. If one pays less than the thing is worth, equality is disturbed; if one receives more than is due, restitution may be required.

This is the world in which usury belongs. When a lender gives money in a mutuum, the borrower receives ownership and owes an equivalent. If the lender demands more than the equivalent simply because of the loan, Aquinas sees the lender receiving more than justice allows. Equality between lender and borrower is disturbed; the lender has taken what is not due. That is a problem of commutative justice.

The framing matters because it prevents us from reducing usury to a purely social or distributive problem. Usury may certainly have distributive consequences. It may transfer wealth upward, impoverish the vulnerable, concentrate land, power, and opportunity, and distort the common good — and Scripture is deeply attentive to these consequences. Nehemiah's crisis, for example, is not merely an issue between individual lenders and borrowers; it is a communal breakdown in which debt, interest, taxes, land, and elite power combine to threaten the restored people. But Aquinas's direct analysis of usury focuses on the commutative question: has one person received more from another than the structure of the transaction permits?

That focus makes the injustice precise. One could condemn usury because it harms the poor, and that would often be true. One could condemn it because it produces inequality, which may also be true, or because it expresses greed, which may be true as well. Aquinas wants to show something sharper: that usury is unjust even at the level of the exchange itself. The lender asks to be paid for what he has no right to sell. This is why the mutuum is so important. If the loan transfers ownership of a consumable thing, and the borrower owes an equivalent, then charging for the use of the thing disturbs the equality of the exchange. The lender claims both equivalent repayment and a price for use — but in a consumable good, the use is not separately saleable. He has received more than is due.

It also explains why restitution is required. In commutative justice, when equality has been disturbed by unjust taking, it must be restored; if the lender has received an unjust surplus, he must return it. Usury is not merely an interior vice but an external imbalance between persons — and the externality is crucial. A greedy person may desire unjust gain and never receive it. That is spiritually dangerous, but it is not yet the same as having taken from another. Usury actually received involves a transfer. The borrower has paid what was not due; the lender has received what was not owed; the ledger itself bears the mark of the injustice, and the remedy must therefore also reach the ledger.

Distributive justice still matters in the background. A society's laws, institutions, and economic structures determine who has access to credit, who bears risk, who receives protection, who is exposed to predatory lending, who owns assets, and who has bargaining power. These are questions of the common good, and a just society must answer them rightly: it must not leave the poor at the mercy of exploitative creditors, or structure markets so that the vulnerable pay more because they are vulnerable, or allow private claims to consume the conditions of communal life. But commutative justice gives a sharper interpersonal test. In this transaction, between these parties, has equality been preserved? Was the price just? Was the defect hidden? Was the loan gratuitous, as a mutuum must be? Was the surplus tied to a real external title? Was the compensation proportionate? Did one party receive more than was due?

The later doctrine of extrinsic titles depends on this test. Poena conventionalis can be understood as a claim that equality has been disturbed by the borrower's wrongful delay: if the borrower fails to repay on time and thereby causes harm or violates the agreement, a proportionate penalty may restore or defend justice — but a penalty engineered or expected as revenue creates inequality rather than repairing it. Damnum emergens can be understood as compensation for actual loss: the lender has suffered a real detriment because of the loan or the borrower's conduct, and compensation restores equality — but if the loss is fictitious or inflated, the lender receives more than is due. Lucrum cessans is more difficult, because the alleged inequality concerns a gain not yet possessed; later theologians will ask whether a concrete foregone profit can be treated as a real detriment. If it can, compensation may restore equality. If it cannot, the lender is selling possibility as if it were possession. Periculum sortis asks whether the lender's exposure to loss changes the equality of the relation: if capital is genuinely at risk, surplus may correspond to real vulnerability; if the risk is merely rhetorical, the surplus is unjust. In each case the question is commutative — what is due between these parties, given the true structure of the relation?

This gives Christian moral finance a discipline that modern economic language often lacks. Modern finance frequently speaks in aggregate terms: efficiency, growth, liquidity, market clearing, capital allocation, expected return, risk-adjusted pricing. These categories are not useless; they can describe real phenomena. But they can also obscure the interpersonal question. A transaction may be efficient in aggregate and still unjust between the parties. A market may clear at a price that exploits desperation. A rate may reflect risk and still crush the vulnerable. A fund may allocate capital efficiently and still extract too much from the enterprise. Commutative justice refuses to let aggregate usefulness erase interpersonal justice — and this is one of Aquinas's continuing gifts. He asks what is due not only to the system, but to the person. He does not let "the market" become the moral subject. The buyer, seller, lender, borrower, partner, worker, and neighbor remain visible.

At the same time, distributive justice prevents commutative justice from becoming too narrow. A series of formally fair exchanges can still produce an unjust social order if the background distribution of power, property, and opportunity is distorted. A poor person may "agree" to harsh terms because the social structure has left no alternative. A founder may accept extractive capital because mission-aligned capital is unavailable. A worker may accept low wages because ownership and bargaining power are badly distributed. A country may accept damaging debt because global structures leave it few choices. Aquinas's categories invite us to hold both levels together: commutative justice asks whether the exchange itself is equal; distributive justice asks whether the larger order distributes goods, burdens, protections, and opportunities rightly. Usury can violate both. It violates commutative justice when the lender receives what is not due from the borrower, and it violates distributive justice when a society tolerates credit structures that systematically transfer wealth from the vulnerable to the powerful, or distributes access to fair capital unjustly.

For the purposes of this book, however, commutative justice is the doorway into the technical doctrine, because it explains why Aquinas cares so much about whether the lender has a title. Without a title, the surplus is not due. If it is not due, taking it is unjust. If it is unjust, restitution is required. The language of "due" is therefore decisive at every step. A just price is the price due in the exchange; restitution is the return due after unjust taking; a loan repayment is the equivalent due under the mutuum. A penalty is due only if delay or breach creates a just ground. Compensation is due only if loss is real; foregone gain, only if it is sufficiently concrete and causally lost; risk return, only if risk is genuinely borne.

This is what makes the extrinsic titles so morally demanding. They are not excuses for gain; they are claims about what is due, and the claim must match reality. If no real loss exists, no compensation is due. If no culpable delay exists, no penalty is due. If no concrete foregone gain exists, no lost-profit payment is due. If no real risk is borne, no risk premium is due; if no labor is performed, no labor fee; if no cost is incurred, no reimbursement. Commutative justice cuts through verbal inflation. It does not ask what the contract can be made to say. It asks what the parties owe each other in truth.

The distinction between distributive and commutative justice is therefore no abstract philosophical detour. It gives us the grammar for the whole analysis. Usury is unjust because it violates equality in exchange. Just price matters because sale also belongs to equality in exchange. Restitution matters because broken equality must be repaired. The extrinsic titles matter because they determine whether an apparent surplus is actually due.

For modern non-extractive finance, this framework is invaluable, because such finance must be just at both levels. It must be distributively just: it must fit within a broader order that protects mission, labor, community, ecology, and the common good. And it must be commutatively just: each payment, fee, penalty, preference, repayment right, governance claim, and surplus distribution must correspond to something truly due. It is not enough for a fund to have a good mission if its contracts extract unjustly from portfolio companies, or for a company to create social value if its financing structure shifts all downside onto workers, or for investors to bear some risk if they also claim disproportionate control and priority. It is not enough for a fee to be market-standard if it does not correspond to real labor or cost, or for a penalty to be disclosed if it is designed to profit from distress. Commutative justice asks every claim to stand trial. What is due? Why is it due? To whom, and from whom? By what title? Is the amount proportionate? Does the structure preserve equality, or disturb it? These questions are not optional piety added to finance. They are the substance of justice.

Moral finance must therefore operate at two levels at once. It must design just systems, and it must judge particular exchanges; it must care about the common good, and it must care whether one person receives too much from another; it must address structural inequality, and it must demand restitution for concrete injustice. The usury doctrine belongs especially to the second level, but it cannot be separated from the first. A society in which the poor must borrow to survive will face usury differently from a society in which basic needs are secure. A society in which mission-aligned capital is scarce will pressure entrepreneurs toward extractive finance. A society in which capital is concentrated will give lenders more power to demand surplus. Distributive injustice creates the conditions in which commutative injustice becomes common.

But Aquinas still insists that each transaction must be judged. The lender cannot excuse unjust surplus by pointing to social custom; the borrower's consent does not erase inequality; the market's acceptance does not make the payment due. Justice asks more. That is why the doctrine remains so challenging. It does not allow us to hide behind either personal intention or systemic abstraction. It asks whether this claim, here, now, between these parties, corresponds to what is truly due. That is the heart of commutative justice — and without it, the doctrine of usury cannot be understood.

sections.title: 6.3 Restitution as repair of broken equality
paragraphs: If commutative justice is the justice of equality between persons, then restitution is the act by which broken equality is repaired — which is why Aquinas treats it immediately after his discussion of the parts of justice. In Summa Theologiae II-II, q.62, he asks what restitution is, whether it is necessary for salvation, whether one must restore what one has not taken, whether one must restore exactly what was taken, and to whom restitution must be made. These questions may seem far from finance at first. In fact, they are essential for understanding usury.

Usury, for Aquinas, is not merely a bad attitude toward money, not merely greed, not merely a failure of generosity. It involves receiving what is not due — an unjust taking — and therefore, if usury has been received, restitution is required. This changes the moral seriousness of the doctrine. If usury were only greed, repentance might seem to require only interior sorrow: the lender could feel remorse, resolve to be less greedy, and move on. But if usury is unjust gain, the problem is external as well as internal. Something has moved from borrower to lender that should not have moved. The ledger is wrong. Equality has been broken, and the borrower — or whoever holds the just claim — must be restored.

Aquinas's account of restitution makes the logic plain. Justice concerns what is due to another. If I have another person's good, or if I have received more than I should, I am not merely spiritually disordered; I am in possession of something that is not rightly mine, and to keep it is to continue the injustice. Restitution is therefore not optional generosity. It is justice. This point matters enormously for the whole history of usury. The usurer's problem is not solved by giving alms from unjust gain while keeping the principal fruits of the injustice, nor by pious language, religious donation, or future good intentions. If the surplus was unjustly taken, it must be returned where possible. Charity cannot substitute for restitution.

That principle will haunt the entire development of the extrinsic titles. A lender who claims poena conventionalis must ask whether the penalty was truly owed: if it was engineered, excessive, or expected as a substitute for interest, the amount received was not due, and restitution may be required. The claim of damnum emergens faces the same audit — was the loss real? If the "damage" was fictional, inflated, or unrelated to the loan, the compensation was unjust gain, and restitution may be required. Lucrum cessans must answer for concreteness: was the foregone profit concrete enough, causally linked, and proportionately valued? If the claim was merely abstract opportunity cost, the payment may not have been due. And periculum sortis must answer for exposure: was the principal genuinely at risk? If repayment was effectively guaranteed, collateralized, senior, or shifted onto the borrower while the lender collected a risk premium, the premium may have been unjust, and restitution may be required. Restitution is the moral backstop that prevents titles from becoming mere labels. It asks not only whether a payment can be justified in theory, but whether this payment was actually owed in truth. If not, the injustice remains until repaired.

This also explains why the scholastic tradition cares so much about identifying the proper recipient of restitution. If one has taken unjustly from a particular person, one should restore to that person; only if that person cannot be found, or circumstances make direct restitution impossible, may other remedies be considered. The default is personal, because commutative justice concerns relations between persons. The wrong is not abstract. Someone paid; someone received; someone was deprived; someone must be restored.

In modern finance, this principle is often obscured. Unjust gains can be dispersed through institutions, funds, intermediaries, fees, carry, dividends, securitizations, management companies, and investors far removed from the original borrower. A harmful financial structure may enrich many parties indirectly; the borrower may not even know who ultimately benefited. The injustice becomes distributed, and therefore harder to repair. But Aquinas's principle remains: unjust gain creates an obligation of repair. A modern non-extractive finance practice must therefore ask not only how to avoid future extraction, but how to respond when extraction has already occurred. Can excessive fees be refunded? Can punitive penalties be waived or returned? Can repayment obligations be restructured, governance rights relinquished, and upside captured through unjust terms redirected? Can affected workers, founders, suppliers, or communities be restored, and mission assets protected after having been placed at risk? Restitution makes moral finance concrete. It is easy to say that a structure was imperfect. It is harder to change the accounts. But justice requires more than diagnosis. It requires repair.

Aquinas's treatment of restitution also matters for future goods, which becomes important for the later debate over lucrum cessans. In q.62, he considers whether one must restore only what one has actually taken, or also what someone was prevented from obtaining, and he recognizes that preventing another person from obtaining a future good can create an obligation — though the analysis differs from restoring something already possessed. Here is one of the later openings for the doctrine of foregone profit. If one can be liable for preventing another from obtaining a good that was on the way, then perhaps a lender who gives up a concrete and reasonably certain gain by lending may claim compensation. Aquinas himself is cautious in the usury article, insisting that the lender may not contract for profit he has not yet made. But his broader account of restitution gives later Thomists a principle to develop — a good example of how his thought is both strict and fertile. He rejects easy opportunity-cost reasoning in the mutuum; he also recognizes that future goods are not always morally irrelevant. The question becomes whether the future good was sufficiently concrete, causally connected, and reasonably certain to create a claim in justice, and the doctrine of restitution thereby becomes one of the bridges between strict anti-usury teaching and later commercial nuance.

It also helps distinguish actual loss from hoped-for gain. If I take your horse, I must return the horse or its value. If I damage your field, I must repair the damage. If I prevent you from receiving a good you were just about to obtain, compensation may be due in some measure. But if you merely hoped for a possible gain that might never have materialized, the claim is weaker. Justice does not treat every dream as property. The distinction is crucial for lucrum cessans: a concrete foregone gain may sometimes stand close enough to a real detriment to be considered, but abstract opportunity cost does not — restitution requires reality, and it cannot be built on fantasy.

Restitution disciplines damnum emergens just as firmly. Actual loss must be actual. The lender may be restored to the position he should have occupied, but not enriched beyond it; if compensation exceeds the loss, the excess itself becomes unjust gain. Restitution can therefore apply in both directions. The borrower may owe compensation for real damage — and the lender may owe restitution if he claimed more damage than was real.

This introduces a principle that modern finance badly needs: no double-counting of claims. A lender cannot claim the same harm under multiple titles. He cannot charge a late penalty, default interest, an administrative fee, a risk premium, and damages all for the same underlying burden unless each corresponds to a distinct and real title; otherwise, compensation becomes accumulation. Restitution requires that each payment correspond to what is truly due, and no more. The same principle applies to risk. If the lender has already been compensated for risk through a higher return, he cannot also shift the loss entirely onto the borrower and still claim to have borne the risk. If capital is senior, collateralized, and protected, then a risk premium must be scrutinized. Restitution asks whether the payment actually corresponds to exposure, or whether capital was paid for a vulnerability it did not bear.

Restitution therefore forces truthfulness in accounting. It asks not only what payments were made, but why; whether each payment has a title; whether titles overlap; whether the amount fits the harm; whether the party receiving payment was actually the party harmed or exposed, and the party paying truly responsible; whether the payment restores equality or disturbs it further. This is why restitution belongs at the heart of a moral-finance dashboard. A dashboard that tracks only repayment, return, and yield is morally incomplete. It should also track claims, titles, harms, costs, risk allocation, and overpayment. It should ask whether surplus received by capital has been justified, whether any unjust surplus has been returned, and whether the structure produces repair or accumulation.

Aquinas also reminds us that restitution can be costly. To restore what was unjustly taken may require real sacrifice: the lender may have spent the money, the institution may have booked the revenue, investors may have expected distribution, and the unjust gain may have been incorporated into budgets, salaries, reserves, or returns. The fact that restitution is difficult does not erase the obligation. This is spiritually important, because unjust financial systems often become self-protective. Once extraction has occurred, many people become dependent on its proceeds. The institution says it cannot afford to return the money; the fund, that it owes duties to investors; the lender, that the borrower agreed; the creditor, that the payment has already been allocated. The system develops reasons why repair is impractical. Aquinas does not let difficulty erase justice. Prudence matters, of course: restitution must be ordered rightly, weighing who is owed, what can be returned, whether direct restoration is possible, and how to avoid creating new injustices. But the basic obligation remains. One may not peacefully keep what belongs to another.

That is a severe word to modern finance, because many financial gains are legally secure but morally questionable. They may have been extracted through information asymmetry, distress, excessive penalties, misaligned control, unjust risk-shifting, or mission-capturing terms. The law may not require restitution. The contract may be enforceable. The market may call the return normal. Aquinas still asks whether the payment was due.

Restitution is also linked to truth, because one cannot repair what one refuses to name. If a lender insists that every payment received was legitimate, no restitution will occur. The first act of repair is often recognition: this was not due; this was excessive; this title was false; this fee was not tied to real cost; this penalty was punitive beyond justice; this risk premium did not correspond to risk; this control right allowed capital to capture what it should have protected. Moral finance therefore requires confession in the literal sense: telling the truth. This is why the old confessional context of usury matters. The usurer came not only as a market actor, but as a sinner, and the confessor had to ask what had been taken and what must be restored. The point was not shame for its own sake. The point was conversion that reaches economic reality. A renewed moral economy needs something analogous — not necessarily the same ecclesial form in every context, but the same pattern: examination, truth-telling, judgment, restitution, amendment. A fund must be able to examine its terms; a lender, to name unjust gain; an investor, to recognize when return came from extraction; a company, to see when capital structures harmed mission; an institution, to repair what it has distorted. Without restitution, moral finance becomes branding.

This is why q.62 belongs before q.77 and q.78 in our reading of Aquinas. Before we discuss just price and usury, we need to understand repair, because justice is not only about avoiding future wrong. It is about restoring what has already been disordered. In just price, if one sells for more than the thing is worth, restitution may be owed. In usury, if one charges for the use of money in a mutuum, restitution may be owed. In false extrinsic titles, if one receives compensation without real title, restitution may be owed. The doctrine has teeth because it reaches backward into the transaction and says: this must be undone.

For modern non-extractive capital, this raises a challenging but necessary question: what would it mean for a fund, lender, or investor to have a restitution policy? Such a policy would not merely forswear future misconduct. It would identify overcharges, excessive penalties, duplicated fees, unjust control benefits, and returns received under false risk allocation; it would build mechanisms for refunding, crediting, restructuring, or redirecting unjust gains; and it would treat borrowers and portfolio companies not merely as counterparties but as parties to whom justice may be owed, making repair part of governance. That may sound radical. In the tradition of Aquinas, it is simply what justice requires.

Restitution is the memory of equality after inequality has occurred. It says that the past can be repaired; that unjust gain need not remain embedded in the present; that confession can become accounting, and accounting can become conversion; that the ledger must be made to tell the truth. Without restitution, the usury doctrine is only a theory. With restitution, it becomes a demand, and the demand is simple. If you have taken what was not due, give it back.

chapters.part: Part III — Aquinas: Justice, Just Price, Usury, and the Theological Grammar of Surplus

chapters.title: Just Price and the Moral Structure of Exchange
chapters.label: Chapter 7

sections.title: 7.1 Where Aquinas treats just price
paragraphs: Aquinas treats the question of just price in Summa Theologiae II-II, q.77, immediately before his question on usury — and the placement is itself a lesson. He does not discuss buying and selling in one part of the Summa and usury somewhere unrelated. He sets them side by side inside the treatise on justice, after his treatment of restitution, because just price and usury are both problems of commutative justice. Both ask whether one person has received more from another than justice permits.

In q.77 Aquinas asks about "cheating" in buying and selling. The word sounds narrow, as if only outright fraud were in view, but his treatment is broader: he is asking how injustice can enter exchange through price, defects, concealment, and trade for gain. The question has four articles. In Article 1 he asks whether it is lawful to sell a thing for more than it is worth — the classic just-price article, which asks whether seller and buyer are morally bound by the value of the thing exchanged, or whether one may knowingly take advantage of another's ignorance, need, or willingness to pay. In Article 2 he asks whether a sale is rendered unjust by a defect in the thing sold — not price now, but quality, since selling a defective thing as sound can injure the buyer. In Article 3 he asks whether the seller is bound to reveal defects, bringing truth and disclosure into the structure of exchange: the moral problem is not only what is transferred but what is known, hidden, and communicated. And in Article 4 he asks whether it is lawful, in trading, to sell a thing for more than one paid for it — the question of merchant profit, and of when gain in trade is ordered toward need, service, household support, or public utility, and when merely toward avarice.

Together the four articles form a compact moral theory of exchange, and they prepare directly for the usury question. In buying and selling, injustice occurs when one party receives more value than he gives, or withholds information that would affect the exchange. In usury, injustice occurs when the lender receives more than the mutuum itself permits, unless some genuine extrinsic title justifies the surplus. The underlying issue is the same: equality in exchange. That is why just price must come before usury. A reader who has not understood Aquinas's concern for equality in sale will misread his concern in lending as the mere repetition of a traditional ban, when in fact he is applying a broader principle — one person must not take more from another than is due.

The just-price analysis also keeps the usury question from shrinking to the formal contract of loan. Injustice can hide in sale as well: a seller can overcharge, a buyer underpay, a defect be concealed, a sale on credit carry a hidden charge for time, a merchant seek profit that serves real needs or profit driven by endless accumulation. The moral field is broader than lending — which matters historically, because many evasions of the usury prohibition took the form of sales, exchanges, and price differences. If interest on a loan is forbidden, one can try selling at a higher price for delayed payment; if direct usury is condemned, one can dress the loan as an exchange contract, a bill, a repurchase agreement, a sale with deferred terms. Aquinas's treatment of just price provides the tools for seeing through these costumes. In q.78 he notes that charging more simply because payment is delayed has the character of usury — a point that depends on seeing the relation between price and time. If a thing has a just price today and the seller charges more solely because he waits for payment, the extra amount is not payment for the thing's value but payment for time — and that resembles a loan. Just price and usury overlap: a sale can become a hidden loan, a price difference can hide interest, a delay can be monetized, a defect can be concealed, and a market exchange can violate justice even when both parties consent.

The lesson reaches modern finance directly, because most modern financial arrangements do not look like simple loans. They come embedded in sales, leases, service contracts, convertible instruments, preferred equity, revenue-based financing, subscription models, platform terms, licensing arrangements, factoring, supply-chain finance, and exit rights. The old usury question can appear in forms never called interest, and the just-price question in forms never called sale. Aquinas's method equips us to ask the only question that survives the relabeling: what is really being exchanged, and is the exchange just?

The articles also show that Aquinas does not reduce value to subjective willingness to pay. He does not say a thing is worth whatever someone agrees to give for it, and he offers no mechanical formula either. He treats price as morally related to the thing's worth and to the equality between buyer and seller — a range of acceptable prices may exist, and practical judgment is needed, but the fact that one party can be induced to accept a price does not make the price just. This challenges modern assumptions head-on. Much modern economics treats price as the outcome of supply, demand, information, bargaining power, and market conditions; if the price clears the market, it is treated as real, and moral questions, if asked at all, are asked separately. Aquinas thinks differently. A price can injure. A price can be unjust. A price can give one person more than is due from another. The market may reveal something, but it does not canonize the result. This matters for usury because interest rates are also treated as market prices — the price of credit, of risk, of liquidity, of time — and the just-price framework asks whether such prices correspond to what is truly due. A rate can be market-clearing and still exploit need. A penalty can be disclosed and still excessive. A preferred return can reflect investor expectations and still distort equality. A fee can be customary and still correspond to no real labor or cost. The market does not absolve the moral question.

The defect and disclosure articles carry into finance just as directly. In sale, a hidden defect makes the exchange unjust because the buyer does not receive what he believes he is receiving. In finance, the hidden defects are information asymmetry, undisclosed risks, opaque fees, complicated covenants, misrepresented downside, hidden dilution, and governance terms the borrower does not fully understand. The moral question is not whether the terms appear somewhere in the document; it is whether the other party can reasonably understand the nature of the exchange. Concealment can be formal or practical, and a term disclosed in legal language still functions as hidden if the weaker party cannot realistically grasp its implications. None of this cancels a party's responsibility to understand what they sign. But truthfulness in exchange is more than minimal legal notice: one must not profit from what the other party does not know and would reasonably need to know. Modern finance is full of exactly these dangers. A founder may not understand how liquidation preferences behave in downside scenarios; a borrower, how default interest compounds; a small company, the control implications of its covenants; a consumer, the layering of fees; a municipality, its derivatives exposure; a social enterprise, how investor rights can override mission years later. Just price, defects, and disclosure are all in play.

Article 4, on merchant profit, is crucial enough to receive its own section below, but its shape belongs here. Aquinas does not condemn all merchant profit. Trade may serve real needs — a merchant may transport goods from where they are abundant to where they are needed, incur labor, risk, and expense, and earn a moderate profit for household support or public utility. But Aquinas remains wary of trade pursued merely for gain, because the desire for profit has no natural limit; trade can be ordered toward service or toward avarice, and the moral difference lies in the end, the moderation of the gain, and the real usefulness of the activity. This prepares the later scholastic discussions of merchant capital and foregone gain: if merchant activity can be legitimate and useful, a merchant's foregone gain is not pure fantasy, and money committed to trade may have a concrete commercial use — though the danger remains that "profit I could have made" swells into an unlimited justification for charging more.

So the just-price discussion gives both permission and discipline: profit permitted where trade serves life, need, household, and public utility; profit disciplined where trade becomes accumulation for its own sake. The same pattern will organize the extrinsic titles. Damnum emergens permits compensation for real loss and disciplines fictional loss. Lucrum cessans may permit compensation for concrete foregone gain and disciplines abstract opportunity cost. Periculum sortis may permit return for real risk and disciplines risk rhetoric without vulnerability. Poena conventionalis may permit penalty for wrongful delay and disciplines engineered default. Aquinas's economic reasoning is not anti-commerce; it is anti-disorder. It forbids not all gain but unjust gain, and it asks whether exchange remains ordered toward justice, need, truth, and the common good.

For a modern non-extractive capital framework, that distinction is the whole game. The goal is not to eliminate surplus, profit, return, fees, or compensation, but to ask whether each claim corresponds to something real and just. Does the merchant serve? Does the lender restore? Does the investor share risk? Does the manager perform labor? Does the penalty repair harm? Does the price reflect value? Does the structure preserve mission and life? Aquinas's just-price question generalizes into the moral-finance question: is the exchange truthful, proportionate, and ordered toward a legitimate good?

This is why q.77 belongs at the heart of this book. Before Aquinas condemns usury, he has already established that economic exchange can be unjust when one party takes too much, hides defects, or seeks gain without proper order. The usury question is not an isolated medieval obsession; it is one application of a larger doctrine of just exchange. And the doctrine restores moral seriousness to price itself. A price is not just information. It is a claim — this is what you owe for this good, this service, this risk, this delay, this capital, this opportunity, this right — and Aquinas asks whether the claim is true. The question has not aged. When a company charges a distressed borrower a fee, is the fee true? When a fund charges management costs, are the costs true? When an investor demands a preferred return, when a lender imposes default interest, when a seller charges more for delayed payment, when a platform extracts rents from dependency — is the claim true? Just price is, at bottom, about truth in exchange: the thing not misrepresented, the defect not hidden, the price not injuring, the profit not curdling into avarice, the weaker party's need not becoming the stronger party's opportunity.

Seen this way, usury is a false price. It prices the use of money in a mutuum as though that use were separable and still belonged to the lender; it treats the borrower's time with the money as something the lender can sell after ownership has passed. It is an untrue price. The just-price question and the usury question belong together because both ask whether the economic claim corresponds to reality — which is why Aquinas treats them side by side, and why any renewed moral economy must do the same.

sections.title: 7.2 The just price as equality in exchange
paragraphs: For Aquinas, the just price is not merely a market fact. It is a matter of equality between persons — which is why the question belongs inside the treatise on justice at all. Buying and selling are not morally neutral transfers. They are relations in which one person gives and another receives, one may know more and another less, one may have urgent need and another bargaining power. In such relations, justice asks whether equality has been preserved.

His central question in Summa Theologiae II-II, q.77, a.1 is whether it is lawful to sell a thing for more than it is worth, and the answer runs on commutative justice. If the seller knowingly receives more than the thing is worth, the buyer is injured; if the buyer knowingly pays less, the seller is injured; in either case one party has received more than is due. The just price is therefore not the price that can be obtained. It is the price that preserves equality in the exchange.

This does not commit Aquinas to a single, mathematically exact number known with perfect precision. Medieval just-price thought typically allows a range, because value depends on circumstances, place, time, need, abundance, scarcity, labor, risk, and common estimation; human judgment is required, and price is not a heavenly figure dropped into the market from outside history. But neither is price whatever the stronger party can extract. The just price is a moral claim about what the thing is worth in the exchange — concerned with truth, proportion, and neighborly justice — and it asks whether the payment corresponds to the good transferred or one party is taking advantage of the other. The structure is exactly the one that will govern usury. In sale, one may not charge more than the thing is worth. In lending, one may not charge more than the mutuum permits. In both, the problem is receiving what is not due.

Aquinas's concern runs deeper than external compulsion, and here he parts company with much modern thinking: a sale can be unjust even when the buyer agrees. The buyer may agree because he does not know the true value, because he is under pressure, because a defect has been concealed, because his need is urgent. Consent matters, but it is not enough. Modern contract thinking tends to make voluntary agreement morally central — parties consented, terms were disclosed, no fraud in the narrow legal sense occurred, so the exchange stands. Aquinas is more demanding. Consent does not turn an unequal exchange into a just one; a person can voluntarily pay too much or accept too little, and the moral question remains whether one party received more than was due. This is not contempt for freedom. It is realism about freedom's distortions — by ignorance, need, inequality, and desire. A desperate person may consent to terms a just person should not impose. A buyer may accept a price because he lacks information; a borrower may agree to usury because he needs money; a founder may accept extractive investment terms because no patient capital is available. Consent is real, and it does not settle justice.

The just price therefore protects the weaker party from the stronger party's opportunism, and never more clearly than in cases of necessity. If someone desperately needs food, medicine, shelter, or transport, the seller may be able to demand far more, and the buyer's willingness to pay proves nothing but the buyer's need. To treat necessity as permission for extraction is to confuse market power with moral right. Scripture had already made the point through the poor borrower and the cloak; Aquinas gives it philosophical and juridical form. And the modern instances multiply without effort: a distressed borrower accepts a high rate, a small company harsh terms, a founder liquidation preferences that later undermine the mission, a country damaging debt, a worker a bad wage, a community platform dependence. In each case the fact of agreement does not answer the moral question. The just-price tradition asks whether the exchange reflects justice or desperation.

Truth is equally central. A price is not merely a number; it is a statement about value. When a seller knowingly charges more than the thing is worth, the price lies — it says "this is what the thing is worth" while extracting more. When a seller hides a defect, the exchange lies by omission. When a lender charges for the use of money in a mutuum, the price lies by treating use as separable from ownership when it is not. A just economy is not one in which prices merely clear markets; it is one in which prices correspond, as far as humanly possible, to real value, real cost, real risk, real service, real loss, real labor, and real need. An unjust economy is one in which prices detach from truth and become expressions of power. Prices are rarely easy to determine — but difficulty does not remove moral responsibility, the complexity of value does not make all prices just, and the existence of a range does not license any price within someone's willingness to pay.

In Aquinas's world, "common estimation" does important work here. The value of a thing is not fixed by the private preference of buyer or seller; it is socially discerned, a community's working sense of what goods ordinarily are worth given quality, scarcity, place, and use. That keeps price flexible without letting it become purely subjective or purely opportunistic. There is no need to romanticize this — medieval markets could be unjust, and common estimation could carry hierarchy, exclusion, and misinformation — but the principle stands: price belongs to a moral community, not to the isolated will of two parties in a vacuum. For non-extractive finance the implication is sharp. The "price" of capital cannot be settled by what the entrepreneur will accept and what the investor expects. It must be judged in relation to mission, risk, loss, labor, sustainability, alternatives, and the life of the enterprise. A market return may be commonly expected among investors without being just for this transaction; a founder's acceptance may show need, not fairness; a fund's hurdle rate expresses investor appetite, not moral title. The just-price framework asks: what is this capital claim truly worth, and what does it do to the other party?

Which brings the argument to injury. Selling above worth injures the buyer as buying below worth injures the seller — price injustice is a form of harm, an interpersonal wrong and not a mere failure of efficiency. The same logic applies to usury: if the borrower pays more than is due under the mutuum, the borrower is injured, whether the injury is small or large, obvious or hidden, immediate or gradual. And because injury is real, restitution follows. If the price was unjust beyond the tolerable range, the party who received too much may be obliged to restore; justice is not satisfied by observing that the transaction is over, because the inequality remains until repaired. Modern finance resists this instinctively — once a contract is signed and performed, the payments are treated as settled, the return collected, the fee charged, the market moved on. Aquinas's account of commutative justice keeps asking whether what was received was truly due, and if it was not, the moral obligation does not vanish when the transaction closes. Just price and restitution belong together.

At the same time, Aquinas does not turn just price into impossible perfectionism. Human transactions cannot be calibrated exactly; minor variations are tolerated because practical exchange requires flexibility. What violates justice is substantial inequality, especially knowingly imposed. That is a workable standard for modern application. A non-extractive fund does not need omniscience or the perfect fee. It needs to avoid substantial injustice: not knowingly structuring returns that overburden the enterprise, not hiding costs, not exploiting information asymmetry, not pricing capital as if mission, labor, and community were secondary to investor expectation — staying within a range that can be defended as just. The just price is not a formula. It is a discipline of moral proportionality: has the stronger party refrained from taking the maximum possible where the maximum would injure? Does the payment correspond to the thing, service, risk, or burden actually supplied?

The discipline finally reaches purpose, because a thing's worth is not merely what it can command but what it is in relation to human use and need. Food has a purpose; housing, tools, and medicine have purposes; capital has a purpose too — to enable life, work, production, service, stewardship, and the common good. When a price prevents a good from serving its proper end, or turns urgent need into gain, the moral problem deepens. Aquinas's just-price thought is teleological, not merely economic, and modern finance mostly abstracts from purpose: capital seeks return wherever return can be found, a price is justified by market demand, a fee by industry norm, a premium by risk model. The framework asks instead whether the exchange remains ordered toward the good of the persons and community involved. In mission-driven settings this is decisive. A social enterprise, a steward-owned company, an open-source infrastructure provider should not be priced as if its mission were irrelevant; the capital structure must be judged by whether it allows the mission to live. A technically acceptable return becomes unjust when it forces the enterprise away from its purpose. A price for capital that destroys the good being financed is not a just price.

So the just price as equality in exchange becomes a foundation for non-extractive finance: every financial claim tested by truth and proportionality. A repayment amount should correspond to what is due; a fee to labor or cost; a premium to risk actually borne; a penalty to wrongful delay or harm; a return to participation, not domination — and no price should be determined by the borrower's desperation. It also fixes the difference between market price and moral price. The two may coincide, but they are not identical: market price reports what can be obtained under current conditions, moral price asks what may be taken in justice, and when conditions are shaped by scarcity, monopoly, distress, ignorance, or unequal power, the gap between them grows wide. The just-price tradition exists precisely because "what can be obtained" is not "what is due." That cuts against both sides of the familiar ideological quarrel — against the laissez-faire assumption that voluntary exchange suffices for justice, and against the simplistic anti-market reaction, since the tradition never denies the need for price, exchange, judgment, and compensation. It does not abolish commerce; it moralizes it. Aquinas's position is neither "price is irrelevant" nor "price is whatever the market says," but "price must preserve justice in exchange" — a demanding middle path.

For the history of usury, the consequence is exact. Lending is one form of exchange among others, with its own structure: in sale, the just price is tied to the worth of the thing; in a mutuum, the just repayment is tied to the equivalent of what was lent, unless an extrinsic title creates a further claim; in both, receiving more than is due violates equality. And this is why Aquinas will judge usury unjust even when the borrower consents. If consent alone cannot justify an unjust sale price, consent alone cannot justify an unjust loan surplus; the borrower's promise to pay more creates no moral title if the lender had no right to require it. The insight is severe and liberating at once — severe because it judges transactions that law and market would permit, liberating because it refuses to identify justice with the stronger party's ability to obtain agreement. It protects the moral reality of the weaker party even when the contract says otherwise. In a world where almost everything can be priced, the claim is quietly revolutionary: the price of a thing, the cost of capital, the rate of return, the fee for service, the penalty for delay, and the premium for risk are all morally answerable. They must correspond to reality; they must not exploit need, conceal defects, injure the neighbor, or let one party take more than is due simply because he can. The just price is equality made economic — and without that idea, the doctrine of usury cannot be understood.

sections.title: 7.3 Special value to the owner
paragraphs: Aquinas's treatment of just price includes a subtle but consequential qualification: a thing may be worth more to its owner than it is worth in the ordinary market. At first hearing this sounds like an escape hatch — if things can be worth more to their owners, every seller can discover some special attachment or private valuation and charge accordingly. That is not Aquinas's point. He is not saying that subjective desire can inflate price without limit. He is saying that parting with a thing can sometimes impose a real loss on the owner, and that justice may take this into account. The distinction will do heavy work later, in damnum emergens and — far more controversially — in lucrum cessans.

The basic idea is simple. A thing may have a common price in the market while its owner suffers a special detriment in giving it up: the thing is especially useful to him, or replacing it will be difficult, or he needs it for his work, or parting with it imposes a concrete loss the ordinary price does not capture. In such cases the seller may be compensated not merely for the object in the abstract but for the loss caused by being deprived of it. This is not "I like it very much, therefore I may charge whatever I want." Aquinas is concerned with real detriment, not arbitrary sentiment: the additional value must correspond to something objective enough for justice to weigh. The seller may not exploit the buyer's need by claiming special value — and neither is the seller required to suffer a real loss without compensation. The frame is still commutative equality. If a seller gives up a thing whose loss burdens him particularly, a price that ignores the burden may fail to preserve equality, because the buyer receives not only the thing but the seller's surrender of something that had particular value in the seller's circumstances.

This complicates the simple notion of market price, because common estimation does not always capture the particular relation between a person and a good. A tool has ordinary value in the market and much greater value to the craftsman whose work depends on it. A horse has ordinary value, and greater value to the man whose livelihood rides on that particular animal. A store of grain has ordinary value now, and greater value to the merchant already committed to holding it for a later season. A sum of money has ordinary value, and greater value to someone who must meet an obligation or fund an imminent transaction. And here the just-price discussion begins to touch the usury debates. If parting with a thing causes an actual loss, compensation begins to look like damnum emergens — the seller or lender being restored for a real detriment, not charging because the other party benefits. If parting with a thing interrupts a concrete gain already on the way, the issue approaches lucrum cessans — the owner did not yet possess the future gain, but the thing was ordered toward a use that made the gain reasonably expected, and later theologians will spend centuries debating whether, when, and how such foregone gain can be compensated.

Aquinas himself is cautious. In the usury article he clearly allows compensation for actual loss but rejects contractual compensation merely because the lender makes no profit from his money: the lender, he says, must not sell what he does not yet have and may be prevented in many ways from having. Yet his broader treatment of just price and restitution hands the later tradition its conceptual material. If a good's value to its owner can include the real loss caused by parting with it, then perhaps, in some concrete cases, money already ordered toward trade or obligation has a special value to its owner too.

The key word is concrete. Aquinas opens no door to abstract opportunity cost. He does not say that every owner may charge for every possible alternative use, or that money deserves compensation simply because it could have earned elsewhere. The claim must be tied to a real circumstance; the special value must be more than imagination, preference, or a generalized appetite for gain. That discipline is what separates his idea from the modern habit, in which opportunity-cost reasoning floats at high abstraction — capital could have been invested elsewhere, could have earned the market rate, could have gone into another fund, another asset class, another project, another security, and therefore every use of capital must pay the capital provider what might have been earned in the alternatives. Aquinas's special value asks narrower questions: did this person, in these circumstances, suffer a real detriment by parting with this thing? Was the good actually needed? Was the use concrete? Was the loss causally connected? Is the added price proportionate? Is the other party being charged for a real burden, or for a hypothetical benchmark? The difference is enormous. A concrete loss can be restored. A hypothetical universe cannot be billed.
