The special-value idea is therefore both necessary and dangerous — necessary because justice must sometimes account for particular circumstances, since arithmetic market equivalence is not always enough; dangerous because almost any owner can claim that his thing was specially valuable, and without discipline the idea becomes a way to escape just price altogether. The scholastic tradition handles the danger by demanding reality, proportionality, and connection to the transaction: the special value must not be a pretext for exploiting the buyer, must correspond to a real loss or burden actually borne, and must not convert the buyer's need into the seller's windfall. The same test explains why damnum emergens is morally cleaner than lucrum cessans. Actual loss is present and identifiable, so compensation can be measured; foregone gain reaches into the future, may be real in some cases, and is easy to exaggerate — the later controversy grows from exactly that uncertainty. Aquinas's principle sits at the boundary between the two: value is not exhausted by general market estimation, and value does not collapse into subjective desire; the owner's real circumstances matter, and possibility may not be priced without limit.

For modern non-extractive finance the principle is unexpectedly practical. Consider a mission-driven fund. It has real costs and real constraints: capital may be scarce; donors or investors entrusted funds for a particular purpose; liquidity must be held for future commitments; staff, due diligence, monitoring, legal, and accounting all cost money; capital tied up in one enterprise cannot serve another. None of this is imaginary, and a simplistic anti-return position that says "the fund is mission-driven, therefore capital should be free" forces the provider of capital to absorb every real burden — exactly what Aquinas's special-value logic warns against. Justice may allow compensation for what is actually sacrificed. But the same logic can be abused from the other side. The fund can say: our capital is scarce, therefore every portfolio company pays our target return; we could have invested elsewhere, therefore the enterprise owes the market benchmark; our investors expect liquidity, therefore the company bears the full cost. That is inflating real constraints into generalized entitlement. The special-value principle says: be precise. What is the actual burden, and the real cost? What concrete use is being foregone — and what is the mission-aligned alternative, as opposed to the extractive benchmark? What loss does this transaction cause? What amount would restore rather than enrich? What should the fund bear itself, as part of its mission, and what may justly be charged to the enterprise? This is not easy; it is exactly the kind of moral accounting Aquinas invites.

And the principle cuts in the other direction too, which may be its most important modern service: it exposes the hidden underpricing of what vulnerable parties surrender. A company's shares, assets, data, intellectual property, governance rights, or mission lock may carry special value beyond ordinary market price. To transfer control rights may impose a loss the cash investment never registers. To accept capital with exit pressure may endanger the mission itself. To pledge essential assets may create vulnerability far beyond the nominal value of the collateral. A just price for capital must therefore account not only for the investor's cost but for what the enterprise gives up. Modern finance prices capital almost entirely from the investor's side; Aquinas asks us also to price the recipient's sacrifice. If a steward-owned company gives an investor governance rights, what is that surrender worth? If a social enterprise accepts a liquidation preference, what mission risk has been created? If an open-source infrastructure company accepts extractive capital, what future commons is endangered? If a founder signs a personal guarantee, what household vulnerability has been pledged? If a company takes short-term capital at the cost of long-term mission control, what has really been sold? These are just-price questions, and they reveal that capital transactions are never only about how much money enters the enterprise. They are also about what leaves: control, future upside, resilience, mission flexibility, time, attention, data, governance, freedom.

Extraction, in fact, often works precisely by undervaluing what the weaker party gives up. The founder gives up control, and the term sheet prices only the capital. The worker gives up stability, and the model prices only labor cost. The community gives up data, and the platform prices only the service. The company gives up mission flexibility, and the investor prices only risk. The borrower gives up collateral essential to survival, and the lender prices only default protection. The just-price tradition tells us to look at the whole exchange — what each side gives, loses, and gains, and what the transferred thing is worth not only in market terms but in relation to life, work, mission, and need.

The special-value principle also resists the commodification of everything. Some things are worth more to their owners because they are not interchangeable units at all: land, tools, homes, family goods, mission assets, community institutions, sacred objects. This does not mean they can never be sold or pledged. It means justice must consider what their loss does. Leviticus taught this about land — not merely a commodity, because it belongs to God and sustains family inheritance. Exodus taught it about the cloak — not merely collateral, because it covers the poor man at night. Aquinas gives the same moral intuition a general just-price form: a thing's value may depend on its relation to the person who holds it. Scripture and scholastic justice meet here, and the meeting is worth spelling out. The cloak has special value to the poor man because it is his covering; the land, to the family, because it is inheritance; the tool, to the craftsman, because it enables work; the capital, to the merchant, because it is ordered toward trade; the mission lock, to the steward-owned company, because it protects purpose; the governance right, because it shapes the future. A moral economy must learn to see these values. The danger of modern abstraction is that it treats fungibility as universal — money, shares, assets, claims, risk, and labor all fungible — when life is not fungible in the same way. The meaning of a good depends on the life it supports. The same object is surplus to one person and essential to another; the same collateral is security to the lender and survival to the borrower; the same governance right is protection to the investor and a mission threat to the enterprise. Just price must attend to that asymmetry.

So special value to the owner is no minor footnote. It is one of the places where just-price theory becomes sensitive to context without becoming relativistic — particular circumstances matter, and they stay under the discipline of justice. For the extrinsic titles ahead, it will do significant work: in damnum emergens, supporting compensation for real loss caused by parting with something; in lucrum cessans, raising the harder question whether a concrete future use belongs to a thing's value to its owner; in periculum sortis, reminding us that exposing capital to loss changes the value of the claim; in poena conventionalis, helping ask whether delayed repayment deprived the lender of something specifically needed. In every case the same discipline applies: the claim must correspond to reality. Special value is not a blank check but a call to deeper truthfulness about what is actually being lost, transferred, or endangered — the truthfulness without which finance cannot serve life. A capital structure that prices only the investor's opportunity and ignores the enterprise's sacrifice is unjust; a loan that prices only lender risk and ignores borrower vulnerability is unjust; a sale that prices only market demand and ignores essential need is unjust; a contract that prices only financial assets and ignores mission, community, and livelihood is morally incomplete. The just price must ask what the exchange means to both sides. Aquinas's insight condenses to this: common price matters, and context matters too — the owner's real loss, the buyer's need, the thing's purpose — and the exchange must preserve equality not in the abstract but in the actual relation between persons. Special value to the owner is not an escape from justice. It is justice made more attentive.

sections.title: 7.4 Merchant profit in q.77, a.4
paragraphs: Aquinas does not condemn all profit in trade. The point needs stating plainly, because his critique of usury is regularly misread as one expression of a general hostility to commerce. He is cautious about trade, and deeply alert to the moral dangers of gain-seeking — but he does not say that every merchant profit is unjust. In Summa Theologiae II-II, q.77, a.4, he asks whether it is lawful, in trading, to sell a thing for more than one paid for it, and his answer is careful, because the merchant's case is genuinely ambiguous. The merchant buys a thing and sells it for more. Unlike the farmer, he did not grow it; unlike the craftsman, he did not substantially transform it; unlike the laborer, he seems to have produced nothing directly. He buys in order to sell dearer. Is that not simply gain from exchange?

Aquinas's answer depends on the end of the activity. Trade pursued merely for gain is morally dangerous, because the desire for gain has no natural limit — money can always seek more money, and here Aquinas stands close to Aristotle: the merchant's calling can slide into the endless pursuit of profit for its own sake, and when accumulation becomes the point, trade becomes spiritually disordered. But trade can also serve genuine human needs. A merchant may bring goods from where they are abundant to where they are scarce; he may preserve, transport, store, organize, and make available what others need; he assumes labor, expense, and risk; he supports his household; he serves the public utility. In such cases a moderate profit is morally permissible. Profit, in short, is not justified simply because the merchant can obtain it, and not condemned simply because it exists. It is judged by its relation to real service, need, labor, risk, moderation, and the common good — an account that is neither anti-commercial nor market-absolutist, and that yields a principle the rest of this book will lean on: gain is morally judged by the form of participation that produces it.

This is directly relevant to usury because it shows Aquinas distinguishing kinds of surplus. He is not allergic to gain as such; he is opposed to gain that lacks a just title. Merchant profit can have a title — in labor, service, risk, and public utility. Usury lacks one when it is taken merely from the mutuum. The same distinction explains why lucrum cessans will later become plausible. If merchant activity can be legitimate and merchant profit morally ordered, then a merchant's interrupted commercial use of capital is not obviously irrelevant: a merchant who lends money that was otherwise concretely committed to trade may have foregone a real and licit gain, and later theologians will ask whether that foregone gain can be compensated. Aquinas himself is cautious — but q.77, a.4 explains why the later question arises at all. A merchant's money is not always idle. It may be part of a real commercial activity, and if the activity is morally legitimate, its interruption may matter.

His caution deserves equal emphasis, because legitimacy in principle does not make every claimed profit just. The desire for gain can become limitless. The merchant may serve need, and may exploit it; accept risk, and shift it; incur costs, and inflate them; provide real service, and position himself as a tollbooth. Merchant profit therefore remains under standing moral examination — which is exactly the examination modern financial intermediaries require. They present themselves in the merchant's role: moving capital from where it is abundant to where it is needed, screening opportunities, bearing risk, conducting due diligence, monitoring performance, structuring contracts, providing governance, supporting growth. These can be real services. A fund can genuinely help capital reach enterprises that need it; an investor can genuinely support stewardship, strategy, and resilience; a lender can genuinely provide liquidity at the right moment. And intermediation can also become extraction. The intermediary may collect fees regardless of outcome, claim return while shifting risk, manufacture complexity that justifies its own toll, control access to capital and charge heavily for passage, use information asymmetry to capture value, speak of serving enterprises while steering them toward investor exit — less a merchant serving public utility than a gatekeeper monetizing dependence. Aquinas's test cuts through the self-description. The question is never simply whether the intermediary earns more than it paid, but whether the gain corresponds to real service ordered toward a legitimate good. Does it actually move needed resources? Does it bear real cost or risk? Does it add knowledge, trust, stewardship, or coordination? Does it serve the common good, and is the profit moderate? Or does it merely occupy a position of power and extract because it can? That is a just-price question — and it is the usury question in its broadest form, the moral title of surplus.

Moderation carries special weight here, because even legitimate profit can become excessive. A merchant may serve a real need and still charge too much; a fund may provide real value and still demand a disproportionate return; a lender may bear real administrative costs and still overcharge; a consultant may perform real labor and still exploit dependency. The presence of a title does not justify unlimited gain — a principle that will recur in every extrinsic title. A real penalty must still be proportionate. A real loss is compensated only to the extent of the loss. A real foregone gain must not become a claim to every imagined return. A real risk does not justify unlimited upside. A real service must not become rent extraction.

Aquinas also asks about the end: why is the profit sought? To support household life, to serve public utility, to make needed goods available — or simply to accumulate? The external acts may look identical while the moral orientation differs. Intention alone is not enough — a merchant may intend good and charge unjustly, a fund may intend mission and structure extraction, a lender may intend help and impose harmful terms — but intention still matters, because economic acts form character and direct institutions, and a practice ordered toward service will be designed differently from one ordered toward maximum accumulation. For non-extractive finance this is decisive. A fund that exists to serve enterprises should design its returns, fees, governance, and downside allocation accordingly, not claim a noble purpose while adopting the ordinary extraction logic. If the end is service, the structure must show service; if stewardship, the governance must show stewardship; if solidarity, the risk allocation must show solidarity; if public utility, the fees and returns must stay moderate enough not to consume the utility they claim to serve. Aquinas would not be satisfied with mission language. He would ask whether the exchange is just.

The merchant article also ties profit to labor. The merchant earns because he does something — transports, stores, arranges, bears inconvenience, manages uncertainty, connects supply with need — and profit becomes more defensible the more tightly it is tied to activity that serves the real economy. This bears on management fees and fund administration. A fee for real labor can be legitimate: due diligence, legal structuring, accounting, reporting, governance support, accompaniment, mentoring, and monitoring all take time and skill, and a moral financial institution need not pretend such labor is costless. But the fee must remain tied to actual service, not become a way to extract regardless of whether value is created or mission served. The title must match reality. The same pattern explains why the later Franciscan Monti di Pietà could charge administrative fees without necessarily committing usury. They were not to profit from the poor, but they had real costs — staff, buildings, records, pledges, administration, enforcement, governance — and recovering real, moderate costs sustains a service rather than exploiting a need. It is the moral pattern of legitimate merchant profit: gain tied to real service and public utility. The danger of drift attends it always: a service institution can become revenue-seeking, cost recovery can become profit, administration can become bureaucracy, a charitable tool can become an extractive machine. Aquinas's standard of moderation and public utility has to stay active over an institution's whole life, not just at its founding.

What the article finally dissolves is the false opposition between morality and commerce. Aquinas does not imagine that holiness requires abolishing buying, selling, transport, profit, and trade. Economic life is part of human life: goods must move, households must be supported, communities need provision, and someone must bear the risk and the labor. The problem is not commerce; the problem is disordered commerce. That is good news for non-extractive business. Moral economy does not require denying economic reality — a company can sell, earn, pay wages, recover costs, and build reserves; a fund can pay staff and sustain operations; a merchant can profit from real service — all of it within the discipline of justice, moderation, truth, and the common good. Aquinas offers a positive vision of business: economic activity is virtuous when it serves real needs in a just way. The merchant is justified not by the bare fact of profit but by service, and the same should be true of capital. Capital should not be justified by the bare fact that it is capital, but by what it does — enabling work, sharing risk, supporting mission, sustaining useful production, repairing loss, serving the common good. When capital claims surplus without such service, it has begun to resemble the disordered trader who seeks gain for its own sake.

This is why q.77, a.4 is the bridge between just price and usury. It shows that Aquinas can affirm economically useful surplus while condemning surplus without title; it prepares us to understand why a partner may share profit while a usurer may not, why a merchant may earn moderate gain while a lender in a mutuum may not charge for use, why labor and risk matter, why purpose matters, why moderation matters. The modern application states itself in pairs. Not only "What return does capital demand?" but "What service does capital render?" Not only "What does the market allow?" but "What does justice permit?" Not only "Can this intermediary capture value?" but "Has it created or served value in a way that justifies its gain?" Not only "Is the profit legal?" but "Is the profit ordered toward life, work, household, community, mission, or public utility?" Aquinas's answer to merchant profit is neither permissive in the laissez-faire sense nor condemnatory in the anti-commercial sense. It is discerning: profit is morally possible, and it must explain itself — moderate, tied to service, never an infinite appetite, never fed on ignorance or need, always ordered toward human goods. That discernment is exactly what the rest of this book seeks to recover for finance.

The usury tradition, rightly understood, is not a rejection of all surplus but a demand that surplus have a just title, and legitimate merchant profit shows what such a title looks like: real service, real labor, real risk, real usefulness, moderation. The usurer has none of it. He does not transport goods or transform them, does not expose capital in partnership, does not repair a loss. He charges for the use of money in a contract where ownership has passed; his gain is tied not to service but to claim. That is why Aquinas can permit the merchant's moderate profit and condemn usury in the very next question. The difference is not that one involves money and the other does not. The difference is the moral form of the surplus. Profit that serves life can be just. Profit that feeds on need is not.

chapters.part: Part III — Aquinas: Justice, Just Price, Usury, and the Theological Grammar of Surplus

chapters.title: Aquinas on Usury
chapters.label: Chapter 8

sections.title: 8.1 The central text
paragraphs: Aquinas treats usury directly in Summa Theologiae II-II, q.78, "On the sin of usury." It is the text most often cited in discussions of Aquinas and interest, and it should never be read in isolation. It arrives after his treatments of justice, commutative justice, restitution, buying and selling, just price, defects, disclosure, and merchant profit — so that by the time Aquinas reaches usury, the moral framework is already built: exchange must preserve equality; taking more than is due injures the neighbor; unjust gain requires restitution; profit can be legitimate when tied to real service, labor, risk, or public utility; and legal consent alone does not make an exchange just. Usury appears inside that architecture.

The question has four articles. In Article 1, Aquinas asks whether it is a sin to take usury for money lent — the core article, and the home of the classic argument: to take usury for money lent is unjust because one sells what does not exist separately from the thing itself. Money, like wine or wheat, is a consumable good in the relevant sense; its use consists in its consumption, spending, or alienation; therefore to charge separately for its use while also demanding repayment of the principal is to sell the same thing twice, or to sell what cannot be separately sold. In Article 2, he asks whether one may demand any other kind of consideration for money lent — the article on which the whole later extrinsic-title tradition hangs. The lender may not require payment beyond principal as if it were owed for the loan itself; yet Aquinas allows certain things that are not payment for the use of money: compensation for loss incurred, repayment of expenses, free gifts not required by the loan, and profit-sharing where money is entrusted by way of partnership rather than mutuum. In Article 3, he asks whether one is bound to restore money received through usury, and his answer follows from commutative justice and restitution: if usury is unjust gain, what was unjustly taken must be restored. Usury is not merely a sin of intention; it creates an imbalance in the external relation between lender and borrower. In Article 4, he asks whether it is lawful to borrow money under usury — a pastoral and practical question. Aquinas distinguishes the sin of taking usury from the situation of a borrower who, under pressure or need, submits to the unjust terms imposed by another. The borrower may sometimes licitly borrow from a usurer if necessity requires, even though the lender sins by demanding usury. The one who imposes the unjust claim and the one who submits to it under need are not morally identical.

The structure is elegant: the first article defines the injustice, the second explores the possible qualifications, the third gives the remedy, the fourth addresses the borrower's predicament. And the treatment is strict without being crude. Aquinas condemns gain from the loan itself, and does not say that every payment associated with lending is automatically usury. He rejects selling the use of money, and allows compensation for actual loss. He rejects contractual payment for profit not made, and permits profit-sharing in genuine partnership. He requires restitution from the usurer, and declines to condemn automatically the needy borrower who submits to usurious terms.

The whole doctrine depends on the line between the mutuum itself and what lies outside it. In a mutuum, the lender gives ownership of money or another fungible consumable to the borrower, and the borrower owes equivalent repayment — that is the contract. If the lender demands more simply because he lent, the surplus is usury. But if some other reality exists — actual loss, expense, partnership, gift, risk, or (in later theology, under conditions) concrete foregone gain — the moral analysis changes. Here begins the distinction between intrinsic and extrinsic titles. Aquinas does not present the later fourfold taxonomy in mature form; he writes no chapter called "the four extrinsic titles," and does not systematically develop poena conventionalis, damnum emergens, lucrum cessans, and periculum sortis as later theologians will. But the architecture is already present. The loan itself gives no right to surplus. Something outside the loan may, in some cases, create a separate claim. That separate claim must be real. If it is not real, the surplus is usury.

This is why q.78 matters so much to everything that follows. The later questions all orbit its central distinction: whether actual loss can include broader liquidity harm; whether foregone profit can be compensable when concrete and morally certain; whether risk to principal can justify surplus in a loan-like arrangement; whether a late penalty can be stipulated in advance without becoming disguised interest; whether the absence of money, carentia pecuniae, can be priced; how merchant capital, public debt, exchange contracts, and insurance-like arrangements should be classified. The question is never merely whether the lender receives more. The question is why. If the "why" is "because I lent you money," Aquinas says no. If it is "because you damaged me," he opens a door. If it is "because we are partners and my capital is at risk," he opens another. If it is "because I did not make the profit I might have made," he is cautious and, in q.78, largely closes the door. If it is "because time passed," he rejects it. That makes q.78 a hinge text — at once a prohibition (one may not sell the use of money in a mutuum) and a sorting device (usury must be distinguished from other possible claims). The later extrinsic titles are attempts to make the sorting device more precise under the pressure of real economic life.

The central text also corrects a durable modern misunderstanding. Aquinas did not believe money could never be associated with productive activity. He knows money can be entrusted to a merchant by way of partnership; he knows merchants can earn profit; he knows loss can be compensated. His point is more specific: in a mutuum, the lender has transferred ownership and may not charge for use as though ownership remained. Miss that specificity and Aquinas seems naïve about capital; see it and he becomes much more challenging, because he is saying that the moral form of the contract determines whether the provider of money has any claim on the fruits. Partner — perhaps yes. Lender in a mutuum — no, not without another title. That is still a devastating question for modern finance: does capital truly participate, or does it merely claim? Many modern instruments blur exactly this line. Preferred equity claims to be risk capital while securing priority repayment. Revenue-based finance claims to share upside while imposing repayment obligations that burden the enterprise regardless of outcome. Venture capital claims partnership while using control rights to force exit. Debt claims a risk premium while holding collateral, guarantees, and seniority. Management fees claim service while functioning as extraction regardless of value created. To every such arrangement, q.78 puts one question: what is the moral form of the claim? If the capital provider is truly exposed, truly serving, truly bearing loss, or truly compensating actual harm, the analysis may differ. If the provider is protected while demanding more merely because capital was advanced, the old usury question returns.

Aquinas's treatment also shows that usury is not merely a problem of poverty lending. Scripture is most severe where the poor are concerned, and Christian preaching has always focused on exploitation of the needy; but Aquinas's formal argument applies to the mutuum as such. Even when the borrower is not poor, the lender may not demand payment for the use of money in a loan for consumption, because the injustice lies in the structure of the claim. This does not flatten the special gravity of exploiting the poor — a usurious loan to a desperate person is worse, because it adds exploitation of vulnerability to the injustice of the surplus — but the conditions that intensify the injustice do not define it. The deeper question is always whether the surplus is due.

Article 2 deserves particular attention because it supplies the first set of distinctions against overgeneralization. Aquinas allows the lender to receive back exactly what was lent; allows compensation for loss incurred; allows repayment of expenses; allows the borrower's genuinely free gift of gratitude; allows partnership profit where money is entrusted to a common venture rather than lent. He rejects payment demanded because the lender did not profit from the money, and rejects charging more for delayed payment where the extra is simply payment for waiting. Already, then, he is distinguishing moral categories — principal repayment, actual loss, expenses, free gift, partnership profit, foregone profit, payment for time — and refusing to treat them alike. This is the beginning of moral finance as classification. Article 3 then gives the doctrine teeth: the usurer must restore, because unjust gain belongs, morally, to the one from whom it was taken; the lender may have legal possession but not moral title, and restitution repairs the broken equality. And Article 4 supplies pastoral realism. People live inside unjust economic systems; a borrower may need money with no non-usurious alternative, and in such cases the borrower's act must be distinguished from the lender's — he may use the usurer's sin without approving it, as one may suffer injustice without causing it. Moral theology must not burden the vulnerable as it burdens the exploiter. The modern parallel is exact: founders, workers, households, and communities often accept extractive capital because nothing better is available, and their consent neither makes the structure just nor makes them equally responsible for its injustice.

For this book, q.78 is the pivot between ancient moral concern and the later technical development. Everything before it prepares the question — Scripture, Aristotle, Roman law, canon law, commutative justice, restitution, just price, merchant profit. Everything after it develops the implications — penalties, actual loss, foregone gain, risk to principal, labor, expense, public utility, institutional drift, and modern non-extractive capital. Aquinas gives the central rule: in a mutuum, surplus from the loan itself is unjust. He also gives the central opening: surplus may be permissible if it corresponds to something real outside the loan itself. The entire later tradition lives in the tension between that rule and that opening. The rigorists fear the opening will swallow the rule; the more permissive scholastics fear that the rule, applied rigidly, will ignore real loss, real risk, and real commerce. The task of moral theology is to preserve both justice and truth — no unjust gain from the loan itself, and no denial of genuine compensation where justice requires it. That is why q.78 remains the central text. It does not answer every later question. It tells us how to ask them.

sections.title: 8.2 The consumable-good argument
paragraphs: Aquinas's most famous argument against usury begins with a simple distinction: some things are used without being consumed, and other things are used by being consumed. A house can be lived in for a time and still stand when the use is finished; a field can be worked and remain; a tool can be used and returned; a horse can be ridden and given back. In such cases the use of the thing can be separated from the ownership of the thing, and because the thing survives its use, one can charge for temporary use while retaining ownership. Rent makes sense. But wine is used by being drunk, bread by being eaten, grain — in the relevant sense — by being consumed, planted, or alienated, and money by being spent. Here the use cannot be separated from the thing itself. To use it is to consume, spend, or alienate it, and once used, the identical thing is gone.

This is what Aquinas means by treating money as a consumable good in the context of a mutuum, and the point is not physical but legal and economic. Coins go on existing somewhere; digital balances transfer rather than vanish. The claim is about what the borrower does: he uses money by spending or alienating it. He cannot both use the money and return the same money as if it were a rented house. The money passes into exchange, and the borrower later owes an equivalent. In a loan for consumption, ownership passes; the borrower becomes owner of the money and spends it as his own; the lender no longer owns the money but a claim to equivalent repayment. The argument follows directly. If the lender demands repayment of principal, that is legitimate: the borrower received 100 and owes 100. But if the lender also demands payment for the use of the 100, he is charging for something that cannot be sold separately from the thing itself. In a consumable good, the use is the consumption. To charge for the thing and for its use is to charge twice. Aquinas compares it to selling wine and then charging separately for the drinking of the wine — absurd, because the drinking is the use, not a second commodity. Likewise the lender cannot transfer ownership of money and then charge rent for the borrower's use of what has become the borrower's own.

That is the formal core of the usury argument, and it is worth being precise about what kind of claim it is. It is not primarily an empirical claim that money never contributes to production. It is a moral-legal claim about what is being sold in the contract: if the lender has transferred ownership of a consumable thing, he may not sell its use as if that use remained his to sell. This is why usury is contrary to justice — the lender receives more than is due by charging for a nonexistent separate use, treating the use of money as separable from money itself and as still his after the transfer, so that the borrower pays not only the equivalent of what he received but a price for something that cannot be separately priced in that contract.

The argument feels strange to modern readers because modern finance talks constantly of the price of credit, the rental price of capital, the time value of money, the cost of funds — phrases that make renting money sound obvious. Aquinas denies that it is possible in a mutuum. A house can be rented because it remains the owner's and its use is separable from ownership. Money transferred in a loan for consumption cannot be rented in that sense, because ownership passes and the money is used by being spent. The lender can be repaid the equivalent; he cannot charge rent for use as though the money remained his. From a Thomistic perspective, "renting money" is morally misleading language unless the underlying structure is not a mutuum at all. If the capital remains the investor's and is placed into a partnership, profit-sharing may be possible. If the capital is genuinely at risk in an enterprise, another analysis applies. If the lender suffers actual loss, compensation may be owed; if a service is performed, a fee may be justified. But where ownership passes and equivalent repayment is owed, charging for use remains the problem — which makes Aquinas subtler than both his defenders and his critics tend to admit. He is not saying money cannot be involved in productive activity. He is saying the lender's entitlement depends on the moral form of the transaction: whoever wants a share in productive gain must enter a form that shares risk or otherwise supplies a real title, not lend in a mutuum and then claim the fruits of what the borrower does with money that has become the borrower's.

The application to modern business finance is direct. If capital is advanced to a company that must repay the principal regardless of success, and the investor receives a fixed surplus merely for the passage of time, the structure looks like a mutuum with interest. If the investor's capital is genuinely exposed to loss and return depends on the enterprise's success, the structure begins to look like partnership or risk-bearing investment, and the moral analysis changes. The consumable-good argument does not abolish capital finance; it forces finance to be honest about its form. Are you a creditor, or a partner? Did you transfer ownership, or retain an exposed stake? Do you demand repayment regardless of outcome, or share loss? Are you charging for time, or being compensated for real risk, labor, or loss? The usury problem arises when capital wants to be creditor and partner at once, one-sidedly — fixed repayment like a creditor, surplus like a participant; protection from loss, plus a claim on gain; the borrower treated as owner of the money for the purpose of repayment obligation, but not for the purpose of keeping the fruits of use. The consumable-good argument exposes the inconsistency. Once the money is the borrower's, the borrower owes the equivalent; the lender may not also demand the fruits of use unless some separate title exists.

The argument connects straight back to truth in exchange. Usury prices something falsely: it treats the use of money in a mutuum as a separable, rentable thing, which it is not. The price corresponds to no reality — an unjust price for a nonexistent object of sale. As overcharging violates equality in sale, charging for the use of money violates equality in lending, and in both cases the exchange is untrue. Nor are wine and wheat arbitrary examples. Lend wine in a mutuum and you may demand equivalent wine, not an added charge for the drinking; lend grain and you may demand equivalent grain, not a fee for the eating or planting as such; lend money and you may demand equivalent money, not a price for the spending. Modern readers may object that money is not consumed the way wine is — true at the physical level, and not decisive, because Aquinas's point concerns use within the contract. Money is used by being alienated in exchange; the borrower cannot enjoy its use while preserving the same money for return; he uses it by letting it go. That holds in a digital economy as well: a borrower who receives funds uses them by transferring, spending, investing, or paying them away, owes an equivalent, and the structure remains mutuum-like wherever ownership and repayment obligation take that form. Modern money can also be capital, funding enterprises and generating returns — but then the question is who owns the capital and who bears the risk. If the borrower owns the money and owes repayment regardless of outcome, the lender is not automatically entitled to the productive fruits. If the lender wants those fruits, the contract must be something other than a simple mutuum, or a genuine extrinsic title must exist.

The same logic disciplines the titles in advance. Against lucrum cessans: the lender says, "Had I not lent the money, I could have used it profitably," and Aquinas's response in q.78, a.2, ad 1 is cautious — the lender may be compensated for actual loss, but may not contract for compensation merely because he makes no profit from the money, since he must not sell what he does not yet have and might never obtain. The bare fact that money could have been used does not make its hypothetical fruits a separable object already owned by the lender. Later theologians will carve out exceptions for concrete foregone gain, but the warning stands: possibility is not possession. Against carentia pecuniae: if the lender's plea is "I am without my money during the term, therefore I must be paid," the consumable-good analysis asks whether this is simply another way of charging for use — for if mere absence of money suffices, every mutuum bears interest and the doctrine collapses; something more specific must be shown. And for damnum emergens, the argument clarifies why Aquinas allows it: compensation for a real loss suffered because of the loan is not payment for the use of money but restoration of damage — the payment attaches to an external harm, not to the consumable use itself. For non-extractive finance the boundary is the whole point: cost recovery, loss compensation, and risk-sharing can be legitimate, and they must never become disguised rent on money. The test is always whether the payment corresponds to something real outside the bare use of funds.

The argument also challenges the modern treatment of time. Much financial theory begins from the premise that time itself makes money now worth more than money later. Aquinas does not deny that time can matter economically; he refuses to let time alone create a title. The lender does not own time, and does not own the use of money once ownership has passed, so the passage of time must be connected to some real title before it can justify payment. Time may reveal loss; time may involve risk; time may include labor; time may cause delay damage; time may interrupt a concrete use. But time itself is not automatically a commodity the lender can sell. This is among the most radical aspects of the whole argument. Modern finance asks what money over time is worth; Aquinas asks whether the lender has any right to sell the use of what no longer belongs to him. The starting points could hardly be further apart. The consumable-good argument will not settle every modern question by itself — it needs supplementing with analysis of inflation, risk, productive capital, partnership, institutional cost, and complex instruments — but it remains a powerful moral test because it blocks the automatic conversion of time into yield. Before capital charges for time, ask what capital owns. Before capital claims return, ask what capital risked. Before capital prices use, ask whether the use is separately saleable. Before capital demands surplus, ask whether the surplus corresponds to reality.

There is finally a spiritual edge to the argument. The usurer treats a claim as though it were a productive thing; he makes money appear to generate offspring simply by passing through time; he converts the borrower's use of what is now the borrower's own into a revenue stream, blurring ownership, use, and obligation in his own favor. In doing so he trains himself to see money not as servant of exchange, nor as gift to help the neighbor, nor as capital to be risked in common enterprise, but as a self-expanding claim. That is the deeper danger of money breeding money — not only a technical mistake but a spiritual formation into false entitlement. The consumable-good argument resists the formation. It tells the lender: you gave this as a mutuum; it became the borrower's; you may receive the equivalent; if you want more, show the title. Do not pretend the use remained yours. Do not sell what you no longer own. Do not charge twice. The argument may need careful interpretation in modern contexts, and it does not eliminate every possible return to capital. But it permanently unsettles the assumption that money, once lent, naturally deserves more money back. In Aquinas's framework the mutuum is simple: what was mine became yours; you owe me the equivalent; I may not charge you for using what became yours. That is the consumable-good argument, and it is the heart of his critique of usury.

sections.title: 8.3 Mutuum versus societas
paragraphs: Aquinas's condemnation of usury does not mean that capital may never share in profit — one of the most important points in his entire treatment. He is strict about the mutuum, and he is not blind to the moral difference between a loan and a partnership. In Summa Theologiae II-II, q.78, a.2, ad 5, he distinguishes money lent in a mutuum from money entrusted to a merchant or craftsman by way of societas, and the distinction is decisive. In a mutuum, ownership passes to the borrower, who owes an equivalent later; the lender has a claim to repayment and no direct share in the borrower's business; if the borrower profits, the profit is the borrower's, and if he fails, the debt remains. The lender is a creditor. In a societas, the capital is placed into a common undertaking and its provider remains exposed to the fortunes of the enterprise. The parties are joined in a shared venture: if it gains, the provider of capital may share the gain; if it loses, he may lose. That is why Aquinas permits profit-sharing in partnership. The profit is not payment for the use of money in a mutuum; it is a share in the result of a common enterprise in which capital is genuinely at risk. The investor receives profit not because money breeds money but because he participated in a venture whose outcome was uncertain.

Aquinas does not yet speak the mature language of periculum sortis, but he supplies the conceptual structure that makes the later title possible, and the decisive fact is risk. The creditor stands outside the enterprise with a claim; the partner stands inside it with exposure. That difference changes everything, and it blocks two opposite errors at once. The first error treats every return to capital as usury — but where capital is genuinely contributed to a partnership and remains at risk, profit-sharing rests on a real title, participation in the enterprise and exposure to its loss. The second error treats every capital return as partnership profit — but if the transaction is actually a mutuum, calling the lender a "partner" does not make him one. Where repayment is guaranteed, where the borrower bears the downside, where capital is protected while claiming surplus, the moral form remains loan-like, and the surplus must be judged accordingly.

This matters now because contemporary capital dearly loves the language of partnership while preserving the protections of debt. Venture investors call themselves partners; private equity firms are literally organized as partnerships; banks announce partnerships with their clients; impact investors speak of partnership with mission-driven enterprises. Some of this is sincere, and some of it is even structurally true. Aquinas's distinction asks the harder question: does the capital truly share the fate of the enterprise? The test is not whether the parties are friendly, whether the investor gives advice, whether the capital provider cares about the company, or whether the documents use the vocabulary of alignment. The test is structural. Can the capital be lost? Does repayment depend on success? Is downside shared? Is upside paired with vulnerability? Does the investor stand with the enterprise when things go badly — or hold priority, collateral, guarantees, control rights, and exit rights that protect capital while others bear the loss? The mutuum-versus-societas distinction is a moral x-ray for those questions.

It also shows that risk-sharing is relational, not merely financial. A partner is not simply someone who receives a variable return; a partner is someone joined to the undertaking, in something like a common fate. The parties may contribute different things — money, labor, skill, reputation, tools, knowledge, networks — but they are related by participation in a shared good, not only by fixed repayment. Partnership profit is therefore morally tied to solidarity: the investor may receive because the investor has become vulnerable with the enterprise, the return arising from within a shared venture rather than extracted from outside it. For non-extractive capital, the implication is a direction of travel: wherever possible, move from creditor logic toward partnership logic — and not by changing vocabulary, but by changing risk allocation, control, repayment, governance, and exit. Does capital absorb loss before mission is destroyed? Does capital wait when the enterprise needs time, and return only when the enterprise can bear it? Does it avoid personal guarantees that transfer enterprise risk onto household vulnerability, and liquidation preferences that let investors recover while workers, suppliers, and mission are sacrificed? Does it take upside only where it takes downside? Does it support the common undertaking rather than stand over it? These are the modern forms of the mutuum-versus-societas question.

The distinction also warns against priority repayment dressed as partnership. If capital is repaid first regardless of the enterprise's condition, its provider may be closer to creditor than partner; there may be cases where priority corresponds to a real and just title, but it can never simply be assumed, and in the stricter Franciscan and anti-usury reasoning, priority repayment looks suspicious precisely because it protects capital before the living enterprise and its human participants. The key question is brutally simple: what happens when there is not enough? When things go well, nearly every structure looks like partnership — investors receive return, founders continue, workers are paid, mission advances, everyone smiles. The moral form of capital is revealed in failure. If revenue falls, who waits? If assets are liquidated, who is paid first? If the company struggles, whose claims are reduced? If the mission is threatened, whose rights yield? If loss must be absorbed, whose balance sheet takes the hit? The answers reveal whether the capital was truly in societas or merely wearing partnership language. Aquinas's permission is permission tied to form: the investor may share profit because the investor shares risk. Remove the risk and the title weakens; remove the common undertaking and it weakens further; preserve only the upside and the structure becomes morally suspect.

The same analysis sorts modern equity. Equity is usually assumed to be risk-bearing by definition — it can lose value, and equity holders are paid after creditors — and in that respect it can resemble societas more than mutuum. But not all equity is equally partnership-like. Preferred equity, liquidation preferences, redemption rights, anti-dilution protections, veto rights, forced-exit provisions, and control terms can make equity behave in creditor-like ways: legally equity, morally drifting toward protected claim. Aquinas would not be impressed by the label. The question is whether capital truly participates in common risk or uses legal form to secure asymmetric power. The mirror case exists too: some debt instruments carry genuine risk-sharing features. Revenue-based finance, recoverable grants, subordinated loans, patient capital, and profit-participating instruments can be more partnership-like than ordinary debt, if repayment truly adapts to the enterprise's condition and principal can really be lost. Legal debt can be structured with the moral features of solidarity; legal equity can be structured with the moral features of domination. The analysis must go deeper than legal category, into the actual allocation of risk, control, and claim.

Control deserves its own scrutiny. In a partnership some shared governance is appropriate, because the parties are engaged in a common undertaking — but control must be ordered toward the common good of the venture, not merely the protection of capital. An investor who uses governance rights only to secure exit, maximize return, or force repayment is behaving less like a partner than like a creditor with power. Modern venture capital often combines genuine risk-bearing with strong control rights, and the rights are not all equivalent: some protect against fraud, mismanagement, or mission drift; others hand investors the power to force outcomes that serve capital at the expense of founders, workers, users, and mission. The moral question is not just whether the investor bears some risk but whether the governance structure reflects genuine common enterprise. Aquinas's societas logic implies that shared profit should be tied to shared purpose, not only shared exposure. A partner in the full moral sense is not merely one who risks money; he participates in a common good, and the return to capital should live inside a governance structure that respects the enterprise's purpose. Otherwise risk-bearing turns merely speculative — capital accepting possible loss in exchange for the power to capture upside, with no true commitment to the good being built. Here the Christian account must go beyond a thin financial idea of risk. Gambling involves risk. Speculation involves risk. Predatory finance can involve plenty of risk. The moral legitimacy of partnership profit depends not only on exposure to uncertainty but on participation in an activity that is itself legitimate and ordered toward a good — which is why the merchant-profit analysis of q.77 matters here, and why periculum sortis, when it matures, cannot mean "wherever there is risk, surplus is justified." It must mean: where capital is genuinely exposed in a just undertaking, compensation or profit-sharing may be justified. Risk is necessary, never sufficient; the venture must be morally ordered and the return proportionate.

Labor belongs in the same frame. A partner may contribute capital while another contributes labor or skill, and profit-sharing can be just though the contributions differ — but that raises the question of how gains divide between capital and labor. The mere fact that capital is at risk does not entitle capital to unlimited upside; labor, skill, entrepreneurship, and stewardship also contribute. This is acute in start-ups and mission-driven enterprises, where founders contribute years of unpaid or underpaid labor, personal risk, reputation, creativity, and vocational commitment; workers carry the operational fragility; communities provide trust and context; users create network value. If capital claims disproportionate upside simply because it provided money, the structure can be extractive even where capital genuinely bore some risk. Societas requires proportionality among contributions. Aquinas offers no modern cap-table theory, but his justice framework demands that profit-sharing correspond to real participation — money being one contribution among several, not the only one.

Exit, too. A creditor exits by being repaid. A partner exits by some form of separation from the common undertaking, ideally without destroying it. A capital provider who demands an exit that forces the sale of the company, undermines the mission, or transfers control to extractive owners has made the supposed partnership questionable — true partnership does not treat the enterprise as a vehicle for investor liquidity. For steward ownership the point is structural: if a company exists to serve a mission beyond shareholder exit, capital must be shaped accordingly, and a partner in such an enterprise accepts limits on exit, return, and control. Otherwise the capital is not participating in the mission; it is using the mission as an investment wrapper. Participation, in Aquinas's sense, is participation in this enterprise, with its proper end — not merely in financial upside.

The contrast with the mutuum yields one last, slightly uncomfortable honesty: debt can sometimes be more truthful than pseudo-partnership. A simple loan with clear principal repayment and no excessive surplus may be morally cleaner than an "equity partnership" that captures governance and upside while pretending alignment. The problem was never that debt is bad and equity good. The problem is whether the form is truthful and just. A modest, transparent, non-extractive loan may serve life; a complex, controlling, mission-capturing equity instrument may extract; a genuine partnership may be beautiful; a fake partnership is worse than honest debt. The analysis must follow substance — which is why the distinction is a diagnostic tool, not an automatic endorsement of equity or condemnation of debt-like structures. If capital is lent as a mutuum, it may not claim partnership profit. If it is contributed as societas, it shares profit only because it shares risk and purpose. If a contract mixes features, the analysis must identify which claims correspond to which titles — exactly the work the later extrinsic-title tradition takes up. Damnum emergens asks whether the lender has suffered loss; lucrum cessans, whether he has lost a concrete gain; poena conventionalis, whether delay creates a new title; periculum sortis, whether risk to principal changes the relation; stipendium laboris, whether labor deserves compensation; carentia pecuniae, whether the absence of money can be priced without destroying the doctrine. All of them orbit the question Aquinas poses through mutuum and societas: is the surplus tied to a real moral form, or demanded from the loan itself?

The practical method for modern non-extractive finance is to test every capital instrument along a spectrum. At one end, pure mutuum: ownership passes, equivalent repayment is owed, no surplus without title. At the other, genuine societas: capital, labor, and enterprise share fate, risk, governance, and gain in proportion. Between them lie the hybrids — secured loans, subordinated debt, revenue shares, preferred equity, redeemable equity, convertible notes, guarantees, recoverable grants, capped-return instruments, mission-locked capital. The task is not to force each into a medieval category but to use the categories to ask better questions. Does this instrument behave like debt, equity, partnership, insurance, service, or sale? What happens in failure, and in success? Who controls the enterprise, who absorbs loss, who receives surplus? Is capital's return proportionate to its vulnerability? Is the mission protected from capital's exit needs? Is the language of partnership matched by the structure of partnership? These are Aquinas's questions in modern dress, and they give the book one of its central constructive pillars: capital may share in surplus when it truly shares in risk, purpose, and vulnerability; capital may not claim partnership profit while preserving creditor protection. That principle does not decide every case, but it cuts through a great deal of confusion — and it points toward a positive vision. The answer to usury is not the refusal of all return; it is truthful form. If capital wants return, let it participate: share downside, support the enterprise's good, govern modestly, accept limits, be patient, stand with the borrower, founder, worker, and mission when the storms come. Let capital become partner rather than master. That is the moral promise hidden in Aquinas's distinction between mutuum and societas.

sections.title: 8.4 Actual loss versus profit not made
paragraphs: Aquinas's most important opening toward the later doctrine of the extrinsic titles appears in Summa Theologiae II-II, q.78, a.2, ad 1, in a distinction that will shape centuries of debate: a lender may receive compensation for a loss incurred through lending, but he may not demand compensation merely because he did not make a profit from the money. This is the root of the later contrast between damnum emergens and lucrum cessans. Damnum emergens means actual loss arising: the lender has suffered a real detriment because of the loan — an expense incurred, damage caused by the borrower's conduct, having to borrow elsewhere, an obligation missed when repayment failed. Something real has been lost, and compensation for it is restorative; it does not pay the lender for the use of money, it repairs an injury. Lucrum cessans means gain ceasing: the lender claims that by lending, he lost the opportunity to profit elsewhere — not the loss of something possessed, but the failure to obtain something he might have gained. That is a far more delicate thing. The gain is future, uncertain, and not yet owned, and to demand compensation for it risks selling what one does not yet have.

Aquinas accepts the first and resists the second, and his reason is precise. Compensation for actual loss is not usury, because the lender is not selling the use of money; he is avoiding or repairing a detriment external to the loan itself. But a lender who demands payment because he did not profit is demanding compensation for something he never possessed. The expected profit might never have materialized; many things could have prevented it; to sell that expected gain is to sell what is not yet his. Justice may repair reality, but it must be cautious about pricing possibility. Modern finance treats the two as close relatives — if I could have earned elsewhere, lending to you cost me that return; opportunity cost becomes a species of loss; the capital provider experiences the absence of gain as deprivation. Aquinas insists on the stricter line. Not every unearned profit is a loss in justice, and not every possible alternative creates a claim against the borrower. Did you actually lose something — or are you charging for a future you did not yet possess?

The difference matters because without it, the usury prohibition simply collapses. If every profit not made counts as compensable loss, every loan can bear a return, since the lender always could have done something else with the money — traded, invested, bought land, purchased goods, lent to another, kept liquidity. If all such possibilities are billable, the borrower always owes more than principal, the mutuum is no longer gratuitous, and opportunity cost has quietly replaced the usury doctrine. Aquinas blocks that move. He does not deny that lending can inconvenience or harm the lender — he allows actual loss — but he refuses to let abstract possibility become a title. And the refusal is spiritually significant, not merely technical. The desire for unrealized gain can become infinite. There is always some imagined alternative, always a better return that might have been earned, always a benchmark, always a portfolio allocation that might have performed differently. If the lender's imagination of possible gain becomes the borrower's obligation, the borrower is made responsible for the lender's entire horizon of desire. Aquinas refuses this. Justice concerns what is due, not every gain one might have wanted.

The distinction also preserves the meaning of gift and help. A person who lends to a neighbor, especially a neighbor in need, gives up the possibility of using the money elsewhere, and that sacrifice may be real in a broad sense. But not every sacrifice creates a claim. Some sacrifices belong to charity, some to neighborly obligation, some to the discipline of mercy — and if every act of help must be compensated for every possible foregone advantage, help becomes indistinguishable from investment. Scripture had already drawn the boundary: Deuteronomy commands the lender not to harden his heart as the release year approaches, though repayment is visibly uncertain; Leviticus commands that the poor brother be upheld so that he may live; Exodus commands the cloak returned. None of these texts denies that generosity costs the lender. They place some costs under the command of mercy, and Aquinas's distinction between actual loss and profit not made stands in continuity with that biblical discipline. Actual harm may be repaired in justice; not every missed gain may be charged to the needy borrower.

None of this makes lucrum cessans disappear — commercial life will force the question, and later theologians will develop the title extensively. A merchant's money may not be idle: it may be concretely committed to trade, a sum set aside for a specific profitable transaction, a stock of goods with a predictable future sale. If lending, or being deprived of the money, interrupts that concrete use, the lost gain begins to look like a real detriment. And Aquinas himself supplies some of the tools for the development: in his treatment of restitution he recognizes that preventing someone from obtaining a future good can sometimes create an obligation, and in his treatment of just price he recognizes that a thing may have special value to its owner because parting with it causes loss. Cajetan and others will use exactly these principles to nuance the rejection. But in q.78 his immediate answer stays restrictive, because he sees the danger: future gain becoming the standing pretext for interest. He is not naïve — he knows future goods can matter — and he will not convert every possible future profit into present entitlement. The later tradition lives inside that tension. Hostiensis had already opened space for lucrum cessans with restrictions; Peter John Olivi will think hard about merchant capital and future value; Sant'Antonino, Bernardino, Cajetan, Summenhart, Soto, Navarrus, Molina, Lessius, Lugo, and Alphonsus will all wrestle with how far foregone gain can be recognized without destroying the prohibition. The title will be increasingly accepted, always under conditions, and the stricter Thomistic warning will keep sounding underneath: mere possibility is not enough.

That is why q.78, a.2, ad 1 is foundational: it draws the line the later centuries will debate, adjust, and sometimes stretch. Actual loss may be compensated. Profit not made may not simply be sold. For modern finance, the line is explosive. A fund says: if we put capital into this enterprise, we cannot put it elsewhere; the enterprise must compensate us for the return we gave up. Aquinas would ask: what exactly did you give up? Was there a concrete alternative, licit and reasonably certain? Were you actually going to pursue it? Was the gain morally on the way, or merely possible? Is the borrower responsible for your entire investment universe? A venture fund says: our investors expect a certain return, so portfolio companies must be structured to deliver it. Aquinas would ask whether investor expectation creates a title in justice against the company — desire, expectation, and benchmark are not the same as due. A lender says: the rate reflects what capital could earn in the market. Aquinas would ask whether "the market" names a concrete foregone gain or an abstraction — and whether the alternative gain would itself have been morally legitimate, since one cannot justify a claim against a borrower by pointing to an alternative extraction. A mission-driven fund says: we need to be sustainable. Aquinas would distinguish: real costs, real losses, and real stewardship labor may be compensable, but sustainability must not become a name for whatever return capital would like. The title must be specific.

The distinction also catches damnum emergens being stretched into lucrum cessans without being named. A lender calls something "loss" when what is meant is "return I did not earn"; a fund calls underperformance a loss; an investor calls lack of yield a cost. Aquinas forces clarity. Did something actually go out? Was something actually damaged? Was an obligation actually missed, a cost actually incurred? Or did a hoped-for gain simply fail to appear? Only the first set belongs to damnum emergens — a sorting with direct consequences for accounting. In a moral-finance ledger, actual costs and losses belong in a different category from opportunity costs: administrative expense, legal fees, monitoring costs, emergency liquidity damage, and documented collection costs on one side; foregone return, alternative deployment, and investor hurdle rates on the other. Mixing them manufactures moral confusion. Proportionality follows the same logic. If the title is actual loss, the measure is the loss: a lender who lost 10 is not restored by 20, an administrative cost of 3 does not justify a fee of 15 under the name of cost recovery, and a penalty for delay should correspond to the specific damage or to a proportionate disciplinary function. If the title is foregone gain, the measure is far harder — what gain, how likely, over what time, licit or not, net of costs and risks, and would the lender actually have obtained it? That uncertainty is exactly why Aquinas is cautious. Modern finance resolves the uncertainty with market rates, discount rates, and benchmarks; Aquinas would not necessarily reject all estimation, but he would not let estimation erase moral uncertainty. A benchmark is not a title. A model is not justice.

The distinction protects borrowers from being charged for the lender's internal portfolio logic — a borrower needs money for a concrete purpose, and to make him responsible for everything capital might have done elsewhere is to impose an abstract financial horizon onto a concrete human relation. It is most troubling where the borrower is vulnerable: the poor borrower of Exodus does not owe the lender for every profitable thing the lender might have done instead of helping him; the poor brother of Leviticus is not a debtor to the lender's opportunity set; Deuteronomy's open hand is not governed by market benchmarks. Aquinas gives that scriptural instinct philosophical and juridical form. And the same distinction protects the lender too, because Christian moral economy is not sentimentality about one side. If the borrower's conduct causes real loss, or lending produces a specific detriment, justice may permit compensation — the lender is also a person, his goods also matter, and commutative justice runs both ways. Aquinas does not say lenders have no rights. He says lenders have the rights that correspond to reality: a right to principal; possibly to compensation for actual loss; possibly to expense reimbursement; possibly to profit-sharing in partnership — but no right to every profit imagination can supply.

For non-extractive capital, the discipline writes itself as a set of separations: actual loss from missed return; cost recovery from yield; mission sustainability from investor appetite; concrete foregone use from abstract opportunity cost; restorative compensation from capital entitlement. Lose those separations and non-extractive finance quietly becomes ordinary finance with gentler language — it begins by recovering costs and ends by defending target returns, begins by compensating real loss and ends by billing opportunity cost, begins by sharing risk and ends by protecting capital while charging for risk. Aquinas's distinction is the drift-brake. Every surplus claim gets sorted. Is this damnum emergens? Show the actual loss. Is this lucrum cessans? Show the concrete foregone gain, and be careful. Is this poena conventionalis? Show the culpable delay and the proportionality. Is this periculum sortis? Show the real risk to principal. Is this labor? Show the work. Is this expense? Show the cost. Is it merely desired return? Then do not call it justice.

There is a spiritual teaching in the line as well. It tells the lender that not every unrealized possibility is owed to him; it disciplines the imagination of entitlement; it prevents capital from turning the future into property before the future has happened. This may be the deepest point in the whole chapter. Usury is partly a sin against the future — it claims future increase as if already owned and makes the borrower responsible for producing it — and lucrum cessans, undisciplined, does something similar, converting the profit I might have had elsewhere into your obligation. Aquinas says no, or at least not so easily. The future is not yours to sell simply because you can imagine it. Actual loss belongs to justice; imagined profit belongs, most of the time, to desire. The hard cases lie between, and later theology will spend centuries in that borderland. But the first distinction remains load-bearing, because without it all moral finance is lost. The lender may be restored. The lender may not automatically be enriched.

sections.title: 8.5 Waiting for payment and hidden usury
paragraphs: Aquinas is especially alert to the possibility that usury can hide inside delay. Usury does not always arrive as a loan with explicit interest. It can arrive as a sale with delayed payment, a higher price for credit, a penalty clause, a fee for waiting — any arrangement in which time quietly becomes the thing being sold. The form changes; the question does not: is someone receiving more merely because payment is postponed?

In Summa Theologiae II-II, q.78, a.2, ad 7, Aquinas addresses the case directly. A seller charges more because he allows the buyer to delay payment, and the answer is strict: if the higher price is charged simply because the seller waits, the arrangement has the character of usury. The delay functions like a loan; the extra amount is tied not to the value of the thing sold but to time. This is the crucial bridge between just price and usury. A sale and a loan are different contracts — one sells a thing for a price, the other gives a consumable and receives an equivalent later — but a sale on credit becomes loan-like when the extra charge is not part of the thing's value but a price for deferred payment. The seller says, in effect: have the thing now, pay later, and pay more because I am waiting. Aquinas sees the danger at once. Waiting for payment can be used to smuggle usury into sale. Not every difference between a cash price and a credit price will be simple to judge — a seller may incur actual costs from delayed payment, may bear real risk, the good's price may genuinely vary over time, there may be administrative burdens, and later theologians will work through such cases in detail — but the core warning is fixed: time alone is not a title.

Run the numbers and the point is hard to evade. A seller offers a good for 100 now or 110 in a year. If the extra 10 is simply the price of waiting, the seller has effectively sold the good for 100 and lent that price for a year at 10. The sale contains a hidden loan; the surplus attaches to deferral, not to the good's worth; the transaction is legally a sale and morally on its way to usury. This is why q.77 sits immediately before q.78: the just price of the good must not be confused with the price of time. And the issue, once again, is truth in exchange. What is being sold — the good? time? the use of money the seller is, in effect, leaving with the buyer? Aquinas makes the seller answer. Later centuries of disguised-usury analysis grow from exactly this concern: if direct interest is forbidden, the same economic substance hides inside a higher deferred price, a repurchase agreement, a bill of exchange, a penalty expected from the beginning, a fee for waiting, a manipulated sale. The tradition learns to ask not what the contract is called but what role time plays in the surplus. Where time is merely the occasion in which real loss, risk, expense, or delay damage occurs, compensation may be analyzable. Where time itself is the commodity being priced, Aquinas is deeply suspicious.

Here he collides squarely with the modern concept of the time value of money — the premise, foundational to discounting, investment valuation, bond pricing, project finance, and the cost of capital, that money now is worth more than money later. His framework does not map neatly onto modern finance, but it puts a searching theological question to it: may the mere passage of time be converted into a claim against another person? His answer is not that time never matters. It is that time matters only through morally relevant realities that occur in time. Time may bring actual loss; time may bring risk; time may involve labor; time may bring inflation or currency change, which later thinkers will have to analyze; time may bring wrongful delay; time may interrupt a concrete alternative use. But time itself, abstracted from these realities, is not something the lender owns and may sell. The lender does not own time; he does not own the borrower's future; he does not retain ownership of money that has passed in a mutuum. Waiting can be costly, and not every cost is due from the borrower. Waiting can be inconvenient, and inconvenience is not always compensable. Waiting can involve opportunity, and opportunity is not possession. Waiting can create risk, and risk must be real and borne by the one claiming compensation. And sometimes waiting is precisely what charity requires — Deuteronomy 15 commands the lender not to harden his heart as the release year approaches, when lending has become financially unattractive by design. The biblical imagination does not treat waiting and uncertainty as automatic reasons to demand more from the poor; it treats them as occasions for trust, mercy, and obedience. Aquinas gives that discipline legal-theological form: extra charged merely for delay has the character of usury.

This does not abolish late-payment penalties, and the distinction he preserves matters enormously. Wrongful delay can cause real harm: a debtor who promises repayment on a date and culpably fails may leave the creditor unable to meet obligations, out of pocket, wrongfully deprived of what is owed. Compensation or penalty may then be justified — this is the basis of poena conventionalis and morae poena. But a penalty for wrongful delay is morally different from a price for agreed delay. The first says: you failed to do what you owed, and the failure created a new injustice. The second says: because payment comes later, I charge more for time. One responds to breach and may restore or discipline justice; the other monetizes waiting and may hide interest. Hence the tradition's sharp concern with penalties expected from the beginning. If a contract sets a due date everyone knows the borrower will miss, and then imposes a penalty, the penalty is not penal at all — it is a planned return, the "delay" fictional, the transaction designed to produce surplus through stumbling. This is poena conventionalis in fraudem — a penalty in fraud of the usury prohibition — and Aquinas's warning about delayed payment anticipates the whole later anti-fraud analysis. The moral issue is never the label "sale," "penalty," or "credit term," but whether the surplus is tied to a just title.

Modern finance is dense with the analogous structures. Default interest that becomes a major profit center. A credit-card business that depends economically on late fees. Supplier payment terms with financing costs hidden in the invoice. Buy-now-pay-later products that present themselves as convenience while monetizing delay. Venture instruments that accrue growing repayment obligations; convertible notes that accumulate claims which pressure the company later; revenue-based financing with repayment multiples that function like time-priced capital; extension fees, monitoring fees, and restructuring fees that increase precisely when the borrower is weakest. Aquinas's question applies to every one of them: is this charge tied to a real cost, loss, risk, or service — or is it a price for time under another name? The question matters most where the borrower is vulnerable. If delay arises from distress, illness, market shock, late receivables, or genuine inability, a charge for delay deepens the very harm moral finance exists to repair; a late penalty proportionate between commercial equals can be oppressive imposed on a poor household or a fragile enterprise.
