The creditor must ask whether he is restoring justice or harvesting distress. The Exodus cloak returns here. A creditor who charges more when the borrower is late may say he is enforcing discipline — perhaps he is. He may also be holding the cloak overnight, using the borrower's weakness as a source of gain, structuring the contract so that the borrower's difficulty becomes the lender's yield.

Aquinas's strictness protects the borrower from that dynamic, and it protects the lender's soul as well. A lender who profits from delay may come to desire delay — to prefer default to timely repayment, to design terms that look reasonable but generate revenue when borrowers stumble, to grow attached not to justice but to arrears. That is spiritually deforming. A true penalty is something the lender would rather not receive: its purpose is to deter breach or repair harm, not to generate income, and a lender who welcomes delay because delay produces surplus has already changed the moral form of his claim. For non-extractive design the implications are concrete. Late fees should not be revenue centers; default interest should not be a business model; grace periods should be real and cure rights meaningful; hardship should be considered; penalties should be proportionate; delay charges should correspond to actual cost or damage; the lender should prefer timely repayment to penalty income; and where the borrower is in genuine distress, restructuring may be more just than escalation. None of this is softness. It is moral clarity — accountability without extraction, discipline without monetizing failure, repayment expectations without turning delay into yield.

The same analysis separates the honest credit sale from the loan disguised as one. A seller may legitimately weigh many things in a price — cost, risk, storage, transport, scarcity, quality, the value of the good — but if the difference between the immediate and the deferred price is simply the price of waiting for money, the sale has taken on a loan's character: the seller is financing the buyer, and the financing must be judged under usury principles. Modern trade credit and vendor financing live exactly here. Not every difference in payment terms is unjust — businesses incur real costs when receivables are delayed, from administration to default risk to financing — but the moral accounting must be explicit. What does the higher price correspond to: cost recovery, risk, service, or interest hidden in the invoice? Aquinas demands the difference be named truthfully. Early-payment discounts face the same test, since a "discount" can be simply the inverse of a charge for delay; the label answers nothing, and the substance decides.

Underneath all of it lies the deepest issue: whether finance has turned time into a master. Biblical time is punctuated by Sabbath and release — it is not simply a field for accumulation. Aristotelian money exists for exchange, not infinite increase. The Roman mutuum transfers ownership and creates a claim to equivalent repayment. Aquinas draws the three together: the lender cannot demand more merely because time has passed. Modern finance runs on the opposite assumption — time is money, delay is chargeable, liquidity has a price, future payments must be discounted, capital must be compensated for waiting — and Aquinas declines to let any of it pass unexamined. He asks whether time has become an idol, a domain where capital expects automatic growth. Not all temporal valuation is thereby forbidden; but time-based claims must be morally grounded in something real — loss, risk, labor, cost, concrete foregone gain, or another just cause. For a non-extractive framework this yields a workable rule: charges that grow merely because time passes are morally suspect unless tied to a real, documented, proportionate title. Taken seriously, the rule would transform familiar structures. It distinguishes ordinary repayment schedules from compounding claims; it scrutinizes default interest and automatic accruals; it questions preferred returns that accumulate regardless of enterprise health; it examines whether repayment multiples are time-based extraction; it forces funds to justify extension fees by actual cost or risk; it discourages any structure that makes delay profitable for capital. Above all it asks whether time is being used to help life mature or to tighten a claim. In Christian moral economy, time does not belong primarily to capital. It belongs to God, and it includes Sabbath, patience, growth, repentance, mercy, maturation, and release. A financing structure that turns every passing month into a larger claim may be economically familiar; it is theologically dangerous. Aquinas's warning about delayed payment is no medieval technicality. It is a challenge to the financial colonization of time. Waiting can be generous, risky, costly, patient, merciful — and waiting does not automatically entitle the one who waits to more. If more is claimed, the title must be shown. That is Aquinas's discipline, and one of the deepest safeguards against hidden usury.

sections.title: 8.6 Aquinas beyond the Summa
paragraphs: The Summa's usury question is the central text, but it is not the only place where Aquinas's thought matters. To read him fully, q.78 must sit alongside his broader treatments of justice, restitution, just price, partnership, future goods, and delayed repayment — and it must be remembered that he wrote before the extrinsic titles reached mature form. He is not the final manualist synthesizer. He is the architect, whose principles later theologians will extend, dispute, and sometimes stretch. That is why he can be both stricter and more fertile than a summary suggests. Stricter: he clearly condemns surplus from the mutuum itself, rejects charging for the use of money, rejects payment merely for profit not made, suspects higher prices for delayed payment, insists on restitution, and issues no blanket permission for opportunity cost, risk premiums, or commercial interest. More fertile: he distinguishes loan from partnership; allows compensation for actual loss; recognizes that preventing someone from obtaining a future good can sometimes require restitution; recognizes that a thing may have special value to its owner; recognizes that merchant profit can be licit when ordered toward household support or public utility; recognizes that delay can cause harm. Those principles create the openings later theologians will develop into the full doctrines of damnum emergens, lucrum cessans, poena conventionalis, and periculum sortis. Aquinas stands at the center of the tradition not because he says everything, but because he gives the grammar.

One important text beyond the Summa is De malo, q.13, a.4, ad 14, where Aquinas discusses late repayment and recognizes that a creditor may suffer harm when repayment is delayed. It shows he is not blind to the real effects of time. He does not think delay is irrelevant, or that the lender must absorb every harm caused by the borrower's failure to pay. What he rejects is the automatic monetization of time as such — and the distinction is essential. If a borrower wrongfully delays and thereby harms the lender, compensation may be appropriate: payment not for the mere passage of time but for the damage caused by delay, a different moral object belonging to the logic of restitution rather than the logic of usury. Here is the seed of morae poena and the late-payment analysis: a penalty legitimate where delay is culpable and harmful, provided it is not a disguised price for time, not expected from the beginning as a revenue model, not engineered by impossible deadlines. The same pattern holds for actual loss. In the Summa, the lender may agree with the borrower on compensation for loss incurred — the root of damnum emergens; he is not selling the use of money but avoiding or repairing loss. The later tradition will argue over what counts: legal costs, collection costs, emergency borrowing, inability to meet obligations, damage from nonpayment, administrative expense, liquidity harm — some expansions plausible, others dangerous. The principle is Aquinas's: real loss can be restored without usury, because restoration is not profit.

His treatment of restitution in q.62 matters beyond the usury article too, for there he recognizes that one may owe restitution not only for things already taken but sometimes for preventing another from obtaining a good. That principle bears directly on lucrum cessans. In q.78 Aquinas resists contractual compensation for profit not made, because the lender should not sell what he does not yet have; q.62 gives later theologians a way to ask whether some future goods are concrete enough to matter in justice — if a person was on the way to receiving a good and another unjustly prevented it, the loss is not pure fantasy. This is why later Thomists can argue over lucrum cessans while claiming, with some right, to stand in Aquinas's tradition: they are reading different parts of him together. The question becomes: when is a foregone gain real enough to be treated as a loss? Aquinas remains cautious, fearing that speculative future profit will become a pretext for ordinary interest, but his broader principles do not render every future-oriented claim impossible. They demand discipline — the future gain concrete, causally connected, reasonably certain, and not merely an imagined opportunity — which is exactly what makes him useful for modern opportunity-cost reasoning. He will not let us collapse actual loss into hypothetical gain, and he will not force us to pretend all future goods are morally irrelevant. He teaches us to ask how real the future claim is.

The just-price material adds a further layer. In q.77 he allows that a thing may have special value to its owner, since parting with it can impose a real detriment not captured by ordinary market price — relevant later to both damnum emergens and lucrum cessans, because a merchant's capital already ordered toward a concrete transaction, or a good held for a specific future sale, may be worth more to its owner than the market says, and parting with it may interrupt a real economic use. The principle stays disciplined — special value is not a blank check, cannot justify arbitrary pricing, must correspond to real circumstances — but it shows that Aquinas's account of value is more contextual than a crude reading suggests. And q.77, a.4 on merchant profit matters in the same way: he permits moderate profit where trade serves household support or public utility, which means commerce itself can be legitimate — and if commerce is legitimate, the interruption of concrete commercial activity may be morally significant, another root from which lucrum cessans will grow. His warning travels with the permission: trade pursued merely for gain is dangerous because the desire for gain has no natural limit, so the fact that one could have profited never automatically makes the borrower responsible for that profit. The gain must be tied to real, licit, ordered activity.

The mutuum–societas distinction is perhaps his most important non-restrictive principle. In q.78, a.2, ad 5 he allows profit-sharing where money is entrusted to a merchant or craftsman by way of partnership and remains at the investor's risk — the foundation for all later discussion of risk-bearing capital and periculum sortis. The key is exposure: the investor is not a lender with a guaranteed claim but a participant in the venture, and profit is the fruit of shared enterprise rather than payment for the use of money. This makes Aquinas far more useful for modern finance than a simplistic anti-interest reading allows. He gives a way to affirm genuine risk-sharing without blessing creditor extraction: capital may serve enterprise, in a form that matches its claim. If capital wants profit, it must participate. If it wants safety, it may not also claim the fruits of risk as if it had borne them. That is the heart of non-extractive capital design.

Reading Aquinas beyond the Summa also means respecting the developmental character of his own thought. He wrote across contexts, genres, and stages — the Commentary on the Sentences, the disputed questions, the Summa itself — and these do not always function identically. Modern scholars such as Pierre Januard have drawn attention to Aquinas's treatment of risk and commercial uncertainty beyond the simplified textbook account. This does not mean Aquinas secretly endorses modern interest; it means his analysis of risk, uncertainty, and contract form is more nuanced than q.78 alone would show. He is regularly flattened from two directions — into a rigid anti-finance thinker who believed money could never be productive in any sense, or into a proto-modern economist whose principles justify interest the moment commerce grows complex — and both readings miss the tension he actually holds. Aquinas is neither naïve nor permissive. He understands loss, partnership, merchant profit, delay, restitution, and the occasional moral weight of future goods. And he insists that the lender may not sell the use of money in a mutuum, that hypothetical profit is not possession, that time itself is not a title, and that unjust surplus must be restored. The tradition after him is a long argument over how to preserve all of these truths at once.

For the same reason, Aquinas should not be cast as the sole inventor of the extrinsic titles. He did not produce all four in mature form. Poena conventionalis has roots in legal penalty clauses and canon-law analysis before and around him. Damnum emergens is clearly supported by his principle of compensation for actual loss, and develops through the canon-law interesse. Lucrum cessans is resisted by him in q.78, then admitted by later theologians under conditions, using his own other principles. Periculum sortis he never formulates as a title, though his distinction between mutuum and partnership supplies its conceptual foundation. Aquinas is central, not solitary. The doctrine is not "what Aquinas invented" but a long conversation among Scripture, Aristotle, Roman law, canonists, scholastics, Franciscans, Dominicans, Salamancans, Jesuits, and later manualists. This matters for this book: the four extrinsic titles should not be presented as if they dropped fully formed from the Summa. They are historical developments, each with its own genealogy, shaped by practical pressure, legal creativity, pastoral concern, and theological debate. Aquinas gives decisive principles; Hostiensis, Olivi, Scotus, Alexander of Alexandria, Cajetan, Summenhart, Navarrus, Soto, Molina, Lessius, Lugo, Alphonsus, and Concina all matter after him.

Reading him whole guards against two mistakes. The first uses Aquinas as a blunt prohibition that cannot speak to complex finance — ignoring his distinctions around loss, partnership, restitution, and merchant profit. The second uses his openings to justify modern finance too quickly — ignoring his strict rejection of surplus from the mutuum itself, his suspicion of speculative future gain, his refusal to monetize time. The right reading is more demanding than either: every surplus claim must be morally located. If it comes from the bare mutuum, it is unjust. If from actual loss, prove the loss. If from delay, prove the harm and the culpability. If from foregone gain, prove concreteness and causality, and beware speculation. If from risk, prove the principal was genuinely exposed. If from partnership, prove shared fate. If from labor, prove the work. If from cost, prove the cost. That is Aquinas's method extended, and for modern non-extractive finance it is invaluable, because it refuses both ideological extremes — it does not demand that all capital be costless, and it does not bless all capital return. It asks for moral accounting. A fund may recover actual costs, pay for real stewardship labor, share return where it truly bears risk, and be compensated for concrete loss. It may not impose a target return because capital expects growth, call opportunity cost a loss without showing concreteness, claim a risk premium while protecting itself from loss, structure penalties as yield, or use mission language to hide creditor domination. This is not anti-finance; it is finance under judgment.

Aquinas beyond the Summa teaches one more thing: humility. The tradition had to keep thinking because economic life kept changing. He did not close every question; he opened a disciplined way of asking them, and later theologians had to apply his principles to new contracts, markets, institutions, and risks. So must we — and applying Aquinas does not mean baptizing whatever finance currently does. It means submitting modern finance to the same moral anatomy: ownership, use, risk, loss, title, equality, restitution, purpose. The enduring question is never whether Aquinas would have approved a specific modern instrument by name — he did not know our instruments. The question is whether the instrument can survive his analysis. What is the contract really? Has ownership passed? Is the claim fixed or contingent? Is capital truly at risk? Is the surplus tied to real loss, labor, cost, partnership, or risk? Is time being sold as such? Is the opportunity cost concrete or abstract? Is the borrower vulnerable? Does the structure preserve equality? If unjust gain has been taken, will it be restored? These are the questions Aquinas leaves us, and his work beyond the central article shows that the answers must be precise, not rhetorical. He is never satisfied with "interest bad" or "commerce good." He asks what is due in justice. That is the grammar later generations inherit.

Aquinas, then, is not the end of the story. He is its center of gravity. The chapters ahead move past him into the full development of the extrinsic titles, and the reader should carry his core discipline forward: do not sell what is not yours; do not charge for what cannot be separately sold; do not confuse possible gain with actual possession; do not call protected credit partnership; do not monetize time without title; do not keep what was unjustly taken; and do not let financial form conceal moral substance. That is Aquinas beyond the Summa — not a closed manual, but a moral method for judging capital.

chapters.part: Part III — Aquinas: Justice, Just Price, Usury, and the Theological Grammar of Surplus

chapters.title: What Is an Extrinsic Title?
chapters.label: Chapter 9

sections.title: 9.1 Intrinsic versus extrinsic claims
paragraphs: The doctrine of the extrinsic titles begins with a deceptively simple distinction: some claims arise inside the mutuum itself, and other claims arise outside it. This is the hinge on which the entire scholastic analysis of usury turns. If a payment is demanded because money has been lent and time has passed, the claim is intrinsic to the loan itself — the lender is asking for more than the equivalent principal simply because the borrower has had the use of the money, and in the strict scholastic account that is precisely what the usury prohibition forbids. But if a payment is demanded because something else has happened — the borrower repaid late, the lender suffered an actual loss, the lender was deprived of a concrete and otherwise available gain, the lender truly bore the risk of losing the principal, the lender incurred labor or expense — then the surplus claim may not be intrinsic to the mutuum at all. It may be attached to a burden standing outside the loan. That is the basic meaning of an extrinsic title.

The background is the contract the last several chapters have anatomized. The mutuum is a loan for consumption: the lender gives money, grain, wine, oil, or some other fungible good; ownership passes; the borrower owes back not the identical coins or sacks but the tantundem, the equivalent amount of the same kind and quality. The intrinsic claim of the mutuum is therefore simple — repayment of equivalent principal — and the simplicity is morally decisive. Once the money is transferred, the lender no longer owns it; he holds a claim for repayment, not a continuing ownership claim over the money's use. This is why Aquinas argues that charging for both the money and its use is selling the same thing twice: in a consumable good the use cannot be separated from the thing, and if ownership has passed, the lender cannot sell the borrower the "use" of what the borrower now owns. The scholastic objection to usury is not an emotional dislike of profit. It is an argument about ownership, justice, and the moral structure of the contract — the lender may receive back what was lent, and may not, merely by virtue of the loan, demand more, because to do so is to convert the borrower's need, time, or dependence into a revenue stream.

Yet it does not follow that every payment above principal is automatically usury, and here the tradition was far subtler than its reputation. The crucial question was never merely "Was more than principal received?" but "Why was more than principal received?" If the answer is "because the lender lent money," the surplus is intrinsic and falls under the prohibition. If the answer is "because the lender suffered a real loss," the payment may be restorative. If "because the borrower culpably delayed repayment and caused damage," it may be penal or compensatory. If "because the lender actually bore the risk of losing the capital," it may resemble the return due a partner rather than the gain of a usurer. If "because the lender performed real labor or incurred real administrative expense," it may be reimbursement rather than interest.

This is the moral grammar of the extrinsic titles, and one feature of it deserves emphasis before anything else: the titles do not abolish the usury prohibition. They presuppose it. They make sense only in a world where the mutuum itself remains gratuitous and surplus from the loan itself remains morally suspect. Modern readings regularly miss this, treating the titles as loopholes by which medieval theologians slowly learned to tolerate interest. There is some historical truth in the charge — the titles could be stretched, abused, and eventually used to rationalize ordinary interest, and later chapters will watch it happen. But in their strict form they were not loopholes. They were diagnostic instruments, and the question they asked was exact: what, precisely, is being compensated?

Not all compensation is extraction. If the lender's barn burns because repayment was wrongfully delayed, he may be restored. If he must pay legal costs to recover what was owed, those costs may be reimbursed. If a charitable lending institution must pay clerks, appraisers, guards, and accountants, those expenses may be covered. If capital is placed genuinely at risk in a partnership, its provider may share the gain because he shares the possible loss. And the reverse holds with equal force: not every contractual label creates a genuine title. A "late penalty" can be engineered to function as ordinary interest. A claimed "loss" can be fictional. A claimed "foregone profit" can be nothing more than abstract opportunity cost. A claimed "risk premium" can be charged even where the lender has shifted every meaningful downside onto the borrower through collateral, guarantees, seniority, and enforcement rights. The title may be named while the moral reality remains usury. So the distinction between intrinsic and extrinsic claims has to stay disciplined. An extrinsic title is not a magic word; it is a claim that must be proven. It must point to a real burden external to the mutuum. It must be proportionate to that burden. It must not double-count the same harm. It must not be pre-arranged so that the lender secretly desires the borrower's failure. It must not convert another person's vulnerability into predictable yield.

What the tradition offers here is a sharper vocabulary than the modern word "interest." Modern finance collapses many different things into one category — interest rate, cost of capital, return, yield, discount rate, risk premium, liquidity premium, penalty, fee. The older tradition pulls them apart and asks whether a payment restores a loss, compensates labor, prices genuine risk, reimburses expense, punishes culpable delay, or merely rewards the ownership of money as such. The distinction matters because moral theology is not satisfied with accounting categories. Two payments can look identical in a spreadsheet and carry different moral forms. A ten-euro payment may be just as reimbursement of a real administrative cost, and unjust as an extraction from a poor borrower who simply needed time. A five percent return may be licit where capital was genuinely at risk in a partnership, and usurious where the lender demanded guaranteed repayment, collateral, penalties, and upside while bearing no meaningful downside. The question is never only how much is paid. The question is what the payment is for.

This is exactly why the extrinsic titles matter for modern non-extractive finance. The old doctrine does not ask anyone to pretend that lending, investing, administration, risk, and delay are costless. It asks that the costs be named honestly; that restoration be distinguished from extraction; that surplus be traced to service, loss, risk, or participation rather than to the borrower's dependence. In that sense the titles are not a retreat from the usury prohibition but the prohibition made more precise: the mutuum itself yields only repayment of equivalent principal, while real economic life — with its losses, risks, delays, expenses, and forms of cooperation — is given its honest due. The moral task is neither to condemn every surplus nor to bless every return. It is to ask what kind of surplus this is. Does it come from the loan itself, or from something outside it? Does it restore equality, or exploit inequality? Does it compensate a real burden, or monetize another person's need? That is the question with which the doctrine of the extrinsic titles begins.

sections.title: 9.2 Interesse before "interest"
paragraphs: Before there was "interest" in the modern financial sense, there was interesse — and the difference is not a matter of spelling but of moral logic. In modern English, "interest" means the price of borrowing money: principal plus an agreed percentage over time, treated as the normal cost of capital, the market price of credit, the compensation due for the time value of money. In ordinary financial language, interest is intrinsic to the loan; it belongs to the very structure of debt. The older Latin term meant something else. Interesse is "that which stands between" — the difference, the loss suffered because of an intervening event. In legal and scholastic usage it referred to compensation for damage, loss, or injury: a way of measuring the gap between the position a person should have occupied and the worse position in which another's action had left him. It was not, in its strict form, a return on capital because capital had been lent.

Translating interesse too quickly as "interest" therefore misleads. It makes medieval authors sound as if they were already speaking the language of modern banking, when they were usually doing something different — not asking "What rate of return should money earn?" but "What loss has been caused, and what compensation would restore justice?" The difference is decisive, and a simple case shows it. A borrower promises repayment on the first of May. If he repays on time, there is no additional claim: the mutuum is fulfilled, the lender receives the equivalent principal, equality is restored. But suppose he wrongfully delays — and because of the delay the lender cannot meet an obligation of his own, must borrow elsewhere and pay a fee, loses goods he would otherwise have preserved, incurs legal costs recovering what was owed. The lender's claim for something more does not arise from the fact that he lent money. It arises from the damage caused by the borrower's failure to repay what was due. The additional amount is not the price of the loan; it is compensation for the injury. That is interesse in the older sense — a word that belongs to the language of restitution before it belongs to the language of finance, oriented toward repair, closely tied to damnum emergens, the actual loss arising from non-performance.

And it is still not modern interest. It is not automatic, not universal, not due merely because money has been absent for a period, not calculated as a normal yield on principal. It depends on a real injury, caused by the borrower's action or failure; it must be proportionate; it must restore rather than enrich. Seen from here, the tradition's two commitments stop looking contradictory. The scholastics could condemn usury and permit compensation because they never began from the collapsed modern category in which all surplus above principal is "interest." They began by distinguishing the moral reasons for payment. A payment demanded because money was lent is one thing; a payment required because an actual loss was caused is another. A payment extracted because the borrower needs time is one thing; a payment made because wrongful delay injured the lender is another. A payment claimed because capital deserves growth is one thing; a payment made because justice requires repair is another. The doctrine of interesse sits exactly at those points of distinction.

It also explains the tradition's near-obsessive anxiety about language. A contract could be worded as compensation and function as interest. A penalty could be called a penalty and be expected from the beginning. A claim for loss could be inflated with imaginary gains. A title could be named and not be present. The moral question could never be answered from the label attached to a payment; one had to ask what the payment actually did — restore a real loss, or create a hidden return on the loan? The ambiguity grew more dangerous as commercial life grew more complex. Merchants did not hold money idly: it stood behind trade, inventory, shipping, bills of exchange, investment. A delay in repayment might genuinely disrupt commercial activity; a loan might genuinely foreclose another opportunity. The boundary between actual loss and foregone profit became harder to police, and interesse began to stretch — compensation for damage sliding toward payment for lost gain, restitution beginning to resemble return. This stretching is one of the central historical dramas of the extrinsic titles. The language of interesse opened a legitimate moral space between absolute gratuitousness and forbidden usury, and that same space could expand until it threatened the whole doctrine. If every lender may claim he is "without his money," and being without money always makes him worse off, then interesse becomes indistinguishable from ordinary interest, and the old word has been transformed into the very thing the tradition resisted.

So the history of interesse deserves careful handling. It is not a primitive form of interest, and not a meaningless legal fiction. It is a morally serious attempt to measure damage without blessing extraction — an insistence that justice may require more than the return of principal while every such payment stays tied to something outside the loan itself. The modern world has largely lost the distinction. We assume money is automatically productive, time automatically monetizable, liquidity automatically priced, capital naturally entitled to return. The scholastic tradition asks us to slow down and check whether the additional payment is truly compensation, or whether the language of compensation is hiding a yield. For non-extractive finance the discipline is immediately practical: a fund may need to recover administrative costs, be compensated for real losses, structure penalties for genuine default, account for the risk that capital will not return — and it must not let every burden, inconvenience, or theoretical opportunity convert automatically into a claim on the borrower. Interesse is a discipline of moral accounting. It asks not whether money has been paid but what the money corresponds to: a real loss? a documented injury? a causal connection? a proportionate amount? a payment that restores equality, or one that produces gain from another's dependence? It is one of the key bridges between the older usury doctrine and the titles to come, holding open the space in which compensation can be distinguished from extraction — gratuitous lending without injustice toward the lender, justice toward the lender without domination over the borrower. The word "interest" has forgotten this moral tension. Interesse still remembers it.

sections.title: 9.3 The four-title family
paragraphs: The extrinsic titles did not all emerge at once, did not develop with equal clarity, were not equally accepted, and did not pose the same moral dangers. Together they form a family of arguments about when a payment above principal might be justified without becoming usury. The family has four central members — poena conventionalis (or morae poena), damnum emergens, lucrum cessans, and periculum sortis — and around them stand several adjacent titles: compensation for labor, reimbursement of expenses, the absence of money, and insurance-like risk-bearing. Some of the adjacent titles stayed narrow. Others became dangerous precisely because they approached the logic of ordinary interest.

The first title is poena conventionalis, called morae poena when the emphasis falls on delay: the agreed penalty for failure to repay or perform on time. Its moral intuition is straightforward — if the borrower wrongfully fails to repay when repayment is due, and the failure causes injury or violates a just contractual obligation, he may owe something beyond the principal, not as the price of the loan but as a penalty for breach. And the title is immediately dangerous, because a late penalty is the easiest disguise interest has ever worn. If the repayment date is unrealistic, if the penalty is excessive, if default is expected, if the lender quietly prefers late repayment because the penalty is profitable, then the "penalty" is no longer penal; it is a hidden yield. The scholastic concern ran past the text of the contract to the intention, the incentive structure, and the practical likelihood of default. A penalty the borrower cannot realistically avoid is not morally different from interest just because the contract calls it a penalty.

The second is damnum emergens, actual loss incurred — the cleanest and least controversial of the majors. If the lender suffers a real loss because of the loan or the borrower's failure to repay, he may be compensated: the claim is restorative, repairing damage rather than enriching the lender for having lent. Aquinas gives the classic formulation in allowing compensation for a loss incurred while refusing to let the lender sell a profit not yet possessed. The line is subtle and crucial. A lender made worse off in a real, demonstrable way may be restored in justice; a lender who says "I might have made money elsewhere" is speculating. Actual loss belongs to restitution; hoped-for gain belongs to speculation. This made damnum emergens the central safe title — covering legal costs, collection expenses, liquidity harm, damage caused by delay, and other concrete losses — though even here abuse remained available. Losses could be invented, exaggerated, double-counted, or priced in advance as though every loan automatically generated compensable damage. The title held only where the loss was actual, causally connected, documented or reasonably demonstrable, and proportionate.

The third is lucrum cessans, profit foregone — the most morally unstable of the traditional titles, standing at the boundary between compensation and opportunity cost. It claims compensation not for something already lost but for a gain that would have been made had the money not been lent. The intuitive case is the merchant whose capital was already committed to a concrete and profitable use: lend that capital away, and a determinate commercial opportunity is missed — not abstract growth but a particular gain reasonably expected from a specific alternative use. The danger is equally intuitive. Generalize lucrum cessans, and every lender can claim that money could always have been invested somewhere; every loan then carries an opportunity cost, every opportunity cost becomes compensable, and the usury prohibition collapses into the modern assumption that capital is always entitled to a market return. This is why Aquinas resisted it as a loan title — compensation for loss incurred, yes; sale of a profit not yet possessed, no — and why later theologians who reopened the question, drawing on his own principles of restitution and special value to the owner, never escaped the tension. Lucrum cessans can be morally plausible where the foregone gain is concrete, intended, licit, causally interrupted, and reasonably certain. It becomes usurious as abstract opportunity cost — the metaphysical claim that money, because it could have grown elsewhere, deserves growth here.

The fourth, periculum sortis, risk of principal, is structurally different from the other three. Poena concerns delay or breach; damnum, actual loss; lucrum, foregone gain — all events around the loan. Periculum sortis concerns the risk structure of the transaction itself, and its central question is: who bears the downside? A lender whose principal is guaranteed, secured, senior, collateralized, and enforceable regardless of the borrower's fortunes has not placed capital at risk in the strong moral sense; the borrower bears the burden of repayment, and the lender bears at most ordinary default risk, itself minimized through legal enforcement. If ordinary default risk automatically justified surplus, nearly every loan would be interest-bearing by another name. But where capital is genuinely exposed to loss, the moral structure changes and the lender begins to resemble a partner — which is why Aquinas's distinction between mutuum and societas matters so much here. In partnership, capital remains at risk; its provider may share profits because he shares losses; the return arises from participation in a common venture, not from a fixed claim against the borrower. Periculum sortis therefore foreshadows the whole modern debate about debt, equity, venture capital, liquidation preferences, guarantees, and mission-locked capital. The issue is never what an instrument is called but whether downside is genuinely shared or shifted. An equity investor armed with strong control rights, senior claims, preferred returns, liquidation preferences, redemption rights, and exit pressure may in practice offload much of the risk onto founders, workers, and mission — formally risk-bearing, structurally extractive. A patient, subordinated, loss-absorbing capital provider may hold the stronger moral claim to upside precisely because it truly accepts downside.

Around the four majors stand the adjacent categories. Stipendium laboris, compensation for labor: lending, investing, administering funds, managing pledges, conducting due diligence, keeping accounts, and doing stewardship work all take human effort, and the tradition recognized — especially around the Monti di Pietà — that charitable credit institutions could not survive if forbidden to recover the real costs of operation. Paying clerks, maintaining buildings, safeguarding pledges, and keeping books is not charging interest; but the charge had to stay tied to actual labor and institutional necessity. Sustainability was permitted; extraction was not. Reimbursement of expenses overlaps damnum emergens but has its own practical life: postage, notarial fees, appraisal, accounting, enforcement, administration — recoverable when real and proportionate, and dangerous the moment cost recovery becomes a revenue stream undisciplined by accounting and governance. More dangerous still is carentia pecuniae, the lack or absence of money: a claimed compensation for being deprived of one's money for a term, cousin to the modern liquidity premium, the lock-up fee, the time value of money — and for exactly that reason it stands at the edge of ordinary interest. If the mere absence of money is compensable, the passage of time itself has been monetized: the lender is paid not for a specific loss, a concrete foregone gain, or genuine risk, but for not having the money. Finally there are the insurance-like claims connected to capital risk — one party assuming the danger that another's capital may be lost, or a claim discounted because repayment is uncertain — which make risk itself an object of valuation. They matter greatly in the later development of periculum sortis, and they carry its characteristic danger: risk language justifying return even where the risk has been shifted away from the one collecting it.

Set side by side, the family forms a spectrum. At one end stands damnum emergens, the most concrete and restorative title; close to it, expenses and labor, where amounts can be documented and tied to real cost. Poena conventionalis is also concrete but more dangerous, because penalties can be engineered. Lucrum cessans moves toward the future — not what was lost from possession but what was not gained. Periculum sortis moves from event to structure — not what happened after the loan but how the capital itself was exposed. And carentia pecuniae stands near the edge, where the absence of money threatens to become payment for time itself. The spectrum displays both the brilliance and the danger of the scholastic achievement. The brilliance is the refusal of crude categories: the tradition never said "all surplus is evil" or "all contracted return is legitimate," but asked what kind of surplus was claimed, what burden justified it, and whether the burden was real. The danger lives in the same subtlety, because every distinction can become a loophole and every title a pretext. A penalty becomes expected revenue; a loss becomes fictional; a foregone profit becomes abstract opportunity cost; a risk premium becomes return without downside; administrative cost becomes fee extraction; liquidity compensation becomes the time value of money; partnership becomes disguised domination. The four-title family must therefore always be read together with the anti-abuse logic that surrounds it. The titles are moral tests: is the payment above principal tied to reality — real delay, real loss, real foregone gain, real risk, real labor, real expense — and has that reality been exaggerated, manipulated, or generalized until it is ordinary interest in disguise?

For modern non-extractive finance, the family supplies a remarkably precise diagnostic vocabulary. Late fees are tested as poena. Administrative charges are tested as labor or expense. Reimbursements are tested as damnum emergens. Claims about alternative deployment are tested as lucrum cessans. Returns to capital are tested as periculum sortis. Lock-up premiums are tested against the danger of carentia pecuniae. And every one of them is tested against the deeper question: does this payment restore, sustain, and share risk — or does it extract? The four titles do not solve the problem automatically. They teach us how to see it.

sections.title: 9.4 The moral test
paragraphs: The doctrine of the extrinsic titles finally comes down to a test — not a slogan, not a loophole, not a mechanical checklist that blesses a contract because the correct Latin word has been attached to it. The test asks whether the surplus payment is tied to something real outside the mutuum, or is merely payment for the use of money; whether the lender is being restored, compensated, reimbursed, or genuinely rewarded for shared risk, or is simply using the borrower's need as an opportunity for guaranteed gain. It does not begin by asking whether the contract is legal, whether both parties signed, whether the market would bear the price, or even whether a payment above principal appears in the text. It begins with the deeper question: what is this payment for?

Is the surplus tied to actual loss? Then the title may be damnum emergens — and the loss must be real, not imagined, inflated, presumed, or folded into a general return expectation. It must be caused by the loan, by the borrower's wrongful delay, or by some concrete burden the lender would not otherwise have borne. A real legal cost may be reimbursed; a real administrative expense recovered; a real liquidity harm compensated; a real loss from missed repayment restored. The title fails where the alleged loss is a story told after the fact to justify a desired yield. It fails where the same loss is counted twice — once in fees, once in penalties, once in pricing, once again in return expectations. It fails where a fictional damage claim is built into every loan regardless of what occurs. Damnum emergens is restorative only when it corresponds to actual damage; otherwise it is a respectable name for extraction.

Is the surplus tied to culpable delay? Then the title may be poena conventionalis or morae poena. The borrower promised repayment at a certain time and failed; the failure may have caused injury, forced recovery efforts, missed obligations, real damage; and a penalty may serve a legitimate disciplinary function, marking the seriousness of the obligation and discouraging negligent delay. But the penalty is morally dangerous because it is so easy to engineer. If the due date is unrealistic, if the borrower is likely to miss it, if the penalty is excessive, if the lender expects default or prefers it because the penalty pays, the penalty has ceased to be penal and become a hidden price of time. The diagnostic question is simple and searching: would the lender genuinely prefer repayment on time? If yes, the penalty may still be a penalty. If no, it is suspect — a lender who hopes for default is not protecting justice but designing the borrower's failure into his revenue model.

Is the surplus tied to concrete foregone gain? Then the title may be lucrum cessans, the most delicate of the tests, because it reaches past actual loss into future possibility. A merchant may have had money already committed to a specific transaction; a lender may have been deprived of a concrete, licit, intended, reasonably certain profit because the money was lent instead. The claim is never that "money could have earned something somewhere." The claim is that this money, in this person's hands, under these circumstances, was ordered toward a determinate use that the loan interrupted. The title must be handled narrowly: the foregone gain concrete, more than vague possibility, causally connected to the loan, valued reasonably, not demanded in advance in a way that converts the loan into ordinary interest, and never universalized into the doctrine that every lender deserves compensation because every unit of capital has an opportunity cost. Here the modern world is most vulnerable, because contemporary finance holds opportunity cost as an axiom — capital has alternative uses, so the cost of capital here is the return foregone there. That logic is powerful in financial modeling and morally explosive if imported into usury doctrine without discipline, because it turns lucrum cessans from a narrow claim about interrupted concrete gain into a universal metaphysics of money: money, because it could have grown, ought to grow. The scholastic test resists. It asks not whether the lender can imagine a better use, but whether there was a real, licit, intended, reasonably determinate use that the loan actually caused to cease. Without that discipline, lucrum cessans becomes the doorway through which modern interest walks into the house.

Is the surplus tied to genuine risk of capital? Then the title may be periculum sortis, and the question is not primarily what happened after the loan but what structure the parties created at the beginning. Did the capital provider truly bear the risk of losing the principal, or did the contract shift the downside to the borrower while preserving the upside for the lender? Careful attention to legal and economic reality is everything here. It is not enough that every lender faces some chance of nonpayment; ordinary default risk cannot be allowed to swallow the prohibition. The stronger question is whether the capital is truly loss-absorbing. Is repayment guaranteed? Is there collateral? Is the claim senior? Can the lender enforce against unrelated borrower assets? Are there personal guarantees? Does the borrower still owe even if the project fails? Is the lender insulated while the borrower, the workers, the suppliers, or the mission absorb the damage? If the lender collects a return for risk while the borrower bears the meaningful downside, the title is false — risk has been named but not shared, and the contract speaks the language of periculum sortis while preserving the structure of domination. But if capital can actually be lost, if the investor is subordinated, if repayment depends on the success of the common enterprise, if upside and downside are paired, then the moral form is moving away from mutuum toward partnership, and the surplus may be participation rather than extraction.

Or is the surplus merely payment for the use of money? This is the question underneath all the others. Strip away the labels, remove the Latin, ignore the spreadsheet category, and ask what remains. If the surplus remains when there is no actual loss, no culpable delay, no concrete foregone gain, no genuine risk, no labor, no expense, and no partnership, then it is not extrinsic. It is intrinsic — payment for the loan itself, the price of having money over time — and in the classical doctrine, that is usury.

This is how the extrinsic titles become a moral diagnostic system: they force finance to name the reason for surplus and refuse to let "return" stay morally vague. The questions are as current as any term sheet. A late fee must be tested — proportionate response to real delay, or profit center? A management fee — real labor and stewardship, or extraction regardless of service? A reimbursement — actual cost recovery, or disguised margin? A preferred return — compensation for genuine risk, or capital's guaranteed growth before anyone else is made whole? A liquidation preference — real loss-bearing capital, or downside shifted onto founders, workers, and mission while upside is preserved? A risk premium — who actually bears the risk? An opportunity-cost claim — what concrete alternative was truly lost? A lock-up premium — actual deprivation, or time monetized? The discipline is demanding because it refuses both naive condemnation and naive permission: it does not say every surplus is unjust, or every contractual return justified. It asks for moral accounting, for proof, for proportionality, for a contract whose form corresponds to the reality of the burden.

At the deepest level, the test asks whether finance restores equality or exploits inequality. Compensation restores equality; reimbursement restores it; proportionate penalties may restore the order broken by culpable delay; risk-sharing may create just participation in common gain; labor compensation may sustain the institution that serves the borrower. These forms of surplus can serve life. Extraction does something else: it begins from inequality and deepens it. It sees borrower need as opportunity, uses time as leverage, designs penalties as revenue, invokes risk while shifting loss to others, turns dependence into yield, and lets capital grow because another person or community is vulnerable. The extrinsic titles were developed to prevent exactly this confusion — to make it possible to say that not all surplus is usury while refusing to let usury hide inside every surplus. What matters is never only the amount repaid but the moral structure of the claim. Does the payment correspond to a real burden external to the mutuum? Is the burden documented, proportionate, and counted once? Does the lender prefer justice to default, repayment to penalty, partnership to domination? Does the contract share risk, or merely price risk while shifting it? Does the surplus restore, sustain, participate — or extract? This is the moral test. Everything that follows in the history of the extrinsic titles is an attempt to answer it.

chapters.part: Part IV — The Extrinsic Titles: Four Ways Surplus Might Be Restorative Rather Than Extractive

chapters.title: Legal Penalty Before Theological Title
chapters.label: Chapter 10

sections.title: 10.1 Roman and canon-law penalty clauses
paragraphs: The title of poena conventionalis began not as a theological doctrine but as a legal device. Long before scholastic theologians asked whether a penalty clause could become a disguised form of usury, jurists already knew that contracts needed ways to secure performance. A promise without consequence is a fragile thing: debtors delay, sellers fail to deliver, contractors abandon the work half-finished, parties perform badly, late, or not at all. The law, long acquainted with human nature, developed devices by which parties could agree in advance on what would happen if an obligation was breached.

A penalty clause, at first sight, has nothing to do with lending. It is a payment attached to non-performance: one party promises to do something, and if he does not do it, he owes a penalty. The penalty may discipline the will, encourage performance, simplify the proof of damages, or compensate the injured party for the burden of breach. In ordinary legal terms, it gives teeth to the promise.

The theological problem arises only when this device is attached to a loan. A borrower who receives money under a mutuum has one primary obligation: to return the equivalent principal at the agreed time. The lender may not demand surplus from the loan itself. But what if the contract says, "If you do not repay on the appointed day, you must pay an additional sum"? Is that additional sum usury, or a legitimate penalty for breach?

The question is anything but artificial, because repayment dates matter in practical life. A lender who expects repayment on a certain day may have obligations of his own; if repayment is delayed, he may suffer inconvenience, loss, or risk. Even where no precise loss can be shown, the borrower's wrongful delay may still be a real violation of justice — he is retaining what is owed, preventing the creditor from receiving what should already have been restored. A penalty clause therefore appears to have a perfectly legitimate function: it protects the due date and discourages wrongful delay.

Yet the same device can easily become a fraud on the usury prohibition. A lender may formally lend gratuitously while building an expected surplus into the contract through the penalty clause. The loan says one thing; the economic structure does another. The lender does not openly charge interest — he creates conditions under which the borrower is likely to default, then collects the penalty. In form the surplus is conditional, received because of breach; in substance it is expected, received because time has passed. This is why poena conventionalis becomes one of the earliest and most revealing tests of the scholastic anti-usury tradition. It forces theologians and canonists to distinguish law from moral reality. The contract may say "penalty" — but does the penalty truly punish culpable delay, or does it merely rename interest?

Roman law provided the basic architecture. Obligations could be strengthened through stipulations: a debtor could promise a sum in case of non-performance, and penalties could be attached to delay, to breach, or to failure to fulfill an agreed act. In that world the penalty was not primarily a usury device at all; it was a general contractual instrument. Canon law inherited this legal imagination and placed it under a theological discipline. The Church did not merely ask whether the penalty was valid in civil law. It asked whether the penalty was compatible with justice, charity, and the prohibition of usury — for a legal form could be valid and still morally corrupt, a clause enforceable and still a pretext for taking what ought not to be taken. The canon-law concern is captured in the phrase poena nec in fraudem — a penalty not in fraud of the usury prohibition. A penalty might be permitted when truly penal and not fraudulent, tolerated when attached to genuine breach and not used to sell time; it could secure repayment, but it could not become a cloak for yield.

To see the distinction, imagine two loans. In the first, the borrower promises to repay on the first of June. The date is realistic, the borrower has reason to expect funds, the penalty for late repayment is modest and proportionate, and the lender wants the money repaid on time. If the borrower fails without excuse, the penalty marks the wrongfulness of the delay and compensates the lender for the disruption. That penalty is at least morally intelligible: it is not demanded from the loan as such; it arises from breach. In the second, the borrower promises to repay on the first of June, but everyone knows this is almost impossible. The due date is too short, the borrower is already desperate, and the penalty is large. The lender does not expect timely repayment — indeed he prefers default, because default is what makes the transaction profitable. That is no longer truly a penalty; it is interest wearing a legal mask.

This is why the penalty title required more than formal analysis. It required attention to intention, proportionality, expectation, and practical structure: whether the borrower was realistically able to avoid the penalty, whether the penalty was proportionate to the harm, whether the creditor was trying to secure performance or to generate revenue, whether the delay was accidental, culpable, engineered, or expected — whether, in short, the clause restored justice or created a business model around default.

The move from Roman penalty clause to canon-law moral title therefore marks a real development. The jurist sees a clause attached to breach, and asks whether the parties agreed and whether the obligation can be enforced. The theologian asks whether the clause truthfully corresponds to breach, whether enforcement would become extraction, and whether the agreement respects the moral form of the mutuum. This does not mean canon law rejected penalty clauses; it means canon law subjected them to anti-fraud scrutiny. A penalty could be valid when it was not used to evade the usury prohibition, acceptable when the lender did not profit from the loan itself but was protected against wrongful non-performance — licit, in short, when it remained conditional, proportionate, and genuinely unwanted.

That last condition is the crucial one. A true penalty is something the creditor would rather not collect. It exists to prevent breach, not to make breach desirable; the lender wants repayment, not default. This is what makes the penalty morally different from ordinary interest: the lender's desired outcome is not the passage of time with surplus but the timely restoration of principal. Here is the seed of the later scholastic diagnostic — would the lender rather be repaid on time? If yes, the clause may still be a genuine penalty. If no, it has probably crossed the line: discipline has turned into extraction, and the borrower's failure has been transformed into the lender's profit.

The early legal history of poena conventionalis thus displays the pattern that will recur throughout the extrinsic titles. A real moral claim exists. Borrowers should not be free to delay repayment without consequence; lenders should not necessarily bear every harm caused by another's breach; contracts need discipline, institutions need enforceability, and justice requires that obligations be taken seriously. But every real moral claim can be imitated by a false one. The title can be named while the substance is absent; the penalty can be written into the contract while the lender's real intention is profit from time; the legal device can begin as a tool of order and end as a mechanism of domination. The penalty clause becomes the first great laboratory of extrinsic-title reasoning because it teaches that the usury question can never be answered by the presence or absence of surplus alone. One must ask what the surplus is attached to — the mutuum itself, or a wrongful breach; a price of time, or a discipline of justice; the protection of repayment, or the monetizing of failure. In that distinction the whole moral drama of poena conventionalis begins.

sections.title: 10.2 The usury problem
paragraphs: The penalty clause becomes morally dangerous the moment it is attached to a loan. In itself, a penalty for non-performance is not difficult to understand. If a person promises to deliver goods and fails to deliver them, a penalty may be owed; if a builder misses an agreed completion date without excuse, a penalty may be owed; if a merchant does not perform his contract, a penalty may secure the seriousness of the obligation. In every case the penalty is attached to breach, not to the mere passage of time.

But the mutuum is different. In a loan for consumption the borrower receives ownership of the money or other fungible good and promises to return the equivalent principal. The loan itself is formally gratuitous: the lender's intrinsic claim is the return of the tantundem — the same amount of the same kind and quality — and he may not demand a surplus simply because the borrower has had the use of the money. Hence the problem. If the loan must be gratuitous, can the lender attach a penalty to late repayment at all, or does the penalty become a way of doing indirectly what cannot be done directly?

The problem is anything but theoretical, because in practice the difference between a penalty and interest can wear very thin. A contract may state that no interest is charged; it may even proclaim itself charitable, friendly, or gratuitous — and then include a clause providing that if repayment is not made on the appointed day, the borrower owes an additional amount. If the repayment date is realistic, the penalty moderate, and default genuinely unwanted, the clause may remain penal. If the date is unrealistic, the penalty large, and default expected, the transaction begins to look like a loan at interest. The form is gratuitous; the substance is not. This is the usury problem of poena conventionalis. The lender can hide yield inside delay, preserving the appearance of a free loan while designing the contract so that the borrower is likely to owe more than principal. He does not sell the use of money openly; he sells it through the structure of default.

The danger is especially acute because a late penalty appears so morally defensible. The borrower has promised to repay; repayment dates matter; delay can injure the lender; a borrower should not be allowed to retain what is owed indefinitely. A penalty can therefore seem not only lawful but just — and precisely for that reason it can become an unusually effective disguise for usury, borrowing the language of justice while quietly monetizing time.

The scholastic tradition therefore had to distinguish two things that can look identical on parchment: a true penalty and a disguised price of time. A true penalty is conditional upon wrongful delay, avoidable by timely repayment, and proportionate to the seriousness of the breach or the harm caused. It exists to secure performance — the lender would rather receive the principal on time — and it is not the expected profit of the transaction but a remedy for failure. A disguised price of time is formally attached to delay but materially expected as part of the bargain. The lender may set a repayment date the borrower is unlikely to meet, fix the penalty so high that it functions like interest, renew or roll over the debt again and again, build the penalty into his revenue expectations, and end by making more from delay than from timely repayment. Such a penalty no longer disciplines breach; it makes breach profitable.

The question, then, is never simply whether a penalty clause exists, but whether the penalty is in fraudem usurae — in fraud of the usury prohibition. A contract can be fraudulent in this moral sense even when no one lies about the words. The clause may be written plainly, the borrower may sign it, the due date may be stated and the penalty specified — and the transaction can still be morally deceptive if the clause is structured to generate a predictable surplus from the loan. The deception lies not in the grammar of the contract but in the moral form of the arrangement. This is why the tradition grew so attentive to expectation: whether the lender expected repayment on time, whether the borrower had a reasonable path to repay, whether the penalty was an exceptional consequence of breach or the normal way the lender earned his money — whether delay was treated as a wrong to be avoided or as a profitable event to be anticipated.

Here the penalty title reveals something deeper about usury itself. Usury is not merely excessive pricing, the charging of too much; it is a disorder in the relationship between creditor and debtor. The creditor's gain becomes linked to the debtor's weakness — the borrower's delay, inability, or distress becomes the occasion of the lender's profit — and a relationship that should be ordered toward restoration becomes a relationship ordered toward extraction. The penalty clause can intensify this disorder. In an ordinary interest-bearing loan, the lender profits as time passes. In a disguised penalty loan, the lender profits when the borrower fails. That may be even more morally troubling, for the lender now has an incentive to prefer disorder over order, breach over performance, default over repayment: the contract appears to punish failure while the lender's economic interest is aligned with failure. This incentive problem later becomes central to the scholastic analysis. A true penalty is supposed to deter default; if the lender wants the penalty, the clause has become perverse. It no longer serves justice; it creates a standing temptation against justice, encouraging the lender to design, tolerate, or even desire the borrower's failure.

This is why a penalty cannot be judged by amount alone. A small penalty may be unjust if it is systematically expected from vulnerable borrowers; a larger penalty may be defensible if it is genuinely exceptional, proportionate to real damage, and attached to culpable breach in a commercial setting where timely performance matters. The moral question is not only arithmetic but structural. The same is true of consent. Modern contract thinking often leans heavily on consent: the borrower agreed, therefore the term is legitimate. Scholastic moral theology is more suspicious. A desperate borrower may agree to a penalty because he has no better option; a poor borrower may accept unrealistic terms because immediate need overwhelms future prudence; and a lender may exploit that vulnerability while preserving every appearance of voluntary agreement. Consent alone does not purify a clause that functions as extraction.

This matters because the biblical and theological background of the usury prohibition is concerned above all with the vulnerable borrower. The poor neighbor of Exodus, Leviticus, and Deuteronomy is not an abstract market actor freely optimizing his capital structure. He is a person under pressure, who may need food, seed, clothing, or relief. To lend to such a person and then profit from his inability to repay on time is to violate the moral world that gave birth to the doctrine.

The penalty problem therefore forces a double vigilance. On one side, the tradition must not make lending impossible by forbidding every consequence for delay. A borrower who wrongfully withholds repayment can harm the lender; justice does not require the creditor to absorb every injury; obligations must be real, timely repayment may genuinely matter, and a morally serious credit system needs discipline. On the other side, the tradition must not allow discipline to become domination. The creditor must not convert enforceability into profit from distress; the penalty must not become a predictable yield; the due date must not be a trap; the borrower's failure must not become the lender's business model.

This is the core usury problem of poena conventionalis: the same clause can either protect justice or corrupt it. It protects justice when it preserves the seriousness of repayment, deters culpable delay, compensates real harm, and remains something the lender would rather not collect. It corrupts justice when it monetizes time, engineers default, exploits vulnerability, or lets the lender gain from the borrower's failure. The title is therefore morally possible but never morally automatic. A penalty clause does not become licit merely because it is conditional, or agreed, or enforced by civil law. It becomes licit only if it remains truly external to the loan itself — connected to breach, harm, or culpable delay, and not to the mere fact that money has been lent over time.

This is why poena conventionalis is an ideal starting point for the study of the extrinsic titles: it shows the pattern in its simplest form. A real external title may exist, and it may justify payment above principal; but the same title may be simulated, and the moral task is to distinguish the title from the pretext. The old theologians were not naive about any of this. They knew that contracts can tell lies without using false words — a loan calling itself gratuitous while expecting surplus, a penalty calling itself punishment while functioning as yield, a borrower appearing free while trapped by need, a lender claiming justice while hoping for default. The usury problem begins precisely there: when the law says "penalty," but the moral substance says "interest."

sections.title: 10.3 The core distinction
paragraphs: The entire moral analysis of poena conventionalis turns on one distinction: true penalty versus disguised price of time. A true penalty is attached to a wrong; a disguised price of time is attached to the loan. The first responds to breach and protects the order of justice; the second monetizes duration and recreates usury under another name.

The distinction sounds simple. In practice it is difficult, because the same words can describe both arrangements: the same contract can use the language of penalty, the same borrower can promise repayment by a stated date, the same clause can provide that an additional sum is owed if repayment is late. On the surface the forms look alike. The moral difference lies beneath the surface, in the relation between the payment and the reality it claims to address.

A true poena conventionalis is an agreed penalty. The parties stipulate in advance that if an obligation is not fulfilled, a certain consequence will follow — in the context of a loan, that a borrower who does not repay at the appointed time may owe an additional sum. The penalty is conditional, avoidable, attached to non-performance, and never the desired outcome of the contract. A morae poena is a penalty for delay, and its moral focus is more specific: the borrower is late; he retains what should have been repaid and prevents the lender from receiving what is due; if the delay is culpable and causes harm or disorder, the penalty may mark the wrongfulness of that delay.

Both forms must satisfy the deeper requirement: poena nec in fraudem, a penalty not in fraud of the usury prohibition — not a device for taking surplus from the loan while pretending to punish breach, not an indirect way of selling time, not a means of doing secretly what the tradition forbids openly. The phrase is the key to the whole analysis. It shows that the medieval question was never merely whether a penalty clause existed, but whether the penalty was being used honestly. A clause can be legally explicit and morally fraudulent; it can say exactly what will happen and still be structured as a trap; it can be voluntarily accepted and still exploit necessity; it can be enforceable and still violate the moral form of gratuitous lending.

A penalty is not fraudulent when it preserves the lender's legitimate interest in timely repayment. The date is reasonable, the borrower has a real possibility of paying on time, the amount is proportionate, the lender prefers performance to breach, and the clause serves to deter delay rather than to make delay profitable. If the borrower pays on time, the lender is satisfied: the transaction has achieved its intended end. A penalty becomes fraudulent when those conditions are reversed — the due date too short, the borrower unlikely to meet it, the penalty disproportionate, the lender expecting to collect it, the transaction profitable only or chiefly because of default. If the borrower pays on time, the lender is disappointed. Breach is then no longer the failure of the contract but its hidden success.

This is the moral inversion the tradition fears. A true penalty makes default undesirable; a usurious penalty makes default desirable. The one protects repayment and is ordered toward restoration; the other feeds on non-repayment and is ordered toward extraction. The diagnostic question therefore becomes: what outcome does the lender actually want? If he wants the principal back on time, the penalty may still be penal. If he wants the borrower to be late, the penalty has become morally corrupt. The lender's desire reveals the structure of the transaction — the clause may speak of justice while the lender's incentive is aligned with disorder.

This is why Duns Scotus's later concern about the lender who prefers late repayment is so important. It shifts attention from contract wording to economic incentive: a contract may be formally clean and still morally disordered if it rewards the lender when the borrower fails. The question is not only "What does the clause say?" It is also "What behavior does the clause encourage?" — timely repayment, or profit from delay?

The same principle applies to proportionality. A penalty must be proportionate to the harm or to the seriousness of the breach. If it is modest, bounded, and connected to real disruption, it can plausibly function as discipline; if it is excessive, compounding, or unrelated to actual damage, it begins to look like yield. The more a penalty exceeds the burden it claims to address, the more likely that the burden is merely a pretext. Avoidability is equally important. A true penalty must be something the borrower can realistically avoid. If he is given a repayment date that no prudent person expects him to meet, the penalty is built into the transaction from the beginning — no longer the consequence of an unexpected breach but the planned price of the loan. The borrower's "default" has been manufactured by the structure of the contract.

This is especially dangerous in lending to the poor. A desperate borrower may accept terms that already contain his failure. He may need grain, food, rent money, medicine, or urgent relief; he may agree to repay quickly because refusal would leave him worse off immediately, even though repayment is unlikely. In such a setting a penalty clause can become a mechanism by which vulnerability is converted into revenue. The borrower signs the contract, but the lender designs the impossibility.

The scholastic insistence on anti-fraud analysis is therefore not technical fussiness but pastoral realism. It recognizes that creditor power can hide inside legal form, that the poor can consent to their own exploitation when need is severe, that a clause can be named as penalty while functioning as interest — and it refuses to let the vocabulary of contract override the reality of domination. At the same time, the tradition does not deny that culpable delay matters. Borrowers are moral agents too: they can act unjustly, wrongfully withhold repayment, and impose costs on others. A doctrine that forbade every penalty would risk making lending fragile, especially in commercial contexts where timing matters and non-performance can injure others. The point is not to make debtors immune from responsibility but to keep responsibility from being weaponized by creditors.

The core distinction can thus be stated as a set of paired tests. A true penalty is exceptional, avoidable, proportionate, and attached to culpable delay; disguised interest is expected, built in, calibrated for yield, and attached to time. A true penalty protects the lender from harm and is something he would rather not collect; disguised interest uses the borrower's delay as profit and is something he hopes to collect.

The paired tests also clarify why poena conventionalis belongs among the extrinsic titles at all. The penalty is licit only if the reason for surplus is extrinsic to the mutuum: the lender receives more not because money was lent but because a separate wrong has occurred — failure to repay as promised, wrongful retention of what is owed, damage caused by delay. The surplus is tied to breach, not to lending. But once the penalty is expected, the extrinsic character disappears. The penalty becomes part of the price of the loan; it is no longer outside the mutuum but folded into it, and what was supposed to be an external title has become an intrinsic return. This is the pattern that will reappear in every later title. Damnum emergens is legitimate when it restores real loss, abusive when the loss is fictional. Lucrum cessans is plausible when a concrete gain is truly foregone, abusive when every lender invokes abstract opportunity cost. Periculum sortis is just when capital is genuinely at risk, abusive when the lender prices risk while shifting the downside to the borrower. In each case the title is morally valid only when it corresponds to reality; when it becomes a formula for yield, it collapses into usury.

The practical consequences are clear. A morally serious penalty clause should have a realistic due date and room for genuine hardship. It should be proportionate to actual or reasonably anticipated harm, should not compound without limit, should not be designed around expected default, should not be booked internally as ordinary revenue, and should not be combined with other fees in a way that double-counts the same injury. Above all, it should be structured so that timely repayment is the lender's preferred outcome — which is perhaps the simplest moral test of all. If repayment on time is good news for the lender, the penalty may still be a penalty. If repayment on time is bad news, because the lender has lost his chance to collect the surplus, the penalty has betrayed itself.

The phrase poena nec in fraudem therefore remains remarkably contemporary. Modern contracts are filled with late fees, default interest, acceleration clauses, penalty charges, cure periods, grace periods, restructuring fees, and enforcement costs. Some of these are legitimate tools of order; others are profit centers built around borrower distress. The old distinction still asks the right question: is this clause here to secure performance, or to monetize failure? A non-extractive approach to finance must recover exactly this discipline.
