It cannot simply abolish every consequence for late payment — that would be impractical and sometimes unjust — but neither can it allow default to become a business model. Penalties, if they exist at all, must be designed as reluctant instruments of justice rather than desired sources of return.

The true penalty says: repay on time, and all is well. The disguised price of time says: fail to repay, and the lender profits. Between those two sentences lies the moral boundary of poena conventionalis.

chapters.part: Part V — Poena Conventionalis / Morae Poena: Penalty for Delay

chapters.title: Early Theological and Canonical Development
chapters.label: Chapter 11

sections.title: 11.1 Robert of Courçon
paragraphs: Robert of Courçon stands near the beginning of the scholastic effort to distinguish a true penalty from a disguised form of usury. He was an Englishman, born about 1160 in Derbyshire, possibly at Kedleston, who crossed to Paris to study under Peter the Chanter — the master whose circle made a specialty of asking what Christian morality actually required in the marketplace — and stayed to teach theology himself. His Summa treats usury at length and with feeling. Rome noticed: cardinal in 1212, papal legate the next year preaching the crusade, and in 1215 author of the University of Paris's first great statutes — the ones that renewed the ban on teaching Aristotle's natural philosophy. He died in February 1219 at the siege of Damietta, in the Nile delta, on the Fifth Crusade — about as far from a Paris lecture hall as a theologian could get. He belongs to the world before the great syntheses — before Aquinas, before the later Salamanca refinements, before the extrinsic titles hardened into the familiar categories of the manuals — and precisely for that reason he is important. In him we see the problem while it is still raw.

The question does not yet come wrapped in the apparatus of later moral theology; it arises where Robert lived, in penitential judgment, pastoral discipline, and the early university. Christians are lending; borrowers are failing to repay; creditors are attaching penalties to contracts to secure performance. Some of those penalties may be legitimate, and others may be usury in disguise. The problem is practical before it is systematic: what should confessors, theologians, and canonists say when a lender has received more than principal because a borrower failed to repay on time?

Robert's treatment is marked by suspicion. He does not bless the penalty clause as a clean solution; he sees what it can become — a way of profiting from a loan while avoiding the appearance of usury. A creditor may say, "I did not charge interest. I only collected the penalty that the borrower agreed to pay if he repaid late." For Robert the explanation is not enough: the moral theologian must ask whether the penalty was genuine or expected from the beginning as the lender's gain. The central intuition of the whole later doctrine is already present here — the penalty must not become the lender's profit from the loan. If the lender uses the clause to obtain a surplus he could not licitly demand as interest, the penalty is morally corrupt: the name of the payment does not purify the transaction, and a usurious payment does not cease to be usurious because it is collected after default rather than during the term.

His caution also reflects the biblical and pastoral atmosphere surrounding usury. Lending to those in need had long been treated as an arena of spiritual danger: the lender was not merely a market actor but a Christian dealing with a neighbor, often a vulnerable one, and to lend so that the borrower's distress becomes the occasion of gain violates the moral grammar of charity. A penalty clause can intensify the danger precisely because it appears to punish fault while possibly exploiting weakness. This is why Robert does not simply ask whether the borrower consented: consent is morally relevant but not decisive. A borrower may consent under pressure — because he needs money, grain, food, or relief — and may agree to a penalty he has little realistic chance of avoiding. If the lender knows this and structures the transaction accordingly, the contract is predatory even though the borrower has formally agreed.

The most striking feature associated with Robert's early approach is the suggestion that where such a penalty is admitted, the surplus should be directed to the poor rather than retained as creditor profit. It is a revealing compromise. The borrower's delay may be a genuine wrong and the penalty may rightly mark it, but the creditor's enrichment remains suspect, so the money is sent elsewhere. And the logic is shrewd. If the penalty exists for punishment and moral discipline, a lender who cannot keep it has no reason to engineer delay or quietly hope for breach; the clause can deter without becoming his revenue, and his interest falls back into alignment with timely repayment. The early tradition, in short, understood the incentive problem about as well as anyone has since: the danger was never only that the borrower pays too much, but that the lender's heart becomes attached to the borrower's failure — that he comes to prefer late repayment because lateness pays.

Robert does not offer the final doctrine. Later authors will distinguish more carefully among penalty, damages, loss, intention, fraud, and civil-law enforceability; they will ask whether a creditor may retain a penalty when actual damage has been suffered; they will analyze proportionality, expectation, and abuse. But his early treatment already contains the seed of it all — a penalty clause may be morally possible, and creditor profit from penalty remains dangerous — because poena conventionalis sits at the edge of usury. If the payment is truly attached to culpable delay, it may be external to the loan; if it is expected from the beginning, it has become internal to it. Robert's suspicion is aimed at exactly that edge, where a penalty can serve justice and can equally be a fiction.

Behind the caution, the Church was trying to preserve the gratuity of the mutuum without denying the realities of contractual life. A borrower who promises repayment and then culpably delays has done something unjust; the creditor may suffer harm; a community needs mechanisms to secure good faith. What the creditor must not do is exploit that need for discipline to obtain what the usury prohibition forbids. Robert's instinct is therefore protective in every direction at once. He protects the borrower from interest disguised as penalty, the creditor — at least in principle — from wrongful delay, the poor by proposing that penalty surplus be turned toward them, and the doctrine itself from being hollowed out by clever drafting.

That last protection matters, because the history of the extrinsic titles is a history of moral nuance and of moral evasion together: every legitimate exception in this book will sooner or later be tried on as a costume. The theologians knew it from the beginning — abuse was not discovered after the titles expanded. It was part of the problem from the start.

Robert of Courçon thus lets us see poena conventionalis in its first theological light: not yet a settled extrinsic title with fully developed conditions, but a contested device, admitted — if at all — under moral suspicion. The early attempt was not to make lending profitable but to distinguish punishment for default from gain from lending — the distinction that will govern the whole later development. The creditor may not profit from the mutuum as such; if a penalty is allowed, it must be because something has happened outside it — culpable delay, breach, disorder, injury — and even then the tradition stays anxious about who receives the surplus and what incentives the clause creates. Robert's importance lies in that anxiety: the penalty title was morally suspicious before it was doctrinally standardized. The tradition did not begin by asking how more payment might be permitted. It began by asking how a legal penalty could be kept from becoming a spiritual fraud — and its starting point was accordingly not convenience, not contract technique, not creditor profit, but vigilance.

sections.title: 11.2 Raymond of Peñafort
paragraphs: Raymond of Peñafort brings the penalty clause into the confessional, which in the thirteenth century is where moral theory went to meet actual human beings. Raymond was a Catalan, born around 1175, who held the chair of canon law at Bologna before joining the new Dominican order in 1222. For confessors he wrote the Summa de casibus poenitentiae — a working manual of cases of conscience, composed in the mid-1220s and revised a decade later — which became the standard handbook of the age. Gregory IX then set him to organizing the Church's scattered legislation, and the result — the Decretals of 1234 — remained the core of canon law until 1917. Elected Master General of the Dominicans in 1238, he resigned after two years and lived on to January 1275, within reach of his hundredth birthday if the traditional birth date is right. Canonized in 1601, he is the patron saint of canon lawyers, which seems only fair: he compiled their book.

If Robert of Courçon shows the early anxiety about creditor profit, Raymond supplies the moral language for handling it. His concern is not what a payment has been named — penalty, gift, compensation — but whether it is truly distinct from usury in its intention, its cause, and its moral form. He writes for priests deciding real cases: a borrower has paid more than principal, and the creditor says the extra was not interest — a penalty, perhaps, or compensation, or a gift, or something agreed at the outset and only triggered later. The confessor must ask: is this restitution? Punishment? Damages? Or usury disguised by vocabulary?

Raymond's treatment of interesse is especially important. In his moral world interesse does not mean modern interest but compensation for loss — "not gain, but avoidance of loss" — and the phrase is decisive: it marks the difference between a creditor restored to the position he should have occupied, which may be just, and a creditor enriched because he lent money, which remains usury. The distinction will later become central to damnum emergens, and it shapes the penalty title too. A penalty for delay is morally defensible only insofar as it is connected to some real wrong, injury, or disorder caused by the delay. It is not a way of making money from money, not a premium for the passage of time, not a hidden return on the loan. It is tied to the borrower's failure to do what justice required.

The problem, as ever, is that human beings can manipulate form. A lender may call a payment interesse while using it to obtain gain. A borrower may be told the loan is gratuitous while everyone understands that some additional amount is expected. A "gift" may be voluntary in name and demanded in substance; a penalty may be conditional in its wording and predictable in practice. Raymond's contribution is to insist that the moral analysis look past the label to the intention and expectation embedded in the transaction.

This is why expected gifts loom so large in the usury tradition. A creditor might say: "I did not require interest. The borrower gave me something freely." But if the so-called gift was expected from the beginning, if the borrower knew that future access to credit depended on it, or if the lender intended it as part of the bargain, then the gift is not really gratuitous. It is a concealed price, usurious in moral character even though it lacks the legal form of interest. The same logic applies to penalties. A penalty expected from the beginning is no longer purely penal; a lender who structures a loan so that the penalty will probably be paid is not merely protecting himself against breach but arranging to receive surplus from the loan. The trigger may be delay, but the delay has become part of the economic design, and the surplus is not truly external to the mutuum.

None of this makes subjective intention the whole story — a lender cannot purify an abusive contract by claiming good intentions, and a borrower's consent cannot make every clause just. Intention must be read together with structure. What did the parties expect? What did the contract encourage? Was the payment truly avoidable, the penalty proportionate? Did the lender actually suffer loss? Was the supposed gift or penalty freely given, or built into the bargain? So Raymond moves the analysis from surface form to moral reality: the issue is not whether the payment was stipulated but whether the stipulation corresponds to justice.

His formula — not gain, but avoidance of loss — is therefore a governing principle, and it tells us how to read the extrinsic titles generally. A title is morally serious when it prevents the creditor from being unjustly harmed. It becomes corrupt when it allows the creditor to be positively enriched by the borrower's need or delay. The boundary is not always easy to draw, but the principle is clear: restoration is not extraction.

This is why Raymond belongs to the genealogy of both poena conventionalis and damnum emergens. The penalty for delay and the compensation for actual loss are distinct titles but historically and morally close — a penalty may be justified because delay causes loss, compensation because the creditor has been damaged — and both require an external burden, can be abused, and must be kept from becoming ordinary return on a loan.

The pastoral setting keeps this concrete. A confessor must be alert to spiritual self-deception: the creditor before him may not be lying in any simple sense, but sincerely believe that because the contract says "penalty," or because the borrower "offered" a gift, the transaction is clean. Moral theology asks the deeper question — what does justice require, and what does charity forbid? A person can obey the appearance of a rule while violating its substance, and usury hides precisely there, in the gap between form and substance.

The weight Raymond gives to expectation also reveals the relational nature of the doctrine. Usury is not merely an accounting error; it is a disorder in the relation between lender and borrower. A lender who expects gain from the borrower's need has already bent the relationship toward extraction; a gift the borrower cannot safely refuse is a burden; a penalty expected because the borrower will probably be late is a trap. In each case, what matters is not only the movement of money but the moral posture of one person toward another.

Raymond thus deepens the anti-abuse logic of the penalty title. The question is never only whether the borrower was late, but whether the lender has converted lateness into gain; not only whether a penalty was agreed, but whether the agreement was used to evade the prohibition on charging for the loan itself; not only whether the creditor received more, but whether the "more" was truly tied to loss, delay, or injury — or expected as the hidden fruit of lending. A penalty as compensation for injury can be morally distinct from the price of the loan: if the borrower's delay injures the lender, the lender may be restored; if the failure imposes costs, he may be reimbursed; if the borrower culpably retains what is owed, a penalty may discipline the breach. But none of these claims can be allowed to become a standing entitlement to surplus.

His thought is therefore practical and severe at once — practical because he recognizes that losses happen, delays matter, and creditors can be harmed; severe because he refuses to let the creditor's advantage hide inside friendly forms. Gifts, penalties, and even compensation can all be usurious when they are not what they claim to be. The moral issue is never the name of the payment, but its cause.

For modern readers, Raymond is unexpectedly sharp. Contemporary finance usually treats form as decisive: a fee is a fee, a penalty is a penalty, a voluntary payment is voluntary, a contract is a contract. Raymond would ask what the parties actually expected, whether the borrower could refuse, whether the lender was avoiding loss or seeking gain, whether the payment restored equality or created profit from dependency. And his questions apply directly to modern late fees, default interest, restructuring charges, "voluntary" founder concessions, advisory fees, and side arrangements. However the payment is described, if it is expected from the beginning as the lender's return, it has the moral character of interest, and the structure must be read in the light of expectation.

Raymond of Peñafort thus gives the penalty title a rule of discernment it never lost: do not confuse the avoidance of loss with the pursuit of gain. Compensation may be just, and so may penalty — but when either becomes a path to creditor enrichment from the loan itself, the old sin has merely changed its clothes.

sections.title: 11.3 Hostiensis
paragraphs: With Hostiensis, the penalty title moves toward canonical systematization — and acquires its formula. Henry of Susa, better known as Hostiensis, was born around 1200 at Susa in Piedmont, where the Alpine road comes down into Italy, and became by common consent the most brilliant canonist of his century. He took his degree in both laws at Bologna, taught canon law at Paris, served Henry III of England on an embassy to Innocent IV, and rose through the Church: bishop of Sisteron in 1244, archbishop of Embrun in 1250, and from 1262 cardinal-bishop of Ostia, the see that gave him his name. His great Summa on the Decretals, written during the Embrun years, was so admired that posterity called it simply the Summa aurea, the Golden Summa; his still larger Lectura on the Decretals occupied him to his death at Lyon in 1271. He did not invent the problem of the penalty clause — Robert of Courçon and Raymond of Peñafort show theologians and confessors already worrying about penalties, expected gifts, compensation, and disguised usury. What he did was give it a sharper canonical form, bringing it into the legal-theological architecture of the ius commune, where Roman law, canon law, pastoral judgment, and scholastic moral reasoning increasingly speak to one another. The question remains the same: can a creditor receive more than principal when the additional payment is attached to breach or delay — and if so, how does the Church prevent that payment from becoming a fraud on the usury prohibition?

His answer is remembered through a formula: poena nec in fraudem — a penalty, but not in fraud. The phrase is brief and contains an entire moral world. A penalty may be possible, legally stipulated, even morally acceptable — but only if it is not being used to evade the prohibition on usury. Hostiensis does not treat the penalty as a magic exception. He treats it as a dangerous legal form that must be examined: legitimate when it truly responds to non-performance, illicit when arranged as a substitute for interest. The creditor may not say "I charge no usury" while designing the contract so that the borrower predictably owes more.

Nor is the concern merely semantic. It is not enough that the surplus is called a penalty rather than interest; the moral question is whether the payment is genuinely external to the loan. Is it attached to a real breach? To culpable delay? Is it proportionate to the wrong or the loss? Is it something the borrower could realistically avoid? Or is it the expected mechanism through which the lender receives his gain?

His legal culture was built for this anti-fraud work, being deeply concerned with the way the same external form can conceal different moral realities: a sale can conceal a loan, a partnership a guaranteed return, a gift a demanded payment, a penalty interest. The jurist's task, for Hostiensis, is not simply to classify the words of the contract but to test whether the legal form is being used truthfully — which is why nec in fraudem matters so much. Fraud here need not mean crude deception; it can mean evasion, the use of a permitted form to achieve a forbidden substance. A transaction may be transparent in its wording — plainly written, understood by both parties, stipulated in advance — and still fraudulent in its moral purpose: if the clause exists in order to produce the lender's return from the loan, it is in fraud of the law and the Gospel.
The anti-fraud principle also explains the famous verse-list associated with Hostiensis and the exception tradition:

Poena nec in fraudem, lex commissoria, gratis
Dans, socii, pompa; plus sorte modis datur istis.

The line is a compressed memory device — one of the rhymes by which canonists kept track of the licit exceptions, thirteen on the count associated with Hostiensis. It gathers several cases in which more than the principal may, under certain conditions, be received: penalty not in fraud, forfeiture or penalty clauses, gifts freely given, partnership, and certain public or ceremonial circumstances. Confessors and jurists needed such practical categories: they had cases to answer and distinctions to remember, and they had to know when "more than principal" might not be usury.

But the very existence of such a list is morally ambiguous. On one side it protects nuance: it keeps the usury doctrine from becoming crude and recognizes that not every surplus is extracted from the mutuum itself — a partner may share profits, a true gift may be received, a penalty may respond to breach, a creditor may be compensated for something other than the loan. On the other side, a catalog of exceptions can be studied by the wrong students: once the exceptions are written down, clever parties can learn to imitate them, and the list meant to distinguish justice from usury becomes a handbook for evasion. The lender's question quietly changes from "Is this just?" to "Which label should I attach to this surplus so that it appears permissible?" — and moral discernment gives way to legal engineering.

Poena nec in fraudem is Hostiensis's resistance to that corruption, built into the exception itself: the label is not enough, and the exception is valid only if it is not being used as a fraud. The penalty must be real, the gift free, the partnership genuinely risk-sharing; the legal form must correspond to the moral substance, or the exception becomes a lie. For poena conventionalis, this means the lender must not desire the penalty as revenue — not build it into the expected return, not design the due date as a trap, not set the amount excessively, not exploit the borrower's vulnerability — so that the clause secures repayment rather than monetizing non-repayment.

A penalty clause carries a peculiar psychological danger: it lets the lender keep a clean conscience while profiting from a borrower's failure. "I did not ask for interest," he can say. "I only enforced the contract." Hostiensis asks the next question: what kind of contract did you make? Was the penalty there to prevent breach, or was breach the event that made the contract profitable? In this way he deepens the tradition's attention to moral substance — to how one structures a demand and not only what one demands, what one intended and expected to receive and not only what one received, whether the contract exploited the borrower's likely inability to perform and not only whether he signed, whether the clause preserves justice and not only whether civil law permits it.

This is why Hostiensis matters for the larger history of the extrinsic titles. He embodies the canonical move from scattered pastoral concern toward disciplined categories: the penalty title is admitted, but only under anti-fraud conditions; the law supplies forms, and theology judges their use; the lender protected from wrongful delay, the borrower from disguised usury. The same pattern will appear in every later title. Damnum emergens will be allowed, but not fictive damages; lucrum cessans will be admitted by later authors, but not imaginary opportunity cost; periculum sortis will be recognized, but not risk premiums charged by those who have shifted the downside onto others. In each case the title is real only if the burden is real. The name does not create the moral fact.

Hostiensis's contribution is therefore not simply one more item in a technical legal genealogy. It is a principle of moral analysis — surplus above principal may be received in certain modes, but only when those modes do not become instruments of fraud — and his phrase should be read almost as a warning label: penalty, yes, but not as evasion; contract, yes, but not as disguise; enforcement, yes, but not as extraction; legal form, yes, but only when joined to moral truth.

For modern finance, the insight is startlingly relevant. Contemporary contracts bristle with fees, default rates, cure penalties, preferred returns, restructuring charges, break fees, monitoring fees, advisory fees, and reimbursement clauses. Each may have a legitimate purpose, and each may become a vehicle for extracting surplus while preserving legal respectability. Hostiensis teaches us to ask not only whether the clause is enforceable but whether it is nec in fraudem — not in fraud of justice. A modern non-extractive finance contract must therefore do more than avoid the word "interest." It must avoid the moral substance of usury: any penalty truly reluctant, proportionate, avoidable, and tied to breach; a lender who does not profit from the borrower's failure; a legal architecture that has not become a machine for moral evasion.

This is the enduring value of Hostiensis. He gives the tradition a compact rule for a recurring danger: whenever a surplus claim is permitted, ask whether the permission has become a pretext. The penalty may be allowed — but only if it is not fraud.

chapters.part: Part V — Poena Conventionalis / Morae Poena: Penalty for Delay

chapters.title: Aquinas, Scotus, and the Anti-Abuse Logic
chapters.label: Chapter 12

sections.title: 12.1 Aquinas on damages for delay
paragraphs: Aquinas does not treat poena conventionalis as a free-standing loophole in the usury prohibition. He treats delay, damages, and compensation inside the deeper architecture of justice, and that is exactly why his contribution matters so much. He does not begin with the practical question a lender would ask — may I add a penalty clause to a loan? He begins with the nature of the mutuum, the equality that commutative justice requires, and the difference between selling the use of money and repairing an injury. Only inside that larger moral grammar can damages for delay be understood at all.

The central text remains Summa Theologiae II-II, q.78, where Aquinas asks whether it is a sin to take usury for money lent. His answer is severe. To take payment for the use of money lent in a mutuum is unjust, because money, like wine or wheat, is consumed in its use; to sell the thing and the use of the thing separately is to sell the same thing twice. Once ownership of the money has passed to the borrower, the lender may claim the equivalent principal and nothing more, for he cannot charge the borrower for the use of what now belongs to the borrower. This principle governs everything that follows. If a late charge is really a payment for the borrower's use of money over time, it is usury, and calling it a penalty does not change its substance. The lender may not do indirectly what he may not do directly.

Yet Aquinas never says that a lender must absorb every harm connected to a loan. In q.78, a.2, ad 1, he distinguishes compensation for loss incurred from payment for profit not made: the lender may be compensated for a loss he has suffered, but he may not sell a gain he does not yet possess. That distinction becomes the Thomistic root of damnum emergens and the Thomistic obstacle to lucrum cessans, and it bears directly on penalties for delay, because delay can cause actual damage. If the borrower wrongfully retains what is owed, the creditor may be injured: he may need the funds to meet obligations of his own, incur costs, suffer a concrete loss because what should have been restored was withheld. In that case the additional payment is not demanded as the price of the loan but as compensation for harm. The moral ground has shifted from the mutuum itself to the injury caused by the failure to repay.

This is also why q.78, a.2, ad 7 matters so much. Aquinas rejects the practice of charging a higher price merely because payment will come later. If a seller raises his price simply because the buyer will pay in the future, the transaction takes on the character of a loan: the delay has been monetized, and the higher price is attached not to any separate loss, labor, risk, or expense but to waiting itself. Here Aquinas hands the whole doctrine one of its clearest principles: waiting, by itself, is not a commodity that may be sold. Delay can still matter morally, but it must be connected to some real burden. If delay causes loss, the loss may be compensable. If delay is culpable, it may require restitution. If delay merely means the lender waits longer for his repayment, and the charge grows simply because time has passed, the charge is usurious.

His treatment of restitution in q.78, a.3 deepens the point. A person who unjustly takes or retains what belongs to another owes restitution; wrongful retention is not morally neutral. If the borrower keeps what should be repaid, the creditor is deprived of what is due — and the injustice lies not in the original loan but in the failure to restore. That creates a possible ground for compensation, but the ground is, once again, not the use of money as such; it is the injury of withholding what is owed. This is the crucial anti-abuse logic. Aquinas recognizes damage from delay without endorsing payment for time, allows restoration without allowing extraction, gives the lender a claim when justice has been violated — and refuses to let the lender convert the structure of delay into a revenue model. The same careful balance appears in De malo, q.13, a.4, ad 14, where Aquinas is often read as acknowledging that late repayment can create a compensable harm. He is not abandoning the prohibition there, and he is not granting an automatic right to interest upon lateness. He is recognizing that if the debtor's delay injures the creditor, the creditor need not bear that injury without remedy.

The distinction can be stated simply — compensate wrongful delay, but do not monetize time — and it is far easier to state than to preserve. A payment for wrongful delay looks, from the outside, like a payment calculated after time has passed. So does a payment for time itself. The external forms resemble each other; the moral objects differ. In one case the payment repairs an injury; in the other it sells the use of money. And for Aquinas the moral object is the whole question. What is being sold? What is being restored? What is being claimed? A lender may not sell the borrower the use of money that has become the borrower's property, but he may be made whole for damage the borrower's unjust conduct has caused. The first is usury; the second is restitution. This is why his position cannot be filed either under rigid anti-commercialism or under proto-modern interest theory. He is stricter than modern finance, because he denies that money as such deserves a return merely for the passage of time; and he is more nuanced than a crude prohibition of all surplus, because he recognizes that justice may require compensation for real harm.

Applied to poena conventionalis, the Thomistic rule means a penalty is legitimate only insofar as it tracks genuine breach, harm, or restitution. If the borrower is late and the delay causes loss, compensation is morally intelligible. If the penalty simply grows with time, like an expected return, it is suspect. If the due date is structured so that delay is likely, the penalty has become a disguised price of credit. And if the lender would rather collect the penalty than receive his principal on time, the clause fails the Thomistic test outright. Aquinas forces one question: what is the payment attached to? Attached to the loan itself, or to the mere passage of time, it is usury; attached to a higher price for waiting, it may well be; attached to real damage from wrongful delay, it may be compensation; attached to restitution for unjust retention, it may restore equality.

The reach of this analysis into modern debate is hard to overstate. Modern contracts routinely treat default interest as a normal escalation of yield — one rate before default, a higher rate after — justified as administrative burden, increased risk, or discipline. Aquinas would ask whether the higher payment corresponds to actual harm, real cost, or culpable delay, or whether it simply monetizes the borrower's distress. A late fee that covers actual collection costs may be defensible; so may a grace period that encourages timely repayment without exploiting hardship, or a proportionate penalty for culpable non-payment. But a compounding default rate that becomes a major source of lender profit begins to look like exactly what Aquinas rejects: payment for time under the cover of delay. A non-extractive fund faces the same discipline. It need not pretend that late repayment creates no problems; it may need mechanisms for accountability, recovery of real administrative costs, and ways of distinguishing good-faith hardship from negligent non-performance. What it must not do is design delay as a profit center, hope for default, or let penalties quietly substitute for yield.

This is the moral spine Aquinas gives the penalty title. The creditor may be restored, but not enriched by the loan; delay may be repaired, but not sold as time; wrongful retention may trigger restitution, but never a standing right to growth; contractual discipline is just only while it serves repayment rather than extraction. The whole question compresses into a single Thomistic distinction: the lender may receive compensation because he has been harmed, not because he has waited. That distinction will shape every later debate over poena conventionalis and damnum emergens, and the dangerous expansion toward lucrum cessans — and it remains one of the most useful principles available to modern moral finance. Every charge for delay must answer the question Aquinas leaves us with: are we repairing an injustice, or selling time?

sections.title: 12.2 Duns Scotus and the incentive problem
paragraphs: Duns Scotus sharpens the problem of poena conventionalis with a question that is morally simple and economically devastating: does the lender actually want the borrower to repay on time?

The question cuts straight through formalism. A contract may be carefully drafted, the loan described as gratuitous, the penalty formally conditional, the borrower's agreement duly recorded, the lender ready to explain that the clause merely protects him against delay. Scotus presses past all of it. When the borrower repays punctually, is the lender satisfied — or quietly disappointed, because the penalty has slipped through his fingers? With that question the analysis shifts from wording to incentives.

The earlier tradition had already sensed the danger of disguise. Robert of Courçon was suspicious of creditor profit from penalties; Raymond of Peñafort insisted that compensation be "not gain, but avoidance of loss"; Hostiensis gave the principle its canonical shape as poena nec in fraudem — penalty not in fraud of the usury prohibition; Aquinas separated compensation for real loss from payment for time. Scotus adds a further diagnostic: look at what the clause makes the lender desire.

A true penalty is designed to prevent default. It is a guardrail. It tells the borrower that repayment matters, that delay is not morally indifferent, that wrongful failure to perform will carry consequences — and its author hopes never to invoke it. In such a structure the lender prefers not to collect; he wants performance, and the penalty exists in order not to be used. A usurious penalty reverses the preference. Timely repayment yields the lender only his principal; late repayment yields principal plus penalty; and once the penalty is substantial, expected, or repeatedly collected, the lender's interest has become aligned with delay. Default has been made attractive — to the creditor. That is the incentive problem, and the moral danger runs deeper than the borrower's paying more. The lender's will becomes disordered. He begins to benefit from what should count as a failure of justice, to prefer that the borrower fall short, to treat the borrower's default as his opportunity. The contract no longer merely disciplines breach; it monetizes breach.

This matters because usury was never only an error in pricing. It is a deformation of relationship. In a just lending relationship the lender wants the borrower restored to stability and the debt repaid according to justice; in an extractive one the borrower's weakness is what pays. The penalty clause can become the clearest example of the deformation, because it hands the creditor a standing reason to prefer disorder. Scotus's insight therefore explains why intention cannot be separated from structure. It would be too easy to ask only what the lender subjectively says he intends: a lender may profess his desire for punctual repayment while designing a contract that makes lateness the more profitable outcome, and a contract may reveal intentions that polite language conceals. Incentives are not the whole of morality, but they are evidence. They show what the arrangement rewards.

The diagnostic question — would the lender rather be repaid on time? — is powerful precisely because it joins inner intention to outward structure. Where the penalty is modest, proportionate, avoidable, and genuinely exceptional, timely repayment remains the lender's preferred outcome and the penalty serves the repayment obligation. Where it is large, expected, or designed around likely default, the service runs the other way: the penalty no longer serves repayment; repayment has been arranged to serve the penalty, with the borrower's failure producing the creditor's gain. The same analysis exposes the artificially short repayment period, in which a due date that looks clear and contractual is unrealistic under the circumstances, so that the borrower formally has an opportunity to avoid the penalty and practically does not. Such a default is not an accident; it is manufactured, part of the expected economics of the transaction. An excessive penalty betrays itself the same way, by exceeding the disciplinary or compensatory purpose it claims: when the amount is far larger than any plausible harm, the payment stops looking like restoration and starts looking like yield — the contract says "delay," but the amount says "profit."

Scotus's test grows sharper still across repeated lending. A one-off penalty clause may pass as a device for discipline. But when a lender writes penalty structures into loan after loan and earns from late payment again and again, the pattern itself becomes morally revealing: the business model depends on default. What might have seemed exceptional in a single case becomes ordinary in a portfolio, and the penalty has become a revenue line. Modern finance knows the pattern intimately. Whole business models rest on penalties, defaults, late fees, overdraft charges, compounding default rates, restructuring fees, and other payments triggered by borrower distress — each charge individually describable as a consequence of non-performance, the institution structurally reliant on the sum of them. Scotus's question exposes the fault line: does the lender want healthy repayment, or does the lender make more money when borrowers stumble? A non-extractive financial structure must be able to answer that question clearly. If timely repayment is bad for the lender's revenue model, something is wrong; if borrower distress is profitable, the relationship has become morally inverted; if defaults are not merely risks to be managed but sources of expected yield, the penalty has ceased to be a reluctant instrument of justice.

Nor does the logic stop at ordinary loans. Venture financing, revenue-based financing, convertible instruments, preferred equity, and restructuring agreements can all contain clauses that reward investor control precisely when the company struggles. Some protections may be legitimate — investors may need safeguards against bad faith, negligence, or mission drift — but they become suspect the moment the investor benefits from the enterprise's weakness. The deeper question is Scotus's, transposed: does the capital provider profit more when the borrower or company fails to flourish? Moral finance, he teaches, must care about incentive alignment. It is not enough for a contract to carry acceptable labels; it must make the right things desirable — timely repayment, borrower flourishing, shared success — and it must not make default, distress, or dependency profitable.

This is why the penalty title requires more than a formal condition. Conditionality alone does not make a payment extrinsic, because the condition itself can be engineered: a lender can structure the very event that triggers the surplus, and a penalty can be perfectly conditional and still usurious when the condition is the hidden path to gain. The true anti-abuse test must therefore include an incentive analysis. Is the due date realistic? Is the penalty avoidable, proportionate, genuinely exceptional? Does the lender book the penalty as compensation for injury or as expected revenue? Does he prefer timely repayment? Does the contract encourage him to cooperate with the borrower's success, or to wait for the borrower's failure? These questions are not modern additions to an old doctrine; they are latent in the medieval concern itself. Scotus makes explicit what the earlier tradition already sensed — usury can hide not only in words, but in incentives.

It follows that poena conventionalis can never be evaluated one clause at a time. The whole relationship matters. A penalty may be moderate in isolation and abusive in context, formally avoidable and practically inescapable, sincerely called exceptional while the portfolio quietly depends on it; the moral reality emerges from the pattern. Scotus's contribution is therefore not simply a technical refinement but a moral anthropology of the creditor. What does the creditor love? What outcome does he desire — justice, repayment, and the borrower's restoration, or the profitable event of default? A true penalty disciplines the borrower for the sake of right order; a usurious one tempts the lender to love the borrower's disorder. That is the danger Scotus exposes, and once seen, it cannot be unseen.

sections.title: 12.3 Late medieval abuse patterns
paragraphs: By the late medieval period the moral danger of the penalty clause was no longer theoretical. The abuse patterns were visible, and the basic device was simple. A lender would make a loan that appeared, on its face, to respect the usury prohibition: principal due on a stated date, no interest charged explicitly, the loan itself — truthfully, at the level of words — gratuitous. But the contract would also contain a penalty clause: if the borrower did not repay on time, an additional sum would be owed. In a just case, such a clause could discipline culpable delay. In the abusive case, the penalty was the real economic point of the transaction. The lender had no need to charge interest openly; he could create a situation in which the borrower would predictably fail, and then collect. The contract preserved the appearance of orthodoxy while reproducing the substance of usury.

The recurring forms became familiar. The first was the artificially short repayment period. The borrower would be given a deadline formally clear and practically unrealistic — he needed time for a harvest, a shipment, a sale, or wages to arrive; perhaps he was already in distress; perhaps everyone involved knew that repayment by the stated date was unlikely. The contract set that date anyway, and when the borrower missed it, the lender could say, truthfully, that the penalty was owed because of delay. But the delay had been engineered. That is morally different from the borrower who freely and culpably fails to repay despite a reasonable opportunity to do so. In the abusive case the due date is a trap; the default is not an unexpected failure of the transaction but the mechanism by which it becomes profitable; the penalty is not an external remedy but the hidden price of the loan.

The second was the excessive penalty. Even where the repayment date was plausible, the penalty might stand in no proportion to the harm of delay: a small late payment triggering a large charge, a brief delay generating a substantial surplus, an amount bearing no relationship to actual loss, administrative burden, or the seriousness of the breach. Its size revealed its purpose — not the restoration of equality but the production of yield. Proportionality therefore became a central test. A penalty must not be so large that it ceases to be penal and becomes profitable; it must not turn a minor delay into a major transfer of wealth; it must not punish beyond the injury. Once the penalty exceeds the moral burden it claims to address, it discloses another purpose: extraction.

The third was expected default — the pattern that most plainly confirms the concern of Duns Scotus. The lender might formally prefer timely repayment while the economics of the arrangement said otherwise: loans repeatedly structured so that penalties were likely, penalties arriving as a regular part of income, default no longer exceptional but expected. The penalty clause had become a revenue model. And this abuse was especially dangerous because it could hide inside a portfolio. Each borrower appeared to have defaulted individually; each penalty could be justified as the consequence of that borrower's failure; only when many borrowers defaulted under similar terms did the broader structure become visible. The lender had not merely protected himself against occasional breach. He had built a system in which breach was normal and profitable.

The fourth was the borrower who could not realistically avoid the penalty at all — because the repayment date was too short, because his income was uncertain, because he had no alternative source of funds, or because his need was so urgent that he accepted terms he could not prudently fulfill. His formal consent resolved nothing; a person can agree to a trap, because desperation narrows the field of choice. Here is one of the reasons scholastic moral theology cannot be reduced to contract formalism. The borrower's signature is not enough. The lender must not exploit the borrower's likely failure, and a penalty that is legally avoidable but practically unavoidable is morally suspect: if the lender knows the borrower probably cannot escape it, the penalty already belongs to the expected exchange.

The fifth abuse is the general pattern that unites the others: the transformation of penalty into hidden yield. The loan is presented as free, the surplus as conditional, the condition as breach — and in reality the penalty functions as the lender's return on capital: calculated, expected, repeated, desired. It is not an accident; it is the point. At that moment the extrinsic title collapses. The penalty is no longer external to the mutuum but internal to it, and the lender receives more not because of a real and exceptional wrong but because the contract was designed to produce more. The name remains poena while the substance has become usury.

Seen together, the abuse patterns reveal the practical wisdom behind the earlier cautions. Robert of Courçon's suspicion of creditor profit, Raymond of Peñafort's line between avoiding loss and seeking gain, Hostiensis's requirement that the penalty be nec in fraudem, Aquinas's refusal to monetize time, Scotus's incentive test — all converge here, not as abstract scruples but as responses to predictable patterns of evasion. They also show why a merely legal solution was insufficient. A penalty clause could be valid in civil law, enforceable, clearly written, even voluntarily accepted, and still be morally false. Where the law saw a condition, moral theology saw an incentive; where the law saw consent, it saw vulnerability; where the law saw breach, it asked whether the breach had been engineered. The tradition drew deeply on legal categories, but it refused to let legal form be the final judge: a contract can be valid and unjust, a clause clear and predatory, a penalty enforceable and usurious. The question is always whether the structure corresponds to the reality of justice.

The abuses also expose the moral role of time. In a true penalty, time matters because a promise was broken: the date was meaningful, the borrower failed to perform, and harm or disorder followed. In hidden usury, time matters because the lender is selling delay: the later the borrower pays, the more the lender gains, and the penalty becomes a way of pricing duration while avoiding the language of interest. This is why the late medieval theologians and canonists had to remain severe. Admit the penalty clause too broadly, and the prohibition on usury would be emptied from within — every lender could write a short due date, attach a penalty, and claim that any surplus came from default rather than from the loan. Usury would not disappear; it would become contractual choreography.

So the question of poena conventionalis becomes, in the end, a question of moral design. What sort of behavior does the contract reward? What does it make profitable, and what does it make costly? Does it encourage repayment, or default — support borrower responsibility, or exploit borrower weakness — protect justice, or monetize failure? A well-ordered penalty clause makes breach unattractive to the borrower without making it attractive to the lender: it encourages performance without creating a creditor appetite for non-performance, a shield against wrongful delay rather than a harvest from distress. And the modern implications are direct, because contemporary finance contains exactly the same abuse patterns in updated form — unrealistic repayment schedules, compounding default interest, excessive late fees, penalty pricing, automatic escalation clauses, refinancing traps, restructuring fees, and revenue models dependent on borrower failure. The vocabulary has changed; the moral structure is familiar. A clause says "penalty." The spreadsheet says "yield." The late medieval tradition teaches us to read the spreadsheet.

It teaches, finally, that abuse is not always hidden in secrecy. Sometimes it hides in plain sight, inside normal clauses, standard forms, and accepted practices, and the question is never whether a penalty exists but whether the penalty has become the lender's expected profit. With that, the first stage of the penalty title reaches its practical conclusion. A penalty may be allowed, but only if it remains truly penal; a repayment date may be enforced, but only if it is realistic; a delay may be punished, but only if the delay is culpable and the penalty proportionate; a lender may be protected, but not enriched by the borrower's failure. The moral boundary can be stated plainly: a penalty that protects timely repayment may serve justice, and a penalty that makes late repayment profitable serves usury.

chapters.part: Part V — Poena Conventionalis / Morae Poena: Penalty for Delay

chapters.title: Late Medieval, Salamanca, and Manualist Settlement
chapters.label: Chapter 13

sections.title: 13.1 Bernardino of Siena
paragraphs: By the time we reach Bernardino of Siena, the penalty title has entered a more practical and pastoral phase. The early question — can a contractual penalty ever be distinguished from usury? — has not disappeared, but it is now being worked out in a wider world of preaching, confession, commerce, urban life, charitable lending, and moral reform.

Bernardino (1380–1444) is not merely a technical theologian of contracts. He is a Franciscan missionary preacher — canonized in 1450, only six years after his death — a moral teacher, and a reformer concerned with the concrete life of Christian cities. His economic thought grows out of that pastoral world: merchants, lenders, artisans, poor households, urban markets, civic order, and the temptations that arise when money becomes a tool of domination. His approach to the penalty clause is therefore practical in the deepest sense. He is not asking only what can be defended in theory. He is asking how Christian economic life can be disciplined so that commerce does not devour the vulnerable.

The penalty clause remains a dangerous instrument in his world. Bernardino inherits the earlier tradition's concern that poena conventionalis can be worn as a mask: the lender claims the loan itself is free while using delay to generate surplus; the clause says "penalty" while the structure says "interest." And his Italy knew such evasions well. Urban credit markets were sophisticated enough that usury rarely appeared crudely, as an explicit rate on a loan. It could hide in sales, exchanges, pledges, penalties, gifts, fees — in any contractual device with room inside it.

This is why standardizing the exceptions mattered. The tradition needed a way to say that some payments above principal could be licit without every payment above principal becoming licit. Bernardino belongs to the process by which the exception categories became recognizable and pastorally usable: penalty, actual loss, labor, expenses, partnership, each named and bounded, so that a moral teacher could tell a real external title from a fraudulent pretext. In this setting poena conventionalis is not rejected outright. Borrowers can fail unjustly; they can delay; they can impose real burdens on creditors and on institutions that depend on timely performance. A Christian moral economy is not required to be indifferent to breach — the command against usury is not a command to make promises meaningless. But the penalty must remain a penalty: not converted into the lender's expected gain, not structured so that the borrower's lateness becomes profitable, not a reward to the creditor for the debtor's failure. Bernardino preserves the anti-abuse logic of the earlier tradition and applies it to the increasingly complex commercial world of late medieval Italy.

His Franciscan context matters especially. Bernardino belongs to the same moral atmosphere that produced and defended the Monti di Pietà, the charitable lending institutions designed to give the poor credit without delivering them to the usurers. Those institutions required careful moral reasoning precisely because they were real: they had costs — staff, buildings, record-keeping, appraisal, the safekeeping of pledges, administrative discipline — and they had to cover those costs without becoming usurious themselves. Anyone concerned with practical institutions of mercy needed a moral vocabulary subtler than "all surplus is forbidden" or "all agreed payment is allowed." The same subtlety informs the penalty title. The question is never whether a lender or institution may protect itself against delay; it is whether the protection remains ordered to justice. Does the charge cover a real burden and discipline a real wrong? Is it moderate, transparent, proportionate? Does it preserve the lender's desire for timely repayment — or does it let the institution flourish financially because borrowers fail?

Bernardino's preaching against usury is severe because he sees how easily money corrupts the moral imagination. The usurer does not simply take too much. He learns to see his neighbor's need as an opportunity; he converts time, distress, and dependence into gain. Disguised usury is spiritually dangerous for exactly this reason — it lets the lender keep the appearance of respectability while continuing the same inward disorder — and the penalty clause is one of its most comfortable disguises, because it gives greed a moral vocabulary. The lender no longer says, "I profit from your need." He says, "I punish your default."

Bernardino's contribution is therefore not a new theoretical permission so much as a practical discipline of suspicion. The exception must be real; the title must correspond to actual moral substance; the preacher, the confessor, and the jurist must ask whether the clause is serving justice or evading it. A penalty that prevents disorder may be tolerable. A penalty that feeds on disorder is usury in another form. This also explains why late medieval moral theology preserves its lists of acceptable cases inside a hedge of warnings. The list is not a license; it is a map of possible distinctions. Poena nec in fraudem means a penalty may be permitted, but only when it is not fraudulent. Damnum emergens means actual loss may be compensated, but not invented. Stipendium laboris means labor may be paid, but not inflated into extraction. Partnership means profit may be shared, but only when risk is truly shared. The moral work lies not in reciting the category but in discerning whether the category is truly present.

For Bernardino this discernment is inseparable from the reform of Christian society, because usury is not a private vice. It damages the community — weakens households, burdens the poor, distorts commerce, corrodes civic trust — and when penalty clauses become disguised interest they do more than harm individual borrowers. They normalize evasion. They teach a city to admire cleverness over justice and make exploitation look lawful. A well-ordered penalty, by contrast, serves the common good: it supports fidelity to promises, protects institutions from wrongful delay, and preserves trust without turning debt into a machine of extraction, disciplining breach while remaining subordinate to the loan's deeper purpose — restoration, assistance, exchange, or cooperation.

The distinction presses hardest where lending serves the poor. A charitable lending institution that charges a moderate fee for administration may serve mercy. But if penalties become a major source of its income, the institution begins to resemble the very usurers it was created to displace — the poor person's failure has become the institution's sustainability model. Bernardino's Franciscan concern resists that inversion. The poor may need discipline; they must not become a revenue base.

Here the continuity with the earlier authors is plain. Robert of Courçon worried enough about creditor profit from penalties to want the surplus directed to the poor; Raymond of Peñafort distinguished avoidance of loss from pursuit of gain; Hostiensis insisted on poena nec in fraudem; Aquinas separated compensation for injury from payment for time; Scotus asked whether the lender preferred timely repayment. Bernardino receives the whole logic and carries it into the field of pastoral economics — and in doing so he helps stabilize the penalty title without domesticating its danger. The category becomes standard; confessors and jurists can use it; but the warning stands. A penalty is acceptable only while it remains external to the loan itself. It must respond to delay, not price delay; protect justice, not exploit breach; be a reluctant remedy, not a desired return.

There is a clear implication for everything that follows. Once exception categories become standard they can be taught, repeated, and applied — and they can also be manipulated. Bernardino's importance lies in holding practical usefulness and moral vigilance together: Christian economic life requires workable institutions, and every workable institution can drift. His treatment of penalty, expense, labor, and charitable lending is therefore not a relaxation of the usury prohibition but an effort to preserve it under real economic conditions — a doctrine that cannot distinguish compensation from extraction will become either impossibly rigid or dangerously permissive, and Bernardino's practical theology walks the narrow road between those failures. Modern non-extractive finance meets him at exactly this point. Funds and institutions may need rules, fees, deadlines, penalties; the question is whether those mechanisms serve the mission or quietly replace it — whether they help the borrower flourish, protect the institution without making distress profitable, recover real costs without building extraction into the model, and remain charitable in substance, not only in branding. Bernardino would teach suspicion of any financial design that depends on the failure of those it claims to serve. If the poor, the founder, the worker, or the mission must stumble for capital to be satisfied, the structure has lost its way.

The late medieval settlement of poena conventionalis therefore begins not with permission but with disciplined permission. Bernardino makes the category usable while keeping it morally dangerous — under judgment, never a comfortable euphemism for gain from lending. A Christian economy may need penalties; it may not build itself on the hope that debtors fail.

sections.title: 13.2 Sant'Antonino of Florence
paragraphs: Sant'Antonino of Florence (1389–1459) continues the effort to make the exception categories usable without letting them become instruments of evasion. Like Bernardino he stands in the world of practical moral theology — confession, commerce, civic life, charitable lending, merchant practice, the moral governance of Christian society — but Antonino, a Dominican who became archbishop of Florence, adds a further quality: synthesis. In works like his Summa moralis, an early landmark in the moral analysis of commerce and banking, he preserves, organizes, and transmits the exception-list tradition in a form later authors could inherit.

The synthesis mattered because by the fifteenth century the anti-usury doctrine was no longer dealing only with simple loans between isolated persons. It confronted cities, merchants, public debt, charitable credit institutions, pledges, contracts, exchange, and carefully drafted legal forms. The problem was no longer that Christians did not know usury was forbidden. The problem was that many knew it was forbidden and had learned to route around the prohibition through technical devices. Antonino's task was therefore not to condemn usury in general but to help distinguish legitimate surplus from disguised usury in concrete cases. The older categories had to be remembered — penalty not in fraud, compensation for real loss, gifts freely given, partnership, expense, labor: the situations in which more than principal might be received without necessarily violating the mutuum — and each had to stay bounded by moral substance. Poena nec in fraudem remains the standard form. The penalty may be admitted, but only if it is not a fraud; the clause may be present, but it must not be a pretext; the creditor may protect himself against wrongful delay, but he may not use delay to collect what would otherwise be usury.

In Antonino the importance of lists is especially visible. Medieval and late medieval authors gathered exceptions and permitted cases into memorable formulae because lists were practical tools: a confessor needed to know what to ask, a preacher what to warn against, a jurist how to classify the contract, a merchant where the boundaries lay, a charitable institution what it might not do. The list gave shape to discernment. But a list is never enough, and its danger is precisely that it makes moral reasoning look mechanical. If "penalty" appears on the list, every penalty seems permitted; if "gift," every expected gift becomes voluntary; if "partnership," every return-bearing instrument gets described as risk-sharing; if "expense," every fee becomes cost recovery. Antonino's tradition prevents that collapse by retaining the qualifying phrase: not in fraud. The small phrase does enormous work. It turns the category from a loophole into a test. A penalty is not allowed because it is called a penalty; it is allowed only if it truly functions as one — attached to real breach or culpable delay, proportionate, realistically avoidable, not expected as ordinary revenue, not making the lender prefer default, not used to sell time.

In this Antonino preserves the moral architecture the tradition had built — Courçon's suspicion of creditor profit, Raymond's concern for intention and expectation, Hostiensis's poena nec in fraudem, Aquinas's line between compensation and payment for time, Scotus's incentive question, Bernardino's pastoral suspicion of commercial evasion — all of it converging in the settled late medieval category. The penalty title is real, but guarded.

His Florentine context is no accident. Florence was not an abstract theological classroom; it was a commercial and banking city, where money, credit, reputation, exchange, public finance, and civic ambition were woven together. Moral theology in such a setting could not remain naive. The same intelligence that built trade networks and financial instruments could build moral evasions, and a bishop and moral theologian in such a city needed both charity and suspicion. That is also why the penalty title stayed important. In commercial life, dates matter: delivery dates, repayment dates, settlement dates, market opportunities, and civic obligations all depend on performance. A world without consequences for delay would not be just. But a world in which every delay becomes an occasion for gain is unjust in the other direction. Antonino's inherited tradition holds the two truths together — obligations are serious, and creditor power is dangerous. The analysis must ask whether the penalty is ordered toward performance or toward profit. Ordered toward performance, it supports trust: it says that promises matter and that one may not casually impose harm by failing to fulfill them. Ordered toward profit, it corrodes trust: it says the creditor benefits when the debtor fails. The same clause can protect civic order or become a mechanism of exploitation.

By keeping the exception lists alive, Antonino also transmits the deeper distinction between intrinsic and extrinsic claims to the later manualist settlement. More than principal may sometimes be received, but only when the reason for the surplus is external to the mutuum — when it corresponds to something real: breach, loss, expense, labor, risk, partnership, or a truly free gift. The title must be present in substance, not only in name. This is one of the key lessons of the whole late medieval period: moral theology does not abolish economic complexity, it disciplines it — asking whether complexity serves justice or hides injustice, what a surplus is for, and how legal form measures up against moral truth.

Antonino's treatment of poena conventionalis belongs to this disciplined realism, and it yields a usable gradient of suspicion. The more predictable the penalty, the more suspect. The more profitable, the more suspect. The less avoidable, the more suspect. And the more a lender depends on penalties for revenue, the more likely the whole structure has become usurious.

The pastoral imagination remains crucial, because Antonino is not thinking only of merchants who bargain from roughly equal positions. He is thinking of ordinary Christians, poor borrowers, debtors under pressure, communities vulnerable to creditor power. For the weak borrower a penalty clause can be especially oppressive: it can turn temporary difficulty into long-term bondage, make a debt grow precisely when the debtor is least able to bear it, convert a moment of need into a spiral of dependence. This concern ties the penalty title back to Scripture, for the biblical prohibition of usury was never merely a technical rule about finance. It is a defense of the poor neighbor against extraction: the cloak must be returned at night; the poor must be allowed to live; the hand must be opened even when the release year approaches; the righteous person does not lend at interest. If a penalty clause recreates the very domination Scripture condemns, no legal ingenuity can save it.

At the same time, the tradition does not romanticize the debtor. Borrowers can act unjustly; delay can be culpable; promises can be broken; creditors and charitable institutions can be harmed by bad faith. The penalty title remains possible because justice requires responsibility on both sides. But the remedy must not exceed the wrong, and the cure must not become another disease. That balance is exactly what non-extractive finance must recover. A modern fund, lending institution, or mission-locked vehicle may need default provisions, reporting deadlines, repayment schedules, and consequences for non-performance — without them it may become unsustainable, or unjust to its other participants. But if default provisions become revenue, if penalties become expected yield, if distress becomes an opportunity for capital to enlarge its claim, the structure has crossed the line. Antonino's inherited phrase remains a practical rule for contract design: do not ask merely whether a penalty is allowed; ask whether this penalty is truthful. Is it a reluctant safeguard, tied to actual breach, proportionate? Does it preserve the desire for timely performance, protect the vulnerable from spiraling debt, remain external to the loan itself? If yes, it may serve justice. If no, it is a disguise.

Sant'Antonino's role in the history of the extrinsic titles is preservation with discipline: he keeps the categories available to a complex economy without letting them become comfortable excuses, and passes the practical wisdom forward — more than principal may sometimes be received, but only when the moral reason is real. A penalty not in fraud is possible. A penalty in fraud is usury with better paperwork.

sections.title: 13.3 Salamanca and Jesuit refinements
paragraphs: With Salamanca and the Jesuit moral theologians, the penalty title enters a still more refined world of legal validity, proportionality, intention, and commercial context. The basic rule is inherited: a penalty may be accepted only if it is not a fraud on the usury prohibition. But the practical questions grow more detailed. What counts as fraud? How much may be stipulated? Does civil law matter? Can compensation be agreed in advance? How should intention be read when the contract sits inside ordinary commercial practice?

The School of Salamanca did not invent these questions; it faced them in a changed economic world. Sixteenth-century commerce was complex — credit, exchange, trade, insurance, public finance, merchant capital, global expansion — and its contractual situations were not easily reduced to the picture of a poor borrower taking a small loan. Yet the old danger had not moved. The mutuum still could not become a machine for guaranteed surplus, and the borrower's need, delay, or dependence still could not become the creditor's revenue.

Domingo de Soto (1494–1560), the Dominican whose De iustitia et iure of 1553 became one of the school's foundations, represents the cautious side of the tradition. He is deeply concerned to preserve the gratuity of the mutuum, and he understands that contractual devices can be used to evade moral law. A penalty clause therefore cannot be allowed merely because the parties agreed to it or because the civil law recognizes it; it must be judged by its moral substance. If the penalty is really a payment for the loan, it is usury. If it is genuinely attached to culpable non-performance or real injury, it may be allowed. The caution is essential because the penalty title is so easily universalized: every loan can include a due date, every due date a penalty, every penalty a calculation that produces surplus. If moral theology accepts such clauses without scrutiny, the usury prohibition is emptied from within. Soto's instinct is to prevent that collapse. The exception must remain exceptional, connected to a real wrong and not to the ordinary passage of time.

Navarrus — Martín de Azpilcueta (1492–1586), the Navarrese canonist whose Manual de confesores y penitentes of 1549 became one of the most consulted confessors' guides of the age — belongs to the same broader Salamanca world with a more developed sensitivity to money, exchange, and practical legal arrangements. His work shows late scholastic moral theology learning to account for the realities of trade without surrendering to them. The penalty clause could not be analyzed in abstraction from the commercial world in which it operated: merchants need reliable performance, delay causes real disruption, and a rigid refusal of every penalty could make contracts unstable. But commercial necessity cannot automatically justify creditor gain. The title must still be tied to a real external ground.

Here proportionality becomes central. A penalty may be valid in principle and excessive in amount, and when it far exceeds the injury caused by delay it begins to reveal a different purpose — no longer a discipline of performance but a price of credit. The amount must be measured against the harm, inconvenience, risk, or disorder the delay creates; it must not become a separate profit stream. Legal validity enters the analysis in the same spirit. Civil law may permit certain penalty clauses, set limits, enforce stipulated damages, recognize the parties' freedom to bind themselves — and the Salamanca and Jesuit authors ignore none of it, since moral life takes place inside institutions and jurisdictions. But civil law is not the final measure of justice. A clause may be legally valid and morally usurious. Civil law can guide prudence; it cannot transubstantiate extraction into justice.

Luis de Molina (1535–1600), the Jesuit author of a massive De iustitia et iure, develops this integration of moral theology and legal practice with particular care — attentive to real commercial life and, at the same time, to the dangers of fiction. A conventional penalty may be accepted where it corresponds to a legitimate interest in performance and where the civil law recognizes it within proper bounds. Yet everything turns on honesty in the stipulation. One must ask whether the penalty was meant to secure performance or to obtain gain. If the creditor set it because he reasonably wishes to prevent delay and protect himself from harm, the clause may be defensible; if he set it because he expects the debtor to miss the deadline and wants thereby to obtain surplus, the clause is morally corrupt. The same legal form can carry different moral characters depending on purpose, proportion, and context.

Molina also sharpens the question of advance agreement. Can compensation be fixed before any damage occurs? The matter is delicate. Parties often need to know in advance what consequences will follow breach, and stipulated penalties reduce uncertainty and prevent disputes. But advance agreement can also be the means by which interest is hidden inside the contract from the beginning, and the more automatic and yield-like the penalty becomes, the more dangerous it is. The solution is not to forbid every advance clause but to discipline it: proportionate; tied to a real possibility of harm or culpable delay; not set at a level that betrays an intention to profit; not the expected return of the transaction; not triggered by an artificial default; and always with a creditor who still prefers performance.

Leonardus Lessius (1554–1623), the Jesuit of Louvain whose De iustitia et iure of 1605 became a standard reference, inherits this world of distinctions and gives it systematic precision. Lessius is often associated with a more finance-aware moral theology — one that understands commercial practice, risk, money, and contract with unusual sophistication — and the sophistication cuts both ways. The more precisely one can distinguish titles, the more precisely one can construct evasions. Lessius stands near the edge where late scholastic analysis begins to resemble modern financial reasoning. For penalties, the anti-fraud rule holds unchanged: legal enforceability, commercial custom, and contractual sophistication do not by themselves establish moral legitimacy. The penalty must not be disguised interest; it must correspond to breach or loss; it must be reasonable in amount; it must not reward the lender for default; and it must not be one of several overlapping devices by which the same burden is counted repeatedly.

The Jesuit refinements matter most in their insistence that moral judgment account for context. A penalty in a simple loan to a poor borrower carries different dangers from a penalty in a commercial contract between merchants; a delay in a household loan may create little measurable harm, while a delay in a trade transaction can disrupt obligations, shipments, payments, and reputational commitments. No single rigid rule captures every case. But context does not abolish the usury problem — it only helps determine whether the title is real. And the contextual reasoning is easily misunderstood. It does not exempt commercial actors from moral law; it requires that the external burdens they claim be examined concretely. If a merchant's delay genuinely causes losses, costs, or disruption, compensation is more plausible. If a lender merely invokes "commercial practice" to justify expected yield, the title is false. The reality must be shown, not assumed.

The same holds for intention. The late scholastic and Jesuit authors attend closely to intention, but intention is no private escape hatch: a creditor cannot declare a good intention and thereby sanctify a predatory clause. Intention must be read through structure. Is the due date realistic? Is the penalty proportionate? Is default likely? Does the lender profit from delay, and is the penalty a regular revenue source? Does civil law treat the clause as penal or as an evasion? Do the surrounding circumstances show a desire for performance or a desire for gain? Commercial context, legal validity, proportionality, and intention must be held together, because each alone is insufficient — civil law may permit too much, intention may be self-deceived, commercial custom may normalize injustice, and proportionality itself can be manipulated if the alleged harm is inflated. The moral theologian must examine the whole structure.

This is the lasting contribution of Salamanca and the Jesuits to poena conventionalis: not abandoning the older rule but refining its application, so that "not in fraud" is tested across every dimension at once — legal form, contractual purpose, economic context, likely performance, amount, expectation, civil-law limits, and the lender's actual incentives. The result is a more mature doctrine. A conventional penalty can be licit when it is legally and morally ordered to securing performance, proportionate, attached to real harm or culpable delay, and not desired as profit. It becomes illicit when it is a way of guaranteeing surplus, pricing time, engineering default, exploiting vulnerability, or hiding interest inside a formally gratuitous loan.

For modern non-extractive finance the refinements teach granularity.
