This matters because it complicates the simple opposition between actual loss and foregone gain. Aquinas's anti-speculation principle remains intact: one may not sell what one does not have. But q.62, a.4 shows that not every future good is morally irrelevant. Some future goods may be sufficiently connected to a person that preventing them creates an injury. The future is not owned in the same way as the present, but neither is it always mere fantasy.

Later theologians seized upon this distinction. They asked: if Aquinas admits that preventing someone from obtaining a future good can require restitution, then why can a lender never be compensated for a profit that was truly prevented by a loan? If a merchant had a concrete transaction prepared, if the profit was reasonably certain, if the loan directly caused the transaction to fail, is this not analogous to preventing someone from obtaining a future good? Is lucrum cessans always speculation, or can it sometimes be a genuine injury?

This is the Thomistic tension: in q.78 Aquinas directly rejects the lender's contractual claim for profit not made, while in q.62 he recognizes that preventing future acquisition can be compensable. The later tradition will try to reconcile the texts, some emphasizing q.78 and maintaining the stricter line, others using q.62 to argue that the rejection applies only to vague or speculative profits, not to concrete, determinate, causally frustrated gains.

The distinction between vague and determinate future goods becomes crucial. Aquinas does not say that every possible future acquisition creates a right. If someone merely hoped to become rich, no one owes him compensation because that hope was frustrated; a merchant who vaguely thought he might trade someday has no claim. But if a future good was already morally, legally, or practically approaching him, the case changes. The more determinate the future good, the stronger the claim.

This is why later thinkers developed the idea of "virtual possession." A future profit might not be physically possessed, but perhaps it is so certain, so connected to an existing right or concrete transaction, that it is virtually possessed. The concept is dangerous, but it is not absurd. A signed contract for future payment, goods already purchased for resale, a crop already planted and nearing harvest: these are not the same as a vague hope of future commerce. A profit from a specific transaction already underway may be more than fantasy, even if it is not yet cash in hand.

The restitution principle gives later theologians room to think about such cases: justice can sometimes look forward, accounting for goods that have not yet arrived if they were unjustly prevented from arriving. But forward-looking justice must remain disciplined, not a general right to all imagined gains; the future good must be sufficiently proximate, determinate, and causally connected to the wrongdoing.

Here we can see why q.62, a.4 does not simply overturn q.78. In q.78 Aquinas is concerned with the structure of the loan contract itself, refusing to let the lender build a claim for unrealized profit into the mutuum and sell possibility as part of the loan. In q.62 he is concerned with restitution after wrongful prevention: the wrongdoer has caused another to miss something that, in justice, he should have obtained. The difference between contractual pricing and restitution is morally significant.

Aquinas may therefore be read as saying: do not contract in advance to sell expected profit as a condition of lending; but if someone wrongfully prevents another from obtaining a sufficiently determinate future good, restitution may be owed. This reading preserves both texts. It maintains the anti-usury structure while recognizing that future goods can sometimes matter.

The distinction also explains why later theologians often resisted upfront payment for lucrum cessans. If foregone profit is restitution for a gain actually prevented, it should not be charged automatically before the gain is lost: upfront payment makes the future profit certain for the lender even though the alternative was uncertain, transforming restitution into guaranteed yield. Aquinas's q.62 principle supports compensation for wrongful prevention, not the advance monetization of possibility.

The restitution framework also requires causality. Someone must have prevented the future good; it is not enough that the good failed to arrive. Many expected gains fail for ordinary reasons: markets move, crops fail, buyers change their minds, and human plans collapse without anyone committing injustice. Restitution is owed only where the loss of the future good is morally attributable to another's action. This requirement later becomes one of the central conditions for lucrum cessans: the loan or the delay must truly cause the foregone profit.

It also requires proportion. Aquinas's doctrine of restitution does not authorize unlimited recovery for every imagined consequence. Compensation should correspond to the injury. If the future good was uncertain, compensation may be partial; if the probability was lower, the valuation should reflect that; if costs, risks, and labor would have been required to obtain the profit, they must be considered. The person prevented from gaining should not be placed in a better position than if the gain had been pursued in reality.

If a lender is compensated for a lost commercial opportunity as though the opportunity were riskless, the compensation exceeds the true injury: he receives the imagined profit without bearing the original risk. That is not restitution but a transfer of certainty from the borrower to the lender, and Aquinas's restitution principle, properly read, does not allow it. It requires attention to the real shape of the future good that was prevented, including its uncertainty.

The q.62 principle also helps explain why lucrum cessans became more plausible in commercial settings than in ordinary lending. Merchants often operate with concrete future goods: purchase orders, contracts, inventory cycles, seasonal markets, scheduled obligations. Their future gains may be more determinate than the vague possibilities of an idle money-holder, and if lending interrupts such an operation, the claim of foregone profit may resemble wrongful prevention of a future good. But that is precisely why the merchant case cannot be universalized: the concreteness is doing the moral work.

A habitual lender, by contrast, faces the circularity problem. If his "future good" is simply the interest he would have earned by lending to someone else, then q.62 cannot rescue him. The future good must be just, and Aquinas's restitution principle cannot require compensation for being prevented from committing injustice. A foregone usurious gain is not a moral injury; it is a mercy. Any appeal to q.62 must therefore first ask whether the future good was licit.

This point has powerful implications for modern finance. A fund may say that by making patient, mission-aligned capital available, it gives up higher returns elsewhere. But the moral question is not only whether those higher returns were available. It is whether they were just. If the alternative returns depend on extractive leverage, predatory debt, exploitative labor, or risk shifted onto weaker parties, then their loss cannot automatically be treated as a compensable injury. The Christian tradition does not require borrowers to compensate investors for abstaining from injustice.

The q.62 principle therefore opens a path, but not a highway. It allows consideration of future goods, but only under the discipline of justice. The future good must be real enough, licit enough, proximate enough, and causally prevented enough to matter; the compensation must be proportionate and should not become a guaranteed price for the loan; the borrower's vulnerability must remain visible; and the lender's projection must not be treated as ownership.

This is why later Thomists could appeal to q.62 without simply betraying Aquinas: he rejected speculative lucrum cessans, they could argue, not every case of prevented gain; q.78 guards against selling unrealized profit as part of the loan, while q.62 allows restitution where a concrete future good has been wrongfully blocked. The reconciliation is not effortless, but it is intelligible.

Still, the danger remains. Once the door is opened, one must guard it carefully, because the language of "prevented future good" can be stretched: a business plan becomes a future good, then a forecast, then a benchmark return, then a portfolio target, then a hypothetical investment. If every projected return becomes compensable, then q.62 is being misused, and Aquinas's restitution principle has been converted into the very speculation q.78 warned against.

The challenge, then, is to hold the two texts together. Q.78 says: do not sell profit not yet possessed as part of a loan. Q.62 says: if someone is unjustly prevented from obtaining a future good, restitution may sometimes be owed. Together they produce a disciplined doctrine of foregone profit. Future goods are not nothing, but they are not automatically property; prevented gain can matter, but it must be concrete; compensation can be just, but it must not become yield.

This tension is one of the most fruitful parts of Aquinas's economic thought, because it prevents both rigidity and laxity. Against rigidity, q.62 reminds us that justice may need to account for goods not yet in hand. Against laxity, q.78 reminds us that the future cannot be sold as though already possessed. The two texts together require practical judgment.

For non-extractive finance this is exactly the kind of judgment needed. A modern capital provider may sometimes suffer a real opportunity loss by making patient, mission-aligned capital available, but the claim must not be automatic; it must be tested. Was the alternative concrete? Was it morally licit? Was it actually intended? Was the expected gain reasonably certain? Did this transaction truly prevent it? Is the compensation proportionate? Is it charged after the loss becomes real, or built in as a guaranteed return? Does it support the borrower's life and mission, or does it burden them with capital's imagined future?

Aquinas gives us no spreadsheet formula for these questions. He gives us something better: two principles that must correct each other. Do not sell what you do not yet have; but do not ignore a future good that has truly been unjustly prevented. The entire later doctrine of lucrum cessans lives between those two sentences.

sections.title: 20.4 ST II-II, q.77, a.1
paragraphs: The second latent opening in Aquinas appears not in his treatment of usury but in his treatment of buying and selling. In Summa Theologiae II-II, q.77, a.1, Aquinas asks whether it is lawful to sell a thing for more than it is worth. The question belongs to just-price theory, not directly to lucrum cessans. Yet this text becomes important because Aquinas recognizes that a thing may be worth more to its owner than to others because of some special loss or special use connected to that owner, and this principle later becomes one of the bridges by which theologians try to defend compensation for foregone gain.

The basic rule in q.77 is that exchange should preserve equality: a seller may not knowingly sell a thing for more than its worth in a way that injures the buyer, just as a buyer may not knowingly buy for less than its worth in a way that injures the seller. Buying and selling belong to commutative justice, and voluntary exchange is not morally self-validating. Consent does not erase injustice; if the price seriously violates equality, the contract wounds justice even if both parties appear to agree.

This matters for usury because Aquinas's whole account of financial morality is governed by equality in exchange. The problem with usury is not merely that biblical law prohibits it; the lender demands more than is owed under the structure of the mutuum, selling the use of money in addition to the money itself even though in consumable goods use and substance cannot be separated. He disrupts equality by charging twice. In q.77 the same concern appears in another form: a price must correspond to the thing's worth, not merely to the stronger party's advantage.

But q.77 also contains a nuance. Aquinas recognizes that a thing can have a special value to its owner that exceeds its common market value. If selling the thing causes the owner a particular loss, that loss may be considered. The owner is not always bound to part with the thing at the ordinary market price if, because of his particular circumstances, he would be harmed by losing it. The price may account for the loss caused by parting with the good.

This is not a general permission to overcharge; Aquinas is not saying that sellers may inflate prices because they feel attached to their goods or can exploit another's need. The point is more precise. Value is not always exhausted by common estimation alone: a good may be ordered to a particular use, serve a function for its owner, and impose a real burden in the parting. Where that burden is genuine, it may be morally relevant to price.

Later theologians saw the importance of this principle. If a good may be worth more to its owner because of the use the owner would have made of it, then perhaps money used in commerce may also have a special value to the merchant. Not because money as such naturally bears fruit, but because this money, in this person's hands, was ordered toward a concrete activity. If the merchant parts with it by lending, and thereby loses the use for which it was destined, perhaps the loss of that intended use can be considered.

This is the just-price bridge to lucrum cessans. The argument does not say simply, "Money could have earned profit, therefore the borrower must pay." That would be too broad. It says that a particular owner may suffer a real detriment by parting with a particular good, because that good had a concrete use in his circumstances, and that if this is true in sale, it may illuminate some lending cases. The key is concreteness: the money must not be treated as abstract capital with universal entitlement but tied to a real, intended, licit use.

A simple example can clarify the point. Suppose a farmer owns a horse worth a certain price in the local market. To another person, it is simply a horse. To the farmer, it may be necessary for plowing his field, and if he is required to sell it, he loses more than the generic market value. He loses the use that allowed him to cultivate his land. Aquinas's principle allows us to see that the farmer's circumstances matter. The value to the owner is not pure sentiment; it can reflect real economic and practical dependence.

Now apply the analogy to merchant capital. Suppose a merchant has money already intended for a specific purchase of goods, not imaginary but part of his ordinary trade: the profit is not guaranteed, but the transaction is concrete. Lending the money instead deprives him not merely of coins but of the commercial use toward which those coins were ordered, and later theologians could argue that this resembles the special owner value Aquinas recognizes in q.77. The money has a value to this owner because of what it was about to do.

Yet the analogy must be handled carefully. Aquinas's q.77 case concerns selling a thing that remains the seller's until sold. The usury case concerns lending money in a mutuum, where ownership passes to the borrower, and Aquinas himself, in q.78, remains cautious and rejects contracting for profit not made. Therefore q.77 cannot simply override q.78. It provides a principle, not a blanket permission. It suggests that intended use can affect value; it does not automatically justify charging foregone profit on a loan.

The difference between sale and loan is central. In a sale, the seller transfers ownership of a thing in exchange for a price, and if the thing is specially valuable to him, he may require a price that reflects the burden of parting with it, provided he does not exploit the buyer unjustly. In a mutuum, the lender transfers ownership of consumable money and is owed equivalent principal. To add a charge for the expected use of the money risks making the borrower pay for something external to his ownership. That is why the later use of q.77 must pass through the discipline of extrinsic-title analysis.

Still, q.77 opens an important conceptual possibility: value may be relational to use. One of the pressures on the old usury doctrine was the realization that money in commercial life is not always inert; it may be integrated into a merchant's operations, needed to buy inventory, pay workers, fulfill contracts, or maintain a business cycle. Depriving the merchant of that money may cause a harm not captured by simple repayment of principal.

The strictest anti-usury position might respond: if the merchant wishes to profit, he should trade or enter partnership, not lend. There is force in that answer, but q.77 makes the matter less simple. If the merchant lends because another is in need, and doing so deprives him of a concrete use of the money, the tradition must ask whether justice allows any compensation for the sacrifice. Refuse all compensation, and lending is discouraged precisely where it would help; allow too much, and usury reopens. The whole difficulty of lucrum cessans lies here.

Aquinas himself does not resolve the difficulty in favor of a broad title, but he gives later thinkers a language for discussing it. Special owner value becomes a way to talk about the loss caused by parting with a good, and combined with q.62 on preventing future goods, q.77 allows later Thomists to argue that some foregone profits are not merely speculative: tied to concrete uses, they may be treated, cautiously, as compensable.

Cajetan will later become especially important in this reconciliation, trying to harmonize Aquinas's direct rejection of lucrum cessans in q.78 with the more flexible principles of q.62 and q.77. The basic move is to distinguish between speculative future profit and determinate future profit. Aquinas rejects selling what one does not have. But if a profit is sufficiently determinate, perhaps it is not merely imaginary, and if a good has special value because of its intended use, perhaps the owner can be compensated when that use is lost. The reconciliation depends on the degree of concreteness.

Here "virtually possessed" future gain re-enters the discussion: a profit not yet in hand may be so closely connected to present arrangements (a signed contract, a shipment already arranged, a seasonal sale nearly certain to occur) that its loss is morally significant. Q.77 helps explain why. The present good is already ordered toward a future use, and that use can affect its value to the owner.

Yet this line of reasoning carries danger. The more broadly special owner value is interpreted, the more every owner of money can claim it. An investor says his money is worth more to him because he could deploy it in a fund; a banker, because he can lend it elsewhere; a private equity firm, because it has a target return; a venture fund, because every euro is part of a portfolio strategy. Suddenly the special use of money becomes universal, and if everyone has a special use, the exception disappears into the rule.

This is why q.77 must be read through q.78. Aquinas's just-price nuance cannot be detached from his anti-usury caution. Special owner value may matter, but it cannot become a general license to monetize opportunity. The owner must show a real and particular burden; the intended use must be concrete; the value must be proportionate; the claim must not exploit the other party's need. Above all, the payment must not function as a price for the mere use of money.

The danger is even greater in modern finance, because capital is almost always narrated as having alternative use. Money is never just money; it is portfolio allocation, dry powder, liquidity reserve, deployment capacity, option value. Every euro can be surrounded by a cloud of strategic possibilities. If q.77 is interpreted without restraint, all of those possibilities can be folded into "special value to the owner," and Aquinas's careful nuance becomes a universal opportunity-cost doctrine he himself would likely have resisted.

For non-extractive finance the correct lesson is more disciplined. Q.77 allows us to ask whether capital has a particular mission-aligned use that is being sacrificed; it does not allow us to assume that capital deserves market return. If a community fund has money earmarked for a specific project, and lending it elsewhere causes that project real harm, the special use may matter; if a steward-owned enterprise diverts reserves to help another and misses a concrete obligation, the special value may matter. But if an investor simply says, "I could have earned more elsewhere," the title remains weak.

The moral test must therefore be precise. What was the intended use? Was it concrete or generic? Was it licit and mission-aligned? Was the owner actually prepared to use the money that way? Was the borrower's request or delay the cause of the lost use? What was the value of the use, net of costs and risks? Is the compensation proportionate? Does the claim burden a vulnerable borrower? Does the structure still preserve the gratuity of the mutuum, or has it become interest under another name?

This returns us to Aquinas's central concern with equality. Just price is not whatever the owner can demand, not subjective attachment converted into legal claim, not the market power of the seller, but an attempt to preserve fairness in exchange. Special value to the owner matters only insofar as ignoring it would create injustice. It does not permit the owner to dominate the buyer or borrower through private valuation.

The insight is deeply relevant today. Investors often present their return requirements as objective, but from a moral perspective these are often forms of special owner valuation: capital is worth this much to me because of my expectations, obligations, strategy, or alternatives. Aquinas would ask whether those expectations are just, whether those obligations should be borne by the borrower, and whether the claimed value reflects a real burden or merely the owner's desire for yield.

Thus q.77, a.1 contributes to the debate in a paradoxical way, opening the door by recognizing that intended use can affect value and limiting it by subordinating all price to justice. The owner's special circumstances matter, but they do not rule: the buyer or borrower remains a neighbor, the exchange must preserve equality, and a claim of special value must be truthful, proportionate, and non-exploitative.

Read together, q.62 and q.77 explain why later theologians could not simply ignore foregone profit. Q.62 says that preventing someone from obtaining a future good can require restitution. Q.77 says that a good may have special value to its owner because of the loss caused by parting with it. These principles create room for a limited, disciplined lucrum cessans. But q.78 remains the warning: the lender may not sell what he does not yet have, nor demand profit from the loan itself.

The task of the later tradition is to hold all three texts together. If q.78 stands alone, lucrum cessans seems almost entirely excluded. If q.62 and q.77 stand alone, opportunity cost may expand too far. Together, they create a demanding moral framework. Future gain can matter, but only when it is concrete. Special value can matter, but only when it reflects real burden. Compensation can be just, but only when it restores rather than extracts.

This is the Thomistic bridge. It does not abolish the usury prohibition, does not bless modern interest, does not make opportunity cost automatic. It simply recognizes that the world is complex enough that some future-oriented losses deserve consideration. The bridge is narrow, and the guardrails are essential. Without q.77, Aquinas may look too rigid for later commercial life. Without q.78, q.77 can be misused to justify nearly anything. The wisdom lies in reading them together: a thing may have special value because of its intended use, but money does not thereby acquire a universal right to grow; an owner may be harmed by parting with a good, but the borrower does not thereby become responsible for all the owner's imagined alternatives.

The final question remains the same. Is the claimed surplus tied to a real, concrete, just, and proportionate burden external to the loan, or is it merely payment for capital's unrealized ambition? Aquinas gives later theologians the tools to ask that question. He does not make it easy to answer.

sections.title: 20.5 The Thomistic Tension
paragraphs: The debate over lucrum cessans does not arise because Aquinas is careless. It arises because Aquinas is precise in more than one direction. In his treatment of usury, he draws a hard line: compensation for actual loss may be permitted, but compensation for profit not made may not be contracted for in a loan. Yet in his wider treatment of justice, he recognizes that preventing someone from obtaining a good can require restitution, and that a thing may have special value from its intended use: future goods can sometimes matter. The tension is not accidental; it is built into the moral reality he is trying to describe.

On one side stands Summa Theologiae II-II, q.78, a.2, ad 1, the text later rigorists could quote with force: the lender may not demand compensation for profit not made, because he must not sell what he does not yet possess, and the profit, being uncertain, may never have arrived. To make the borrower pay for it turns a possible future into a present claim. In the mutuum the danger is sharpest, because the lender has already transferred ownership and is owed only the equivalent principal. The loan itself does not bear fruit for him.

On the other side stand the broader principles of restitution and just price: q.62, where someone who prevents another from obtaining a future good may owe restitution, and q.77, where a thing may be worth more to its owner because of the use to which it is ordered or the loss caused by parting with it. The future, these principles seem to say, is not morally irrelevant. A person may be harmed not only by losing what he already has, but by being prevented from receiving what he was truly on the way to obtaining.
This creates the central Thomistic tension: Aquinas directly rejects lucrum cessans as a loan title, yet indirectly supplies principles later used to defend it.

The tension can be sharpened further. If future goods can never be considered, q.62 becomes unintelligible: justice would be blind to real injuries that prevent a person from obtaining wages, inheritance, sale proceeds, or harvest. If future goods can always be considered, q.78 becomes fragile: every lender can say that the money would have been used for something profitable, every loan can be framed as preventing a gain, and every borrower can be asked to compensate the lender for a future that did not occur.
The later tradition therefore had to ask: what kind of future good was Aquinas excluding in q.78, and what kind of future good was he recognizing in q.62 and q.77?

One possible answer is that Aquinas excludes speculative future profit but recognizes determinate future goods. In q.78 the lender's claim is too indefinite: he might have made a profit, but the profit is not possessed, not certain, and not necessarily connected to the borrower's act, and to charge for it in the loan contract would be to sell hope. In q.62 the future good may be more determinate: someone was actually on the way to receiving it, and it was unjustly prevented. Restitution responds to a concrete injury, not a general possibility.

Another possible answer is that Aquinas distinguishes contractual pricing from restitution after injury. In q.78 the lender may not build compensation for expected profit into the loan as part of the bargain; that would turn the mutuum into a profit-generating contract. In q.62 restitution concerns a wrong that has occurred: compensation is then not the price of lending but the repair of an injury. The timing and the moral structure differ.

A third possible answer is that Aquinas permits consideration of special value in sale but not in the same way in the mutuum. In q.77, the owner who sells a thing may account for the particular loss caused by parting with it; but lending money for consumption is not selling a non-consumable good, for in the mutuum, ownership passes, use and substance coincide, and the lender is owed the equivalent principal. The special-value principle may illuminate certain cases; it cannot erase the structure of the loan.

These reconciliations are not evasions but attempts to preserve the whole Aquinas. The strict reading of q.78 captures something essential: lucrum cessans is dangerous because it sells what is not yet possessed. The broader reading of q.62 and q.77 captures something equally real: justice sometimes must account for future-oriented harms. The question is how to prevent the second insight from destroying the first.

This is why later commentators disagreed. Those who feared the collapse of the usury prohibition emphasized q.78. The lender's expected profit, they argued, is too uncertain to be charged; a loan must not become a way of securing gain from another's need; the lender who wants profit should enter a partnership or commercial transaction where risk and ownership are properly structured, not demand profit while preserving the creditor's right to repayment. Those who wanted to account for commercial reality emphasized q.62 and q.77. Some future profits are not vague possibilities: a merchant may have a concrete transaction ready, his money virtually committed, the gain reasonably certain. If he lends the money and thereby loses that opportunity, he suffers a real detriment, and compensation in such a case need not be payment for the use of money but for a lost good that was sufficiently determinate.

Both sides have truth. The rigorist sees the danger of fiction, protects the borrower from the lender's imagined future, and guards the gratuity of the mutuum. The permissive commentator sees the danger of ignoring real sacrifice, protects the lender from being made poorer by an act of assistance, and attends to merchant life. The tradition develops because both concerns are real.

The tension also raises a deeper question: when does commercial expectation become morally weighty? Not all expectations are equal. A fantasy of profit has little weight. A vague plan has more, but not much. A habitual business practice has more still, a specific negotiated transaction has greater weight, and a legally enforceable contract may have greater weight again. Somewhere along this spectrum, the future good becomes concrete enough that preventing it seems injurious. The line is difficult to draw.

Aquinas does not give a full technical answer, because his purpose in q.78 is not to construct a theory of commercial opportunity cost but to explain why usury is sinful. He therefore draws the line in a way that protects the doctrine: actual loss may be compensated; profit not made may not be sold. Later theologians, faced with more complex cases, returned to the wider Thomistic corpus to ask whether this line could be nuanced without betrayal.

Cajetan's later reconciliation will be especially important. He will argue that a future gain may sometimes be so determinate that it is treated as virtually possessed. In such a case, compensation for its loss may not violate Aquinas's principle, because the claim is no longer based on a merely possible profit. But this reconciliation depends entirely on the strength of the word "virtually." If virtual possession means a concrete, morally certain, causally interrupted gain, the argument has force. If it means any plausible investment opportunity, the doctrine collapses.

This is the same problem modern finance faces, though in different language. Investors routinely speak as though expected returns are real before they happen. A projected IRR becomes a target, the target becomes a promise, the promise becomes a contractual claim, and the claim becomes enforceable against the company. The future return, never yet possessed, becomes structurally protected. Aquinas's q.78 warning speaks directly to this transformation. It asks whether capital has converted expectation into entitlement.

But q.62 and q.77 also speak to modern finance. They remind us that not all future-oriented claims are fictional. A mission-driven lender may genuinely suffer if funds are diverted from a concrete project. A cooperative may lose a specific opportunity because repayment is delayed. A community institution may be prevented from obtaining a grant match, a property, or a contract because promised funds did not return. To say "future goods never count" would be unjust. The question is not whether they count, but how.

A non-extractive reading of Aquinas must therefore preserve the tension rather than flatten it. It must refuse abstract opportunity cost but allow concrete prevented goods to be considered; reject target-return logic but allow restitution for real commercial injury; protect borrowers from capital's imagined alternatives but also protect lenders from actual detriment caused by lending. It must distinguish market expectation from moral claim.

The practical test might be stated this way. The more a claimed profit depends on imagination, market benchmarking, or generalized capital opportunity, the closer it is to the danger Aquinas rejects in q.78. The more it depends on a concrete, licit, intended, proximate, and causally interrupted good, the closer it is to the restitution logic Aquinas recognizes in q.62 and the special-value logic of q.77.

The test does not eliminate judgment; it demands it. It forces the moral analyst to ask what kind of future is being claimed. Was it a dream, a plan, a practice, a contract, or a near-possession? Was it licit? Was it certain enough? Was it prevented by the borrower? Was it valued with humility? Was it charged in a way that preserves the difference between compensation and yield?

The Thomistic tension is therefore not a problem to be solved once and for all but a discipline to be maintained. Resolve it entirely in favor of q.78, and we risk ignoring real future-oriented harms. Resolve it entirely in favor of q.62 and q.77, and we risk turning every future possibility into a claim against the borrower. Aquinas forces us to live inside the difficulty, and the difficulty is a gift. It prevents moral theology from becoming either crude or captured: the crude view that every surplus is identical, the captured view that every return is justified by opportunity cost. It teaches us to ask what kind of surplus this is, what kind of future is being claimed, and whether the borrower is being asked to pay for reality or for projection.

In the later history of lucrum cessans, this tension will never fully disappear. Olivi will bring merchant capital into sharper focus. Fifteenth-century theologians will begin to admit foregone profit under conditions. Cajetan will develop the Thomistic reconciliation, Salamanca and the Jesuits will make the conditions more explicit, Lugo will fold lost profit into a broader theory of detriment, and Alphonsus will accept the title cautiously as part of the common teaching. Each step remains answerable to Aquinas's double witness: do not sell what you do not yet have; do not ignore a future good that has truly been prevented.

Between those two commands lies the whole moral drama of lucrum cessans: whether finance can recognize real sacrifice without enthroning capital's imagined future, whether compensation can remain restorative when it begins to look forward, whether the borrower will remain a neighbor or become the debtor of every profit the lender might have made. Aquinas does not let the tradition escape that drama; he teaches it to reason within it. The first to take up the reasoning in earnest was a Franciscan vowed to the strictest poverty his order knew, whose sharpest pages would be written about merchant capital.

chapters.part: Part VII — Lucrum Cessans: Foregone Profit and the Dangerous Birth of Opportunity Cost

chapters.title: Olivi and the Commercialization of Lost Gain
chapters.label: Chapter 21

sections.title: 21.1 Peter John Olivi
paragraphs: Peter John Olivi enters the history of lucrum cessans as one of its most fascinating and most misunderstood figures, and the facts of his life account for both distinctions. He was born around 1247 at Sérignan, in the Languedoc, where the vineyards run down toward the Mediterranean, and at about twelve he was given to the Franciscans at nearby Béziers. He studied at Paris, became the intellectual champion of his order's rigorist party — the men later called the Spiritual Franciscans, who held that the vow of poverty bound a friar not merely to own nothing but to use little, the doctrine of usus pauper — and spent much of his career paying for it. In 1283 a commission of seven Parisian masters censured a sheaf of propositions drawn from his works and had his writings confiscated. He defended himself so ably that the order's general chapter cleared him at Montpellier in 1287 and sent him to teach at Santa Croce in Florence. He died at Narbonne in March 1298, still in good standing. The good standing did not survive him. Pilgrims took to crowding his tomb in numbers that alarmed the authorities; in 1318 the tomb was demolished and his bones were carried off, no one knows where; in 1326 his great commentary on the Apocalypse was condemned outright.

It is one of history's neater ironies, then, that this man — vowed to poverty, censured, posthumously exhumed — produced the most searching medieval analysis of capital anyone had yet written. In the mid-1290s, living at Narbonne amid one of the busiest commercial landscapes in Europe, its streets full of merchants, notaries, and money-changers, he composed a treatise on contracts, usury, and restitution, the Tractatus de contractibus. In it he observed that money firmly committed by its owner's intention to a probable profit is no longer mere money: it carries "a certain seminal character of profit, which we commonly call capital" — quod communiter capitale vocamus. Capitale was the merchants' own word for working stock; Olivi was, so far as anyone has found, the first theologian to take it seriously enough to give it a definition. And because his books were banned, the definition traveled under an assumed name. A century and a half later Bernardino of Siena — the most celebrated preacher in Italy, and eventually a canonized saint — kept a manuscript of the treatise in his library and worked long stretches of it, verbatim and unattributed, into his own sermons on economic life. The Church had burned Olivi's books, and a saint retailed their contents to all of Italy anyway.

None of this makes sense if you imagine Franciscan poverty and economic sophistication as opposites. In fact the tradition that renounced property produced some of the Middle Ages' subtlest thinking about money, credit, ownership, and use, precisely because Franciscans could not take possession for granted. A friar who has vowed to own nothing must still eat, dress, and live somewhere, and so is forced to work out, distinction by distinction, what possession actually is — the way a man on a strict diet ends up knowing more about food than anyone else at the table.

Olivi does not matter because he "permits interest." That reading badly distorts him. He matters because he takes seriously a fact that earlier anti-usury reasoning could obscure: money in a merchant's hands may not be idle. It may already be ordered toward exchange — bound up in trade, circulation, and expected gain — so that lending it away deprives the merchant not merely of a sum of coins but of a concrete commercial use. At that point lucrum cessans stops being an abstract possibility and becomes part of a theory of merchant capital.

The key word is capital. Nothing here disturbs the strict Roman-law analysis that Aquinas made his own: money lent in a mutuum passes into the borrower's ownership; the borrower owes back the equivalent; the lender cannot sell the use of the money separately from the money itself. That structure stands. Olivi simply asks us to look also at the lender's side, before the loan is made. What was this money? Stored purchasing power, waiting for nothing in particular? Or merchant capital — money intended, arranged, and morally directed toward trade?

The answer decides everything. If the money was idle, the claim of foregone profit is weak; "money could always earn something somewhere" is less an argument than an appetite. But if the money was already inside an active commercial process, the claim grows teeth. The merchant may have intended to buy wool, grain, cloth, or spices; he knows his markets, has his suppliers, has arranged his transport, and holds a realistic expectation of resale — wool bought at Narbonne does not carry itself to the buyer. Interrupt that process with a loan and a gain may truly cease. The lost profit is not a fantasy pinned to abstract capital; it is the interrupted fruit of a concrete commercial vocation.

This is Olivi's distinctive contribution: he taught moral theology to see that not all money is economically situated alike. Money hidden in a chest, money lent to a poor neighbor, money placed in a merchant partnership, money committed to a purchase, money circulating through trade — the coins are identical, and their moral-economic forms are not. The legal category of the mutuum remains essential; it simply does not exhaust the economic story.

His Franciscan formation is not incidental to the insight. The poverty controversy forced the order to distinguish, with lawyerly precision, ownership from use, dominion from necessity, legal title from evangelical poverty. Men trained on those distinctions were primed to notice that the same object can be morally different depending on how it is held, used, claimed, or shared — which is exactly the perception Olivi brings to money.

None of it requires believing that money breeds. Money is not magically fertile, and Aristotle's suspicion of money begetting money, like Aquinas's objection to selling the use of a consumable, remains perfectly intelligible. What money can do is take its place inside a productive human activity — trade, with its labor, knowledge, risk, service, transport, scarcity, timing, and judgment. Profit arises from those activities, not from coins as such. But if money is necessary to the activity, then stripping the merchant of the money may strip him of the activity's expected gain.

This shifts the whole shape of lucrum cessans. The title is no longer "I could have earned a profit"; it is "this money was already ordered toward a concrete, licit, productive use, and lending it caused that use to cease." The move from abstract possibility to interrupted commercial form makes the claim stronger — and considerably more demanding. The merchant must show that the money really had this character. He must show intention, probability, and causality, and he may not simply wrap ordinary lending in the vocabulary of commerce.

Olivi's position is therefore bounded on every side. He is not saying that anyone holding money may charge for whatever it might have earned, or that the market return on capital is automatically owed, or that a poor borrower must compensate a rich lender for all the profitable uses the lender can imagine. He is saying that in commercial life, money may sometimes already have the character of capital committed to productive activity, and that interrupting such activity can be morally relevant. That is all — and it is a great deal.

It matters, too, that this came from a Franciscan rather than from some secular celebrant of accumulation. Olivi's commercial realism sits inside a theological world shaped by poverty, use, restraint, and the common good, and it refines the critique of avarice rather than abolishing it. A merchant's profit may be legitimate when it arises from real service, labor, risk, and socially useful exchange — and it remains morally judged all the same. Commerce is never self-justifying; it must be ordered, and the ordering is the point. Capital, in Olivi's account, gains no sovereignty. It gains the possibility of being embedded in morally meaningful activity, its legitimacy hanging on the activity, the risk, the labor, the intention, the justice of the exchange, and the social function of the trade. Money does not become fruitful by metaphysical right; it joins a fruitful process when joined to human work, skill, risk, and need-serving exchange.

This is also why Olivi is not simply a precursor of modern capitalism, however tempting some modern readers have found that claim. He does not reduce moral theology to market functionality, or mistake efficiency for justice, or hold that whatever capital can earn it is morally owed. He brings commercial practice inside a framework that keeps asking the old questions: whether the gain is just, whether the borrower is exploited, whether the transaction serves real need, whether the claim reflects truth rather than greed.

His importance for lucrum cessans, precisely stated, is that he made the merchant's case harder to dismiss. Aquinas had worried that the lender may not sell what he does not yet have. Olivi does not deny the worry; he asks whether some commercial gains are rooted deeply enough in present activity that their interruption should count as real detriment. A merchant's capital is not merely waiting. It is poised — aimed at an intended operation, standing between possession and realization. It is not the harvested fruit, but it may be the planted field, the purchased seed, the arranged transport, the negotiated sale. Its future is uncertain, but not empty. That narrow strip of ground is exactly where lucrum cessans lives.

Because the ground is morally delicate, Olivi's advance also multiplies the questions. If capital must be ordered toward trade, how ordered must it be — is general merchant status enough, or must a specific transaction be in view? What degree of certainty is required, and how should risk discount the claim? What if the expected profit depended on exploitative scarcity, or the trade itself was unjust, or the loan relieved someone in serious need? These questions cannot be waved away. Olivi made the problem more realistic and, in the same stroke, more complicated.

He also set the terms for every later debate about productive capital and sterile money. The old formula that money is sterile never denied that human beings can profit by using money; it denied that money lent in a mutuum gives the lender, by itself, a right to profit. Olivi's contribution was to show that money can be commercially fruitful without the mutuum becoming intrinsically fruitful. The fruit belongs to the activity, not to the bare loan — a distinction the next section takes up in earnest.

The distinction carries straight into modern finance. Investors like to say that capital is productive and therefore deserves a return. Olivi would force a more careful statement: capital is not productive in isolation; it becomes productive when embedded in enterprise, labor, risk, skill, infrastructure, trust, and social need, and the moral claim to surplus depends on participation in that productive process. An investor who shares the risk and contributes to the activity may legitimately share the profit. An investor who lends with guaranteed repayment and demands surplus anyway has walked straight back into the old usury question.

Followed honestly, Olivi points toward partnership rather than guaranteed debt. Where the provider of capital stays exposed to the outcome, profit can be shared because risk and enterprise are shared. Where the provider demands repayment regardless of outcome, takes collateral, shifts the downside onto the borrower, and still claims the profit the capital might have made elsewhere, the claim turns deeply suspect. The language of productive capital cannot sanctify risk-shifted debt.

This is where Olivi speaks most directly to non-extractive finance. A non-extractive investor should not ask, "What could my money have earned?" — too abstract — nor announce, "Because this enterprise may become productive, I deserve a fixed return," which claims the fruit while refusing the vulnerability. The better questions are Olivi's own, updated: How is the capital participating in the life of the enterprise? Does it absorb loss? Does it support real work? Does it serve production, community, and mission? Does it share upside only where it also shares downside? Does it remain subordinate to the enterprise's purpose? Productive use matters, and productive use is not enough. An investor who stands outside the risk while borrowing the language of participation has merely modernized the demand for interest. If money is to be treated as capital, its provider must accept capital's moral conditions: uncertainty, exposure, stewardship, and service.

The Franciscan in Olivi sharpens the point further. An account of commerce built on capital's self-assertion was never available to him; his had to be built on use ordered to life. Economic activity exists for provision, exchange, community, and the support of human vocation, and profit may be tolerated or justified where it serves those ends. The moment profit becomes the ruling purpose, the Franciscan critique returns in force: capital must be converted from master to servant.

His sensitivity to merchant capital also foreshadows the later Franciscan wrestling with the Monti di Pietà, the charitable lending institutions the order's preachers would help bring into being. Their questions belonged to the same moral world: how lending can serve the poor without collapsing financially, whether administrative costs may be charged and labor compensated, how an institution stays sustainable without becoming usurious. The Franciscans were never content with abstract condemnation; they wanted institutions that could actually serve vulnerable people without betraying the Gospel. And institutions, it turns out, can invoke this title too. A charitable lender may say that money lent to one borrower cannot help another; a mission fund, that capital tied up in one project is lost to a second; a cooperative bank, that delays impair its capacity to serve members. The claims may be perfectly real. The Franciscan discipline is to handle them as questions of service, not entitlement — the point being to sustain the institution's mission, never to maximize capital's return.

What emerges is an account of opportunity considerably more morally serious than the modern one. Modern opportunity cost is neutral and quantitative: the value of the next-best alternative, whatever it happens to be. For Olivi, the morally relevant opportunity must be real, concrete, ordered, and embedded in a just activity; the lost gain matters because it belonged to a legitimate commercial or service function, not because capital holds an abstract right to its best return. This may be one of the most valuable lessons the whole tradition has to teach. Opportunity is not morally neutral — some opportunities should be refused, some profits should not be made, some returns arise from injustice — and a Christian account of foregone profit must therefore ask not only whether the profit was likely but whether it was good.

At the same time, Olivi will not let anyone romanticize poverty into economic incompetence. Serving the poor takes material intelligence; sustaining institutions takes accounting; supporting trade takes an understanding of how merchants actually operate; building non-extractive finance takes a working knowledge of how capital moves, where losses arise, how opportunities are structured, and when claims are real. Franciscan poverty never meant ignorance of money; it meant freedom from money's domination. And that freedom is precisely what lets Olivi be commercially realistic and morally critical at once — granting that merchant capital differs from idle coin, while insisting that the merchant's claim is never self-validating. It must still pass the tests of justice, poverty, use, and charity.

In the development of lucrum cessans, then, Olivi stands for the title's commercialization in the exact sense: he tied foregone profit to the real operations of trade, and thereby made it plausible. He did not thereby create a universal right to interest. His achievement is not the liberation of capital from theology but the more careful placement of capital within it — which is why later thinkers could draw on him without becoming moderns. The questions survived him intact: Is the gain concrete? Licit? Intended? Causally prevented? Proportionate? Compatible with the borrower's dignity? Does it serve life? What Olivi left behind is not a formula but a reorientation. Do not think of money only as sterile coins, and do not think of capital as sovereign either; think of economic goods according to their use, their purpose, their risk, their social function, and their relation to justice — then ask whether the gain that ceased was truly part of a morally ordered activity, or merely the imagined fruit of capital's desire to grow. Every modern appeal to opportunity cost is that question in disguise. Olivi helps us ask it better.

sections.title: 21.2 Productive Capital Versus Sterile Money
paragraphs: The controversy over lucrum cessans turns, in large part, on a deceptively simple question: what kind of thing is money? If money is merely a consumable medium of exchange, then the lender who transfers ownership of it in a mutuum is owed the equivalent principal and nothing else; to charge more is to sell the use of the money in addition to the money itself, which is precisely what Aquinas condemns. But if money can become capital — organized toward productive commercial use — then the lender who parts with it may sometimes lose more than bare possession. He may lose a concrete opportunity for gain.

Olivi's importance lies in forcing that distinction into view and stating it with precision. He does not say that money is productive by nature; that would be crude, and it would collapse at once into a justification of interest. He says that money can be placed within a productive human activity. Money in a merchant's hands may belong to an ordered process — buying goods, transporting them, storing them, selling them, bearing risk, exercising judgment, serving demand. The money itself does not breed; but without it the activity cannot happen. It is commercially potent because it is embedded in a productive form of life.

That is different from saying all money deserves a return, and the difference preserves the older doctrine. The claim that money is "sterile" was never a denial that commerce exists or that money can be used profitably. It was a claim about the moral structure of the loan. The lender in a mutuum does not remain owner of the money, does not share the borrower's risk as a partner, and need contribute no labor, skill, or service; his claim is to the equivalent principal, and the loan as such entitles him to nothing more.

Olivi sharpens this by distinguishing money in abstraction from money in use. Money sitting idle is not money ordered toward trade; money held as reserve is not money committed to a purchase; money lent to a poor neighbor is not money placed in partnership with a merchant; money circulating as commercial capital is not money demanded back with a guaranteed surplus. The same coins take different moral-economic forms depending on how they are situated.

For lucrum cessans the consequence is direct. A lender who says only "money could have earned something" makes a weak claim; he treats money as abstract opportunity. A merchant who says "this money was already destined for a specific transaction, and because I lent it, that transaction failed" makes a stronger one; the profit that ceased was the expected fruit of a real commercial activity, interrupted. Call the first abstract productivity and the second concrete productive ordering. Abstract productivity — money can always be invested somewhere, so every use of money has an opportunity cost — tends toward universal interest. Concrete productive ordering grounds, at most, a limited claim for a particular foregone profit. Olivi's insight is thus expansive and restrictive at once. It dismantles the oversimple picture of money as always idle, and it demands attention to the actual form of this money's use: the title arises from the interruption of a real commercial arrangement, never from money's general capacity to earn.

The same distinction organizes the difference between capital and debt. Productive capital participates in an enterprise. It is exposed to uncertainty, contributes to an activity that may succeed or fail, shares gain and loss, and earns — when it earns — through participation. Debt commonly seeks repayment regardless of outcome, fortified by collateral, guarantees, seniority, covenants, and enforcement rights. A lender so protected does not participate in the productive activity; he stands outside the enterprise, holding a claim against it. If he then demands surplus by appeal to the productivity of capital, he wants the language of capital without the vulnerability of capital — and the claim is morally suspect for exactly that reason.

Modern finance blurs this line habitually. Capital is productive, runs the refrain, therefore capital must be paid. The scholastic question is sharper. Is this capital actually participating in production, or functioning as protected debt? Does it share the downside, or merely claim the upside? Is it subordinated to the enterprise's life, or senior to it? Can it be lost, or must the borrower bear the loss? Is the return tied to actual success, or demanded regardless of outcome? Productivity is not an aura surrounding capital; it is a relation among goods, work, risk, skill, time, need, and social usefulness. Money becomes morally productive only when embedded in such a relation — not because its owner could have invested it, and not because a spreadsheet assigns it a target return.

Three conditions keep the title honest. First, actual intention. A merchant cannot claim after the fact that the money could have been productive; the productive use must have belonged to his practical horizon before the loan, or the title becomes a story told by the lender to make interest look like compensation. Second, reasonable probability. The expected gain must have a basis in real commercial practice — goods available to buy, markets available to sell in, knowledge of prices, ability to transport, an ordinary expectation of profit — and it must be discounted for costs, risks, and possible failure. The compensable loss is never the highest imaginable gross return; it is the sober estimate of a net gain that was actually likely enough to matter. Third, licitness. Money may be productive in unjust ways — predatory lending, exploitative trade, speculation in necessities, monopoly manipulation, extraction from vulnerability — and profitability of that kind grounds no just claim. A foregone unjust profit is not a compensable injury. It is a temptation refused.

The Franciscan setting keeps the whole analysis truthful. The point of distinguishing productive capital from sterile money is not to enthrone the merchant but to tell the truth about economic activity: commerce can move goods, distribute scarcity, support households, and sustain communities, and commerce can exploit, hoard, deceive, and dominate. The productivity of capital is therefore morally conditional, judged by the ends it serves. Handled this way, the phrase "sterile money" keeps its exact truth. As a claim that money can never be involved in productive activity, it is false; as a claim that money, by itself and by the mere passage of time, generates no moral right to gain, it is true. The tradition defends the second claim, and Olivi prevents the first from smuggling in economic unreality. Money does not bear fruit by nature, but it can be seed within human activity — and the fruit belongs to the activity, not to the money alone.

Modern opportunity-cost reasoning runs the smuggling operation in the other direction. Because there is always a market, there is always an alternative; because there is always an alternative, there is always an opportunity cost; because there is always an opportunity cost, the borrower should pay. Olivi's framework interrupts the chain at its first link. It asks whether the alternative was concrete, intended, licit, and causally prevented, and it refuses to let abstract market availability stand in for any of that.

The difference shows plainly in practice. Suppose a mission-aligned fund holds capital specifically earmarked for a steward-owned open-source infrastructure company. The capital is diverted temporarily to rescue another portfolio company, and the first company loses a concrete grant match or procurement opportunity as a result. There may be a real lost gain or actual detriment here: the opportunity was specific, mission-related, and causally forfeited. But if the same fund says merely, "We could have earned 12 percent in a conventional venture fund," the moral character of the claim changes entirely. That is not Olivian productive capital. It is generic benchmark thinking.

The borrower's side follows the same logic. A borrower may be responsible for the specific harm caused by non-payment or delay, and may even, in rare cases, owe compensation for a concrete profit caused to cease. He does not owe tribute to capital's general capacity for growth; to impose that burden is to make the borrower pay for the lender's abstract financial identity. A non-extractive fund, for its part, may recognize real opportunity losses — money lent to one enterprise cannot serve another, and capital allocation has consequences — but must interpret them through mission rather than return. The question is never "What is the highest return we could have earned?" It is "What concrete good, consistent with our mission, was prevented — and is compensation necessary for justice or sustainability?"

Olivi's account also dissolves a false dichotomy that still cramps this debate. One can grant that capital can be productive without accepting that debt should carry guaranteed yield; grant that merchants suffer real lost profits without universalizing opportunity cost; grant that financial institutions need sustainability without licensing extraction from vulnerability. Modern usage makes these middle positions hard even to state — we call almost everything "capital," call all payments "returns," and treat loans, equity, preferred shares, convertible notes, revenue-based finance, grants, guarantees, and partnership shares as interchangeable items in a financial toolkit. Scholastic analysis reaches beneath the instrument to its moral form. Is ownership transferred? Who bears the risk? What is owed regardless of outcome, and what is contingent? What is being compensated, what is being sold, and what future is being claimed? The productive-capital distinction is, in short, a tool for moral diagnosis: it separates real productive participation from protected ownership — partnership from debt, service from rent, risk-bearing from risk-shifting, concrete lost gain from abstract opportunity cost.

It even illuminates so contemporary a creature as venture capital. On paper, venture investment looks closer to partnership than to mutuum: the capital can be lost, the upside is uncertain, returns depend on success. But the same investment can turn extractive through liquidation preferences, control rights, forced exits, founder dilution, and pressure to maximize growth at the expense of mission. The legal form of risk-bearing is not enough; the actual allocation of power and downside must be examined. A genuinely Olivian audit asks whether the investor's capital serves the enterprise or rules it — whether it helps the enterprise become more fully itself, supports useful production and work and community, and accepts the vulnerability of shared outcome, or instead imposes capital's need for return and bends the mission toward exit.

This is why the productive-capital distinction must travel with stewardship. Capital may be productive — productive of what, for whom, under whose governance? A factory can be productive and exploit its workers; a platform can be productive and undermine digital sovereignty; a fund can be productive and accelerate extraction. Productivity alone is not a moral criterion; it must be ordered to life. The Franciscan dimension returns here with full force. To recognize the productive use of money is not to worship growth but to see how material means can serve human purposes — and to guard against the means becoming master. The Franciscan response to that danger was never to destroy the means but to dispossess the soul of its domination by them.

Within the narrow history of lucrum cessans, all of this compresses into a rule. Foregone profit can be considered only when the profit belonged to a morally ordered use of capital — not a generalized claim of money's fertility, not a disguised price of time, not capital's demand to be protected from all sacrifice, but a concrete gain, rooted in licit productive activity, truly caused to cease. Put simply: sterile money deserves no yield, and productive capital may participate in fruit — but participation requires vulnerability, service, and a truthful relation to the productive activity. Where capital refuses vulnerability and still demands fruit, it becomes extractive. Where it shares risk and serves real work, it may become just. That is Olivi's lasting value. He moved the tradition beyond a flat account of money without letting money become sovereign, making room for commercial reality without surrendering to financial abstraction — and he left the decisive question standing. Does this claim to surplus arise from real, just, risk-bearing participation, or from the old desire to make money breed money while someone else bears the cost?

sections.title: 21.3 Franciscan Nuance
paragraphs: The Franciscan role in this history keeps surprising people. You would expect the medieval world's most radical apostles of poverty to be its least helpful interpreters of commerce, and the opposite is nearer the truth. Because the Franciscans took poverty, use, ownership, and money with total seriousness, they produced some of the most nuanced economic reflection of the age. They did not denounce commerce from a safe distance. They asked how money, property, credit, labor, and institutions could be ordered toward life without becoming instruments of domination.

Olivi's thought is a fruit of that discipline, not a departure from it. Franciscan poverty demanded a refined moral vocabulary: what it means to use a thing without owning it, to possess without clinging, to let goods serve need rather than status; where use ends and dominion begins; what separates administration from stewardship, and stewardship from exploitation. The questions are spiritual, and they are also, unavoidably, economic. They train the mind to see that legal ownership does not exhaust moral reality — which is why a Franciscan could judge a coin, a cloak, a field, a pledge, a loan, or a merchant investment not by its external legal category alone, but by how it was used, toward what end, under what conditions, and with what effect on the poor, the community, and the soul. That sensitivity catches distinctions a cruder moralism misses.

Read in this light, Olivi's account of merchant capital is a characteristic Franciscan performance, because it refuses two opposite errors at once. It refuses economic unreality. Serving the poor requires institutions; institutions require administration; administration requires labor. Lending requires bookkeeping, pledges, buildings, staff, risk controls, and accountability; trade requires capital, transport, timing, knowledge, and risk. A purely abstract condemnation of surplus, made in the name of purity, ends by leaving the poor no creditor but the actual usurer — an outcome Franciscan practical intelligence declined to accept. And it refuses to let practical necessity become an excuse for extraction. That an institution has costs does not mean it may seek profit from the poor; that capital is useful does not mean capital may rule; that a merchant may earn legitimate profit does not mean all profit is legitimate; that foregone gain is sometimes real does not mean every opportunity cost may be charged. Franciscan nuance consists in building institutions that can survive without surrendering their soul.

The later Franciscan defense of the Monti di Pietà belongs to exactly this moral universe. The Monti were charitable lending institutions — the first founded at Perugia in 1462, with Franciscan preaching behind it — designed to lend small sums to the poor against pledges, at charges far below what the usurers demanded. They ran headlong into a practical bind: administration costs money. If lending to the poor requires staff, records, storage, and buildings, someone must pay for them. Charge nothing, and the institution collapses; chase profit, and it betrays its purpose. The Franciscan answer was not simply "charge interest." It was more careful: certain payments may be justified as reimbursement for labor, expense, and institutional necessity, provided they remain ordered to sustainability rather than gain. That is not lucrum cessans in the narrow sense, but it is the same disciplined realism. Not every payment above principal is usury — and every payment above principal must be morally specified. What is it for? Whom does it serve? Is it proportionate, and necessary? Does it protect the poor or burden them? Does it sustain charity, or monetize vulnerability? Olivi's treatment of merchant capital follows the same pattern. He does not flatten all surplus into usury; he requires every surplus to correspond to a real commercial reality; and he sees the practical necessity and the spiritual danger at the same time.

Mission-driven funds today face a thoroughly Monti-like problem. They want to provide capital on just terms and to avoid extraction, and they have real costs: due diligence, legal work, governance, accounting, portfolio support, auditing, reporting, stewardship, administration. Ignore those costs and the institution may fail; turn them into a profit center and the institution drifts. The Franciscan question was never whether money may be charged, but what the charge is for — and whether it remains ordered to service.

Foregone profit poses the same test in modern dress. A non-extractive fund may sometimes give up a concrete, mission-aligned opportunity by deploying capital elsewhere, and may need to account for that in order to remain sustainable. But the moment it begins benchmarking itself against conventional venture capital or private equity returns, the logic inverts. The fund begins to treat extractive alternatives as its baseline. It begins to experience its own restraint as loss. It begins to ask borrowers to compensate it for not becoming what it was founded to resist. Franciscan nuance has a name for that condition: mission drift.

So Olivi's recognition of productive capital is no license to import modern opportunity-cost logic wholesale; it is a call to moral specificity. Which capital, and whose? Ordered toward what use? What gain — how certain, and how just? Interrupted by what act, and charged to whom? With what effect on the poor, the borrower, the worker, the community, and the mission?

Nor does poverty excuse anyone from competence. Evangelical poverty never required economic ignorance; if you mean to serve the poor without exploiting them, you must understand economics better than the exploiter does — costs, incentives, drift, accounting, risk, governance — or watch good intentions be captured by bad structures. The Monti made the lesson institutional: charitable lending required institutional design. The modern corollary is direct. A non-extractive fund cannot merely declare itself ethical. It must build structures that resist drift; it must distinguish reimbursement from yield, stewardship costs from rent, risk-sharing from risk-shifting, patient capital from disguised control, and mission lock from marketing language; it must know when a payment is needed to sustain service and when the same payment has become a claim of domination. The Franciscan tradition gives us permission to be practical and a command to remain poor in spirit — both at once, and neither negotiable.

All of this complicates the easy contrast between "Franciscan" and "commercial." The Franciscans were not simply anti-commercial; they were anti-avarice, anti-domination, anti-hoarding, anti-exploitation. Commerce, rightly ordered, could move goods, support households, and serve social need — but it was never morally autonomous, and the merchant could be legitimate only as a servant of real human use, never as an idol of accumulation. That is how Olivi could appreciate merchant capital while remaining Franciscan to the bone. Trade is a real human activity requiring skill, risk, and capital; its legitimacy depends on its ordering; and profit is justified, where it is justified, because it arises from service, labor, risk, prudence, and the provision of goods in ways compatible with justice — never merely because it is possible.

For lucrum cessans, this yields a rule modern readers still need: a foregone profit is not morally relevant simply because it would have been profitable. It must be profit from a morally ordered activity. If the lost gain would have come from exploiting scarcity, manipulating desperation, imposing unjust prices, or extracting from the vulnerable, it creates no just title; the amount of a gain is never separable from the moral quality of the activity that would have produced it. The point bites hardest in a world of financial benchmarks. An investor says: by choosing patient capital, I gave up the higher return I could have earned in conventional finance. The Franciscan reply is a question. What was that return made of? Leverage that pressured workers? Exits that destroyed missions? Monopoly rents, ecological damage, speculative bubbles, value extracted from companies rather than added to them? If so, the lost return is no clean moral baseline; it may be precisely the gain one was called to renounce. Not every foregone gain is a loss. Some are acts of liberation; some profits are not ours to seek; and some returns should be left on the table because the table itself was unjustly built.

Yet the same tradition refuses to romanticize unsustainability. A mission that cannot pay its staff, maintain its records, audit its books, or protect its assets may end by serving no one; there is a difference between renouncing extraction and refusing the material conditions of service. The Monti were controversial precisely because they stood in this difficult middle — charging small amounts so that charitable lending could continue, their defenders explaining why such charges were not usury, their critics warning that even small charges could drift. The tension has never resolved, because it cannot. Every non-extractive institution must keep asking what it must charge to remain faithful and alive, what it must refuse to charge lest it become extractive, and how to prevent sustainability from becoming profitability, and profitability from becoming domination. The Franciscan answer was never a single number but a discipline: governance, transparency, proportionality, and spiritual vigilance.

Olivi's contribution to lucrum cessans should therefore be placed within this broader institutional imagination. The goal was never to maximize foregone-profit claims but to recognize real economic sacrifice where justice requires it, while refusing to let that recognition harden into a theology of capital's entitlement. The merchant's lost gain may matter; the lender's administrative cost may matter; the institution's sustainability may matter — as servants of life, never as masters. This is also why the tradition keeps arriving at stewardship. Stewardship is not ownership absolutism but responsible use under a higher purpose. The steward does not ask, "How much can I extract from this asset?" The steward asks, "What is this for, and how must it be cared for so that it serves the good?" Applied to capital, stewardship means that money is judged by the life it enables, not by the yield it commands.

Seen whole, non-extractive finance is a contemporary Franciscan problem. How can capital be used without being idolized, institutions sustained without becoming extractive, risk shared without exploiting vulnerability? How can returns be limited, disciplined, and tied to real service — and accounting made a practice of truth rather than a technology of concealment? How can finance remain poor in spirit even while it handles money? Olivi does not answer all of these questions, but he helps us ask them. He teaches that money can be commercially meaningful without being sovereign; that foregone gain can sometimes be real without becoming universal opportunity cost; and — through the Franciscan world he inhabited — that use matters, poverty matters, service matters, and profit remains under judgment.

The nuance, finally, is this. Franciscan moral theology is neither anti-institutional nor pro-capital; it is practical without being captured. It can defend a small charge for the Monti without blessing usury, recognize merchant capital without enthroning money, admit lost gain under strict conditions without turning every possible return into a debt — and build systems of credit while remembering the poor borrower's face. That last memory is decisive. Technical finance forgets faces; it sees risk profiles, repayment schedules, expected returns, opportunity costs. Franciscan finance must see the person, the household, the worker, the community, the mission, and Christ among the poor — and if a title, fee, penalty, or lost-profit claim makes those faces disappear, it has already begun to drift. The Franciscan contribution to lucrum cessans is in that sense not merely historical but spiritual and institutional: economic intelligence joined to poverty of spirit, sustainability joined to restraint, capital joined to service, and every claim to surplus judged by whether it helps goods circulate toward life or traps persons in subordination.

Olivi's merchant may truly have lost a gain. The Monti truly needed to recover their costs. A modern non-extractive fund truly must sustain its stewardship. None of it gives capital permission to forget its place. Capital is useful. It may even be fruitful. But it is not lord.

chapters.part: Part VII — Lucrum Cessans: Foregone Profit and the Dangerous Birth of Opportunity Cost

chapters.title: Fifteenth-Century Acceptance and Thomist Reconciliation
chapters.label: Chapter 22

sections.title: 22.1 General Opinion Begins to Shift
