paragraphs: The history of lucrum cessans is not a straight line from prohibition to permission. It is a slow and uneasy movement from suspicion toward conditional acceptance. The title begins as a dangerous possibility: perhaps a lender may be compensated, not only for actual loss incurred, but for a profit that ceased because of the loan. Aquinas resists this in the context of the mutuum. Hostiensis opens the door narrowly. Olivi makes the merchant case more plausible by showing that money in trade may function as productive capital. By the fifteenth century, the general opinion begins to shift.

The shift does not mean the usury prohibition disappears; it means theologians and canonists become more willing to admit that foregone profit can sometimes be real enough to matter. The earlier suspicion remains; the title is still dangerous, still a potential pretext for ordinary interest. But the commercial world has become too complex, and the intellectual tools too refined, for every claim of lost gain to be dismissed as mere speculation.

The movement is gradual because the moral risk is obvious. If every lender can say, "I could have earned elsewhere," then the prohibition against usury collapses. The lender no longer needs to charge for the use of money directly; he can charge for the gain he claims to have lost by lending. The borrower still pays more than principal, the lender still receives a return, and the loan still becomes economically interest-bearing. Only the explanation has changed.

The fifteenth-century shift therefore had to be bounded. The emerging acceptance of lucrum cessans did not originally mean that all capital has a standing right to opportunity-cost compensation. It meant that some lenders, in some circumstances, could plausibly suffer a real detriment when a concrete, licit, intended profit was interrupted. This is why the title remained attached to merchant practice rather than abstract possession of money. The money had to be going somewhere. The gain had to have a shape. The lender had to be able to say more than, "I might have earned."

Fabio Monsalve's reconstruction of the late scholastic chronology is useful here: the titles did not develop at the same speed or with the same level of controversy. Damnum emergens was accepted earlier and more easily because it concerned actual loss. Lucrum cessans took longer because it concerned expected gain. Periculum sortis would be still more structurally explosive, because it concerned risk to principal and could be generalized even more easily. The titles form a spectrum from concrete damage to increasingly abstract forms of detriment.

The fifteenth century marks the point at which lucrum cessans becomes more broadly thinkable. The older caution does not vanish, but theologians begin to admit that opportunity cost, in a disciplined sense, may have moral relevance. A merchant deprived of capital may be prevented from carrying out a real trade; a lender who assists another may be sacrificing a specific commercial gain, not merely giving up idle money. If so, justice may require some compensation.

But the title remains bounded, because the tradition continues to distinguish concrete interrupted profit from abstract opportunity cost. Concrete interrupted profit points to a definite commercial act: goods to be purchased, a contract to be fulfilled, a seasonal market to be entered, an obligation to be met. Abstract opportunity cost points only to the general fact that money can be useful elsewhere. The fifteenth-century shift concerns the first, not the second.

The distinction is essential. Without it, lucrum cessans becomes a universal solvent: every lender becomes a merchant in imagination, every idle coin becomes capital in potential, every borrower becomes responsible for the lender's best unrealized alternative. The title ceases to be an exception and becomes the normal justification for interest. The late medieval authors who admitted the title were aware of this danger, and their acceptance was therefore accompanied by caution, conditions, and pastoral scrutiny.

Why did opinion begin to shift? One reason is the growing recognition of commercial reality. Medieval Europe was not economically static. Trade, banking, public finance, exchange fairs, and urban credit were all developing, and theologians could not simply apply village categories to every case. Money was often integrated into chains of obligation and trade, and to lend it could interrupt real economic activity. A moral theology that ignored this would be too blunt.

Another reason is the gradual refinement of the concept of compensation. Earlier authors had already accepted that interesse could repair loss. Once compensation for actual loss was admitted, the question naturally arose whether a lost future gain could ever be a kind of loss, and Aquinas's q.62 made it unavoidable. If preventing someone from obtaining a future good can require restitution, then the future cannot be excluded entirely. The debate shifts from whether future goods matter to which future goods matter enough.

A third reason is the influence of merchant-oriented thinkers such as Olivi. His recognition of productive capital gave later authors a way to distinguish idle money from money already ordered toward trade. The lender who had no concrete use for the money could not claim much. But the merchant whose capital was already practically committed to commerce could say that lending caused a real sacrifice. This did not make money naturally fruitful. It made the loss of a particular use compensable.

A fourth reason is the pastoral need to encourage useful lending without encouraging usury. If a person with capital is asked to lend in a way that causes him real commercial sacrifice, a strict refusal to compensate might discourage assistance, especially where the borrower's need is real but the lender is not wealthy enough to absorb every loss. The tradition had to find a way to distinguish exploitative profit from compensation for genuine sacrifice.

Yet the pastoral concern cuts both ways: a doctrine too strict may discourage lending, a doctrine too loose may exploit borrowers, and the movement toward acceptance occurs within this tension. The theologian must protect the lender from real harm without allowing the lender to monetize every possible advantage. The borrower must not be made the insurer of capital's ambition, and the lender must not be punished for charity. The title exists in the space between these two injustices.

This is why lucrum cessans remained more suspect than damnum emergens. Actual loss points to a burden already visible; lost profit points to a future whose reality must be argued. The moralist must ask: was the profit truly expected, or merely hoped for? Was the alternative use concrete, or generic? Was the gain licit, or exploitative? Was the loan the cause, or only one factor among many? Was the amount proportionate, or inflated? Was the title invoked before the loss was real, turning uncertainty into guaranteed yield?

The acceptance of lucrum cessans therefore represents not a capitulation but a refinement: the tradition increasingly recognizes that future-oriented commercial harms can be real, while insisting that such harms be proven, limited, and morally examined. The title develops through casuistry rather than doctrinal reversal. It enters through cases, not slogans.

Sant'Antonino of Florence and Bernardino of Siena will be important here, both of them working where practical moral theology, urban commerce, and pastoral concern meet. They do not simply discard the older prohibition; they try to guide Christians through a world in which trade and credit are unavoidable. Their acceptance of lucrum cessans in some circumstances reflects the growing consensus that real commercial sacrifice may be compensable, and their warnings against disguised usury preserve the older moral grammar.

Cajetan will later give a more explicitly Thomist reconciliation, arguing from q.62 and q.77 that some future gains are determinate enough to be treated as virtually possessed. The move allows later Thomists to accept the title under conditions while claiming fidelity to Aquinas; whether it succeeds depends on how carefully the conditions are maintained.

Conrad Summenhart also belongs to this transitional landscape, his systematic attention to contracts and extrinsic causes marking the move from medieval case discussion toward early modern moral-economic analysis. By Salamanca and the Jesuits lucrum cessans will be treated with increasing sophistication, the conditions clearer still: concrete alternative use, actual or virtual intention, causality, reasonable certainty, proportionate valuation, and avoidance of automatic upfront payment. The fifteenth century is the moment when the title begins to move from exceptional possibility toward broader acceptance.

The development should not be read as simple moral decline, though it is tempting to tell the story that way: first the Church condemned usury, then clever theologians invented loopholes, and eventually interest became normal. There is some truth in the warning; the titles could be abused, and some expansions did make the doctrine easier to evade. But the theologians were also trying to solve real problems of justice. Commercial sacrifice does exist, future goods can be wrongfully prevented, lenders can be harmed by lending, and institutions serving the poor can collapse if they cannot recover real costs. A moral doctrine that cannot handle these realities becomes brittle.

The better story is that the tradition was trying to keep compensation from becoming extraction as the economy grew more complex, and lucrum cessans was one of the hardest tests of that effort. Admit too little, and real harm goes uncompensated; admit too much, and opportunity cost becomes a universal right of capital. The shift begins a more permissive settlement, but the moral tension remains unresolved.

For modern non-extractive finance this history is close to home. Opportunity cost is no longer exceptional; it is assumed. Investors compare every deployment against alternatives. Funds benchmark themselves against markets. Philanthropic capital measures concession, patient capital is described as taking a discount, and mission-aligned finance is asked to explain why it does not pursue higher returns. The language of lucrum cessans has become common sense, even when the Latin is forgotten.

The fifteenth-century theologians would warn us that this common sense is dangerous. That capital could have earned elsewhere does not automatically create a moral claim: the alternative must be concrete, licit, intended, and causally prevented. A market benchmark is not a moral title, a target return is not a lost profit, and a hypothetical opportunity is not the same as a real interrupted transaction. The borrower should not be made responsible for the lender's whole landscape of unrealized possibility.

The shift also warns against excessive simplicity, though: non-extractive finance cannot simply pretend that opportunity costs never matter. Capital deployed in one mission project cannot be deployed in another. A fund that makes a patient loan may give up a concrete use of money elsewhere. A cooperative lender may suffer if repayment delays prevent service to other members. Some future-oriented harms are real, and the task is to distinguish them from abstract claims of return.

The general opinion begins to shift, then, because reality presses upon doctrine. Commerce forces theology to become more precise, and precision brings danger: once foregone profit is admitted, even narrowly, it must be guarded against expansion, tethered to actual commercial form rather than floating into the general atmosphere of capital markets.

The fifteenth-century acceptance of lucrum cessans is therefore a hinge in the history of moral finance, the tradition becoming more realistic and more vulnerable at once. The title is admitted because some foregone gains are real, and it remains dangerous because not every imagined gain is owed. What it recognizes, that future profit can sometimes cease in a way justice must consider, opens the path toward opportunity-cost reasoning, which, generalized, can turn every borrower into the debtor of capital's imagined future.

sections.title: 22.2 Sant'Antonino of Florence
paragraphs: Sant'Antonino of Florence belongs to the moment when lucrum cessans begins to move from narrow possibility toward cautious pastoral acceptance. He is not a revolutionary overturning the older usury doctrine. He is a bishop, moral theologian, and practical guide to Christian life in a commercial city, and Florence was not an abstract theological setting. It was a dense world of merchants, bankers, artisans, family firms, public finance, charitable institutions, and poor borrowers. In such a world, the question of foregone profit could not remain merely theoretical.

Antonino's importance lies in his ability to preserve the older suspicion of usury while recognizing the real pressures of urban commercial life. He understood that the Christian moralist could not simply say "no surplus ever" and leave it at that. Nor could he allow every merchant or lender to baptize profit-seeking under the language of compensation. His task was pastoral and diagnostic: to identify when a payment above principal corresponded to a real external title, and when it was merely a disguised charge for lending.

On lucrum cessans Antonino represents a cautious acceptance: foregone profit may be compensable in some circumstances, especially where the lender is genuinely deprived of a concrete use of money. But the title remains bounded: not ordinary interest, not the automatic price of time, not a general right belonging to every holder of capital. It applies where lending causes the lender to lose a real, licit, and sufficiently determinate gain.

Antonino works in a world where merchant life is morally complex, and merchants are not simply villains in his analysis. Commerce can serve real needs: it can move goods, support households, employ workers, and sustain cities. But commerce can also become avaricious, manipulative, and exploitative. The merchant's profit is not automatically unjust, but neither is it morally self-justifying. It must be judged by intention, moderation, service, risk, labor, and the common good.

That same balanced approach shapes his treatment of foregone profit. If a merchant lends money that he would otherwise have used in trade, and if the loan prevents him from making a real gain, the claim of lucrum cessans may be intelligible. The merchant is not necessarily charging because money was lent; he may be asking to be compensated for a commercial opportunity sacrificed. But the sacrifice must be real, the gain must not be imaginary, and the claim must not be a mask for habitual lending at interest.

Borrower need is important here: the loan usually arises because someone needs help, and the Christian lender is called to charity, not exploitation. Yet charity does not always require the lender to absorb every consequential loss, especially if the lender is not wealthy or if the loss is substantial and concrete. Antonino's pastoral realism allows room for this: the lender may be generous without being required to impoverish himself through every act of assistance. But the borrower's need also intensifies the danger. A desperate borrower may accept terms that a free borrower would reject, and the more vulnerable the borrower, the more carefully the title must be examined.

The key distinction is between sacrifice and revenue. If the lender truly sacrifices a concrete commercial gain to help another, compensation may be considered. If the lender structures the transaction so that lending itself becomes a recurring source of revenue, the title has changed its nature. Lucrum cessans in the strict sense is tied to a particular foregone gain; usury is tied to the lender's expectation that money lent should return with more. Antonino's acceptance of the former does not imply permission for the latter.

This is why habitual lending remains suspect. A merchant who occasionally lends money needed for trade may claim that a specific commercial opportunity was lost. But a professional lender who habitually lends for gain cannot simply say that he lost the opportunity to lend profitably elsewhere. That would be circular: his lost profit would be another interest payment, and the title would justify usury by comparison with usury. Antonino's caution preserves the older rule that the alternative gain must itself be licit.

The title also depends on intention. Did the lender actually intend to use the money in trade? Was the opportunity already in view, or was the lost profit invented after the fact? Intention alone is not sufficient (people intend unrealistic things), but without it the title has no moral substance: a person cannot be compensated for a gain he never actually planned to pursue. The claim must arise from a real orientation of the money toward use.

The opportunity must also be sufficiently determinate. It is not enough to say that money is generally useful. A merchant must be able to point to a transaction, a trading practice, a seasonal opportunity, or some other concrete use. The more specific the opportunity, the stronger the claim; the more general the claim, the more it becomes opportunity cost in the abstract. Antonino's world of pastoral cases is precisely where this distinction matters. Confessors had to ask what happened, not merely what could have happened.

Valuation is another difficulty. Even where the lost profit is real, how much was lost? The expected gain must be adjusted for costs, risks, labor, and uncertainty. The lender cannot claim the highest imaginable profit as though it were guaranteed, and if he would have had to bear risk in the commercial transaction, the compensation should not give him the profit without the risk. Otherwise the borrower pays for a purified fantasy of commerce: all gain, no uncertainty.

The point is central to the moral meaning of lucrum cessans: a foregone profit is not a guaranteed profit. If compensation gives the lender certainty that he would not have enjoyed in the alternative transaction, it may overcompensate him. Antonino's caution belongs here. To compensate lost gain justly, one must value the gain soberly; one must not transform a possible harvest into a certain debt.

His acceptance therefore remains pastoral rather than ideological: not a theory in which capital deserves return, but an answer to cases where refusing to compensate a real sacrifice might be unjust, rooted in the practical life of conscience. What did the lender actually give up? What did the borrower actually cause? What does justice require? What does charity require? Where does compensation end and profit-seeking begin?

The pastoral method is especially important in Florence, where commerce and religion were deeply intertwined. A merchant might fund churches, support charities, participate in civic life, and also engage in morally ambiguous financial practices. A poor borrower might depend on credit but be vulnerable to exploitation. Public debt might support the city but burden citizens, and banking might facilitate trade but also tempt Christians into usury. Antonino's moral theology had to move within this complexity. A blunt rule could miss reality; a permissive rule could bless sin.

His position is therefore part of a broader moral discipline. He accepts the title only when it remains tied to a real external burden: the payment must arise not from the loan itself but from the loss of a concrete gain caused by the loan, the claim must be proportionate and non-fraudulent, the borrower must not be made to bear speculative possibilities, and the title must not become a standard clause in every loan.

His approach also anticipates later manualist diagnostics. We can already hear the questions that will become more explicit in Salamanca and the Jesuit tradition. Was the alternative use real? Was it licit? Was there intention? Was the gain probable? Did the loan cause the loss? Was compensation proportionate? Was it demanded upfront? Was the borrower vulnerable? Was the same harm counted elsewhere? Was the title a genuine title or a pretext?

Antonino also helps us see why the shift cannot be reduced to moral laxity. Accepting lucrum cessans was partly an attempt to prevent another kind of injustice: making a lender bear a real and serious loss simply because he helped someone else. A Christian moral economy cannot depend upon pretending that costs do not exist. If lending causes real sacrifice, the sacrifice may need to be named. But naming sacrifice is not the same as monetizing every opportunity. The distinction is everything.

Mission-driven capital providers face similar questions today. A fund may deploy capital patiently to one enterprise and thereby lose a concrete opportunity to support another. A cooperative lender may suffer when delayed repayment prevents it from serving members. A charitable institution may have to choose between absorbing losses and remaining sustainable. In these cases, foregone gain or foregone use may be real. But the title must be handled in Antonino's spirit: carefully, concretely, and under the discipline of charity.

The danger today is that we begin not with concrete sacrifice but with market benchmark. We say: conventional capital could have earned this; therefore patient capital has lost that. Antonino would force a more careful analysis. What specific good was lost? Was the alternative morally licit? Was it part of the institution's vocation? Did the borrower cause its loss? Is compensation necessary to preserve the mission, or is it simply an attempt to import conventional return expectations into a moral structure?

Borrower need therefore remains central. A non-extractive finance structure exists to serve enterprises, workers, communities, and missions, not to preserve capital from all inconvenience. If the borrower is already vulnerable, a claim for foregone profit must be scrutinized with particular severity. Does the claim restore justice, or does it burden need? Does it help sustain a shared mission, or does it make the borrower pay for the lender's unrealized advantage? Does it strengthen solidarity, or weaken it?

His pastoral realism also suggests that one-size-fits-all answers will fail. A wealthy lender who can easily absorb a foregone gain stands differently from a small merchant whose household depends on trade; a desperate borrower differently from a commercial borrower seeking advantage; a concrete lost transaction differently from a vague market opportunity; a one-time act of assistance differently from habitual lending. Moral theology must attend to these differences.

Yet such attention must not become relativism. The underlying rule remains: surplus from the mutuum itself is usury, and extrinsic titles justify surplus only when they correspond to real external burdens. Antonino's acceptance of lucrum cessans operates within this rule. He does not let the title become a universal permission, and he does not erase the difference between lending and partnership, compensation and yield, charity and revenue.

The most important lesson from Antonino may be this: commercial realism must remain pastoral. It is not enough to understand markets; one must understand souls. The lender's desire can disguise itself as loss. The borrower's need can be exploited through consent. A contract can appear reasonable while training both parties in avarice, and a legitimate title can become a habit of extraction. The confessor's task is to see not only the transaction, but the formation of the persons within it.

For modern finance, this is a bracing thought. Financial instruments do not merely allocate cash flows; they shape moral perception. If every patient act is booked as foregone return, investors learn to see patience as injury. If every delay becomes monetized, lenders learn to prefer compensation over mercy. If every mission-aligned investment is measured against extractive alternatives, mission becomes a discounted version of greed. Antonino's pastoral lens asks what kind of people a financial structure creates.

In that sense his cautious acceptance is no technical permission detached from spiritual formation. It is a narrow act of justice in concrete cases, not an invitation to build an entire financial imagination around lost profit. The lender may sometimes be compensated, but he must still learn generosity. The borrower may sometimes owe compensation, but he must not be reduced to a revenue source. The institution may need sustainability, but it must not drift into self-preservation at the expense of its purpose.

Antonino therefore stands as a bridge between the earlier caution of Aquinas and the later systematization of Salamanca: foregone profit can matter, but only within a moral world of confession, charity, borrower need, merchant responsibility, and anti-usury suspicion. His acceptance is not modern opportunity cost but pastoral compensation for concrete sacrifice, and the distinction must not be lost. Lucrum cessans remains morally tolerable only while it remains a servant of justice; the moment it becomes the ordinary language of capital's entitlement, it has ceased to be Antonino's title and has become the return of usury by another name.

sections.title: 22.3 Bernardino of Siena
paragraphs: Bernardino of Siena belongs beside Sant'Antonino as one of the great fifteenth-century moral interpreters of commerce. He was a Franciscan preacher, reformer, and practical theologian whose economic thought grew out of the pulpit as much as the classroom. He wrote not as a detached theorist of capital markets but for merchants, lenders, artisans, civic officials, and poor borrowers trying to live faithfully in cities shaped by trade, credit, inequality, and temptation. His treatment of money is therefore both spiritually urgent and economically concrete.

This matters for lucrum cessans because Bernardino sees the same difficulty that runs through the whole tradition. On one side, Christian moral theology must condemn usury: the demand for gain from the loan itself, especially where another's need becomes the lender's opportunity. On the other side, it must not become so economically crude that it cannot distinguish exploitative gain from legitimate commercial profit, real service, labor, risk, and sacrifice. Bernardino's practical genius lies in holding those two concerns together.

As a Franciscan, Bernardino inherits a tradition deeply suspicious of avarice and domination, but also deeply invested in practical responses to poverty. This is the same world that will defend the Monti di Pietà: charitable lending institutions that offered credit to the poor at modest charges to cover administration and labor. The Franciscan concern was not merely to denounce usurers, but to replace them. A sermon against usury was not enough if the poor still had nowhere else to go. Moral theology had to become institutional.

That practical orientation shapes his understanding of commerce. Merchants are not automatically sinful; trade can perform a real social function: merchants move goods from places where they are abundant to places where they are scarce, bear risk, exercise skill and judgment, and support households and cities. Profit can therefore be legitimate when it arises from useful service, labor, risk, and prudent activity ordered toward the common good.

But Bernardino also knows that commerce is morally dangerous. The same merchant who serves the city can exploit scarcity, the same skill that enables distribution can become deception, the same credit that supports trade can become domination, and the same profit that sustains a household can become avarice. He therefore does not sanctify commerce simply because it is useful. He subjects it to moral judgment: profit is permitted where rightly ordered, but condemned where it arises from fraud, manipulation, hoarding, or the mere power to demand.

This is precisely the moral environment in which lucrum cessans becomes thinkable but dangerous. A merchant's money may be part of an active commercial life, intended for a trade, a purchase, a journey, or a resale rather than sitting idle. If that money is lent and the merchant thereby loses a concrete profit, the claim of foregone gain may be morally relevant. But such relevance depends on the same conditions that govern all legitimate profit: reality, service, risk, moderation, and justice.

In Bernardino's world lucrum cessans cannot mean that every possessor of money may charge for the return he might have earned; that would be the collapse of the prohibition. It must mean something narrower: a real and particular profit, arising from licit commercial activity, was prevented because the lender parted with the money. The title therefore depends on concrete merchant life, not abstract financial possibility.

His treatment therefore belongs to the fifteenth-century shift rather than to modern opportunity-cost theory: not a move from "interest forbidden" to "capital deserves market return," but from simple suspicion of profit not made toward a more careful recognition that some foregone profits may be real sacrifices. Bernardino's contribution is pastoral and commercial at once: he can see that the merchant who lends may give up something real, and he can also see that the language of sacrifice can become a cloak for greed.

The anti-abuse logic remains central. If a lender claims lucrum cessans, the moralist must ask what profit ceased. Was there a real trade? Was the lender genuinely prepared to undertake it? Was the profit reasonably probable? Was it licit? Did the loan cause its loss? Was the amount claimed proportionate? Was the borrower in need? Was the lender acting from charity, justice, or avarice? Without such questions, lucrum cessans becomes a loophole.

Bernardino's Franciscan context also keeps the poor borrower visible. The doctrine of lost profit can easily center the lender's sacrifice and forget the borrower's need. The lender says, "I could have earned." The borrower says, "I needed help." A Christian moral theology must hear both, but it must not let the lender's imagined alternative drown out the borrower's vulnerability. Bernardino's preaching against avarice keeps the human and spiritual stakes in view. Money is not an abstract asset. It is a social power that can either serve or enslave.

The point mattered because urban credit was growing complex. Poor people borrowed for survival, merchants for trade, cities for public finance; families pledged goods, and money circulated through formal and informal networks. In such a world usury could hide in many forms: penalties, exchange contracts, delayed payment, fictive damages, lost-profit claims. The lender did not need to be crude. Bernardino's moral theology is therefore vigilant. It knows that sin can become sophisticated.

The vigilance does not prevent him from recognizing real costs; this is the Franciscan balance. Lending to the poor requires administration; trade requires risk; public utility may require moderate profit; a merchant may be harmed by losing the chance to trade. But every such claim must remain subordinate to charity and justice. The purpose of economic life is not the multiplication of claims, but the right ordering of use. Money must circulate toward human need, not accumulate as power over the needy.

A modern fund may claim that it gives up market return by providing patient capital, and that may sometimes be true in a practical sense. Bernardino would not stop there. He would ask whether the foregone return was tied to a concrete, licit, mission-compatible alternative. He would ask whether the borrower should bear that cost, whether the claim helps sustain service or simply imports conventional finance's expectations into a moral project, and whether the fund's language of sacrifice has become a form of entitlement.

The distinction between sustainability and extraction is central. Support for practical institutions does not mean blessing unrestricted return. The Monti di Pietà could charge to cover costs because without such charges they could not serve the poor. But cost recovery is not the same as profit maximization, administration is not rent-seeking, and stewardship labor is not capital's dominion. The moral question is always: what is the payment for, and whom does it serve?

In the case of lucrum cessans, the same logic applies. A payment for foregone profit may be justified if it prevents the lender from being unjustly harmed by a concrete act of assistance. But it becomes extractive if it gives capital a general claim to compensation for every unrealized return. The first protects real sacrifice. The second protects capital from having to love. Bernardino's whole moral world stands behind that difference.

The spiritual language is not decorative; it belongs at the center of the doctrine. Usury was condemned not merely for violating a technical rule about consumable goods but for forming the soul wrongly: it trained the lender to see another's need as revenue, the borrower to accept subordination as necessity, and the community to tolerate a world in which money breeds power. Bernardino's preaching attacks precisely this deformation.

Lucrum cessans risks a subtler deformation. It trains the lender to see another's need as the cause of his missed opportunity: the borrower becomes the person who prevented profit, the act of lending becomes a sacrifice to be priced, and charity becomes a negotiable loss. Again, this does not mean foregone profit is never real; it means the title must be held under spiritual discipline, without which it transforms the imagination.

This is why Bernardino's practical commerce is never separated from moral exhortation. He does not merely classify contracts; he preaches conversion. The merchant must examine his conscience. Is he seeking moderate gain for household and public utility, or is he driven by avarice? Is he serving real needs, or manipulating them? Is he bearing risk, or shifting it? Is he using money as a tool, or being used by it? These questions are as relevant to lucrum cessans as to ordinary trade.

His thought also helps us distinguish merchant profit from lender profit. A merchant may earn profit by buying, transporting, storing, and selling goods; his gain can arise from labor, knowledge, risk, and service. A lender, in a mutuum, simply transfers ownership of money and receives a claim to equivalent repayment. The foregone-profit title becomes plausible only when the lender's money was otherwise embedded in merchant activity. If there is no such activity, the claim loses force. This preserves the distinction between commerce and usury.

Modern finance often blurs this distinction by calling all money "capital" and all capital "productive." Bernardino's world would force us to ask what the capital is actually doing. Is it enabling work, trade, and service, or merely commanding payment? Is the capital provider sharing risk, or enforcing repayment? Is the return connected to productive participation, or to bargaining power? These questions prevent the language of productivity from becoming an automatic justification for return.

Bernardino also belongs to the broader Franciscan pattern of institutional moral imagination, whose goal is not merely to identify sinful contracts after the fact but to build better alternatives. The Monti di Pietà are the clearest example: institutions designed to provide credit without usury, to serve the poor while covering necessary costs, to discipline lending through public and charitable purpose. This is deeply relevant to the modern project of non-extractive capital. Critique is not enough. One must design institutions that make just finance possible.

Such institutions require rules against drift, and Bernardino would have understood this. A charitable institution can become self-serving, a cost-recovery charge can grow into a revenue stream, a moral exception can become a business model, and a title admitted for necessity can become habitual entitlement. The more practical the institution, the more it needs spiritual and governance safeguards.

For lucrum cessans, the drift pattern is especially clear. First, compensation is admitted for a concrete lost merchant profit; then for regular commercial opportunity; then for being deprived of money; then it is benchmarked against market return; then it becomes ordinary interest. Bernardino's caution belongs at the beginning of this chain. He does not deny the first step. But his whole moral world warns against the later ones.

This makes his position more subtle than either rigorist rejection or modern acceptance. He does not treat all foregone gain as fiction, nor all foregone gain as compensable. He asks whether the gain was real enough, just enough, and causally interrupted enough to matter. He asks whether compensation would serve justice or feed avarice, and whether the borrower's need has become the lender's opportunity.

For modern non-extractive finance, Bernardino's lesson might be stated this way: do not let opportunity cost become the grammar of mission. A mission-driven fund may need to account for real sacrifices, recover costs, and avoid harming itself in ways that prevent future service. But the moment it begins to measure itself primarily against the profits it could have earned elsewhere, it is in danger. It has begun to let the wider market define its imagination.

A Bernardino-inspired fund would ask different questions. What is necessary for sustainability? What is sufficient for stewardship? What charges correspond to real work, risk, loss, or foregone mission-aligned use? What returns would compromise the borrower's mission? What structures protect the vulnerable? What prevents managers or investors from benefiting from distress? What keeps capital in service rather than command?

Such questions show why Bernardino's contribution is not merely historical: he gives us a way to think about finance as moral formation, where the issue is not only whether a payment can be justified but what kind of economic community it creates. Does it create trust, mutual service, responsible trade, and protection for the poor? Or does it create a world in which every relationship is measured by lost yield?

In the development of lucrum cessans, then, Bernardino represents practical commerce under Franciscan judgment: he recognizes the merchant's reality and that lost profit can sometimes be a real sacrifice, but refuses to let the recognition become capital's automatic defense. The title must remain concrete, limited, and spiritually suspect, used to protect justice rather than to normalize gain from lending.

That is why he belongs in this chapter. The fifteenth century begins to accept lucrum cessans more widely, but its best representatives treat it not as a loophole but as a dangerous instrument, used rightly only by those willing to remain under judgment. The merchant may be compensated for real sacrifice, and the Christian must still be converted from avarice.

sections.title: 22.4 Cajetan
paragraphs: Cajetan (Tommaso de Vio) stands at one of the most important points of reconciliation in the history of lucrum cessans. He is a Thomist, deeply committed to reading Aquinas carefully. Yet he also belongs to a later world in which the conditional acceptance of foregone profit had become increasingly difficult to ignore. His task, therefore, is not to choose between Aquinas and commercial reality but to ask whether a properly bounded doctrine of lucrum cessans can be reconciled with Aquinas himself.

The task is not easy, because Aquinas's direct statement in Summa Theologiae II-II, q.78, a.2, ad 1 appears severe: compensation may be contracted for loss incurred but not for profit not made, since the lender may not sell what he does not yet have. A future profit is uncertain and may never be obtained. To charge for it in a loan risks treating a merely possible gain as the lender's property, making the borrower pay for a future that did not yet belong to the lender.

Cajetan cannot simply erase this text; as a Thomist, he must respect it. But he reads it in light of Aquinas's wider doctrine of justice: II-II, q.62, where preventing another from obtaining a future good may require restitution, and II-II, q.77, where a thing may have special value to its owner because of its intended use or the loss caused by parting with it. These texts do not directly authorize lucrum cessans as a loan title, but they complicate a simple rejection.

The reconciliation depends on a distinction between speculative and determinate future gain. Aquinas rejects the sale of a profit not yet possessed because such a profit may be merely possible. But what if it is not? What if it is concrete, proximate, intended, morally licit, and reasonably certain? What if the lender, especially a merchant, was already ordered toward a specific transaction, and the loan caused that transaction to fail? In such a case, Cajetan can ask whether the future gain has enough moral solidity to be treated as a real detriment when it is prevented.

This is the idea sometimes described as virtual possession. A profit may not yet be in the lender's hands, but it may be so closely connected to present arrangements that its loss is not mere disappointment. It is more like being prevented from receiving a good already on its way. A merchant with a specific purchase arranged, a known resale opportunity, a seasonal market, or a contract in view stands differently from an idle lender imagining vague returns. The gain has not yet arrived, but it may be virtually present in the structure of the activity.

The strength of the reconciliation depends entirely on how strictly virtual possession is understood. If it means a concrete and causally interrupted gain, the argument has real force. If it means any plausible financial opportunity, the doctrine collapses. Cajetan's task is to preserve Aquinas's anti-speculation principle while admitting that some expected gains are not merely speculative. The line is narrow.

The first condition is concreteness. A lender cannot claim lucrum cessans simply because money could have been useful elsewhere. The foregone profit must be tied to a determinate use: some identifiable commercial act, obligation, or opportunity that was interrupted. The more general the claim, the weaker the title. "I could always have earned something" is not Cajetan's case. "I was about to complete this specific transaction, and lending prevented it" is much closer.

The second condition is intention. The lender must genuinely have intended to pursue the profitable use. A lost profit invented after the loan cannot justify compensation. The money must already have been practically ordered toward the use. This need not always mean a signed contract, but it does require more than imagination: the lender's ordinary activity, concrete plans, preparations, and circumstances matter. Without intention, the supposed profit was never truly in the lender's path.

The third condition is licitness. The foregone gain must have been morally legitimate. A lender cannot claim compensation because he refrained from usury, fraud, exploitation, or any other unjust gain. This point is sometimes underdeveloped in modern opportunity-cost reasoning, but it is essential in a Christian moral framework. Only a justly obtainable good can become the basis for restitution or compensation. A sinful gain that ceased is not a compensable injury.

The fourth condition is causality. The loan or the borrower's action must truly cause the profit to cease. It is not enough that the lender failed to earn during the loan; the market might have changed, the lender might have lacked skill, the goods might not have sold. The claimed gain must be lost because the money was lent or because repayment was delayed. Without causality, the borrower is made responsible for the lender's general business uncertainty.

The fifth condition is proportionate valuation. Even if the profit was concrete and causally lost, compensation must not exceed the true value of the lost gain. Costs must be deducted, risk considered, the chance of failure acknowledged, and the labor and time required for the alternative transaction not ignored. A projected gross return is not a compensable net profit, and a possible profit is not a guaranteed profit. Compensation must not place the lender in a better position than the one he would likely have occupied.

The last condition is especially important. Compensated for the full expected profit without bearing the risk that would have accompanied the transaction, the lender receives a purified version of the future: gain without danger, commercial fruit without commercial exposure. That is not restitution but overcompensation. Cajetan's reconciliation must therefore remain realistic about uncertainty. Virtual possession does not mean absolute possession.

Through this framework, Cajetan can claim fidelity to Aquinas while allowing a limited lucrum cessans. Where the future gain remains speculative, q.78's rejection holds; where it is determinate enough to be treated as virtually possessed, q.62 and q.77 open the possibility of compensation.

The reconciliation is elegant, but dangerous. Its danger lies in the elasticity of "determinate enough." Once theologians admit that future gain can be virtually possessed, later users may stretch the concept. A merchant's arranged transaction becomes a merchant's ordinary practice, ordinary practice becomes general commercial opportunity, commercial opportunity becomes market return, market return becomes target return, and target return becomes a contractual claim. At the end of the chain, Aquinas's warning has been swallowed by the very principle meant to nuance it.

Cajetan's argument therefore requires strict guardrails. The title must not be universalized or treated as belonging to every lender; it must not be applied automatically, paid simply because time has passed, or reduced to a percentage of principal; it must not be invoked where the alternative is vague; and it must not become the ordinary price of money.

This is why Cajetan matters so much for the later tradition: he gives Thomists a way to accept lucrum cessans without openly contradicting Aquinas, and he shows that the acceptance must stay disciplined to remain Thomistic. The title is not a triumph over Aquinas but an attempt to apply his whole doctrine of justice to complex cases.

The reconciliation also clarifies the difference between foregone profit and opportunity cost. Modern opportunity cost begins with the best alternative available to capital; Cajetan begins with a concrete good sufficiently connected to the lender that its prevention causes real detriment. These are not the same. Opportunity cost is broad, comparative, and often abstract; it belongs easily to portfolio theory. Cajetan's lucrum cessans is narrow, causal, and concrete; it belongs to restitution and commutative justice.

The distinction is not merely technical. It changes the moral imagination. If the baseline is opportunity cost, the lender asks, "What could I have earned elsewhere?" If the baseline is Cajetan's disciplined lucrum cessans, the lender asks, "What concrete, just gain was actually prevented?" The first question tends toward entitlement, the second toward proof. The first centers capital's alternatives, the second centers justice in the specific case.

For modern non-extractive finance, Cajetan offers both a resource and a warning. The resource is that not every future-oriented claim must be dismissed. A patient capital provider may sometimes give up a concrete mission-aligned opportunity; a cooperative fund may be harmed if delayed repayment prevents it from financing another specific member; a steward-owned enterprise may lose a definite project because funds were unavailable. Such cases may deserve compensation, or at least moral recognition. The warning is that these cases must not become generic benchmark logic. A non-extractive fund cannot simply say, "We could have earned more in the market, so this borrower must compensate us." That is not Cajetan's lucrum cessans but abstraction. The alternative must be concrete and morally ordered, part of the fund's real vocation, not an extractive opportunity disguised as a baseline, and causally prevented by the transaction at hand.

Cajetan's emphasis also has implications for contract design. If foregone profit is truly concrete and determinate, a contract should be able to describe it without hiding behind vague formulas. What opportunity is being preserved? What loss would occur if repayment is delayed? How will the loss be measured? What costs and risks are deducted? When is the compensation triggered? Is it capped? Is it subject to proof? Is it waived in hardship? Does the lender benefit from default? These design questions translate Thomistic reconciliation into modern governance.

They also prevent the title from being converted into target IRR. If a fund begins with a desired return and then names that return lucrum cessans, the moral order has been reversed. Cajetan begins with the concrete lost good, not the desired yield. The amount follows from the injury; it is not imposed by portfolio expectation. Any non-extractive use of lucrum cessans must preserve this order.

Cajetan also deepens the relation between lucrum cessans and damnum emergens. Actual loss is safer because it deals with damage already arisen; foregone profit is more dangerous because it deals with gain not yet realized. But if a future gain is virtually possessed, its loss begins to resemble actual detriment. This is the conceptual move that later authors, especially Lugo, will expand. Lost gain becomes "loss of a sort," and the boundary between the titles becomes less sharp.

The expansion can be useful, and perilous. If too many future gains are treated as virtually possessed, the category of loss becomes enormous. A lender can always say he lost certainty, liquidity, optionality, or return. Cajetan's narrow reconciliation can become a broad theory of capital's grievance. This is why his argument must remain anchored in the concrete.

Cajetan's broader theological importance is that he shows how a tradition develops without simply reversing itself: the later acceptance of lucrum cessans can be read not as betrayal but as an attempt to apply Aquinas's principles to cases he did not fully systematize. But development is faithful only if it preserves the underlying moral structure. If the development abolishes the distinction between compensation and gain from the loan itself, it is no longer development. It is contradiction.

The reconciliation therefore stands or falls by its fidelity to the anti-usury core: no surplus demanded from the mutuum itself, no sale of a merely possible future, no borrower made liable for the lender's whole imagined field of alternatives. Compensation is possible only where a concrete, just, causally interrupted gain is sufficiently determinate to count as real detriment.

That formulation is demanding, but it is exactly what makes the title morally usable: it accounts for real commercial sacrifice without surrendering to financial abstraction, honoring the rule that one must not sell what one does not have while recognizing that some goods not yet possessed may be close enough to possession that their unjust prevention matters.

Cajetan is in this sense not merely a commentator but a hinge between Aquinas and the early modern moral economists. He gives the later tradition a Thomistic path toward conditional acceptance. Salamanca, Molina, Lessius, Lugo, and Alphonsus will all move in a world where lucrum cessans has become more acceptable under conditions, and Cajetan helps explain how that acceptance could be seen as continuous with Aquinas rather than simply opposed to him.

But the final lesson remains caution. A bridge is not a highway. Cajetan builds a narrow bridge from actual justice to foregone profit. It can carry concrete cases; it cannot carry the entire weight of modern opportunity-cost finance without collapsing. The future may sometimes be close enough to matter, and it is not therefore owned by capital.

sections.title: 22.5 Conrad Summenhart
paragraphs: Conrad Summenhart belongs to the transition from late medieval moral theology into the more systematic contract analysis of the early modern period. Born at Calw in the Black Forest around 1455, he taught theology at Tübingen until his death in 1502, and his seven-part treatise on contracts (the Opus septipartitum de contractibus, printed at Hagenau in 1500) treats the loan, obligation, and usury with a jurist's appetite for order. He stands after the first major openings toward lucrum cessans, after the practical Franciscan and Dominican engagements with commerce, and before the full flowering of Salamanca and Jesuit moral theology. His importance lies in helping gather the scattered cases, titles, and distinctions into a more comprehensive treatment of contracts and extrinsic causes.

By the time Summenhart writes, the question is no longer whether the usury prohibition exists (that remains foundational), nor simply whether actual loss can be compensated (damnum emergens is already comparatively secure). The harder questions concern the expanding borderlands: foregone profit, risk, deprivation of money, exchange contracts, merchant practice, and the many ways in which a payment above principal might be justified, or disguised. Summenhart belongs to this world of classification.

The classificatory work matters because the more complex the economy becomes, the easier it is for sin to hide inside form. A simple usurer demands more than principal because he lent money; a sophisticated usurer can invoke penalty, damages, lost profit, risk, exchange, or contractual custom. The moral theologian must therefore do more than repeat the prohibition: what kind of contract is being made, what kind of surplus is being claimed, and does the named title correspond to the real substance of the transaction?

Summenhart's work on contracts contributes to this more systematic moral analysis. The extrinsic titles cannot be understood merely as isolated exceptions; they belong to a wider moral anatomy of exchange. In one case, surplus may arise from labor. In another, from expense. In another, from actual damage. In another, from a concrete gain prevented. In another, from risk genuinely borne. In another, from an exchange difference or a partnership share. The task is to identify the moral object of the payment.

For lucrum cessans this is especially pressing. By the late fifteenth and early sixteenth centuries the title is increasingly accepted in some circumstances, and the acceptance makes systematization more urgent, not less: a dangerous title cannot be left vague, so the conditions must be clarified. What counts as a profit? How concrete must it be? Must the lender be a merchant? Must there be actual intention? How certain must the gain be? Must the alternative use be licit? May the compensation be agreed in advance? May it be deducted upfront? How should risk and costs be discounted?

Summenhart's transitional role is to help moral theology toward these structured questions. The title cannot rest on sentiment; "I could have earned" is not enough. The lender must show that the gain was connected to a real, morally permissible activity, that the loan or delay was the cause of its cessation, that the amount is proportionate, and that the claim has not become habitual interest under another name. These principles will become more explicit in later Salamanca and Jesuit authors, but Summenhart belongs to the path by which they become thinkable as a system.

He also matters because he helps bridge medieval theology and early modern economic life. The medieval authors had already developed the core grammar: mutuum, interesse, damnum emergens, lucrum cessans, penalty, partnership, just price, and the distinction between the loan itself and external titles. The early modern authors would refine these in light of expanding commerce, currency exchange, global trade, and sophisticated legal practice. Summenhart stands between them, helping transform a body of cases into a more analytical treatment of contracts.

The transition matters because case lists alone can become dangerous. Medieval writers preserved exception lists (situations in which something more than principal might be received without usury), useful because they named distinctions. But they could also become mechanical. A lender might search the list for a label rather than examine the moral substance: "penalty" becomes enough, "loss" becomes enough, "foregone profit" becomes enough. The list becomes a menu of excuses.

A more systematic contract analysis tries to prevent this by asking why the title works. Why may actual loss be compensated? Because justice restores what was damaged. Why may labor be paid? Because work has been performed. Why may partnership profit be shared? Because capital and activity are exposed to common risk. Why may foregone profit sometimes be considered? Because a concrete future good may have been causally prevented. Why may risk matter? Because genuine exposure to loss can be a burden. The question moves from naming the title to explaining its moral structure.

This is the movement Summenhart represents: moral theology analyzing contracts according to both legal form and economic reality, where the name of the contract is not enough. A sale can hide a loan, a penalty can hide interest, an exchange can hide usury, a partnership can hide guaranteed debt, and a claim of lost profit can hide ordinary return on money. Moral analysis must therefore look through the form to the substance.

The substance-over-form analysis is essential for the history of lucrum cessans. The title is legitimate only if it remains external to the loan. If the lender receives more because he has suffered the loss of a concrete gain, then the payment may be compensatory. If he receives more because money was lent and time passed, then the title has failed. The same words can describe either case. Only analysis of substance can distinguish them.

Summenhart's transitional work also prepares the later scholastic habit of identifying conditions. The more dangerous the title, the more conditions it requires. Damnum emergens asks: was there actual loss? Lucrum cessans must ask more: was the gain concrete, intended, licit, probable, and caused to cease? Periculum sortis must ask still more: was the principal truly at risk, or was the risk shifted away from the lender? The movement from titles to conditions is one of the great achievements of the tradition.

The development also reveals the inner connection among the titles. At first they appear separate: penalty for delay, actual loss, foregone profit, risk to principal, labor and expense. But in practice they overlap. A delay may cause actual loss; actual loss may include lost opportunity; lost opportunity may be linked to risk; labor and expense may be embedded in institutional lending. Summenhart's systematic attention to contracts helps create the intellectual space in which these overlaps can be analyzed without dissolving all boundaries.

The danger is that systematization can make permission easier: the more technically sophisticated moral theology becomes, the easier it is to game. A clever lender structures the transaction to fit the permitted category, a merchant presents ordinary profit as lost gain, a financier frames interest as compensation for deprivation. The more precise the map, the more tempting it becomes to find the road around the prohibition.

This is why the spiritual purpose of the doctrine must not be lost. The point of contract analysis is not to make more surplus payments permissible. The point is to tell the truth. If a payment is compensatory, it should be recognized as such; if it is extractive, it should be named as such. The sophistication of the analysis must serve justice, not evasion. Summenhart's place in the tradition is valuable only if his systematizing impulse remains under the discipline of the anti-usury core.

That core remains clear. The mutuum itself does not generate profit for the lender; the borrower owes the equivalent principal; any surplus must come from a real title external to the loan. The more abstract the title, the more scrutiny it requires: foregone profit looks toward a future not yet possessed and must be kept concrete, while risk, if it becomes a universal premium, must be tied to real loss absorption.

In this sense he prepares the later Salamanca method. Salamanca will inherit a world in which the titles are already named, debated, and partially accepted; its theologians, working amid commercial expansion, empire, monetary change, and civil-law development, will need a moral theology capable of classifying contracts with precision. Summenhart helps provide part of that bridge.

His importance also lies in the movement from local cases to general moral-economic analysis. Earlier usury discussions arise from particular pastoral problems (a late payment, a merchant loan, a penalty clause, a pledge, a grain sale); by Summenhart's period the tradition increasingly seeks a general account of contractual justice. What makes an exchange equal? What makes a payment compensatory? What makes a claim external to the loan? What role do intention, risk, loss, and future gain play? These are no longer isolated questions. They form a moral system.

For modern readers the systematic turn is extremely useful, because contemporary finance is built out of contract forms: loans, equity, preferred shares, convertible instruments, revenue-based financing, guarantees, covenants, default provisions, and liquidation preferences all create complex patterns of rights and obligations. A moral theology that only says "interest bad" or "market terms good" cannot evaluate them. It needs a Summenhart-like habit of contractual diagnosis.

But modern readers also need the warning. Contractual sophistication can hide domination. A preferred return may function like interest. A liquidation preference may shift downside to founders and workers. A penalty may be engineered revenue. A risk premium may remain after risk has been eliminated by collateral and guarantees. A foregone-profit claim may be nothing more than a target IRR with a scholastic label. The title must be tested against reality.

This is why his transitional position is so relevant to non-extractive finance. Non-extractive capital cannot be built merely by rejecting conventional labels; it must develop its own moral accounting, saying what each payment is for. It must distinguish capital repayment, cost recovery, stewardship labor, actual damages, concrete foregone mission-aligned opportunity, and genuine risk-sharing. It must prevent double-counting, and it must ask whether returns are tied to service or to power.

In a modern fund, the equivalent of Summenhart's contract analysis might become a review template. What is the legal form? What is the moral form? Who owns the capital? Who bears risk? What surplus is claimed? Which title justifies it? Is the title documented? Is the amount proportionate? Is the borrower vulnerable? Does the structure incentivize default, distress, or mission drift? Does it preserve the enterprise's life? Does capital serve, or rule?

The fifteenth-century movement toward acceptance of lucrum cessans therefore ends not with a simple yes, but with a need for system. Sant'Antonino and Bernardino show how foregone profit can be admitted in pastoral and commercial cases. Cajetan shows how the title can be reconciled with Aquinas if the future gain is sufficiently determinate. Summenhart helps move these insights into a more systematic analysis of contracts and extrinsic causes. Together, they prepare the terrain for Salamanca and the Jesuits.

Yet the basic problem remains unresolved. Every refinement that makes the title more realistic also makes it more usable, every condition that clarifies it can become a drafting technique, and every distinction that protects justice can be turned into a loophole by those seeking gain. The history of lucrum cessans is therefore a history of permanent vigilance.

Summenhart's role is to give that vigilance intellectual form: moral theology must understand contracts well enough to judge them, identifying the real title behind the payment. It must distinguish actual loss from foregone gain, foregone gain from risk, risk from labor, labor from rent, rent from domination. It must refuse both crude condemnation and clever evasion.

In the end, Summenhart belongs to the tradition because he helps make moral finance analytical. He stands between the preacher and the jurist, between the confessor and the contract drafter, between medieval case lists and early modern systems. He reminds us that financial morality is not only a matter of good intentions; it is a matter of form, substance, causality, title, proportion, and truth. The title lucrum cessans could be admitted only if it could be analyzed, and Summenhart helps build the tools for that analysis.

chapters.part: Part VII — Lucrum Cessans: Foregone Profit and the Dangerous Birth of Opportunity Cost

chapters.title: Salamanca and the Jesuit Expansion
chapters.label: Chapter 23

sections.title: 23.1 Domingo de Soto
paragraphs: By the time the debate over lucrum cessans reached the School of Salamanca, the question was no longer whether Christian theology should take commerce seriously. It clearly had to. The sixteenth century was not the village economy of an earlier moral imagination: Spanish merchants, bankers, royal officials, confessors, theologians, and jurists all faced a world in which money moved across distances, time mattered, exchange rates fluctuated, credit instruments circulated, and a commercial opportunity could be lost for want of ready capital. The problem was not whether this world existed but whether its existence should be allowed to dissolve the older moral distinctions between a loan and a partnership, between compensation and profit, between justice and usury.

Domingo de Soto stands at this threshold. A Dominican theologian of Salamanca, he inherited the Thomistic structure with unusual seriousness, and he did not deny that lenders could suffer loss, or that merchants might sometimes forego real gain by parting with money already ordered toward profitable use. But he remained cautious, precisely because lucrum cessans was the most dangerous of the extrinsic titles. Actual loss could be documented, and a penalty for culpable delay could be distinguished from a payment for time; those titles had concrete anchors. Foregone profit was more elusive. It invited imagination, hypothetical accounting, and the claim available to every owner of money: "Had I not lent this money, I could have gained elsewhere."

For Soto, that move was perilous because it threatened to universalize the title. Once lucrum cessans became universal, the usury prohibition could remain formally intact while being practically emptied of force. The lender would no longer be saying, "Pay me because this loan itself bears interest." He would be saying, "Pay me because I might otherwise have profited." And if that claim could be made by any lender, at any time, simply because money might always have found some profitable employment, the old doctrine would collapse into a verbal game: the sin of usury condemned in theory while ordinary interest was reconstructed under the name of compensation.

Soto's caution therefore belongs to the anti-abuse logic of the tradition. He is best read not as economically naive but as a theologian who saw the moral danger of abstraction. Lucrum cessans could be legitimate only if the lost gain was concrete rather than merely possible, tied to a real alternative use and not to the bare metaphysical idea that money is always capable of increase. A merchant whose capital was already committed to a determinate transaction, and who was interrupted in an actual commercial course of action, has a stronger claim than a passive lender who merely imagines what he could have earned and may simply be demanding yield on the grounds that capital should not be idle.

The distinction preserves the gratuity of the mutuum. In the strict loan for consumption, ownership passes to the borrower, and the borrower owes the tantundem, the equivalent amount of the same kind and quality. The lender may not sell the "use" of money, because in a consumable loan the use cannot be separated from the money itself: the borrower does not rent the coins and return the same ones after use; he receives ownership, consumes or spends the thing, and returns an equivalent. To demand surplus from the loan itself is therefore to sell what has already been transferred, or to charge twice for the same thing.

The question raised by lucrum cessans is whether the surplus is truly from the loan, or from something external to it, and Soto's answer is careful: only in restricted circumstances can the lost profit be treated as genuinely external. The lender must not merely point to a world of possible investments. He must show that the loan deprived him of a determinate gain that he was positioned and intending to pursue. The claim must be morally specific, something more than the modern language of opportunity cost, in which every choice is measured against every other possible use of capital. For Soto, that kind of abstraction is precisely what turns compensation into extraction.

His position also reflects a Dominican concern for the integrity of commutative justice. Justice in exchange does not permit one party to use superior bargaining power, legal sophistication, or economic necessity to obtain more than is due. If the borrower is in need, the lender's moral responsibility increases rather than decreases. A theology of lending cannot be built around the creditor's imagination of missed opportunities while ignoring the borrower's vulnerability, which is why the scholastic question is always double: what has the lender really lost, and what burden is the borrower being asked to bear?

Soto's restraint matters because of what came next. Salamanca would become more commercially sophisticated than earlier medieval theology, analyzing exchange, money, risk, price, and profit with extraordinary subtlety, and the later Jesuit thinkers, especially Molina, Lessius, and Lugo, would give more expansive accounts of lost profit and capital risk. Soto marks the Dominican checkpoint before that expansion. He reminds the tradition that the recognition of commercial reality must not become a license for moral evasion.

Theologically, he protects the difference between a loan and a partnership. A lender who wishes to share profit can enter a form of association in which he also shares risk; a lender who insists on the security of a creditor while claiming the gain of an investor has made the moral form suspect. The same concern will reappear in the later debates over periculum sortis: who bears the downside? If the borrower must repay regardless of outcome, while the lender demands additional compensation because the money might otherwise have earned profit, the structure may still be extractive, however carefully it is dressed in the language of loss.

Soto's caution is not a historical footnote. Much of contemporary investment logic rests on generalized opportunity cost: capital is assumed to deserve growth because it could always have been placed somewhere else, and a required return is then treated as neutral, technical, morally obvious. Soto's framework interrupts that assumption. It asks whether the alleged lost gain is real, concrete, intended, probable, and causally connected to the loan, or whether it is simply the ordinary claim of capital to be compensated for not being elsewhere.

The question remains central for non-extractive finance. A financing structure may legitimately reimburse real costs, compensate genuine loss, and reward shared risk, but it must be far more careful with claims based on foregone profit, because the more abstract the claim becomes, the more dangerous it is. "I lost this specific, documented opportunity because I made this loan" is one kind of statement. "My capital could have earned something somewhere" is another. The former may be restorative; the latter easily becomes the theological ancestor of target return, hurdle rate, and guaranteed yield.

Soto therefore gives the Salamanca treatment of lucrum cessans its disciplined starting point. He does not abolish the title, and he does not pretend commerce has no opportunity costs; he refuses to let opportunity cost become an all-purpose justification for surplus. His caution protects the heart of the older doctrine: surplus must be tied to a real external burden, not merely to the lender's power, expectation, or imagined alternative. The mutuum remains gratuitous, compensation remains possible, and compensation must remain compensation, or it becomes usury by another name.

sections.title: 23.2 Martín de Azpilcueta / Navarrus
paragraphs: If Domingo de Soto represents the cautious Dominican checkpoint in the Salamanca treatment of lucrum cessans, Martín de Azpilcueta, known as Navarrus, represents the widening of moral theology into the world of money, exchange, and commercial circulation. He does not simply ask whether a lender may claim compensation for foregone profit. He asks what money is doing in a changing economy, how value shifts across place and time, and how confessors should judge transactions in a world where trade, currency, exchange contracts, and credit practices had become increasingly complex.

Azpilcueta's importance lies partly in this wider horizon. He is famous for his analysis of money, scarcity, and exchange, and he saw with unusual clarity that money does not have the same value in every place or circumstance: its purchasing power changes, its abundance or scarcity matters, and exchange between currencies and regions cannot be morally analyzed as if money were a static object with a single obvious value. None of this makes him a modern economist in the anachronistic sense, but it shows Salamanca theology beginning to confront the real dynamics of commercial life.

That broader context matters for lucrum cessans because lost profit is not an isolated issue. It arises within a world in which money may be tied to trade, exchange, inventories, bills, and expected commercial movement. A merchant who lends money may not simply be giving up inert coins. He may be interrupting a chain of exchange, deprived of funds that a concrete business operation actually needed, missing a determinate commercial opportunity. In such cases, the question becomes sharper: is the lender asking for payment merely because time has passed, or because the loan has caused a specific commercial detriment?

Azpilcueta's moral significance lies in his attention to this distinction. His tradition refuses to collapse all surplus into usury, and refuses just as firmly to let commercial sophistication sanctify extraction. Exchange contracts, currency dealings, and compensation for loss may all be legitimate. They may also conceal loans, becoming ways to receive more than the principal while avoiding the appearance of usury. The moral theologian must therefore look beneath the legal form to the substance of the transaction.

Exchange is where this mattered most. Medieval and early modern merchants often used bills of exchange to move value across distance, and a bill could be a genuine exchange contract, involving different currencies, different places, and real services of transfer and conversion. But it could also become a disguised loan, especially when the exchange element was fictive, circular, or arranged primarily to generate a return over time. The scholastic problem was not what an instrument was called but whether it truly functioned as an exchange.

Here Azpilcueta's world of money and exchange intersects with the question of lost gain. If money can be productively deployed in commerce, then depriving a merchant of money can sometimes cause real economic loss. But if that insight is generalized too far, every lender can claim that money is always productive somewhere, and the more sophisticated the commercial system becomes, the more tempting it is to treat liquidity itself as automatically deserving compensation. Azpilcueta's context therefore intensifies the same danger Soto had recognized: lucrum cessans must not become a universal permission slip for interest.

The Salamanca contribution is to make the analysis more concrete. Instead of asking abstractly whether money can generate profit, one must ask: whose money, in what circumstances, with what intention, toward what use, and with what causal connection to the alleged loss? Was the lender a merchant actually engaged in trade? Was the money already destined for a specific transaction? Was the foregone profit probable, determinate, and morally licit? Did the borrower's use of the money truly prevent the lender from gaining it? Or is the lender simply invoking commerce as a cloak for ordinary yield?

Navarrus helps us see why the old usury doctrine could not remain a simple denunciation of all surplus (the commercial world had become too complicated for that), and why it could not safely be abandoned either. The very complexity of money made it easier to hide domination inside apparently legitimate instruments. Exchange could be real or fictive, compensation restorative or extractive, lost profit concrete or imaginary, and the moral task was to distinguish them without being dazzled by the technical form of the contract.

This is one of the great strengths of Salamanca moral theology. It does not treat finance as morally neutral machinery; it treats finance as a field of human action, and therefore as a field of conscience. The confessor, jurist, and theologian must ask not only what the contract says but what it does. Does it move money across regions? Does it compensate a real loss? Does it enable commerce? Does it distribute risk fairly? Or does it manufacture a surplus claim from the borrower's dependence?

Azpilcueta's treatment of money and exchange thus prepares the way for the more systematic accounts of lucrum cessans. Molina will press the questions of merchant intention, real alternative use, foreseeability, and causality; Lessius will move still further into the logic of money markets and the deprivation of money over time; Lugo will later gather these lines into a more permissive synthesis. Navarrus belongs at the point where the analysis becomes unmistakably commercial. He helps shift the discussion from a narrow question about lending to a broader moral inquiry into the circulation of capital.

For modern non-extractive finance, the point carries directly. A financing instrument cannot be judged merely by its name: a "loan," an "exchange," a "revenue-share," a "SAFE," or a "preferred equity" round may have a legal form that obscures its moral substance.
