What matters is how the instrument allocates risk, control, time, priority, and surplus. Does the investor truly participate in the venture? Does the company retain enough freedom to serve its mission? Is the claimed return tied to real service, real risk, or real loss? Or is the structure designed to guarantee capital's growth while shifting vulnerability to founders, workers, suppliers, or communities?

Azpilcueta's world was not ours, but the diagnostic problem is familiar: money moves through instruments that can be morally ambiguous, technical sophistication can serve justice or hide extraction, and the presence of exchange, currency, or commercial opportunity does not automatically justify gain. It merely requires a more careful moral anatomy.

His contribution to the history of lucrum cessans is not that he "allowed" or "disallowed" foregone profit in some mechanical way; it is that he situated the problem inside a broader theology of money in motion. In a commercial society, lost gain may sometimes be real; but because money is always capable of being imagined elsewhere, the claim must be disciplined, tied to a concrete interrupted use rather than to the abstract power of capital.

Navarrus stands between Soto's caution and the later Jesuit expansion, seeing why commercial reality matters and why moral theology must not surrender to it. Money may circulate, exchange may be legitimate, profit may sometimes be lost. The borrower must not become a revenue stream merely because the lender can imagine a more profitable world in which the loan was never made.

sections.title: 23.3 Luis de Molina
paragraphs: With Luis de Molina, the Salamanca treatment of lucrum cessans becomes more systematic, more commercially attentive, and more dangerous. Molina does not merely inherit the older debate about whether a lender may be compensated for profit foregone. He works inside a world in which commerce has become sufficiently complex that moral theology must ask precise questions about intention, alternative use, foreseeability, causation, and valuation. The question is no longer only whether lost profit can ever be compensated but how one distinguishes a real lost profit from an invented one.

This is the great Molina problem. By the sixteenth century, it was no longer plausible to say that money was always economically sterile in the ordinary sense. Money in the hands of a merchant could be part of a larger productive process: it could buy goods for resale, finance transport, settle exchange obligations, or carry a trader into a commercial opportunity that would otherwise be lost. To lend such money could impose a real cost, interrupting a planned transaction and depriving the lender of a gain that was not merely imagined but reasonably expected.

Molina gives moral theology tools for taking this reality seriously. He asks, in effect: did the lender have a real alternative use for the money? Was that use actually intended, or at least virtually intended as part of the lender's ordinary commercial activity? Was the profit reasonably foreseeable? Was there a causal connection between making the loan and losing the profit? Could the loss be valued without fantasy? These questions make lucrum cessans more usable in practice, and they reveal its danger in the same stroke: the more precise the title becomes, the more easily it can be translated into commercial technique.

The framework begins with intention. A merchant who has money already destined for trade stands in a different position from a person who simply possesses money and later claims that it might have been invested. The difference is not merely psychological; it is moral and evidentiary. Intention links the money to a determinate course of action and shows that the lender did not invent a hypothetical alternative after the fact. The title depends on the claim that the lender was deprived of something real: not a fantasy, not a bargaining story, not a generalized expectation that money deserves growth.

Yet Molina's notion of intention is not limited to a single explicit plan written down in advance; commercial life runs on habitual patterns, standing practices, and ordinary readiness. A merchant may not know the exact transaction that would have occurred on a particular day and may still be engaged in a line of business in which available capital is normally turned over for gain. This creates the possibility of what later moralists would describe as actual or virtual intention. The lender need not always identify a single lost sale with mathematical certainty, but the claim must still be grounded in a real commercial vocation and not merely in abstract ownership of money.

Here the treatment becomes both pastorally useful and morally unstable. Useful, because real commerce does not always provide perfect documentation: a merchant's loss may be probable, contextual, and commercially intelligible even where it cannot be proven like a broken window or a burned barn. Unstable, because the same logic expands quickly: if ordinary commercial readiness is enough, how far does the title extend? To every merchant? To every household with savings? To every investor? To every institution that could always have placed capital somewhere else?

Molina's answer depends on discipline. The alternative use must be real, the profit reasonably foreseeable, the loss caused by the loan, and the compensation proportionate to the lost gain. The title cannot be treated as an automatic property of money itself. It is not enough to say, "I lent money, and money can earn profit." One must be able to say, "This loan deprived me of this kind of gain, in these circumstances, with this degree of probability."

The emphasis on causality is essential: without it, lucrum cessans becomes an all-purpose excuse. The lender must not simply identify an attractive alternative after the fact; the loan must be the reason the gain ceased. If the profit would not likely have occurred anyway, or if the lender had no real capacity to pursue it, or if the opportunity was merely speculative, then the claim fails. Moral theology here functions almost like forensic accounting. It asks what actually happened, what would probably have happened, and whether the borrower is being asked to repair a real detriment or to finance the lender's imagination.

Foreseeability plays a similar role. Molina's more permissive approach does not authorize compensation for any possible gain: the expected profit must be sufficiently probable to count as a real moral loss. That does not mean guaranteed (commercial profit rarely is), but it must be more than remote possibility. A merchant who gives up a regular, ordinary, and morally licit trading opportunity is different from a lender who says that, in theory, money might have been placed into some profitable venture. The first claim has a definite shape. The second is only the modern opportunity-cost argument in early form.

Valuation must also be restrained: even where compensation is allowed, the lender may not demand more than the value of the lost gain. The title is restorative, not generative: it repairs a deprivation rather than converting the loan into a vehicle for enrichment. Molina remains inside the older architecture of commutative justice, whose purpose is to restore equality between parties when one has suffered a real detriment because of the transaction, not to create a general entitlement to return on capital.

Molina's commercial sophistication is easy to misread. He is not baptizing modern finance before its time, or saying that capital has an inherent right to yield, or that every lender may charge for the time during which money is absent. He is trying to preserve the difference between a gratuitous mutuum and an external claim arising from real detriment. But because he gives greater room to commercial detriment, his framework becomes one of the bridges from medieval usury doctrine toward early modern finance.

The bridge is morally ambiguous. On one side, Molina protects justice for the lender: it would be unjust to require a person to suffer a real, concrete, and causally connected loss simply because the transaction took the form of a loan, and if the borrower's request deprives the lender of a determinate commercial gain, compensation may be appropriate. On the other side, once foregone profit is admitted as compensable, broader claims follow. The lender's world of missed possibilities can expand indefinitely, and the title can slip from "this profit ceased" to "profit as such was unavailable," and finally to "capital must be paid for being withheld."

Molina's importance for later Jesuit thought lies in this disciplined expansion. Lessius will push further into the logic of money's productive use, term, and deprivation; Lugo will synthesize the common teaching and set out conditions under which lucrum cessans may be accepted. Molina provides the moral infrastructure they build on: actual or virtual intention, real alternative use, foreseeability, causality, and proportional valuation. These conditions are the difference between a bounded title and a disguised return.

For modern non-extractive finance, Molina is unavoidable. We cannot simply pretend that capital has no alternative uses. A mission-driven fund that commits money to one enterprise may truly be unable to deploy that money elsewhere; a patient investor may forego other mission-aligned opportunities; a non-extractive financing structure may need to acknowledge real opportunity costs in some limited sense if it is to remain sustainable. But Molina would not allow this to become a generic hurdle rate. He would ask: what alternative use was actually intended? Was it morally licit? Was it concrete? Was the gain reasonably foreseeable? Did this financing truly cause the gain to cease? Is the claimed compensation proportionate, documented, and non-extractive?

This is a very different moral logic from modern portfolio theory. Modern finance often treats opportunity cost as universal: every unit of capital is measured against the next-best alternative, and return expectations are normalized through markets, benchmarks, risk-adjusted pricing, and target rates. Molina's framework is more personal, concrete, and moral. It asks whether this lender, in these circumstances, suffered this determinate detriment because of this loan, and it resists the abstraction by which capital becomes a self-referential claimant, demanding growth simply because growth was possible elsewhere.

Molina both advances and endangers the tradition: advances it by making it capable of judging real commercial life, endangers it because the tools he develops can be generalized beyond his limits. Once lost profit is recognized, one must constantly guard against its inflation. The title must not become a metaphysical claim that money naturally bears fruit. It must remain a claim about a real, causally connected, morally legitimate profit that ceased.

Molina therefore stands at the center of the Salamanca expansion of lucrum cessans. He gives the title practical criteria, makes the doctrine more commercially intelligent, and forces the tradition to confront its most difficult question: whether one can recognize the real cost of foregone opportunity without enthroning opportunity cost as the hidden god of finance. The answer depends on whether compensation remains tied to concrete detriment, or capital is allowed to transform every unrealized possibility into a claim upon the borrower.

sections.title: 23.4 Leonardus Lessius
paragraphs: With Leonardus Lessius, the doctrine of lucrum cessans moves closer to the edge of modern finance. The older scholastic problem remains the same: when may a lender receive more than the principal without committing usury? But Lessius works in a world in which money markets, commercial practice, exchange, credit, and contractual ingenuity have become increasingly sophisticated, and he treats money not merely as an object that can be consumed but as something whose absence can matter economically. That makes his analysis powerful and dangerous at once.

He does not abandon the old usury doctrine; he remains within the tradition that distinguishes the mutuum from external titles: a lender may not demand surplus merely because a loan has been made; the loan itself, considered as a loan for consumption, remains gratuitous; the borrower owes the equivalent principal; and any further claim must be justified by something outside the bare loan, whether loss, delay, risk, labor, expense, or some other real detriment. But Lessius presses hard on what counts as detriment in a commercial world.

His treatment of lucrum cessans follows the line already opened by Molina. If money was truly destined for profitable use, and if the lender is deprived of that use by making the loan, then a claim for compensation may arise. The lender is not selling the use of money as such; he is being compensated for a profit that has ceased because the money is no longer available for the intended transaction. The logic is still restorative: the lender is to be made whole, not enriched.

Yet his analysis also broadens the field: he is more attentive than many earlier theologians to money's commercial usefulness over time. In an active economy, money is not simply a heap of sterile metal: it can be inventory in motion, exchange capacity, readiness for trade, participation in a network of profitable opportunities. To lack money for a period may therefore be a real disadvantage. This is where the related title of carentia pecuniae enters the discussion.

Carentia pecuniae means the lack, absence, or deprivation of money. It is related to lucrum cessans, but not identical to it. Lucrum cessans asks whether a specific profit has ceased because the lender made the loan. Carentia pecuniae asks whether the lender may be compensated simply for being without his money for a determinate term, especially where that absence restricts his commercial freedom. The first title points toward a lost gain. The second points toward liquidity deprivation.

This is an extraordinarily important development, and in strict scholastic terms an extraordinarily dangerous one. Once the deprivation of money itself becomes compensable, the line between compensation and ordinary interest becomes very thin. Every lender is without money during the term of a loan; every lender can say that liquidity has value and that being unable to recall money imposes some burden. If carentia pecuniae is accepted without strict limits, the old usury doctrine can be dissolved from within. The lender need not say, "I charge for the loan." He can say, "I charge because I am without money."

Lessius's genius is that he sees the commercial plausibility of this claim. His danger is that the claim is almost too plausible. In modern finance, liquidity preference is obvious: a lock-up has value, a term commitment has value, and capital unavailable for one use cannot be used for another. The modern apparatus of term structure, liquidity premium, and time value of money rests on intuitions his world was beginning to articulate. But scholastic theology cannot simply accept these intuitions without moral scrutiny, because doing so risks transforming time into yield and money into a thing that naturally deserves growth.

The crucial question is therefore whether carentia pecuniae names a real external burden or merely renames the loan's duration. If the lender suffers a concrete deprivation because he cannot use the money in a particular way, the claim may resemble lucrum cessans. But if the claim is simply that the lender is deprived of money whenever he lends, the title becomes universal: it no longer distinguishes compensation from usury; it simply provides a new vocabulary for interest.

His broader money-market logic marks a significant shift. Earlier authors often wanted a clear event: a late payment, a destroyed good, a missed commercial opportunity, a provable loss. Lessius moves toward structures: the term of a loan, the availability of money, the commercial value of liquidity, the lender's inability to call funds back. Moral theology begins to analyze not only discrete injuries, but the economic architecture of capital commitment.

The structural turn is both necessary and risky. Necessary, because modern finance is built through structures: capital is not lost only in obvious accidents; it is tied up, subordinated, delayed, collateralized, or made unavailable, and these arrangements affect who can act, who can survive, and who bears vulnerability. A moral theology that ignores them becomes naive. Risky, because the more structural the title becomes, the easier it is to make the title automatic: every structure can be priced, every deprivation monetized, every inconvenience to capital converted into a charge imposed on the borrower.

Lessius forces the tradition to face one of its deepest tensions. The lender's situation can matter: he may suffer because money is unavailable for a term, miss real opportunities, be constrained in trade, bear real commercial burden. But the borrower's situation matters too. The borrower may be in need, may be the weaker party, and may be forced to pay not for any real injury caused, but for the fact that capital dislikes being unavailable. At that point, carentia pecuniae stops being restorative and becomes extractive.

This is why later theologians would worry about the title. Juan de Lugo, although more permissive in some respects, saw clearly that if carentia pecuniae were admitted broadly, hardly any loan would remain usurious. The title could swallow the rule. The very thing the tradition had refused, payment for the passage of time in a loan, could return under the name of deprivation, the older prohibition still honored verbally while its moral content was evacuated.

Lessius is therefore not a permissive author to be celebrated or condemned but a diagnostician of a changing world. He sees that money in commerce has a use-profile that the earlier simple examples of wine, wheat, and coins did not fully capture, that liquidity can matter, that being without money can be a burden. But his analysis also reveals how close scholastic reasoning comes to modern money-market assumptions once it begins to treat the absence of money as compensable in itself.

Lessius is indispensable to non-extractive finance, because contemporary financing is built around precisely these questions. What is the value of locked capital? What is the moral status of a liquidity premium? When is a term commitment a real service? When is patient capital legitimately compensated for its patience? When does a return reflect genuine risk, sacrifice, or stewardship labor? And when is it simply the old usurious claim that money must be paid because it has been absent for a time?

A non-extractive framework cannot answer these questions by pretending liquidity has no value; that would be false. Patient capital can be costly, a fund that commits capital to one company may truly be unable to support another, and a lender or investor may accept constraints that deserve recognition. But Lessius's danger warns us that liquidity value cannot be converted into an automatic right to yield. The claim must still be examined. What exactly was lost? Was the deprivation concrete? Was the borrower responsible for it? Was the compensation proportionate? Was the lender also bearing downside? Or was the borrower simply made to pay because capital was unavailable to itself?

The distinction matters especially in mission-driven investment. A fund may say that it needs returns because its capital is locked up, and that may be partly true. But if the return is guaranteed, senior, collateralized, and insensitive to the venture's actual outcome, then the borrower may be carrying the burden of the lender's liquidity preference without receiving genuine risk-sharing in return. The language of patience can conceal impatience, the language of sacrifice can conceal entitlement, and the language of term commitment can conceal ordinary extraction.

Lessius brings us to the threshold of a modern question: can the time-structure of capital be morally priced without making time itself the object sold in a mutuum? The older usury doctrine says no, if the payment is simply for the passage of time in a loan. The more expansive late scholastic doctrine says perhaps, if the payment corresponds to a real external burden. Lessius explores the "perhaps" with unusual seriousness, and the more seriously we take his analysis, the more carefully we must guard the boundary.

What follows is not that carentia pecuniae must always be rejected, but that it must never become automatic. It stays subordinate to the deeper moral distinction between compensation and extraction: to be without money may be a burden, and not every burden borne by capital may be shifted to the borrower; not every inconvenience deserves yield, not every term deserves a premium, and not every liquidity claim is morally external to the loan. Lessius helps us see why the extrinsic titles became more subtle as commerce matured, and why subtlety can become dangerous. Once the absence of money is monetized, moral theology stands at the edge of modern interest. Whether it falls over that edge depends on whether the title remains concrete, proportionate, and truly external, or becomes the ordinary demand that capital be paid for waiting.

sections.title: 23.5 Juan de Lugo
paragraphs: With Juan de Lugo, the late scholastic treatment of lucrum cessans reaches one of its most mature and permissive forms. The title has traveled a long road. In Aquinas, compensation for actual loss was admitted while compensation for profit not yet made was treated with deep suspicion; in Hostiensis and the later canonists, the possibility of foregone profit began to emerge under narrow conditions; in the fifteenth century, the title became increasingly accepted in concrete commercial cases; in Salamanca and the Jesuit tradition, the analysis became precise about intention, causality, probability, valuation, and the commercial use of money. Lugo gathers these developments and gives them a more systematic form.

His importance lies not only in his conclusions, but in the conditions he imposes. Lugo does not simply say that a lender may charge for lost profit; he places the claim inside a disciplined moral structure. Three conditions are especially important. First, the loan must truly cause the foregone profit. Second, nothing may be demanded beyond the value of that profit. Third, compensation should not be paid upfront. Together they reveal both the breadth and the limits of the late scholastic settlement.

The first condition, causality, is the heart of the matter. The lender must be able to show that the loan caused the profit to cease. It is not enough that the money might have been profitable, that money in general is useful, or that an attractive alternative investment can be identified after the fact. The moral claim depends on a real link between lending the money and losing the gain. Without that link, lucrum cessans becomes a fiction: it ceases to be compensation and becomes ordinary interest under another name.

The causal requirement keeps the title anchored in commutative justice. The borrower may be required to repair a real detriment caused by the transaction; he may not be made responsible for every unrealized possibility in the lender's financial imagination. The foregone profit must have a determinate relation to the loan, and the lender must not turn the borrower into the guarantor of a world in which the lender always could have done better.

The second condition, proportionality, is equally important. Even when lost profit is real, the lender may not demand more than its value. The title is restorative, not expansive: it authorizes no premium, no penalty, no target return, no indefinite claim on the borrower, only compensation to the extent that a real gain has ceased. The amount must correspond to the detriment. Anything beyond that begins to look like surplus from the loan itself.

Proportionality is one of the ways Lugo preserves the older anti-usury logic inside a more permissive doctrine. He is saying not that capital has a right to grow, but that a person should not be unjustly harmed by being deprived of a real, probable, morally licit gain. The lender may be restored to equality. He may not use the title to convert a loan into a profit engine.

The third condition, that compensation should not be paid upfront, may be the most revealing. If the lender deducts the alleged lost profit in advance, the title becomes nearly indistinguishable from interest: the borrower receives less than the stated principal while being obligated to repay the full amount, and the supposed compensation for a future lost gain is folded into the structure of the loan from the beginning. This is precisely the danger the earlier theologians feared: the title becomes a pretext. The lender no longer waits to see whether the loss actually occurs or how much it is worth; he prices the claim into the transaction as yield.

By rejecting or restricting upfront payment, Lugo maintains the logic of compensation. A claim for lost profit should follow the reality of the loss, not be capitalized automatically into the loan, because the moral status of the payment depends on its relation to an actual detriment. If the payment is imposed in advance, regardless of whether the loss materializes, it begins to function as a fixed return for the use of money. The external title has been internalized into the loan.

Lugo's position became part of what could be described as a sententia communis, a common teaching, in the more permissive late scholastic and manualist tradition. Under strict conditions, lucrum cessans could be admitted. Mere possibility of profit was not enough, but concrete, probable, causally connected foregone gain could justify compensation. The settlement did not abolish the usury prohibition; it tried to preserve it while recognizing that commercial lending could impose real opportunity-related detriments.

The settlement was, of course, inherently unstable. Once lost profit is admitted, the pressure to generalize it becomes intense: in a commercial society, everyone can imagine profitable alternatives, and in a financialized society, every capital holder can compare every use of money to every other possible deployment. Lugo's conditions are designed precisely to prevent that slide. That they are necessary at all shows how dangerous the title has become.

Lugo also draws the major titles into a broader theory of detriment. Actual loss, foregone gain, and risk to principal can be understood as different ways in which the lender may suffer, or assume, a burden external to the loan itself. It is a powerful synthesis: it shows that the extrinsic titles are not random loopholes but attempts to identify real burdens that disturb equality between the parties. It also risks expanding the language of loss. If foregone gain is a loss "of a sort," and risk is a loss "of a sort," then much depends on how carefully those "sorts" are distinguished.

This is why Lugo matters for modern non-extractive finance: his framework is sophisticated enough to recognize real costs of capital without collapsing into the assumption that capital automatically deserves return, and his three conditions translate directly into contemporary diagnostics.

First: did this financing actually cause the investor or lender to forego a concrete alternative? Not a vague market benchmark, not a generic return expectation, not a theoretical claim that money could have earned elsewhere, but a concrete, mission-compatible, morally licit, reasonably probable alternative use.

Second: is the claimed compensation limited to the value of that foregone gain? Or has the title been inflated into a target IRR, a preferred return, a compounding rate, or a hurdle that exceeds any identifiable detriment?

Third: is the compensation demanded upfront or embedded automatically in the instrument? If so, the structure may no longer be compensating a realized or reasonably established loss. It may simply be charging for time, dressed in the language of opportunity cost.

These questions cut deeply into contemporary finance. A fund may claim that it needs a return because its capital could have been elsewhere. Lugo would neither dismiss that claim immediately nor accept it abstractly. He would ask: elsewhere where? With what intention? With what probability? With what moral legitimacy? What profit was truly lost? How was it valued? Why should this borrower bear it? And why is it being charged in advance?

The scrutiny lands especially hard on venture capital and private equity, where expected returns are often justified by portfolio logic. The investor does not usually point to one specific alternative transaction that was lost because of this investment; he appeals to a broader opportunity set, a risk class, a benchmark, a fund model, a required return. From Lugo's perspective, such claims may describe a business model, but they do not automatically establish an extrinsic title. A portfolio may require returns to satisfy investors. That does not prove that a particular founder or company owes compensation for a concrete lost gain.

The same applies to modern "cost of capital" language, which often functions like a universalized lucrum cessans: capital has an expected return because it could be invested elsewhere, every project must clear the hurdle, and every borrower or company must compensate capital for not choosing the next-best alternative. Lugo's conditions challenge this abstraction, insisting that compensation remain tied to a real causal detriment, not to capital's generalized self-comparison.

At the same time, Lugo prevents non-extractive finance from becoming simplistic. Anyone serious about building sustainable mission-aligned capital structures cannot pretend that capital has no cost, that funders have no alternatives, or that foregone gain is never real. A non-extractive fund may actually give up a concrete opportunity in order to support a particular enterprise; it may accept a lower return, a longer term, or higher uncertainty than it otherwise would, and some compensation may be legitimate. Lugo's test forces that compensation to be specific, proportionate, and morally accountable.

That is the difference between disciplined lucrum cessans and ordinary opportunity-cost ideology. Disciplined lucrum cessans says: a real gain, already reasonably within reach, ceased because of this loan, and compensation is limited to that loss. Opportunity-cost ideology says: every use of money must compensate capital for not being used elsewhere. The first can belong to justice; the second easily becomes extraction.

Lugo stands near the culmination of the permissive scholastic tradition, but he is not a simple advocate of interest. He is a systematizer of conditions, who recognizes that commercial life can produce real foregone gains and knows that without causality, proportionality, and timing discipline, the title becomes a fraud upon the usury prohibition. His thought makes the doctrine more usable and the need for moral audit more urgent. His achievement is to admit the title without letting it float free (keeping it from floating free again is the work he leaves behind), and in the history of lucrum cessans he marks the moment when the permissive view becomes common teaching, but never unconditional teaching: the title is valid but dangerous, able to restore equality or to disguise domination, to compensate a real lost gain or to become the ancestor of the modern claim that capital must always be paid for its unrealized alternatives.

sections.title: 23.6 Alphonsus Liguori
paragraphs: By the time the question of lucrum cessans reaches Alphonsus Liguori, the debate has entered the world of the later manuals. The dramatic medieval and early modern struggle has not disappeared, but it has been organized into a more settled pastoral form. The confessor needs rules; the merchant needs guidance; the penitent needs to know whether a particular contract is sinful, doubtful, or permissible. Alphonsus therefore receives the long scholastic tradition not as an abstract intellectual puzzle but as a practical moral inheritance, applied to souls, contracts, consciences, and ordinary economic life.

He represents the mature Catholic manualist settlement. Lucrum cessans is no longer treated as simply impossible. The stricter Thomistic suspicion remains part of the background, but the majority view has shifted: under proper conditions, compensation for foregone profit may be allowed. Yet Alphonsus does not thereby convert lost profit into a general right to interest. He accepts the title, and accepts it as dangerous: a possible extrinsic claim rather than an intrinsic property of the mutuum.

The loan itself remains gratuitous: the lender cannot demand surplus simply because money has been lent, and the borrower owes the equivalent principal, not a rent for the use of the money. If more than principal is received, the moral justification must come from outside the loan. That is the whole meaning of an extrinsic title, and it is why Alphonsus's acceptance of lucrum cessans does not abolish the usury prohibition. It preserves the older grammar while admitting that real foregone profit may sometimes constitute a compensable burden.

But what kind of foregone profit? Here Alphonsus remains disciplined. Mere possibility is not enough: a lender cannot simply say, "I might have made money with this sum," or invoke the abstract fact that money, in a commercial society, can often be put to profitable use. The profit must have a sufficient degree of reality. It must be connected to a concrete alternative use, something the lender was actually positioned to obtain, not something imagined after the fact to justify a charge. The title concerns a gain that truly ceased, not a gain that could be rhetorically constructed.

This is why Alphonsus belongs to the tradition of moral moderation rather than financial permissiveness. He understands that the older rigorist position can become too narrow if it refuses to acknowledge real commercial detriment: a merchant deprived of working capital may suffer genuine loss, a lender may be asked to forego a morally legitimate gain in order to assist another, and justice may allow compensation in such cases. But he also sees that the opposite danger is greater in practice. Once lost profit is admitted, lenders may begin to treat every loan as a missed opportunity and every borrower as responsible for capital's unrealized gain.

The manualist settlement therefore depends on careful conditions. The foregone profit must be reasonably certain, or at least sufficiently probable in the concrete circumstances; causally connected to the loan; morally licit; valued proportionately; not double-counted alongside other titles; and not converted into a universal charge. Above all, it must remain a title of compensation, not a mechanism for ordinary yield.

Alphonsus's pastoral importance lies in his refusal to treat moral theology as either impossibly rigid or casually permissive. He is often associated with a balanced approach to conscience, avoiding both laxism and excessive rigorism, and that balance is visible here. The lender should not be forced to absorb every real detriment merely because usury is forbidden; the borrower must not be made to carry the lender's speculative imagination. The moral law protects both sides, and it gives special vigilance to the danger of creditor power.

The problem with lucrum cessans is not only that the claim may be false; it is that the lender usually holds the stronger position: he can define the contract, set the terms, describe the opportunity cost, and demand compensation before the borrower has any meaningful ability to contest the story. If the title is not carefully restricted, it becomes a way for the stronger party to impose hypothetical losses on the weaker. Alphonsus's insistence that mere possibility is insufficient protects the borrower from being charged for a fantasy.

The word "fantasy" may sound too sharp, but it captures the moral danger. A missed profit can be real; it can also be imagined, inflated, selected, or retrospectively invented, since the lender can always picture a better use of money. Modern finance institutionalizes this imagination through benchmarks, expected returns, hurdle rates, and portfolio targets. Alphonsus's logic resists the move. He requires that the gain have enough concreteness to be treated as a true detriment, not merely as an unrealized possibility among countless unrealized possibilities.

This makes Alphonsus especially useful for translating the tradition into contemporary non-extractive finance. Many modern investors assume that capital has a cost because it could always be deployed elsewhere. In Alphonsian terms, that is not yet a moral title; it is a background fact. To become a title, the alleged foregone profit must be concrete, licit, probable, causally connected, and proportionate, and the claim must be capable of moral explanation. "Our fund model requires this return" is not the same as "this specific gain was lost because this capital was made available to you."

A non-extractive capital structure may need to account for real sacrifice: patient capital may genuinely forego other mission-aligned uses, a fund may have operational needs, a lender may incur actual damages, an investor may bear genuine risk. The moral tradition asks only that each claim be named honestly, and the demand is not anti-finance; it is anti-fiction. Is this a cost? Is this labor? Is this risk? Is this actual loss? Is this foregone profit? Or is it simply the desire for return, translated into technical language?

Alphonsus helps us see why the permissive settlement never fully eliminates the need for scrutiny. A title can be valid in principle and abusive in practice, and the fact that lucrum cessans is allowed under conditions does not mean every invocation of opportunity cost is justified. The more common the title becomes, the greater the danger that it will be used mechanically. What began as a careful exception can become a standard clause, then a default percentage, then a pricing convention.

This is the recurring pattern in the history of the extrinsic titles. A real burden is recognized; a name is given to it; a legitimate case is distinguished from usury. Then the name becomes portable: it travels into new contracts, becomes a formula, a way of receiving surplus while preserving plausible deniability. Alphonsus's manualist discipline attempts to prevent this drift. The title must remain morally examined; it must not become automatic.

His treatment also sits under the shadow of Vix pervenit, Benedict XIV's eighteenth-century restatement of the usury doctrine. That text insists that gain from the loan itself remains usury, while allowing that legitimate extrinsic titles may exist. Alphonsus's position is fully intelligible within that frame. He does not deny the possibility of extrinsic titles, and neither does he allow them to erase the core prohibition. The crucial distinction remains: surplus from the loan itself is illicit; compensation for a real external title may be licit.

Alphonsus is thus a guardian of the distinction rather than an innovator. He receives the late scholastic expansion and places it into pastoral order, showing how a mature moral tradition can permit limited compensation without surrendering its central concern. The lender may be restored. The lender may not be transformed into a guaranteed beneficiary of the borrower's need.

For modern readers, this may be the most useful thing about him. We often treat opportunity cost as morally obvious because it is economically familiar, and Alphonsus asks us to slow down: familiarity is not moral proof. The fact that capital could have earned elsewhere does not automatically mean that the borrower owes for that elsewhere. The claim must be tested, made concrete, limited, and subordinated to justice.

Alphonsus therefore gives us the late pastoral form of lucrum cessans: accepted but bounded, useful but dangerous, legitimate in concrete cases but never universal. Mere possibility of profit is rejected; real foregone gain may be compensable; the loan remains gratuitous; the title remains extrinsic; the purpose remains the restoration of equality, not the enthronement of capital's expectation. At the end of the long debate, he does not return us to a simple medieval world before commerce, and he does not deliver us into modern finance's automatic logic of return. He stands between them, insisting that moral theology must be realistic without becoming captured. Profit may cease and compensation may be just, but opportunity cost must not become the route by which usury returns.

sections.title: 23.7 Modern Diagnostic Checklist
paragraphs: The history of lucrum cessans leaves us with a title that is both necessary and dangerous. It is necessary because real foregone profit can exist. A person may lend money and thereby lose a concrete, morally legitimate gain that would otherwise have been obtained: a merchant may miss a planned transaction, a mission-driven fund may be unable to support another enterprise, a cooperative lender may tie up capital in one place and thereby forego a specific use elsewhere. To pretend that such losses never occur would be morally simplistic.

But lucrum cessans is dangerous because it can so easily become the theological ancestor of generalized opportunity cost. Once a lender may say, "I lost what I could otherwise have earned," the claim can expand without limit: every unit of capital can imagine an alternative, every fund can point to a benchmark, every investor can invoke a hurdle rate, every lender can say that money elsewhere would have grown. At that point, foregone profit is no longer a concrete detriment. It has become an ideology of capital's entitlement.

A modern diagnostic checklist is therefore needed, and it should not begin with vocabulary. Whether the contract uses the language of interest, yield, preferred return, liquidity premium, or opportunity cost settles nothing; names can mislead. The real question is whether the claimed surplus is tied to a genuine external burden, or whether it is merely payment for the use of money under another name. The questions below translate the late scholastic conditions into modern moral-accounting terms.

The first question is whether there was a concrete alternative use. Lucrum cessans cannot rest on the abstract idea that money is always useful. It must point to something more definite: a trade not undertaken, a project not funded, an inventory purchase not made, a mission-aligned investment not pursued. The alternative need not always be proven with absolute certainty, but it must have enough shape to be morally intelligible. "We could have earned somewhere else" is not enough. "We had a specific, probable, licit opportunity that we gave up in order to make this financing available" is a much stronger claim.

The second question is whether the alternative use was licit. A lender cannot claim moral compensation for being prevented from making an unjust gain. If the foregone opportunity would itself have been exploitative, speculative in a destructive way, dependent on injustice, or contrary to the lender's stated mission, then it cannot ground a legitimate title. In non-extractive finance, this matters deeply: a mission fund should not justify pressure on one enterprise by pointing to a more lucrative but morally misaligned opportunity elsewhere. The foregone gain must be not only financially plausible, but morally acceptable.

The third question is whether there was actual intention or virtual intention. Actual intention means the lender really meant to use the money in a determinate way. Virtual intention is broader: the lender may not have identified one exact transaction, but the money was genuinely part of an ongoing practice in which capital was normally and concretely deployed for gain. A working merchant, for example, may not always be able to name the precise sale that was lost, but may still show that available capital would ordinarily have been turned over in trade. By contrast, a passive lender who invents an alternative only after the loan is made has a weak claim.

The fourth question is causality, the core Lugo condition. Did the loan truly cause the gain to cease? It is not enough that the lender made a loan and also failed to earn some other profit; the failure to earn must be attributable to the loan. If the alternative was uncertain, unavailable, morally impossible, already missed, or unrelated to the money lent, the causal link is broken. Without causality, lucrum cessans becomes a story told by capital about what it wishes had happened.

The fifth question is reasonable valuation. Even if a gain was truly foregone, the compensation must be limited to the value of that gain. It may not be inflated into a target return, a preferred yield, a compounding rate, or a generalized market benchmark, unless that benchmark genuinely corresponds to the concrete lost opportunity. The valuation should account for probability, costs, uncertainty, and the fact that the foregone gain was never guaranteed. A possible profit cannot be valued as if it were a certain profit, and a risky gain cannot be charged as if it were already possessed.

The sixth question is whether compensation is deducted or demanded upfront. Upfront deduction is morally suspect because it makes the alleged lost profit function like ordinary interest: the borrower receives less, owes more, and bears a charge before the loss has been established. This was one of Lugo's warnings. If the title is truly compensatory, it should remain connected to the actual or reasonably demonstrable loss; built into the transaction automatically from the beginning, it begins to look less like restoration and more like pricing the loan.

The seventh question is whether the title has been universalized. A valid lucrum cessans claim is specific: it does not apply to every lender, every loan, every term, or every sum of money, and the more standardized the claim becomes, the more dangerous it is. If every financing instrument includes a return justified by opportunity cost, without showing the concrete opportunity foregone, then lucrum cessans has ceased to be an extrinsic title. It has become ordinary interest under a scholastic name.

The eighth question is double-counting. Foregone profit must not be counted again under another name. A lender may not claim lost profit, risk premium, late penalty, liquidity premium, administrative fee, and management fee where these charges overlap in substance; each title must name a distinct burden. If the same deprivation of money is counted as lucrum cessans, carentia pecuniae, and periculum sortis, the borrower may be paying several times for one underlying reality. Moral accounting requires separation, documentation, and restraint.

The ninth question is whether the claim is merely that "capital could always have earned somewhere." This is the modern form of the danger, the point at which lucrum cessans dissolves into generalized opportunity-cost ideology. The tradition does not deny that capital often has alternative uses. It refuses to make the borrower responsible for the entire universe of unrealized alternatives. A concrete lost gain may be compensable. The metaphysical fertility of capital is not.
These questions can be gathered into a practical checklist for modern contracts:

1. Was there a concrete alternative use of the capital?
  2. Was that alternative use morally licit and mission-consistent?
  3. Did the lender actually intend, or virtually intend, to pursue it?
  4. Did the loan truly cause the foregone gain?
  5. Was the gain reasonably probable, rather than merely possible?
  6. Is the valuation proportionate and discounted for uncertainty?
  7. Is compensation limited to the value of the foregone gain?
  8. Is the compensation avoided as an upfront deduction or automatic charge?
  9. Is the title applied only in specific cases, rather than universalized?
  10. Is the claim separated from other titles to avoid double-counting?
  11. Is the borrower being asked to repair a real detriment, or to satisfy capital's general expectation of return?
  12. Would the structure still make moral sense if the borrower were poor, vulnerable, mission-driven, or dependent?
  13. Does the contract preserve the difference between compensation and extraction?

The final question is the most important. Does the claim restore equality, or does it create domination? A valid lucrum cessans claim should leave the parties more justly ordered: it compensates a real sacrifice made by the lender without turning the borrower's need into a revenue stream, and it stays limited, intelligible, and accountable. It should not create a structure in which capital receives a guaranteed return simply because it could imagine other places to go.

The checklist cuts against some of the market's deepest assumptions. The language of opportunity cost is everywhere: in investment committees, venture capital, public finance, philanthropy, and even mission-driven capital. It often sounds neutral, mathematical, and inevitable, but the scholastic tradition forces a more moral question: opportunity for whom, at whose expense, with what proof, and under what obligation?

For non-extractive finance, lucrum cessans must therefore be handled with unusual care. It may sometimes justify compensation; it should never be allowed to become the ordinary architecture of return. The moment foregone profit becomes generic, the title has drifted, and the moment every financing arrangement begins with the premise that capital must be compensated for not being elsewhere, the old usury problem has returned in modern dress.

The diagnostic discipline of the tradition is therefore not antiquarian. It is a tool for moral clarity: it makes finance name its claims, ties every surplus to a demonstrated burden, separates real sacrifice from entitlement, and asks whether the structure serves life or merely protects the expectation that money, simply by being withheld for a time, must always come back with more.

chapters.part: Part VII — Lucrum Cessans: Foregone Profit and the Dangerous Birth of Opportunity Cost

chapters.title: Why Periculum Sortis Is Different
chapters.label: Chapter 24

sections.title: 24.1 Definition
paragraphs: Periculum sortis means the risk of the principal: the risk of the capital sum, the risk that the sors itself may be lost. It is the fourth and most structurally disruptive of the major extrinsic titles. Where poena conventionalis concerns culpable delay, damnum emergens concerns actual loss incurred, and lucrum cessans concerns profit foregone, periculum sortis concerns exposure to downside. It asks whether the lender, investor, or capital provider is genuinely at risk of losing the capital itself.

The word sors can mean the principal sum, the capital originally advanced, the stake. In usury analysis this matters because the ordinary mutuum requires the borrower to return the equivalent principal. The lender gives money or another fungible good, ownership passes, and the borrower owes the tantundem, the equivalent amount of the same kind and quality. In the pure form of the loan for consumption, the lender's principal claim remains intact and the borrower bears the burden of repayment. The capital provider may face practical default risk, but legally and morally the borrower still owes the full equivalent.

Periculum sortis becomes relevant when this structure changes, or when the lender can plausibly claim that the principal itself is genuinely exposed. If the capital provider may lose the capital depending on the outcome of the venture, the transaction begins to resemble partnership, investment, sea loan, commenda, insurance, or some other risk-bearing form rather than a simple guaranteed loan. The question then becomes whether the surplus payment is compensation for bearing real capital risk rather than payment for the use of money as such.

This is why periculum sortis matters for the history of moral finance: it pushes the usury debate beyond the question of time and into the question of downside. The older prohibition is most forceful where the lender demands a guaranteed surplus while the borrower bears the economic vulnerability. The lender's capital must come back, and more must come back with it. But if the lender can actually lose the principal, the moral structure is different. The surplus may be not a payment extracted from the borrower's need but the lender's share in a risky venture.

Yet the title is dangerous precisely because every loan involves some risk: death, fraud, insolvency, business collapse, and ordinary misfortune can all prevent repayment. If every possibility of default justified surplus, every loan could become interest-bearing and the prohibition would collapse. A lender would only need to say, "I might not be repaid," and that is true of nearly every human contract.

The central problem of periculum sortis is therefore not whether risk exists (risk always exists) but what kind of risk counts. Is ordinary default risk enough? Is the risk of nonpayment by a poor or unreliable borrower enough? Must the lender assume special risk beyond the normal risks of lending? Must repayment be contingent on the success of a venture? Must the lender lose legal recourse if the project fails? Must the capital be subordinated, unsecured, or genuinely loss-absorbing? These questions determine whether periculum sortis remains a real extrinsic title or becomes a universal justification for interest.

The strictest version of the tradition answers that ordinary default risk cannot be enough. In a normal mutuum the borrower remains obligated to repay. The lender may face the practical risk that the borrower will not or cannot pay, but that does not transform the loan into a partnership. The lender still has a claim: he may pursue repayment, enforce against the borrower, use collateral, guarantees, or legal remedies. The risk is a risk of enforcement, not necessarily a shared risk of the enterprise.

A stronger form of periculum sortis arises where the capital provider's claim is genuinely contingent. If the venture fails, the capital may be lost; if the ship sinks, the sea loan may not be repaid; if the trading expedition fails, the investor in a partnership may suffer loss; if the enterprise produces no surplus, the investor may receive nothing. In such cases the provider of capital is not merely waiting for repayment but participating in the uncertainty of the undertaking. The capital is exposed to the outcome.

This distinction between risk of enforcement and risk of participation is crucial. A lender who has a fixed claim, collateral, guarantees, seniority, and legal recourse may say that repayment is uncertain, but the borrower is still the one who carries the main economic burden; the lender's downside is protected as far as possible. A partner or genuinely subordinated investor, by contrast, may have no claim to repayment if the venture fails. The capital provider's fortune is tied to the fortune of the enterprise. That is a different moral form.

Aquinas supplies the architecture for this distinction, though he does not develop periculum sortis as a named title in the later sense. In his discussion of usury he distinguishes the mutuum from partnership. If money is transferred by way of a loan, the borrower owns it and owes the principal. But if money is entrusted to a merchant by way of partnership, the capital owner may share in the profit because he also bears the risk. Profit-sharing becomes licit where the capital remains exposed. The moral issue is not merely money's productivity but who bears loss.

This is why periculum sortis becomes the most important extrinsic title for modern finance. Modern debt, venture capital, private equity, revenue-based finance, preferred equity, convertible instruments, guarantees, liquidation preferences, and collateral structures all depend on the allocation of downside, and legal form alone is insufficient. An instrument may be called "equity" and still protect capital through preference, control, redemption rights, and exit pressure. Another may be called "debt" and include genuine loss-sharing, subordination, or repayment tied to available surplus. The scholastic question is: who can actually lose?

This is also why periculum sortis cannot be reduced to a "risk premium" in the modern sense. Modern finance often treats risk as something that can be priced into return while the borrower or company remains obligated to protect capital: the investor demands a higher yield because the borrower is riskier. From a scholastic perspective, the key question is not whether the lender faces statistical uncertainty but whether the lender actually bears the downside in the moral and legal structure of the transaction. Risk that is priced but shifted away is not the same as risk that is shared.

The title therefore forces a distinction between loss-bearing and loss-shifting. If capital receives extra return because the enterprise is uncertain, but the contract still allows capital to recover first, seize collateral, enforce guarantees, impose penalties, or leave workers, founders, suppliers, or communities absorbing the failure, then the risk has not been shared so much as monetized. The lender has charged for risk while retaining protection against it. That is exactly the structure a moral theology of periculum sortis must scrutinize.

At its best, periculum sortis points toward solidarity. The capital provider says: "I will not demand gain unless I also bear the possibility of loss." This aligns surplus with participation, makes profit contingent upon the success of the shared undertaking, and refuses the asymmetry in which the borrower must pay regardless of outcome while the lender claims extra reward for having faced uncertainty. Properly understood, the title is not a loophole for charging more but a demand that return be joined to vulnerability.

At its worst, periculum sortis becomes the most powerful wedge by which usury doctrine is hollowed out. If every lender can charge because every borrower might default, then risk becomes the universal justification for interest. The older moral test disappears: the lender need not prove actual loss, culpable delay, concrete foregone profit, or genuine participation in downside. He simply invokes uncertainty, and the title becomes indistinguishable from modern risk-adjusted pricing.

The difference between these two versions is the difference between non-extractive capital and extractive capital that has merely improved its vocabulary. A non-extractive structure uses periculum sortis to ask whether capital is truly at risk with the enterprise. Is repayment contingent on success? Is capital subordinated to mission, labor, or community? Are returns capped or tied to real surplus? Are losses shared proportionately? Does the investor lack unilateral power to extract repayment regardless of consequences? These are the questions that make the title morally serious.

In this sense periculum sortis is not merely one title among others; it is the bridge between usury doctrine and the moral structure of investment. It helps explain why partnership profit can be legitimate while guaranteed interest remains suspect, and it helps expose modern instruments that claim to be risk-bearing while shifting risk downward. The issue is not whether capital is present but whether capital serves, participates, and shares vulnerability, or demands protection for itself and uncertainty for everyone else.

The definition must therefore be narrow enough to preserve the usury prohibition and broad enough to recognize real investment. It cannot mean merely that repayment is never absolutely certain; it must mean that the capital provider genuinely bears risk to the principal in the structure of the transaction. The sors must be exposed. The capital must be capable of being lost, not merely delayed, inconvenienced, or made statistically uncertain. Only then can surplus plausibly be understood as compensation for risk rather than payment for the loan itself.

This is the threshold on which Part VIII stands. The next several chapters will trace how the title emerged through partnership, sea loans, and risk-bearing contracts; how Aquinas provided the architecture without the later terminology; how Alexander of Alexandria gave an early named formulation; how the Spanish scholastics and Jesuits expanded the title; and how later critics warned that risk could become the most dangerous pretext of all. The whole debate turns on one simple but devastating question: when capital receives more, was it truly willing to lose?

sections.title: 24.2 Why This Title Is Structurally Different
paragraphs: Periculum sortis is not simply another item in the list of extrinsic titles; it changes the angle of the whole inquiry. Poena conventionalis, damnum emergens, and lucrum cessans all concern burdens that arise around the loan: delay, loss, or missed gain. They ask whether some event connected to the transaction has injured the lender in a way that may be restored without turning the loan itself into a source of profit. Periculum sortis asks a more structural question: what kind of transaction is this, and who is actually exposed to loss?

That makes it the most architecturally important of the titles. A late penalty can be added without changing the loan's basic form. Actual loss can arise from circumstances surrounding the loan. Foregone profit can be claimed because the lender's money was unavailable for another use. But capital risk concerns the internal distribution of vulnerability. It asks whether the capital provider is merely a creditor awaiting repayment, or has truly joined the uncertain fate of the undertaking.

The difference is not cosmetic. In a standard mutuum, ownership passes to the borrower and the borrower owes the equivalent principal. The lender's legal and moral claim is fixed: the tantundem must be returned. The borrower may use, consume, spend, or transform the money, but the obligation remains. If the venture fails, the harvest comes in poor, or the business collapses, the debt still exists, unless it is forgiven, restructured, or rendered unenforceable by law or circumstance. The creditor's claim is structurally protected.

In a partnership, commenda, sea loan, or genuinely loss-bearing investment, the structure is different. The provider of capital may have no unconditional claim to repayment; the return of principal may depend on the success of the voyage, trade, enterprise, or project. Profit is possible because loss is possible. The capital provider participates in the venture's uncertainty rather than standing outside it with a fixed claim against the borrower, which is why the moral analysis cannot be limited to the presence or absence of surplus. It must ask how the surplus is related to downside.

This is the distinctive contribution of periculum sortis: it moves the analysis from event-based compensation to structural participation. The first three titles can often be analyzed after the fact. Was there a delay? Was it culpable? Was there actual damage? Was there a foregone gain? Was it concrete? Periculum sortis requires us to analyze the contract before the fact. What happens if the project fails? Who absorbs the loss? Who has legal recourse? Who is senior? Who is collateralized? Who can walk away? Who remains obligated?

This is why ordinary default risk is not enough. A creditor always faces the possibility that the debtor will fail to pay, but if the debtor remains legally bound and repayment can be pursued through collateral, guarantees, enforcement, or claims against other assets, the creditor has not necessarily shared the enterprise's risk. He has faced collection risk: real, but not the same as participation in loss, and the borrower still carries the burden of the failed outcome.

The title therefore turns on the difference between risk as uncertainty and risk as burden. Modern finance often treats risk as uncertainty: a probability distribution, a volatility measure, a default likelihood, a risk-adjusted return. Scholastic moral theology is more interested in risk as moral burden: when loss happens, who must bear it? Statistical uncertainty may justify caution; it does not automatically justify surplus. What matters is whether the lender is exposed in such a way that capital can genuinely be diminished or lost without the loss simply being transferred to the borrower.

The distinction is especially important because risk can be priced while still being shifted. A lender may charge a higher rate because the borrower is risky, while also demanding collateral, personal guarantees, seniority, covenants, penalties, default interest, and enforcement rights. In that case the lender has monetized risk while attempting to avoid bearing it. The borrower pays for being risky, but still bears the consequences if things go wrong. Such a structure may be legal and commercially ordinary. The logic of periculum sortis asks whether it is morally coherent.

A genuinely risk-bearing structure looks different. The capital provider's return is tied to the success of the enterprise; the capital may be subordinated; repayment may be limited to available surplus; losses may reduce or extinguish the claim; the investor may lack recourse to unrelated borrower assets; the provider of capital may have to wait behind workers, suppliers, community obligations, or mission commitments. In such cases the capital provider is not charging for risk but participating in it.

This is why periculum sortis reaches toward the modern distinction between debt and equity, though it cannot be reduced to it. Debt often resembles the mutuum because it preserves a fixed claim to repayment; equity often resembles societas because it shares upside and downside. But legal labels are not decisive. Debt can be structured with real loss-sharing features, and equity can be structured with preferences, redemption rights, liquidation priorities, drag-along rights, and governance powers that protect capital while placing pressure on founders, workers, and mission. The scholastic question cuts beneath the label: who can lose, and who is protected?

This makes periculum sortis more radical than it first appears. It does not merely justify a risk premium; it interrogates the moral architecture of capital itself, asking whether profit is joined to vulnerability, whether surplus is earned by participation or extracted through protected priority, whether the capital provider is a partner in uncertainty or a creditor insulated from the consequences of the very venture from which surplus is sought.

The title also reveals why priority matters. If capital is repaid before labor, mission, community, ecological repair, suppliers, and the long-term life of the enterprise, capital may be formally at risk but practically privileged: a senior claim can make loss possible in theory while shifting actual loss downward in practice. The stricter Franciscan intuition is especially sensitive here. A structure that protects capital first may fail the deeper moral test even if it contains some statistical uncertainty, because the question is not only whether capital can lose in the abstract but where capital stands in the order of loss.

The implications for modern non-extractive investment are direct. A fund may say that its return is justified by risk, but the moral question is: what risk? If the fund has downside protection, repayment priority, collateral, contractual control, and the ability to force an exit, its risk may be less than the risk borne by the entrepreneur, the workers, or the community. If the entrepreneur can lose vocation, reputation, livelihood, health, relationships, and mission while capital merely suffers a delayed or reduced return, the language of risk needs to be examined carefully.

Periculum sortis therefore introduces a relational account of finance. It refuses to treat risk as an abstract property of capital alone. Risk exists among persons and institutions; it is distributed by contract, shaped by law, intensified by need and bargaining power, and hidden in governance rights, liquidation waterfalls, guarantees, collateral, and default clauses. A moral analysis of risk must ask not only how much uncertainty exists, but who is made vulnerable by the structure.

This also explains why periculum sortis belongs with stewardship. A steward does not merely demand return while protecting himself from loss but accepts responsibility for the life of the thing entrusted. In finance this means capital must be willing to serve the enterprise rather than dominate it. If capital claims upside while refusing downside, it acts less like stewardship and more like ownership absolutism, treating the enterprise as an instrument for its own preservation and growth.

The structural difference of periculum sortis can be summarized in one diagnostic contrast. The first three titles ask: did something happen that justifies compensation beyond principal? The fourth asks: what has the lender become in relation to the borrower or the enterprise? A creditor, a partner, an insurer, a co-adventurer, a steward, or a protected extractor? The answer determines whether surplus looks like restoration, participation, or domination.

This is why the title is indispensable for analyzing modern capital structures. It is not enough to ask whether a return is capped, moderate, patient, or socially motivated. A capped return can still be extractive if it is guaranteed and senior; a patient loan can still dominate if the borrower must carry all the downside; a mission investment can still undermine mission if it uses the language of risk while protecting capital through priority and control. The title requires us to look at the full loss waterfall.

For the same reason, periculum sortis is structurally different from lucrum cessans as well. Lucrum cessans asks what gain the lender did not receive because the money was lent; periculum sortis asks whether the lender can lose the money because of the transaction's outcome. The first can easily become opportunity-cost logic, the second risk-premium logic, and both are dangerous when universalized. But periculum sortis has the deeper transformative potential, because it points toward genuine risk-sharing. It asks not how capital should be compensated but whether capital has accepted vulnerability.

This is the heart of the matter. The moral legitimacy of surplus depends not only on what is paid but on the structure that makes payment possible. If the borrower bears all loss while capital receives protected gain, surplus tends toward extraction; if capital shares loss and receives gain only through participation, surplus becomes more intelligible as just. Periculum sortis names this difference, which is why it is not merely an extrinsic title but a test of whether finance has become a relationship of shared fate.

sections.title: 24.3 The Danger
paragraphs: The danger of periculum sortis is simple: every loan has risk. No repayment is absolutely certain, and even the most secure debtor may fail. A crop can be destroyed, a ship can sink, a king can default, and a borrower can disappear, deceive, or die. Human affairs are contingent. If the mere possibility of nonpayment were enough to justify surplus, every loan could be made interest-bearing.

This is what makes periculum sortis the most dangerous of the extrinsic titles. Poena conventionalis can be limited by asking whether the delay was culpable, whether the due date was realistic, and whether the penalty was proportionate. Damnum emergens can be limited by asking whether actual loss was documented, caused, and restorative. Lucrum cessans can be limited by asking whether a concrete, licit, probable gain really ceased. But risk is everywhere: it can be invoked before anything has happened, priced into the structure from the beginning, and generalized across whole classes of borrowers, until usury looks like prudence.

The title therefore threatens to universalize interest through the language of uncertainty. The lender need not say that he charges because time has passed, nor claim to sell the use of money; he need only say, "I may not be repaid." Once that is enough, the usury prohibition remains formally intact but practically empty. The rule says that gain from the loan itself is forbidden; the market replies that every loan contains risk, and every risk deserves a premium. The result is a doctrine defeated by its own exception.

The tradition therefore had to distinguish narrow from broad versions of the title. The narrow version says that surplus may be justified only when the lender truly bears risk to the principal in the structure of the transaction. The capital must be genuinely capable of being lost; repayment must depend, in some real way, on the outcome of the enterprise, voyage, trade, or project; the lender must not simply possess a fixed legal claim against the borrower regardless of outcome. In this narrow version, periculum sortis points toward partnership, commenda, sea loan, insurance-like risk-bearing, and genuine investment.

The broad version says that any risk of nonpayment may justify surplus, and this is the dangerous version. It treats ordinary default risk as sufficient. The borrower still owes the principal; the lender still has legal recourse; collateral may still be pledged, guarantees still demanded, default penalties still imposed; the lender may still stand senior to workers, suppliers, community, and mission. Yet because repayment is not absolutely certain, the lender claims a premium. Risk has been invoked, but not truly shared.

The difference between these two versions is decisive. In the narrow version, surplus is linked to vulnerability accepted by capital; in the broad version, surplus is linked to vulnerability imposed on the borrower. The narrow version asks whether the lender can lose; the broad version reasons that the borrower might fail and must therefore pay more. One turns capital toward participation; the other turns borrower weakness into capital's justification for gain.

This is the moral inversion at the heart of the danger. A borrower who is more fragile may be charged more because the lender's risk is higher, but if the lender remains structurally protected, the higher charge can deepen the borrower's fragility. The poorer borrower pays more because he is poor; the less secure enterprise pays more because it is insecure; the mission-driven company pays more because it lacks collateral. Risk pricing then becomes a way of extracting from vulnerability while appearing merely rational.

Modern finance treats this as obvious: higher risk requires higher return, and in many contexts that seems like common sense. The scholastic tradition asks a more uncomfortable question: who created, bears, and benefits from that risk? If the lender receives higher compensation because the borrower is vulnerable, while the borrower remains legally obligated and economically exposed, then the premium may be less a reward for shared risk than a tax on weakness.

This does not mean that risk is irrelevant. It would be unjust to demand that a capital provider accept genuine exposure without any possibility of proportionate return; the tradition asks capital not to be blind but to be honest. A return justified by periculum sortis must correspond to risk actually borne by the capital provider, not merely risk observed in the borrower. It must be tied to loss absorption rather than default probability alone, and it must distinguish between participating in uncertainty and charging others for being uncertain.

The practical danger is that contracts can blur this distinction. A lender may describe itself as taking risk while building in protections that shift loss elsewhere: collateral, security, personal guarantees, covenants, acceleration rights, default interest, liquidation priority, control rights, or mandatory exit provisions. Each clause may be defensible on its own. Together, they may create a structure in which the lender's downside is cushioned while the borrower's downside is intensified, so that the lender charges for risk while refusing, as far as possible, to bear it.

This is why periculum sortis requires a full loss-waterfall analysis. One cannot judge the title by looking only at the stated return; one must ask what happens in failure. Who gets paid first? Who waits? Who is written down? Who can enforce, and who can seize assets? Who loses labor, livelihood, reputation, vocation, mission, or community? Who has legal remedies, and who has only consequences? The moral location of risk is revealed not in success but in failure.

The title is also dangerous because it can be double-counted. A lender may charge a risk premium, require collateral, impose penalties, claim administrative fees, deduct upfront charges, and demand compensation for foregone profit; but if all these charges are responses to the same underlying uncertainty, the borrower is paying multiple times for one burden. Scholastic moral reasoning resists this. Each title must name a distinct reality, and risk cannot be invoked as a general atmosphere that justifies every surplus claim.

Another danger is that periculum sortis can obscure the difference between risk and speculation. Genuine risk-bearing can be morally legitimate when it supports real enterprise, shared work, and common benefit. But speculation may seek return precisely from volatility, distress, or asymmetry: the capital provider profits not by serving a productive undertaking but by positioning itself advantageously within uncertainty. In such cases risk language can become morally flattering. It makes the speculator appear brave, even when the structure allows others to absorb the deepest harms.

The danger intensifies in venture capital and private equity. These forms often present themselves as risk capital, and sometimes they are: investors can lose everything, accepting uncertainty and receiving nothing if the company fails. But specific contract terms matter. Liquidation preferences, participating preferred shares, redemption rights, anti-dilution provisions, board control, drag-along rights, and exit pressure can change the moral structure, so that capital is legally equity but economically privileged, sharing some risk while controlling the conditions under which others bear more.

This is why periculum sortis cannot be satisfied by the word "equity." The title demands substantive vulnerability. If capital claims the upside of ownership, the governance power of control, and the downside protection of debt, the structure becomes morally suspect. The investor may say it bears risk because the company could fail, but if in partial failure the investor is protected first while everyone else absorbs the damage, the risk has not been shared so much as ranked.

The strict Franciscan concern about priority repayment belongs here.
