If capital is repaid before mission, labor, community, and the continued life of the enterprise, the moral claim to risk-bearing must be examined, because a structure can be loss-exposed at the outer edge while arranging ordinary distress so that others suffer first. That is not necessarily full participation in risk; it may be priority disguised as courage. And the danger is not only theoretical. It appears wherever capital receives the language of heroism for accepting uncertainty while the deepest vulnerability falls on others. To call both losses simply "risk" is morally imprecise: founders lose years of life and vocation, workers lose jobs, communities lose essential services, suppliers go unpaid, and the mission is compromised, while capital writes down an investment and moves on.

A scholastic reconstruction of periculum sortis must therefore be more exact. Risk has depth, order, and quality: legal risk is not the same as existential risk, delayed repayment is not the same as loss of livelihood, and portfolio loss is not the same as community collapse. A fund that loses one investment among many is not exposed in the same way as an entrepreneur whose entire life is bound up with the enterprise. The title must take account of these asymmetries.

This does not mean that capital can receive no return unless it suffers as much as everyone else; it means the justification for return must be truthful. If the return is said to arise from risk, then risk must be genuinely borne, and if capital is protected by priority, collateral, diversification, governance power, and exit rights, those protections must be part of the moral analysis. The title cannot be evaluated by abstract probability alone.

The deepest danger of periculum sortis is that it can convert vulnerability into price. The borrower's weakness becomes the lender's premium, the enterprise's uncertainty becomes capital's yield, and the possibility of failure becomes a reason to increase the burden placed on the party most likely to fail. This is the point at which risk pricing becomes extraction. It may be economically rational, legal, and standard; moral theology asks whether it serves justice.

For non-extractive finance, the answer must be structural. It is not enough to cap returns or use benevolent language; the contract must allocate downside in a way that corresponds to the claim being made. If the investor receives surplus because of risk, the investor must accept real loss absorption. If the capital is called patient, it must not punish distress too quickly. If it claims partnership, it must not act like senior debt. If it invokes mission, it must not abandon mission when repayment is threatened.

The danger of periculum sortis thereby becomes a diagnostic tool. Whenever a return is justified by risk, we should ask: risk to whom? Risk of what? Risk borne how? Risk protected by which rights? Risk priced once, or several times? Risk shared with the borrower, or shifted onto the borrower? Risk in legal form, or risk in economic substance? Risk that serves enterprise, or risk that feeds on vulnerability?

The title survives only if these questions are answered honestly. Otherwise periculum sortis becomes the most elegant path from the medieval usury prohibition to modern risk-adjusted return, and the tradition's warning is severe: risk can justify surplus only when it is real, proportionate, and actually borne by the one who claims the surplus. It cannot be a general excuse for charging the vulnerable more, cannot be invoked while contract terms shift the downside away, cannot be counted again and again under different names, and cannot, above all, become the principle that every uncertain loan deserves yield. If that happens, the title has swallowed the prohibition, and risk has become usury's most sophisticated disguise: the name remains scholastic, the function has become financial, and capital, once again, receives more while arranging that others lose first.

chapters.part: Part VIII — Periculum Sortis: Risk of Capital and the Moral Structure of Downside

chapters.title: Prehistory: Partnership, Sea Loans, and Risk-Bearing Contracts
chapters.label: Chapter 25

sections.title: 25.1 Societas
paragraphs: Before periculum sortis could become a named extrinsic title, Christian moral theology already possessed a more basic and more secure category for risk-bearing finance: societas, the partnership. A partnership was not a loan. It did not transfer ownership of consumable money to a borrower who then owed the equivalent principal. It created a relationship of shared undertaking, in which the parties joined for a common enterprise under some arrangement for shared gains, shared losses, labor, capital, and responsibility. That made societas one of the tradition's most important moral alternatives to the mutuum.

The difference is fundamental. In a mutuum, the borrower receives ownership of the thing lent and becomes obligated to return the equivalent; the lender's claim is fixed. In a partnership, the capital provider does not stand outside the enterprise as a creditor. He participates in the venture's fortune. If the enterprise succeeds, he may share in the profit; if it fails, he may lose. The moral legitimacy of surplus therefore rests not on the loan itself but on participation in a common risk-bearing activity.

This is why Aquinas's distinction between the mutuum and the partnership matters so much. When he rejects usury, he does not reject all gain from capital. He rejects gain demanded from a loan for consumption, where the lender has transferred ownership and yet seeks payment for the use of what no longer belongs to him. But he allows that one may entrust money to a merchant by way of partnership and share in the profit, because the capital remains at the owner's risk. The money is not merely lent; it is placed into a venture whose outcome is uncertain.

The phrase "at the owner's risk" is doing the moral work here. Profit is not justified because money automatically produces money. Profit is justified because the owner of capital has exposed his capital to loss within a shared enterprise: he has not demanded guaranteed increase from another's need, and he has accepted that the venture may fail and his capital may not come back. Later discussions of periculum sortis will develop exactly this insight.

Societas, then, is the positive form of the risk title, the shape morally intelligible risk-bearing takes before anyone abstracts it into a premium. The partner is not compensated for the statistical possibility of nonpayment. He joins the adventure, placing capital under the uncertainty of a real undertaking; his gain depends on the venture's gain, his loss follows the venture's loss, and the surplus he receives, if any, is not a charge imposed on the other party but his share in a common result.

Notice how far this stands from a guaranteed loan wearing a risk premium. There the borrower owes principal and surplus whether the financed activity succeeds or not. The lender may say he is taking risk, but the borrower remains bound; if the project fails, the obligation survives it. In a partnership, failure changes the claims. The capital provider cannot insist on being made whole while the active partner absorbs the consequences, because the capital is part of the venture, not merely a claim against it.

None of this made partnerships morally simple: the Roman and medieval legal traditions classified them in various ways, and questions could always arise about fraud, unequal bargaining power, the division of labor, the division of gains, and the allocation of losses. But the basic category mattered because it gave theologians a way to say: profit from capital is not always usury. The question is not whether capital receives something, but whether it has remained outside the risk while demanding a return, or has entered into shared vulnerability.

Critics of usury doctrine often miss this point. The tradition was not hostile to commerce, enterprise, investment, or profit as such. It was hostile to a specific asymmetry: guaranteed gain to the lender from a consumptive loan, while the borrower carries the economic burden. Partnership offered a different structure, gain tied to participation, labor, risk, and common enterprise, and it showed that the tradition could tell extractive lending apart from productive cooperation.

The partnership model also clarifies what stewardship means. A partner does not merely own a claim; he has a stake in the life of the enterprise, and must care about its success, not only its repayment schedule. That does not automatically make every partnership virtuous, but it changes the moral posture. The capital provider's interest is bound to the enterprise's flourishing. If the venture is destroyed, he suffers too. There is at least the possibility of shared fate.

That shared fate is the heart of periculum sortis in its morally strongest form. Risk is not an excuse to charge more; risk is the condition that makes shared profit intelligible. The capital provider says, in effect, "I may receive more if the enterprise succeeds, because I may also receive less or nothing if it fails," which is not at all the same as saying, "You must pay me more because you might fail to repay." The first joins the venture; the second prices the borrower's fragility.

The difference becomes pointed in modern investment contracts. Many instruments are called "equity" without functioning like true partnership. Preferred shares may carry liquidation preferences. Investors may receive priority repayment before founders, workers, or mission commitments see anything. Redemption rights may allow capital to force repayment; control rights may let investors compel an exit even when the enterprise's mission would be harmed by it. In such cases the legal label of equity conceals a creditor-like priority.

The traffic runs the other way too. A revenue-based financing agreement may cap returns, defer payments when revenue falls, avoid personal guarantees, and accept genuine downside. A patient loan may subordinate capital to wages, taxes, suppliers, and mission obligations. A recoverable grant may return funds only if the project succeeds. None of these is societas in the strict legal sense, but they can come closer to the moral logic of shared risk than some forms of preferred equity manage.

The lesson of societas, then, is not that modern finance should swap all debt for equity, but that moral analysis must look beneath legal labels to the actual allocation of risk, control, and surplus. Who participates in the venture? Who has a fixed claim? Who benefits from success? Who suffers in failure? Who has the power to force repayment or exit? Who waits, who absorbs loss, and who is protected?

In the older scholastic framework, societas also helps preserve the gratuity of the mutuum. If a person wants a guaranteed claim to the return of principal, he should not also demand the gains of partnership; if he wants to share profit, he must share risk. The two forms cannot be casually combined without moral danger. A lender who takes the legal security of debt and the economic upside of partnership is not building a more sophisticated contract; he may be building a disguised form of domination.

Which is precisely the issue in many contemporary capital structures. Investors often seek debt-like downside protection with equity-like upside: priority, collateral, covenants, liquidation preferences, anti-dilution rights, control rights, and exit rights, all while claiming the moral dignity of risk capital. The scholastic tradition would ask whether that combination is coherent. If capital is truly a partner, it must not arrange the relationship so that others lose first while it gains first.

The moral beauty of societas, at least in its ideal form, is that it binds gain to common work and common uncertainty. Capital is not allowed to remain aloof from the fate of the borrower; it is invited into relationship, and its profit becomes a share in a venture both parties made possible rather than a toll on another's need. That does not eliminate every problem of inequality, but it gives moral theology a structure in which surplus reads as participation rather than usury.

For non-extractive finance, societas offers a deep design principle: return should follow shared fate. If capital receives more when things go well, it should receive less when things go badly. If it claims participation in upside, it should accept participation in downside. If it wants the language of partnership, it should accept the vulnerability of partnership. The more a structure protects capital from loss, the more suspect its claim to partnership becomes.

This also explains why periculum sortis cannot be treated as a mere pricing tool. Modern finance translates risk into return almost by reflex: higher risk, higher required return. Societas teaches a different order of thought. First comes relationship, then shared undertaking, then shared exposure; only then does shared profit become intelligible. Risk is not a number attached to a loan; it is a condition of moral participation.

The partnership tradition also remembers that capital is not the only contribution. One partner may bring money, another labor, another expertise, another access, another reputation, another management. Justice requires attention to all of them, which matters for modern enterprises, where founders, workers, communities, and even ecosystems contribute value and bear risk that contracts often fail to recognize. A societas worth the name does not let financial capital treat every other contribution as subordinate.

In this sense the recovery of societas is not antiquarian. It reimagines finance around participation rather than extraction, asking not simply how to compensate capital for risk but how to design institutions in which capital, labor, mission, community, and stewardship are ordered toward a shared good. Partnership becomes not only a contract form but a moral image of economic life.

Societas stands at the beginning of the prehistory of periculum sortis because it is the tradition's clearest answer to the problem of capital and gain, an answer the later risk title must never be allowed to forget. If capital remains a creditor, surplus is suspect. If capital becomes a partner, surplus may be just, and only because capital has also become vulnerable: the legitimacy of profit depends on the willingness to share loss.

sections.title: 25.2 Commenda
paragraphs: If societas gives the general moral architecture of partnership, the commenda gives one of its most important commercial forms. It was a practical instrument for long-distance trade, above all in the medieval Mediterranean. One party, often a capital provider who stayed at home, entrusted goods or money to a traveling merchant, who used that capital in a commercial venture, usually overseas or across a long trade route. If the venture succeeded, the parties divided the profits according to an agreed formula. If it failed, the capital provider could lose the capital, and the traveling merchant could lose his labor, his time, his effort, and sometimes his reputation.

That structure made the commenda morally significant, because it did not function like a simple loan. The active merchant was not a borrower who owed the principal regardless of outcome; the silent capital provider was not a creditor waiting to be repaid. The capital was committed to an undertaking and exposed to the fortunes of trade, voyage, market, theft, weather, war, and commercial judgment. Profit was possible because loss was possible. The return to capital was tied to periculum sortis, risk of the principal, rather than to the mere passage of time.

The commenda also solved a practical problem. Some people had capital but could not or would not travel. Others had skill, courage, local knowledge, or commercial access, but not enough capital to trade with. The commenda joined these contributions without reducing one party to a debtor and the other to a creditor, which made it a powerful vehicle for trade and, just as important, a moral alternative to usurious lending.

In the simplest moral terms, the commenda says: capital may receive gain when capital joins risk. The silent partner's profit is not a fixed payment owed merely because money was advanced; it is a share in the result of the venture. If no profit is made, there may be nothing to share. If the capital is lost without fault, the capital provider bears that loss, and the traveling merchant may not owe the tantundem as he would in a mutuum. The contrast can be stated sharply. In a mutuum, the borrower must return the equivalent principal even if the money was used badly, lost, spent, or consumed. In a commenda, the capital provider's claim is tied to the commercial outcome: the active merchant must act faithfully and competently, but he is not a guarantor of the capital against the ordinary hazards of the venture. His obligation is fiduciary and practical, not debtor-like, and that changes the moral form of the relationship.

This is why the commenda appears in the prehistory of periculum sortis: it shows that medieval commercial practice already contained risk-bearing capital that did not have to be condemned as usury. The tradition did not need to pretend that trade could occur without capital, or that capital could never receive more than it put in. It needed to distinguish risk-sharing investment from guaranteed gain on a loan, and the commenda made that distinction visible in the ordinary business of the ports.

The form also reveals that capital risk is not the only kind of risk. The silent partner risks the principal. The traveling merchant risks labor, opportunity, bodily safety, time, and reputation, sometimes his standing in the commercial community along with it. The capital provider's risk is financial; the merchant's is existential and practical. A just arrangement has to consider the contributions and vulnerabilities of both, and it would be too simple to hand the capital provider all the profit because he supplied the money. Without the merchant's work, judgment, and exposure, the capital would have sat at home doing nothing.

The point carries into modern finance with uncomfortable ease. Contemporary investment often overvalues financial capital because it is easily measured, contractually protected, and legally privileged. But enterprises are built from more than money. They require founders, workers, customers, communities, and trust, little of which appears on a term sheet. The commenda reminds us that a morally serious investment structure must ask what each party contributes and what each party risks. Money is one contribution. It is not the whole enterprise.

At its best, the commenda embodies complementarity: one party contributes capital, the other labor and commercial agency; neither alone is sufficient, and the profit, if there is profit, arises from their cooperation. The moral basis of distribution is participation in a common undertaking, a very different logic from a lender's demand to be repaid with surplus whatever happens to the borrower's activity.

But the commenda had its own moral dangers, and the scholastics knew it. Any contract form can be abused. A supposed commenda could be structured so that the traveling merchant effectively guaranteed the capital while the capital provider still claimed a share of profit, and the arrangement then resembled a loan with disguised interest. The active merchant could be pressured into obligations that shifted too much of the downside onto him. The legal name settled nothing; the theologian or confessor still had to ask who truly bore the loss. Here is a theme that recurs through the whole scholastic analysis: legal form is not moral substance. A contract may be called partnership but function like debt. It may be called exchange but conceal a loan. It may be called investment but impose repayment obligations that eliminate any real risk to capital. The commenda was licit not because of its name but because of its structure when properly formed: the capital provider's principal genuinely exposed, the active merchant's obligation something other than repay-no-matter-what, the profit contingent upon success.

The commenda thereby clarifies the difference between risk-bearing and risk-shifting. In a genuine commenda, the silent partner cannot claim the upside while forcing the active merchant to insure the downside. The capital provider has accepted periculum sortis; if the venture fails without fault, he bears the loss. If the structure instead requires the merchant to restore the capital regardless of ordinary commercial failure, then the capital provider has shifted the very risk that was supposed to justify his profit. The title collapses into pretext.

That distinction bears directly on venture capital and private equity. These forms claim to be risk capital, and in some respects they are: investors may indeed lose their investment if the company fails. But the details matter. Liquidation preferences, redemption rights, control provisions, protective covenants, forced sale rights, and senior claims can shift practical risk away from capital and onto founders, workers, or the mission of the enterprise. The arrangement may remain equity in law while ceasing, morally, to resemble a true commenda or societas. It becomes a hybrid that takes partnership upside while preserving creditor privilege.

The commenda also raises the question of control. The silent partner provides capital but does not undertake the journey or conduct the trade, so there must be trust: the active merchant must have enough freedom to act. If the capital provider controls every decision while avoiding the practical burdens of the journey, the moral relationship changes underneath the paperwork. Modern investors exercise considerable control through board seats, consent rights, vetoes, reporting requirements, and exit rights. Some control may be legitimate stewardship. But excessive control can transform partnership into domination, especially when capital also receives priority.

A moral reading of the commenda does not romanticize medieval trade. It extracts a structural insight: capital may rightly share in profit when it is truly committed to the venture and exposed to loss, and the active entrepreneur may rightly share because labor, skill, and risk are real contributions too. A just contract aligns claim, contribution, risk, and control. When these come apart, the form becomes morally unstable.

For non-extractive finance, the commenda offers a model of capital that travels with the enterprise rather than standing over it. The capital provider does not merely ask, "What return do I receive?" He asks: What venture am I joining? What risk am I accepting? What contribution am I making? What claim do I have if things go well? What loss do I bear if things go badly? That is a very different posture from capital that demands fixed repayment, priority, and surplus while describing itself as mission-aligned.

The commenda also helps us think about silent capital and active stewardship. Many modern funds are silent in the sense that they do not build the enterprise day by day; founders and workers do that. Yet capital often receives stronger legal claims than the people who contribute their lives and labor. A commenda-like moral analysis would ask whether financial capital's share is proportionate to its real exposure and contribution, and whether the active stewards of the enterprise retain enough freedom, dignity, and upside to make the partnership just.

That has consequences for repayment design. In a non-extractive structure, capital might receive a capped share of surplus only when surplus exists. It might accept slower repayment when the enterprise is fragile. It might subordinate itself to wages, taxes, suppliers, and mission-critical reinvestment. It might waive claims in failure rather than pursue founders personally. These features approximate the commenda logic far more closely than a fixed debt obligation with a risk premium does, because they make return contingent upon shared success.

The same logic disciplines the language of "patient capital." Patience is not only a longer time horizon. A patient creditor may still expect full repayment with accrued return, no matter what happens. True patience includes vulnerability to outcome: the capital provider waits not only for time to pass but for the enterprise to flourish, and if flourishing does not occur, the waiting does not automatically convert into a claim against the borrower. That is closer to partnership; the alternative is delayed extraction.

At the same time, the commenda reminds us that risk-bearing capital deserves moral recognition. Non-extractive finance should not treat all return as suspect. If capital truly accepts the possibility of loss, supports real enterprise, refrains from domination, and shares gain proportionately, then surplus may be just. The problem is not that capital receives. The problem is when capital receives without sharing fate, or receives first while others lose more deeply.

The prehistory of periculum sortis passes through the commenda because this contract made shared commercial risk concrete: it put money into trade without a usurious loan, linked gain to exposure, joined silent capital to active labor, and made profit contingent rather than guaranteed, forcing moral theology to distinguish the creditor who must be repaid from the partner who may lose. Capital's claim to surplus is strongest when capital has genuinely entered the adventure; if it stays safely at home while others bear the journey, the danger, the labor, and the failure, its claim weakens. The commenda is not merely a medieval trade instrument; it is a moral image of finance as shared venture, and a warning against every structure that wants the profits of partnership without the vulnerability of partnership.

sections.title: 25.3 Foenus Nauticum
paragraphs: The foenus nauticum, or sea loan, is one of the most revealing premodern forms of risk-bearing finance, sitting as it does at the boundary between loan, investment, insurance, and partnership. In its basic form, capital was advanced for a maritime venture, and repayment depended on the safe arrival or successful completion of the voyage. The sea was dangerous; if the ship was lost, the lender's principal could be lost with it; and for that reason the lender could receive a higher return than would be permissible in an ordinary mutuum. The crucial point was not that time passed while the ship was away. The crucial point was that the capital stood exposed to maritime risk.

That made the sea loan morally different from a simple loan for consumption. In a normal mutuum, the borrower receives ownership of money or other fungible goods and owes the equivalent principal regardless of what happens; the lender may face practical uncertainty about repayment, but the borrower remains obligated. In a sea loan, by contrast, repayment could be contingent on the successful outcome of the voyage itself. If the ship arrived safely, the lender might be repaid with a substantial premium. If the ship was lost, the lender might lose the principal. The claim was tied to the adventure.

The foenus nauticum, then, provides a concrete image of periculum sortis. The lender's sors, the principal, is not merely delayed or inconveniently out of reach; it is genuinely at risk, and it may not come back at all. The surplus, when permitted, was justified not as payment for the use of money alone but as compensation for assuming the risk of loss. The lender had, in a sense, insured or underwritten the venture. He had accepted a danger that would otherwise fall more heavily on the merchant or the shipper, and his return corresponded, at least in principle, to that exposure.

This is why the sea loan placed such pressure on strict usury doctrine. It looked like a loan, used the language of lending, and involved repayment with more than principal. But its moral structure was not identical to the ordinary mutuum, because the maritime condition mattered: the lender's gain was paired with the possibility of his loss. The form forced theologians and jurists to ask whether all surplus above principal was usury, or whether risk-bearing transformed the nature of the transaction. The answer depended on the allocation of risk. If the lender truly bore the maritime risk, then the higher return could be defended as compensation for bearing it. If the contract was structured so that the borrower had to repay whatever became of the ship, then the supposed sea loan collapsed back into an ordinary loan with interest. The name alone settled nothing. The moral question was whether repayment was genuinely contingent upon the risky event.

A voyage may be dangerous, in other words, but the danger must be borne by the party claiming the premium. A lender who charges for danger while shifting the danger back to the borrower holds a hollow title: he has monetized risk without sharing it. That is precisely the danger the later discussions of periculum sortis will have to confront on dry land.

The sea loan also shows why risk-bearing finance can be socially useful. Maritime trade required capital, and it involved hazards that individual merchants could not always bear alone. By advancing capital under conditions tied to the voyage, the lender could enable trade while absorbing part of the downside. There is nothing inherently extractive in that. In a properly structured sea loan, the lender's return is linked to a real service: the assumption of a risk that makes the venture possible.

Here the analogy to insurance becomes important. The sea lender does not merely provide money; he also accepts a particular, defined danger, and the premium may be understood as payment for bearing that risk, much as an insurer receives a premium for assuming a specified danger. This does not make the transaction morally simple. Insurance itself can be fair or exploitative, proportionate or excessive, socially useful or speculative. But it clarifies why the sea loan was never quite the same thing as a guaranteed interest-bearing loan: the lender had taken on a defined exposure.

The foenus nauticum thereby separates two questions that modern finance often blends together. First, has capital been made available? Second, has risk been borne by the capital provider? In an ordinary loan, the first may be true while the second stays limited, especially where repayment is guaranteed, collateralized, or enforceable against the borrower's other assets. In a genuine sea loan, the second question is the whole point. The lender receives more not simply because his money was unavailable for a term, but because his money could disappear with the ship.

This made the sea loan an important precedent, and a dangerous one. It provided a historical case in which surplus above principal seemed morally intelligible because the lender's principal was exposed to loss, and later theologians could reach for the same logic when discussing other forms of commercial risk. If a capital provider truly bears the risk of the principal, perhaps surplus may be licit. But the analogy had a slope, and the slope was real: once maritime risk justified a return, could ordinary business risk justify one? Could debtor unreliability? Could any default risk at all? The narrow lesson of the sea loan is defensible: return may be justified when principal is genuinely at risk in a defined venture. The broad lesson is dangerous: all risky lending deserves a premium. The history of periculum sortis is, in large part, the history of that tension, the tradition laboring to preserve the difference between a capital provider who truly shares the fate of a risky undertaking and a creditor who merely charges more because the borrower might fail.

The maritime setting makes the difference vivid. If the ship sinks, who loses? In a genuine sea loan, the lender loses the capital. In a disguised usurious loan, the borrower still owes. The storm exposes the contract: the same physical event, the shipwreck, reveals two different moral forms, one in which capital has joined the venture's danger, and one in which capital has priced the danger and left the merchant crushed by debt after the disaster.

Which is why a moral theology of finance must always ask what happens in failure. Success makes almost any structure appear harmless: the ship arrives, the lender is repaid, the merchant profits, and everyone may be satisfied. The moral architecture is tested when the ship does not arrive. Does the lender's claim disappear, diminish, or wait? Or does the borrower remain bound? Does the premium compensate a shared risk, or does it add to the burden of catastrophe? The answers determine whether the arrangement resembles partnership, insurance, or usury.

For modern non-extractive finance, the foenus nauticum is deeply instructive. Many contemporary instruments claim a return because the financed enterprise is risky. The sea-loan test asks: if the enterprise fails, does capital lose with it? If yes, the return may be tied to real risk-bearing. If no, or only superficially, then the language of risk may be misleading. A lender with collateral, guarantees, priority, and enforcement rights may face some probability of nonpayment, but that is not the same as accepting the shipwreck.

The test matters most for the instruments that live nearest the line: venture capital, revenue-based finance, recoverable grants, subordinated debt, mission-oriented investment. A venture investor may genuinely lose principal if the company fails, yet liquidation preferences and control rights still shape who loses first and who controls the response to distress. A revenue-based investor may share risk if payments fall with revenue and claims are capped, but minimum payments, personal guarantees, or default penalties slide the structure back toward creditor protection. A recoverable grant may resemble a sea loan when repayment occurs only upon success. In every case, the moral substance depends on the contingency.

The sea loan also warns against romanticizing risk. Maritime danger was real, but the existence of danger did not automatically make every premium just. The premium could be excessive. The borrower could be desperate. The lender could exploit what he knew and the merchant did not, and the contract could be drafted to preserve the lender's advantage in every weather. Risk-bearing is a necessary condition for a legitimate periculum sortis claim, but not always a sufficient one; proportionality, fairness, intention, and the borrower's vulnerability still matter. Even when capital truly bears risk, the reward may be disproportionate: a vulnerable entrepreneur may give away too much upside because no other capital is available; a fund may demand control rights that exceed its real exposure. Scholastic moral analysis would not stop at the fact of risk. It would also ask whether the reward is proportionate to the risk and whether the structure serves the common good of the enterprise.

The foenus nauticum therefore belongs in this book not as an odd maritime exception, but as a foundational case for thinking about capital, danger, and justice, and it foreshadows the central modern question: when is capital a creditor, and when is it a participant? The sea loan's answer is practical and dramatic. If the ship is lost and the capital is lost with it, the lender has participated. If the ship is lost and the borrower still owes, the lender has remained a creditor. The same advance of money can carry two different moral meanings, and the difference is decided by who bears the shipwreck. That makes the foenus nauticum a powerful image for non-extractive capital: capital that serves life must be willing, in some measure, to board the ship. It cannot stand safely on shore, charge for the danger of the voyage, and demand repayment after the wreck. Surplus may be just when return and vulnerability travel together; the language of risk becomes corrupt when capital demands the premium but refuses the storm.

sections.title: 25.4 Insurance-like Contracts and Exchange Contracts
paragraphs: Alongside societas, the commenda, and the foenus nauticum, medieval and early modern commerce developed a wider family of contracts that placed pressure on the strict usury prohibition: insurance-like contracts, exchange contracts, bills of exchange, currency dealings, and arrangements for moving value across distance and time. These forms matter here because they show that not every payment above principal can be understood through the simple categories of a loan: some payments compensate risk, some service, some exchange, transport, uncertainty, or the assumption of an obligation. And the very sophistication of these forms made them dangerous: each could be real, and each could be used as a disguise.

Insurance-like contracts are especially important for understanding periculum sortis. In a genuine insurance arrangement, one party receives a premium for assuming a specified risk. The premium is not paid because money has been lent but because the insurer has taken on the burden of a possible loss. If the insured event occurs, the insurer must pay; if it does not, he keeps the premium. The structure is not identical to a mutuum, because the payment is tied to risk transfer rather than to the use of consumable money.

This matters for moral theology because risk-bearing can be a real service. A merchant whose ship may be lost, whose goods may be stolen, whose cargo may be damaged, has something to gain from transferring part of that danger to another party. The party assuming the risk does something economically and morally distinct from lending: he bears exposure, he makes the venture possible or at least less ruinous, and he stands ready to absorb a specified loss. A payment for such a service is not automatically usury.

Yet insurance brought its own moral dangers, and the theologians catalogued them. The premium might be excessive. The insured might be desperate. The insurer might exploit superior knowledge, the contract might encourage recklessness, the risk might be poorly specified. Above all, a supposed insurance arrangement might conceal a loan. If a contract is called insurance but in substance guarantees a return to capital without real risk transfer, the moral form has changed, whatever the document says. The name of a contract cannot do the work that its structure refuses to do.

Here is the recurring scholastic insight in its most compact form: contracts must be judged according to their substance, not merely their legal vocabulary. If a payment is made for risk, the risk must be real. If a payment is made for exchange, the exchange must be real. If a payment is made for service, the service must be real. If a payment is made for loss, the loss must be real. Otherwise the contract becomes a verbal screen for usury.

Exchange contracts presented a parallel problem. In a world of multiple currencies, cities, fairs, and trade routes, exchange was a legitimate commercial activity. Money in Bruges was not simply money in Florence, Venice, Seville, or Antwerp: different currencies had different values, risks, availability, and uses, and a merchant might need funds in one place and payment in another. The service of transferring value across distance could be real. Currency conversion could be real. A bill of exchange could facilitate trade without a single coin physically traveling, and the man who provided such services was not merely lending money for consumption: he enabled commercial circulation, connecting places, currencies, and obligations. Properly structured, this belongs to exchange rather than usury.

But exchange contracts were notoriously susceptible to abuse. A bill of exchange could be genuine, or it could be fictive. The parties could dress a loan in exchange language, run a circular exchange in which no value needed to move at all, or manipulate place, currency, and timing until the exchange reliably produced what was, functionally, interest. The contract would appear to be an exchange, but morally it would be a loan with surplus. This is why later scholastic authors, and modern historians such as Raymond de Roover, paid such close attention to bills of exchange. The problem was never that exchange was illicit but that exchange could become a mask, and the moral theologian had to ask whether there was genuine diversity of place, currency, risk, and service, or whether these elements had been staged to justify gain. Once again, the issue was not the presence of surplus alone, but the title under which surplus was claimed.

Insurance-like contracts and exchange contracts therefore sharpen the central question of the extrinsic titles: what is the surplus for? Is it for the loan itself? Is it for risk assumed? Is it for a real service? Is it for exchange? Is it for loss? Is it for labor? Or is it simply payment for time and capital, routed through a more respectable form? The question grows more important as commerce grows more complex. In a simple village loan, the moral form is relatively visible: one person lends grain or money, another must repay, surplus is demanded. In a developed commercial economy, the same economic effect can be produced through several legal forms, and a usurious loan can be hidden inside a sale, a lease, an exchange, an insurance contract, a partnership, a penalty clause, a discount, a premium, or a fee. The theologian must therefore become a diagnostician of structure.

That diagnostic task is not anti-commercial. It takes commerce seriously enough to distinguish its legitimate forms from its corruptions. Real insurance should not be condemned as usury, nor real exchange, nor real risk-bearing investment. But neither should usury be blessed merely because it has picked up the vocabulary of insurance, exchange, or partnership.

The deeper moral issue is whether the contract distributes burdens truthfully. In genuine insurance, the insurer assumes risk. In genuine exchange, the exchanger provides conversion or transfer. In genuine partnership, the capital provider shares the outcome. In genuine lending, the lender transfers ownership and receives back the equivalent principal. Each form has its own justice, and injustice arises when one form is used to obtain the benefits of another while avoiding its burdens.

The relationship between insurance and periculum sortis makes this especially clear. A party who assumes risk may receive a premium, and a premium without exposure is extraction: if the same party shifts the risk back onto the borrower or the insured, through exclusions, guarantees, recourse rights, or hidden conditions, then the premium becomes suspect, because the title is valid only to the extent that the burden is actually borne.

Exchange contracts raise a similar issue with time. A bill of exchange may legitimately involve time, because settlement occurs later or elsewhere. But if the time element becomes the real source of gain, and the exchange element is only a fiction, then the transaction drifts toward usury. The tradition's concern was not that time is present; time is present in almost all human action. The concern was whether time itself was being sold, in a loan traveling under another name.

Modern finance is filled with analogous structures. Derivatives, swaps, factoring, invoice discounting, revenue contracts, convertibles, insurance products, guarantees, hedges, currency instruments, and structured finance can all serve real economic purposes. They can allocate risk, provide liquidity, stabilize cash flow, transfer exposure, and make enterprise possible. But they can also obscure who bears loss, who receives surplus, and whether capital is being compensated for real service or merely protected in its demand for growth.

A scholastic moral analysis would not begin by condemning these forms. It would begin by asking what they are really doing. Is a derivative hedging a real exposure, or creating speculative extraction? Is invoice discounting compensating early payment and collection risk, or imposing hidden interest on a vulnerable supplier? Is a guarantee protecting a lender from extraordinary loss, or shifting all downside away from capital while a risk premium is still charged? Is a convertible instrument sharing venture risk, or combining creditor protection with equity upside? Is a currency transaction serving trade, or disguising a loan?

The same questions apply to mission-driven finance. A non-extractive fund may need insurance, hedging, currency management, guarantees, or exchange services. None of these is morally unclean simply because it is financially complex. But complexity must not be allowed to hide extraction. The fund must be able to name each payment: What is this fee for? What burden does it compensate? Who bears the risk? Is the charge proportionate? Is the same risk being charged for elsewhere? Does the contract serve the enterprise, or does it protect capital at the expense of the enterprise?

Insurance-like contracts also invite a deeper reflection on solidarity. At their best, they distribute risk so that no one party is destroyed by misfortune, and that can be a form of mutual aid. A community can pool risk, a cooperative fund can absorb shocks, and a mission-aligned investor can protect founders from catastrophic downside. In such hands, risk-bearing finance serves life; it keeps vulnerability from becoming ruin. At their worst, however, the same mechanisms allow powerful parties to offload danger while keeping profit. A financial institution may insure itself, hedge itself, collateralize itself, and then charge others for the residual uncertainty. A capital provider may appear bold while being structurally protected. Risk does not disappear. It migrates downward, to whoever is least protected, and the poor, the worker, the founder, the supplier, the public, or the environment becomes the final bearer of loss.

This is precisely what periculum sortis should help us see. The title asks not only whether risk exists, but where it goes. Insurance-like contracts and exchange contracts make that question more complicated and more necessary at once, revealing that risk can be sliced, moved, priced, hidden, multiplied, and renamed. Moral theology must follow the risk through the structure.

The historical importance of these contracts is that they forced scholastic theologians to become more precise. It was no longer enough to say that surplus above principal was always usury, and it was not safe to say that any sophisticated contract justified surplus. The tradition had to develop a language of titles, causes, burdens, intention, proportionality, and fraud, and to ask, form by form, whether the surplus arose from something truly external to the loan, or whether the external title had become a pretext. This is why insurance and exchange belong in the prehistory of periculum sortis. They show that risk can be a legitimate object of contract. They also show that risk language can be manipulated, and that without the tradition's distinctions between real risk-bearing and risk-shifting, real exchange and fictive exchange, the usury prohibition could be evaded by technical skill alone.

For modern non-extractive finance, the lesson is direct. Complexity is not the enemy; hidden extraction is the enemy. A contract may be sophisticated and just, or simple and exploitative. What matters is whether the form truthfully reflects the moral reality: capital that takes surplus for bearing risk must bear the risk, payment for exchange requires an exchange, a fee for service requires a service, and compensation for loss requires a loss that can be shown.

The old theologians understood that finance can become a theater of names, in which a loan is called exchange, interest is called penalty, yield is called compensation, and extraction is called risk. The task of moral theology is to look through the names and ask what is actually happening. Insurance-like contracts and exchange contracts make that task harder. They also make it unavoidable. At the threshold of the later doctrine of periculum sortis, they leave us with a disciplined question: has the party receiving surplus truly assumed a burden that justifies it? If yes, the payment may belong to justice. If no, the payment may be usury in another form, and the complexity of the contract changes nothing about the moral test except the care needed in applying it.

chapters.part: Part VIII — Periculum Sortis: Risk of Capital and the Moral Structure of Downside

chapters.title: Aquinas: The Architecture Without the Title
chapters.label: Chapter 26

sections.title: 26.1 ST II-II, q.78, a.2, ad 5
paragraphs: Aquinas does not give us the later, fully developed doctrine of periculum sortis as a named extrinsic title. He does, however, give us the architecture without which the title cannot be understood. The key text is Summa Theologiae II-II, q.78, a.2, ad 5, where Aquinas distinguishes sharply between a loan and a partnership. The distinction became one of the foundations of the whole later tradition, because it explains why gain from a mutuum is usurious while profit from risk-bearing participation can be legitimate.

The objection Aquinas is answering concerns whether one may receive something beyond the principal when money has been given to another. His response is precise. If a person entrusts money to a merchant by way of partnership, he may licitly share in the profit, because the money remains in some sense at his risk. But if the money is given as a loan, the borrower becomes owner of the money and must return the equivalent. In that case, to demand more than the principal is to demand usury.

Everything turns on the phrase "by way of partnership." Aquinas is not saying that money can never be involved in profitable enterprise, that merchants may never receive capital, or that the owner of capital may never receive profit. He is saying that the moral form of the transaction matters. If the capital provider remains exposed to the venture, the surplus has a different character than if he transfers ownership through a loan and retains a fixed claim to repayment.

Here is the decisive difference between creditor and partner. The creditor says: "You owe me the equivalent principal, regardless of what happens to your use of the money." The partner says: "My capital is joined to the undertaking, and my gain or loss depends upon its outcome." The creditor's claim is fixed; the partner's claim is contingent. The creditor stands outside the borrower's enterprise with a right to repayment. The partner participates in the enterprise's uncertainty.

Behind this stands the nature of the mutuum itself. In a loan for consumption, the borrower receives ownership of the thing lent. Money, like wine or grain, is consumed in its use; the borrower does not return the identical thing but an equivalent. Because ownership has passed, the lender cannot separately sell the use of the money. To charge for the use of what has already become another's property is to sell the same thing twice. The just claim of the lender is therefore the return of the tantundem, not a surplus for use.

Partnership changes the analysis because the money is not simply alienated in the same way. The capital is committed to a joint undertaking. The owner of capital does not merely hand over money and receive a fixed debtor's obligation; he remains connected to the outcome. If the merchant succeeds, the capital owner may share in the gain. If the merchant fails without fault, the capital owner may suffer loss. The capital is productive only by being exposed.

This is why the text matters so much for periculum sortis. Aquinas does not need the later term to state the principle: profit-sharing is licit where capital remains at risk. The legitimacy of gain is tied to vulnerability. The capital provider may receive more because he may also receive less. This is not usury, because the surplus is not demanded from the loan as such; it arises from participation in a risky common enterprise.

The moral contrast is stark. In the usurious loan, the lender seeks guaranteed gain while shifting the uncertainty of use to the borrower. In partnership, the capital provider accepts that the outcome of the undertaking affects him too. That does not make every partnership just, nor every profit virtuous, but it changes the category: the payment is not rent charged on a consumable loan but a share of the fruit of a venture in which the capital provider has remained exposed.

Aquinas thereby offers a structural criterion rather than a merely verbal one. The question is not whether the contract mentions "profit," "loan," "investment," or "partnership." The question is where ownership, claim, and risk lie. If the borrower owns the money and owes the equivalent principal unconditionally, the structure is a mutuum. If the capital provider remains at risk in the undertaking, the structure begins to resemble societas. Moral theology must look to the substance.

This is why the text remains so relevant for modern finance. Many contemporary instruments blur the line between creditor and partner. Investors may want equity upside with debt-like protection. Lenders may demand warrants, convertibility, default interest, collateral, personal guarantees, and covenants. Venture investors may claim to be risk capital while using liquidation preferences and control rights to improve their position in downside scenarios. The Thomistic question is not what the instrument is called. The question is whether capital has truly accepted the venture's risk.

Aquinas would also force us to distinguish practical uncertainty from structural risk. Even a creditor can fail to be repaid. That does not necessarily make him a partner. If the borrower remains obligated, if the lender can sue, seize collateral, accelerate repayment, or enforce guarantees, the lender still holds a fixed claim. He may face collection risk, but he has not necessarily joined the enterprise's loss. Partnership requires more than the possibility that enforcement may fail. It requires that the capital provider's claim itself be contingent upon the outcome.

This distinction guards against a broad and dangerous reading of periculum sortis. If ordinary risk of nonpayment were enough, every loan could bear a risk premium. Aquinas's architecture points in a stricter direction. The morally relevant risk is not merely that the borrower may default. It is that the capital provider's principal is actually committed to the venture and can be lost within its ordinary operation. He is not merely exposed to the borrower's failure to pay. He is exposed to the venture's failure to succeed.

The same architecture underwrites a critique of modern "risk-adjusted return." Modern finance often says that riskier borrowers must pay more. Aquinas's distinction asks whether the lender is truly bearing the risk or simply charging the borrower for being risky. If the borrower still owes full repayment and the lender remains senior, collateralized, and protected, then the higher return may not be payment for shared risk at all. It may be extraction from vulnerability. The borrower's fragility becomes the lender's yield.

In the partnership model, by contrast, the capital provider's gain is tied to the venture's flourishing, and he cannot simply demand payment when the undertaking fails. That creates a different moral relationship. The capital provider has reason to care about the enterprise as a whole, not only about repayment. He may still be self-interested; he may still be unjust in other ways. But the structure itself binds his outcome to the outcome of the venture.

This is the beginning of a theology of shared fate. Aquinas does not use the phrase, but his distinction points toward it. Usury is suspect because it separates gain from shared vulnerability. Partnership is more morally intelligible because it reunites them. The capital provider may receive surplus because he has not insulated himself entirely from loss. Return becomes connected to participation.

The implications for non-extractive finance are direct. A financing structure that wishes to claim the moral dignity of investment must not merely provide capital; it must ask how the capital is exposed. If repayment is fixed, guaranteed, senior, collateralized, and enforceable regardless of outcome, then surplus is morally difficult to justify by appeal to risk. If repayment is contingent, subordinated, capped, tied to available surplus, and genuinely loss-absorbing, then the structure moves closer to Aquinas's partnership logic.

This does not mean every non-extractive instrument must be legal equity, nor that every debt instrument is automatically immoral. It means the moral justification for surplus must match the structure. If a lender claims only reimbursement of actual costs, that is one analysis. If a lender claims compensation for delay or actual loss, that is another. If a capital provider claims a share of upside because of risk, then Aquinas asks whether capital truly shares downside. The title must fit the burden.

The text also disciplines the language of stewardship. A steward does not use another's need merely as an occasion for gain; he entrusts resources to a purpose and accepts responsibility for how they serve it. Partnership is not automatically stewardship, but it is structurally closer to stewardship than a protected claim that extracts surplus regardless of outcome. The partner's capital is at stake with the enterprise. The creditor's capital stands over against the borrower as a claim.

The later tradition will debate how far the principle can be extended. Can special risk in a loan justify surplus? Can risk of nonpayment be priced? Can the value of a risky claim be discounted? Can insurance of capital be charged? Molina, Lessius, Lugo, and their critics will develop these questions in more technical ways. But Aquinas gives the moral baseline: profit is licit when capital is truly placed at risk in a common undertaking; surplus from a guaranteed loan remains usury.

The power of II-II, q.78, a.2, ad 5 is that it prevents two opposite errors. It prevents the rigorist error of treating all profit from capital as usury: Aquinas clearly allows profit-sharing where capital is entrusted by way of partnership. And it prevents the permissive error of treating all capital provision as investment: a loan remains a loan, and if the borrower owns the money and owes the principal unconditionally, the lender may not simply rebrand surplus as profit.

For the purposes of this book, the distinction is foundational. It teaches that the morality of finance depends on contract anatomy. Who owns the capital? Who bears risk? Who has the claim? What happens in failure? Is gain tied to participation, or to the borrower's obligation? These questions are not modern additions to the tradition. They are already present in Aquinas's careful distinction between mutuum and societas.

In the end, Aquinas leaves us the simplest and most enduring test. If capital wants the fruit of partnership, it must accept the risk of partnership; if it insists on the security of a loan, it must not demand the gain of partnership. The later title of periculum sortis will grow from this root, but the root itself is already clear. Surplus becomes morally intelligible when capital is truly vulnerable to loss. Without that vulnerability, the language of risk ends up doing usury's work.

sections.title: 26.2 The Thomistic Rule
paragraphs: The Thomistic rule can be stated simply: in a mutuum, the borrower owns the money and owes the equivalent principal; in partnership, the capital remains exposed to the undertaking. The first structure permits restitution of the tantundem. The second may permit profit-sharing. The moral difference lies not in the material object, money, but in the form of the relationship the contract creates.

The rule is more powerful than it looks, because Aquinas is not drawing a legal distinction between two technical categories. He is identifying two different moral worlds. In the mutuum, the lender transfers ownership; the borrower receives the money as his own and owes an equivalent; and because the thing lent is consumed in its use, charging separately for that use is charging for what now belongs to the borrower.

Partnership has a different moral anatomy. The capital provider does not hand over money in exchange for a fixed claim; he places capital into a common undertaking, where it is not protected as loan principal is. It may succeed, fail, increase, diminish, or disappear according to the fortune of the enterprise. Profit-sharing is therefore not a payment for the use of money in a consumptive loan. It is a share in the outcome of a venture to which the capital provider remains joined.

That establishes the moral difference between guaranteed debt and risk-bearing capital. Guaranteed debt preserves the lender's claim regardless of outcome; risk-bearing capital makes the claim depend on outcome. The creditor asks to be repaid. The partner waits to see whether the enterprise produces anything to distribute. The creditor's relationship to the borrower is one of obligation. The partner's relationship to the venture is one of participation.

The rule thereby prevents a confusion running in both directions: it is not wrong for capital to receive profit simply because it is capital, and it is not right for capital to receive profit simply because it has been provided. The decisive question is whether capital has accepted the form of vulnerability that corresponds to profit.

Here is the root of the later doctrine of periculum sortis. The risk of the principal is morally relevant because it changes the nature of the surplus. When principal is guaranteed, surplus looks like payment for the loan. When principal is genuinely at risk, surplus may look like compensation for exposure or participation in gain. The later theologians will argue about how broad the principle should become. But the Thomistic baseline is clear: the right to profit follows participation in risk, not mere provision of money.

The rule also clarifies why ordinary default risk is insufficient. A lender may always fail to be repaid; human affairs are uncertain. That does not automatically make the lender a partner. If the borrower remains obligated, if the lender retains a fixed claim, and if legal recourse remains available, the lender's structure is still creditor-like. He faces the risk that the borrower will not fulfill the obligation, but the obligation itself remains. He has not necessarily accepted the venture's outcome as his own.

The difference between enforcement risk and venture risk is essential. Enforcement risk means the creditor might not successfully collect what is owed. Venture risk means the capital provider's claim is itself dependent on the success or failure of the undertaking. In enforcement risk, failure is a problem of collection; in venture risk, failure changes what is owed. The former is present in almost every loan. The latter belongs to partnership, investment, sea loans, commenda-like arrangements, and other genuinely contingent forms.

Without this distinction, periculum sortis becomes too broad. Every lender can say that repayment is uncertain; every creditor can demand a premium for possible default; the usury prohibition would be emptied of content. Aquinas's rule resists that collapse, and the resistance can be put in one line: risk matters when it is structurally borne, not merely statistically observed. The creditor who charges more because the borrower might fail is not necessarily bearing the borrower's risk. He may be pricing the borrower's weakness while preserving the borrower's obligation.

This is one of the most important points for modern finance. A loan to a fragile enterprise may be "risky" in financial terms. But if the lender demands collateral, personal guarantees, default interest, covenants, acceleration rights, seniority, and legal recourse, then the lender has built a structure designed to reduce or shift that risk. The borrower may still carry the existential burden of failure: workers may lose jobs, suppliers may go unpaid, the mission may collapse. The lender may suffer a loss, but only after using contractual tools to protect the claim. That is not partnership.

Aquinas's rule asks us to look at what happens when things go wrong. If the enterprise fails, does the capital provider simply lose the capital, or does the borrower remain bound? Does the investor share the downside, or does capital stand first in line? Does the provider of funds have a claim against the whole person and property of the borrower, or only a claim to a share of surplus if surplus exists? The answers reveal whether the transaction is closer to mutuum or societas.

Priority deserves the same examination. Even where capital is formally at risk, it may be protected by senior claims. Priority does not eliminate all risk, but it changes its distribution. If capital is repaid before labor, community, mission, and the long-term health of the enterprise, then capital may be less vulnerable than others. The stricter reading of periculum sortis would ask whether capital's claimed return is proportionate to the risk it actually bears after all protections, preferences, and priorities are taken into account.

This is why modern equity does not automatically satisfy the Thomistic rule. Legal equity may still contain creditor-like features. Preferred equity may receive liquidation preference. Investors may have redemption rights. Control rights may allow capital to force a sale. Anti-dilution clauses may protect investors from downside while exposing founders. Participating preferred structures may let capital receive both priority repayment and upside participation. In such cases the investor claims the language of partnership while retaining significant protection from loss. The moral question is not the legal label but the real exposure.

Conversely, some debt-like forms may approximate partnership more closely than their names suggest. A revenue-based agreement that requires payment only from actual revenue, caps total return, pauses automatically in hardship, avoids personal guarantees, and subordinates itself to wages and essential obligations may share risk more meaningfully than a preferred equity instrument with aggressive control rights. A recoverable grant repaid only upon success may be closer in spirit to the sea loan or commenda than to ordinary debt. The Thomistic rule cuts beneath categories.

The consequences for non-extractive finance are demanding. A fund cannot justify surplus merely by saying it is taking risk. It must show where the risk sits. Is the principal genuinely vulnerable? Is repayment contingent? Are returns tied to real surplus? Are losses absorbed by capital, or pushed onto the enterprise? Does the contract allow capital to recover first while others bear deeper harm? Does the structure place capital in relationship with the mission, or over against it as a protected claim?

The rule also disciplines opportunity-cost arguments. A lender may say that money could have been used elsewhere. But in a mutuum, the lender has chosen the security of principal. To claim both security and foregone-profit compensation risks combining the benefits of debt and investment. If the lender wants the return associated with productive use, Aquinas points toward partnership: put the money at risk in the venture, share in gain because you share in loss. Do not preserve the creditor's claim while demanding the investor's reward.

This is especially relevant to capital structures that seek downside protection and upside participation at the same time. Warrants attached to loans, convertible debt, preferred returns, redemption rights, and liquidation preferences can all create hybrid claims. Hybrids are not automatically immoral, but they require careful analysis. Does the hybrid allocate risk and reward fairly, or does it allow capital to remain protected while capturing additional upside? Does it serve the enterprise, or does it create a one-way option for the funder?

The rule would also challenge the assumption that legal enforceability settles moral legitimacy. A contract may be enforceable and still unjust. A borrower may consent under pressure. A founder may accept terms because no other capital is available. A poor household may pledge collateral because survival requires it. The Thomistic distinction between loan and partnership is therefore not merely about formal consent. It is about the objective justice of the exchange: what is being claimed, and on what basis?

At the same time, the rule avoids romanticism. It does not require all capital to be donated, deny legitimate profit, condemn commercial partnership, or pretend that risk-bearing is costless. It gives a morally intelligible place to investment: capital may receive profit when it participates in a venture and remains exposed to loss. None of this is hostility to capital. What it refuses is guaranteed gain without vulnerability.

The rule can therefore be summarized as a moral proportionality between claim and exposure. A fixed claim belongs with principal repayment. A variable claim belongs with shared outcome. A higher claim requires a stronger title. A claim to upside requires acceptance of downside. The more capital is protected, the weaker its appeal to risk; the more capital is genuinely vulnerable, the stronger its claim to participate in surplus.

For modern contract review, this yields a simple but demanding sequence of questions. Is the instrument structured as a loan, partnership, investment, insurance, or hybrid? Who owns the capital after transfer? Does the recipient owe the principal regardless of outcome? What happens if the project fails? Is there collateral or recourse? Are there personal guarantees? Is capital senior or subordinated? Is return fixed or contingent? Does capital share losses, or merely charge for the possibility that the borrower may fail?

These questions expose the moral form of the transaction. A contract that calls itself partnership but guarantees capital's return may fail the partnership test. A contract that calls itself debt but limits repayment to actual surplus may be less creditor-like than its label suggests. A contract that claims a risk premium while shifting risk downward may be morally incoherent. Aquinas gives us the grammar to see this.

The Thomistic rule is ultimately a rule against asymmetry. It refuses to let one party take the upside of ownership, the security of creditorship, and the moral language of stewardship all at once. If capital is a creditor, let it be a creditor and receive what is due. If capital is a partner, let it be a partner and share fate. If capital claims risk, let it bear risk. If capital claims service, let it serve. What it may not do is claim every title while accepting only the burdens that suit it.

This is why the rule remains central to a renewed moral economy. The question is not simply whether finance is legal, efficient, or innovative, but whether its claims correspond to its vulnerabilities. Does capital receive because it has restored loss, borne risk, performed labor, or joined enterprise? Or does it receive because it has power to demand more? Aquinas's distinction between mutuum and partnership gives us a way to answer. The rule is both ancient and contemporary: guaranteed principal belongs to the logic of the loan; shared profit belongs to the logic of shared risk. When finance respects this distinction, surplus can become morally intelligible. When finance confuses the two, extraction begins.

sections.title: 26.3 Why Aquinas Matters for Modern Finance
paragraphs: Aquinas matters for modern finance because he refuses to let legal form do the work of moral truth. His distinction between mutuum and partnership is not an antiquarian curiosity. It is a diagnostic tool for examining the structure of capital, and it asks the questions beneath every modern financing contract: who owns the money, who bears the risk, who has the claim, who receives the surplus, and what happens when the undertaking fails.

Modern finance often treats capital as a neutral input. Money is supplied, time passes, risk is priced, and return is expected; the language is technical, mathematical, and apparently impersonal. Aquinas forces a more basic moral inquiry. Is the capital provider a lender or a partner? Is the return fixed or contingent? Does the borrower owe the principal regardless of outcome? Does the investor share downside, or only upside? Is surplus being claimed because capital has truly participated in a risky undertaking, or because capital has the power to demand growth?

The questions bite because modern finance frequently blends categories that Aquinas held apart. A lender may want a creditor's right to repayment, but also a warrant, conversion right, or success fee. A venture investor may claim to be risk capital, but also demand liquidation preferences, veto rights, anti-dilution protection, redemption rights, and control over exit. A private equity structure may call itself ownership while loading the company with debt, extracting fees, and placing repayment pressure on the operating business. These structures may be legal, ordinary, even celebrated as sophisticated. Aquinas asks whether their claims correspond to their burdens.

The Thomistic distinction is especially helpful because modern labels can mislead. Debt is not always morally identical to the medieval mutuum, and equity is not always morally identical to societas. Some debt-like instruments share risk in meaningful ways; some equity-like instruments protect capital with creditor-like priority. The moral question is not what the contract is called but how it distributes ownership, control, downside, and surplus.

Consider guaranteed debt. If the borrower must repay principal regardless of the outcome of the financed activity, the structure resembles the mutuum. If surplus is added simply because time has passed, the old usury problem appears. If surplus is justified by actual administrative cost, documented loss, culpable delay, or another genuine extrinsic title, the analysis changes. But the basic structure remains important: the lender has a fixed claim, the borrower bears the obligation, and the lender may face some risk of nonpayment without the contract ever making him a partner in the venture's fate.

This is why seniority, collateral, and guarantees matter. A loan with collateral does not merely provide capital; it gives the lender a claim against specific assets. A personal guarantee extends the claim beyond the business into the life of the borrower. Seniority places the lender ahead of others when things go wrong. Covenants restrict the borrower's freedom, and default clauses accelerate pressure at precisely the moment of weakness. Each provision may be defensible in some contexts, but together they reveal how risk is allocated. Aquinas would ask whether capital is actually sharing risk, or merely protecting itself against the borrower's risk.

The same applies to modern equity. Ordinary shares may share upside and downside, but preferred equity often changes the structure. A liquidation preference can allow investors to be repaid before founders or common shareholders receive anything. Participating preferred shares can allow investors to take both priority repayment and a share of remaining upside. Redemption rights can pressure the company to repurchase shares. Anti-dilution protections can shift the cost of later difficulties onto founders and employees. Board control can allow capital to determine the company's direction. These rights may make investment more attractive. They also complicate the claim that capital is simply sharing risk.

From a Thomistic perspective, the moral danger is the combination of protected downside and captured upside. If capital receives priority in failure and disproportionate benefit in success, it begins to resemble a hybrid of creditor and owner that takes the advantages of both forms: the dignity of partnership with the protection of debt. Aquinas's rule warns against exactly this asymmetry. If capital wants partnership profit, it should accept partnership vulnerability. If capital wants creditor security, it should not demand the full fruits of partnership.

This is not a call to abolish financial complexity. Modern enterprises need many kinds of capital: some projects require loans, some equity, some grants, guarantees, revenue shares, recoverable grants, cooperative financing, or subordinated instruments. Aquinas does not give us a ready-made term sheet. He gives us a moral anatomy. He teaches us to ask whether the form of the claim matches the form of the risk.

That teaching is crucial for steward ownership and mission-locked enterprise. A company may be legally steward-owned, asset-locked, or mission-driven, and its financing can still create extraction. If outside capital has priority claims, excessive control rights, guaranteed returns, or exit pressure, the mission may be subordinated in practice even if protected in legal language. The enterprise may belong formally to stewards, but economically to capital. Aquinas helps reveal the contradiction by asking what happens when mission and repayment conflict.

Digital sovereignty makes the question sharper still. Infrastructure companies, open-source enterprises, cybersecurity firms, AI companies, and platform cooperatives all need capital. But if that capital demands exit, control, or guaranteed growth, the governance of essential infrastructure can be pulled away from the people and communities it serves. A structure may begin with public-interest language and end with capital-driven control. The old question, who owns, who governs, who bears risk, who receives surplus, becomes a question of technological sovereignty.

Aquinas also exposes the moral insufficiency of "market rate." Modern finance often assumes a return is justified because similar capital receives similar return elsewhere. Aquinas does not begin with market comparison. He begins with justice in the transaction: what is being sold, what is being transferred, what is being risked, what is owed. A market rate tells us what is customary. It does not tell us whether the custom is just. A whole market can normalize extraction.

The point extends to opportunity cost, which contemporary finance treats as almost self-evident: capital must be compensated because it could have been elsewhere. Aquinas's distinction asks whether the lender has chosen the form of a loan or the form of participation. If the lender wants the safety of principal, the appeal to missed speculative gain is morally limited, and the claim that capital "could have earned elsewhere" cannot automatically be imposed on the borrower.

Aquinas likewise helps distinguish risk-bearing from risk-pricing.
