Modern finance often prices risk without sharing it: a riskier borrower pays more even when the lender uses collateral, seniority, covenants, and guarantees to reduce actual exposure, and the borrower's fragility becomes, once again, the lender's yield. Aquinas's partnership logic asks whether capital has truly become vulnerable with the borrower, or whether it is charging for vulnerability while remaining structurally protected. The question goes to the heart of non-extractive finance.

The difference can be stated in contemporary terms. A non-extractive instrument should not merely ask, "What return compensates the investor for risk?" It should ask, "What risk is the investor actually willing to bear, and how should return be shaped in light of that shared vulnerability?" This reverses the ordinary order. Instead of using return expectations to build protections for capital, the structure begins with the needs of the enterprise and the just distribution of risk. Return follows service, participation, and loss-bearing.

Aquinas also matters for accounting. If surplus is justified by actual loss, labor, expense, risk, or foregone profit, then each title should be named and documented, not hidden inside a single blended return. A management fee should correspond to management labor. A late fee should correspond to culpable delay and actual inconvenience. A risk return should correspond to real loss exposure. A cost reimbursement should correspond to cost. This kind of moral accounting prevents the same burden from being charged several times under different names.

In modern funds this matters enormously. Management fees, carried interest, preferred returns, monitoring fees, transaction fees, default penalties, liquidation preferences, warrants, and redemption rights can interact in ways that make the total claim of capital opaque. Each element may be defended separately; together, they may produce extraction. Aquinas's framework invites a contract audit: what does each payment compensate, and is the underlying burden real?

This is also where Aquinas corrects a narrow legalism. A contract may be valid under civil law and still morally questionable. Consent may be formally present but economically constrained. A founder may accept terms because there is no alternative. A small supplier may accept invoice discounting because survival requires liquidity. A worker-owned or steward-owned company may accept capital terms that later undermine its mission. Aquinas's concern for commutative justice asks whether the exchange itself preserves equality, not merely whether signatures were obtained.

At the same time, Aquinas protects us from a simplistic anti-capital stance. He does not say that capital can never receive profit; he allows profit where capital is truly placed into partnership. This matters for building realistic alternatives. Non-extractive finance cannot depend only on gifts. It must find legitimate ways for capital to support enterprise without dominating it, and Aquinas gives one such path: capital may share in surplus when it shares in risk and serves a common undertaking.

The design implications are practical. Returns can be capped rather than unlimited. Repayment can be tied to actual surplus rather than fixed schedules. Capital can be subordinated to wages, taxes, suppliers, and mission-critical reinvestment. Personal guarantees can be avoided. Default penalties can be limited by grace periods and hardship provisions. Investor control can be restrained by steward ownership. Losses can be shared explicitly. These are not ethical add-ons. They change the moral species of the transaction.

Beneath all of this, Aquinas helps us see why capital structure is spiritual, not merely technical. Finance forms relationships. A contract is a moral pedagogy: it trains the enterprise in a certain order of love, teaching a company whom it must serve first, shaping whether founders act from mission or fear, determining whether workers and communities are protected or sacrificed, influencing whether technology serves the common good or the exit preferences of investors.

This is why the distinction between mutuum and societas belongs in a book about capital that serves life. The issue is not whether ancient categories can be mechanically applied to modern instruments. The issue is whether the moral logic remains true. Guaranteed gain without shared vulnerability is dangerous. Profit joined to risk-bearing participation is more defensible. Legal form must be tested against economic substance. Surplus must justify itself.

Aquinas therefore gives us the grammar for resisting financial fatalism. Modern finance often presents its terms as inevitable: risk requires return, capital requires growth, markets set price, investors need protection, exit is normal, seniority is prudent. Aquinas slows the conversation. He asks what is owed in justice. He asks whether money has become a claim over life. He asks whether capital is serving the enterprise or arranging the enterprise to serve capital.
For non-extractive finance, the Thomistic test can be put plainly:
If capital receives more, what burden has capital actually borne?

If the answer is labor, cost, documented loss, real foregone gain, or genuine risk-sharing, then surplus may have a moral title. If the answer is simply time, power, custom, market expectation, or the borrower's need, then the claim is suspect. Aquinas gives us the courage to make that distinction.

In the end, Aquinas matters for modern finance because he reveals that the structure of a contract is a moral statement. A loan says one thing. A partnership says another. A hybrid may say something confused. A non-extractive structure must say something truthful: capital is here to serve, to share risk, to support mission, and to receive only what corresponds to its real contribution and vulnerability. Anything else may be legal finance, but it is not yet moral economy.

chapters.part: Part VIII — Periculum Sortis: Risk of Capital and the Moral Structure of Downside

chapters.title: Alexander of Alexandria and the First Named Formulation
chapters.label: Chapter 27

sections.title: 27.1 Alexander of Alexandria / Alexander Lombard
paragraphs: The title periculum sortis enters the story cautiously, almost timidly. It first appears not as a mature doctrine, ready to bear the weight of modern risk pricing, but as a fragile recognition that there may be cases in which a lender, or more precisely a provider of capital, is exposed to something morally different from the ordinary expectation of repayment. The question is no longer simply whether the lender has suffered delay, incurred an actual loss, or given up a concrete commercial gain. The question is whether the capital itself has been placed in danger.

This is why Alexander of Alexandria, also known in some sources as Alexander Lombard, matters. He was born at Alessandria in Piedmont around 1270 (the town supplied the name), joined the Franciscans, and taught theology at Paris; in 1313 the order elected him minister general, and he died in office at Rome the next year. His Tractatus de Usuris, composed around 1307 (it then sat in manuscript for six centuries, until a modern edition in 1962), is often associated with one of the earliest explicit formulations of periculum sortis as a possible title. He does not yet give us the full late-scholastic doctrine; that will come later, especially through the Spanish and Jesuit moral theologians. But Alexander marks a conceptual threshold. He helps make it thinkable that risk to the principal (the sors) might be morally relevant when judging whether a surplus payment is unjust usury or legitimate compensation.

The term itself is revealing. Periculum means danger, hazard, or risk. Sors means the principal, the capital sum, the amount placed into the transaction. Periculum sortis, then, is not merely the inconvenience of being without money, nor the opportunity cost of a better investment forgone. It is the danger that the principal itself may not return, and in this the title differs sharply from lucrum cessans: lost profit asks whether the lender sacrificed a gain that could otherwise have been made; risk of principal asks whether he may lose the capital itself.

This distinction is morally decisive. In a strict mutuum the borrower becomes owner of the money and owes back an equivalent amount; the lender is not a co-owner of the venture, does not share directly in its success or failure, and holds instead a claim to repayment. The classical usury problem arises precisely because the lender demands not only the equivalent principal but also something more by reason of the loan itself. If repayment is guaranteed, if the borrower bears the whole burden of failure, and if the lender receives additional surplus simply because time has passed, the surplus looks like usury in its classic form.

But if the lender's capital is genuinely at risk, the moral structure begins to change. In that case the surplus may not be a charge for the bare use of money but compensation for exposure to loss. This is why periculum sortis stands so close to partnership, sea loans, commenda arrangements, and the other risk-bearing forms of finance. It asks whether the person supplying capital has remained exposed to the downside. If so, the relationship may no longer be adequately described as simple credit; it may contain something closer to participation.

Alexander's importance lies in naming this possibility before the tradition had fully stabilized it. Earlier Christian moral theology certainly recognized the difference between a loan and a partnership. Aquinas had already drawn the crucial distinction: one may not charge for the use of money in a mutuum, but one may entrust money to a merchant by way of partnership and receive a share of the profit, because in that case the capital remains at the owner's risk. What Alexander helps introduce is the idea that risk to principal might itself function as an extrinsic title, a reason external to the mere loan that could justify receiving something beyond the principal.

Yet this early formulation must be handled with care. It would be anachronistic to treat Alexander as though he had already invented modern risk-adjusted return. He did not. The medieval concern was not to produce a generalized theory of capital markets, but to distinguish morally different contracts from one another. The question was not: "What return does this risk deserve in the market?" The question was: "Is this surplus truly attached to an external burden, or is it merely a disguised price for lending money?"

That is why periculum sortis was dangerous from the beginning. It opened a real and necessary door, and one that could easily be abused. Where the risk is genuine, the title has moral force: a lender who can truly lose the principal may be compensated for a real exposure. But if every lender can say, "There is always some risk that I will not be repaid," then the whole usury prohibition begins to unravel. Ordinary default risk would justify interest everywhere, for every loan carries some uncertainty, every creditor could claim danger, and every surplus could be renamed compensation.

This is the unresolved tension that Alexander's early formulation leaves to later theologians. How much risk is enough? Must the risk be extraordinary, or is ordinary nonpayment sufficient? Must the lender's claim be legally subordinated or contingent? Does collateral eliminate the title? Does a personal guarantee shift the risk back to the borrower? If the lender can enforce repayment against unrelated assets, is the capital truly exposed to the fortunes of the venture? These are not merely technical questions. They are moral questions about who bears downside, who receives upside, and whether the language of risk is being used to describe reality or conceal domination.

Alexander of Alexandria therefore stands at the beginning of a new stage in the doctrine. With poena conventionalis, the tradition asked whether a penalty was truly a penalty or merely planned revenue. With damnum emergens, it asked whether compensation restored an actual loss. With lucrum cessans, it asked whether foregone gain was concrete enough to be compensable. With periculum sortis, it now had to ask whether capital was genuinely exposed to loss.

This final title presses most directly into the architecture of finance itself. It asks not only what happened after the loan, nor what the lender might otherwise have done, but what the transaction is. Is it a guaranteed claim? Is it a shared venture? Is it insurance-like risk bearing? Is it partnership in substance, even if not in name? Or is it a loan dressed in the language of risk?

That is why Alexander matters even though the title consolidates later. He belongs to the moment when scholastic moral theology begins to notice that the morality of surplus cannot be judged by vocabulary alone. A contract may call itself a loan, a partnership, an investment, an exchange, or an insurance arrangement. But the deeper question is always the same: who can lose, who must pay, and who is protected from the consequences of failure?

In this sense the first named appearance of periculum sortis is not a minor technical footnote but the beginning of a moral inquiry that leads directly into modern finance. For once risk enters the analysis, the old doctrine of usury can no longer be reduced to a simple condemnation of "interest." It becomes an inquiry into the structure of downside: whether capital is actually sharing danger, or merely demanding guaranteed growth while others bear the cost of loss. Alexander did not complete that inquiry. But he helped open it, and in doing so he gave the tradition one of its most important and most perilous tools.

sections.title: 27.2 Early Fourteenth-Century Significance
paragraphs: The early fourteenth century is a decisive moment in the history of usury doctrine because the inherited categories were beginning to strain under the pressure of commercial life. The old distinction between a gratuitous loan and a profit-sharing partnership remained foundational. The mutuum still named the contract in which ownership of the money passed to the borrower, who owed back an equivalent amount. Societas still named the partnership in which capital remained exposed to the fortunes of the venture. Yet actual economic practice often moved along the edges of these categories. Merchants advanced funds, investors assumed hazards, debtors promised repayment under uncertain conditions, and commercial actors sought ways to finance trade without falling into the sin of usury. Theologians and canonists therefore had to ask whether the older legal categories were sufficient to describe the moral substance of increasingly complex transactions.

This is the setting in which the early appearance of periculum sortis becomes significant. The title names something that had long been present in practice but had not yet been fully stabilized in doctrine: the danger that the principal itself might be lost. Earlier discussions had already made room for compensation when a creditor suffered a real loss, when a debtor culpably delayed repayment, or when a lender gave up a determinate gain. But periculum sortis presses the analysis deeper, asking whether the risk built into the transaction itself may justify surplus. It shifts attention from the events surrounding the loan to the internal allocation of downside.

This shift changes the moral question the tradition had learned to ask of the other titles: not whether the lender suffered an actual loss (damnum emergens), truly forwent a concrete gain (lucrum cessans), or met culpable delay with a penalty that is genuinely penal rather than revenue-seeking (poena conventionalis), but whether, with periculum sortis, the capital itself can be lost, and who bears that loss if the venture fails.

That question is structurally close to Aquinas's distinction between lending and partnership: profit-sharing is allowed where money is entrusted to a merchant by way of partnership, because the owner of the capital bears the risk of loss. But Aquinas did not develop a named title of periculum sortis as later authors would. The early fourteenth-century significance of Alexander's formulation is that it begins to turn this structural distinction into an explicit analytical title: not only is partnership profit licit when capital is at risk, but risk to principal may itself be a morally relevant ground for compensation.

This does not mean the doctrine was settled. Quite the opposite. The title appears before it is widely solid, a possibility rather than a mature common teaching. That distinction between early appearance and late consolidation is crucial. Many doctrines in moral theology do not arrive fully formed. They appear first as intuitions, then as case distinctions, then as cautiously admitted exceptions, then as more systematic teachings, and finally as either settled principles or contested compromises. Periculum sortis follows this pattern. Its early appearance signals that theologians had begun to recognize the moral relevance of capital risk; its later consolidation shows how difficult that recognition was to discipline.

The difficulty lies in the ambiguity of risk itself. Risk can name genuine exposure to loss, but it can also become a rhetorical device. A lender may say, truthfully, that every borrower might fail to repay; every loan contains some possibility of default. If that ordinary possibility alone is enough to justify surplus, the usury prohibition loses its force: the lender no longer needs to show actual loss, culpable delay, or concrete foregone gain, only to point to uncertainty. Risk then becomes the universal solvent of the doctrine. The theologians of the early fourteenth century were not modern financial economists; they had no formal theory of probability, portfolio diversification, expected value, or risk-adjusted return. But they did understand the moral danger of using a real burden as a pretext.

This is the recurring pattern in the extrinsic titles. A penalty can be just, but an engineered penalty is usury in disguise. Compensation for actual loss can be just, but fictive damages are a fraud. Foregone profit can sometimes be compensable, but abstract opportunity cost can swallow the whole prohibition. So too with risk: genuine exposure to loss may justify surplus, but generalized default risk can turn every loan into an interest-bearing instrument.

This is why periculum sortis had to develop slowly. It touched the deepest fault line in the doctrine: the difference between risk-sharing and risk-shifting. If the capital provider truly shares downside, the moral structure begins to resemble partnership; if he receives guaranteed repayment, secured by collateral, seniority, guarantees, or enforcement against the borrower's other assets, the claim remains much closer to a loan. The language of risk may still appear in the contract, but the substance must be examined: is the investor truly exposed to failure, or has failure been transferred to the borrower?

The early appearance of periculum sortis therefore marks a new analytical sophistication in usury doctrine. Theologians were beginning to see that the moral analysis of surplus could not stop at the nominal form of the contract; it had to ask how the contract allocated loss. That mattered especially in commercial finance, where transactions could be designed to appear as one thing while functioning as another. A contract might look like a partnership but behave like secured debt, look like a sale but conceal a loan, or look like compensation for danger while guaranteeing the creditor's gain and imposing ruin on the borrower.

Once capital risk becomes thinkable as an extrinsic title, late scholastic authors can begin to analyze risk more explicitly. Medina, Molina, Lessius, and Lugo will develop the logic further, asking whether uncertainty of repayment affects the value of a claim, whether risk can be priced, and whether risk-bearing may justify surplus. The title will eventually become part of the mature scholastic repertoire. But its early appearance remains unstable, because the central question has not yet been answered: what kind of risk counts?

That instability is not weakness; it is the sign of a tradition wrestling honestly with new economic realities. The scholastics were not merely defending an old rule against clever merchants, nor blessing whatever commerce required; they were trying to distinguish compensation from extraction in situations where the difference was no longer obvious. Periculum sortis became necessary because capital could be placed in real danger. It became dangerous because creditors could use the language of danger to justify guaranteed gain.

The early fourteenth-century significance of the title, then, is twofold. First, it names risk to principal as a morally relevant fact, a genuine development: capital exposed to loss differs from capital protected by a fixed claim. Second, it introduces a problem that will haunt the doctrine from that point forward. If risk can justify surplus, the tradition must specify what kind of risk, how much risk, borne by whom, under what legal structure, and with what proportionality between danger and return.

In that sense Alexander's moment is not the end of a doctrinal story but the beginning of a new line of inquiry. The title is named before it is safe, visible before it is disciplined, opening a door that later theologians must guard. And this is precisely why it matters: periculum sortis brings us to the edge where medieval moral theology begins to look startlingly modern. It asks the question that every serious moral analysis of finance must still ask: is capital truly sharing risk, or is it merely charging others for the privilege of bearing it?

sections.title: 27.3 The Unresolved Question
paragraphs: The early appearance of periculum sortis raises the question that the tradition could not avoid: how much risk is enough?

This is the question that makes the title both necessary and dangerous. Necessary, because genuine exposure to loss really does change the moral form of a transaction; dangerous, because some degree of risk can be found almost everywhere. No debtor is immortal, no business is perfectly secure, and no legal system can absolutely guarantee repayment in every circumstance. If mere possibility of nonpayment justified surplus, every loan would become interest-bearing by definition, and the prohibition would remain in words while disappearing in practice.

The problem, then, is not whether risk exists but what kind of risk matters morally. Ordinary default risk is not yet enough. A lender may always face the possibility that a borrower will fail, disappear, die, become insolvent, or resist collection. But in the ordinary mutuum this risk exists alongside a legal and moral claim to repayment. The borrower still owes the principal; the lender may pursue it, and his claim does not rise and fall with the success of the borrower's use of the money. Even if the borrower's project fails, the borrower remains bound to repay. In such a case the lender's risk is not partnership risk. It is enforcement risk, collection risk, or credit risk, but not necessarily shared venture risk.

Periculum sortis was morally strongest where the capital provider could truly lose the principal because the transaction itself made repayment contingent. In a partnership, the investor may gain if the venture succeeds and lose if it fails. In a sea loan, repayment may depend upon the safe arrival of the ship. In a commenda-like arrangement, capital may be placed into a commercial enterprise whose outcome is uncertain. Here risk is not accidental to the transaction; it is built into the structure, and the capital is exposed to the fate of the venture.

By contrast, in a secured loan, risk may be reduced or shifted. If repayment is guaranteed by collateral, personal sureties, senior claims, penalty clauses, or enforceable recourse against the borrower's other assets, then the capital provider may keep the vocabulary of risk while avoiding much of its substance. The borrower, the worker, the family, the supplier, or the community may absorb the actual downside. The lender may still be inconvenienced, still face delay and some probability of non-recovery. But the moral question is whether he is truly sharing the loss, or whether he has structured the transaction so that loss falls primarily on someone else.
This is the heart of the unresolved question: is ordinary default risk sufficient, or is special assumed risk required?

A broad interpretation says that any genuine uncertainty of repayment diminishes the value of the lender's claim. A risky claim to one hundred is not worth the same as a certain claim to one hundred. On this reasoning, surplus may compensate for the lower present value of a risky obligation. This line of thought becomes more visible in later scholastic and early modern discussions, as theologians become more attentive to valuation, commercial uncertainty, and the practical world of trade. It has an intuitive appeal. Risk is real. A claim that may not be paid is worth less than a claim certain to be paid, and to treat them as identical may ignore economic reality.

But a narrow interpretation worries that this reasoning proves too much. If every uncertain claim is worth less than a certain claim, then every loan can be priced upward for risk. The lender need not share in a venture, accept contingency, or subordinate his claim. He need only say that repayment is not mathematically certain, and the language of risk could become a universal permission slip. The usury prohibition then becomes a technical relic, preserved only in cases of impossibly riskless loans. This is why rigorists resisted broad uses of the risk title.

The deeper issue is whether risk is being shared or merely priced. To price risk while shifting it to the borrower is not the same as bearing risk with the borrower. A creditor may charge a risk premium because the borrower is fragile, undercollateralized, or desperate. But if the contract still requires repayment regardless of outcome, and if failure leaves the borrower ruined while the creditor retains legal recourse, the premium may intensify domination rather than express solidarity. The creditor is not necessarily participating in the borrower's risk. He may be monetizing the borrower's vulnerability.

This is where periculum sortis becomes one of the most important titles for modern moral finance. It forces us to look past the presence of risk and ask about its allocation. Who can lose principal? Who must repay after failure? Who has collateral? Who holds seniority? Who can force liquidation? Who can walk away? Who is protected by legal form? Who is exposed by economic necessity? The moral question is not simply whether the transaction is risky in the abstract. It is whether the person receiving surplus actually bears the risk for which that surplus is claimed.

The same question also disciplines proportionality. Even when risk is genuine, it does not follow that any amount of surplus is justified; compensation must remain proportionate to the burden. A small possibility of loss cannot justify unlimited upside; a modest exposure cannot justify a claim that consumes the borrower's future. If the surplus is framed as compensation for risk, the amount must correspond to the risk actually borne; otherwise the title becomes an excuse for extraction.

There is also the problem of double-counting. A lender might claim a penalty for delay, compensation for actual loss, foregone profit, administrative fees, and a risk premium all in the same transaction. Each title may have some legitimate use in principle, but the same underlying burden cannot be counted repeatedly. If default risk has already been addressed through collateral, guarantees, covenants, fees, and penalties, an additional risk premium may no longer be restorative; it may be cumulative extraction. The scholastic concern for specific titles, proof, and proportionality is therefore not antiquarian fussiness but a method for preventing moral inflation.

The unresolved question also exposes the limits of legal form. A contract may be called equity while functioning like debt. It may be called investment while guaranteeing repayment. It may be called partnership while giving one party control, priority, and exit rights that protect it from meaningful downside. Conversely, a contract may be legally structured as debt but contain real loss-sharing features. The scholastic method asks us to examine the moral substance: who owns, who governs, who bears loss, and who receives surplus?

This is why the early doctrine of periculum sortis cannot be reduced to a primitive anticipation of modern interest theory. The scholastics were not simply saying, "Risk deserves return." Their question was sharper and more moral: "Is the return being claimed for a risk that the capital provider actually bears?" That distinction is decisive. Modern finance often speaks as though risk and return are naturally paired. The scholastic tradition asks whether they are paired in the actual contract, or whether return has been secured for one party while risk has been pushed onto another.

Alexander of Alexandria's early formulation leaves this question open. Later authors will attempt to answer it in different ways: some will move toward broader acceptance of risk pricing, others will warn that the movement hollows out the usury prohibition. Molina, Lessius, and Lugo will help consolidate the title. Concina and other rigorists will later attack its permissive use. Benedict XIV will insist that gain from the loan itself remains usury, even while leaving room for legitimate extrinsic titles. The tension never fully disappears.

And perhaps that is fitting. Periculum sortis names a real moral fact, but one that resists mechanical application. It cannot be judged by formula alone. It requires attention to legal structure, economic substance, intention, proportionality, vulnerability, and actual loss allocation. It asks whether finance creates fellowship in risk, or a hierarchy in which one party is protected and another is exposed.

The unresolved question, then, is not only medieval. It is ours. How much risk is enough? What kind of risk counts? Who bears the downside? Can principal actually be lost? Or has the language of risk become another way for capital to demand guaranteed growth from those least able to bear failure?

The answer cannot be found merely by naming the title, only by examining the transaction. Periculum sortis is legitimate only where risk is real, borne by the claimant, proportionate to the surplus, and not already shifted elsewhere. Without those disciplines, it becomes the most dangerous of the extrinsic titles: the opening by which compensation turns into extraction without ever giving up the vocabulary of justice.

chapters.part: Part VIII — Periculum Sortis: Risk of Capital and the Moral Structure of Downside

chapters.title: Late Medieval Hesitation and Spanish Scholastic Transition
chapters.label: Chapter 28

sections.title: 28.1 Why the Title Remained Unstable
paragraphs: Periculum sortis remained unstable because it touched the most explosive question in the whole usury tradition: whether a lender could receive surplus simply because repayment was uncertain.

The earlier extrinsic titles had their dangers, but their boundaries were somewhat easier to see. Poena conventionalis concerned delay: a borrower had failed to repay on time, and the question was whether a penalty could be imposed without turning it into hidden yield. Damnum emergens concerned actual loss: a lender had suffered damage, and the question was whether compensation restored equality or enriched him. Lucrum cessans was more dangerous, since it concerned a profit not made, but even there the tradition could ask whether the foregone gain was concrete, intended, probable, and caused by the loan.

Periculum sortis was different. It concerned the danger that the principal itself might be lost, which made it morally serious (real exposure to loss does change the nature of a transaction) and uniquely dangerous, because every loan contains at least some possibility of nonpayment. If any uncertainty of repayment were enough to justify surplus, the old prohibition would collapse into a theory of risk-priced debt. Usury would not disappear by being refuted. It would disappear by being renamed. This is why the title took so long to settle: it was not obvious how to distinguish genuine capital risk from ordinary creditor risk.

In a partnership, the distinction is relatively clear. The capital provider shares in the venture; if it succeeds he may share in gain, and if it fails he may lose his capital. In a sea loan, repayment may depend on the safe arrival of the ship. In a commenda-like structure, the capital may be exposed to the commercial outcome. In such cases risk is not merely accidental. It is built into the moral form of the transaction. But in an ordinary mutuum the borrower still owes the principal even when his project fails. The lender may face collection risk, but the borrower bears the primary burden of failure, and the lender's claim is not naturally subordinated to the outcome of the venture. It is a fixed obligation. Hence the hesitation: was the risk of nonpayment enough to justify a return, or did periculum sortis require something more like true loss-sharing?

The hesitation was intensified by the relative safety of damnum emergens, the cleanest title because it fit neatly within commutative justice. If a lender suffered a real, documentable loss because of the loan or its delayed repayment, compensation could be understood as restoration: he was not being paid for the mere use of money; he was being made whole. That logic preserved the gratuity of the mutuum while allowing justice to repair injury. Lucrum cessans was more controversial, but it too developed limiting questions. Was there a real alternative use? Was the lender actually engaged in commerce? Was the profit sufficiently probable? Did the loan truly cause the gain to cease? Was the compensation proportionate? The questions did not eliminate the danger of abstraction, but they helped discipline it, and lost profit could be admitted in bounded cases without necessarily turning every lender into a person entitled to market return.

Periculum sortis, however, pressed toward universality more quickly. Risk is pervasive. It exists not only in merchant ventures but in ordinary lending, public debt, municipal finance, household credit, and commercial exchange. Once risk becomes compensable, it becomes tempting to treat every creditor as bearing a compensable burden. The borrower's unreliability, the debtor's poverty, the uncertainty of law, the instability of markets, the lack of collateral, the possibility of delay: all of it can be gathered under the heading of risk. The title therefore threatened to become a general theory of interest.

That threat explains the late medieval hesitation. The tradition had to preserve the difference between capital genuinely at risk and capital merely owed under uncertain conditions, and this was no trivial technicality: it was the difference between participation and domination. A partner shares a venture's fate, and his profit comes with exposure to loss. A creditor may stand outside the venture and still demand repayment, and his surplus may come with legal protection, collateral, priority, and enforcement rights. To call both "risk-bearing" is to blur a morally decisive line.

The instability of the title also reflected a deeper uncertainty about valuation. Later scholastics would increasingly recognize that an uncertain claim to one hundred is not worth the same as a certain claim to one hundred. The insight is important: a claim exposed to nonpayment really does differ in value from a guaranteed one. Once it enters the usury debate, though, it raises a hard question. Does the reduced value of a risky claim justify charging more at the beginning? Or does such pricing convert risk into a standing excuse for interest?

The problem becomes sharper when risk is used against the vulnerable. Suppose a poor borrower is charged more because he is less likely to repay. From one perspective, the lender is pricing risk. From another, he is extracting more precisely from the person least able to bear it: the poorer the borrower, the higher the charge; the more fragile the debtor, the greater the surplus demanded. Risk pricing can therefore become a moral inversion: vulnerability itself becomes the reason for a heavier burden.

This is why periculum sortis could not simply be accepted as an economic fact. The scholastic question was not whether risk exists, but whether charging for it restores justice or exploits weakness. If the lender truly bears the downside, compensation may be reasonable. If the borrower bears the downside and the lender merely charges more because the borrower might fail, the title becomes morally suspect. It may describe the lender's anxiety, not necessarily his participation in loss.

The title also remained unstable because it could overlap with other claims. A lender might charge for actual loss, for foregone profit, for late payment, for administrative expense, and for risk of nonpayment, and each claim might appear plausible in isolation; together, they could bury the borrower under cumulative surplus. So the tradition asked whether the same burden was being counted multiple times. If risk was already addressed through collateral, guarantees, penalties, or higher prices, could it also justify a separate premium? If the lender was protected by legal priority, could he still claim to be exposed in a morally significant way?

Here the late medieval hesitation becomes especially relevant for modern finance. Contemporary contracts often layer protections: seniority, collateral, covenants, liquidation preferences, personal guarantees, default interest, fees, conversion rights, redemption rights, and control provisions. Each protection may be justified as risk management. Together they can shift nearly all downside away from capital while preserving upside for capital. The language of risk remains, but the substance of risk-bearing disappears.

The late medieval theologians did not describe the problem in modern financial vocabulary, but they saw its moral shape. They knew that a title could be true in principle and false in use, that legal form could conceal economic substance, and that a payment described as compensation could become extraction if the burden was fictive, exaggerated, duplicated, or imposed on the vulnerable. Periculum sortis remained unstable because it required the tradition to judge not only the existence of risk, but the structure of risk.

The instability carried into the Spanish Scholastic transition. As commerce expanded and moral theologians became more attentive to market valuation, the title became harder to ignore. Medina, Juan de la Peña, Molina, Lessius, and Lugo would all contribute to a more sophisticated analysis of risk, repayment probability, and the value of uncertain claims. But the hesitation did not disappear. It could not, because the danger was built into the title itself.

Periculum sortis names a real burden. Capital can be lost; risk can be borne; loss exposure can justify compensation. Yet the same title can also give usury its most elegant cover. It can transform every borrower's fragility into a lender's claim, turn uncertainty into yield, and make guaranteed growth appear morally justified because risk was once present somewhere in the transaction.

The title remained unstable, then, not because medieval theologians were confused but because they were alert. They understood that the moral difference between risk-sharing and risk-shifting is easy to name and difficult to enforce, and the whole question of periculum sortis turns on that difference. Is capital truly exposed to loss? Or is it protected while charging others for exposure they themselves must bear?

That unresolved tension is precisely why the title became so important. It forced moral theology to look inside the architecture of finance: to ask where loss goes, who is protected, and whether surplus is tied to real vulnerability in the capital provider or extracted from vulnerability in the borrower. And it refused to let the mere word "risk" settle the matter.

sections.title: 28.2 Medina
paragraphs: Medina stands at an important transitional point in the development of periculum sortis. (The man behind the single name is Juan de Medina, 1490–1546, who taught theology at Alcalá; his treatise on restitution and contracts, the Codex de restitutione et contractibus, was printed at Alcalá in 1546.) By the time the Spanish Scholastics take up the problem, the question is no longer merely whether capital risk can be named; Alexander of Alexandria had already helped make that thinkable. The sharper question is how risk should be judged in practical lending and commercial life. Is risk relevant only when the capital is placed into a partnership-like venture? Or can the risk of nonpayment in an ordinary loan affect the moral valuation of the transaction?

Medina is significant because he represents an early movement toward risk pricing. He does not simply repeat the older distinction between the safe mutuum and the risky societas. He begins to treat the reliability of repayment itself as morally and economically relevant. A claim against a solid debtor is not the same as a claim against an uncertain one: collateral, reputation, and stable income on one side; doubtful repayment on the other. The probability of repayment begins to matter.

The move is subtle, but important. In the stricter medieval analysis, the central issue was whether the lender had transferred ownership of the money in a mutuum and retained only a claim to equivalent repayment. If so, surplus from the loan itself was usury, and risk-bearing was clearer in partnership, sea loans, commenda, or other structures where capital remained exposed to the outcome of a venture. Medina's thought begins to move risk analysis closer to the creditor's claim itself. If repayment is uncertain, is the claim worth less than its nominal amount? And if so, may the lender receive more to compensate for that uncertainty?

Here the logic of valuation enters the usury debate. A certain claim to one hundred and a doubtful claim to one hundred carry the same face value and different practical values; a debtor almost certain to repay and one who may not repay at all are not economically equivalent. Medina's importance lies in letting this reasoning press upon moral theology. Risk is no longer only an external feature of adventurous trade. It becomes attached to the debtor, the contract, and the probability of repayment.

Yet this is precisely where the danger begins. Once the debtor's risk characteristics become relevant, the road opens toward differential pricing of credit: a more reliable borrower pays less, a less reliable borrower pays more. In modern terms this seems obvious, since riskier loans carry higher rates. Within the scholastic tradition, the obviousness is morally explosive. It risks turning borrower vulnerability into a source of creditor gain: the weaker the borrower, the higher the charge; the more precarious the household, the more expensive the loan; the more uncertain the repayment, the more the debtor must promise.

This is why Medina's transitional role must be read carefully. He helps moral theology acknowledge something real (uncertainty affects value), but the tradition cannot simply adopt risk pricing as a morally neutral mechanism. It must still ask whether the lender actually bears the risk, whether the price is proportionate, whether the borrower is being exploited, and whether the surplus remains external to the mutuum or becomes a disguised payment for the loan itself.

The risk characteristics of the debtor are therefore morally ambiguous. They may reflect a real burden: a lender who truly faces a higher chance of losing the principal might claim restoration rather than extraction. But the debtor's weakness may be precisely the reason Christian moral theology becomes suspicious. The biblical tradition does not say: charge the poor more because they are less creditworthy. It says: do not use the poor person's need as an occasion for gain. It commands openhandedness, release, and neighborliness precisely where repayment may be fragile.

Medina's significance is that he brings these two moral intuitions into contact. The first intuition is commercial: risk changes value. The second is theological: vulnerability must not become a revenue stream. A mature doctrine of periculum sortis has to hold both together. It cannot deny risk, and it cannot allow risk to become a justification for domination.

The rate adapted to risk is therefore not merely a technical matter but a moral diagnosis of the relationship between creditor and debtor. If the rate reflects genuine exposure to loss and remains proportionate, the case may differ from ordinary usury. If the rate is imposed because the borrower lacks alternatives, it may simply monetize dependence. And if the lender has collateral, recourse, guarantees, and priority while the borrower faces ruin, then the lender's claim to risk must be examined with suspicion. The existence of uncertainty is not enough; the allocation of uncertainty is what matters.

This is why Medina's movement toward risk pricing cannot be separated from proof and proportionality. How is the risk assessed: from real knowledge of the debtor's circumstances, or from a general category of borrower weakness? Is the premium proportionate to the actual danger of losing the principal? Is the danger already covered through pledges, sureties, or legal enforcement? Does the borrower have realistic freedom to accept or refuse? Does the structure help the borrower live, work, and recover, or does it tighten the chain?

The Spanish Scholastic context made these questions unavoidable. Expanding commerce, public finance, international trade, exchange contracts, and increasingly sophisticated credit relationships forced theologians to think more carefully about uncertainty. The older categories remained essential, but they needed finer tools. Medina's contribution belongs to this movement: the analysis shifts from the simple presence or absence of risk to the valuation of uncertain repayment.

Yet the shift remains incomplete. Medina does not eliminate the older concerns but intensifies them: by making the debtor's risk relevant, he makes the danger of abuse more acute. If risk can affect the permitted surplus, then moral theology must become more exacting, not less. It must ask what kind of risk is being priced, who bears it, how it is measured, whether it is already secured, and whether the resulting charge remains within the bounds of justice.

Here periculum sortis begins to look recognizably modern, and here it becomes most spiritually dangerous. Modern credit markets routinely classify borrowers by risk and charge accordingly. The scholastic tradition asks the question modern markets often avoid: does this system merely reflect risk, or does it deepen the vulnerability that created the risk in the first place? A theology of finance cannot stop at the actuarial fact. It must ask whether the contract serves the borrower's life or profits from the borrower's fragility.

Medina therefore belongs to the transition from medieval title-analysis to early modern moral economics. Risk, he shows, is not only a property of maritime adventure or partnership capital; it can appear in the uncertain value of a repayment claim itself. But he leaves the tradition with a dangerous question: once risk is attached to the debtor's reliability, how can the doctrine prevent risk pricing from becoming a moral cover for charging the vulnerable more?

The answer can be neither to deny the reality of risk nor to bless every risk premium; it must lie in disciplined moral analysis: genuine exposure, real possibility of loss, proportionate compensation, no double-counting, no disguised payment for time, and special caution where borrower vulnerability is the very source of the claimed surplus. In this sense, Medina does not settle periculum sortis; he sharpens it. He draws the tradition closer to the modern problem by recognizing that uncertainty affects value, and he reveals why a Christian account of finance cannot be satisfied with the language of risk alone. It must always ask whose risk is being priced, whose vulnerability is being burdened, and who actually bears the loss when things go wrong.

sections.title: 28.3 Juan de la Peña
paragraphs: Juan de la Peña belongs to the same transitional world as Medina: the world in which scholastic moral theology had to confront risk not merely as a rare feature of maritime adventure or partnership, but as a pervasive problem within ordinary commercial life. (Peña, born around 1513, was a Dominican trained under Melchor Cano; in 1561 he won the Vísperas chair of theology at Salamanca and held it until his death in 1565.) His significance is that he helps carry the debate over periculum sortis into a more precise question about price, probability, and moral classification. If repayment is uncertain, does that uncertainty affect the just value of the claim? And if it does, can a creditor morally receive more than the principal without falling into usury?

This is the point at which the old categories begin to strain. The mutuum had been morally intelligible because it was simple: ownership of the money passed to the borrower, who owed back an equivalent amount. The lender could not sell the use of the money because, in a consumable loan, the use passed with the money itself; to demand a surplus for the loan as such was to demand payment for what no longer belonged to him. The logic was clear, even elegant. But commerce is rarely elegant. Claims differ in security, and debtors differ in reliability. Repayment may be likely, doubtful, delayed, collateralized, guaranteed, disputed, or contingent. A right to receive one hundred from a solvent and honorable merchant is not the same practical thing as a right to receive one hundred from a debtor of uncertain means; the nominal amounts match; the values do not. Juan de la Peña's importance lies in belonging to the discussion in which that difference becomes morally visible.

The question of risk therefore becomes a question of price. What is the just value of an uncertain claim? If a person sells, discounts, transfers, or otherwise values such a claim, should its uncertainty be taken into account? In one sense, the answer seems unavoidable. No prudent person treats a doubtful claim as equal to a certain one; a claim exposed to danger is worth less than one protected from it.

Yet the moral consequence is not straightforward. Once uncertainty affects value, it becomes possible to argue that a creditor who accepts a doubtful obligation may receive a greater return, having accepted a claim worth less than its face amount. Risk becomes not only a factual problem but a valuation problem, and the theologian must ask whether the surplus is truly compensation for the reduced value of the claim, or whether the language of valuation has been used to justify interest on a loan.

Juan de la Peña's place in the tradition matters because this line of reasoning sits near the edge of later controversy. On the one hand, it makes moral theology more realistic: it stops theologians pretending that all repayment claims are equal, acknowledges that uncertainty is not imaginary, and recognizes that risk changes the economic substance of a transaction. In this respect, it helps prepare the way for Molina, Lessius, and Lugo, who will treat uncertain claims and risk-bearing with increasing sophistication. On the other hand, the same reasoning threatens to dissolve the older prohibition. If every uncertain claim may be valued below its face amount, then every lender may demand a premium for accepting the uncertainty of repayment. The creditor can say: "I do not charge for the use of money. I charge because my claim is risky." The form of the argument has changed, but the borrower still pays more than the principal, and the doctrine must decide whether this is a genuine extrinsic title or merely a new vocabulary for the old vice.

The controversy turns on the difference between valuation and extraction. Uncertainty may truly reduce the value of a claim; it does not follow that every charge built upon it is just. A claim can be risky because the lender has genuinely exposed capital to loss. It can also be risky because the borrower is poor, politically weak, commercially desperate, or structurally disadvantaged. To charge more in the second case may not restore equality; it may deepen it.

This is the theological problem hidden inside risk pricing. Modern finance often treats risk as morally neutral: the higher the risk, the higher the required return. The scholastic tradition cannot be satisfied with that formula. It has to ask why the risk exists, who bears it, and whether the additional return burdens precisely the person already exposed. If the borrower is charged more because he is fragile, the language of risk may become a way of monetizing fragility.

Juan de la Peña's significance therefore lies not only in the movement toward valuation under uncertainty, but in the controversy that follows from it. The more precisely theologians analyze risk, the more they must discipline its use. The title cannot be allowed to float free from the actual structure of the transaction. Is the lender's principal truly endangered? Is repayment contingent upon the success of the venture? Are there guarantees, collateral, or legal remedies that shift the real loss back to the borrower? Is the surplus proportionate to the risk actually borne? Is the same risk already compensated through another title?

These questions show why periculum sortis is more dangerous than it first appears. It sounds concrete: the principal may be lost. But the moment it enters valuation, it becomes capable of abstraction. Risk can be described statistically, commercially, rhetorically, or legally; it can be made visible in one place and hidden in another. A creditor may appear to bear risk because repayment is uncertain, while in fact the borrower bears ruin if repayment fails. A financier may demand a return for risk while also holding priority, security, covenants, penalties, and control rights. The word "risk" by itself tells us almost nothing. The moral analysis must trace the actual path of loss.

This is also why the debate over Juan de la Peña belongs within the broader Spanish Scholastic transition. The Spanish theologians were not naive opponents of commerce; they were among the most sophisticated moral analysts of money, exchange, price, contract, and conscience in early modern Europe. They understood that markets produce real information and prices can reflect real conditions, and also that markets can disguise coercion, especially where need and power are unequal. Their task was not to reject valuation, but to judge it.

The question of risk and price therefore becomes a test case for the whole tradition. Can moral theology acknowledge that uncertainty affects value without turning uncertainty into a blank check? Can it recognize that capital exposed to loss deserves different treatment from capital fully protected by legal claims? Can it distinguish between a just discount for danger and an unjust premium charged to weakness? Can it preserve the anti-usury principle while admitting that not all surplus is usury?

Juan de la Peña helps bring these questions into view. He stands near the moment when periculum sortis begins to move from an exceptional insight toward a broader analytical tool. The movement is necessary, because real commercial risk cannot be ignored, and perilous, because the broader the tool becomes, the more easily it is abused. The title must be held close to its moral root: compensation for risk actually borne by the capital provider. If it becomes compensation for risk shifted onto the borrower, it has betrayed itself.

The later controversy over risk pricing is therefore no accidental scholastic quarrel; it is the inevitable result of asking a question modern finance still struggles to answer: what is the moral difference between pricing risk and sharing risk? Juan de la Peña's contribution helps us see that this difference cannot be settled by mathematics alone. It requires moral judgment about power, vulnerability, contract structure, and the real allocation of loss.

In this sense, his role in the history of periculum sortis is transitional but essential. He helps carry the doctrine into the terrain of uncertain claims and risk-adjusted valuation, and he exposes the danger of that terrain. Once risk becomes price, the tradition must ask whether price still serves justice; once uncertainty becomes a title, it must ask whether the title restores equality or legitimates domination. The unresolved problem remains, and it is not a technical one. A risky claim may indeed be worth less than a certain claim. But a vulnerable borrower is not merely a risk category. He is a neighbor. And Christian moral theology cannot permit the language of risk to erase that fact.

sections.title: 28.4 Salamanca Context
paragraphs: The Salamanca context is the setting in which periculum sortis begins to move from a fragile medieval possibility toward a more systematic early modern doctrine. The title had appeared earlier, and figures like Medina and Juan de la Peña had pressed the questions of risk, debtor reliability, and uncertain claims. But Salamanca gave these questions a broader intellectual home, where moral theology, canon law, civil law, commerce, confession, and practical economic life all met in one dense field of analysis.

The theologians of Salamanca were not writing in a world of abstract loans between isolated individuals. They were writing in a commercial and imperial world marked by long-distance trade, currency exchange, public debt, merchant contracts, colonial expansion, fluctuating prices, complex credit, and rapidly changing legal forms. Money did not sit still. It moved through bills of exchange, merchant partnerships, public obligations, private loans, and commercial ventures, and the old usury doctrine had to decide whether its inherited categories could still distinguish justice from exploitation in an increasingly sophisticated financial environment.

This did not mean abandoning the older doctrine. The gratuity of the mutuum remained a core principle; gain from the loan itself remained morally suspect; the lender who transferred ownership of consumable money and demanded a guaranteed surplus for that transfer still stood within the traditional usury problem. Salamanca did not simply bless interest. But neither did it ignore the complexity of actual finance. Its task was to determine when a payment above principal was truly attached to an external title and when it was merely a disguised price for the use of money.

In this setting, periculum sortis became increasingly important because practical finance continually raised questions about risk. Borrowers differed in reliability. Some debts were secured, others doubtful; some claims were enforceable, others exposed to political, commercial, maritime, or legal uncertainty. Some capital was placed into ventures where it could genuinely be lost, while other capital was protected by collateral, guarantees, priority, or recourse against the debtor's assets. The moral theologian could not simply ask whether a document used the word "loan" or "partnership." He had to ask how the transaction actually distributed risk.

Borrower reliability was one central issue. A claim against a dependable, well-capitalized merchant was not a claim against a debtor of uncertain solvency; the probability of repayment mattered. Yet the fact created a moral dilemma: to recognize different probabilities of repayment is to acknowledge reality; to charge vulnerable debtors more because they are vulnerable is to risk turning fragility into profit. Salamanca had to hold together two truths: uncertain repayment affects value, and the needy borrower must not be treated as a revenue opportunity.

Collateral and security sharpened the question. If a lender claimed compensation for risk while holding strong collateral, how much risk did he truly bear? If a pledge fully covered the principal, could he still demand surplus for danger to the principal? If a surety or guarantor stood behind the debt, had the risk been shifted to another party? If the lender could enforce repayment against unrelated assets, was the capital really exposed to the success or failure of the borrower's project? Such questions show why periculum sortis required more than a general appeal to uncertainty. It required analysis of legal and economic substance.

Legal enforceability also mattered. A claim that can be enforced through courts, pledges, penalties, or seizure of property is different from a claim that rises and falls with the venture itself. The more enforceable and secured the claim, the more it resembles a protected debt; the more contingent and subordinated the claim, the more it resembles risk-bearing capital. And the distinction did not map perfectly onto legal labels. A contract could be called an investment while functioning like a secured loan; another could be legally debt-like while containing genuine contingency. The Salamanca method therefore pushed beyond names toward moral anatomy.

Practical finance forced this kind of analysis. Merchants needed credit. Cities needed funding. Trade required advances, and currency exchange involved delay, distance, and uncertainty. Commercial life could not function without mechanisms for allocating capital and risk; the question was not whether finance should exist, but whether it would serve the common good or exploit weakness. Salamanca's contribution was to bring rigorous moral reasoning into the details of contracts, rather than leaving finance either condemned in the abstract or morally unexamined in practice.

This is why the Spanish Scholastics became so important for the later history of the extrinsic titles. They inhabited a world in which damnum emergens, lucrum cessans, and periculum sortis could not remain merely theoretical categories. Merchants, confessors, judges, and civic authorities needed guidance. Could compensation be agreed in advance? Could a doubtful claim be discounted? Could a lender receive something for risk of nonpayment? Could a merchant charge more because money was tied up in a risky transaction? Could public debt or exchange contracts be distinguished from usurious loans? Such questions required careful distinctions.

In the case of periculum sortis, the central distinction remained risk-sharing versus risk-shifting. If the capital provider genuinely bore the possibility of losing the principal, surplus could be understood as attached to a real burden. If, however, the borrower remained bound to repay regardless of the venture's outcome, while the lender was protected by collateral, seniority, or legal recourse, then the claim to risk became weaker. The mere fact that collection might be inconvenient or uncertain did not automatically transform the creditor into a partner.

This is where Salamanca's concern for probability and valuation had to be tempered by Christian moral anthropology. It is economically true that a risky claim has a different value from a certain claim. But the debtor is not only a probability of repayment. He is a person embedded in household, vocation, community, and need, and a theology of finance cannot reduce him to a risk score. The more vulnerable he is, the more carefully the theologian must ask whether the transaction supports life or deepens dependence.

The Salamanca context therefore exposes the double edge of sophistication. More refined financial analysis can serve justice by distinguishing real risk from fictive risk, real loss from imaginary loss, and partnership from disguised debt; the same sophistication can also multiply evasions. The more complex contracts become, the easier it is to hide usury beneath exchange, insurance, penalty, damages, opportunity cost, risk premium, or administrative charge. Moral theology must therefore become more precise as finance becomes more inventive.

This precision required attention to intention, causality, proportionality, and proof. Intention mattered because a title could be invoked in bad faith. Causality mattered because the surplus had to be connected to the burden claimed. Proportionality mattered because compensation could not exceed the loss, risk, or burden it purported to address. And proof mattered because fictive titles are always easier to invent than real ones are to document. These criteria were not bureaucratic details; they were spiritual safeguards against extraction.

The Salamanca context also reveals why periculum sortis could not be isolated from the other titles. Risk of capital overlaps with actual loss, because risk may become loss. It overlaps with lost profit, because risky deployment may prevent another gain. It overlaps with penalties and security, because default risk may be handled through contractual remedies, and it overlaps with labor and expense, because risk management itself may require administration. The moral danger is that all of these titles can be stacked until the borrower pays several times for the same underlying uncertainty. A disciplined doctrine must prevent such double-counting.

This is especially important because the Spanish Scholastic transition begins to move toward the world we now inhabit. Modern finance routinely separates, prices, trades, insures, discounts, securitizes, and reallocates risk. But the moral question remains recognizably scholastic: who actually bears the downside? If capital receives a premium for risk while legally avoiding loss, the title is suspect; if the borrower pays for risk that has already been shifted elsewhere, the title is suspect; and if the structure makes the lender whole while the borrower absorbs ruin, the language of periculum sortis has been severed from its moral root.

Salamanca did not solve all of this once and for all. It could not; the questions were too complex, and later authors would disagree about how far risk pricing could go. Molina, Lessius, and Lugo would develop more permissive and systematic treatments. Rigorists would later worry that the tradition had given away too much. Benedict XIV would insist again that gain from the loan itself remains usury, while leaving room for genuine extrinsic titles. The tension survived because it belongs to the subject matter.

The importance of the Salamanca context, then, is not that it eliminates the instability but that it gives the instability a method, teaching the tradition to ask concrete questions. Is the borrower reliable? Is repayment probable? Is the claim secured? Can the lender enforce repayment? Is the capital genuinely exposed? Is the return proportionate? Is the surplus compensation, participation, or domination? Those questions allowed moral theology to enter the details of finance without surrendering its central concern for justice.

The movement from Alexander to Salamanca is thus a movement from naming risk to analyzing risk. Alexander helps make periculum sortis thinkable; Medina and Juan de la Peña begin to connect risk with repayment probability and valuation; Salamanca places those questions within a broader moral, legal, and commercial framework. The result is not a simple permission for interest, but a more demanding inquiry into the structure of downside. That inquiry remains indispensable, because finance becomes morally legible only when we know where loss goes. What endures from Salamanca is the refusal to accept the language of risk at face value: it must be tested against the actual structure of the contract, the vulnerability of the borrower, the protections of the creditor, and the purpose of the surplus. Only then can periculum sortis remain what it was meant to be, not a loophole for guaranteed growth, but a disciplined title for genuine risk-bearing.

chapters.part: Part VIII — Periculum Sortis: Risk of Capital and the Moral Structure of Downside

chapters.title: Molina, Lessius, and Lugo
chapters.label: Chapter 29

sections.title: 29.1 Luis de Molina
paragraphs: Luis de Molina marks a decisive step in the maturation of periculum sortis. By the time Molina enters the discussion, the problem of capital risk is no longer a marginal exception or an uneasy intuition. It has become part of a broader late-scholastic attempt to analyze contracts according to their real economic substance. Molina's importance lies in bringing valuation, uncertainty, and repayment risk into a more systematic moral framework. He helps the tradition ask not only whether the lender may receive more than principal, but whether the claim the lender is holding is itself worth its face value.

The core insight is deceptively simple: a risky claim to one hundred is not worth the same as a certain claim to one hundred. If repayment is secure, enforceable, collateralized, and practically certain, the claim has one value. If repayment is doubtful, contingent, unsecured, exposed to commercial failure, or dependent on uncertain circumstances, it has another. The nominal amount may be the same, but the moral and economic substance differs. Molina's treatment gives greater room to this difference.

This is not yet modern finance in the full technical sense. Molina is not building a mathematical theory of expected value, probability-weighted cash flows, or risk-adjusted return. He is working within the language of moral theology, law, conscience, and just exchange. Yet his analysis moves in a direction modern readers will recognize: he treats uncertainty as relevant to valuation, recognizes that a claim exposed to nonpayment may be worth less than a secure claim, and helps develop the possibility that surplus may sometimes compensate for the diminished value or danger attached to the claim, rather than being charged for the mere use of money.

This is why Molina matters for periculum sortis: the title had always depended on the idea that capital could be genuinely at risk, and Molina helps articulate what that risk does to the value of a right to repayment. If the lender's principal is exposed, then the lender does not possess the same kind of secure claim as in an ordinary guaranteed mutuum. The risk is not just a psychological discomfort; it changes the substance of the transaction, and the lender holds something uncertain.

This line of thought appears in later summaries of Molina, including the Conimbricenses tradition (the Jesuit commentators of Coimbra), which expresses the point as unequal value between secure and risky claims. The risk of nonrepayment diminishes the value of the claim: a promise to pay one hundred in uncertain circumstances is not equivalent to one hundred safely in hand, nor to a perfectly secure right to one hundred. Once this difference is acknowledged, the moral analysis of surplus becomes more complex. A payment above principal may be described not as interest on the loan, but as compensation for the risk attached to the claim.

Yet Molina's sophistication also makes the danger clearer. If risk diminishes value, and if diminished value can justify compensation, then risk can become a broad justification for surplus: every debt has some risk, every borrower might fail, every repayment claim is subject to some uncertainty. Without limits, Molina's insight could be used to turn the whole usury prohibition into a theory of credit pricing. The very realism that strengthens moral theology could also weaken its discipline.

Molina's importance therefore lies not in making risk a magic word, but in forcing the tradition to analyze risk carefully. It is not enough to say that a claim is uncertain: the uncertainty must be real, borne by the person claiming compensation, not already eliminated by collateral, guarantees, sureties, penalties, or legal priority, and proportionate to the surplus demanded, and it must not merely rename the lender's desire for profit.

This is where Molina's account remains connected to the older scholastic grammar. The extrinsic title must remain extrinsic. The surplus must not arise from the mutuum as such; it must arise from something outside the bare transfer of consumable money: a real burden, danger, loss, or uncertainty borne by the lender. If the lender simply says, "I lent money, and lending is always risky, therefore I may charge more," the title begins to collapse into ordinary interest. But if the lender can show that the principal is genuinely exposed to loss in a specific transaction, the moral case becomes different.

The difference depends on the structure of the contract. Suppose repayment depends on the success of a commercial venture. Suppose the capital provider cannot demand repayment if the venture fails. Suppose the claim is subordinated, unsecured, or genuinely contingent. In such a case the lender (or perhaps more accurately, the capital provider) bears a risk closer to partnership, and the surplus received may be tied to the danger of losing principal. But suppose the lender holds collateral, personal guarantees, seniority, and full recourse against the borrower's assets. Then the claim to periculum sortis is weaker. The capital may be legally protected even if the borrower is economically exposed.

This distinction is crucial because Molina's reasoning can be misunderstood if detached from moral substance. The point is not that uncertainty automatically deserves return, but that a risky claim is not identical to a secure claim, and moral theology must ask whether the surplus corresponds to that difference in value, or whether it exploits the borrower's need. The question, in other words, is not merely actuarial but relational. What does the transaction do to the borrower? What does it protect in the lender? What happens when things go badly?

Molina also links periculum sortis to the broader late-scholastic interest in price and exchange. The Spanish Scholastics were acutely aware that value is not always fixed by face amount or formal category. Market conditions, scarcity, risk, utility, time, distance, and legal uncertainty can affect valuation. Their moral theology was therefore more flexible and commercially aware than caricatures of anti-usury doctrine suggest. But this flexibility was not moral surrender. It was an attempt to bring justice into increasingly complex transactions.

Molina's analysis helps us see why the scholastic tradition cannot be reduced to a simple opposition between medieval moralism and modern financial realism. The late scholastics were realists. They knew that risk changes value, that secure and insecure claims differ, and that commercial life required more than rigid formulas. But they also knew that financial realism without moral discipline becomes a tool of domination. A realistic account of risk must be matched by a theological account of vulnerability.

This is especially important when risk is connected to the debtor's condition. If the debtor is unreliable because he is poor, desperate, politically weak, or economically fragile, then risk pricing can become morally perverse. The more vulnerable the borrower, the more expensive the credit. What appears as compensation for danger may become extraction from need. Molina's framework therefore requires a further question: does the risk premium correspond to risk genuinely borne by the lender, or does it intensify the burden on the borrower who is already exposed?

This question also applies to commercial borrowers. A merchant seeking capital for a risky venture may rightly be treated differently from a borrower seeking food, medicine, housing, or survival. The biblical tradition's special concern for the poor does not disappear in scholastic contract analysis; the old command not to act as a moneylender toward the poor remains in the background. Molina's valuation logic must therefore be read within a wider moral world in which need, vulnerability, and neighbor-love remain central.

The Conimbricenses-style summary of Molina's position helps clarify the intellectual transition. Risk is not merely an accidental inconvenience. It can affect the value of the claim itself. But this does not mean that every lender may simply add a premium. The value difference must be grounded in the actual uncertainty of the claim, not in a fictional or generalized appeal to risk. The compensation must correspond to the burden. And the transaction must not be structured so that the lender receives the premium while the borrower bears the real loss.

Molina therefore brings periculum sortis closer to a mature theory, but he also reveals why the title remains morally delicate. It is powerful because it recognizes that capital exposed to loss differs from capital guaranteed repayment; dangerous because the language of risk can be stretched to cover almost any loan; useful because it asks whether the claim is secure or doubtful; and perilous because it can make the debtor's weakness into the creditor's justification.

For modern non-extractive finance, Molina's insight is indispensable but insufficient by itself. It teaches us to ask whether the capital provider's claim is genuinely at risk, shows that a fixed claim and a contingent claim are not morally identical, and helps us see why loss-absorbing capital differs from guaranteed debt. But it also warns us that risk language must be audited. A return justified by risk must be matched by real exposure to loss, not paired with contractual protections that shift the downside away from capital while preserving capital's upside.

In this sense, Molina stands between medieval usury doctrine and modern finance. He does not abandon the prohibition on gain from the loan itself. But he expands the analytical tools by which theologians can judge whether a surplus is truly from the loan, or from an external burden attached to the lender's claim. His contribution is not a license for ordinary interest. It is a more precise question: what is the value of an uncertain claim, and who truly bears the uncertainty?

That question remains at the center of moral finance. A risky claim to one hundred is not the same as a certain claim to one hundred. But a human borrower is not merely a risk-adjusted claim. Molina helps us see the first truth. A Christian moral economy must never forget the second.

sections.title: 29.2 Leonardus Lessius
paragraphs: Leonardus Lessius brings periculum sortis closer to its decisive late-scholastic consolidation. If Molina helps clarify the valuation problem (that a risky claim to one hundred is not equivalent to a certain claim to one hundred), Lessius presses the doctrine further into the practical world of contracts, commerce, money, and uncertainty.
