He belongs to the moment when scholastic moral theology becomes highly sensitive to the realities of early modern finance, yet still tries to preserve the moral difference between compensation and usury.

Lessius is important because he treats risk of repayment not as a marginal curiosity, but as a serious title within a broader theory of legitimate and illegitimate surplus. The question is no longer simply whether capital is at risk in obvious partnership forms, but whether uncertainty of repayment itself can constitute a burden for which compensation may be received. In this sense, Lessius helps give periculum sortis solid ground. He does not merely inherit the title. He helps make it analytically usable.

The heart of the matter is uncertainty. If the lender's capital is genuinely exposed to nonrepayment, then the lender does not hold a secure equivalent of money in hand. He holds a claim that may fail. That uncertainty can diminish the value of the claim and impose a real burden on the capital provider. Lessius's contribution is to take this burden seriously. Where earlier rigor might have treated any surplus above principal as presumptively usurious unless attached to very concrete loss, Lessius allows a more developed account of risk itself as a compensable factor.

But this development is perilous. To admit compensation for uncertainty of repayment is to approach the edge of modern interest theory. If the possibility of non-payment justifies surplus, then almost every loan can be framed as risky, and every risky loan may seem to warrant a premium. The title that began as a way of recognizing genuine exposure to loss can become the ordinary justification for charging interest. Lessius therefore stands at a crucial boundary: he helps make the doctrine realistic, and in doing so brings it closer to the danger of dissolution.

His treatment must therefore be understood within the wider architecture of the extrinsic titles. Lessius is not simply saying that money naturally deserves growth over time. That would abandon the usury doctrine. The title remains extrinsic. The surplus must be tied to a burden outside the mere transfer of money in a mutuum. Risk must be real, actually borne by the capital provider, proportionate in amount, and no fiction created to disguise ordinary interest.

Still, Lessius's work shows how the center of gravity is shifting. Earlier discussions often began from the simple moral anatomy of the mutuum: ownership passes to the borrower; the lender is owed equivalent principal; surplus for the loan itself is usury. Lessius remains within that tradition, but he is increasingly attentive to the market reality that not all claims to repayment are equal. Some are secure, some doubtful, some exposed to a debtor's solvency, honesty, circumstances, or commercial success. This difference matters, and Lessius gives theology language to address it.

His treatment of periculum sortis also sits near his discussion of carentia pecuniae, the lack or absence of money. This adjacent title concerns the lender's being deprived of the use of his money for a time. It is related to, but distinct from, lost profit and capital risk. Lessius's willingness to analyze carentia pecuniae shows how far scholastic moral theology had moved into the territory that modern finance would later describe in terms of liquidity, term commitment, and time value. But it also reveals the danger. If being without money for a term is treated too broadly as compensable, then the whole doctrine risks becoming ordinary interest by another name.

The relationship between periculum sortis and carentia pecuniae is therefore instructive. Risk concerns the danger of losing principal. Carentia pecuniae concerns the burden of being without money. Lost profit concerns the gain that could otherwise have been made. Actual loss concerns damage suffered. In practice these can overlap: a lender might be without money, lose an alternative profit, face risk of nonpayment, and incur actual damage if repayment fails. But moral discipline requires that these burdens be distinguished rather than stacked indiscriminately. Otherwise, the borrower pays several times for the same underlying fact: that the lender parted with money.

Lessius's sophistication therefore demands an equally sophisticated anti-abuse logic. If periculum sortis is invoked, can the principal actually be lost? If carentia pecuniae, is the absence of money a distinct burden or merely another way of charging for time? If lucrum cessans, was the foregone gain concrete or merely imagined? If damnum emergens, is the loss actual and documented? Lessius's world requires careful classification, because the financial instruments are increasingly capable of moral camouflage.

This is the edge of modern money-market logic. In a money market, capital is priced according to time, liquidity, creditworthiness, risk, and alternative uses. Lessius does not simply endorse such a world in modern terms, but his analysis begins to touch its conceptual foundations. He sees that money's absence, risk of nonpayment, and commercial opportunity are not imaginary; they affect real economic life. But he is also working within a theological tradition that refuses to let these realities become automatic claims to growth.

This tension makes Lessius especially important for a modern non-extractive finance framework. He helps us see why a morally serious analysis cannot simply say "no surplus ever." There can be real risk, real labor, real cost, real loss, and real sacrifice. Institutions that provide capital may need compensation to remain sustainable. Capital genuinely exposed to loss may be treated differently from capital guaranteed repayment. Yet Lessius also shows why every claim to compensation must be tested. The more refined the title, the easier it is to abuse.

In practical terms, Lessius forces us to ask whether capital is truly exposed or merely inconvenienced. If the lender's claim is fully secured, senior, collateralized, and enforceable, then the appeal to periculum sortis becomes weak. If the lender can recover from the borrower regardless of the project's failure, the lender has not truly joined the borrower in the venture's fate. If the lender receives a risk premium while legal documents shift loss onto the borrower, workers, suppliers, or guarantors, then the title becomes morally suspect.

By contrast, if capital is subordinated, unsecured, contingent, or genuinely loss-absorbing, then the analysis changes. A provider of such capital does not merely rent money; he participates in uncertainty, may not recover the principal, and may bear real downside. In that case, surplus may be closer to compensation for risk or participation in a venture than to usury. Lessius's consolidation of periculum sortis gives theology a way to recognize that distinction.

But Lessius's work also shows why the distinction cannot be made by legal labels alone. A contract may call itself partnership while guaranteeing the capital provider's return, call itself debt while actually sharing loss, call itself investment while enforcing repayment through collateral and penalties, or call itself a loan while adding charges for risk, term, opportunity cost, administrative expense, and late payment. The moral theologian must look through the document to the actual structure of claims and losses.

This is where Lessius belongs in the genealogy of the extrinsic titles. Hostiensis and the canonists helped formalize exceptions. Aquinas gave the basic moral grammar of mutuum, partnership, actual loss, and usury. Later medieval authors wrestled with lost profit and commercial reality. Molina sharpened valuation under uncertainty. Lessius consolidates the risk title in a more finance-aware moral theology. He brings the discussion close to the threshold of modern credit markets while still insisting, at least in principle, that the surplus must not arise from the loan itself.

The danger is that this threshold can be crossed almost invisibly. Once risk, absence of money, and alternative use are all admitted as possible titles, the lender's position becomes easier to defend and the borrower's burden easier to rationalize. The doctrine can still invoke the extrinsic titles while permitting ordinary interest in substance. This is why later critics would worry that the late scholastic tradition had stretched the doctrine too far. Lessius stands near the center of that tension.

Yet it would be unfair to reduce him to permissiveness. His contribution is better understood as disciplined realism. He recognizes that financial life contains genuine risks and burdens that earlier formulations could not always describe adequately, and gives confessors and moral theologians tools for analyzing them. He helps distinguish secure claims from risky claims, bare loans from risk-bearing capital, and fictional titles from potentially legitimate ones. The problem is not that he takes risk seriously. The problem is that risk, once taken seriously, must be governed with extraordinary care.

For modern moral finance, Lessius therefore offers both a resource and a warning. The resource is his insistence that uncertainty and capital risk can matter morally. The warning is that the same insight can become the gateway to generalized interest. If risk is real, borne by the capital provider, proportionate, and not otherwise compensated, it may justify surplus. If risk is vague, shifted, duplicated, insured away, or invoked merely because all future repayment is uncertain, it becomes a pretext.

Lessius helps consolidate periculum sortis because he gives the title analytical depth. But in doing so, he also reveals its spiritual danger. The more precisely finance can price risk, the more carefully theology must ask whether the one receiving the price is actually the one bearing the risk. That remains the decisive question. Without it, periculum sortis becomes ordinary interest with a scholastic name. With it, the title can still serve its original purpose: distinguishing genuine risk-bearing from extraction disguised as compensation.

sections.title: 29.3 Juan de Lugo
paragraphs: Juan de Lugo brings the late-scholastic discussion of periculum sortis to one of its most mature and expansive formulations. If Molina sharpened the problem of uncertain claims, and Lessius helped consolidate the title as a serious ground for compensation, Lugo gathers the strands together into a broader theory of detriment. Risk, lost gain, and actual loss begin to appear not as isolated exceptions, but as related ways in which a lender or capital provider may suffer a burden external to the bare mutuum.

This is what makes Lugo so important. He does not merely add one more opinion to a list of scholastic cases; he helps reveal the underlying logic late scholastic moral theology had been moving toward: surplus may be licit when it is not taken from the loan as such, but from a real burden borne by the person who supplies capital. That burden may be an actual loss already suffered, a concrete gain prevented, or a genuine risk of losing the principal. In each case, the surplus is defended not as the natural fruit of money, but as compensation for detriment.

This is also why Lugo is both illuminating and dangerous. His synthesis helps make sense of the extrinsic titles as a coherent family. But by gathering them under a broad theory of detriment, he also expands the space in which surplus can be justified. If risk, lost gain, and actual loss are all treated as forms of compensable disadvantage, then the moral task becomes more complex. The question is no longer simply whether a title exists in name, but whether the alleged detriment is real, borne by the claimant, proportionate, not already compensated, and not a pretext for payment on the loan itself.

Lugo's treatment of periculum sortis fits this broader pattern. Where the lender or capital provider truly takes on risk of losing the capital lent, surplus may be justified, because the lender has accepted a danger that is not intrinsic to a safe mutuum. The claim is not: "Money deserves more money because time has passed." The claim is: "The capital provider may lose the principal, and this exposure to loss is a real burden." If the burden is real, then compensation may be understood as restorative, or as payment for risk-bearing rather than as usury.

But Lugo's reasoning depends on the reality of risk. It cannot function if the risk is merely verbal. A lender who claims risk while holding full security, recourse, and enforceability does not stand in the same position as one whose capital is genuinely exposed to loss. A creditor who can recover from the borrower regardless of the venture's outcome is not sharing the venture's fate in the way a partner does. A financier who charges for risk while shifting loss to the borrower, guarantor, workers, suppliers, or community has not borne the burden for which he claims compensation.

This is why Lugo's synthesis must be read with the older distinction between mutuum and societas still in view. Aquinas had already drawn the basic line: in a mutuum, ownership of the money passes to the borrower, who owes back an equivalent; in partnership, the capital remains at the owner's risk, and profit-sharing can be licit. Lugo's mature doctrine does not abolish that distinction. Rather, it asks how far the logic of risk-bearing can extend into more complex transactions. When does a creditor's claim remain a secure right to repayment? When does it become an exposed, uncertain, loss-bearing claim? And when does the language of risk conceal what is still, in substance, a protected loan?

Lugo's willingness to consider risk as a separate ground for surplus also brings the discussion close to insurance-like reasoning. If a person bears a danger that another does not bear, and if that danger can be valued, then compensation for assuming the danger may be just. The logic is visible in many forms of risk transfer and risk-bearing contracts. The moral issue is not whether danger has value. It does. The issue is whether the person receiving compensation is the one actually bearing the danger.

This point is crucial. Insurance-like reasoning can be legitimate when a party truly assumes another's risk. But it becomes morally deceptive when risk is merely priced without being assumed. A lender may charge a borrower because the borrower is risky. But if the lender has structured the contract so that the borrower remains fully liable and the lender can enforce repayment, then the lender has not necessarily assumed the borrower's risk. He has priced the borrower's weakness. Lugo's synthesis can help expose that distinction, but only if its own logic is applied rigorously.

The relation between risk and lost gain is especially important. A capital provider might forgo a concrete gain by placing capital into a risky transaction, or might face the loss of the principal itself; these are not identical burdens. Lost gain concerns what might otherwise have been earned. Risk of principal concerns what might be lost. Actual loss concerns what has already been suffered. Lugo's broad theory of detriment helps relate them, but it also makes it necessary to keep them distinct. Otherwise, the same economic situation can be used to justify multiple overlapping charges.

For example, a lender might say: I lost the chance to earn elsewhere; I bore risk of nonpayment; I was deprived of my money; I incurred administrative expense; and if payment is late, I deserve a penalty. Each claim may have a place in the scholastic taxonomy. Together they can become a machinery of extraction. Lugo's synthesis therefore requires careful accounting. Which burden is actually present? Who bears it? Has it already been compensated? Is the surplus proportionate? Is the title specific enough to be tested? Without such discipline, the broad theory of detriment becomes too elastic.

This elasticity is the great strength and great danger of Lugo's approach. It allows moral theology to recognize the complexity of real finance. Not all surplus is usury: some restores loss, some compensates for sacrifice, some reflects genuine exposure to danger, and some supports the administration of institutions that lend or invest for good purposes. Lugo helps us see this without collapsing everything into a crude prohibition of all payments above principal.

Yet the same elasticity can also make the doctrine vulnerable to rationalization. If every inconvenience to capital becomes a detriment, then every surplus can be justified. If every risk can be priced, every missed opportunity monetized, every delay penalized, and every cost reimbursed without restraint, the borrower becomes surrounded by titles. The usury prohibition may remain formally intact, while practically emptied of force. This is precisely why later rigorists would look back on late scholastic permissiveness with suspicion.

Lugo's importance for modern non-extractive finance lies in this double lesson. On the one hand, he gives us tools: to distinguish the different kinds of burdens (actual loss, foregone gain, risk to principal), to avoid the simplistic view that any surplus is automatically unjust, and to ask whether a return is connected to a real burden borne by capital. On the other hand, Lugo warns us, perhaps unintentionally, that moral categories can be stretched until they protect the very thing they were meant to judge. A non-extractive finance framework cannot simply list titles and assign charges to each. It must test the whole structure. Does the capital provider actually bear downside? Is the borrower protected from ruin? Are penalties designed to restore or to generate revenue? Are damages actual? Is foregone profit concrete rather than abstract? Is risk compensated once, or counted several times? Does the structure create fellowship, or does it preserve hierarchy under the language of partnership?

Lugo's synthesis also makes clear why periculum sortis belongs near the center of any modern moral analysis of capital. Modern finance is built around the language of risk. Investors demand returns for risk. Lenders price default probability. Venture capitalists justify upside by pointing to portfolio losses. Private equity claims expertise in allocating and managing risk. Banks charge according to creditworthiness. Insurance markets sell risk transfer. The entire system speaks the language of danger, uncertainty, and expected return.

But Lugo's deeper question remains: is the claimant actually bearing the risk? A financier who receives upside while shifting downside to workers, founders, suppliers, taxpayers, or communities cannot simply invoke periculum sortis. A senior creditor protected by collateral cannot claim the same moral position as a subordinated, loss-absorbing partner. A venture investor with liquidation preferences, control rights, and exit pressure may formally hold equity while still shifting unacceptable burdens onto the company and its mission. The moral analysis must trace the path of loss.

This is where Lugo's synthesis can be recovered in a disciplined form. Risk may justify surplus only when it is real, specific, and borne by the person receiving the surplus. Lost gain may justify compensation only when it is concrete, causally connected, and not merely a generalized claim that money could have earned somewhere. Actual loss may justify reimbursement only when documented and not double-counted. The titles must remain instruments of moral truth, not devices for financial creativity.

Lugo therefore stands as one of the most important figures in the history of the extrinsic titles because he helps us see their unity: they are not random loopholes but attempts to answer one question: is the surplus payment tied to a real external burden, or does it arise from the mere fact of lending? His answer is expansive, but not lawless. It gives moral theology room to recognize real economic burdens while still requiring that the surplus be justified by something other than the loan itself.

The challenge is that the broader the theory becomes, the more it depends on conscience, proof, and institutional discipline. A narrow doctrine can be harsh but clear; a broad doctrine can be humane but easily abused. Lugo's synthesis belongs to the second category. It is capable of great moral nuance. It is also capable of being weaponized by those who know how to name every advantage as compensation.

For this reason, Lugo should not be read either as the destroyer of usury doctrine or as a simple defender of modern interest. He is better understood as the theologian of financial complexity. He shows that capital can suffer real burdens, and that justice may require compensation for them. But he also leaves us with the demanding task of discerning whether the burden is real or fictional, borne or shifted, proportionate or inflated, restorative or extractive.

In the end, Lugo's mature synthesis brings periculum sortis into relation with the whole moral architecture of finance. Risk, loss, and foregone gain are not morally irrelevant. But neither are they self-justifying. The question is not whether the language of risk appears; the question is whether capital truly stands where risk falls, and that question must be put to the structure of the transaction and to the dignity of the persons involved.

sections.title: 29.4 Lugo's Unifying Move
paragraphs: Lugo's most important contribution is not simply that he permits periculum sortis in certain cases. It is that he helps gather risk, lost profit, and actual loss into a broader moral theory of detriment, in which the different extrinsic titles are not isolated loopholes floating separately beside the usury prohibition, but related claims. Each says, in its own way, that the payment above principal is not being taken from the loan itself, but from some real burden that stands outside the bare mutuum.

This is Lugo's unifying move. Damnum emergens concerns loss already incurred. Lucrum cessans concerns a concrete gain that has been prevented. Periculum sortis concerns exposure to the loss of principal. These are not identical, and they must not be confused. But they all belong to the same moral family, because each one tries to name a disadvantage suffered by the lender or capital provider. The surplus is defended as compensation for detriment, not as the natural fruit of money.

This is why Lugo can treat risk as a "loss of a sort." Risk is not yet actual loss. The capital has not necessarily disappeared, and the lender may in fact be repaid. But if the principal is genuinely exposed to loss, the lender has accepted a burden that differs from the position of someone holding a secure claim. The danger itself has moral and economic significance. A capital provider who may lose the sors does not stand in the same position as a creditor whose repayment is guaranteed. In that sense, risk can be understood as a kind of detriment, even before it becomes realized damage.

This move is powerful because it gives coherence to the doctrine. Without it, the extrinsic titles can look like a list of exceptions: late penalty, actual loss, lost profit, risk of capital, labor, expenses, lack of money, and so on. Lugo helps reveal the deeper principle beneath the list. A surplus payment may be just when it restores, compensates, or corresponds to a real burden external to the loan. It is unjust when it arises from the loan as such, or when the title invoked is fictional, exaggerated, duplicated, or used as a pretext.

But the same move is also dangerous, because a broad theory of detriment can become too broad. If actual loss, foregone gain, and risk are all gathered under one umbrella, then the umbrella can expand until almost every inconvenience to capital counts as compensable. The lender can say: I was without my money; I faced uncertainty; I lost alternative opportunities; I incurred administrative burdens; I suffered delay; I accepted risk; therefore every surplus is justified.

At that point, the language of detriment no longer disciplines finance. It baptizes it. Lugo's unifying move must therefore be governed by strict moral tests. First, the detriment must be real. A merely imagined loss, vague opportunity, or generic risk is not enough. Second, it must be borne by the person claiming compensation. One cannot charge for a risk that has been shifted to the borrower, the guarantor, the worker, the supplier, the public, or the community. Third, it must be causally connected to the transaction. The loan must actually cause the loss, foregone gain, or exposure claimed. Fourth, the compensation must be proportionate. A small burden cannot justify unlimited surplus. Fifth, the burden must not be double-counted. The same risk cannot justify a premium, a penalty, a fee, a guarantee, and a liquidation preference all at once.

These tests preserve the older logic of commutative justice. The purpose of the extrinsic title is not to create a right to profit, but to restore equality where the lender or capital provider has suffered some external disadvantage. This is clearest in damnum emergens, where actual loss breaks equality and compensation repairs it. Lugo's synthesis extends that restorative logic to other kinds of detriment: the concrete loss of a gain that would otherwise have been made, and the danger of losing principal where capital is genuinely exposed.

The difficulty is that the further the title moves from actual loss, the more fragile the moral claim becomes. Actual loss can often be documented: a barn burned, a fee was paid, a legal cost was incurred, a collection expense arose, a lender had to borrow elsewhere. Foregone gain is harder. It depends on intention, probability, and causality. Risk is harder still, because it concerns something that might happen but has not yet happened. The more abstract the detriment, the greater the need for discipline.

This is why periculum sortis sits at the outer edge of the compensation logic. It may be legitimate, but it is never morally automatic. Risk must be concrete enough to test. Can principal actually be lost? Is repayment contingent? Is the claim unsecured or subordinated? Is the capital provider exposed to the fortunes of the venture? Or does the contract preserve repayment through collateral, guarantees, seniority, covenants, penalties, and recourse? The mere word "risk" cannot settle the matter. The structure must be examined.

Lugo's treatment of risk as a "loss of a sort" also helps explain why later theologians could place periculum sortis under a broad understanding of damnum emergens. Strictly speaking, risk is not actual loss; it is ordered toward possible loss, the exposure to loss. If a person can be compensated for actual damage, perhaps he can also be compensated for accepting a real danger of damage. This reasoning preserves the compensation framework while allowing the doctrine to handle more complex financial realities.

Yet the price of this expansion is increased vulnerability to abuse. Once risk is treated as a kind of loss, one must be careful not to treat every possibility of loss as morally equivalent to loss itself. Otherwise, the title becomes speculative. A lender could demand certain compensation for uncertain harm. Worse, the lender could receive compensation for risk and then avoid the risk through security. The borrower would pay for danger that the lender no longer meaningfully bears.

Here is one of the most important lessons Lugo offers modern finance. Contemporary financial structures often excel at separating risk language from risk substance. Investors demand returns for risk while negotiating protections against loss. Creditors charge risk premiums while taking collateral. Venture capitalists speak of shared upside and downside while using liquidation preferences and control rights to protect capital before founders, workers, or mission. Private equity funds claim entrepreneurial risk while financing acquisitions with debt placed on the company itself. The vocabulary of risk can remain even after risk has been shifted elsewhere.

A Lugo-inspired analysis would not be impressed by vocabulary. It would ask where the downside actually falls. If capital receives compensation for risk, capital must bear the risk. If the borrower bears the risk, then capital's claim to compensation is weakened. If the risk is insured, collateralized, guaranteed, senior, or shifted, then the surplus must be reduced or reclassified. If multiple contractual devices compensate the same risk, the structure becomes suspect. The title cannot be allowed to float free from the actual allocation of loss.

This is also where Lugo's synthesis becomes useful for non-extractive finance. A non-extractive capital structure should be able to name the title under which any surplus is received. Is it reimbursement for actual loss or expense? Is it compensation for concrete foregone gain? Is it payment for genuine risk-bearing? Is it labor or administrative cost? Or is it simply return demanded because capital has bargaining power? Lugo's unifying move gives us a diagnostic framework, but only if we refuse to let the categories become vague.

The broad theory of detriment also clarifies why not all surplus is morally equal. A modest management fee that covers real stewardship work is different from a hidden yield. A late penalty designed to repair actual injury is different from a penalty engineered as revenue. A return on subordinated, loss-absorbing capital is different from interest on a guaranteed claim. A documented liquidity loss is different from an abstract assertion that money always deserves growth. The question is always whether the surplus corresponds to a real burden, or whether the burden has been invented to justify the surplus.

Lugo therefore helps move the tradition from a rule-based analysis toward a more structural moral inquiry. The old rule remains: gain from the mutuum itself is usury. But the application of that rule requires attention to the whole transaction. What is the legal form? What is the economic substance? Who owns the capital? Who bears risk? Who controls the outcome? Who receives surplus? Who is protected if the venture fails? Who is left exposed? These are the questions that determine whether an extrinsic title is real or fictive.

This structural inquiry is especially important because financial contracts can be morally mixed. A transaction may contain some genuine risk-bearing and some extraction, compensate real administrative costs while imposing excessive penalties, share some downside while preserving too much priority for capital, or use a legitimate title but overstate its amount. Lugo's synthesis allows for this complexity. It does not force every transaction into a simple category of licit or illicit at first glance. But it does require careful moral accounting.

The danger, of course, is that careful accounting can become clever accounting. The very sophistication that allows justice to be done can also allow injustice to be disguised. This is why a renewed moral economy cannot rely on titles alone. It needs institutions, documentation, transparency, audit, borrower protections, caps, mission locks, and governance structures that prevent the titles from being manipulated. The scholastic tradition understood this in its own way through confession, canon law, pastoral scrutiny, proportionality, and suspicion of fraud. Modern non-extractive finance must develop analogous safeguards.

Lugo's unifying move is therefore best understood as both culmination and warning. It culminates the late-scholastic effort to make sense of surplus payments in a complex economy, shows that the extrinsic titles are not arbitrary loopholes but moral attempts to distinguish compensation from extraction, and warns that the more expansive the doctrine becomes, the more easily it can be captured by financial ingenuity.

The task, then, is not to reject Lugo's synthesis, but to discipline it. Risk can be a loss of a sort, but not every risk is compensable. Lost gain can be a detriment, but not every imagined opportunity is real. Actual loss can be restored, but not inflated. Expense can be reimbursed, but not transformed into profit extraction. Labor can be paid, but not used as cover for domination. Each title must be named, tested, documented, limited, and integrated into the whole moral structure of the contract.

In the end, Lugo helps us see the extrinsic titles as a single moral grammar. They ask whether surplus is attached to a real external burden, whether the claimant actually bears it, whether the payment restores equality or exploits need, and whether finance shares vulnerability or monetizes it. This is why Lugo remains so important for a modern recovery of moral economy. His synthesis teaches us that capital may sometimes receive more than principal without committing usury, and that every such claim must pass through the fire of moral scrutiny.

For if risk is treated as a loss of a sort, then the decisive question becomes unavoidable: who truly stands to lose? Where the answer is "capital," periculum sortis may have moral force. Where the answer is "the borrower, the worker, the community, or the mission," the title has been turned inside out.

chapters.part: Part VIII — Periculum Sortis: Risk of Capital and the Moral Structure of Downside

chapters.title: Backlash and Moral Danger
chapters.label: Chapter 30

sections.title: 30.1 The Risk-Title as a Dangerous Wedge
paragraphs: The title periculum sortis was necessary because risk is real. Capital can be lost, a claim can be uncertain, and a lender or investor may genuinely expose principal to danger. A moral theology that could not recognize this would go blind at exactly the wrong spot: it could no longer tell guaranteed debt from loss-bearing participation, and it would treat every payment above principal as though it had the same moral meaning, which would be too crude. Some surplus payments restore a loss. Some compensate for labor or expense. Some correspond to genuine risk-bearing. A tradition that wants to distinguish extraction from compensation must have room for such differences.

But periculum sortis was also dangerous, because risk is everywhere. No repayment is absolutely certain. No borrower is perfectly safe. No contract is immune from fraud, insolvency, war, legal change, illness, crop failure, shipwreck, currency instability, or simple human failure. Once risk is admitted as a title, the temptation is obvious: every lender can claim it, every creditor can say his principal is in danger, and every payment above principal can be described not as interest but as compensation for uncertainty. The usury prohibition can then remain formally intact while being practically hollowed out.

This is the risk-title as a dangerous wedge. It begins with a true distinction and can end by dissolving the rule it was meant to clarify. The true distinction runs between a secure claim and a risky claim, between guaranteed repayment and capital exposed to loss, between lending and participation. Widen it too far, and it becomes a general permission for interest. The title that was supposed to identify genuine risk-bearing becomes a vocabulary for ordinary credit pricing.

The move from special risk to general default risk is where the danger lives. Special risk means the capital provider is exposed in a distinctive way: repayment is contingent, the principal may be lost, the claim is subordinated, the capital remains tied to a venture's outcome, or the provider genuinely shares downside with the borrower. In such cases the surplus may have a plausible external basis: the capital provider is not merely selling the use of money but bearing a burden that a secure lender does not.

General default risk is different. It is the ordinary possibility that a borrower may not repay, and it exists in nearly every loan. If this alone justifies surplus, the usury prohibition becomes nearly meaningless. The lender need not share the venture's fate, give up legal recourse, subordinate his claim, or accept loss as part of the transaction's structure. He need only point out that repayment might fail. Risk then becomes not a mark of participation, but an excuse for charging the borrower more.

This is why rigorists objected to broad uses of periculum sortis. Their concern was not that risk never matters, but that it could be invoked so broadly that no usurious loan would remain identifiable. If a risky claim to one hundred is worth less than a certain claim to one hundred, and if every claim is at least somewhat risky, then every lender can demand compensation above principal. The logic seems modest at first. Its consequences are sweeping. What begins as compensation for a special burden becomes a universal justification for gain.

The danger sharpens when risk is attached to borrower weakness. In modern financial language, a risky borrower pays more. In Christian moral theology, this is precisely the point at which suspicion should intensify. The poor, the desperate, the marginal, and the unstable are often riskier borrowers because they have fewer reserves, fewer assets, and fewer alternatives. Charging them more because they are risky may appear rational. It can also mean the lender profits from the very vulnerability that Scripture commands us to protect.

This is the moral inversion at the heart of abusive risk pricing. The borrower's fragility becomes the lender's title. The more precarious the debtor, the stronger the creditor's claim to surplus. The person most in need of mercy becomes the person most burdened by the price of money. Risk language then turns the biblical command inside out: instead of lending to the vulnerable without extraction, the lender extracts more because the borrower is vulnerable.

None of this means all risk-based compensation is unjust; it means risk must be analyzed according to who bears it. If the capital provider truly accepts loss, subordination, contingency, or non-recourse exposure, then the risk is borne by capital. If the borrower remains liable regardless of failure, and if the lender holds collateral, guarantees, penalties, security, or seniority, then much of the risk may in fact be borne by the borrower. In that case, charging the borrower for the lender's supposed risk may be morally incoherent. The borrower pays for a burden that the borrower himself may ultimately carry.

The wedge does its worst when risk is both priced and shifted. A lender may charge a premium because the borrower might default, while also taking collateral that protects the principal. He may add penalties for delay, require personal guarantees, demand priority over other creditors, impose covenants, and retain legal recourse against the borrower's assets. Each protection reduces the lender's actual exposure. The risk premium somehow survives them all. The result is not compensation for risk-bearing, but surplus layered on top of risk avoidance.

Here is one of this history's most important lessons for modern finance: the language of risk often survives long after the contractual structure has transferred much of the downside elsewhere. Investors speak of risk while negotiating liquidation preferences. Creditors speak of risk while taking security. Venture capital speaks of failure while demanding control rights and exit pressure. Private equity speaks of entrepreneurial risk while placing debt on the acquired company. Banks speak of credit risk while requiring collateral and guarantees. In each case, the moral question is not whether the word "risk" appears. The question is where loss actually falls.

The scholastic tradition's suspicion of periculum sortis therefore remains deeply relevant. It stops us from accepting the modern equation of risk and return too quickly. Modern finance often says: higher risk deserves higher return. The older moral tradition asks: higher risk for whom? If capital bears the risk, the claim may be one thing. If the borrower, the worker, the community, or the mission bears the risk, the claim is another. A return cannot be morally justified by risk unless the one receiving the return actually carries the danger.

This is why the title had to be kept narrow, or at least disciplined. To be morally meaningful, periculum sortis must name genuine exposure to loss. Principal must be capable of being lost in substance, not merely delayed in collection. The lender must lack full protection. The claim must not be fully guaranteed. The surplus must be proportionate to the risk actually borne. The risk must not already be compensated through other titles. Above all, the structure must not convert the borrower's vulnerability into the lender's revenue.

Rigorists feared that these disciplines would not hold. They had seen how easily financial actors could redescribe ordinary interest as compensation for danger, and how, once uncertainty enters the analysis, clever distinctions multiply without end. A payment can be called risk compensation, opportunity cost, lack of money, administrative expense, penalty, insurance, discount, exchange, or damages. The forms change. The borrower still pays. The creditor still receives surplus. The prohibition remains in theory while the practice evolves around it.

Their fear was not irrational. It was a recognition of sin in contractual form. Human beings are capable of turning moral distinctions into instruments of evasion, and a doctrine created to protect justice can be captured by anyone with enough sophistication to manipulate it. This is why the extrinsic titles must never be treated as loopholes. They are tests. Each title asks whether the surplus is tied to a real external burden. And each title can become a mask if the burden is invented, exaggerated, duplicated, or shifted.

Periculum sortis is especially susceptible to this abuse because risk is intangible until loss occurs. An actual loss can be documented, a penalty compared to the delay it answers, a foregone profit tested against a concrete alternative. But risk concerns possibility. It lives in the future. It can be estimated, imagined, dramatized, or strategically overstated, which makes it easy to claim and difficult to police. The more abstract the risk, the more dangerous the title.

The movement from special risk to general default risk is therefore not merely a technical development. It is a moral drift. At first, the title protects genuine risk-bearing. Then it recognizes uncertain claims. Then it prices ordinary debtor unreliability. Then it becomes an ordinary feature of lending. At the end of the process, the difference between usury and interest has been relocated from moral substance to legal vocabulary. What was once prohibited as gain from the loan itself is now permitted as compensation for the universal fact that the future is uncertain.

The tradition's backlash against this movement should not be dismissed as nostalgia or economic ignorance. It was an attempt to defend the central intuition of the usury doctrine: money lent to another must not become a tool for guaranteed growth at the other's expense. The lender may be restored for real loss, compensated for genuine burdens, and allowed to share profit where he shares risk. But he may not use the borrower's need, delay, uncertainty, or weakness as a standing claim to surplus.

This is why the risk-title must always be examined with the question of domination in view. Does the contract make the lender vulnerable alongside the borrower, or does it protect the lender while intensifying the borrower's vulnerability? Does it create solidarity in uncertainty, or a hierarchy in which one party receives upside and the other absorbs failure? Does it restore equality, or does it monetize asymmetry?

In a non-extractive framework, periculum sortis can still have a legitimate role. Loss-absorbing capital, subordinated investment, genuine partnership, non-recourse financing, revenue-sharing with downside protection for the enterprise, and mission-aligned risk participation may all involve real capital exposure. In such cases surplus can be morally different from interest on a guaranteed loan, but the title must be tied to actual loss-sharing, not merely to the rhetoric of risk.

The danger of periculum sortis is therefore also its gift. It forces moral finance to become honest about downside: it refuses to let capital claim the dignity of risk-bearing while contractually avoiding the pain of loss, it asks whether the return follows the risk or the risk has been displaced onto someone else, and it exposes the difference between capital that participates and capital that extracts.

For this reason, the backlash against broad risk-title reasoning is not an embarrassment to the tradition. It is part of the tradition's wisdom. It reminds us that every legitimate title can become a pretext, insists that the more powerful the justification, the more rigorous the scrutiny must be, and warns that once risk becomes a universal wedge, the old sin of usury may return wearing the respectable clothing of prudence, valuation, and market realism.

sections.title: 30.2 Daniele Concina
paragraphs: Daniele Concina (1687–1756), a Dominican with twelve volumes of moral theology to his name, enters the story as a voice of backlash. By the eighteenth century, the scholastic doctrine of the extrinsic titles had become both rich and unstable. The tradition had developed tools for distinguishing genuine compensation from usury: late penalties, actual loss, lost profit, risk of principal, labor, expense, and other adjacent titles. These tools allowed theologians to handle real commercial complexity. They also created opportunities for evasion. A lender could now invoke a title, or several titles at once, to justify payments that earlier moralists would have regarded with suspicion.

Concina's importance lies in his resistance to this permissive drift. He saw that the usury doctrine could be emptied from within: never formally denied, simply surrounded by exceptions until almost nothing remained. The language of moral theology could continue in use, the practical result ordinary interest under the names of compensation, risk, loss, delay, or lack of money. Concina's rigorism was therefore not merely a conservative temperament. It was an attempt to defend the substance of a doctrine that he believed had been hollowed out by increasingly elastic distinctions.

The risk-title was one of his major targets because it was among the most elastic of all. Periculum sortis could begin with a real case: a capital provider truly exposes principal to loss and receives compensation for that danger. It could then expand into a general claim: every lender faces some danger of nonpayment, therefore every lender may receive surplus. Concina recognized where this expansion led. If ordinary default risk justifies additional payment, then the distinction between usury and legitimate compensation becomes almost impossible to maintain.

His objection was not that loss is irrelevant, nor that all commercial sophistication is immoral. The stricter anti-usury tradition did not need to deny every form of finance. It could admit partnership, reimbursement, damages, expense, labor, and other real burdens. What Concina resisted was the conversion of possibility into entitlement. The fact that something might go wrong does not automatically give capital a right to guaranteed growth. A mere possibility of nonpayment cannot be allowed to become a standing title for surplus in every loan.

This is why Concina's critique strikes at the conceptual heart of the late scholastic expansion. Molina, Lessius, and Lugo had treated risk, uncertain claims, and detriment with great subtlety. They had recognized that a doubtful claim is not worth the same as a secure one, and allowed that risk of principal might function as a compensable burden. Lugo in particular had gathered risk, lost gain, and actual loss into a broader theory of detriment. Concina worried that this synthesis had become too broad. Once risk itself becomes a kind of loss, the lender can claim compensation before any loss has occurred. Once uncertain future harm is monetized, nearly every creditor can present himself as injured.

The rigorist objection is therefore deeply moral. It asks whether the doctrine has lost its preferential concern for the vulnerable. The biblical and patristic suspicion of usury began from a scene of need: the poor borrower, the pledged cloak, the brother in distress, the neighbor who must be allowed to live. But risk pricing can reverse the biblical instinct. The more fragile the borrower, the riskier the loan. The riskier the loan, the higher the charge. The higher the charge, the more fragile the borrower becomes. What begins as compensation for risk can end as a mechanism for intensifying the very vulnerability that Christian moral theology was meant to protect.

Concina's backlash must be understood against this background. He feared that the theologians had become too clever, and that the distinctions developed to protect justice were being used to defeat it. A penalty could become planned revenue. Actual loss could become fictional damage. Lost profit could become abstract opportunity cost. Risk of principal could become ordinary credit pricing. Lack of money could become the time value of money. Each step might appear modest. Together, they could transform the old prohibition into a collection of verbal safeguards with little practical force.

In this sense, Concina represents the conscience of the doctrine. He reminds the tradition that moral theology is not only a science of distinctions. It is also a discipline of suspicion toward sin. Human beings can manipulate categories; contracts can hide domination; economic necessity can be redescribed as consent; vulnerability can be made to look like risk; extraction can be renamed compensation. A rigorous doctrine is sometimes necessary not because commerce is evil, but because financial intelligence is capable of moral self-deception.

His critique of the risk-title matters all the more because periculum sortis is attractive to modern minds. It seems obvious to us that risk deserves return; we think naturally in risk premiums, credit spreads, probability of default, expected value, and portfolio loss. Concina interrupts this modern instinct. He asks whether the risk is truly borne by the person receiving the return, whether the return is proportionate, whether the borrower's need has become the lender's opportunity, and whether the old sin has simply learned a new vocabulary.

Concina's resistance also helps clarify the difference between risk-bearing and risk-pricing. Risk-bearing means that the capital provider actually stands to lose. The principal may not return; the claim may be subordinated; repayment may be contingent; the capital provider may share the venture's fate. Risk-pricing, by contrast, can occur even when the lender has shifted or limited much of the downside. A creditor may price the borrower's risk while retaining collateral, guarantees, seniority, penalties, and legal recourse. In such a case, the borrower pays for risk, but capital may not fully bear it.

This is the distinction Concina helps us recover, even if his own formulations are stricter than later commercial practice would accept. His rigorism protects a vital question: does the title correspond to reality? If capital claims compensation for danger, is capital actually in danger? If not, the title is fraudulent in moral substance, however defensible in form. The issue is not the name of the contract, but the allocation of loss.

Concina also anticipates a distinctly modern problem: the cumulative layering of protections and premiums. A lender may demand a risk premium because repayment is uncertain. But the same lender may also demand collateral, a personal guarantee, a late penalty, default interest, administrative fees, monitoring fees, legal cost reimbursement, covenants, and priority over other claims. Each provision may be justified separately. Together, they may ensure that the lender is protected while the borrower bears nearly all the downside. Concina's suspicion toward permissive titles helps us see how this accumulation can become extraction.

None of this means that Concina's stricter position resolves every difficulty. A doctrine that is too narrow can fail to recognize genuine burdens. Capital can be truly at risk, commercial finance can require legitimate compensation, and institutions serving the poor incur real costs. A rigid refusal to acknowledge risk might discourage useful lending, partnership, or investment. The challenge is therefore not to choose simply between Concina and Lugo, between rigor and expansion, but to hold their insights together.

Lugo teaches that risk, lost gain, and actual loss may sometimes be real detriments; Concina warns that the language of detriment can become infinitely elastic. Lugo gives the tradition flexibility; Concina demands discipline. Lugo helps moral theology enter the complexity of finance; Concina prevents finance from capturing moral theology. A renewed moral economy needs both: the capacity to recognize legitimate compensation, and the courage to reject clever extraction.

Concina's backlash also prepares the way for Vix pervenit. The Church would have to reaffirm that gain from the loan itself remains usury, even while admitting that extrinsic titles may exist. This balance reflects the unresolved tension that Concina exposed. The doctrine cannot deny every payment above principal, and it cannot permit the extrinsic titles to become pretexts. The line between title and pretext is the line on which the whole tradition depends.

For modern non-extractive finance, Concina is a necessary irritant. He refuses to let us become too comfortable with our own sophistication. He asks whether our models, dashboards, contracts, and classifications are naming real burdens or rationalizing desired returns; whether risk has been measured honestly or inflated strategically; whether losses are truly borne by capital or shifted out of sight onto founders, workers, customers, suppliers, communities, or the public; and whether the borrower's vulnerability is being protected or monetized.

This is why Concina should not be dismissed as merely anti-modern. He saw a danger that modern finance confirms daily. Once risk becomes a universal justification, capital can demand return while insulating itself from loss. Once opportunity cost becomes universal, every use of money demands compensation. Once time itself becomes yield, every delay becomes revenue. And once every inconvenience to capital becomes a title, the borrower stands surrounded by claims.

Concina's rigorism recalls the original moral nerve of the usury doctrine. Lending must not become domination, surplus must not be extracted from need, and the language of justice must not be used to sanctify exploitation. Capital may be compensated for real burdens, but it must not manufacture burdens to secure growth. Above all, the vulnerable borrower must not become the place where financial theory proves its cleverness.

The risk-title remains useful only if Concina's warning is remembered. Periculum sortis can distinguish genuine loss-bearing capital from guaranteed debt. But it can also give usury its most respectable cover. The difference depends on scrutiny: real risk, an actual bearer, proportional compensation, no double-counting, no hidden guarantee, no exploitation of vulnerability.

Concina's role in the story is therefore prophetic. He stands against the drift by which exceptions become norms, titles become formulas, and compensation becomes yield. He reminds the tradition that the purpose of the extrinsic titles was never to help capital escape moral judgment, but to judge capital more precisely. When they cease to do that, rigorism returns as a necessary act of memory.

In the end, Concina forces the question that every theory of non-extractive finance must face: are our distinctions protecting the vulnerable, or protecting the surplus? If they protect the vulnerable, they belong to moral economy. If they protect the surplus, they have become part of the problem.

sections.title: 30.3 Benedict XIV and Vix Pervenit
paragraphs: The controversy over the extrinsic titles could not be left to drift indefinitely. By the eighteenth century, the tradition had reached a point of genuine tension. On one side stood the older and stricter conviction that gain from a mutuum as such is usury; on the other, centuries of increasingly sophisticated moral analysis that had identified real cases where a payment above principal might be justified: damages, delay, lost profit, labor, expense, and risk of capital. The question was not simply whether the usury prohibition still existed. The question was whether it still had teeth.

Benedict XIV's Vix pervenit enters this moment as an attempt to reaffirm the center. The document does not deny that extrinsic titles may exist, does not collapse all surplus payments into usury, and does not pretend that every financial transaction is a simple loan between two individuals. But it insists on the essential point: gain demanded from the loan itself remains usury. No matter how common the practice becomes, no matter how clever the contract, no matter how plausible the economic explanation, the bare mutuum does not generate a right to surplus.

This is the crucial balance. Vix pervenit preserves the distinction between title and pretext. A legitimate extrinsic title may justify something beyond principal. But the title must be real. It cannot be a verbal decoration placed upon what is, in substance, ordinary interest, or a convenient label for the lender's desire to receive more because he has lent money. It must name a real burden external to the loan: a loss, an expense, a danger, a penalty, or some other just ground that does not arise merely from the use of money.

This matters especially for periculum sortis, which had become at once the most powerful and the most dangerous of the extrinsic titles. It could name genuine risk-bearing: capital truly exposed to loss, repayment contingent, principal that could actually be lost, a moral structure different from a guaranteed loan. It could equally become a pretext for universal interest, since every loan contains some uncertainty and every lender can claim risk. Accepted without scrutiny, the claim would leave the old usury doctrine surviving only in name.

Vix pervenit resists that collapse by returning the analysis to the source of the gain. Where does the surplus come from? Is it demanded because of the loan itself, or because of some external and just title? The question is simple in form and demanding in practice. It requires moral theology to examine the whole structure of the transaction: whether the claimed title is causally connected to the payment, whether the amount is proportionate, whether the burden is real, and whether the lender is being restored or enriched.

The document's importance lies in refusing both extremes. It refuses the permissive extreme that would let every payment be justified by some broad appeal to custom, commerce, risk, or opportunity. It equally refuses the crude simplification in which every surplus payment is automatically condemned without examining the reason for it. Benedict XIV's position is therefore not, in any simplistic sense, a case against finance. It is a case against pretext. Moral language must correspond to moral reality.

This insistence becomes essential once financial contracts grow more complex. A transaction may contain several possible titles. There may be administrative costs. There may be real risk. There may be delayed payment, losses caused by the borrower's default, a foregone commercial opportunity. But the presence of one legitimate title does not sanctify the whole transaction, and several titles may not be stacked without limit. The same burden cannot be counted repeatedly. A title must justify only what corresponds to it.

In this sense, Vix pervenit protects the moral architecture of the extrinsic titles and keeps them from becoming loopholes. The titles remain subordinate to the usury doctrine, not replacements for it; they do not abolish the prohibition but refine its application. Their purpose is to distinguish compensation from extraction, not to hand capital a catalogue of ways to secure guaranteed growth.

This is why Benedict XIV's intervention must be read together with Concina's critique, even if the two are not identical in tone or function. Concina represents the rigorist alarm: the doctrine has become too permissive; the titles have expanded too far; usury is being hidden beneath refined distinctions. Vix pervenit gives magisterial weight to the central concern behind that alarm. Whatever legitimate complexities may exist, it says in effect, the basic principle remains in force. The loan itself does not bear fruit, and the phrase "from the loan itself" is decisive. In scholastic terms, the mutuum transfers ownership of consumable money to the borrower, who owes back the equivalent principal, the tantundem. To demand more simply because the loan has been made is to sell what is not there: a separate use of money that cannot be separated from the money itself, or a claim to profit generated by another's ownership and need. The old Thomistic structure remains visible beneath the eighteenth-century reaffirmation.

But Vix pervenit also recognizes that human affairs are not exhausted by the pure mutuum. Other grounds may exist. A lender may suffer actual damage, incur expenses, be wronged by delay, or bear risk in a way that is truly external to the bare loan. The presence of such grounds must be examined, and this is the space in which the extrinsic titles remain possible. The document does not erase late-scholastic casuistry; it reinscribes it under the primacy of the central prohibition.

For periculum sortis, this means the title must be purified by the question of actuality. Can the principal truly be lost? Is the lender genuinely exposed? Or is repayment guaranteed in law or in economic substance? Does collateral cover the claim? Is there recourse against the borrower's assets? Are there sureties, guarantees, or public backstops? Does the lender charge for risk while contractually avoiding risk? If so, the title begins to look like pretext.

The same logic applies to modern finance. A venture investor may say that capital is risky, but if liquidation preferences, control rights, redemption rights, or exit pressure protect capital at the expense of workers, founders, or mission, the risk claim must be scrutinized. A lender may say that a borrower is risky, but if the lender holds collateral, guarantees, covenants, penalties, and seniority, the claim to compensation for risk may be overstated. A fund may say that its return reflects uncertainty, but if downside is shifted to portfolio companies while upside flows to capital, the structure may reproduce precisely the domination that usury doctrine was meant to resist.

Benedict XIV's central concern therefore remains contemporary: financial language must not conceal the source of surplus. "Interest," "yield," "risk premium," "management fee," "preferred return," "liquidity premium," "default charge," "late fee," "commitment fee," "discount," "carried interest": each of these may name something legitimate in some cases. But each may also hide a claim from the loan itself, or from bargaining power, or from another's vulnerability. The moral task is to identify the source.

This is why Vix pervenit is so important for non-extractive finance. It requires every surplus claim to answer a simple but searching question: what exactly justifies this payment? If the answer is actual cost, show the cost. If the answer is loss, document the loss. If the answer is delay, prove the delay and its harm. If the answer is foregone profit, show the concrete alternative and the causal connection. If the answer is risk, show where capital can actually lose. If none of these can be shown, the surplus may be legal, customary, and financially rational, and still morally suspect.

The document also reminds us that legality and morality are not identical. Civil law may permit interest, commercial custom may normalize it, markets may require it. But Christian moral theology asks a different question: does the transaction preserve justice between persons? Does it restore equality, share risk, compensate real burdens, and protect the vulnerable? Or does it allow one party to profit from another's need while hiding behind accepted practice?

This distinction between legal permission and moral sufficiency is essential, because one of the great dangers of modern finance is that legality becomes the horizon of conscience. If the contract is enforceable, the transaction is assumed acceptable; the usury tradition refuses that reduction. A contract can be lawful and still extractive. A payment can be market-standard and still morally disordered. A risk premium can be rational and still unjust, if the risk is not truly borne by the one receiving the premium.

Vix pervenit therefore does not ask us to return simplistically to a pre-modern economy. It asks us to recover a moral question that modern economies often suppress. What is the surplus for? Is it restoration? Is it participation? Is it compensation for real risk? Or is it guaranteed growth extracted from the borrower's obligation? The answer determines whether capital serves life or feeds upon it.

The document's enduring power lies in its refusal to let exceptions swallow the rule. The extrinsic titles remain possible, but they must remain exceptional in the moral sense: tied to specific, demonstrable burdens. They cannot become a generalized entitlement, be invoked in advance as an automatic return, or transform the mutuum into a revenue-generating asset simply because time passes or uncertainty exists.

This is also why the title periculum sortis requires especially careful treatment after Vix pervenit. Risk can be real, but it can also be infinitely expandable. The mere fact that the future is uncertain does not create a right to profit. The capital provider must bear a specific danger that justifies a specific compensation. Where risk is eliminated or shifted, the title weakens. Where risk is genuine and loss-absorbing, the title may stand. Where the title cannot be tested, it should not be trusted.

In the end, Benedict XIV brings the tradition back to its moral center. The extrinsic titles are not escape routes from the usury doctrine. They are instruments for applying it justly. Their purpose is to prevent two errors: condemning legitimate compensation as usury, and blessing usury as compensation. The whole doctrine depends on maintaining that distinction.

Vix pervenit therefore remains a necessary checkpoint in the history of periculum sortis. After Alexander, Medina, Juan de la Peña, Molina, Lessius, Lugo, and Concina, Benedict XIV reasserts the governing principle: gain from the loan itself remains usury; genuine extrinsic titles may exist; the difference must be proved, not presumed. That principle is as important for modern non-extractive capital as it was for eighteenth-century confessors.

For the moral question has not changed. When capital receives more than it gave, what is the reason? If the answer is real loss, real labor, real expense, real foregone gain, or real risk borne by capital, the surplus may be examined as compensation. If the answer is merely that money was lent and time passed, then the old warning still stands. The name may have changed, but the sin has not.

sections.title: 30.4 Modern Diagnostic Checklist
paragraphs: The tradition's debate over periculum sortis leaves us with a practical task: how do we tell whether a modern financial return is genuinely tied to risk of capital, or whether the language of risk is being used to disguise extraction?

This question cannot be answered by vocabulary alone. A contract may call itself a loan, an investment, a partnership, a preferred equity instrument, a revenue-based financing agreement, a convertible note, a SAFE, a profit-share, a redeemable share, or an impact investment. None of these labels settles the matter. The moral question is deeper: who can lose, who must pay, who is protected, and who receives surplus?
A modern diagnostic checklist for periculum sortis must therefore begin with the most basic question: can principal actually be lost?

If the answer is no, the title is weak. If the capital provider is guaranteed repayment, fully collateralized, insured, or protected by enforceable claims against the borrower's general assets, the risk to principal may be more apparent than real. There may still be delay, inconvenience, administrative cost, or collection uncertainty, but those are not the same as genuine loss-bearing capital. A protected creditor cannot claim the moral position of a partner simply because the future is uncertain.

If the answer is yes, the analysis continues. Principal can be lost in many ways. The investment may be subordinated to other claims; repayment may be contingent on the success of the enterprise; the capital may be non-recourse; the investor may have no collateral, no guarantee, no priority, and no claim on unrelated assets. The capital provider may accept that if the venture fails, the money is gone. In such cases periculum sortis may have real force: capital is not merely lending at a price but standing somewhere within the field of loss.

The second question is: who legally and economically bears the loss? Legal form matters, but economic substance matters more. A contract may legally place risk on capital while economic pressure shifts the burden elsewhere. Conversely, a contract may legally look debt-like while containing genuine hardship protections or loss-sharing features. The moral analysis must examine both formal rights and practical consequences. If the company fails, what happens? Who absorbs the unpaid amount? Who loses wages, mission, housing, ownership, livelihood, reputation, or control? Who can walk away?

This question matters because risk can be displaced. A lender may appear to bear default risk, but if the borrower has pledged personal assets, signed a guarantee, granted security, or accepted aggressive enforcement rights, the practical burden of failure may rest largely on the borrower. The lender's risk is then reduced, while the borrower's vulnerability deepens. A return justified by periculum sortis becomes suspect when the actual downside has been shifted away from the person receiving the return.

The third question is: is repayment guaranteed? A guarantee is one of the clearest signs that the risk-title is weak, because a guaranteed claim is not the same as loss-bearing capital. The guarantee may come from the borrower, a parent company, a founder, a public institution, a donor pool, a surety, collateral, or a contractual right to recover regardless of the project's outcome. The stronger the guarantee, the less plausible the claim to compensation for risk of principal.

This does not mean guaranteed claims can never involve any burden. There may be administrative costs, delay costs, or actual damages if payment is late. Those belong to other titles, and they do not automatically create periculum sortis. If principal is effectively protected, the surplus cannot honestly be justified as compensation for capital exposed to loss.

The fourth question is: is the claim collateralized? Collateral changes the moral structure: if the lender can seize pledged assets, the lender's risk falls and the borrower's rises. The pledged asset may be a house, inventory, receivables, intellectual property, equipment, shares, or personal savings. In each case, the lender's exposure is partly transferred onto the borrower's property. The more complete the collateral coverage, the weaker the lender's claim to a risk premium.

But collateral must be examined carefully: some covers only a fraction of the claim, some is difficult to liquidate, some is worth less in distress, and some protects against ordinary nonpayment but not catastrophic loss. The question is not simply whether collateral exists, but how much risk it removes, and from whom. If collateral already compensates or protects the lender, adding a substantial risk premium may double-count the same danger.

The fifth question is: is the capital senior? Seniority determines where loss falls in a capital stack. Senior capital is paid first, subordinated capital later, equity usually last. A senior claim may still face some risk, but it is protected relative to junior claims, workers, suppliers, founders, communities, or mission reserves. A return justified by periculum sortis must be assessed in light of this priority.

This is especially important in venture capital, private equity, and structured finance, where capital can claim to be risky while negotiating rights that place it ahead of others in liquidation. Preferred equity may legally be equity and economically behave like protected debt, if it carries liquidation preferences, redemption rights, cumulative dividends, veto rights, or forced exit mechanisms. The moral question is not whether the instrument is called equity, but whether capital is truly exposed to loss alongside the enterprise or stands first in line when value is distributed.

The sixth question is: does the borrower still owe if the project fails? This is the difference between participation and domination. If the project fails and the borrower still owes the full amount, the capital provider has not fully shared the project's risk. The borrower bears failure twice: first through the failed project, and then through the continuing debt. The lender may suffer delay or collection risk, but the legal burden remains on the borrower.

By contrast, if repayment is contingent on success, if losses are shared, or if the obligation is reduced or extinguished when the underlying project fails, the structure is closer to risk-bearing participation. Non-recourse financing, true revenue-sharing with hardship protections, subordinated recoverable grants, patient capital with loss absorption, and genuine equity may all be more compatible with periculum sortis than ordinary guaranteed debt. But even here, the details matter. A revenue-share that drains the company in bad years may still be extractive. Equity with aggressive control rights may still distort mission. Contingency alone is not enough; the structure must serve life.

The seventh question is: is risk priced proportionately? Even where risk is real, not every return is justified: a small risk cannot justify unlimited upside, and a limited exposure cannot justify a claim that captures the whole future of the enterprise. The surplus must correspond to the burden. This is a basic requirement of commutative justice: compensation that exceeds the risk becomes enrichment.

Proportionality is difficult because risk is uncertain, but difficulty does not eliminate the moral requirement. The parties should ask how the return relates to the amount of capital genuinely at risk, the probability of loss, the severity of possible loss, the presence of other protections, and the social purpose of the transaction. A non-extractive structure should err on the side of protecting the enterprise, workers, mission, and vulnerable stakeholders, rather than maximizing capital's upside.

The eighth question is: are upside and downside shared? The moral force of periculum sortis is strongest where upside and downside travel together. If capital shares in gain because it also shares in loss, the surplus resembles participation. If capital receives upside while avoiding downside, the surplus resembles extraction. If the borrower bears downside while capital receives upside, the title has been inverted.

This is why partnership remains the classic reference point: in true partnership gain and loss are connected, and the partner does not merely charge the other party for the use of capital but participates in a common venture. Modern structures need not replicate the medieval societas exactly, but they should be judged by the same moral question: does the capital provider stand with the enterprise in both success and failure?

The ninth question is: can the lender enforce repayment against unrelated borrower assets? Full recourse is morally significant: if the lender can pursue assets unrelated to the financed project, the claim extends beyond the venture. The borrower's household, future earnings, other business lines, personal property, or community resources may be exposed. This weakens the claim that capital is sharing project risk. The borrower may instead be guaranteeing capital's position with broader life assets.

A non-recourse or limited-recourse structure is morally different. It confines the lender's claim to the financed project or an agreed revenue stream, prevents failure from becoming total personal ruin, and aligns the capital provider more closely with the actual venture. This does not automatically make the structure just, but it strengthens the case that capital is bearing real risk.

The tenth question is: is risk double-counted through fees, penalties, and premiums? Double-counting is one of the most common paths from compensation to extraction. A lender may charge a risk premium, demand collateral, add origination fees, require monitoring fees, impose default interest, collect late penalties, pass on legal costs, and retain seniority. Each item may be defended individually. Together, they may compensate the same risk several times.

A scholastic audit must therefore identify the specific title for each payment. What burden does this fee address? Has that burden already been addressed elsewhere? Is this payment restorative, or does it become yield? If risk is already reduced by collateral, it should not also be priced as though unsecured. If delay is already compensated by a penalty, it should not also be treated as a separate actual loss unless there is evidence of distinct damage. If administrative expense is covered by a fee, it should not be hidden again inside a return target.

The eleventh question is: does the contract create solidarity or domination? This is the final and deepest question. A contract can satisfy many technical criteria and still create domination. It can be legally sophisticated, economically rational, and formally consented to, while still using another's need as an occasion for control. The Christian moral tradition asks not only whether the numbers work, but what kind of relationship the contract creates.

Does the capital provider become a steward, a partner, a servant, an ally? Or does capital become a protected claimant standing over the borrower? Does the structure preserve the borrower's agency, mission, and capacity to recover after failure, or does it tighten control precisely when the borrower is weakest? Does it distribute loss in a way that honors human dignity, or does it make workers, founders, communities, and mission absorb the downside while capital receives priority?

This final question gathers the whole tradition together. Poena conventionalis asks whether delay penalties are restorative or revenue-seeking.
