Damnum emergens asks whether actual loss is repaired or invented. Lucrum cessans asks whether foregone gain is concrete or abstract. Periculum sortis asks whether risk is borne or shifted. All of them finally ask whether surplus serves justice or domination.
A modern diagnostic checklist for periculum sortis should therefore include at least the following:

1. Can principal actually be lost?
  2. Who legally bears the loss?
  3. Who economically bears the loss?
  4. Is repayment guaranteed?
  5. Is the claim collateralized?
  6. Is the capital senior?
  7. Does the borrower still owe if the project fails?
  8. Is risk priced proportionately?
  9. Are upside and downside shared?
  10. Can the lender enforce repayment against unrelated borrower assets?
  11. Is risk double-counted through fees, penalties, guarantees, or premiums?
  12. Does the contract create solidarity or domination?

These questions do not produce an automatic answer. They are not a formula; they are a discipline of moral attention. They force finance to become legible, prevent risk from becoming a magic word, and make capital show where it actually stands when loss arrives.

If capital truly stands where risk falls, periculum sortis may justify a surplus. If capital stands protected while others fall, the title fails. That is the modern meaning of the old scholastic test.

chapters.part: Part VIII — Periculum Sortis: Risk of Capital and the Moral Structure of Downside

chapters.title: Labor, Expense, and the Monti di Pietà
chapters.label: Chapter 31

sections.title: 31.1 The Franciscan Problem
paragraphs: The Monti di Pietà arose from a problem that was at once practical, pastoral, and theological: how can one lend to the poor without either exploiting them or allowing the institution of mercy to collapse?

This was the Franciscan problem. It was not enough to denounce usury from the pulpit. The poor still needed credit. They needed seed, tools, food, dowries, rent money, emergency funds, and temporary relief from crisis. If no lawful source of credit existed, they would turn to those who lent at crushing rates. The moral purity of refusing all institutional lending could leave the poor in the hands of harsher creditors. Yet if a charitable lending institution charged too much, it would become the very thing it had been created to oppose.

The Monti di Pietà were born in this narrow and difficult space. They were designed as charitable pawn-lending institutions, especially associated with the Franciscan Observant movement, that would provide small loans to the poor against pledges. The aim was not capital accumulation. The aim was protection: to rescue borrowers from usurers, stabilize households, and make credit available without turning need into a revenue stream. But even a charitable institution has costs. Someone must receive and record pledges. Someone must store them. Someone must appraise them. Someone must keep accounts. Someone must administer repayments. Buildings must be maintained. Staff must eat. Records must be kept. Losses must be absorbed. Fraud must be prevented.
This created the central question: may a charitable lending institution charge more than the principal to cover its expenses?

For a strict anti-usury logic, the question was dangerous. The mutuum itself did not justify surplus. A loan for consumption required repayment of the equivalent principal, not more. If the Monte lent ten and required eleven, was that not usury? Could the fact that the lender was charitable change the moral nature of the transaction? Could good intentions make surplus lawful? Or was the extra payment still a charge for the use of money?

The Franciscan answer had to be more precise than sentiment. The argument could not simply be: "We are helping the poor, therefore the charge is lawful." That would make intention swallow justice. Instead, the defenders of the Monti had to show that the surplus was not profit from the loan, but reimbursement for real labor and expense. The charge had to be tied to the costs of operating the institution, not to the time value of money, not to the borrower's vulnerability, and not to the lender's desire for gain.

This is why the Monti belong among the adjacent titles. Their small charges are not exactly damnum emergens, because they are not always compensation for a particular loss caused by a specific borrower. They are not lucrum cessans, because the institution is not claiming a profit it could otherwise have earned. They are not periculum sortis, though institutional lending may involve some risk of loss. They are not poena conventionalis, because they are not penalties for culpable delay. They are closer to stipendium laboris and expense reimbursement: compensation for the work and cost necessary to make charitable lending possible.

This distinction matters. The charge is justified, if it is justified, by institutional necessity. It is not a return on capital, not rent on money, not compensation for the mere passage of time. It is payment for the concrete labor and administration without which the charitable lending work cannot continue. If the institution cannot pay clerks, store pledges, maintain records, guard against fraud, and keep its doors open, then its mercy becomes unsustainable. But if it turns those costs into a profit engine, then its mercy becomes compromised.

The Franciscan problem therefore exposes one of the deepest tensions in moral finance: sustainability is necessary, but sustainability can become the language by which extraction justifies itself. Every institution must survive. Not every survival strategy is morally legitimate. A charitable lender may need to recover costs; it may not use cost recovery as a cover for yield. It may need staff, buildings, accounting, and administration; it may not allow the expenses of the institution to become an expanding claim upon the poor.

This tension was especially sharp for the Franciscans because their own charism placed poverty at the center of Christian life. They were not trying to build banks in the modern profit-seeking sense. They were trying to create instruments of mercy within a world where debt could destroy households. Their support for the Monti was not a retreat from the anti-usury tradition, but an attempt to embody it institutionally. They saw that condemnation alone did not free the poor from usury. Alternative institutions were needed.

Here the Monti teach a crucial lesson: moral theology must sometimes become institutional design. It is not enough to say what is forbidden. One must build structures that make the good possible. If the poor need credit, and if exploitative lenders dominate the market, then a just alternative must be created. But that alternative must be governed by the very moral principles it seeks to defend. Its accounting, charges, governance, and mission must all be shaped by the distinction between service and extraction.

This is why bookkeeping mattered. A Monte could not simply claim that its charges covered costs; it needed to know what those costs were. Labor, storage, administration, and losses had to be made visible; otherwise the line between reimbursement and profit would blur, and without accounting, titles become rhetoric. The same discipline holds in every moral-finance institution: if a charge is justified by expense, the expense must be documented; if a fee is justified by stewardship labor, the labor must be real; if a reserve is justified by risk, the risk must be borne.

The Monti also show that small charges can be morally different from interest, but only under disciplined conditions. A fee that covers the cost of administering a charitable loan is not the same as a percentage return demanded because money has been lent. A clerk's wage is not usury. A storage cost is not usury. A reasonable administrative fee is not usury if it corresponds to real work and does not enrich the lender. But the moral difference depends on the purpose, calculation, and limits of the charge. The same amount may be restorative in one structure and extractive in another.

This is the point at which the Franciscan problem becomes relevant for modern non-extractive funds. A fund that refuses extraction still has costs. It needs due diligence, legal work, accounting, governance, reporting, stewardship, technical assistance, compliance, portfolio support, and administration. These functions do not perform themselves: people must be paid, systems maintained, audits run. If no costs are covered, the institution may collapse or become dependent on hidden subsidy. But if costs are over-recovered, or if fees become disconnected from service, then the fund begins to reproduce the extractive logic it rejects.

The Monti therefore help distinguish cost recovery from profit entitlement. Cost recovery asks: what does this institution actually need in order to serve? Profit entitlement asks: what can capital demand because it has power, risk, or opportunity? The first may be compatible with non-extractive finance. The second must be scrutinized. A moral institution may charge enough to sustain its mission. It may not transform mission into a justification for unlimited surplus.

This also means that a non-extractive institution must be willing to audit itself. The danger is not only external usury. The danger is internal drift. A small administrative charge becomes a standard fee. A standard fee becomes a margin. A margin becomes a revenue expectation. A revenue expectation becomes a business model. A business model begins to shape the institution's behavior. Eventually the institution created to protect borrowers may come to rely on borrower payments in ways that subtly compromise its mission.

The Franciscans understood this danger because they understood poverty not merely as an economic condition, but as a spiritual discipline. To serve the poor requires more than good intentions. It requires ongoing resistance to possession, accumulation, status, and institutional self-protection. A charitable lender can become attached to its own survival. It can begin to justify burdens on the poor because those burdens sustain the institution. The Franciscan witness presses the question: does the institution exist for the poor, or have the poor become the means by which the institution exists?

This question is uncomfortable, but necessary, and every mission-driven financial institution faces it. The Monti remind us that even mercy needs governance, and every nonprofit, cooperative lender, impact fund, steward-owned vehicle, and non-extractive capital structure must ask whether the mission remains primary or whether the financial mechanism has taken on a life of its own.

In the history of the extrinsic titles, the Franciscan problem widens the inquiry. We are no longer dealing only with individual lenders and borrowers, but with institutions built to mediate capital morally. The question is not simply whether one creditor may receive surplus from one debtor. It is whether an institution may collect charges in order to make non-usurious lending possible. That requires a different but related moral logic: labor may be paid, expenses may be reimbursed, administration may be sustained, but capital must not turn the poor into a source of gain.

The Monti di Pietà were therefore not loopholes in the usury doctrine. They were experiments in moral infrastructure. They asked whether a society could build credit institutions that served the vulnerable without exploiting them. They recognized that the poor need more than exhortation; they need alternatives. But they also recognized that alternatives must be morally governed, or they will drift.

The Franciscan problem remains ours. How do we build financial institutions that are sustainable but not extractive? How do we pay for real work without creating hidden yield? How do we recover costs without burdening the very people we intend to serve? How do we keep administration from becoming accumulation? How do we ensure that capital, labor, governance, and accounting remain ordered toward mercy?
The answer begins where the Franciscans began: with a refusal to let need become profit, and with a willingness to design institutions that make that refusal practical.

sections.title: 31.2 Monti di Pietà
paragraphs: The Monti di Pietà were among the most important institutional experiments in the history of Christian moral finance. They were not merely charitable funds, nor were they ordinary banks with pious decoration. They were designed to answer a very specific problem: how can the poor obtain small loans without falling into the hands of usurers?

The name itself points to their purpose. A mons pietatis was a "mountain" or fund of mercy: a pooled store of resources intended to serve the needy. These institutions lent small sums, usually against pledges, to people who needed temporary relief. The borrower might bring a cloak, tool, household item, piece of jewelry, or other object as collateral. The Monte would advance money, hold the pledge, and return it when the loan was repaid. The goal was not speculation but mercy organized into durable form.

But mercy, once institutionalized, requires administration. A private almsgiver can hand a coin to a poor neighbor and walk away. A public lending institution cannot. It must receive pledges, record loans, guard property, manage repayments, assess values, prevent fraud, pay staff, rent or maintain buildings, keep accounts, and absorb some losses. The work of charity becomes embodied in procedures, desks, ledgers, locks, scales, clerks, rooms, and rules. Without these, the institution cannot function. With them, it incurs costs.

This is why the Monti became central to the usury debate. They were created to fight usury, yet they often charged small amounts above principal to cover operating expenses. Critics asked the obvious question: if a borrower receives ten and must repay ten plus a fee, is this not usury? Does the lender's charity change the nature of the payment? Can an institution founded to oppose usury require more than principal without contradicting itself?

The defenders of the Monti answered by distinguishing profit from reimbursement. The charge was not supposed to be a return on capital, a price for the use of money, or a reward for the mere passage of time. It was meant to cover the costs of administering the loan. Clerks had to be paid, pledges stored, accounts maintained, buildings supported. If these costs were not covered, the institution would collapse, and the poor would return to predatory lenders.

This is where the Monti connect to the adjacent titles of stipendium laboris and expense reimbursement. Stipendium laboris refers to compensation for labor. Expense reimbursement refers to repayment of costs actually incurred. These are not identical to the four major extrinsic titles, but they belong to the same moral universe. They ask whether a payment above principal is tied to a real burden external to the loan itself. If the burden is real, necessary, proportionate, and not profit-seeking, the payment may be morally different from usury.

This distinction is crucial. The Monte could not simply say, "We are a good institution, therefore our charges are good." Its moral legitimacy depended on the character of the charge. Was the payment truly connected to labor and expense? Was it limited to what was necessary? Was it calculated in a way that avoided profit extraction? Was it transparent? Was it proportionate to the administrative burden? Was the poor borrower being served, or made, little by little, to sustain an expanding institution?

The Monti therefore required a different kind of moral accounting. If the institution claimed to charge for expenses, the expenses had to be identifiable; if it claimed to charge for labor, the labor had to be real. If the charge exceeded the cost, the surplus became morally suspect. The distinction between reimbursement and profit could not remain in the realm of intention alone. It had to become visible in the books.

This is one of the most important lessons the Monti offer to modern moral finance. A fee is not justified merely because it is called a fee. A management charge is not automatically non-extractive because the institution has a mission. An administrative cost is not morally clean simply because administration is necessary. The charge must correspond to reality: real work, real expense, real institutional need. Otherwise, cost recovery becomes a pious name for yield.

The Monti also reveal why collateral is morally ambiguous. On one hand, pledges made the lending model possible: they reduced losses, protected the common fund, and allowed loans to be offered at lower cost. The pledge was not necessarily a tool of domination; it could be a way to make charitable credit sustainable. On the other hand, taking pledges from the poor always carries danger. A cloak, tool, or household object may have value beyond its market price. It may be necessary for work, warmth, dignity, or family life. The institution therefore had to treat pledges not merely as security, but as objects embedded in human need.

Here the biblical memory of the cloak returns. In Exodus, the cloak taken in pledge must be returned by night, because it is the poor person's covering. That text does not abolish pledges altogether, but it subjects them to mercy. A pledge is not just collateral. It may be a person's life-support. The Monti, if faithful to their purpose, had to remember that the poor borrower was not a risk unit, but a neighbor.

This is why charitable pawn-lending required moral restraint. The institution had to avoid lending so little against pledges that the poor were effectively dispossessed, avoid harsh forfeiture, avoid turning unredeemed pledges into a revenue strategy, and avoid designing the system so that default became profitable. The same anti-abuse logic used for poena conventionalis applies here: if the institution benefits from borrower failure, the structure becomes suspect.

The administrative charge also had to avoid becoming proportional interest in disguise. A small fee for processing and storing a pledge may be legitimate. But if the fee rises automatically with time or principal in a way that resembles yield, the usury question returns. Is the borrower paying for actual work, or for the use of money? Does the institution incur more labor because the loan lasts longer, or is time simply being monetized? Does the fee correspond to cost, or does cost provide a cover for return?

This distinction remains decisive for modern analogues. A non-extractive fund may need to charge management fees. It may need to recover legal costs, accounting costs, due diligence costs, audit costs, and stewardship costs. But each charge should be able to answer the Monte question: what real work or expense does this payment cover? If the answer is vague, the charge should be suspect. If the charge grows with assets merely because that is market standard, the moral basis must be examined. If the fee continues after the work has been done, it may have drifted from reimbursement toward entitlement.

The Monti also show that moral finance requires institutional limits. Good intentions at founding are not enough. An institution created to serve the poor can gradually begin to serve itself. Salaries can rise, buildings can expand, administration can become heavy, reserves can become excessive, fees can normalize, and borrowers can become a revenue base. The original mission may still be named, while the actual structure slowly shifts. This is mission drift in its early form.

To prevent this, the Monti needed governance, scrutiny, and public moral accountability. Their defenders had to persuade Church authorities that the charges were not usurious. Their practices were debated and their structures examined. Their legitimacy depended not only on compassion, but on disciplined institutional design. The lesson is clear: if a financial institution claims a moral exemption from ordinary profit logic, it must accept a higher burden of transparency.

This is especially relevant for non-extractive capital today. If a fund claims not to be extractive, it should be willing to show how fees are calculated, how costs are documented, how surplus is capped, how mission is protected, how borrower or portfolio-company vulnerability is respected, and how downside is shared. It cannot simply invoke impact, stewardship, Catholic Social Teaching, solidarity, or mission. The Monti remind us that moral language must become accounting practice.

The Monti di Pietà therefore occupy an important place in the history of the extrinsic titles because they demonstrate that not every payment above principal is usury, but also that every such payment must be justified. Their charges were defended as necessary for labor and expense, not as profit on the loan. That defense was morally serious only insofar as the charges actually remained within those limits.

They also show that non-extractive finance is not costless finance. To serve the poor well requires work. To administer capital justly requires infrastructure. To protect against fraud and drift requires systems. The question is not whether an institution may survive. The question is how it survives, on whose back, and with what limits.

At their best, the Monti were institutions of mercy made operational. They did not merely condemn usury. They built an alternative. They tried to create a form of credit in which the poor could receive help without becoming prey. Their small charges, when properly limited, were not meant to make money fruitful for its own sake. They were meant to make mercy sustainable.

That is the enduring significance of the Monti di Pietà. They teach that moral finance must do more than refuse extraction. It must build institutions that can serve without exploiting, sustain themselves without accumulating unjustly, and keep their accounts ordered toward mercy. Their question remains ours: how can the necessary costs of serving life be covered without turning life itself into the source of profit?

sections.title: 31.3 Bernardino and Antonino
paragraphs: Bernardino of Siena and Sant'Antonino of Florence stand at the center of the moral defense of the Monti di Pietà. They are important because they show that the Franciscan and late-medieval concern with usury was not merely negative. It did not only condemn. It also tried to build. These theologians and preachers understood that if the poor were to be protected from usurers, alternative institutions had to exist. The question was not whether the Church should care about credit. The question was what form credit could take without becoming extraction.

Bernardino's role is especially significant because he combined moral rigor with pastoral realism. He was deeply suspicious of usury, greed, and commercial vice, but he was not blind to the economic conditions of ordinary people. He knew that the poor often needed small amounts of money to survive crises, that artisans, widows, laborers, and households could be trapped by predatory lenders, and that denunciation without institutional alternative could leave them with no practical help. The Monti answered this pastoral problem by creating a structured form of charitable lending.

Bernardino's defense of these institutions therefore did not weaken the anti-usury tradition. It gave it a concrete social form. The point was not to make lending profitable. The point was to prevent the poor from being driven into ruin by those who made lending profitable. A Monte could serve as a kind of public work of mercy: a financial instrument ordered not toward enrichment, but toward relief, stability, and household preservation.

Yet Bernardino also understood that an institution of mercy could drift. A charge above principal might be necessary to cover labor and expense, but it could not be allowed to become a profit claim. The moral boundary was the difference between sustainability and extraction. Sustainability meant that the institution could pay necessary staff, keep accounts, safeguard pledges, maintain buildings, and continue lending. Extraction meant that the institution began to treat the borrower's need as a source of gain.

This distinction is fundamental. The Monti were defended not because they were exempt from moral scrutiny, but because their charges could be interpreted as cost recovery rather than usury. The charge had to correspond to real work and real expense, had to be limited, had to serve the institution's charitable purpose, and could not become a hidden return on capital. Bernardino's practical moral theology therefore insisted that institutional usefulness did not erase the need for moral limits.

Sant'Antonino of Florence deepens this tradition of practical discernment. As a Dominican theologian, archbishop, and moral guide, Antonino stands as one of the great figures of late-medieval pastoral economics. His importance lies in his careful attention to real cases, commercial practice, conscience, and the dangers of disguised usury. He was not content with abstract principles alone; he wanted moral theology capable of guiding merchants, confessors, civic leaders, lenders, borrowers, and institutions.

Antonino's treatment of lending, commerce, and just exchange belongs to a larger project of moral governance. Economic life had to be ordered to justice, charity, and the common good. But this ordering required distinctions. A merchant's profit could be licit when tied to labor, risk, service, or public utility; a penalty, when truly penal and not a fraud; compensation for loss, when real; a charitable lending institution's charge for administration, when necessary and restrained. In each case, the question was not simply whether money changed hands, but why.

This is why Antonino is such an important bridge figure. He preserves the anti-usury tradition while helping it deal with complex urban economies. Florence was not an abstract theological classroom. It was a commercial city, full of banking, trade, guilds, political conflict, poverty, and wealth. A moral theologian in such a setting could not avoid the question of institutions. The poor needed help. Merchants needed guidance. Civic authorities needed forms that could serve the common good. Antonino's moral theology tried to supply that guidance without surrendering to commercial convenience.

Together, Bernardino and Antonino show that the defense of the Monti depended on moral boundaries. The first boundary was purpose. The institution had to exist for the poor, not for investors, administrators, civic prestige, or institutional self-expansion. Its reason for being was mercy. If that purpose changed, the moral status of its charges changed with it.

The second boundary was proportionality. Charges had to correspond to real costs. A small administrative payment might be defensible. A charge designed to generate surplus would not be. The amount mattered because excess reveals intention. A fee far beyond the cost of labor and administration begins to look less like reimbursement and more like yield.

The third boundary was transparency. If the charge is justified by expense, the expense must be visible. The books become part of the argument: without accounting, the moral distinction between reimbursement and profit cannot be maintained, and the institution must be able to show what it costs to operate.

The fourth boundary was anti-abuse. The Monte must not profit from default. It must not design repayment terms so that borrowers are likely to fail. It must not use pledges harshly. It must not turn forfeited goods into a revenue strategy. It must not become dependent on the misfortune of those it serves. Here the anti-abuse logic of the other extrinsic titles returns. A legitimate title becomes illegitimate when the institution begins to desire the event that triggers payment.

The fifth boundary was care for the borrower's dignity. A poor borrower is not merely a client, account, pawn ticket, or credit risk. He is a neighbor. The pledged object may not be merely collateral. It may be connected to work, family, warmth, memory, or survival. The institution's procedures must therefore be shaped by mercy as well as efficiency.

These boundaries are what distinguish the Monti from ordinary profit-seeking finance. An ordinary lender asks whether the loan can produce return. A Monte, at least in its ideal form, asks whether the borrower can be helped without injustice. The difference is not cosmetic: a Monte must not only lend differently, it must want differently, and the wanting shows up in pricing, enforcement, governance, accounting, and institutional culture.

This point is essential. Bernardino and Antonino were not defending a technical loophole. They were defending a moral form. A charitable lending institution could charge for costs only because its purpose, structure, and limits differed from those of a usurer. Remove those limits, and the same charge becomes suspect. The institution's moral identity depends on continual discipline.

This has direct implications for modern non-extractive finance. A mission-driven fund cannot justify fees or returns merely by pointing to its mission. It must show that the charges are ordered to the mission and limited by it. Management fees must correspond to real stewardship labor. Administrative charges must correspond to actual costs. Risk compensation must correspond to real loss-bearing. Any surplus must be governed by the question: does this serve the enterprise, the worker, the community, and the mission, or does it turn them into sources of capital growth?

Bernardino and Antonino also teach that practical institutions need public moral imagination. It is not enough to say that lending should be charitable. One must design an institution capable of doing charitable lending in the real world. This means procedures, reserves, staff, records, governance, and accountability. It also means spiritual vigilance: the institution must continually return to its purpose. Otherwise, practicality becomes the path by which the original moral vision is lost.

The Monti therefore show how moral theology becomes institutional architecture. The scholastic distinction between compensation and usury is not only a matter for confessors and books. It shapes how ledgers are kept, how fees are calculated, how pledges are treated, how defaults are handled, and how administrators are paid. The moral doctrine becomes visible in the operating model.

This is why Bernardino and Antonino matter beyond their historical moment. They represent an approach to finance that is neither naively anti-institutional nor complacently permissive. They know that institutions are necessary, that costs must be covered, and that commerce and credit cannot simply be wished away. But they also know that financial structures must be subordinated to justice and mercy. Practical necessity is real, but it is not sovereign.

The distinction between sustainability and profit extraction is therefore one of their enduring contributions. A charitable or mission-driven institution may need money to continue serving. But the need to survive can become an idol. If institutional sustainability becomes detached from the people served, the institution's charges begin to protect the institution rather than the vulnerable. The Monti are legitimate only insofar as their sustainability remains ordered to mercy.

For modern purposes, the lesson is sharp. A fund, foundation, steward-owned structure, or non-extractive capital vehicle should ask: What costs are truly necessary? Who benefits from each fee? Are charges capped? Are they reviewed? Are borrowers or portfolio companies protected from fee drift? Does the institution become more prosperous when those it serves struggle? Are defaults treated as failures of mercy or as revenue events? Are the accounts intelligible enough to prove that cost recovery has not become extraction?

Bernardino and Antonino would not let us hide behind good intentions. They would ask whether the structure itself is ordered toward the good, whether the institution's revenue depends on the vulnerability of the poor, and whether the charge is truly labor, expense, or loss, or simply usury with a Franciscan face.

Their defense of the Monti is therefore both encouraging and demanding. Encouraging, because it shows that Christian moral theology can inspire practical financial innovation. Demanding, because it shows that such innovation must be governed by strict moral boundaries. The Church can bless institutions that serve the poor. But it must also scrutinize them, because even institutions of mercy can drift toward self-interest.

In the end, Bernardino and Antonino teach that moral finance is not a refusal of practicality. It is practicality converted. It is the design of institutions that can survive without extracting, lend without dominating, charge without profiteering, and administer mercy without losing the spirit of mercy itself.

sections.title: 31.4 Fifth Lateran Council
paragraphs: The controversy over the Monti di Pietà eventually required ecclesial judgment. The question was too important, and too contested, to remain only a matter of local practice or theological debate. These institutions had been founded to protect the poor from usury, yet they charged small amounts above principal to cover their expenses. Critics saw contradiction. Defenders saw necessary institutional mercy. The Church had to decide whether such charges were usurious, or whether they could be justified as something other than profit from the loan.

The Fifth Lateran Council gave decisive support to the Monti di Pietà (the bull Inter multiplices, 1515). Its approval mattered because it recognized an essential distinction: not every payment above principal is usury. A charge may be illicit if it is demanded as gain from the loan itself. But a charge may be legitimate if it corresponds to real labor, real expense, and institutional necessity. The Council's judgment did not abolish the usury doctrine. It clarified how the doctrine applied to a practical institution created for the relief of the poor.

This was a crucial moment in the history of moral finance. The Church was not saying that charitable intention makes any charge lawful. Nor was it saying that the poor may be burdened at will so long as the lender is religious or philanthropic. Rather, it affirmed that a small and necessary payment could be morally different from usurious gain when it existed to sustain the institution's work. The surplus had to be tied to costs, not to capital's claim to increase.

The distinction is subtle but decisive. In a usurious loan, the creditor demands more because money has been lent. The surplus is attached to the mutuum as such. In the properly ordered Monte, the charge is attached to administration: the wages of staff, the keeping of accounts, the custody of pledges, the maintenance of buildings, the operational costs required to continue lending. The borrower is not paying for the mere use of money. The borrower is contributing to the real costs of a charitable credit institution.

This does not make the charge morally trivial. The borrower is still poor. Any additional payment matters. The approval of the Monti therefore had to be read under the discipline of proportionality. A charge that exceeds the actual cost begins to lose its justification. A fee that becomes a source of profit no longer rests on expense reimbursement. A system that relies on borrower failure or forfeited pledges as revenue betrays its purpose. The Council's approval protects the institution only insofar as the institution remains ordered to mercy rather than gain.

The Fifth Lateran Council's approval also shows that moral theology can recognize institutional necessity without surrendering to institutional self-interest. This is important. A purely individualistic view of lending might imagine that every loan is simply a relation between one lender and one borrower. But the Monti were institutions. They required infrastructure, governance, staff, and systems of recordkeeping and accountability. If such an institution could never recover its costs, it could not endure. The Council recognized that mercy sometimes needs administration.

Yet administration is spiritually dangerous. Once mercy becomes institutional, the institution can begin to protect itself. Costs can expand, fees normalize, staff structures grow, reserves swell, and a charitable fund can become attached to its own continuity. The Council's approval must therefore be held together with ongoing scrutiny. Institutional necessity may justify cost recovery, but it does not justify institutional accumulation without limit.

In this sense, the approval of the Monti belongs to the wider logic of the extrinsic titles. The charge is legitimate only if it is extrinsic to the loan as such. It must be grounded in a real external burden: labor, expense, administration, preservation of pledges, accounting, and the maintenance of a charitable service. It is adjacent to damnum emergens because it concerns real costs. It is adjacent to stipendium laboris because it pays for real work. But it must not become a hidden form of lucrum or ordinary yield.

The Council's judgment also demonstrates that the usury doctrine never set itself against finance as such. It set itself against extraction. The Church could condemn usury while supporting institutions of credit. It could oppose profit from the poor while authorizing structures that allowed the poor to borrow safely. It could say no to money breeding money, while saying yes to the careful organization of mercy. This is precisely the distinction that modern readers often miss. The old doctrine did not forbid all financial mediation. It judged the moral source of surplus.

The approval of the Monti therefore has enduring significance. It shows that Christian moral theology can inspire concrete alternatives to predatory finance. The Monti were not simply sermons in institutional form. They were operating systems: funds, ledgers, pledges, clerks, rules, buildings, and procedures. The Church's approval recognized that such systems could serve justice when properly ordered. It also implied that financial design itself is a field of moral theology.

This point is especially important for modern non-extractive capital. A fund, cooperative lender, steward-owned financing vehicle, or mission-locked investment institution may need to charge fees, pay staff, and secure legal support, due diligence, accounting, audit, reporting, and portfolio stewardship. These costs are not automatically extractive. But they must be named honestly, limited, tied to real service, and governed by the mission rather than by capital's appetite.

The Monti also remind us that a just institution must be designed for the people it serves, not merely for its own survival. The question is not simply: can this fee sustain the institution? The question is: does this fee sustain the institution to serve the borrower, or does the borrower now exist to sustain the institution? The Fifth Lateran Council's approval makes sense only under the first condition. The institution exists for mercy. It does not turn mercy into a revenue base.

This distinction matters because "sustainability" is morally ambiguous. In modern institutional language, sustainability often sounds unquestionably good. But any institution can use sustainability to justify growing fees, expanding administration, or protecting itself from sacrifice. The Monti force us to ask: sustainable for what, and at whose expense? A charitable lending institution should be sustainable enough to continue serving the poor, not sustainable by extracting from the poor.

The Council's approval also strengthens the argument that bookkeeping is not morally neutral. If charges are justified by costs, costs must be knowable. Accounts must be kept, expenses separated from profit, labor distinguished from return on capital. Forfeited pledges, late payments, administrative fees, and donations must be tracked. Without this accounting discipline, a lawful charge can drift into usurious practice.

Here the Monti anticipate a central concern of modern moral finance: auditability. A non-extractive financial institution should be able to show why each payment exists. What is reimbursement? What is labor? What is risk? What is reserve? What is surplus? What is mission-related cost? What is capital return? If the institution cannot answer, then it cannot prove that its charges are non-extractive. The Fifth Lateran Council's approval of cost-covering charges implies the need for cost transparency.

The approval of the Monti also complicates any simplistic reading of Franciscan economics. Franciscan poverty did not lead only to withdrawal from economic life. It also generated institutional creativity. The Franciscans saw that the poor needed structures of protection. They were willing to build financial institutions, but only under strict moral conditions. Their poverty sharpened their economic imagination rather than eliminating it.

This is one of the great lessons of the Monti di Pietà: the answer to predatory finance is not merely less finance. It is better finance, governed finance, merciful finance, finance that knows its limits. The Fifth Lateran Council recognized that lending could be organized for the common good when its charges served labor and expense rather than gain from money itself. The Council thereby preserved the usury doctrine while making room for practical mercy.

For modern purposes, the Council's decision suggests a simple but demanding principle: institutional charges can be legitimate when they are necessary to serve life, but they become suspect when they make life serve the institution. A fee for real stewardship work may be just. A fee that grows into hidden yield is not. A reserve for real losses may be prudent. A reserve that becomes capital accumulation without accountability is suspect. A charge for administration may sustain mercy. A charge that burdens the vulnerable for institutional comfort betrays mercy.

The Fifth Lateran Council therefore stands as a major turning point in the history of the adjacent titles. It confirms that labor, expense, and institutional necessity can matter morally. It shows that the usury doctrine is capable of recognizing practical costs. But it also leaves intact the central prohibition: surplus from the loan itself remains illicit. The charge is permissible only because it is not that.

This distinction should shape every modern attempt at non-extractive finance. The question is not whether a fund may have costs. It may. The question is whether those costs are real, transparent, proportionate, mission-ordered, and not converted into capital's entitlement. The Monti were approved because their small charges could be understood as the cost of mercy. That approval becomes morally meaningful only if mercy remains the measure of the cost.

In the end, the Fifth Lateran Council teaches that moral finance must be both principled and practical. It must refuse usury, protect the poor, and build institutions capable of doing so over time. But every institution that handles money must be examined, because the line between service and extraction is never self-enforcing. The Church's approval of the Monti was not permission for financial complacency. It was a disciplined recognition that mercy, if it is to endure, may require administration. But administration must remain servant, not master.

sections.title: 31.5 Lessons for Modern Non-Extractive Funds
paragraphs: The Monti di Pietà offer one of the clearest historical precedents for modern non-extractive finance. They were created to solve a problem that has never disappeared: people and enterprises need capital, but the available forms of capital often exploit their need. The Monti did not respond by saying that credit should not exist. They responded by building institutions whose purpose was to provide credit without usury. In doing so, they showed that moral finance is not merely a doctrine of prohibition. It is also a discipline of institutional design.

This is the first lesson for modern non-extractive funds: condemnation is not enough. If extractive capital dominates the market, then moral critique alone will not help borrowers, founders, workers, or communities. Alternatives must be built. The poor in the late medieval city needed a place to go besides the usurer. Likewise, mission-driven enterprises today need sources of capital other than venture capital that demands exit, private equity that loads companies with debt, or lenders that require guarantees, collateral, and control rights that shift nearly all downside away from capital. Moral economy must become practical infrastructure.

The second lesson is that non-extractive finance still has costs. The Monti needed staff, buildings, ledgers, guards, appraisers, storage, governance, and systems. Modern funds need due diligence, legal work, accounting, auditing, administration, reporting, compliance, portfolio support, impact review, governance stewardship, and sometimes technical assistance. These activities require labor. Labor deserves compensation. An institution that refuses all cost recovery may appear pure, but it may also become fragile, dependent on hidden subsidy, or unable to serve at scale.

The question is therefore not whether costs may be covered. The question is whether cost recovery remains cost recovery. This is the third lesson. A management fee is morally defensible only if it corresponds to real stewardship work; administrative charges, only if they correspond to real administration; due diligence fees, only if due diligence is actually performed and priced proportionately. Legal, audit, and accounting fees must be tied to real services. The moment these payments become disconnected from actual work, they begin to drift from reimbursement into yield.

This is why accounting is morally central. The Monti had to distinguish expenses from profit. Modern non-extractive funds must do the same with far greater precision. Every charge should be classifiable. Is it reimbursement for an actual expense? Is it compensation for labor? Is it a reserve against documented loss? Is it a payment for genuine risk-bearing? Is it a penalty for culpable delay? Or is it simply a return demanded by capital? Without this classification, non-extractive finance remains a slogan rather than a discipline.

The fourth lesson is that fees should be mission-locked. A fund that exists to serve enterprises, workers, communities, or public goods should not allow its fee structure to redirect the institution, by degrees, toward self-preservation or investor satisfaction. Fees should be capped, reviewed, and justified. They should not grow automatically because assets under management grow, unless the work truly grows with them. They should not create incentives to keep companies dependent, prolong unnecessary processes, or maximize administrative complexity. The fee structure must serve the mission, not the other way around.

The fifth lesson concerns due diligence. In extractive finance, due diligence often serves capital protection: how can the investor avoid loss, secure priority, and maximize upside? In non-extractive finance, due diligence must still be serious, but its purpose is different. It should ask whether the enterprise can responsibly receive capital, whether the structure protects mission, whether repayment expectations are realistic, whether downside is fairly allocated, and whether the financing will strengthen or distort the company. Due diligence becomes a form of stewardship rather than a technique of control.

The sixth lesson concerns audit. The Monti needed oversight because charitable institutions can drift. Modern non-extractive funds need the same, and today's contracts are more complex. An independent audit should not merely check whether money was spent legally. It should check whether the moral claims of the fund are true. Are fees actually covering costs? Are returns actually tied to risk-bearing? Are penalties actually restorative? Are hardship clauses being honored? Are portfolio companies being protected from hidden extraction? Are investor rights consistent with the fund's stated mission?

This kind of audit is not an optional add-on; it is part of the moral structure. If a fund claims to be non-extractive, it must be willing to be examined. The claim cannot rest only on intention, branding, or founding documents. It must be visible in the books, contracts, governance, and lived experience of the companies financed.

The seventh lesson is that borrower and portfolio-company protections must be designed in from the beginning. The Monti existed to protect the poor from predatory lenders. A modern non-extractive fund exists, at least in part, to protect mission-driven enterprises from capital structures that extract control, force premature exits, or shift failure onto workers and founders. This means using grace periods, cure periods, hardship clauses, realistic repayment schedules, caps on fees, limits on penalties, and clear rules for what happens when things go badly. The test of a financing structure is not only how it behaves in success, but how it behaves in distress.

The eighth lesson is that collateral and security must be treated with caution. The Monti used pledges, but the pledge remained morally sensitive because it often belonged to a poor person's household life. Modern funds may use security mechanisms, but they must ask what those mechanisms do to the enterprise. Does collateral protect the fund at the cost of mission? Does a personal guarantee place founders or families in danger? Does seniority push losses onto workers, suppliers, or community stakeholders? Does capital claim risk compensation while also protecting itself through security? If so, the moral claim to risk-bearing weakens.

The ninth lesson is that sustainability must not become extraction. The Monti needed to survive in order to serve, but survival can become an idol. The same danger applies to modern funds. A fund may begin with a mission to serve enterprises, then gradually become organized around its own budget, staff, reputation, investor reporting, and growth. What began as service becomes institutional self-protection. A non-extractive fund must therefore ask regularly: are we sustaining ourselves in order to serve, or are the enterprises now serving our sustainability?

The tenth lesson concerns governance. The Monti required public and ecclesial scrutiny because they handled money in the name of mercy. Modern non-extractive funds need governance structures that protect mission from investor pressure, founder charisma, managerial drift, and financial normalization. This may require steward ownership, asset locks, mission locks, independent guardians, borrower representation, community oversight, transparent reporting, or other mechanisms that prevent capital from slipping into control. Governance is not separate from finance. It is the structure that determines whether finance remains obedient to its purpose.

The eleventh lesson is that administration can be a form of charity. This is easy to forget. Ledgers, audits, loan records, payment schedules, classification systems, and governance minutes may seem dry compared with preaching mercy. But if the institution exists to prevent extraction, accurate administration is part of the work of mercy. Bad accounting allows drift. Vague fees hide extraction. Unclear contracts shift risk invisibly. Poor records make accountability impossible. Administration, rightly ordered, protects the vulnerable.

The twelfth lesson is that non-extractive funds should distinguish between reimbursement, compensation, and return. Reimbursement restores an expense. Compensation pays for labor, loss, or genuine risk-bearing. Return, in the extractive sense, is what capital demands because it is capital. A moral fund may reimburse expenses, compensate labor, and allow proportionate surplus where capital truly bears downside. But it must resist the idea that money deserves growth simply because it has been deployed.

This distinction should shape the entire capital stack. Management fees should correspond to work. Administrative fees should correspond to cost. Risk-sharing returns should correspond to actual loss exposure. Late penalties should correspond to real harm and should not be desired as revenue. Reserves should correspond to prudent mission protection, not accumulation for its own sake. Investor distributions should be bounded by the moral purpose of the fund.

The thirteenth lesson is that mission language must be translated into contract terms. It is not enough to say that capital is patient, steward-owned, solidarity-based, Catholic, Franciscan, impact-oriented, or non-extractive. The contract must show it. What happens if revenue drops? What happens if repayment threatens payroll? What happens if the company refuses an exit? What happens if mission conflicts with investor return? What happens if the fund itself faces pressure? A mission that does not govern these moments is not yet structurally real.

The fourteenth lesson is that the fund should not profit from failure. This was a crucial concern in late penalties and charitable pawn-lending. If default, delay, distress, or forfeiture becomes lucrative for the financing institution, incentives are corrupted. A non-extractive fund should prefer healthy repayment, mission preservation, and enterprise flourishing. Its contracts should not make distress more profitable than success. If late fees, default interest, collateral seizure, or restructuring charges become revenue sources, the fund has begun to resemble the systems it was meant to replace.

The fifteenth lesson is that the people served must remain visible. The poor borrower at the Monte was not merely a pawn ticket, and the modern portfolio company is not merely an asset. Founders, workers, users, suppliers, communities, and missions are all affected by financing structure. A non-extractive fund should therefore ask not only whether the investor is treated fairly, but whether the whole ecosystem is protected from unreasonable downside. This is especially important in digital infrastructure, healthcare, food systems, education, and other fields where mission failure harms more than shareholders.

The sixteenth lesson is that non-extractive finance should be willing to be less lucrative than extractive finance. This seems obvious, but it is often avoided. If the point is to prevent extraction, capital cannot expect the same upside it receives in structures designed to maximize capital. Cost recovery, modest compensation, and proportionate risk-sharing are not the same as market-rate extraction with better branding. A fund that promises non-extraction while preserving conventional return expectations may be trying to serve two masters.

The seventeenth lesson is that non-extractive funds need drift countermeasures. The Monti themselves became historical examples of both innovation and vulnerability. Any institution can drift. Modern countermeasures might include capped returns, open-book fee reporting, mission guardians, independent ethics review, borrower feedback mechanisms, periodic contract audits, transparent surplus classification, limits on related-party payments, and rules preventing the stacking of fees, penalties, and premiums. These are not bureaucratic burdens. They are the modern equivalent of confessional scrutiny.

The eighteenth lesson is that technology can help, but only if it is morally designed. A dashboard or simulator can classify payments, test risk allocation, flag double-counting, compare contract forms, and show who bears downside under different scenarios. But the software must be built around the right moral questions. It should not simply optimize returns. It should ask whether surplus is justified, whether titles are documented, whether risk is borne or shifted, whether fees match costs, and whether the structure serves life. In this sense, code can become a kind of moral ledger.

The nineteenth lesson is that non-extractive funds should not hide subsidy. Some mission-driven finance will require grants, donations, public support, volunteer labor, or concessionary capital, and that is not shameful. The Monti themselves often depended on charitable foundations, civic support, or donations to build their lending capital. The danger is pretending that a structure is self-sustaining when it depends on unacknowledged sacrifice. Hidden subsidy can obscure the true economics of mercy. Better to name clearly what is paid by borrowers, what is paid by donors, what is paid by investors, and what is absorbed by the institution.

The twentieth lesson is that capital must be converted from master to servant. This is the deepest continuity between the Monti and modern non-extractive finance. The issue is not merely the amount charged but the role capital plays. Does capital command the institution, or does it serve the institution's mission? Does it demand guaranteed growth, or does it accept limits? Does it stand above the borrower, or alongside the borrower? Does it use legal rights to protect itself from loss, or does it share vulnerability in a disciplined way?

The Monti di Pietà show that Christian moral finance is not anti-institutional, anti-accounting, or anti-practical. It is intensely practical. It asks how mercy can be organized without becoming exploitation, how administration can be paid without becoming extraction, how capital can be pooled without becoming sovereign, and how the vulnerable can receive help without becoming a revenue source.

For modern non-extractive funds, the lesson is clear: build the institution, but bind it. Pay for the work, but prove the work. Recover costs, but cap them. Share risk, but do not merely price it. Use contracts, but make them instruments of mercy. Keep accounts, but let the accounts serve conscience. Protect the fund, but not at the expense of those it exists to serve. Above all, never let the language of mission excuse the return of usury in institutional form.

The Monti were approved because they made a difficult claim plausible: a payment above principal can be lawful when it sustains the work of mercy rather than rewarding capital as such. That claim remains available to modern non-extractive finance. But it remains true only under the same discipline. The payment must be real, limited, transparent, proportionate, mission-ordered, and accountable. Otherwise, the mountain of mercy becomes a mountain of debt.

chapters.part: Part IX — Adjacent Titles, Institutions, and Practical Moral Finance

chapters.title: Carentia Pecuniae and the Edge of Modern Interest
chapters.label: Chapter 32

sections.title: 32.1 Definition
paragraphs: The phrase carentia pecuniae means, quite literally, the lack, absence, or deprivation of money. At first glance, it sounds almost harmless. A lender parts with money for a certain period; during that period the lender no longer has it, and the borrower has use of it instead. Should the lender not be compensated for this temporary deprivation?

This is precisely why carentia pecuniae sits at the dangerous edge of modern interest. It seems to name something real. The lender is indeed without money for a term. The money cannot be used elsewhere, spent, invested, pledged, lent, or held as liquid reserve. In ordinary modern financial language, this sounds like a liquidity premium, a term premium, or even simply the time value of money. But from within the older scholastic framework, the matter is much more delicate. If the mere absence of one's money for a period of time is enough to justify surplus payment, then almost every loan can generate a return simply because time has passed. And if that is true, then the old prohibition against usury has been emptied from within.

To understand the title, we must distinguish it carefully from lucrum cessans. Lucrum cessans concerns profit foregone. The lender says, in effect: "Because I lent this money to you, I was unable to use it in a concrete, profitable activity that I otherwise would have undertaken." In its stricter form, this title requires more than a vague appeal to possibility. It requires a real alternative use, a genuine intention to use the money in that way, a probable or reasonably determinate profit, and a causal connection between the loan and the lost gain. It is not enough to say, "Money can always earn something somewhere." The claim must be anchored in a concrete interrupted opportunity.

Carentia pecuniae shifts the emphasis. It does not necessarily claim that a specific profit was lost, but that being deprived of money for a fixed period is itself a compensable burden. The lender's money is tied up. The lender cannot call it back. The lender accepts a period of illiquidity. The title therefore moves from "I lost this concrete gain" toward "I was without my money for this term." That shift may look small. Morally it is enormous.

In the stricter scholastic imagination, money lent in a mutuum has passed into the ownership of the borrower. The borrower does not owe the identical coins, but an equivalent amount of the same kind. The lender has not rented out a thing while retaining ownership of it, as in a lease. Nor has the lender entered a partnership in which capital remains exposed to shared gain and loss. The lender has transferred ownership and retains a personal claim to repayment of the equivalent principal. This is why the tradition was so suspicious of charging merely for the "use" of money. In a consumable loan, the use of the thing cannot be separated from the thing itself. To sell the thing and then also sell its use is, in Aquinas's classic analysis, to sell the same thing twice.

The title of carentia pecuniae presses against that boundary. It asks whether the lender may be compensated not for the use of money as such, but for the inconvenience, deprivation, or binding of liquidity that results from committing money for a term. This is not exactly the same as selling the use of money, nor exactly the same as compensating actual loss. It lives in the gray zone between them.

This gray zone explains both the attraction and the danger of the title. On the one hand, there may be cases where the lender really does incur a burden by being deprived of funds. A merchant may be unable to meet obligations. A household may lose flexibility. A charitable institution may have to keep reserves available for its mission. A fund may be unable to redeploy capital for a defined purpose. To be without money can be a real cost, especially when liquidity itself has practical value.

On the other hand, once this burden is abstracted from concrete loss, concrete foregone gain, or genuine shared risk, it begins to resemble ordinary interest. The lender need not show that anything actually went wrong, need not prove a specific missed opportunity, and need not bear the downside risk of a partnership. The lender only needs to say: "I was without my money for a time." From there, the logic of modern finance is very close: money today is worth more than money tomorrow; therefore, delayed repayment must command a price.

Scholastic moral theology resists precisely this automatic monetization of time. Time, by itself, is not a moral title. The passage of time does not automatically create a right to surplus. A delay may cause damage, and then damnum emergens may apply. A delay may interrupt a concrete profitable use, and then lucrum cessans may apply under strict conditions. A transaction may expose capital to genuine loss, and then periculum sortis may be relevant. But mere duration is not enough. The lender must be able to name the real burden that justifies compensation.

This is why carentia pecuniae is related to lucrum cessans but distinct from it. Lucrum cessans says: "Because of this loan, this gain ceased." Carentia pecuniae says: "Because of this loan, I was deprived of money for a term." The first points toward a lost use and can remain tied to concrete commercial reality; the second points toward illiquidity itself, and can easily become a universal claim that all money, simply by being absent from its owner, deserves growth.

Here the older theological question returns in a sharper form: what exactly is being compensated? If the answer is actual loss, the title belongs under damnum emergens. If the answer is a concrete foregone gain, the title belongs under lucrum cessans. If the answer is genuine exposure of principal to loss, the title belongs under periculum sortis. But if the answer is simply "the lender did not have the money for a while," then the title risks becoming indistinguishable from interest on a loan as such.

That is why carentia pecuniae must be treated as a border concept. It reveals the pressure that commercial life placed on the inherited usury doctrine; it acknowledges that liquidity matters and that committing money for a term can impose constraints. But it also exposes the point at which compensation can shade into extraction, and at which an external title can become a general price for time.

In modern language, carentia pecuniae is the scholastic ancestor of the liquidity premium and the time value of money. But unlike modern finance, the scholastic tradition does not begin by assuming that money must grow because time has passed. It asks instead whether the absence of money has created a real, demonstrable, proportionate burden external to the loan itself. Only then can compensation be considered. Without that discipline, carentia pecuniae becomes not a title but a solvent, dissolving the distinction between restorative payment and usurious yield.

sections.title: 32.2 Lessius
paragraphs: Leonardus Lessius stands at one of the most important transition points in the history of scholastic moral finance. By the time Lessius writes, the older medieval prohibition against usury has not disappeared; it remains formally intact. A lender may not simply profit from a mutuum as such. The borrower, having received ownership of the money, owes back the equivalent principal. To demand more merely because money was lent remains morally suspect.

And yet the commercial world around Lessius had become far more complex than the world assumed by the simplest form of the usury prohibition. Merchants needed working capital. Trade required timing. Money could be held, deployed, reserved, pledged, converted, exchanged, or locked into ventures. The problem was no longer only the poor borrower in immediate need, though that remained morally central. The problem was also the merchant, the investor, the city, the charitable institution, and the lender whose money might be tied up in one place when it could have been used elsewhere.

Lessius's importance lies in his taking this commercial complexity seriously without simply abandoning the inherited doctrine. He does not say, in modern fashion, that money automatically has a price through time. Nor does he reduce the old usury doctrine to a primitive misunderstanding. Instead, he works within the scholastic apparatus of titles, contracts, intentions, losses, risks, and compensable burdens. His treatment of carentia pecuniae belongs inside this broader effort to distinguish illicit gain from morally intelligible compensation.

The key move is subtle. The lender, according to this line of thought, may suffer a burden not only because a concrete profit has been lost, but because he has committed money for a definite period and therefore cannot recall it. The lender has accepted a term. The money is unavailable. It cannot be used to meet obligations, respond to new opportunities, protect against uncertainty, or serve other purposes. The burden is not necessarily a proven lost profit in the strict sense of lucrum cessans. It is the condition of being without one's money for the agreed duration.

Lessius is therefore associated with the development of carentia pecuniae as a distinct and dangerous title. The title does not simply say, "I could have earned a profit elsewhere." That would be lucrum cessans, and it would require proof of a concrete alternative. Instead, it says something closer to: "By agreeing not to call this money back for a term, I have accepted a real limitation. I have surrendered liquidity. I have bound myself." The compensable element is not simply the loan, but the term commitment attached to the loan.

This distinction matters because it attempts to separate the price of the loan from the price of the lender's restraint. If the lender could call back the money at any moment, the borrower would receive less security. A fixed term gives the borrower stability, permits planning, and protects against sudden recall. In modern language, the lender has provided not only money, but also a commitment of duration. Lessius's analysis allows the question to be asked: is that commitment itself something for which compensation may be due?

There is a real practical insight here. A term is not nothing. A promise not to demand repayment immediately can have value to the borrower. A lender who locks funds away for a year is in a different position from someone who keeps funds instantly available. Liquidity can matter morally because it matters practically. The ability to respond, pay, invest, rescue, or adapt is part of the real economy of human action. Lessius sees that the deprivation of money can be more than a fiction.

But the moral danger appears at exactly the same point as the insight. If the lender may be compensated simply because the money is unavailable for a term, then the old usury prohibition is placed under severe strain. For every ordinary loan deprives the lender of money for some period. Every term loan involves a commitment. Every borrower receives the benefit of time. If carentia pecuniae is admitted too broadly, then the lender no longer needs to show actual loss, concrete foregone profit, or real exposure of capital to loss. The passage of time plus lack of liquidity becomes enough.

Lessius's treatment therefore belongs to the edge of modern money-market logic. It approaches the idea that time-bound liquidity has a price, and begins to see that money available now and money unavailable until later are not economically identical. But the scholastic question remains: does this difference justify surplus in justice, or does it merely describe the economic desire of the lender?

This is where the older categories still discipline the analysis. A term commitment might be compensable if it produces a real burden external to the bare loan. But it cannot be allowed to become a general permission to charge for time. The lender must not smuggle ordinary interest back into the contract by redescribing every loan as a deprivation of liquidity. The title must remain attached to something morally specific: a concrete commitment, a real limitation, a proportionate burden, and a genuine benefit to the borrower that is distinct from the mere use of the principal.

The distinction is difficult to maintain. In practice, carentia pecuniae can slide toward lucrum cessans, toward damnum emergens, or toward ordinary interest. If the lender says, "I lacked money and therefore had to borrow elsewhere," the claim begins to resemble actual loss. If the lender says, "I lacked money and therefore missed a concrete profitable transaction," the claim begins to resemble foregone profit. If the lender says, "I lacked money and therefore deserve compensation because money over time deserves payment," the claim begins to look like usury.

Lessius's importance is that he forces this ambiguity into the open. He does not allow the moral theologian to pretend that liquidity has no value. But neither does his framework allow the financier to pretend that every inconvenience of capital is automatically a moral title. His analysis reveals the increasingly fine line between compensation and yield, between commercial realism and doctrinal erosion, between a legitimate term commitment and the monetization of time itself.

For modern readers, Lessius is especially important because he helps us see how close late scholastic thought came to the conceptual machinery of modern finance. A lock-up period, a liquidity premium, a commitment fee, a term spread, a standby facility: all of these can be read as descendants of the problem that carentia pecuniae names. The lender gives up flexibility. The borrower receives stability. The question is whether the resulting payment is proportionate compensation for a real burden, or whether it has become a normalized return on money because money was absent for a while.

This is why Lessius should not be read simply as a permissive theologian who opened the door to interest. He should be read as a diagnostician of a changing economy. He saw that the moral world of lending could not be reduced to a single abstract formula. Money could be sterile in one contract and commercially entangled in another. A term could be a disguised price for time, or it could be a real commitment with practical consequences. The task of moral theology was not to bless every surplus, but to ask with precision what the surplus was really for.

In this sense, Lessius brings the usury tradition to a threshold. On one side stands the older rule: surplus from the mutuum itself is usury. On the other side stands modern finance: money has a time value, and therefore the future payment must exceed the present advance. Carentia pecuniae stands in between. It tries to say that being without money can matter, without conceding that time itself is a sufficient title. Whether that middle ground can be held is the question that will trouble later authors, especially Lugo.

sections.title: 32.3 Lugo's Criticism
paragraphs: Juan de Lugo saw the danger clearly. If carentia pecuniae were admitted too broadly, the usury doctrine would be in danger of dissolving. The problem was not that the absence of money could never matter. The problem was that the title could become so general that no loan would remain usurious. Every lender could say, "I am without my money." Every borrower could be charged for the term. Every act of lending could be reframed as a compensable deprivation. What had once been forbidden as gain from the loan itself would return under a more sophisticated name.

Lugo's criticism therefore strikes at the heart of the title. A true extrinsic title must be external to the mutuum. It must name something other than the bare fact that money has been lent and will be repaid later. Damnum emergens can do this when the lender suffers an actual loss. Lucrum cessans can sometimes do this when a concrete, intended, probable gain is interrupted by the loan. Periculum sortis can do this when the lender genuinely bears the risk of losing principal. But carentia pecuniae threatens to name nothing more than the lender's ordinary condition after making a loan: the lender no longer has the money.

That is why Lugo worries that the title is too close to the very thing the doctrine prohibits. In a mutuum, ownership passes to the borrower, who owes an equivalent amount rather than the same coins; the lender therefore cannot charge for the "use" of money as if ownership had remained with him. But carentia pecuniae can easily become a backdoor way of doing exactly that. Instead of saying, "Pay me for using my money," the lender says, "Pay me because I am without my money while you use it." The words are different. The economic substance may be the same.

Lugo's concern is not merely semantic. It is structural. If the title is allowed simply because the lender lacks money during the term, then duration itself becomes monetized. Time becomes the source of yield. The longer the borrower has the money, the more the lender may demand. This is the logic of ordinary interest. The old scholastic question, "What external burden justifies the surplus?", is replaced by the modern financial assumption that money over time should naturally generate more money.

This is why carentia pecuniae is more dangerous than it first appears. Lucrum cessans still requires a story. The lender must say what profit was lost, how it would have been gained, why the loan prevented it, and why the amount claimed is proportionate. Damnum emergens requires evidence of loss. Periculum sortis requires genuine exposure to downside. But carentia pecuniae, if generalized, requires very little. It can be invoked wherever money is absent. The title risks turning the lender's ordinary lack of possession into a universal claim on the borrower.

Lugo's criticism therefore protects the difference between a real title and a pretext. A real title identifies a specific moral reason for compensation. It says: this loss, this delay, this risk, this concrete foregone gain, this labor, this expense. A pretext takes a feature present in nearly every loan and treats it as if it were an exceptional burden. Because every loan deprives the lender of money, deprivation alone cannot be enough. Otherwise the exception has swallowed the rule.

This is also where Lugo's broader synthesis matters. Lugo is not a simple rigorist who refuses all surplus. On the contrary, he is one of the major late scholastic figures who helps organize and expand the theory of extrinsic titles. He understands that modernizing commercial life creates real forms of detriment, sees that risk, lost profit, and actual loss can be connected, and is willing to think broadly about compensable burdens. But precisely because he is analytically sophisticated, he also sees the danger of uncontrolled expansion: a title that explains everything explains too much, and for Lugo the moral question is not whether the lender experiences inconvenience (most human action involves inconvenience) but whether the inconvenience is sufficiently determinate, external, and proportionate to justify charging the borrower. To be without money is not automatically a loss. It may become a loss if the lender must borrow elsewhere, misses a concrete transaction, fails to meet an obligation, or bears some identifiable harm. But then the claim should be analyzed under the more specific titles: actual loss, foregone profit, or perhaps risk. Carentia pecuniae by itself is too vague to carry the moral weight.

This is why Lugo's criticism is so important for the later development of moral finance. He resists the move from "money can be absent in a costly way" to "the absence of money is always costly in a way the borrower must pay for." The first claim may be true in particular circumstances. The second claim effectively baptizes the time value of money. It turns a possible burden into a universal law.

The danger is especially clear when the amount charged is calculated automatically by time and principal. A percentage rate applied to the amount lent, increasing with the duration of the loan, looks less like compensation for a particular burden and more like yield on capital. The form of calculation reveals the moral logic. If the payment rises simply because more time has passed, without any corresponding increase in actual loss, foregone gain, or real risk, then the title has ceased to function as compensation.
