It has become interest under another name.

Lugo's criticism also exposes the problem of double-counting. A lender might claim compensation for actual loss, for foregone profit, for risk of principal, for administrative expense, and then also for carentia pecuniae. But if the deprivation of money is already the reason why a concrete profit was lost, or why a concrete loss was incurred, then charging separately for the abstract lack of money risks counting the same burden twice. Scholastic analysis repeatedly returns to this concern: a title must correspond to a real burden, and the same burden must not be multiplied into several streams of payment.

The deeper issue is that carentia pecuniae changes the imagination of money. In the older account, money lent in a mutuum is a consumable good transferred to the borrower, with repayment of the equivalent owed in justice. Surplus requires some further reason. In the emerging modern account, money is treated as an asset that deserves compensation whenever it is not under the owner's control. The absence of money becomes a kind of injury. But if every temporary alienation of money is an injury, then lending itself is no longer gratuitous in principle. It has become a revenue-generating act by nature.

Lugo's objection is therefore not only technical. It is theological. The usury doctrine was meant to prevent the creditor from transforming the borrower's need into the lender's guaranteed growth. It insisted that not every advantage held by the lender can be monetized; it disciplined the creditor's imagination; it asked whether surplus restored equality or exploited dependency. Carentia pecuniae, if left unchecked, weakens that discipline by making the lender's lack of liquidity the central moral fact, rather than the borrower's vulnerability, the contract's substance, or the actual allocation of risk and loss.

This does not mean that liquidity is morally irrelevant. Lugo's point is subtler. Liquidity matters when its loss produces a real and nameable detriment, when the lender's commitment creates an identifiable burden that justice can measure. But liquidity cannot become a metaphysical entitlement to growth. The lender must not be permitted to say, in effect, "Because my money was not with me, it must return to me increased."

In this respect, Lugo helps preserve the architecture of the entire extrinsic-title tradition. The question is always: what is the surplus for? If it is for actual damage, name the damage. If it is for foregone gain, name the gain. If it is for capital risk, show where the principal can truly be lost. If it is for labor or expense, document the labor or expense. But if it is only for the fact that money was absent over time, then the title has slipped into the very logic the tradition sought to resist.

Lugo's criticism of carentia pecuniae therefore marks a boundary. It is a warning that moral reasoning can become too clever for its own good. The more refined the titles become, the easier it is to use them as instruments of evasion. A doctrine created to distinguish compensation from extraction can itself be captured by extraction if its categories are stretched beyond recognition.

The result is a paradox. Carentia pecuniae arises because the tradition wants to be realistic about liquidity, commitment, and commercial time. Lugo criticizes it because that realism can become indistinguishable from surrender to modern interest. The title names a real pressure on the doctrine, but also reveals the point at which the doctrine may break. If absence of money alone suffices, then the lender no longer needs justice to justify surplus. The clock does the work.
And for Lugo, that is precisely the danger. Once time itself becomes yield, usury has not been defeated. It has simply learned a better vocabulary.

sections.title: 32.4 Modern Analogue
paragraphs: The modern analogue to carentia pecuniae is everywhere. It appears whenever finance speaks of liquidity premium, lock-up, term premium, duration, commitment fees, and the time value of money. It appears whenever a lender or investor says that money unavailable for a period of time deserves compensation simply because it is unavailable. In modern financial theory, this claim is so ordinary that it barely seems to require defense. A euro today is worth more than a euro tomorrow. Capital locked up for five years should earn more than capital available tomorrow. Liquidity has a price. Time has a price. Money has a price through time.

The scholastic tradition does not deny that liquidity matters. That would be foolish. A person with cash on hand is not in the same position as a person whose funds are locked away. A charitable institution with reserves can respond to need; one without reserves cannot. A business with working capital can pay wages, buy materials, and survive shocks; one deprived of liquidity may fail. A fund that commits capital for a long period loses flexibility. A lender who cannot call back money has accepted a constraint. In this practical sense, the absence of money can be real, costly, and morally relevant.

But scholastic moral theology resists the automatic monetization of that absence. It asks a question modern finance often does not: what kind of claim is being made when we say that liquidity has a price? Is the payment compensating a real loss? Is it compensating a concrete foregone use? Is it compensating genuine risk? Is it paying for labor, administration, or a service? Or is it simply rewarding money for being away from its owner for a while?

This is where the older doctrine cuts through the surface language of modern finance. A liquidity premium may be morally intelligible when it corresponds to a concrete burden. A lender may have committed funds in a way that prevents meeting known obligations. A fund may have reserved capital for a borrower and thereby declined other specific mission-aligned deployments. An institution may have taken on real costs to maintain standby liquidity. In such cases, the payment can be analyzed under more specific titles: actual loss, foregone gain, labor, expense, or perhaps risk.

But if the liquidity premium is merely a percentage added because money is locked up for time, the scholastic warning returns. The claim may no longer be restorative. It may have become yield. The lender is not identifying a specific harm, cost, or exposure, but saying that the temporary alienation of money itself deserves increase. This is exactly the logic that carentia pecuniae threatened to introduce.

The same applies to lock-up periods. In modern investment practice, a lock-up is often treated as an obvious basis for higher return. The investor cannot withdraw for a fixed period, and so demands compensation for illiquidity. In equity or partnership-like structures, this may be easier to justify because the investor may genuinely share downside risk. The investor's capital may be lost. The return may depend on the success of the enterprise. The illiquidity is tied to participation and risk-bearing.

But in debt-like structures, the moral question is sharper. If the investor has a fixed claim, seniority, collateral, guarantees, and enforceable repayment regardless of the borrower's success, then the lock-up begins to resemble a term loan. If the return is justified mainly by the fact that money is unavailable for a period, then we are back at carentia pecuniae. The lender may be insulated from downside while still claiming surplus because time has passed. That is the danger.

The modern phrase "time value of money" is perhaps the clearest descendant of the problem. In contemporary finance, it functions as a foundational axiom: money now is worth more than money later, because present money can be invested, consumed, or used. Discounting, valuation, interest rates, net present value, and investment decisions all depend upon it. It is one of the basic grammars of modern financial rationality.

From a scholastic perspective, however, the time value of money is not a neutral axiom. It is a moral claim disguised as a technical one. It says that time, attached to money, generates a legitimate claim to increase. The tradition would want to slow that claim down. It would ask: whose time? Whose money? Whose need? What risk? What loss? What concrete alternative? What social relationship? What vulnerability? What kind of contract? What kind of power?

This does not mean that all discounting is immoral, or that every modern financial calculation is usurious. It means that calculation is not justification. Mathematics can describe relative values across time. It cannot, by itself, tell us whether a surplus claim is just. A discounted cash flow model may tell an investor what return would satisfy a portfolio target. It does not tell us whether the borrower should morally bear that cost. A liquidity premium may tell us what the market demands. It does not tell us whether the premium corresponds to compensation rather than extraction.

This distinction is crucial for non-extractive finance. A non-extractive capital structure cannot simply import the modern logic of time value and then decorate it with mission language. It must ask whether the payment above principal is tied to a real moral title. If the investor bears genuine downside risk, a risk-sharing return may be justified. If the investor contributes labor, governance, mentoring, stewardship, or administration, some compensation may be justified. If the investor suffers an actual, documented loss because funds are committed, that may be compensable. If a concrete mission-aligned opportunity was foregone, that may require careful analysis. But a generic claim that "capital locked up for five years deserves X percent" is not enough.

This also helps us distinguish patient capital from merely patient extraction. Patient capital sounds morally attractive because it does not demand immediate repayment. It may give the enterprise room to breathe, avoid short-term pressure, and support mission, work, and community. But patience alone does not make capital non-extractive. If patience is priced as a guaranteed premium, secured by senior claims, enforced through control rights, and insulated from downside, then the capital may still dominate. It has only extended the timeline of extraction.

By contrast, genuinely patient non-extractive capital should share vulnerability. It should be willing to wait without turning time itself into a meter that charges the borrower, should accept that mission, labor, community, and enterprise survival may come before capital's growth, and should seek restoration, participation, and stewardship rather than automatic yield. Where returns exist, they should be tied to real surplus created without crushing the conditions that made that surplus possible.

The older title of carentia pecuniae therefore gives us a diagnostic tool for modern finance. Whenever we encounter a charge for lock-up, illiquidity, duration, or time value, we can ask: is this payment for a real burden, or simply for the absence of money? Is the investor being compensated for a specific loss, concrete foregone use, genuine risk, or actual service? Or is capital claiming growth because the clock has been running?

This question becomes especially important in venture capital, private equity, infrastructure finance, and debt funds. These structures often combine long lock-ups, return targets, priority repayment, management fees, monitoring fees, late fees, liquidation preferences, and risk premiums. Each element may have a plausible explanation when viewed alone. Together, however, they can produce double-counting. The investor may be compensated for risk while shifting risk away; compensated for illiquidity while also receiving control; compensated for foregone opportunity while also receiving priority; compensated for labor through fees while also demanding high returns. The scholastic tradition would insist on disaggregation. Name the title. Prove the burden. Prevent duplication. Preserve the borrower and the mission from domination.

In this sense, carentia pecuniae is not merely an obscure scholastic curiosity. It is the hinge between older moral theology and modern financial common sense. It shows how the question "May I be compensated for being without my money?" can become the much larger claim "Money over time deserves increase." It reveals how quickly compensation becomes yield when time itself is treated as a title.

The tradition's answer is not to deny time, liquidity, or commercial complexity. Its answer is to refuse automaticity. Time may be relevant, but time alone is not enough. Illiquidity may matter, but illiquidity must be connected to a real burden. Lock-up may justify compensation in some cases, but only when the compensation is proportionate and not already covered by another title. The absence of money may cause harm, but the harm must be named.

This is the lasting value of the scholastic resistance to carentia pecuniae. It protects us from treating money as if it possesses a natural right to grow merely because it has been away from home. It reminds us that finance is not morally purified by duration, sophistication, or market convention. The moral test remains the same: does the payment restore equality, compensate real burden, share risk, and serve life? Or does it convert time into a machine for extracting surplus?

Modern finance tends to answer that money has a price through time. The older tradition asks a more human question: what happened during that time, who bore the burden, and why should another person have to pay for it?

chapters.part: Part IX — Adjacent Titles, Institutions, and Practical Moral Finance

chapters.title: Exchange, Bills, Public Debt, and Disguised Loans
chapters.label: Chapter 33

sections.title: 33.1 Foreign Exchange and Scholastic Analysis
paragraphs: Foreign exchange forced scholastic moral theology to confront one of the hardest problems in the usury tradition: not every payment above principal occurs inside an obvious loan. Some transactions look like exchange, some like sale, some like transfer, some like credit. Some are genuinely commercial instruments. Others are loans in disguise. The moral task is not merely to ask whether money has increased, but to ask what kind of contract produced the increase.

This is why the scholastic analysis of foreign exchange is so important. A simple mutuum is relatively easy to describe: one person lends money or another fungible good, ownership passes to the borrower, and the borrower owes the equivalent amount in return. Surplus from the loan itself is usury unless an extrinsic title can be shown. But foreign exchange complicates the picture. Money is not merely lent and returned; it is exchanged across currencies, cities, jurisdictions, dates, and payment networks. The object of the contract may be not only money, but money in another place, money in another currency, money payable at another fair, or money transferred through a bill.

This complexity created room for legitimate commerce and for moral abuse. A merchant in Bruges might need funds payable in Venice. A banker in Florence might issue a bill payable in Lyon. A trader might exchange local currency for foreign currency, not merely as a speculative operation, but because commerce required settlement across distance. Such transactions involved real services: transfer, accounting, correspondence, credibility, risk, and knowledge of exchange rates. They also involved real market differences: money in one place was not always equivalent to money in another. Currency, availability, political conditions, distance, and timing all mattered.

For this reason, scholastic theologians and canonists did not simply condemn all exchange profit as usury. Exchange could be legitimate: a money changer could perform useful work, and a merchant banker could facilitate trade. A bill of exchange could avoid the dangers and costs of physically transporting coin. Differences in currency and place could justify differences in price. If one thing is truly being exchanged for another (florins for ducats, local money for foreign money, present payment here for payment there), then the analysis belongs at least partly to the moral theology of exchange and just price, not simply to the theology of the mutuum.

But the same instruments could also be used to hide a loan. This was the recurring danger. A bill of exchange could be genuine or fictitious. A currency difference could be real or invented. A payment in another place could correspond to actual trade, or it could be a legal fiction designed to generate a return. The contract might say "exchange," while the substance says "loan with interest." Scholastic analysis therefore had to look beneath the name of the instrument to the reality of the transaction.

Raymond de Roover's work is especially helpful here because he showed how sophisticated medieval and early modern finance had become, and how deeply the usury debate was entangled with foreign exchange. The medieval bill of exchange was not a marginal instrument but central to international commerce. It allowed funds to be moved between trading centers without shipping specie, and it supported fairs, banking networks, merchants, and ecclesiastical payments. It made possible a world in which commerce crossed political and monetary boundaries.

Yet precisely because the bill of exchange could combine exchange, credit, and time, it became morally ambiguous. A normal exchange contract might involve payment in one place and repayment in another, with a difference reflecting exchange rates and costs. But when the same parties used exchange and re-exchange to create a guaranteed gain over time, the instrument could become a disguised interest-bearing loan. The famous problem was not merely exchange, but dry exchange: exchange without real commercial substance, exchange that existed only on paper, exchange used to cloak lending at interest.

The scholastic question was therefore: is there a real exchange? Are different currencies involved? Are different places involved? Is there actual transfer? Is the rate justifiable by market conditions, risk, cost, or service? Or is the entire structure a circular route by which one party advances money and receives more money later with no real exchange taking place?

This is the contract-classification problem in one of its most sophisticated forms. The name of the contract cannot settle the matter. Calling something exchange does not make it exchange. Calling something sale does not make it sale. Calling something partnership does not make it partnership. The moral form may differ from the legal form. Scholastic theology therefore asks what the contract does. Who gives what? Who receives what? Who bears risk? Who can lose? Who must repay? What is being priced? What justifies the surplus?

Foreign exchange also illuminates the difference between just profit and usurious gain. A money changer may profit from labor, skill, risk, and service. A merchant banker may charge for the difficulty of transferring funds across distance. Exchange rates may vary because money in one place is not identical in practical value to money in another. These are not necessarily usury; they may correspond to real differences in value and real work performed. But when the difference in price is merely a way to guarantee surplus on a loan, the moral character changes.

The issue becomes even sharper when time enters the transaction. A bill payable later is not the same as payment now. Modern finance immediately sees the time element and reaches for discounting. The scholastic tradition is more cautious. A deferred payment may involve risk, cost, and exchange conditions. But the fact that payment occurs later does not automatically justify a surplus. Once again, time by itself is not enough. The analysis must identify the title: exchange difference, labor, risk, actual loss, foregone gain, or some other real burden. Without such a title, the time element risks becoming ordinary interest hidden inside an exchange contract.

This helps explain why bills of exchange became such a contested field. They were not simply loopholes but necessary commercial instruments in an economy that had outgrown purely local cash transactions. But they were also morally dangerous because they allowed the economic substance of lending to be wrapped in the legal form of exchange. The more sophisticated the instrument, the greater the need for moral diagnosis.

The late medieval and early modern theologians were therefore not naive opponents of finance. They understood that commerce required instruments, networks, and specialized services, and they understood that finance could become a language of concealment. The same contract could support trade or evade moral law. The difference could not always be seen from the surface. It required attention to intention, structure, risk, pricing, and economic reality.

For a modern reader, this is directly relevant. Contemporary finance is filled with instruments whose legal form and moral substance may diverge. Derivatives, convertible notes, preferred equity, revenue-based finance, management fees, payment-in-kind interest, sale-leasebacks, factoring, and structured credit can all be legitimate in some forms and extractive in others. The lesson from scholastic foreign exchange analysis is not that complex instruments are automatically corrupt. The lesson is that complexity increases the need for moral clarity.

The key question is always whether the surplus corresponds to something real. Is there actual exchange? Actual service? Actual risk? Actual loss? Actual partnership? Actual transfer across place and currency? Or is the complexity merely ornamental: a technical costume placed over a simple demand for guaranteed increase?

Foreign exchange thus belongs in this book not as a side topic, but as a crucial bridge. It shows how the usury doctrine moved from simple lending cases into the complexity of commercial finance. It reveals the scholastic capacity to distinguish real economic functions from disguised extraction. It also shows why moral theology cannot stop at slogans. "Interest is forbidden" is too crude if it cannot distinguish a genuine exchange contract from a loan. But "all finance is legitimate if legally structured" is equally crude if it cannot see when exchange has become a mask for usury.

The scholastic tradition learned to ask a better question: what is the transaction really? In foreign exchange, that question became unavoidable. A bill could be a bridge between cities, currencies, and merchants. Or it could be a mirror maze in which a loan returned disguised as exchange. The moral theologian's task was to tell the difference.

sections.title: 33.2 Public Debt and Forced Loans
paragraphs: Public debt created another major pressure point for the scholastic usury tradition. The ordinary usury case imagined a private lender and a private borrower. One person advanced money, another received it, and the borrower owed the equivalent principal. Any surplus demanded from the loan itself required moral scrutiny. But public debt complicated this picture. Cities, princes, republics, and public authorities needed funds for war, infrastructure, famine relief, administration, defense, and political survival. They borrowed, taxed, compelled, delayed, consolidated, and promised future payments. The line between loan, tax, investment, annuity, and civic obligation became difficult to draw.

The medieval and early modern city-state was not a modern sovereign with a developed bond market, central bank, and standardized public securities. It was often a fragile political community, dependent on merchants, guilds, noble families, ecclesiastical institutions, and wealthy households. When urgent funds were needed, the city could not always rely on voluntary gifts or ordinary taxation. It might impose forced loans. Citizens were required to advance money to the public treasury, sometimes in proportion to assessed wealth, sometimes with a promise of future repayment, sometimes with periodic payments attached.

Such forced loans were morally ambiguous from the beginning. If the payment was truly a tax, the citizen was not lending in the ordinary sense: the public authority was collecting what it had the right to collect for the common good. If the payment was truly a loan, the citizen retained a claim to repayment. If the city promised additional payments, the theologian had to ask whether these were compensation, annuity income, public obligation, disguised interest, or something else. The same instrument could look like taxation from one angle and lending from another.

Municipal obligations sharpened the problem. A city might owe money to citizens or outside creditors and promise a regular payment. That payment might be understood as compensation for funds advanced, as a share in public revenues, as an annuity purchased from the city, or as interest on debt. The vocabulary could shift, the structure could shift, and the moral interpretation could shift with it.

The older categories did not disappear, but they became harder to apply. Was the citizen a lender, a taxpayer, a participant in public finance, or a purchaser of a revenue stream? Did ownership of money pass in a mutuum? Was repayment of principal guaranteed? Did the creditor bear risk that the city might fail? Was the payment tied to actual public revenues? Was the arrangement voluntary or compelled? Did the payment compensate loss, reward risk, sustain public necessity, or simply provide a return on money?

Public finance therefore forced theologians to ask whether the identity of the borrower mattered. Lending to a poor neighbor raised one set of concerns, lending to a merchant another, lending to a city still another. A city was not merely an individual debtor. It represented, at least in principle, a political community. Its debts could be linked to defense, famine relief, roads, bridges, hospitals, walls, or civic order. The creditor might be supporting the common good. But the city could also become a machinery of coercion, extracting from citizens, favoring elites, and normalizing public rent payments to the wealthy.

This double possibility is crucial. Public debt can serve the common good, but it can also create a creditor class with claims on the future labor of the community. A city under permanent debt may find its revenues captured before they reach the poor, workers, artisans, or public goods. Taxes collected from many may flow to a few. What begins as emergency finance can become a structure of dependency. The political community may become organized around servicing claims held by those with surplus capital.

The scholastic tradition had to nuance doctrine because public debt did not fit neatly into the simplest private-lending model. Some forms of public revenue purchase could be treated more like annuities than loans. Some forced loans were closer to taxation. Some municipal obligations involved real risk. Some payments could be justified by public necessity. But this complexity did not mean that public debt was automatically morally clean; on the contrary, it required sharper analysis.

Forced loans are particularly revealing because they invert the usual power relation. In ordinary usury, the creditor often has power over the needy borrower. In a forced loan, the public authority has power over the lender. A wealthy citizen may be compelled to advance money to the city. The moral anxiety shifts: is the city unjustly taking private property, or is it legitimately mobilizing resources for the common good? If the city later pays something above principal, is that illicit interest, lawful compensation, or a political settlement for compelled deprivation?

Here again the extrinsic titles become useful. A citizen compelled to lend may suffer damnum emergens if the forced advance causes actual loss. A merchant may lose a concrete commercial opportunity, raising a possible lucrum cessans question. If repayment is uncertain, some form of periculum sortis may be present. If the citizen is deprived of liquidity for a fixed term, the dangerous logic of carentia pecuniae may appear. But none of these titles can be assumed automatically. They must be named, limited, and tested.

Public debt also raises the question of proportionality. Even if compensation is justified, how much is enough? A city may owe restitution for harm caused by forced deprivation. It may need to compensate risk, and to maintain credibility so that future emergency financing remains possible. But if public payments become excessive, perpetual, or transferable in ways that create speculative markets, then compensation can mutate into rent extraction. The community's future revenues become a tradable asset. Public necessity becomes private income.

This was not merely a theoretical possibility. Late medieval and early modern public finance often generated complex markets in civic obligations, annuities, and debt claims. The line between supporting the city and profiting from the city could become thin. A wealthy investor might present himself as a servant of public need while in practice receiving a secure flow of payments backed by taxes. The risk might be socialized, while the income was privatized. The city might borrow for war or crisis, and generations of ordinary people might pay.

The moral problem is therefore not solved by saying "public purpose." A public purpose can justify extraordinary measures, but it can also become a cloak for injustice. War finance, especially, complicates the matter. A city may claim necessity, but the justice of the cause matters. A loan used to defend the vulnerable is not morally identical to a loan used for aggression, vanity, conquest, or dynastic ambition. The scholastic concern for justice cannot stop at the financial instrument; it must also ask what the instrument serves.

At the same time, public debt reveals why an overly simplistic anti-usury doctrine could not suffice. Cities needed mechanisms for mobilizing resources over time; public goods required funding; emergencies could not always be met by immediate taxation; institutions needed stability. Moral theology had to distinguish between predatory lending and legitimate public finance, between usurious gain and civic participation, between forced extraction and shared burden for the common good.

This is why contract classification became so important. A municipal obligation might be a loan, an annuity, a tax refund, a revenue assignment, or a forced contribution with later compensation. Each structure carried different moral implications. The theologian had to ask not only what the document said, but what economic reality it created.

Public debt also exposes the social dimension of usury doctrine. The issue is not only whether one lender sins against one borrower, but whether a community's financial architecture directs surplus toward restoration or extraction. When public finance burdens the many for the secure enrichment of the few, it recreates at civic scale the very domination that the usury doctrine sought to restrain. When public finance mobilizes resources for genuine common need, shares burdens proportionately, protects the vulnerable, and avoids permanent rent extraction, it may serve justice.

The modern relevance is obvious. Public debt today is usually treated as a technical matter of fiscal policy, bond markets, yields, credit ratings, and monetary management. But the older moral questions remain. Who lends to the state? Who pays the interest? Who benefits from public borrowing? What purposes are financed? Are future taxpayers being burdened for present speculation, war, or elite rescue? Are bondholders bearing real risk, or are they protected while austerity is imposed on the vulnerable? Does public debt serve the common good, or does it convert the political community into a revenue stream?

Forced loans may seem distant from modern life, but their moral structure persists. Pension funds, banks, sovereign bondholders, development lenders, central banks, and private creditors all participate in systems where public obligation becomes financial asset. The question is not whether such systems can exist, but whether their claims are ordered toward justice, proportionality, and public purpose, or toward guaranteed extraction from the future.

The scholastic tradition gives us no simple formula for public debt. It gives us something more demanding: a set of questions. Is this really a loan? Is it a tax? Is it an annuity? Is the payment compensation or yield? Who bears risk? Who is compelled? Who benefits? What public good is served? Does the structure protect the vulnerable, or does it mortgage them?

Public debt forced theologians to nuance usury doctrine because the city itself could become both borrower and creditor, servant and exploiter, common good and financial machine. The old moral distinction remained: surplus can restore, participate, or dominate. Public finance is just only when it serves the first two and resists the third.

sections.title: 33.3 Lex Commissoria, Gratis Dans, Socii, Pompa
paragraphs: Medieval authors often tried to summarize complicated moral distinctions in compact lists. These lists were not merely memory devices but tools for confessors, canonists, theologians, and legal practitioners who had to decide whether a payment above principal was usurious or whether it arose from some other legitimate title. The verse associated with Hostiensis is one of the most famous examples: Poena nec in fraudem, lex commissoria, gratis / Dans, socii, pompa; plus sorte modis datur istis.

The meaning, in rough form, is that more than the principal may be received in certain modes: a penalty not made in fraud of the usury prohibition; a lex commissoria; something freely given; partnership; and pompa, often associated with the additional value or cost attached to pomp, ceremony, or expense. The list is compressed, technical, and easy to misunderstand. But it reveals a crucial feature of the medieval usury tradition: the prohibition on usury did not mean that every surplus above principal was automatically illicit. It meant that surplus had to be classified.

The first phrase, poena nec in fraudem, we have already encountered. A penalty may be valid if it is not a fraud on the usury prohibition. The point is not that a creditor may simply attach a penalty and thereby legalize interest. Quite the opposite. The phrase itself contains the warning: the penalty must not be in fraudem, not a trick, not a disguise, not an engineered substitute for yield. A true penalty punishes or compensates wrongful non-performance; a false penalty prices time. The due date must be realistic, the penalty must be avoidable, the lender must prefer repayment on time, and the amount must be proportionate to the injury caused by delay.

The lex commissoria is more difficult because it appears in several legal contexts. Broadly, it refers to a contractual clause by which some consequence follows if a party fails to perform. In sale, pledge, or other arrangements, it could involve forfeiture, cancellation, or transfer of rights on default. Its presence in exception lists shows that medieval jurists recognized that certain contractual consequences might give one party more than the initial principal or equivalent amount, not because money as money had generated offspring, but because a condition had been violated or a contractual structure had changed.

Yet here too the danger is obvious. A forfeiture clause can become oppressive. If a borrower pledges an asset worth far more than the debt, and the creditor takes the whole asset upon default, the clause may become a tool of extraction. A lex commissoria may be formally agreed and still morally abusive. The scholastic instinct is therefore not to bless the clause merely because it has legal form, but to examine proportionality, intention, vulnerability, and substance. Does the clause compensate real harm or enforce a legitimate contractual consequence? Or does it allow the creditor to profit from another's inability to perform?

The phrase gratis dans points in another direction: the person who gives freely. A borrower may voluntarily give something beyond principal out of gratitude, charity, friendship, or honor. Such a gift is not usury if it is genuinely free and not expected, demanded, stipulated, coerced, or built into the transaction from the beginning. The moral difference lies in freedom. A gift that is required is not a gift. A "customary" gift that the lender expects as the price of lending may become usurious. A donation that the borrower feels socially or economically compelled to make may not be truly voluntary.

This distinction was pastorally important because real communities often operated through gifts, favors, patronage, gratitude, and social expectation. The problem was not gratitude itself (gratitude can be beautiful, and a borrower who has been helped may naturally want to thank the lender). The problem arises when gratitude becomes a disguised obligation, especially when the lender's willingness to lend depends on receiving it. The tradition's suspicion here is psychologically subtle: people can demand without formally demanding; they can expect without writing the expectation down; they can use social pressure to turn gift into payment.

The category socii refers to partners. This is one of the most important exceptions because it leads directly back to the distinction between mutuum and societas. In a partnership, the capital provider is not merely a lender with a guaranteed claim to repayment. The capital remains exposed to the fortunes of the venture. Gains may be shared because losses are shared. Profit is not demanded simply because money was advanced; it arises from participation in a common undertaking. The partner may receive more than principal because the partner bears risk and participates in the productive activity.

This is the logic that Aquinas had already recognized when he distinguished lending from entrusting money to a merchant by way of partnership. In the loan, the borrower owns the money and owes the equivalent; in the partnership, the investor's capital remains at risk, and the investor may share in profit because he may also share in loss. The socii category therefore shows that medieval theology was not anti-investment. It was anti-guaranteed gain from a consumptive loan. Partnership profit could be licit where the structure truly involved shared venture, shared uncertainty, and shared downside.

But partnership too could be faked. A contract may be called societas while giving the capital provider guaranteed repayment, priority over everyone else, collateral, control, and a fixed return. In such a case, the name "partner" may conceal the substance of a lender. The scholastic method therefore again asks about reality. Can the capital provider lose principal? Is repayment contingent on the success of the venture? Are gains and losses actually shared? Does the active partner bear all the vulnerability while the capital partner receives protected income? If so, the partnership form may be a mask.

Finally, pompa is the strangest term in the list. It is often connected to the costs, ceremony, display, or circumstances surrounding certain transactions. In practical terms, it points toward cases where additional payment may correspond to expense, labor, public burden, or the particular conditions under which the transaction occurs. It belongs near the family of adjacent titles such as stipendium laboris and expense reimbursement. The point is that some surplus may compensate something real that accompanies the transaction without being a price for the loan itself.

The importance of pompa is not the specific word alone, but the logic it represents. Medieval writers knew that transactions do not occur in abstraction. They involve messengers, scribes, notaries, witnesses, storage, travel, risk, ceremony, administration, and public form. A payment above principal may sometimes cover such real costs. The Monti di Pietà debate later turns on precisely this kind of reasoning: charitable lending to the poor requires staff, buildings, accounting, pledge management, and administration. Reimbursement for such costs is not the same thing as usury, provided it remains proportional and does not become profit extraction.

These lists could clarify moral reasoning, but they could also obscure it. Their advantage was that they prevented crude absolutism, reminding confessors that "more than principal" is not automatically the same as usury. A penalty, a forfeiture clause, a free gift, a partnership, or reimbursement of expense might each explain why surplus appears. The list preserved the analytical flexibility needed for real life.

Their danger was that they could be treated as loophole catalogues. A clever lender could search the list for a label and attach it to a transaction. If a penalty works, call it penalty. If a gift works, call it gift. If partnership works, call it partnership. If expense works, call it cost recovery. The moral theologian's task, therefore, was not only to know the categories but to test them. The title must be real, must correspond to the substance of the transaction, and must not be used in fraudem, in fraud of the prohibition.

This is why the exception-list tradition is so useful for reconstructing the moral logic of the extrinsic titles. It shows that scholastic usury doctrine was never simply a blunt rule mechanically applied, but a disciplined practice of classification. The rule against usury created the starting point: surplus from a mutuum as such is illicit. The lists then identified circumstances in which surplus might arise from something else: penalty, loss, risk, partnership, gift, labor, expense, exchange, or contractual condition.

But the existence of a category never ended the analysis. It began it. The real question was always whether the category described the transaction truthfully. A penalty must be penal, not revenue. A gift must be free, not expected. A partnership must share risk, not merely rename debt. An expense charge must reimburse costs, not generate institutional profit. A forfeiture clause must be proportionate, not predatory. A legal form must not conceal a moral lie.

For modern non-extractive finance, the lesson is immediate. Our own contracts are full of labels: management fee, commitment fee, origination fee, monitoring fee, preferred return, liquidation preference, default interest, advisory fee, carried interest, success fee, redemption premium, downside protection. Each label may name something legitimate. Each may also hide extraction. A management fee may reimburse labor, or it may become rent. A default fee may compensate delay, or it may make default profitable. A preferred return may reflect risk-sharing, or it may guarantee capital's priority over mission. A "gift" to investors may be structurally coerced. A "partnership" may leave founders and workers bearing all the real risk.

The medieval exception lists therefore train us in suspicion without cynicism. They do not tell us to reject every financial instrument; they tell us to ask what the instrument really does, teach us to distrust both moral laziness and legal cleverness, and insist that justice cannot be reduced to the name of a clause.

In that sense, lex commissoria, gratis dans, socii, and pompa are not antiquarian curiosities. They are early forms of a moral audit vocabulary. They ask whether surplus arises from compensation, participation, service, or domination. They remind us that finance is often most dangerous not when it openly violates moral law, but when it wears moral language over extractive substance.

The old verse-list is therefore both helpful and hazardous. Helpful, because it preserves the memory that not every surplus is usury. Hazardous, because every listed exception can become a tool of evasion. The tradition's wisdom lies in holding both truths together: moral finance requires categories, but categories must be tested against reality.

sections.title: 33.4 The Contract-Classification Problem
paragraphs: The whole usury tradition depends on a deceptively simple question: what kind of contract is this?

That question sounds legal, but for the scholastics it was also moral and theological. The name of a contract did not merely determine which rule applied. It revealed, or concealed, the real structure of justice between the parties. A mutuum created one moral relation, a sale another, a partnership another. A lease, deposit, insurance-like arrangement, exchange contract, pledge, annuity, or penalty clause each carried its own moral logic. To misclassify the contract was therefore not a harmless technical error. It could turn extraction into apparent legitimacy.

The simplest case was the mutuum. In a loan for consumption, ownership of money or another fungible good passed to the borrower. The borrower did not owe the same physical object, but an equivalent amount of the same kind and quality. Because the thing was consumed in its use, the lender could not separately sell both the thing and its use. This is why surplus from the loan itself was condemned as usury. The lender's intrinsic claim was to the equivalent principal. Anything more required some external title.

But real economic life rarely stayed simple. A transaction might look like a sale while functioning like credit. It might look like a partnership while protecting one party from loss. It might look like a lease while transferring economic ownership. It might look like insurance while guaranteeing return. It might look like exchange while concealing a loan. The scholastic tradition therefore had to develop a habit of looking beyond formal labels to economic substance.

The first question was: is this a sale? In a sale, one thing is exchanged for another at a just price: the seller gives up ownership of a good, and the buyer pays a price. Profit may be legitimate when it compensates labor, transport, risk, improvement, market service, or the needs of household and community. But a sale can hide a loan. A seller may raise the price merely because payment is delayed, turning the extra amount into a disguised charge for time. Aquinas saw this danger clearly. To charge more solely because the buyer will pay later can have the character of a loan with usury, unless some real difference in value, risk, cost, or loss is present.

The second question was: is this a loan? If the transaction is truly a mutuum, then the old rule applies with full force. The borrower receives ownership and owes the equivalent principal. Surplus requires an extrinsic title: actual loss, culpable delay, concrete foregone gain, genuine risk of principal, labor, expense, or some other real burden. The lender cannot simply say that money was used, or that time passed, or that money could have earned elsewhere in the abstract. The title must be external to the loan and proportionate to the burden.

The third question was: is this partnership? Partnership changes the moral structure because the capital provider does not merely stand outside the venture as creditor. The investor participates in the undertaking; gains and losses are shared; capital remains exposed. Profit can be legitimate because the partner bears downside and contributes to a common enterprise. But here the danger of misclassification is acute. If the supposed partner has guaranteed repayment, seniority, collateral, fixed return, and the power to enforce against the active partner's assets regardless of outcome, then the "partnership" may be debt in disguise. The name socius does not make a person a partner if the structure leaves others bearing all real vulnerability.

The fourth question was: is this insurance or risk transfer? Some contracts concern the bearing of risk. Maritime contracts, insurance-like arrangements, guarantees, and risk-sharing agreements can involve legitimate payment because one party assumes uncertainty that another party wishes to avoid. But risk language can also be abused. A lender may charge a risk premium while shifting nearly all downside to the borrower through collateral, guarantees, covenants, priority, or enforcement rights. In that case, the payment is not really compensation for risk borne. It is yield protected by legal power.

The fifth question was: is this exchange? Foreign exchange, bills of exchange, and money-changing contracts created some of the most sophisticated classification problems in medieval and early modern finance. If one currency is exchanged for another, or if payment is made in one city for settlement in another, differences in price may be legitimate. Distance, currency, availability, risk, transfer service, and market conditions can matter. But exchange can also become a disguise for lending at interest. If the currency difference is fictitious, if the place difference is merely formal, or if exchange and re-exchange are arranged only to guarantee a gain over time, the contract may be a mutuum wearing the clothes of exchange.

The sixth question was: is this a disguised mutuum? This is the question that haunts all the others. A disguised mutuum exists when the legal form differs from the moral substance. The documents may describe sale, partnership, exchange, lease, fee, penalty, or revenue participation. But if one party advances money, the other party is bound to repay the equivalent or more, the capital provider is protected from downside, and the surplus arises because of time, need, or bargaining power, then the structure may be a loan with usury hidden inside it.

This contract-classification problem shows why the scholastic tradition cannot be reduced to a simple anti-interest slogan. The theologians and canonists were not merely asking whether the final amount exceeded the initial amount. They were asking why: what kind of moral relation had been created, and whether surplus came from sale, service, risk, partnership, loss, delay, exchange, or domination.
The method requires several layers of analysis.

First, identify ownership. Who owns the money or asset during the transaction? Has ownership passed to the recipient, as in a mutuum? Has it remained with the provider, as in a lease or certain investment structures? Is ownership shared? Is it conditional? Is it held in trust? Ownership determines who may claim use, profit, and risk.

Second, identify risk. Who can lose principal? Who bears failure? Who is protected by collateral, seniority, guarantees, or public backstop? A person who claims partnership profit while refusing partnership loss is not acting as a true partner. A person who claims risk premium while shifting risk onto the borrower is not truly being compensated for risk borne.

Third, identify the source of surplus. Is the surplus tied to actual loss? To labor? To expense? To delay? To foregone gain? To real exchange difference? To risk? Or is it simply attached to the fact that money was advanced and later returned? The same numerical surplus may be moral or immoral depending on its source.

Fourth, identify proportionality. Even when a legitimate title exists, it cannot justify unlimited payment. A late penalty must correspond to the harm of delay. Cost reimbursement must correspond to actual costs. Risk compensation must correspond to actual exposure. Foregone profit must be concrete and reasonably valued. The title limits the claim.

Fifth, identify intention and incentive. Does the lender want repayment on time, or profit from default? Does the investor want the enterprise to flourish, or merely to trigger control rights? Does the contract reward cooperation, or does it reward failure, distress, and dependency? Duns Scotus's question about late penalties belongs here: would the lender rather be repaid on time? If not, the structure itself reveals moral disorder.

Sixth, identify vulnerability. Is the borrower poor, distressed, dependent, uninformed, or lacking alternatives? Is the counterparty a merchant, a city, a household, a worker cooperative, a startup, a monastery, or a desperate family? Scholastic analysis is not blind to context. The same formal instrument can have different moral weight depending on need, power, knowledge, and bargaining conditions.

Seventh, identify whether the contract serves a good beyond capital's increase. Does it support trade, household stability, public necessity, charitable lending, mission, work, community, or stewardship? Or does it merely organize a claim by which money becomes more money while others bear the fragility of real life?

These questions are especially important because sophisticated finance often multiplies forms. A single modern financing arrangement may contain debt, warrants, preferred equity, liquidation preferences, management fees, monitoring fees, default interest, covenants, board control, redemption rights, anti-dilution provisions, and exit obligations. Each feature may be defensible when viewed alone. Together, they may create a structure in which capital receives priority, control, upside, downside protection, liquidity rights, and penalties, while founders, workers, suppliers, mission, and community absorb the real risk.

The scholastic contract-classification problem therefore becomes a modern moral audit. What is this instrument really? Is it called equity but behaving like debt? Is it called partnership but structured as domination? Is it called a fee but functioning as yield? Is it called risk-sharing while legally transferring downside to someone else? Is it called patient capital while silently metering time? Is it called mission investment while extracting from the mission's future?

The point is not that legal classification is irrelevant. Legal form matters; law structures rights, remedies, enforceability, duties, and expectations. But legal form is not morally sufficient. A contract can be enforceable and still unjust. It can be standard market practice and still extractive, beautifully drafted and still a misrepresentation of the moral substance of the relationship.

This is why the older tradition insisted on substance over label. The category mutuum was not merely a Roman legal artifact. It was a way of seeing when one party had transferred consumable value and retained only a claim to equivalent repayment. The category societas was not merely a business form. It was a way of seeing shared enterprise and shared risk. The category interesse was not modern interest. It was compensation for a real difference created by loss. The categories mattered because they helped name the moral reality.

But categories can also lie. They lie when they are used as costumes. They lie when a penalty is expected revenue, when a gift is coerced, when a partnership guarantees one partner, when exchange is fictitious, when risk is shifted, when fees become return, when time becomes yield, when the borrower's need becomes the lender's opportunity.

The contract-classification problem is therefore not a technical appendix to usury doctrine. It is the central discipline that keeps the doctrine honest. Without classification, every surplus is suspect; with lazy classification, every surplus can be excused, and only careful classification can distinguish compensation from extraction.

This is the bridge from scholastic theology to non-extractive finance. A modern financing contract must be read not only by lawyers, but by moral accountants. It must be examined for ownership, risk, surplus, intention, proportionality, vulnerability, and mission. The question is not simply, "What is legally permitted?" The question is, "What relationship of justice does this instrument create?"

That is the question the scholastics kept asking in different forms: is this a sale, a loan, a partnership, insurance, exchange, or a disguised mutuum? The list may sound medieval. The problem is not. It is the problem of every age in which capital learns to rename its claims.

chapters.part: Part IX — Adjacent Titles, Institutions, and Practical Moral Finance

chapters.title: From Hostiensis to Salamanca: A Compressed Intellectual Genealogy
chapters.label: Chapter 34

sections.title: 34.1 Early Canonists and Confessors
paragraphs: The history of the extrinsic titles did not begin as a neat academic theory. It began in the tangled world of confession, canon law, pastoral judgment, and concrete cases. Before the late scholastics built elaborate systems, and before Salamanca and the Jesuits refined the analysis into a sophisticated moral technology, there were confessors and canonists trying to answer practical questions: may this creditor receive more than principal? Is this penalty legitimate? Is this gift truly free? Is this exchange real? Has the borrower been exploited? Must restitution be made?

The early canonists and confessors mattered because they stood at the crossing point between biblical prohibition, Roman legal categories, ecclesial discipline, and ordinary economic life. Scripture had condemned lending to the poor at increase, the hardening of the heart against the needy, and the transformation of the vulnerable neighbor into a revenue stream. Roman law had supplied the technical category of the mutuum, in which ownership of a consumable good passed to the borrower and the borrower owed back an equivalent. The Church inherited both: the moral vision of covenantal debt restraint and the legal anatomy of consumptive lending.

But pastoral practice required more than repeating prohibitions. Confessors had to guide souls, canonists had to interpret rules, and bishops and ecclesiastical courts had to distinguish genuine wrongdoing from legitimate compensation. Merchants, landowners, monasteries, students, widows, pilgrims, artisans, and poor households all encountered credit in different forms. A crude rule ("never receive more than principal") could protect the poor from exploitation, but it could also fail to explain cases where the lender had suffered actual damage, where a borrower had delayed culpably, where an exchange contract was real, or where a partnership genuinely shared risk.

This is the practical world in which Robert of Courçon, Raymond of Peñafort, and Hostiensis belong. They are not yet the full late scholastic system. They are the bridge from penitential case to legal-theological structure.

Robert of Courçon represents an early Parisian theological moment in which the suspicion of creditor profit remains strong, but the need for case analysis is already visible. He belongs to the world of university theology and pastoral discipline, where usury is treated not merely as a technical irregularity but as a spiritual danger. The creditor's soul is at stake because usury disorders love of neighbor. It converts another's need into one's own gain. It teaches the lender to see the borrower not as brother, but as occasion of profit.

And yet Robert's treatment of penalties shows that early theologians already had to distinguish punishment for wrongdoing from gain from lending. A penalty attached to default might be permissible if it was genuinely penal and not a hidden price for the loan. But Robert remains wary. The surplus is dangerous; it can easily become profit to the creditor. In some early formulations, the surplus associated with a penalty was directed to the poor rather than retained by the lender, precisely so that the creditor would not desire default.

The intuition is powerful, and it anticipates a theme that will recur through the whole tradition. The moral problem is not only what the contract says, but what the contract makes the lender want. Does the lender want repayment, restoration, and the borrower's flourishing? Or does the lender benefit from delay, distress, and failure? A penalty that rewards the creditor for default creates a perverse incentive: the lender becomes financially interested in the borrower's breakdown. Robert of Courçon's caution therefore prepares the later anti-abuse logic of poena nec in fraudem.

Raymond of Peñafort moves the discussion into the world of confessional casuistry. His Summa de casibus poenitentiae belongs to a genre designed not for abstract speculation alone, but for practical judgment in the care of souls. The confessor needed to know when a penitent creditor had sinned, whether restitution was required, and how to distinguish legitimate compensation from usurious gain. Raymond's importance lies partly in this pastoral orientation: the usury doctrine becomes a tool for examining conscience and repairing injustice.

Raymond's treatment of interesse is especially significant. Interesse did not originally mean "interest" in the modern sense of a normal return on capital. It meant something more like the difference between the position one should have occupied and the position one now occupies because of another's action. It was compensatory; it concerned loss, damage, or detriment. In this sense, Raymond helps preserve a distinction that modern readers easily miss: compensation for loss is not the same as profit from a loan.

That distinction is the seed of damnum emergens. If a lender suffers real damage because of the transaction or because of the borrower's wrongful delay, compensation may restore equality rather than violate it. But Raymond also understands how fragile the distinction is. A gift expected from the beginning may be usurious even if it is not written into the contract. A penalty may be usurious if it is really the price of time. A surplus may be unjust even when it wears the appearance of gratitude, compensation, or custom.

Raymond's world is deeply attentive to intention. Did the lender expect the gift? Was the payment demanded? Was the borrower truly free? Was the penalty designed to repair harm, or to generate profit? These questions are essential because usury often hides in expectation. It may not be written down; it may be socially understood. The borrower may know that future access to credit depends on "voluntary" gratitude; the lender may not demand, but may still intend. Raymond's pastoral subtlety helps expose these hidden obligations.

Hostiensis (Henry of Susa) then gives the tradition a more systematic canonical form. He is not the inventor of every exception, but one of the great organizers of the legal-theological material. With Hostiensis, the scattered practical distinctions begin to assume a more recognizable structure. His name is associated with the famous verse-list: Poena nec in fraudem, lex commissoria, gratis / Dans, socii, pompa; plus sorte modis datur istis.

The significance of the list is not simply that it catalogues exceptions. Its deeper significance is that it teaches classification. More than principal may sometimes be received, but only if the surplus arises from something other than the loan itself. A penalty, if not fraudulent. A contractual forfeiture or condition, if just. A free gift, if truly free. Partnership, if risk and profit are actually shared. Expense or circumstance, if genuinely compensatory. The list is a compressed grammar of moral finance.

But Hostiensis's list also contains its own warning. The phrase poena nec in fraudem is crucial: a penalty may be valid only when it is not a fraud on the usury prohibition. The word fraud does enormous work. It tells us that medieval canonists were already alert to legal cleverness. They knew that one could preserve the appearance of obedience while violating the substance of justice. A lender could say "penalty" while intending interest. A lender could say "gift" while expecting payment. A lender could say "partnership" while guaranteeing capital. A lender could say "exchange" while arranging a circular loan.

This anti-fraud principle becomes one of the deepest continuities in the tradition. The Church does not merely ask whether the contract fits a recognized label. It asks whether the label is being used truthfully. Is the penalty really penal? Is the gift really free? Is the partnership really shared? Is the loss really actual? Is the foregone profit really concrete? Is the risk really borne by the lender? Without this scrutiny, the extrinsic titles become loopholes. With it, they remain moral tests.

The movement from Robert of Courçon to Raymond of Peñafort to Hostiensis therefore marks the passage from pastoral suspicion to structured discernment. Robert emphasizes the danger of creditor profit and the need to prevent penalties from becoming revenue. Raymond sharpens the confessional distinction between compensation and gain, especially through interesse and the analysis of intention. Hostiensis organizes the exception-list tradition and gives canonical form to the anti-fraud logic.

Together, they prepare the ground for Aquinas and the later scholastics. Aquinas will supply a deeper metaphysical and moral account of the mutuum: in a consumptive loan, one cannot sell both the thing and its use; surplus from the loan itself is unjust; compensation for actual loss may be allowed; partnership profit differs from usury because capital remains at risk. But Aquinas does not appear in a vacuum. He inherits a world already shaped by canonists and confessors who were sorting the cases, naming the dangers, and trying to protect both justice and practical life.

This early phase also helps correct a common misunderstanding. The extrinsic titles were not originally invented as cynical loopholes by financiers determined to evade the Gospel. They emerged because moral theology had to face real cases. Borrowers defaulted, lenders suffered losses, merchants traded across distance, partnerships shared risk, gifts could be free or coerced, public authorities borrowed, and charitable institutions needed administration. The world was complex, and justice required distinctions.

At the same time, the early canonists knew that distinctions can be abused. The sharper the category, the easier it may be to weaponize. A title can become a mask; a list can become a toolkit for evasion. The confessor and canonist therefore had to cultivate suspicion without becoming simplistic, distinguishing compensation from extraction without pretending that every surplus was identical.

This is why the early canonists and confessors are indispensable to the genealogy of the extrinsic titles. They gave the tradition its practical seriousness. They kept the borrower's vulnerability in view. They insisted on restitution where injustice had occurred. They permitted compensation where equality had genuinely been disturbed. And they repeatedly warned that legal form could conceal moral fraud.

Their work reminds us that moral finance begins not with an equation, but with examination of conscience. What did the lender intend? What did the borrower suffer? What was promised, expected, concealed, or coerced? Did the payment repair a real burden, or did it turn another person's need into gain? Those questions began in confessionals and canon-law commentaries. They remain the questions of non-extractive finance today.

sections.title: 34.2 Thirteenth-Century Theologians
paragraphs: The thirteenth century gave the usury tradition its deeper theological architecture. The early canonists and confessors had developed practical distinctions: penalties, gifts, partnership, damages, and other cases in which more than principal might be received without necessarily collapsing into usury. But the theologians of the thirteenth century asked a more foundational question: why is usury unjust in the first place?

The question mattered because a doctrine built only on rules is fragile. Rules can be evaded, lists manipulated, legal forms rearranged. If the prohibition against usury was to remain morally coherent, it needed an account of the kind of exchange involved in a loan, the nature of money, the structure of ownership, the difference between use and consumption, and the meaning of justice between persons. The thirteenth-century theologians supplied this architecture.

Thomas Aquinas stands at the center of this development. His treatment of usury in the Summa Theologiae is famous because it gives the doctrine its classic form. The basic argument is not that money is evil, or that trade is evil, or that profit is always evil. Aquinas's argument is more precise. In a loan for consumption, such as a loan of wine, wheat, or money, the use of the thing consists in its consumption. One cannot drink wine and still return the same wine. One cannot spend money and still return the same coins. In such a case, ownership passes to the borrower, and the borrower owes back an equivalent. To charge separately for the thing and for its use is to sell the same thing twice.

This argument depends on the Roman legal category of the mutuum, but Aquinas gives it moral and theological force. The lender has transferred ownership. The lender's intrinsic claim is therefore to the equivalent principal. If the lender demands more from the loan itself, the equality of exchange is broken: the borrower is made to pay for what the lender no longer owns. The sin is not merely technical; it is a violation of commutative justice.

Aquinas's importance, however, is not only that he condemns usury. It is also that he carefully identifies the cases in which surplus is not necessarily usury. In ST II-II, q.78, a.2, ad 1, he allows compensation for loss incurred. A lender who suffers actual damage because of the loan may be restored. This is the root of damnum emergens. The lender may not be enriched by the loan, but the lender need not be harmed by it either. Justice restores equality.

At the same time, Aquinas rejects the attempt to charge for profit not made. The lender may not sell what he does not yet have. A hoped-for profit is uncertain and may never have materialized. Here Aquinas sets a boundary against lucrum cessans, or at least against its broad and speculative form. This boundary will become one of the great tensions of the later tradition. Later theologians will ask whether Aquinas's rejection applies to all foregone profit, or only to vague, uncertain, and speculative claims. They will use other parts of Aquinas's own thought (especially restitution for preventing future goods, and special value to the owner) to reopen the question.

Aquinas also gives the tradition its decisive distinction between mutuum and societas. In ST II-II, q.78, a.2, ad 5, he explains that one may entrust money to a merchant by way of partnership and receive profit from the venture, because the money remains at the risk of the one who entrusted it. This is not usury, because the investor is not demanding guaranteed increase from a loan. The investor participates in the uncertainty of the enterprise. Capital remains exposed. Profit follows risk-bearing and participation, not the mere passage of time.

This distinction becomes the theological root of the later analysis of periculum sortis. Aquinas does not yet develop the title in its later form, but he builds the architecture that makes it thinkable. The moral difference between creditor and partner depends on downside. Who bears the loss? Who owns the capital? Who can enforce repayment? Is the return guaranteed, or does it depend on the outcome of the venture? These questions remain at the heart of modern non-extractive finance.

Aquinas also alerts the tradition to hidden usury in delayed payment. If a seller charges more merely because payment is delayed, the transaction may become a loan in disguise. Again, the issue is not the label but the substance. A sale on credit may be legitimate if there are real differences in value, cost, risk, or circumstance. But if the higher price is simply the price of waiting, then time has been monetized. This anticipates the later problem of carentia pecuniae and the modern time value of money.

Aquinas thus gives the doctrine a disciplined structure. Surplus from the mutuum itself is usury. Actual loss may be compensated. Partnership profit is legitimate when capital remains at risk. Speculative foregone gain is rejected. Delayed payment can conceal a loan. These principles do not solve every later case, but they provide the grammar through which later cases must be judged.

Duns Scotus enters the tradition with a different emphasis, especially in relation to penalties and incentives. Whereas Aquinas gives the metaphysical and juridical structure of the mutuum, Scotus sharpens attention to the lender's intention and the incentive effects of contractual design. The question is not only whether a penalty clause exists, but whether the lender wants the borrower to default. Would the lender rather be repaid on time, or would the lender rather receive the penalty?

A late penalty may be formally attached to default, but if the penalty is so profitable that the lender prefers delay, the contract has become morally disordered: the question is devastatingly simple, and it turns the analysis from wording to desire. The creditor's gain depends on the borrower's failure. The clause is no longer merely protective; it is revenue-seeking. Scotus's insight anticipates modern concerns about default fees, penalty interest, predatory lending, and financial structures that profit from distress.

Peter John Olivi, the Franciscan theologian, brings another kind of sophistication. Olivi takes commercial life seriously from within a Franciscan moral imagination, which often surprises modern readers who assume that Franciscan poverty must imply economic simplicity. In fact, Franciscan thought often displayed acute sensitivity to the practical and moral dynamics of trade, credit, valuation, and poverty.

Olivi's contribution matters especially for the later development of lucrum cessans. He recognizes that money used in commerce is not always merely idle money. Capital already ordered toward exchange may have a determinate commercial use. A merchant who lends money that was actually committed to a trading operation may not be in the same position as someone who had no concrete use for the funds. In such a case, the loan may interrupt a real commercial process.

This does not make Olivi a simple defender of interest. His point is subtler. He is not saying that all money naturally deserves return. He is saying that money embedded in a concrete commercial project may have a different practical character from money lying unused. If lending interrupts that project, the lender may suffer a real detriment. Later theologians will develop this insight into a more general theory of foregone profit, though not without controversy.

Olivi's Franciscan context matters here. The Franciscans were concerned with poverty, but they were also deeply involved in the moral analysis of markets and credit, especially through the later debates around the Monti di Pietà. Their concern was not to abolish all economic activity, but to prevent economic relations from becoming domination. Olivi's commercial realism therefore sits alongside a radical concern for evangelical poverty and social inclusion. He helps show that moral theology can be economically intelligent without becoming permissive.

Alexander of Alexandria (also known in some discussions as Alexander Lombard) represents another important development, especially in relation to risk. His Tractatus de Usuris of 1307 is associated with the early naming or systematization of periculum sortis, the risk of principal. Although he technically stands at the beginning of the fourteenth century, he belongs intellectually to the world created by thirteenth-century scholastic analysis. The questions generated by Aquinas, Scotus, Olivi, and the canonists continue in him.

Alexander's significance lies in making capital risk thinkable as an extrinsic title. If a lender truly bears the risk of losing principal, then perhaps a surplus payment is not merely profit from the loan, but compensation for exposure to loss. This idea is structurally different from penalties, actual damages, or foregone profit. It concerns not an event around the loan, but the architecture of the transaction itself. Who bears downside? Can the capital actually be lost? Is repayment guaranteed? Does the lender stand as creditor or as risk-bearing participant?

The danger, of course, is immediate. Every loan has some possibility of nonpayment. If ordinary default risk is enough to justify surplus, then the usury prohibition collapses. This is why periculum sortis remains unstable for a long time. It names something real, but if generalized too quickly, it becomes a universal risk premium. Alexander's importance is therefore not that he resolves the problem, but that he brings it into view.

Together, Aquinas, Scotus, Olivi, and Alexander reveal the increasing analytical sophistication of the usury tradition. Aquinas gives the doctrine its central grammar of mutuum, commutative justice, actual loss, and partnership. Scotus sharpens the analysis of intention and incentives. Olivi brings commercial realism and the possibility of concrete foregone gain. Alexander brings the risk of principal into explicit theological analysis.

They also reveal the emergence of analytical uncertainty. The early rule is clear in principle: do not demand gain from a loan as such. But real transactions are not always clear. Is this a loan or a partnership? Is this compensation for loss or disguised yield? Is this foregone profit concrete or speculative? Is this risk truly borne or merely invoked? Is this penalty protective or desired? Theologians increasingly had to analyze not only contracts, but the moral reality beneath contracts.

This is where the "risk of analysis" becomes important. The danger is not only that a borrower may default, or that a lender may lose money. The danger is that the moral theologian may misclassify the transaction. A structure may be blessed as partnership when it is really debt. A payment may be approved as compensation when it is really extraction. A penalty may be tolerated as protective when it is actually revenue. The more complex the economy becomes, the more important accurate moral diagnosis becomes.

The thirteenth-century theologians therefore did not simply repeat an inherited ban. They deepened it. They gave it concepts: ownership, use, consumption, equality, restitution, intention, risk, partnership, foregone gain, and hidden form. They made it possible to distinguish real compensation from usury without surrendering the prohibition itself.

For modern finance, their legacy is still alive. Aquinas asks whether the return comes from a loan or from shared risk. Scotus asks whether the lender profits from failure. Olivi asks whether capital was genuinely committed to productive use. Alexander asks whether principal can truly be lost. Together, they ask the questions every non-extractive capital structure must answer. What is the contract really? What is the surplus for? Who bears downside? Does the lender want the borrower's flourishing, or the borrower's dependence? Is the return restorative, participatory, or extractive?
The thirteenth century did not close the usury debate. It gave later centuries the tools to argue it properly.

sections.title: 34.3 Late Medieval Moral Theologians
paragraphs: By the late medieval period, the usury tradition had become both more practical and more fragile.
