The great theological architecture was in place: Aquinas had given the classic account of the mutuum, actual loss, and partnership; Scotus had sharpened attention to intention and incentives; Olivi had brought commercial reality into moral theology; the canonists had organized their exception lists and warned against fraud. But the world of commerce kept pressing against the categories. The question was no longer only whether usury was sinful in principle. The question was how to govern real financial life without either permitting exploitation or making necessary economic cooperation impossible. Merchants needed credit, cities needed funding, poor households needed emergency loans, and charitable institutions needed administrative support. Trade crossed distances, and money moved through exchange networks. A moral theology that could only say "no" would fail to guide practice. But a moral theology too eager to say "yes" would become a servant of extraction.

This is the context in which Bernardino of Siena and Sant'Antonino of Florence become so important. They belong to a pastoral and civic world in which preaching, confession, commerce, poverty, public order, and institutional innovation were deeply entangled. They were not abstract theorists writing from a distance but moral theologians of a living economy.

Bernardino of Siena, the great Franciscan preacher, stands at the intersection of evangelical poverty and practical economics. The combination matters. It would be easy to imagine that Franciscan concern for poverty would lead only to denunciation of markets and money. But the Franciscan tradition was often more subtle than that. Because it cared so intensely about poverty, it also cared about the institutions that shaped the lives of the poor. Credit, prices, pledges, wages, reputation, charity, and civic trust were not peripheral matters. They were part of the moral fabric of communal life.

Bernardino's treatment of economic questions reflects this practical moral seriousness. He condemns usury because it turns need into gain and corrodes charity. But he also recognizes the need for morally ordered economic activity. The merchant is not automatically wicked. Profit is not automatically sinful. Trade can serve the common good when it distributes goods, sustains households, assumes real labor and risk, and avoids fraud. The moral issue is not simply whether money increases, but how and why it increases.

This helps explain Bernardino's importance for the Monti di Pietà and the wider development of practical moral finance. Lending to the poor was necessary. Without access to small loans, poor households could fall into desperation, pawn essential goods to predatory lenders, or become trapped by abusive creditors. Yet charitable lending institutions could not operate without costs. Someone had to administer pledges, keep accounts, maintain buildings, pay staff, assess goods, and protect the institution from collapse. The question became whether small charges for administration and labor could be morally distinguished from usury.

Bernardino's world pushed the tradition to see the difference between sustainability and extraction. A charge may be illicit if it is profit from the loan itself. But reimbursement for actual institutional costs may be legitimate if it remains proportionate, transparent, and ordered toward service rather than enrichment. This is an adjacent title, related to damnum emergens and stipendium laboris, but not reducible to ordinary interest. The purpose is not to make capital grow, but to keep a charitable institution alive so that the poor are not abandoned to worse lenders.

The distinction shows that the late medieval tradition was capable of institutional reasoning. The moral unit was not only the individual contract between lender and borrower but also the institution. What does this institution exist to do? Does it serve the poor? Does it recover costs or extract profit? Does it remain accountable to its mission? Does it prevent worse injustice, or does it become a respectable version of the same injustice?

The Monti di Pietà became a test case for these questions. Their defenders argued that modest charges were not usury because they covered necessary costs of operation. Their critics worried that any required payment above principal could violate the prohibition. The debate forced theologians to distinguish more carefully between gain, expense, labor, and institutional necessity. It also exposed the danger of drift. A charge introduced for sustainability could become a habit of revenue. A charitable institution could slowly begin to think like a financial institution. A mission tool could become a profit machine.

Bernardino's contribution lies in holding together moral rigor and practical mercy. He does not want the poor exploited. But he also does not want them deprived of access to nonpredatory credit because of an overly abstract rule. The challenge is to create structures that serve need without feeding on need. This is precisely the challenge of non-extractive finance.

Sant'Antonino of Florence continues and systematizes much of this practical moral theology. As archbishop of Florence, he stood within one of the great commercial cities of Europe. Florence was not an imaginary economy but a world of merchants, bankers, artisans, guilds, civic finance, exchange, poverty, and political complexity. A moral theologian in Florence could not avoid the realities of commerce. The question was how to judge them.

Antonino preserves the exception-list tradition and treats the recognized cases in which more than principal may be received. He works within the inherited categories: penalty not in fraud, free gift, partnership, actual loss, expense, and other circumstances that may justify surplus. But like the best of the tradition, he does not treat these categories as automatic permissions. He is concerned with fraud, intention, proportion, and the real substance of the transaction.

His importance lies partly in his pastoral and civic realism. Moral theology must be usable by confessors, merchants, rulers, and ordinary Christians. It must be able to speak to the actual practices of a commercial society without surrendering the Gospel's judgment on greed. Antonino's work therefore helps transmit the tradition from earlier medieval analysis into the late medieval and early modern world. He becomes one of the figures through whom the older categories remain alive and available for later Salamanca and Jesuit development.

Both Bernardino and Antonino show that late medieval moral theology was neither anti-commercial simplicity nor financial permissiveness. It was a disciplined attempt to govern commerce by justice. They recognized that money, credit, and trade could serve human life. They also recognized that these same instruments could easily become domination. Their task was to identify the difference.

This period also marks the increasing standardization of the extrinsic-title tradition. Penalties, actual losses, foregone gains, risk, partnership, expense, and labor become increasingly available as analytical categories. But standardization creates a new danger. Once categories become familiar, they can be invoked too easily. What began as careful discernment can become formula. A lender can learn the approved words. A contract can be drafted to fit the list. A moral exception can become a business model.

The late medieval theologians therefore had to preserve anti-abuse analysis. A penalty must not be expected revenue. A gift must not be coerced. A partnership must not guarantee the capital provider while exposing only the active partner. Administrative charges must not exceed real costs. Foregone profit must not be imaginary. Risk must actually be borne. This is the recurring discipline: the title must correspond to the truth.

Their work also helps us see why the history of usury doctrine is not a simple story of decline from purity to compromise. There is indeed a risk of erosion. Some titles become broader. Some arguments become more permissive. Commercial practice pushes theologians toward accommodation. Later critics will complain, not without reason, that the doctrine has been hollowed out by exceptions.

But there is also a genuine development of moral intelligence. The tradition learns to distinguish poverty lending from merchant finance, loss from gain, partnership from debt, cost recovery from profit, institutional sustainability from extraction. These distinctions are not corruptions in themselves. They are necessary if moral theology is to govern real life. The danger lies not in making distinctions, but in losing the ascetical and pastoral discipline that keeps them truthful.

For modern readers, Bernardino and Antonino are especially relevant because they help us think institutionally. A non-extractive fund, a steward-owned company, a charitable lender, or a mission-locked investment vehicle faces the same basic problem as the Monti di Pietà: it must be financially sustainable without becoming extractive. It must pay for labor, administration, diligence, accounting, legal work, and stewardship. But it must not convert those needs into a justification for unlimited fees, hidden yield, or capital priority over mission.

The late medieval lesson is therefore concrete. Cost recovery is legitimate only when it remains cost recovery. Labor compensation is legitimate only when it corresponds to real labor. Institutional sustainability is legitimate only when the institution remains ordered to service. Once the charge becomes a source of profit detached from mission, the moral form changes. What began as a remedy can become the disease.

Bernardino and Antonino also remind us that finance must be judged by its effects on the vulnerable. Does the structure protect the poor from worse lenders? Does it help households remain intact? Does it sustain work and community? Does it prevent desperation from becoming someone else's revenue? Or does it cloak extraction in the language of service?

The late medieval moral theologians thus occupy a crucial place in the genealogy of the extrinsic titles. They receive the categories from the canonists and the great scholastics, and they apply them in the commercial and civic realities of late medieval Europe. They defend practical institutions like the Monti di Pietà while insisting on moral boundaries. They standardize the exception lists while warning against abuse. They prepare the way for Salamanca, where the categories will be expanded and systematized still further.

Their enduring contribution is not a single formula, but a posture: practical without being cynical, strict without being useless, merciful without being permissive. They understood that moral finance must serve real human beings in real institutions, and that every institution must be watched, because extraction often begins as a reasonable exception. That is why the late medieval period matters. It shows moral theology learning to build institutions without forgetting sin.

sections.title: 34.4 Early Modern and Salamanca
paragraphs: The early modern period transformed the usury debate by placing the inherited scholastic categories under the pressure of a rapidly expanding commercial world. The older questions did not disappear. The mutuum remained central. The prohibition against gain from the loan itself remained formally intact. The extrinsic titles still had to distinguish compensation from extraction. But the setting had changed. European commerce was becoming more complex, more international, more legally sophisticated, and more dependent on credit, exchange, merchant networks, public debt, and capital deployment.

This is the world of Conrad Summenhart, Cajetan, Domingo de Soto, Martín de Azpilcueta, Medina, Juan de la Peña, and Luis de Molina. It is the world in which late medieval categories become early modern systems. Theologians are no longer dealing only with simple loans, late penalties, or local merchant cases. They are dealing with exchange rates, price movements, risk, insurance-like arrangements, public obligations, colonial trade, merchant banking, money scarcity, and increasingly elaborate contracts. Moral theology has to become technically competent, or it will be ignored.

Conrad Summenhart belongs to the transitional moment between medieval and early modern contract analysis. His work on contracts and extrinsic causes helps carry the older scholastic categories forward into a more systematic framework. Summenhart matters because he treats economic and contractual life with an analytical seriousness that anticipates later developments. The question is no longer merely whether a lender may receive something above principal, but under what contractual cause, title, or circumstance such a payment might be justified.

Summenhart helps clarify the world in which lucrum cessans becomes more thinkable. The older suspicion remains: one must not sell what one does not yet have. A merely possible gain is not a possession. But commerce creates cases in which foregone gain is not purely imaginary. A merchant may have had a determinate use for capital. A transaction may have been planned. A profit may have been reasonably expected. The problem becomes how to distinguish concrete interrupted gain from abstract opportunity cost. Summenhart's transitional role lies in helping make such distinctions more explicit.

Cajetan, or Tommaso de Vio, is especially important because he works as a Thomist interpreter under new commercial conditions. His task is not simply to repeat Aquinas, but to ask how Aquinas's principles apply when the economy has become more complex. This matters particularly for lucrum cessans. Aquinas appears to reject compensation for profit not made in ST II-II, q.78, a.2, ad 1. Yet Aquinas also allows compensation for future goods in certain restitution cases, and recognizes that a thing may have special value to its owner because of its concrete use. Cajetan helps develop the Thomist reconciliation: foregone profit may be compensable when it is sufficiently determinate, but not when it is speculative or universalized.

The reconciliation is delicate. If lucrum cessans is admitted too broadly, every lender can claim that money might have earned something elsewhere, and the usury prohibition collapses into opportunity cost. But if it is rejected entirely, real commercial losses may go uncompensated. Cajetan's importance lies in holding the line between concrete and speculative gain. He helps preserve the Thomistic concern that one must not sell what one does not yet have, while allowing that some future goods may be close enough to possession or expectation that their wrongful interruption creates a compensable detriment.

Domingo de Soto represents a more cautious Salamanca voice. He inherits a world of expanding commerce, but he remains concerned to preserve the gratuity of the mutuum. Soto understands that the extrinsic titles can easily become general permissions for interest. His caution matters especially for lucrum cessans. He worries that if foregone profit is too easily admitted, then the lender's imagination becomes limitless. Every coin can be imagined as potentially productive, and every loan can be said to interrupt some possible gain. The title then ceases to identify a real burden and becomes a justification for ordinary return on capital.

Soto's restraint is not economic ignorance but moral discipline. He wants to prevent the category of compensation from becoming a fiction. A concrete loss may be compensated. A real interrupted use may require analysis. But the mere fact that money could have been used profitably somewhere cannot automatically create a claim against the borrower. Soto helps keep alive the suspicion that opportunity cost, if detached from concrete intention and probability, becomes usury with a more refined vocabulary.

Martín de Azpilcueta, known as Navarrus, brings the Salamanca tradition into close contact with money, exchange, and commercial practice. He is famous in economic history for his reflections on money, price, and exchange, and he belongs to a world in which theologians had to think seriously about monetary conditions. Changes in money supply, exchange rates, and market conditions were not abstract curiosities. They affected prices, debts, wages, trade, and moral judgment.

Navarrus's importance for the extrinsic titles lies partly in this broader commercial awareness. He helps situate lending within a wider monetary economy. Money is not merely a neutral token in a static world; its abundance, scarcity, place, and exchangeability matter. But again, the moral question is not simply whether money has market value. The question is whether a surplus payment corresponds to a just title or merely to the creditor's power. Navarrus's world makes it harder to deny economic complexity, but it does not eliminate the need for moral classification.

Medina and Juan de la Peña are significant especially in relation to risk and the movement toward risk pricing. The older title of periculum sortis had appeared earlier, but it remained unstable. The difficulty was obvious: if risk of nonpayment justifies surplus, then every loan appears to have a title. But in actual commercial life, not all claims are equally secure. A risky claim to one hundred is not worth the same as a certain claim to one hundred. Debtor reliability, collateral, legal enforceability, commercial conditions, and probability of repayment all affect value.

Medina's attention to the risk characteristics of the debtor points toward a more modern valuation logic. A creditor facing a higher probability of nonpayment may claim that the value of the claim is reduced by risk. A payment above principal might then be interpreted not as profit from the loan, but as compensation for uncertainty. This is a powerful insight, but also a dangerous one. It risks transforming every loan into a priced bundle of default probability and return. Once again, the title names something real but threatens to universalize interest.

Juan de la Peña belongs within this same contested field. Risk becomes not only a moral issue but a valuation issue. How should uncertain repayment be priced? Can risk be separated from the loan itself? Is the creditor being compensated for genuine exposure, or merely charging for the ordinary possibility that borrowers sometimes fail? The problem is not solved by naming risk. The theologian must determine whether the lender actually bears it, whether the amount is proportionate, and whether the risk has already been shifted through collateral, guarantees, or priority.

Luis de Molina brings the Salamanca tradition into one of its most sophisticated forms. Molina is intensely attentive to the commercial world and to the practical conditions under which economic action occurs. He does not simply repeat inherited formulas. He examines intention, causality, probability, contractual form, and commercial context. In relation to lucrum cessans, Molina asks whether the lender had a real alternative use for the money, whether the profit was foreseeable, whether the loan truly caused the profit to be lost, and whether the amount claimed is proportionate. Not every holder of money has the title. The title depends on real circumstances.

This is crucial. Molina's framework does not simply collapse into modern opportunity cost. It does not say that because money could always be invested somewhere, the borrower must always pay a return. The lender must show more: a concrete alternative, a genuine intention or commitment, probable gain, and causal interruption by the loan. Molina therefore expands practical moral analysis while trying to preserve the distinction between compensation and ordinary interest.

Molina is also important for periculum sortis. He recognizes that a risky claim is not equal in value to a certain claim. If repayment is uncertain, then the creditor may be exposed to a real detriment. But the same danger persists: if this logic applies to ordinary default risk in every loan, the usury prohibition dissolves. The problem becomes one of moral valuation under uncertainty. The theologian must ask whether the risk is genuine, whether it is borne by the lender, whether it is special or ordinary, whether it is already covered by other contractual protections, and whether the return is proportionate.

The Salamanca tradition thus brings the extrinsic titles into a more expansive and technically aware environment. Its theologians are often more permissive than earlier rigorists, but they are not simply secular economists in clerical dress. They remain committed to the moral distinction between gain from the loan itself and compensation for external burdens. What changes is the range of burdens they are willing to analyze: lost profit, risk, liquidity, exchange, valuation, commercial intention, and legal enforceability.

The expansion has two sides. On the positive side, Salamanca demonstrates the intellectual power of moral theology in a complex economy. It refuses to remain at the level of slogans. It asks detailed questions about contracts, markets, probabilities, intentions, and institutions. It recognizes that justice in commerce requires knowledge of commerce. The theologian must understand the instrument being judged.

On the negative side, the expansion risks erosion. Every new title creates a possible path around the prohibition. Every practical nuance can become a legal strategy. Every recognition of commercial burden can be transformed into a general claim of capital. The more sophisticated the analysis becomes, the more vigilant it must be against capture. Salamanca therefore stands as both achievement and warning.

For modern non-extractive finance, this is precisely the tension we inherit. We need the technical seriousness of Salamanca. We need to understand capital structures, risk allocation, liquidity, opportunity cost, fees, governance rights, accounting, and incentives. A moral critique that does not understand these things will remain vague. But we also need Soto's caution, Cajetan's boundary-setting, Molina's causal discipline, and the anti-fraud instinct of the canonists. Otherwise, technical sophistication becomes the servant of extraction.

The early modern and Salamanca theologians teach us that finance must be examined at the level of structure. It is not enough to ask whether a return is legal or market-standard. We must ask what title justifies it. Is it actual loss? Concrete foregone gain? Genuine risk? Labor? Expense? Exchange? Or is it merely the universal modern claim that capital deserves growth?

They also teach us that moral categories must be neither too rigid nor too elastic. If too rigid, they fail to govern real economic life. If too elastic, they cease to restrain anything. The history of Salamanca is the history of that dangerous middle: moral theology entering the technical machinery of finance without wanting to be swallowed by it.

In that sense, Salamanca is not merely a chapter in the decline of usury doctrine. It is a laboratory. It shows theologians trying to preserve justice while analyzing increasingly modern economic forms. Sometimes they succeed. Sometimes they open doors that later authors will fear cannot be closed. But their central question remains indispensable: not "what does capital want?" but "what does justice permit, and under what title?" That question is the bridge from medieval usury doctrine to any serious contemporary account of non-extractive capital.

sections.title: 34.5 Jesuit and Late Scholastic Systematizers
paragraphs: With the Jesuit and late scholastic systematizers, the doctrine of the extrinsic titles reaches both its greatest analytical sophistication and its point of greatest danger. The inherited prohibition against usury remains. The mutuum still cannot generate surplus from itself. The lender may not simply demand gain because money has been lent. Yet by this stage the world of commerce has become so complex, and the titles so numerous, that the doctrine must be reorganized at a higher level of abstraction.

This is the world of Leonardus Lessius and Juan de Lugo. They inherit the canonists, Aquinas, Scotus, Olivi, the late medieval preachers, the Monti di Pietà debates, Summenhart, Cajetan, and Salamanca. They also inherit the practical realities of early modern commercial life: merchant finance, exchange contracts, risk, delay, liquidity, partnership, insurance-like arrangements, public debt, and the growing sense that money could be economically productive in ways the simplest medieval formulas had not fully captured.

Lessius is one of the key figures in this development, and his importance lies not merely in his conclusions, but in his method. He treats contracts with extraordinary precision. He asks what the parties intend, what the legal form does, what risk is borne, what loss is incurred, what profit is foregone, what service is rendered, and what burden is created. He belongs to a tradition of moral theology that is not content with pious generalities. It wants to understand the machinery of economic life well enough to judge it.

This makes Lessius indispensable for the history of moral finance. He is not simply a theologian who repeats the prohibition against usury, nor is he simply a proto-modern economist who abandons it. He is a systematizer of the threshold. In him, the old categories remain visible, but they are pressed toward the edge of modern financial reasoning.

His treatment of risk is especially important. The title of periculum sortis (risk of principal) had appeared earlier, but Lessius helps consolidate it. If the lender genuinely bears uncertainty about repayment, if the principal is truly exposed, then the surplus may be understood not as gain from the loan itself but as compensation for risk borne. This is a powerful move because it recognizes that not all claims are equal. A certain claim to one hundred and a risky claim to one hundred do not have the same value. The risk of nonpayment changes the moral and economic structure of the transaction.

But the insight is also dangerous. If every loan includes some risk of nonpayment, and if every risk of nonpayment justifies surplus, then the prohibition against usury disappears. Lessius's consolidation of the risk title therefore places enormous weight on the distinction between genuine risk-bearing and ordinary lending. The question cannot simply be whether default is imaginable. The question must be whether the lender actually bears the downside in a morally significant way, whether the risk is proportionate to the return, whether the risk has already been shifted to the borrower through collateral or guarantees, and whether the claim is really loss-absorbing.

Lessius is also crucial for the development of carentia pecuniae, the title based on being without one's money for a term. This is one of the most delicate and dangerous titles because it approaches the logic of the time value of money. The lender commits funds and cannot call them back. The borrower receives not only money, but stability over time. The lender loses liquidity. In a commercial world, this loss of liquidity can be real. Money unavailable for a term is different from money immediately available.

Yet here again Lessius stands at the edge. If the deprivation of money for a term is itself compensable, then almost every loan can generate surplus. The lender is always without money after lending it. The borrower always has time. The loan always involves duration. If carentia pecuniae becomes a general title, then the old prohibition dissolves into ordinary interest. Lessius's analysis therefore reveals how close late scholastic thought comes to modern money-market logic: liquidity, term, duration, and availability become morally visible. But whether they can be priced without monetizing time itself remains contested.

Lessius also develops the relationship between lucrum cessans, damnum emergens, and risk. In practice, these titles overlap. A lender deprived of funds may suffer actual loss. Or the lender may miss a concrete profit. Or the lender may bear the risk of not recovering principal. Or all three may appear in the same case. The danger is double-counting. The same burden may be renamed several times: loss, foregone gain, liquidity deprivation, risk premium, administrative cost. A disciplined moral theology must prevent one real inconvenience from becoming five streams of surplus.

This is where the late scholastic systematizers become so important. They do not merely multiply titles. At their best, they try to organize them into a coherent theory of detriment. The guiding question remains: has the lender borne a real burden external to the loan itself? If so, what is its nature? Is it past damage? Future profit prevented? Risk of capital? Labor? Expense? Loss of liquidity? And how can it be measured proportionately?

Juan de Lugo brings this systematizing movement to a mature synthesis. Lugo's importance lies in the way he gathers the major titles under a broader account of compensable detriment. Risk, lost gain, and actual loss can be understood as related forms of harm or burden. They are not identical, but they can be analyzed together. This makes Lugo extraordinarily useful for modern non-extractive finance because he asks the question at the right level: what burden is capital actually bearing, and does the surplus restore or compensate that burden rather than extract from the borrower?

Lugo is willing to think broadly. He accepts that in some cases lucrum cessans may justify compensation, provided conditions are met. The loan must truly cause the foregone profit. The amount demanded must not exceed the value of that profit. Compensation should not be taken upfront in a way that turns the whole transaction into a disguised interest charge. These conditions show that Lugo is not simply blessing opportunity cost. He is disciplining it. The lost gain must be concrete, causal, and proportionate.

He also treats risk with sophistication. If the lender truly bears risk of the principal, surplus may be justified as compensation for that exposure. But here too the question is whether the risk is real. A lender who is guaranteed repayment, protected by collateral, senior in liquidation, supported by personal guarantees, and able to enforce against unrelated assets is not bearing the same kind of risk as a partner whose capital can actually be lost. The moral question is not whether the contract uses the language of risk, but whether the lender is genuinely vulnerable.

At the same time, Lugo is sharply critical of titles that threaten to dissolve the doctrine. His criticism of carentia pecuniae is especially revealing. If being without money for a term is enough, then no contract remains usurious. Every loan deprives the lender of money, every lender can claim absence, and every borrower can be charged for time. Lugo sees that this title risks converting duration into yield and thereby making the usury prohibition meaningless. His criticism shows that late scholastic sophistication did not necessarily mean surrender. It could also mean greater diagnostic clarity.

This is the paradox of Lessius and Lugo. They expand the analytical field, but they also reveal the dangers of expansion. They understand commercial complexity better than many earlier authors. They see risk, liquidity, opportunity, and contractual structure with greater technical precision. But this precision brings the doctrine close to modern finance, where all burdens of capital can be priced, all time can be monetized, and all risk can become return.

The relation between loss, foregone gain, and risk becomes the central organizing problem. Damnum emergens is the cleanest title because it concerns actual loss. Something has been lost, damaged, paid, or incurred. Compensation restores equality. Lucrum cessans is more dangerous because it concerns something not obtained. The gain may be real, but it may also be imagined. Periculum sortis is structurally powerful because it concerns exposure to loss, but it can become universal if ordinary default risk is enough. Carentia pecuniae is perhaps most dangerous because it treats the absence of money itself as compensable, thereby approaching the time value of money.

Lessius and Lugo help us see these titles as a spectrum. At one end stands concrete damage. At the other stands abstract time. In between are foregone opportunity, risk, liquidity, and contractual commitment. The further one moves from documented loss toward abstract financial possibility, the greater the danger that compensation becomes extraction.

This late scholastic synthesis is therefore not simply a technical chapter in the history of usury. It is the moment when Christian moral theology almost touches the conceptual world of modern finance. The categories are familiar: risk premium, liquidity premium, opportunity cost, term commitment, probability of repayment, valuation under uncertainty. But the moral grammar is different. Modern finance tends to treat these as pricing inputs. The scholastic tradition treats them as possible titles that must be morally tested.

That difference is decisive. In modern finance, if capital is risky, it demands return. If capital is illiquid, it demands return. If capital has alternative uses, it demands return. If time passes, it demands return. The scholastic question is not what capital demands. It is what justice permits. Does the claimed return correspond to a real burden? Is the burden borne by the claimant? Is the amount proportionate? Is the burden already compensated elsewhere? Does the contract protect or exploit the vulnerable? Does the structure create solidarity or domination?

Lessius and Lugo also remind us that moral theology must understand technical instruments without being captured by them. They are finance-aware, but not finance-subservient. They take contracts seriously, but they do not let contract language settle the moral question. They analyze risk, but they ask who bears it. They analyze foregone profit, but they ask whether it is concrete. They analyze liquidity, but they resist turning time itself into a title.

This is exactly the discipline required for non-extractive capital today. We cannot simply condemn every return. Nor can we bless every return because it can be assigned to some financial concept. We must ask whether the return is restorative, participatory, or extractive. We must identify the title, document the burden, test proportionality, and examine the structure of downside.

The late scholastic systematizers give us both permission and warning. Permission to think carefully about real economic burdens. Warning that every real burden can become a pretext when detached from moral limits. Lessius shows how commercial reality presses the doctrine toward technical sophistication. Lugo shows how necessary it is to resist the moment when sophistication becomes dissolution.

Their place in the genealogy is therefore pivotal. After them, the tradition has acquired a powerful set of tools: actual loss, foregone gain, capital risk, liquidity deprivation, labor, expense, partnership, exchange, and anti-fraud analysis. But it has also acquired the danger that these tools may be used to justify almost anything.

The question that remains is whether the doctrine can preserve its soul after becoming so technically refined. Can it still say no? Can it still identify extraction? Can it still protect the borrower, the poor, the mission, and the common good? Can it still distinguish capital that serves life from capital that merely imitates the vocabulary of service? Lessius and Lugo leave us at that threshold.

sections.title: 34.6 Later Settlement and Backlash
paragraphs: By the time we reach the later manualist tradition, the doctrine of usury has become both stable and contested. Stable, because the basic framework is now familiar: gain from the mutuum itself remains illicit; surplus may be justified only by some title external to the loan; actual loss, foregone gain, risk of principal, penalties, labor, expense, partnership, exchange, and related cases must be carefully distinguished. Contested, because the very abundance of titles has made the prohibition seem fragile. The more refined the analysis becomes, the easier it is for critics to ask whether anything meaningful remains of the original ban.

This is the world of Benedict XIV, Alphonsus Liguori, and Daniele Concina. It is a world in which earlier distinctions have hardened into manualist categories, but also a world in which moral theologians continue to worry that the categories have been stretched too far. The question is no longer simply whether usury is wrong. It is whether the doctrine still has teeth after centuries of exceptions, refinements, and commercial accommodations.

Benedict XIV's Vix pervenit is central to this later settlement. The encyclical does not abolish the older doctrine. It reaffirms the basic principle: a loan contract by its nature requires the return of the amount lent, and gain demanded from that loan itself is usurious. The lender cannot claim surplus merely because the borrower has had use of the money. The old heart of the doctrine remains intact.

At the same time, Vix pervenit does not deny the possibility of extrinsic titles. This is crucial. Benedict XIV does not collapse the tradition into a crude rule that every payment above principal is always usury. He preserves the distinction between illicit gain from the loan itself and legitimate compensation arising from some other just title. The point is not to erase the work of the canonists, Aquinas, Salamanca, Lessius, or Lugo. The point is to insist that the titles must remain truly extrinsic. They must not become pretexts.

This is the central concern of the later settlement: title versus pretext. A title names a real reason in justice. A pretext borrows the name of a title while lacking its substance. A late penalty is a title only when delay is culpable, the due date is realistic, the penalty is proportionate, and the lender does not desire default. Actual loss is a title only when loss is real, documented, caused, and not double-counted. Foregone profit is a title only when the alternative use is concrete, intended, probable, and truly interrupted. Risk is a title only when capital can actually be lost and the lender bears that loss. Expense is a title only when it corresponds to actual cost. Labor is a title only when labor has truly been performed. Otherwise, the word "title" becomes a costume for usury.

Benedict XIV's importance lies in preserving this boundary at a moment when economic practice was increasingly comfortable with interest. The surrounding world was moving toward the normalization of money over time. Markets, states, merchants, and lenders increasingly treated interest as ordinary. The Church's doctrine had to decide whether it would simply follow the market or continue to judge it. Vix pervenit keeps judgment alive. It says, in effect: legal custom and commercial convenience do not determine moral truth.

Alphonsus Liguori represents the later manualist settlement in a pastoral key. He inherits a doctrine already full of distinctions and controversies. His task is not to invent the extrinsic titles anew, but to guide confessors through them with prudence. Alphonsus holds together moral seriousness and pastoral moderation. He does not wish to burden consciences unnecessarily, but neither does he wish to declare every common practice innocent merely because it is common.

In the Alphonsian settlement, some titles that had once been controversial are more broadly accepted under conditions. Lucrum cessans, for example, becomes a permissible title in the common teaching when properly limited. But the conditions still matter. A merely possible gain is not enough. The lender cannot simply say, "My money might have earned something." The foregone gain must be sufficiently grounded in reality. The old danger remains: if every lender can invoke abstract opportunity cost, then the doctrine has collapsed.

Similarly, penalties, damages, risk, and expenses are treated with pastoral practicality, but not without limits. The confessor must still ask whether the payment is truly compensatory, whether it is proportionate, whether the lender has intended profit from the loan, and whether the borrower has been injured. Alphonsus's moral theology is therefore not a capitulation to finance. It is an attempt to preserve workable moral guidance in a world where commerce has become too complex for simple slogans.

Yet the very moderation of the later manuals provoked backlash. Daniele Concina represents the rigorist anxiety that the doctrine has been hollowed out. From this perspective, the late scholastic and manualist tradition has multiplied exceptions until the prohibition survives in words but not in practice. The risk title, the lost-profit title, the liquidity title, penalties, exchange contracts, public debt, and other devices all seem to create pathways by which lenders receive more than principal while denying that they are taking usury.

Concina's concern is not absurd. It arises from a real danger within the tradition. Every extrinsic title can be abused. Poena conventionalis can become engineered default. Damnum emergens can become fictive damage. Lucrum cessans can become abstract opportunity cost. Periculum sortis can become universal risk premium. Carentia pecuniae can become the time value of money. Exchange can become dry exchange. Partnership can become guaranteed debt. Expense reimbursement can become institutional profit. A doctrine meant to restrain extraction can be colonized by extraction if its categories are stretched beyond recognition.

Concina's backlash therefore performs an important function. Even if one does not accept the most rigorous conclusions, his critique reminds the tradition that moral theology must be able to say no. A doctrine that can always find a title for surplus has ceased to be a doctrine. It has become a legitimating machine. Concina forces the question: are the titles still tests, or have they become permissions?

That question remains essential. The purpose of the extrinsic titles was not to create loopholes but to preserve justice in complex cases; the titles were meant to distinguish compensation from extraction. But when the commercial imagination becomes dominant, the same titles can be inverted. They can become a way to guarantee capital's growth while maintaining theological respectability. That inversion is precisely what the backlash fears.

The later settlement and backlash therefore reveal the internal drama of the entire tradition. On one side stands the need for nuance. Not every surplus is usury: real losses exist, real risks exist, real labor exists, real expenses exist, real exchange differences exist, and real foregone opportunities may sometimes exist. A moral theology that refuses to recognize these things becomes unjust in the opposite direction, forcing one party to bear burdens that should be restored.

On the other side stands the need for discipline. Not every burden claimed by capital is real: not every risk is borne by the lender, not every opportunity cost is concrete, not every fee is reimbursement, not every partnership shares downside, and not every exchange is genuine. A moral theology that recognizes every claimed burden becomes a servant of capital rather than a judge of it.

Benedict XIV, Alphonsus, and Concina belong together because they show the three movements required for a living doctrine. Benedict reaffirms the principle: gain from the loan itself remains usury, and extrinsic titles must be real. Alphonsus translates the accumulated distinctions into pastoral guidance, seeking a prudent path through complex practice. Concina warns that prudence can become permissiveness, and that a tradition of exceptions can lose its ascetical edge.

The modern relevance is direct. Today, nearly every financial return can be described in morally plausible language. A late fee compensates delay. A management fee compensates labor. A preferred return compensates risk. A liquidity premium compensates lock-up. A success fee compensates value creation. A monitoring fee compensates oversight. A liquidation preference compensates early risk. A discount rate compensates time. Each explanation may be partly true. Each may also be a pretext.

The later tradition teaches us that the question cannot be settled by vocabulary. We must test substance. Does the payment restore equality, or does it create domination? Does it compensate a real burden, or monetize bargaining power? Does the investor bear downside, or shift it to founders, workers, suppliers, community, or mission? Does the contract serve the common good, or simply protect capital's guaranteed growth?

The backlash also helps us recover the ascetical dimension of the usury doctrine. The doctrine was never only about technical fairness. It was about disciplining the desire of the creditor. It asked the person with surplus not to turn another's need into a revenue stream. It asked capital not to imagine itself as naturally entitled to increase. It asked Christians to see the borrower as neighbor before counterparty. If the extrinsic titles lose this ascetical dimension, they become spiritually dangerous even when technically sophisticated.

This is why the later settlement should not be read simply as the end of the story. It is a mirror. It shows the tradition wrestling with the same problem that modern non-extractive finance must face. How can we recognize legitimate compensation without normalizing extraction? How can we build institutions that are sustainable without making capital sovereign? How can we price real burdens without turning time, need, risk language, and legal complexity into machines of accumulation?

The answer cannot be pure rigorism, because real burdens must be compensated. But it also cannot be permissive sophistication, because cleverness is one of extraction's favorite tools. The answer must be disciplined discernment: title, proof, proportionality, anti-fraud analysis, and attention to vulnerability.

Benedict XIV, Alphonsus Liguori, and Daniele Concina leave us with this unresolved but fruitful tension. The doctrine survives only if it can do both things at once: permit compensation where justice requires it, and refuse every attempt to convert compensation into guaranteed yield. That is the later settlement. That is the backlash. And that is the burden any renewed moral economy must inherit.

chapters.part: Part X — The Great Syntheses and the Historians Who Help Us Reconstruct Them

chapters.title: Modern Scholarship and What Each Scholar Contributes
chapters.label: Chapter 35

sections.title: 35.1 John T. Noonan
paragraphs: Any modern reconstruction of scholastic usury doctrine must begin with John T. Noonan. His classic study, The Scholastic Analysis of Usury (1957), remains one of the indispensable modern works on the subject, not because it answers every question, but because it shows the full historical drama of the doctrine. Noonan helps us see that the Christian teaching on usury was not a flat, static prohibition mechanically repeated across the centuries. It was a living and contested tradition of moral reasoning, shaped by Scripture, Roman law, canon law, scholastic theology, commercial practice, pastoral judgment, and institutional pressure.

Noonan's great contribution is historical breadth. He follows the doctrine across time, watching it develop from patristic and canonical condemnation into increasingly refined scholastic distinctions. He shows how theologians and canonists struggled with the same recurring problem: how to preserve the prohibition against gain from a loan as such while also recognizing cases in which a payment above principal might arise from something else. Penalties, damages, exchange contracts, partnership, annuities, public debt, risk, and foregone profit all enter the story. The doctrine does not remain simple because economic life does not remain simple.

This is why Noonan is so useful for the present book. He makes visible the difference between usury doctrine as slogan and usury doctrine as analytical tradition. The slogan says, "Interest was forbidden." The analytical tradition asks: what kind of contract is this? Is it a mutuum? Is the surplus intrinsic to the loan? Is there an external title? Is the payment compensation, penalty, partnership profit, exchange difference, risk premium, or hidden yield? Noonan helps recover the intellectual world in which these questions mattered.

He also helps correct the common modern caricature that medieval theologians simply failed to understand economics. The scholastic tradition did understand a great deal about contract form, risk, ownership, exchange, intention, fraud, damages, and commercial practice. It may not have used modern economic vocabulary, but it was far from naive. One of the striking lessons of Noonan's work is that scholastic authors often asked more morally precise questions than modern finance does. Modern finance asks what return capital can command. Scholastic moral theology asks what title, if any, justifies the return in justice.

At the same time, Noonan does not romanticize the tradition. He shows development, tension, pressure, and, in some readings, erosion. The doctrine becomes more complex partly because theologians are genuinely trying to distinguish compensation from exploitation. But it also becomes more complex because commercial society keeps finding ways to press against the prohibition. Every refinement creates the possibility of evasion. Every exception can become a loophole. Every title can be stretched.

Noonan is especially helpful in tracing the way the extrinsic titles gradually alter the practical force of the usury prohibition. Damnum emergens is relatively easy to understand because actual loss fits naturally within restitution. A lender who suffers a real, provable loss because of the borrower's action may be restored without necessarily profiting from the loan. But lucrum cessans is more dangerous because it moves from actual loss to profit not made. If strictly limited to concrete foregone gain, it may still be compensatory. If generalized into opportunity cost, it threatens to normalize interest. Periculum sortis raises a similar danger. If the lender truly bears risk of principal, compensation may be intelligible. But if ordinary default risk justifies return, the doctrine may collapse. Noonan's history allows us to see these shifts as part of a long movement from prohibition toward accommodation.

This does not mean that Noonan should be read as the final word. His framework can sometimes make the history appear as a gradual, almost inevitable movement from medieval prohibition to modern interest. That story is powerful, but it can become too linear, and it can understate the continuing moral intelligence inside later scholastic analysis. Lessius and Lugo, for example, are not merely agents of decline. They are also serious thinkers trying to preserve the difference between title and pretext in a far more complex commercial world. The fact that their distinctions are dangerous does not mean they are foolish.

Noonan's usefulness is therefore greatest when read alongside other scholars. Odd Langholm helps deepen the analysis of scholastic value, price, and exchange. Giacomo Todeschini opens the Franciscan and social-belonging dimensions of medieval economic thought. Raymond de Roover clarifies the technical world of exchange and banking. Fabio Monsalve helps organize the late Spanish scholastic treatment of the extrinsic titles. Wim Decock brings Lessius into the history of contract law and moral theology. Pierre Januard complicates any simplistic reading of Aquinas on risk. Noonan gives us the great narrative arc, but the arc needs to be filled in, corrected, and deepened by more specialized work.

For the purposes of this book, Noonan's chief value is that he shows how the usury doctrine became a moral diagnostic system. The doctrine was not simply "no more than principal." It became a way of asking whether surplus came from the loan itself or from some external burden. That diagnostic method is precisely what we need for modern non-extractive finance. When a fund charges management fees, default fees, preferred returns, liquidity premiums, risk premiums, monitoring fees, or carried interest, the old question returns: what is the title? What is the payment for? Does it compensate labor, loss, risk, or service? Or does it merely guarantee capital's growth?

Noonan also helps us see the danger of moral drift. A doctrine may begin with a clear evangelical and biblical intuition: do not exploit the needy borrower; do not make money from another's vulnerability; do not treat the neighbor as a revenue stream. Over time, however, legal and commercial distinctions multiply. Each distinction may have a reason. But the cumulative effect may be to weaken the original moral force. This is not only a medieval problem but a modern institutional one. Mission drift often happens through reasonable exceptions.

That insight is central to a renewed moral economy. Non-extractive finance cannot survive by good intentions alone. It needs categories, rules, accounting, governance, and audit. But it must also constantly ask whether its categories have become self-justifying. Are management fees still cost recovery? Are risk premiums still tied to real downside? Are returns still linked to mission-aligned surplus? Are penalties still protective, or have they become revenue? Are legal forms still serving the moral form, or concealing its loss?

Noonan's history is therefore more than background; it is a warning. The tradition's movement from prohibition to titles, from titles to refinements, from refinements to widespread permissibility, shows how difficult it is to keep capital morally disciplined once it becomes fluent in exception. But it also shows that the answer is not to abandon distinctions. Without distinctions, we cannot tell the difference between exploitation and legitimate compensation. The answer is to keep the distinctions accountable to their original purpose.

That original purpose was not hostility to all finance. It was the protection of justice. It was the insistence that surplus must not arise merely from another person's need, delay, weakness, or dependency. It was the refusal to let money claim growth by nature. It was the demand that the lender remain a neighbor.

Noonan helps us recover the seriousness of that tradition. He shows us its complexity, its development, its compromises, and its unresolved tensions. He also gives us the historical confidence to say that moral finance has a deeper lineage than modern markets admit. Long before contemporary debates about predatory lending, private equity, venture capital, public debt, or non-extractive capital, Christian theologians were already asking: when does compensation become extraction?

That is why Noonan belongs at the head of this chapter on modern scholarship. His work opens the archive, shows the terrain, and gives us the broad map of the scholastic struggle. But it also leaves us with the task this book has taken up: to read that history not merely as the story of how interest became possible, but as the story of how moral theology tried (and must still try) to distinguish capital that restores from capital that dominates.

sections.title: 35.2 Odd Langholm
paragraphs: Odd Langholm belongs among the indispensable modern historians of scholastic economic thought because he helps us see the intellectual machinery behind the doctrine. If John T. Noonan gives us the broad historical drama of usury's development, Langholm gives us the finer analytical structure: value, price, exchange, need, utility, risk, and the ways medieval theologians tried to reason morally about economic life before the emergence of modern economics as a separate discipline.

Langholm's importance lies partly in his refusal to treat scholastic economic thought as primitive economics wearing theological clothing. He reads the scholastics as serious analysts. They were not modern economists, and they should not be forced into modern categories. But neither were they economically naive. They asked detailed questions about equivalence in exchange, the just price, merchant profit, fraud, compulsion, scarcity, estimation, and the social conditions under which value is formed. Their work belongs to moral theology, but it is also a sophisticated theory of economic action.

This matters for the usury question because usury doctrine cannot be understood apart from the wider scholastic account of exchange. The problem of usury is not only a problem of loans. It is a problem of equality. In commutative justice, one party must not take more than is due from another. A sale, a lease, a partnership, an exchange contract, a wage bargain, and a loan all create different forms of relation, but each must be judged by whether it preserves or violates justice between persons. Langholm helps us see that the usury doctrine sits inside this larger moral universe.

In that universe, price is not simply whatever the strongest party can obtain. The just price is not a fixed mathematical point detached from market reality, but neither is it pure bargaining power. It is formed through common estimation, need, scarcity, quality, labor, risk, utility, and social judgment. The scholastics understood that prices move. They understood that merchants may buy in one place and sell in another. They understood that costs and risks matter. But they also insisted that exchange remains morally accountable. Market price may inform justice, but it does not abolish justice.

Langholm shows how deeply scholastic authors cared about the distinction between value and exploitation. A thing may be worth more in one context than another. A merchant may create value by transporting goods, assuming risk, storing goods, or making them available where they are needed. A seller may sometimes charge more because the good has special value, or because circumstances have changed. But the same fluidity can be abused. Scarcity can be manipulated. Ignorance can be exploited. Necessity can be priced. A person in distress can be made to pay not because the thing is justly worth more, but because they have no alternative.

This framework illuminates the extrinsic titles. Damnum emergens is intelligible because justice permits restoration for actual loss. If one party's action causes another to be worse off, compensation may restore equality. Lucrum cessans is more difficult because it concerns value that would have arisen but did not. Langholm's work on value and expectation helps us understand why this title becomes so contested. A future profit may be real enough to matter in commercial life, but it may also be speculative. The moral theologian must ask whether the claimed value is grounded in concrete circumstances or merely imagined by the lender.

The same is true for periculum sortis. Risk affects value. A claim exposed to real uncertainty is not equivalent to a claim that is secure. A merchant who risks capital in trade is not in the same position as a creditor who is guaranteed repayment. Langholm's attention to scholastic value theory helps us see why risk-bearing can become morally relevant. But it also helps us see why risk language must be disciplined. Risk changes value only when it is truly borne. If legal structure shifts downside away from the capital provider, then the appeal to risk becomes morally hollow.

Langholm also helps us avoid a common mistake in reading the scholastics: the temptation to separate their economic analysis from their moral anthropology. Medieval theologians did not imagine economic actors as isolated utility maximizers. They understood persons as neighbors, sinners, householders, merchants, rulers, poor borrowers, religious communities, and members of a moral order. Economic action was not morally neutral because human action is not morally neutral. Buying, selling, lending, pricing, and profiting all reveal the shape of desire.

This is why scholastic economic thought remains so useful for a book on non-extractive finance. Modern finance tends to reduce economic action to contract, price, risk, and return. Scholastic thought asks what kind of relationship is being created. Is the other party being treated as neighbor or opportunity? Does the contract restore equality or deepen dependency? Does profit arise from service, labor, risk, and usefulness, or from another person's weakness? Does price reflect real value, or does it monetize vulnerability?

Langholm also situates scholastic thought historically without flattening it into a single position. There is no one simple medieval theory of economics. Authors and contexts differ. Theologians, canonists, jurists, and confessors emphasize different things. The treatment of usury, just price, merchant profit, risk, and exchange develops over time. Langholm helps us see this development as an intellectual tradition rather than as a set of isolated quotations.

This is particularly important for the history of the extrinsic titles. The titles did not appear all at once in finished form. They emerged from many overlapping problems: penalties for delay, damages, merchant losses, risk-bearing partnerships, exchange contracts, public finance, and the practical need to distinguish compensation from gain. Langholm's broader work on scholastic economic reasoning gives us the background against which these titles become understandable. They are not random exceptions. They are attempts to preserve commutative justice under increasingly complex economic conditions.

At the same time, Langholm's scholarship helps us see where the tradition becomes unstable. The more scholastic authors recognize the complexity of value, the more difficult it becomes to maintain simple boundaries. If future value matters in pricing, why not in lending? If risk affects value in trade, why not in credit? If scarcity affects price, why not liquidity? If merchant skill and opportunity matter, why not foregone profit? Each question has moral force. Each also creates danger.

This is the great tension of scholastic economic thought. It is realistic enough to see that economic value is not static. But it is theological enough to resist the idea that value is simply whatever markets, power, or capital can command. Langholm helps us appreciate that tension rather than resolve it too quickly. The scholastics were not anti-market in any simple sense, nor market absolutists; they were trying to place exchange inside justice.

For modern readers, this is a major corrective. We often imagine moral finance as a choice between technical economics and ethical exhortation. Either one understands markets and therefore becomes permissive, or one condemns markets and becomes irrelevant. Langholm's scholastics show a third possibility: technical moral reasoning. One can understand price, risk, exchange, and commercial practice while still judging them by justice, charity, and the common good.

This third possibility is exactly what a renewed moral economy requires. Non-extractive finance cannot rest on sentiment. It must understand the instruments it judges. It must know how return is generated, how risk is allocated, how fees accumulate, how liquidation preferences work, how opportunity cost is invoked, how collateral shifts downside, and how governance rights create power. But technical understanding must not become moral surrender. Langholm's reconstruction of scholastic economic thought helps model this combination of analytical seriousness and moral accountability.

His work also helps us understand why the just price and usury belong together. Both doctrines resist the reduction of justice to consent. A person may consent under pressure. A borrower may agree because they are desperate. A seller may accept a low price because they have no alternative. A founder may sign extractive investment terms because the company will otherwise die. Formal agreement does not settle the moral question. The scholastics knew this. Langholm helps us see how deeply this concern shaped their economic thought.

This is especially relevant to venture capital and private equity. Modern investors often defend terms by pointing to sophistication, consent, and market practice. The founder signed, the company agreed, the valuation was negotiated, the term sheet was standard. But the scholastic tradition would ask more. What was the bargaining context? Was there distress? Was the price just? Was the return tied to real risk or to control? Was the downside shared? Did the contract preserve the mission, workers, and community, or did it make them subordinate to capital's exit?

Langholm's contribution is not that he directly answers these modern questions, but that he gives us the medieval intellectual resources to ask them well. He shows that scholastic economic thought was already concerned with the moral formation of value. He shows that price, exchange, and profit were never merely technical matters. He shows that the usury doctrine belongs to a larger attempt to prevent economic life from becoming a theater of domination.

There is also a caution in using Langholm. His work is detailed, historical, and conceptually careful. It resists easy appropriation. We should not simply recruit medieval scholastics as if they had already endorsed modern non-extractive finance, steward ownership, or impact investing. They lived in different legal, theological, and economic worlds. Their categories must be translated, not copied. But careful translation is exactly what this book is trying to do.

Langholm helps us perform that translation with discipline. From him we learn to take scholastic concepts seriously on their own terms before applying them to modern finance. Justum pretium, mutuum, societas, interesse, damnum emergens, lucrum cessans, and periculum sortis are not slogans. They are analytical tools formed inside a moral universe. If we detach them from that universe, we will either romanticize them or misuse them.

The central lesson Langholm offers is that medieval economic thought was a theology of ordered exchange. It asked how goods, money, risk, labor, and need could circulate without violating justice. It recognized that human beings must trade, borrow, lend, invest, and organize institutions. But it also recognized that each of these activities can become a site of sin. Economic sophistication does not remove the need for virtue. It increases it.

For the argument of this book, Langholm therefore supplies the deep background. Noonan shows the historical drama of usury's development. Langholm shows the moral-economic world in which that drama makes sense. He helps us see that the extrinsic titles were part of a larger scholastic attempt to distinguish legitimate compensation from unjust advantage, service from exploitation, and value from domination.

That distinction remains the heart of non-extractive finance. Capital must be allowed to serve, repair, participate, and sustain. But it must not be allowed to rename domination as price, or extraction as value. Langholm's scholastics knew that the market can reveal value, but they also knew that it can conceal injustice. Their question remains ours: what would exchange look like if it were governed by justice rather than by the maximum claim capital can enforce?

sections.title: 35.3 Giacomo Todeschini
paragraphs: Giacomo Todeschini is indispensable because he helps us see that medieval economic thought was never only about contracts, prices, and loans. It was also about belonging. His work on Franciscan economics, poverty, credit, and social inclusion reveals a dimension of the usury tradition that can easily disappear if we treat the doctrine as merely technical. The medieval debate over money was also a debate over who counted as trustworthy, who could participate in the community, who was excluded, and how economic behavior revealed one's moral and spiritual status.

This matters deeply for a book about non-extractive finance. Usury is not merely a bad pricing mechanism. It is a disorder of relationship. It changes the social meaning of the borrower. The debtor becomes an opportunity. The poor become risky. The excluded become costly. The creditor becomes entitled. A financial relation that should serve life becomes a mechanism for sorting persons into the worthy and the unworthy, the creditworthy and the disposable, the included and the excluded.

Todeschini shows how Franciscan thought developed a sophisticated moral-economic vocabulary around poverty and use. The Franciscans were not simply hostile to economic life. Their radical poverty forced them to think with unusual precision about ownership, use, exchange, necessity, and the social function of goods. If one renounces ownership, what does it mean to use things justly? If goods are not absolute possessions, how should they circulate? If wealth is entrusted rather than possessed without limit, what obligations follow?

This Franciscan lens changes the way we read the usury debate. The central issue is not only whether a lender has a legal title to surplus. It is whether the structure of lending supports or destroys the social body. Does credit allow the poor to remain participants in community? Does it restore households, work, and dignity? Or does it mark some people as permanently inferior, dependent, and exploitable?

The Monti di Pietà become especially significant in this light. They were not merely clever institutional solutions to a technical usury problem. They were attempts to build credit institutions that could protect the poor from predatory lending while preserving social participation. Small loans against pledges were not meant to generate profit for capital. They were meant to prevent desperate people from falling into worse forms of dependency. The issue was not simply "may the institution charge a small fee?" The deeper question was: can a Christian community create financial structures that keep vulnerable people inside the circle of economic life?

Todeschini helps us see that this question is social, not only contractual. A usurious society does not merely contain unjust loans. It produces forms of exclusion. Those without capital must pay to survive. Those with capital define the terms of participation. Creditworthiness becomes a moralized category. The poor are made to appear unreliable, dangerous, or burdensome, while the wealthy appear prudent and deserving. Finance then becomes a technology of social ranking.

The usury doctrine has often been narrated as a story of theological rigidity slowly giving way to economic reality. Todeschini complicates that story. The medieval concern was not simply that money should not breed money. It was that economic relations formed moral communities. Lending, pricing, pledging, and exchanging were ways of defining who belonged to the city, who could be trusted, who could act, who could flourish, and who would be pushed outside.

In this sense, Franciscan economics is not merely about poverty as lack. It is about poverty as a site where the truth of the economy is revealed. How a community treats those without surplus capital shows what that community believes goods are for. If goods exist for domination, the poor become instruments of gain. If goods exist for use, life, and communion, then credit must be ordered toward restoration rather than extraction.

Todeschini's attention to economic citizenship is particularly important for modern finance. Today, inclusion is often measured by access: access to credit, access to banking, access to investment, access to markets. But access alone is not enough. One can be included on extractive terms. A payday loan includes the borrower in the credit system. A predatory mortgage includes the homeowner in the housing market. A venture capital term sheet includes the founder in the startup ecosystem. Inclusion can become another form of capture if the terms of inclusion transfer disproportionate value, risk, or control to capital.

The Franciscan question is sharper: does the financial structure allow the person or community to live? Does it restore agency? Does it preserve dignity? Does it keep households, workers, founders, and missions from being reduced to collateral for someone else's return? Does it make participation possible without requiring surrender to domination?

This is why Todeschini's work belongs in the genealogy of non-extractive finance. Non-extractive finance cannot be satisfied with "access to capital" as its goal. Access to capital may be good, but only if the capital serves life. If the structure demands growth that the enterprise cannot bear, if it imposes exit pressure that distorts the mission, if it subordinates labor and community to investor priority, if it turns vulnerability into yield, then access has become extraction.

Todeschini also helps us understand the moral meaning of trust. Credit is never only a calculation. It is a judgment about persons. The Latin root of credit is tied to belief and trust. To extend credit is to believe that another can receive, use, and return value. But in societies structured by inequality, trust is unevenly distributed. Some people are presumed reliable; others must pay more because they are presumed risky. Some are given time; others are charged for delay. Some are rescued; others are punished. Finance distributes not only money, but credibility.

The medieval usury debate is therefore also a debate about the abuse of trust. The lender who takes usury violates trust by turning the borrower's need into profit. The borrower is not met as neighbor, but evaluated as income stream. The relation becomes spiritually disordered because the creditor's gain depends on the debtor's dependency. Todeschini's work helps us see that this disorder has communal effects. It shapes who is honored, who is ashamed, who is inside, who is outside.

The Franciscan tradition's emphasis on use rather than absolute possession is another powerful contribution. If goods are given for use, then ownership cannot be morally absolute. The owner is not sovereign over wealth as if wealth had no social purpose. Wealth is entrusted, and it must circulate toward life. This does not abolish property or contract, but it relativizes them. Property is morally judged by use. Capital is judged by what it enables or destroys.

This is directly relevant to steward ownership and mission-locked governance. A steward-owned company does not treat ownership as an unlimited right to extract. It separates governance from speculative profit rights, protects mission, and limits capital's claim. In a Franciscan key, this can be understood as a modern institutional attempt to discipline ownership by use. The question is not "how much can owners take?" but "how can the enterprise remain ordered toward its purpose?"

Todeschini does not give us a ready-made modern legal form, but he gives us a way of seeing. He helps us interpret financial structures as practices of inclusion or exclusion, communion or domination, use or possession, trust or suspicion. This is why his scholarship is so important for the moral economy project. He widens the frame beyond the contract and asks what kind of society the contract helps create.

This also changes how we understand the extrinsic titles. A title is not merely a technical permission to receive surplus. It is a test of whether a payment corresponds to a real burden without destroying social belonging. A late penalty may be compensatory in one case, but exclusionary in another if it traps vulnerable borrowers in spirals of default. Cost recovery may sustain a charitable lender, but become exclusionary if fees price out the poor. Risk compensation may be legitimate when risk is truly borne, but destructive when "risk" becomes the language by which already-excluded people are charged more for being poor.

Todeschini's work therefore pushes us to add a social diagnostic to the contractual diagnostic. It is not enough to ask: is there a title? We must also ask: what does the title do to belonging? Does it restore the borrower to participation, or mark the borrower as permanently costly? Does it strengthen the community, or create a creditor class living from the vulnerability of others? Does it support economic citizenship, or deny it?

This is especially important in the modern context of digital sovereignty, venture capital, and infrastructure finance. Ownership and finance determine who participates in the digital world, who governs platforms, who controls data, who benefits from public infrastructure, and who is excluded from technological power. A financing structure that extracts from mission-driven companies does not merely affect investors and founders. It shapes the public ecology of technology. It determines whether infrastructure serves communities or becomes another enclosure.

Todeschini helps us see that the moral economy of finance is inseparable from the moral economy of membership. Who gets to build? Who gets to govern? Who gets to remain? Who gets pushed out? Who pays for access? Who receives trust? Who bears suspicion? These are financial questions because they are theological questions about the body politic and the body of Christ.

His contribution also helps recover the Franciscan dimension of this book. Much of the technical usury tradition is often read through Dominican and scholastic lenses, especially Aquinas, Cajetan, Soto, and later systematizers. That is necessary. But the Franciscan tradition adds something essential: a sharper eye for poverty, use, exclusion, and the social meaning of economic life. Olivi, Bernardino, the Monti di Pietà, and the wider Franciscan moral imagination show that finance must be judged by whether it allows the vulnerable to live.

This is where Todeschini is most valuable. He teaches us that medieval economic thought was not only about avoiding individual sin. It was about forming a Christian society in which goods circulated toward use, participation, and salvation rather than greed, exclusion, and domination. That vision may not translate directly into modern pluralistic finance, but it challenges us at the root.

For non-extractive capital, the lesson is clear. A financing structure is not moral merely because it is legal, voluntary, market-rate, impact-branded, or even mission-aligned in its stated intention. It must be judged by the social relation it creates. Does it include on humane terms? Does it protect the vulnerable party? Does it keep capital from becoming a gatekeeper of belonging? Does it allow enterprises, workers, founders, and communities to remain agents of their own purpose?

Todeschini belongs in this chapter because he prevents the history of usury from becoming too narrow. He reminds us that the Church's moral concern with finance was never merely arithmetic. It was about the ordering of community. It was about whether money serves communion or exclusion. It was about whether the poor remain neighbors or become instruments.
