In that sense, Todeschini gives us one of the deepest questions in the whole book: what kind of belonging does this financial structure create? If the answer is dependency, humiliation, exclusion, or capture, then the structure remains morally suspect no matter how clever its titles. If the answer is restoration, participation, trust, and life, then finance begins to recover its proper place: not as master of the community, but as one of the tools by which the community learns to care for its members.

sections.title: 35.4 Raymond de Roover
paragraphs: Raymond de Roover (1904–1972), the Belgian historian of medieval and Renaissance banking whose The Rise and Decline of the Medici Bank appeared in 1963, is indispensable because he helps us understand the technical world of medieval and early modern finance from the inside. If Noonan gives us the grand history of scholastic usury doctrine, Langholm gives us the intellectual structure of value and exchange, and Todeschini restores the social and Franciscan dimensions of belonging, de Roover brings us into the counting house. He shows how merchants, bankers, money changers, bills of exchange, exchange fairs, and commercial instruments actually worked.

The usury debate was never only about abstract theology; it was also about instruments. A theologian could not judge foreign exchange without understanding what a bill of exchange did. A confessor could not distinguish legitimate exchange from disguised usury without knowing how exchange and re-exchange were structured. A canonist could not identify fraud merely by looking at the name of a contract. The moral question depended on technical reality.

De Roover's work on scholasticism, usury, banking, and foreign exchange is especially valuable because it corrects two opposite mistakes. The first mistake is to imagine medieval theologians as economically ignorant men condemning what they did not understand. The second mistake is to imagine medieval bankers as merely modern financiers trapped inside outdated religious language. De Roover shows a more interesting world: theologians and merchants were engaged in a complex negotiation over real instruments, real risks, real services, and real evasions.

The bill of exchange is central to this story. In its legitimate form, it was a practical and often necessary commercial instrument. It allowed merchants to move funds across distance without physically transporting coin. It enabled payment in another city, in another currency, at another fair, or through another banking network. It reduced risk, supported trade, and reflected real differences in place, currency, availability, and timing. A banker or merchant who facilitated such exchange performed a real service. Profit from that service was not automatically usury.

But the same instrument could be abused. A bill of exchange could become a way to lend money and receive more later while preserving the appearance of an exchange transaction. The key danger was fictitious exchange or dry exchange. If there was no real need to transfer funds, no genuine currency difference, no real commercial movement, and no meaningful exchange risk, then the transaction might simply be a loan disguised as exchange. The parties could arrange exchange and re-exchange so that one advanced money now and received more money later. The legal form was exchange; the moral substance was interest-bearing debt.

De Roover helps us see why this was difficult. The line between legitimate exchange and disguised loan was not always visible from the surface. Both could involve bills, time, different cities, and differences in amount. The moral theologian had to ask whether the exchange difference corresponded to something real. Was money genuinely being transferred between places? Were currencies actually exchanged? Were rates determined by market conditions? Did the banker perform a service? Was there risk? Or was the entire apparatus designed to create a fixed return on an advance?

This is the contract-classification problem at a high level of sophistication. De Roover's historical work shows that scholastic theologians had to become analysts of financial substance. It was not enough to ask whether the contract said "exchange." They had to determine whether the transaction functioned as exchange. This is precisely the same discipline needed today when examining convertible notes, preferred equity, revenue-based financing, sale-leasebacks, synthetic instruments, derivatives, or "partnerships" that behave like debt.

De Roover also helps us understand why the scholastics did not simply condemn bankers as such. Money changing, transfer, safekeeping, accounting, correspondence, and settlement could serve real economic purposes. The merchant banker could be a facilitator of trade, not merely a usurer. This matters because Christian moral theology was not meant to abolish commercial life. It was meant to discipline it. The question was not whether finance could perform useful work. The question was whether the payment received corresponded to that work, risk, or service, or whether it extracted surplus from a disguised loan.

This distinction is central to the whole tradition of extrinsic titles. The same numerical surplus can have different moral meanings depending on its source. If the surplus compensates actual exchange service, it may be legitimate. If it compensates real risk, it may be legitimate. If it compensates labor, administration, or transfer across place, it may be legitimate. But if the surplus is merely a fixed gain from money advanced over time, then it remains morally suspect, no matter how elaborate the instrument.

De Roover also helps explain the importance of Sant'Antonino of Florence and the late medieval exception-list tradition. Antonino lived in one of the great commercial centers of Europe, where banking and exchange were not theoretical matters. His moral theology had to speak into a world of merchants, bills, credit, public debt, and civic finance. De Roover's attention to Antonino helps show how scholastic moral analysis interacted with actual commercial practice. The theologian was not speaking into a vacuum. He was interpreting instruments that shaped the daily life of Florence and beyond.

It is tempting to narrate the development of usury doctrine as a simple progression: the Church condemned interest; commerce became more complex; theologians invented exceptions; eventually interest became acceptable. De Roover complicates this by showing that some of the "exceptions" corresponded to real economic differences. Exchange profit was not always a trick. A bill was not always a disguised loan. A banker was not always a usurer. Moral theology needed to distinguish real finance from false finance.

Yet de Roover also shows why commercial complexity could become a field of evasion. The more complex the instruments, the more opportunities existed for disguise. A straightforward usurious loan could be condemned easily. A structured exchange transaction required expertise to unravel. Moral evasion often becomes more effective as financial technique becomes more sophisticated. The sin does not disappear; it becomes harder to see.

This is one of de Roover's most important lessons for modern non-extractive finance. Complexity is not proof of legitimacy. A financial structure may be technically elegant, legally enforceable, market-standard, and still morally false. It may assign names to payments that do not correspond to their substance. A fee may function as interest. A partnership may function as secured debt. A risk premium may be charged by someone who has shifted away the risk. An exchange contract may function as a loan. A liquidity premium may simply monetize time. The theologian, or moral auditor, must understand the instrument well enough to see through it.

De Roover also helps us appreciate the importance of accounting. Bills of exchange, banking networks, and merchant finance depended on records. Who owed what? In what currency? Payable where? At what date? At what rate? To whom? Through which correspondent? Moral analysis could not be separated from the documentary structure of the transaction. The truth of the contract was partly visible in its books. If the books showed circular exchange with no commercial substance, that mattered. If they showed real transfer, service, risk, and settlement, that mattered too.

This connection between moral theology and bookkeeping is directly relevant to modern non-extractive capital. A fund that claims to be non-extractive must be able to show, in its own records, what each payment is for. Is this a management cost? Is this reimbursement? Is this risk-sharing return? Is this penalty? Is this actual loss? Is this foregone gain? Is this mission-aligned surplus? Or is this simply yield under another name? Like medieval exchange, modern finance must be audited not only legally, but morally.

De Roover also gives us a way to think about digital and cross-border finance today. Medieval bills of exchange solved problems of distance, currency, trust, and settlement. Modern financial infrastructure solves similar problems through payment systems, platforms, banking APIs, stablecoins, correspondent networks, and digital rails. These systems can serve real exchange and coordination. They can also hide extraction, fees, arbitrage, and power. The moral question remains: does the instrument serve human exchange, or does it create a disguised claim by which capital extracts from dependence?

For this book, de Roover's contribution is therefore both historical and methodological. Historically, he illuminates the world of bills, banking, and exchange that forced scholastic theologians to refine their analysis. Methodologically, he teaches us to examine financial instruments in detail before judging them. We cannot make moral claims about finance from too great a distance. We must know how the transaction works.

But de Roover also warns us not to be seduced by technical understanding. To understand an instrument is not to bless it. The banker may perform a real service, or may hide usury inside exchange. The bill may transfer value across distance, or may circle value back with profit. The contract may serve trade, or may simulate trade in order to justify yield. The moral task is to tell the difference.

This makes de Roover especially important for a renewed moral economy. Non-extractive finance will require new instruments, or at least old instruments used differently. Steward-owned capital, revenue-sharing agreements, capped returns, redeemable equity, patient capital, mission-locked funds, public-interest infrastructure finance, and community investment vehicles all involve technical design. They must be understood structurally. Good intentions will not be enough. The instrument itself must embody the moral claim.

De Roover helps us see that this has always been true. Medieval theologians did not only preach against greed. They asked whether the bill was real. Whether the exchange was real. Whether the risk was real. Whether the service was real. Whether the surplus was real compensation or hidden usury. Their discipline was not anti-financial; it was anti-fraud.

The modern equivalent is clear. A financing structure should not be judged by its label: impact, patient, catalytic, blended, mission-aligned, steward-owned, non-extractive. It must be judged by what it actually does. Does capital bear downside? Are fees tied to costs? Are returns capped and proportionate? Is mission protected? Are workers and founders subordinated? Is time monetized? Is risk shifted? Is liquidity priced twice? Are penalties revenue? Are there circular flows that make extraction look like service?

De Roover's scholarship equips us to ask these questions with historical humility. The medieval world was not ours. Bills of exchange are not convertible notes. Florentine bankers are not venture capital funds. But the moral problem of disguise remains strikingly continuous. Finance constantly invents forms that can serve real needs or conceal unjust gain. The task of moral theology is to understand the form well enough to discern the truth.

That is why Raymond de Roover belongs in this chapter. He teaches us that the history of usury cannot be written from the pulpit alone; it must also be written from the ledger. And if we want capital that serves life, we must be willing to read the ledger with theological eyes.

sections.title: 35.5 Fabio Monsalve
paragraphs: Fabio Monsalve is especially useful for this book because he brings focused attention to the late Spanish scholastics and to the extrinsic titles as a family of moral-financial arguments. Where some histories of usury doctrine emphasize the broad movement from prohibition to permissibility, Monsalve helps us see the inner logic of the titles themselves: damnum emergens, lucrum cessans, and periculum sortis as distinct but increasingly related ways of thinking about compensable detriment.

The late scholastic tradition is often misunderstood. It is easy to treat Salamanca and the later scholastics as a transitional stage on the way to modern interest, as if their only historical role were to weaken an older prohibition until ordinary finance could emerge. There is some truth in that story, but it is incomplete. The late scholastics were not merely dissolving doctrine. They were trying to determine what justice required in a commercial world where loss, risk, exchange, and opportunity had become increasingly difficult to separate.

Monsalve's contribution is to clarify that development. He helps show how the extrinsic titles emerge, become named, gain acceptance, and eventually become organized into a broader theory. In particular, he is useful for understanding the chronology and relative moral status of the major titles. Damnum emergens is accepted earlier and more easily because it concerns actual loss. Lucrum cessans is more controversial because it concerns profit not obtained. Periculum sortis becomes increasingly important as theologians confront risk, uncertainty, and the valuation of claims.

The sequence shows a movement from the concrete to the more abstract. Actual loss is easiest to justify because something has been damaged, paid, lost, or incurred. The lender is not asking to profit from the loan, only not to be made worse off. This fits naturally within commutative justice and restitution. If equality has been disturbed by a real loss, compensation can restore it.

Foregone profit is harder. The lender says not, "I lost what I had," but "I failed to gain what I would have gained." The claim may be true. A merchant may have had a concrete, intended, probable use for the funds. Lending the money may have interrupted that use. But the claim may also be fictive. Anyone can imagine a missed opportunity after the fact. Every sum of money can be imagined as potentially profitable. If lucrum cessans becomes abstract opportunity cost, then the usury doctrine is in danger. Monsalve's work helps us see why this title had to be both admitted and disciplined.

Risk of principal is different again. Periculum sortis does not primarily concern an event after the loan, such as delay or damage, nor a lost alternative use. It concerns the structure of the transaction itself. The lender may be exposed to the possibility that the principal will not be recovered. A risky claim is not the same as a certain claim. In that sense, risk can affect value. But here too the danger is obvious. Every loan contains some risk of nonpayment. If that ordinary risk is enough to justify surplus, then every loan can become interest-bearing.

Monsalve's reading of the late scholastics reveals the internal discipline of the titles. The titles are not merely labels. Each one has conditions. Each one names a particular kind of burden. Each one can be abused if detached from proof, causality, proportionality, and moral substance. The late scholastics increasingly recognize the relatedness of the titles, but they also need to preserve their differences.

Juan de Lugo is especially important in Monsalve's reconstruction because Lugo gathers the major titles under a broader account of detriment. Risk, lost gain, and actual loss can be understood as different forms of harm or burden. This is a powerful synthesis. It allows moral theology to see that a lender may be disadvantaged in more than one way. The lender may lose something already possessed, fail to receive something reasonably expected, or bear exposure to the loss of capital itself. All of these can be treated as ways in which equality may be disturbed.

But Lugo's synthesis is also dangerous if read carelessly. If every inconvenience, possibility, risk, or absence of money becomes a "loss of a sort," then the doctrine loses its ability to restrain. A universalized theory of detriment can become a universal justification for return. Monsalve's work helps us see this tension clearly. Lugo matters because he brings coherence to the titles, but his coherence must not become overextension.

For this book, Monsalve is particularly helpful because we are not simply asking whether medieval and early modern theologians "allowed interest." We are asking how they distinguished compensation from extraction. The extrinsic titles are best understood as a diagnostic system. They ask: what burden does the surplus correspond to? Is the burden actual loss, foregone gain, risk of principal, labor, expense, or delay? Is the burden real? Is it external to the loan? Is it caused by the transaction? Is it proportionate? Is it already compensated elsewhere?

Monsalve helps organize this diagnostic system historically. He shows that the titles were not equally obvious, equally accepted, or equally safe. Damnum emergens is close to restitution. Lucrum cessans moves toward opportunity. Periculum sortis moves toward risk pricing. Together, they form a spectrum. As one moves from actual damage to expected gain to uncertain capital exposure, the moral analysis becomes more commercially realistic but also more vulnerable to abstraction.

This spectrum is directly relevant to modern finance. A modern investor may claim a return because of actual costs incurred. That resembles damnum emergens or expense reimbursement. The same investor may claim a return because capital could have been deployed elsewhere. That resembles lucrum cessans. The investor may claim a return because capital is at risk. That resembles periculum sortis. The investor may also claim a liquidity premium because capital is locked up, which touches carentia pecuniae. Modern finance often combines all these claims at once.

The danger is that the claims accumulate. A fund may charge management fees for labor, origination fees for transaction costs, preferred returns for time, risk premiums for uncertainty, liquidation preferences for downside protection, default interest for delay, and carried interest for upside. Some of these may be legitimate in some contexts. But together they can create double-counting and domination. Monsalve's reconstruction of the titles helps us disaggregate the claims. What is each payment for? Which title justifies it? Has the same burden been counted twice? Is the capital provider actually bearing the burden named?

This disaggregation is essential for non-extractive capital. Without it, mission language can hide conventional financial accumulation. A fund can say it is patient, catalytic, or impact-oriented while still demanding the full modern stack of capital claims: fees, priority, downside protection, upside participation, liquidity premiums, and control rights. Scholastic analysis, especially as clarified through Monsalve's work, asks us to separate each claim and test it.

Monsalve also helps us avoid an overly Dominican or Aquinas-only reading of the tradition. Aquinas is foundational, but the doctrine does not stop with him. Later commercial life raised questions Aquinas did not systematize in full. The Spanish scholastics and Jesuit systematizers had to address new forms of exchange, credit, risk, and opportunity. Their answers must be judged critically, but they cannot be ignored. They show moral theology trying to remain alive inside economic change.

This is important for our own project because modern non-extractive finance cannot simply quote Aquinas and stop. Aquinas gives us the grammar: the mutuum, commutative justice, actual loss, partnership, and the danger of selling what one does not yet have. But contemporary finance presents problems that require further analysis: portfolio theory, venture capital, revenue sharing, capped returns, mission locks, downside allocation, governance rights, liquidity, and systemic risk. The late scholastics show one historical example of moral theology extending itself into new economic terrain.

Monsalve also shows that the late scholastics were not careless about proof. The titles require causality and evidence. A loss must be shown. A foregone profit must be connected to a real alternative. Risk must be borne, not merely invoked. This is a deeply modern insight. Moral finance requires documentation. It is not enough to claim a title; one must show the burden.

This is where accounting becomes important. A non-extractive fund should be able to document the moral basis of its payments. Administrative costs should be traceable. Losses should be evidenced. Risk-bearing should be visible in the legal structure. Foregone opportunities should not be vague appeals to market return. Penalties should be tied to actual harm and should not become revenue. The titles require books, records, and audit.

Monsalve's reconstruction also points to the theological importance of proportionality. Even when a title is real, it does not justify unlimited payment. Actual loss permits restoration, not enrichment. Foregone gain permits compensation only up to the value of the lost gain. Risk permits compensation only in proportion to genuine exposure. Administrative cost permits reimbursement, not profit extraction. The title measures and limits the claim.

This principle is crucial in modern capital design. A risk-bearing investor may deserve upside, but not infinite upside if the downside is capped or shifted. A fund manager may deserve compensation, but not unlimited fees detached from labor. A lender may deserve compensation for delay, but not a penalty structure that makes default profitable. A mission-aligned investor may deserve a return, but not one that hollows out the mission it claims to support.

Monsalve therefore contributes more than historical detail. He gives us a way to structure the moral audit of capital. His work helps us ask whether a modern financial return corresponds to a specific, bounded, documented burden or whether it is simply the generalized claim of capital to growth. That is the central distinction of this book.

There is, however, a caution. Because Monsalve helps clarify the late scholastic expansion of the titles, one might be tempted to treat that expansion as full authorization for modern finance. That would be a mistake. The late scholastics operated within a moral universe in which usury remained a sin, the mutuum remained restricted, and surplus required justification. Their categories were not meant to bless the modern assumption that capital naturally deserves return. They were meant to discipline surplus by tying it to real burdens.

For this reason, Monsalve should be read as a guide to moral structure, not as a permission slip. His work helps us reconstruct how the tradition thought, where it expanded, and where it became vulnerable. It helps us understand why damnum emergens, lucrum cessans, and periculum sortis matter, but it also helps us see why they must be handled carefully.

In the architecture of this book, Monsalve stands at the point where historical theology becomes diagnostic method. The extrinsic titles are no longer scattered exceptions. They become a matrix. Actual loss, foregone gain, and risk of capital become distinct tests of whether surplus is restorative or extractive. Lugo's synthesis becomes especially important because it shows how these tests can be gathered under a broader theory of detriment, but only if the theory remains disciplined by proof, causality, proportionality, and anti-fraud analysis.

That is why Fabio Monsalve is essential. He helps us see the extrinsic titles not as loopholes and not merely as steps toward modern interest, but as a contested moral technology. They are tools for asking whether capital is being compensated for something real or whether it is learning to disguise its demand for guaranteed growth. And that question remains the heart of non-extractive finance: what exactly is capital being paid for?

sections.title: 35.6 Wim Decock
paragraphs: Wim Decock is especially important for understanding the transition from scholastic moral theology into modern contract thinking. His work on Leonardus Lessius, early modern contract law, and the moral theology of economic life helps us see that the late scholastic tradition was not simply a set of opinions about interest. It was a sophisticated legal-theological analysis of obligations, promises, risk, consent, restitution, and contractual justice.

The extrinsic titles are not merely economic concepts but contract concepts. They ask when one party may justly receive more than principal, but they do so by examining the structure of the obligation. What was promised? What was transferred? Who owns the capital? Who bears risk? What loss was caused? What gain was prevented? What burden was voluntarily assumed? What does the contract actually do?

Decock's work helps us recover this juridical precision. Late scholastic moral theologians were deeply interested in contracts because contracts shape moral relations. A contract is not only a private instrument for coordinating preferences. It is a way of creating obligations in justice. It can repair equality, but it can also break equality. It can express trust, but it can also conceal domination. It can make cooperation possible, but it can also formalize exploitation.

Lessius stands at the center of Decock's contribution. Lessius is one of the figures through whom scholastic theology comes closest to modern commercial reasoning without simply becoming modern finance. He understands that contracts can be complex. He sees that money may be committed, risk may be borne, opportunities may be lost, liquidity may be surrendered, and parties may structure obligations in ways that do not fit neatly into the simplest medieval categories. But he still works within a moral universe in which gain from the mutuum itself remains forbidden unless a real external title exists.

Decock shows how Lessius's contract analysis is both expansive and disciplined. Lessius is willing to take commercial practice seriously. He does not treat every financial innovation as automatically corrupt. He asks whether a particular contractual form creates a legitimate title. But this does not mean that the market settles the moral question. The contract must be analyzed according to justice, not merely enforceability.

This is especially important for the title of periculum sortis. Decock's work helps clarify how capital risk becomes thinkable in contract terms. If the lender genuinely bears the risk of losing principal, the lender's position is not the same as that of a creditor with a guaranteed claim. A risky claim has a different value from a certain claim. If the lender truly assumes exposure, a payment may be analyzed as compensation for risk rather than gain from the loan as such.

But the word "truly" matters. Risk cannot merely be invoked. It must be located in the contract. Can principal actually be lost? Does the borrower remain personally liable regardless of outcome? Is there collateral? Are there guarantees? Is the claim senior? Can the lender enforce repayment against unrelated assets? Is the lender protected while still charging for risk? Decock's contract-sensitive reading helps us see that risk is not a vague atmosphere around finance; it is legally and economically allocated.

This insight is essential for modern non-extractive finance. Investors often claim return because they are "taking risk." But a moral audit must ask what risk they actually bear. Preferred equity, liquidation preferences, redemption rights, security interests, covenants, board control, personal guarantees, and seniority can all reduce or shift downside. If the investor claims a risk premium while the structure pushes most real fragility onto founders, workers, suppliers, customers, community, or mission, then the appeal to risk becomes morally suspect.

Decock also helps us understand carentia pecuniae, the title based on being without one's money. In Lessius, this title becomes visible as a contractual commitment: the lender agrees not to call back money for a term. The borrower receives stability. The lender gives up liquidity. This can be analyzed not simply as a loan, but as an obligation of duration. The lender has bound himself.

Yet this is precisely where the danger appears. If every commitment of money for a term justifies surplus, then every loan can generate interest. The lender is always without money after lending it. Every term loan deprives the lender of liquidity. If that deprivation alone becomes a title, the usury doctrine dissolves. Decock's attention to Lessius helps us see both sides: the genuine contractual insight and the dangerous proximity to modern time-value logic.

Decock helps us avoid simplistic readings of Lessius. Lessius is not merely a villain who opened the door to interest, nor merely a hero who discovered modern contract freedom. He is a sophisticated moral theologian working at the edge of a changing economy. His categories are powerful because they recognize real commercial burdens. They are dangerous because those same burdens can be generalized into ordinary yield.

Decock's broader work on contract law also matters because it situates late scholastic moral theology as a precursor to modern legal thought. Early modern theologians and jurists were not only discussing sin in the confessional. They were developing concepts of consent, obligation, promise, restitution, unjust enrichment, and contractual fairness that would shape later legal development. Economic life was still morally thick. Contract was not yet imagined as a purely autonomous private space outside theological judgment.

This has profound implications for the modern reader. Today, contract law often assumes that informed consent and legal enforceability do much of the moral work. If sophisticated parties agree, the contract is presumed legitimate. But the scholastic tradition asks deeper questions. Consent can be pressured. Bargaining power can be unequal. Legal form can conceal moral substance. A contract can be valid and still extractive. Decock's work helps recover that older, richer understanding of contract.

This is especially relevant in venture capital and private equity, where the contract is often treated as the final moral authority. The founder signed the term sheet, the company accepted the valuation, the investors negotiated their rights, the covenants are standard, the liquidation preference is market practice. But scholastic contract analysis would not stop there. It would ask: what obligations does this structure create? Who is protected? Who is exposed? Who has control? Who receives upside? Who bears downside? What happens in failure? Does the contract serve the enterprise's purpose, or subordinate the enterprise to capital's exit?

Decock helps us see that late scholastic moral theology had the tools to ask such questions because it refused to separate contract from justice. Contract was a moral form, not merely an expression of will, and it had to be examined according to the kind of relationship it created.

This is also why Decock matters for the transition toward money-market logic. Lessius and related authors begin to analyze liquidity, risk, time, and opportunity in ways that resemble modern finance. But the resemblance should not be confused with identity. Modern finance often treats these features as pricing inputs. Late scholastic moral theology treats them as possible titles requiring justification. The difference is decisive.

A risk premium in modern finance is usually a market price. In scholastic terms, it must be a title grounded in real risk borne. A liquidity premium in modern finance is usually compensation for lock-up. In scholastic terms, it must be tested against the danger of carentia pecuniae. Opportunity cost in modern finance is a basic assumption of rational allocation. In scholastic terms, it must be disciplined by the requirements of concrete alternative use, causality, and probability. Decock's scholarship helps us locate the moment where these languages begin to overlap, while still preserving their moral difference.

His work is also important because it reveals the role of legal imagination in economic transformation. Finance changes not only when people become greedier or markets become larger, but when contractual forms make new claims thinkable. Once liquidity deprivation can be named, it can be priced. Once risk of principal can be named, it can be priced. Once foregone gain can be named, it can be priced. Naming can clarify justice, but it can also enable extraction. A title can become a product.

This is one of the central warnings for non-extractive finance. We too are creating names: patient capital, steward ownership, mission lock, non-extractive return, catalytic investment, recoverable grant, revenue-based finance, capped upside, solidarity finance. These names may clarify moral intention. But they can also become brands that hide conventional extraction. Decock's reading of scholastic contract law warns us that every named form must be tested against its actual obligations.

The connection to audit is unavoidable. A contract-sensitive moral theology must read the documents. It must examine repayment rights, priority, security, governance, control, default provisions, information rights, exit rights, fees, redemption rights, and distribution waterfalls. It must ask whether the moral title claimed in the narrative is actually embodied in the legal structure. If the story says "partnership" but the contract says "guaranteed priority," the contract tells the truth. If the story says "risk-sharing" but the investor is insulated from loss, the structure tells the truth. If the story says "mission-aligned" but the exit rights force sale against mission, the rights tell the truth.

Decock's contribution is therefore methodological as much as historical. He teaches us to read moral theology and contract law together. The moral question is not outside the contract. It is inside the contract, in the allocation of rights, duties, remedies, risks, and powers.

For the architecture of this book, Decock helps us understand Lessius not only as a theologian of usury, but as a thinker of contractual modernity. Through Lessius, the extrinsic titles become more precisely connected to legal obligation. Periculum sortis becomes a question of risk allocation. Carentia pecuniae becomes a question of term commitment and liquidity. Lucrum cessans becomes a question of interrupted opportunity and causal loss. Damnum emergens becomes a question of compensable damage. All of these require contractual analysis.

This also explains why Decock's work is so important for the transition from moral theology to law and economics. The categories that modern finance treats as normal (risk, time, liquidity, opportunity, contractual freedom) were once morally contested. They had to be justified. They had to be named as titles. They had to be limited. Recovering that contestation helps us resist the modern tendency to treat financial claims as natural.

Decock therefore belongs in this chapter because he gives us a bridge: from scholastic theology to contract law, from usury doctrine to modern finance, from confessional analysis to legal structure. He shows that the history of usury is also a history of how contracts learned to carry, conceal, or discipline moral claims.

The lesson for non-extractive capital is clear. Do not trust labels: read the contract. Do not trust mission language: read the repayment rights. Do not trust the word "risk": read the downside allocation. Do not trust the word "partnership": read the guarantees, preferences, and control rights. Do not trust the word "patient": read what time does to the payment. Do not trust the word "stewardship": read who governs and who can force exit.

Wim Decock helps us see that moral finance must be legally literate. Capital becomes extractive not only through greed, but through clauses. And if capital is to serve life, the clauses themselves must be converted.

sections.title: 35.7 André Lapidus
paragraphs: André Lapidus is important because he helps us see the usury tradition through the lens of information, uncertainty, risk, and analytical instability. Where many histories of usury focus on law, doctrine, or commercial practice, Lapidus draws attention to the epistemic problem beneath the doctrine: what do the parties know, what do they not know, and how does uncertainty affect the justice of a financial claim?

Usury doctrine is not only about money; it is about knowledge under conditions of time. A loan reaches from present to future. The lender gives now and receives later. The borrower uses now and promises later. Between the two moments stand uncertainty, possibility, delay, loss, failure, opportunity, risk, and interpretation. The extrinsic titles arise because the simple loan relation becomes morally complicated when something changes between advance and repayment.

Lapidus is especially useful for understanding why the titles cannot be judged merely by their names. A penalty for delay may be legitimate, but only if the delay is wrongful, avoidable, and not engineered. Actual loss may be compensable, but only if the loss is real and caused by the transaction. Foregone profit may be relevant, but only if the lost opportunity was concrete rather than imagined. Risk of principal may justify surplus, but only if the lender truly bears the risk. In every case, the moral problem depends on information: what can be known, proven, expected, estimated, and distinguished from fiction?

This is why Lapidus's attention to uncertainty is so valuable. Medieval and scholastic theologians were not only deciding whether surplus was allowed. They were deciding how one could know whether the surplus corresponded to a real burden. The title is not self-validating; it must be interpreted. The facts must be established. The structure must be understood. The incentives must be examined. The claim must be tested against reality.

Consider poena conventionalis. On paper, a penalty is simple. The borrower promises to repay by a certain date. If the borrower does not repay, a penalty is due. But the moral analysis is not simple. Did the lender choose a realistic due date? Did the borrower have a fair chance to avoid default? Was the penalty proportionate? Did the lender expect or desire the borrower's delay? Was the contract designed so that default was likely? The information problem is not merely factual but interpretive. The same clause can be protective in one context and predatory in another.

Lapidus helps us see that the medieval concern with fraud is partly a concern with information asymmetry. The creditor may know more than the borrower. The creditor may understand the legal consequences better. The creditor may structure the contract to appear legitimate while hiding its true economic function. The borrower may see only the immediate need. The confessor or theologian must therefore uncover the real transaction beneath the stated transaction. Moral judgment requires analysis.

The same is true of periculum sortis. Risk is not a simple word. It must be located. Who bears uncertainty? Who bears loss if the project fails? Who can enforce repayment? Who is protected by collateral, guarantees, seniority, or legal remedies? A lender may claim risk because repayment is never absolutely certain. But if the contract shifts most downside to the borrower, the risk claim may be exaggerated or false. Lapidus's framework helps us see that the moral problem of risk is also a problem of classification under uncertainty.

This is particularly important because risk can be used rhetorically. Modern finance often invokes risk as a universal justification for return. Venture investors take risk. Lenders take risk. Private equity takes risk. Banks take risk. But the scholastic question is more exact: what risk, borne by whom, and compensated how? If risk is merely asserted, it becomes a magic word. If it is analyzed, it becomes a moral question.

Lapidus also helps clarify why lucrum cessans is so unstable. Foregone profit is a claim about a future that did not happen. The lender says: "Had I not made this loan, I would have gained something else." But the alternative world is invisible. It must be reconstructed through intention, probability, commercial practice, available opportunities, and causal connection. The lender may be telling the truth. The lender may be exaggerating. The lender may be imagining after the fact. The theologian must distinguish a real interrupted opportunity from a speculative narrative.

This is why the late scholastic conditions around lucrum cessans matter so much. The alternative use must be concrete. The gain must be reasonably probable. The lender must have intended or virtually intended the alternative. The loan must truly cause the gain to cease. The amount must be proportionate. These conditions are not arbitrary restrictions. They are epistemic safeguards. They protect the title from becoming a fiction.

Lapidus's attention to information also illuminates the contract-classification problem. Many financial structures are ambiguous because they combine features from different contract types. A bill of exchange may be real exchange or disguised loan. A partnership may be real shared enterprise or secured debt in costume. A penalty may be compensation or revenue. A fee may be cost recovery or yield. A risk premium may be compensation or extraction. Classification requires knowing not only the legal form but the economic substance.

In this sense, the usury tradition is an early form of moral due diligence. It asks for documents, facts, intentions, incentives, causality, proportionality, and risk allocation. It is not satisfied by the name of the instrument. It wants to know what actually happens. Lapidus helps us see the analytical sophistication required by that task.

His work also connects medieval usury doctrine to the broader economics of uncertainty. In modern finance, uncertainty is often treated as something to be priced. Risk becomes premium, volatility becomes return expectation, illiquidity becomes spread, and default probability becomes yield. But the scholastic tradition does not immediately transform uncertainty into a right to profit. It asks whether uncertainty creates a real burden borne by the claimant, and whether compensation for that burden is just.

That difference is critical. Modern finance often moves from uncertainty to price almost automatically. Scholastic moral theology inserts a judgment. Is the uncertainty voluntarily assumed? Is it necessary to the transaction? Is it borne by the lender or shifted to the borrower? Does it justify compensation, or has it become a pretext for yield? Does the payment reflect shared risk, or does it make one party secure while another bears ruin?

Lapidus's focus on information therefore helps this book articulate one of its central claims: non-extractive finance requires epistemic honesty. It is not enough to intend good. It is not enough to choose better labels. The structure must reveal who bears what. The accounting must show what each payment is for. The legal documents must match the moral story. The risk model must not hide ruin. The dashboard must not convert human vulnerability into attractive portfolio metrics.

This connects directly to the idea of a usury metric or moral finance simulator. Any attempt to evaluate a contract for usury must deal with incomplete information. It must ask what can be inferred from the contract, what requires external evidence, and what cannot be known without further inquiry. For example, a contract may reveal that repayment is senior and secured. It may reveal penalty rates, management fees, liquidation preferences, or redemption rights. But it may not reveal whether a lender had a concrete alternative use, whether a borrower was under distress, or whether the investor privately expected default. A serious analyzer must therefore distinguish between observable structure and uncertain moral facts.

Lapidus helps us see why this distinction matters. Some moral risks are visible in the contract. Others require context. Still others require prudential judgment. A good moral audit should therefore produce not only a verdict, but a map of uncertainty. Which claims are well supported? Which titles are documented? Which are asserted but unproven? Which structures create incentives toward extraction? Which questions must be asked before judgment can be final?

This is particularly important for periculum sortis. A contract may claim that capital is at risk, but the moral analyzer must inspect the waterfall, security, guarantees, seniority, covenants, and enforcement rights. If the investor is protected in most downside scenarios, the risk title weakens. But if the capital is genuinely subordinated, loss-absorbing, and dependent on enterprise success, the title becomes more plausible. Risk is not a label. It is a structure.

Lapidus also sharpens the connection between risk and time. The future is uncertain because it has not happened. A lender may want to charge for that uncertainty. A borrower may need time to act. The contract allocates the burden of the future. Does the future belong to capital as a claim to yield? Or does the future remain open to shared uncertainty, mercy, adjustment, and mission? This is not only an economic question but a theological one.

The usury tradition resists the idea that time automatically belongs to the lender. Time may cause damage. Time may interrupt opportunity. Time may expose capital to risk. But time itself is not a title. Lapidus's work helps us see why: because the moral meaning of time depends on what happens, what was known, what was expected, and who bears the uncertainty. To monetize time automatically is to erase the difference between real burden and abstract claim.

This is why his work belongs in this chapter on modern scholarship. Lapidus adds analytical depth to the genealogy. Noonan gives the broad doctrinal history. Langholm gives value and exchange. Todeschini gives belonging and Franciscan social meaning. De Roover gives banking and exchange technique. Monsalve gives the structure of the late scholastic titles. Decock gives contract law and Lessius. Lapidus adds uncertainty, information, and the risk of misclassification.

His contribution is especially relevant because modern finance often hides behind complexity. The more complex the instrument, the harder it is to see who bears what. Information asymmetry becomes moral asymmetry. The sophisticated party can design, price, and justify; the vulnerable party can only accept. A non-extractive framework must therefore make hidden structure visible.

This is where Lapidus's perspective becomes not only historical but programmatic. Moral finance must reduce ambiguity where ambiguity serves extraction. It must expose risk allocation. It must disclose fees. It must document titles. It must distinguish modeled return from moral entitlement. It must show when compensation is grounded and when it is speculative. It must treat uncertainty not as a license to charge, but as a call to truthfulness.

For the purposes of this book, Lapidus helps us frame the extrinsic titles as information-sensitive moral tests. Each title asks for evidence. Poena asks whether delay was culpable and not engineered. Damnum asks whether loss was real and caused. Lucrum asks whether the lost gain was concrete and probable. Periculum asks whether principal was genuinely at risk. Carentia pecuniae asks whether absence of money produced a real burden or merely monetized time. Without evidence, titles become stories capital tells about itself.

This is the lasting lesson. Usury is not always obvious. Extraction often hides in uncertainty, classification, and information asymmetry. The moral theologian must therefore be an analyst. The non-extractive financier must be an auditor. The contract must be read not only for what it says, but for what it conceals.

Lapidus helps us recover that discipline. He reminds us that the problem is not only greed, but opacity. Capital can dominate by knowing more, structuring more, and hiding more. A renewed moral economy must therefore make finance legible again, so that compensation can be distinguished from extraction, and so that risk can no longer be used as a veil for power.

sections.title: 35.8 John Munro
paragraphs: John Munro is important for this book because he helps ground the usury debate in concrete economic history. Some scholars illuminate the theological architecture of the doctrine. Others explain the contract law, the canon law, the Franciscan social imagination, or the internal logic of the extrinsic titles. Munro helps us see the financial world in which these questions had to be answered: late medieval commerce, public debt, merchant credit, damages, annuities, and the practical mechanisms by which money moved through cities, fairs, firms, and states.

Usury doctrine can become misleading if treated only as an abstract debate about whether "interest" is permitted. The actual historical question was more complex. What did lenders and borrowers do? What instruments did they use? What losses did they incur? How did cities finance themselves? How did merchants handle delay, default, exchange, and public obligations? What counted as a loan, a sale, an annuity, a forced contribution, or an exchange contract in practice?

Munro's work is especially helpful for understanding damnum emergens in concrete commercial cases. Actual loss is the cleanest of the major extrinsic titles because it is closest to restitution. If a lender suffers a real loss because of the borrower's action, compensation can restore equality. But the historical question is: what did such losses look like? They were not always theoretical. Delay in repayment could prevent a merchant from meeting obligations. It could force someone to borrow elsewhere. It could generate legal costs, collection costs, lost goods, missed payments, reputational harm, or liquidity damage. In a tightly connected commercial world, one delayed payment could cascade through many obligations.

Munro helps us see why actual loss mattered so much. In a world of merchant networks, fairs, credit chains, and civic obligations, money unavailable at the wrong time could create real damage. A lender might not be asking for gain from a loan, but for restoration after a borrower's failure caused measurable harm. This is why damnum emergens could be accepted earlier and more easily than the more dangerous titles. It did not require the theologian to imagine what might have happened in an alternative world. It asked what loss actually emerged.

Yet Munro's historical attention also helps preserve the limits of the title. Actual loss must be actual. It must be caused. It must be documented or at least reasonably demonstrated. It cannot be a generalized charge applied to every loan in advance. The danger is that a real category of damage becomes standardized into a fee or premium. Once that happens, damnum emergens begins to lose its restorative character. It becomes not compensation after loss, but priced expectation before loss.

This distinction between post-loan damage and automatic advance pricing is crucial. If a borrower's late repayment causes the lender to incur collection costs, replacement borrowing, or other real expenses, compensation may be just. But if every loan includes an automatic "damage" charge regardless of whether damage occurs, the charge begins to resemble ordinary interest or hidden yield. Munro's attention to real commercial cases helps us keep the title tied to evidence rather than abstraction.

Munro also matters for public debt and late medieval finance. Cities, princes, and public authorities often needed money, sometimes urgently. They might impose forced loans, issue obligations, sell annuities, pledge revenues, or borrow from citizens and merchant-bankers. These instruments did not fit neatly into the simplest usury framework. A forced loan might be partly tax and partly debt. An annuity might be understood as the sale of a revenue stream rather than a loan. A municipal obligation might support the common good or create a rentier class. Public finance forced theologians to classify contracts carefully.

Munro helps us see that public debt was not merely an exception to private lending rules. It was a field in which the moral meaning of financial obligation became politically charged. If a city borrowed for defense, famine relief, infrastructure, or public necessity, the transaction had a different social context from a private usurious loan to a desperate household. But public purpose did not automatically purify the instrument. Public debt could also burden the many for the benefit of the few. Taxes collected from artisans, laborers, and households could flow to wealthy creditors holding secure claims on civic revenues.

This is where economic history deepens moral theology. A doctrinal category may look legitimate in isolation, but its institutional effects may be unjust. A municipal obligation may be legally valid. A public annuity may be contractually clear. A forced loan may be authorized by civic necessity. But who ultimately pays? Who receives? Who bears risk? Who has political power? Does the structure serve the common good, or does it transform public necessity into private income?

Munro's work also helps us understand why late medieval and early modern theologians could not avoid finance. Cities, merchants, and rulers were not operating in a world where money could be treated as static. Credit relations were woven into trade, taxation, war, production, and public administration. To judge usury without understanding these mechanisms would be pastorally and politically inadequate. Moral theology had to become historically and institutionally literate.

This institutional literacy is exactly what modern non-extractive finance requires. It is not enough to ask whether an individual clause is defensible. We must ask how the structure functions in practice. A management fee may correspond to real labor. But does the total fee structure drain the enterprise? A risk premium may correspond to real exposure. But is the risk actually borne by the investor? A public guarantee may support useful investment. But does it socialize losses while privatizing gains? A mission fund may claim patience. But does its repayment waterfall place capital ahead of workers, suppliers, community, and mission?

Munro's historical work helps us resist abstract moral bookkeeping detached from economic reality. The same title can function differently in different institutional settings. A charge that restores equality in one case may become extraction in another. A public debt that mobilizes resources for the common good in one case may become a permanent claim of capital over civic life in another. Moral analysis must therefore attend not only to title and contract, but also to the surrounding system.

This is especially relevant to the problem of post-loan damage. In theory, damnum emergens is straightforward. In practice, damage often emerges through networks. A delayed payment causes a missed obligation. The missed obligation causes penalty charges elsewhere. The lender must borrow at unfavorable terms. A merchant loses reputation. A city delays wages. Workers suffer. Suppliers are unpaid. The original borrower may not foresee the whole chain. Moral judgment must therefore trace causality without exaggerating it.

Munro helps us see both the reality and the danger of this tracing. On the one hand, finance is relational. Losses propagate. A narrow view of damage may undercompensate genuine harm. On the other hand, too broad a view can justify excessive claims. If every downstream inconvenience becomes chargeable to the borrower, then the title becomes unlimited. The discipline of proportionality is therefore essential. Compensation must correspond to real, attributable damage, not to every imagined ripple.

His attention to economic history also sharpens our reading of lucrum cessans. In a commercial world, foregone profit may sometimes be more concrete than Aquinas's stricter rejection seems to allow. A merchant may have goods to buy, a voyage to finance, an exchange operation to complete, or an obligation to meet. Lending money may genuinely interrupt a planned commercial action. But history also shows how easily such claims can become speculative. Merchants always have possible uses for money. The line between concrete alternative use and generalized opportunity cost must therefore be guarded.

Munro's public-finance work also connects with the problem of annuities. An annuity might be structured as the sale of a right to receive periodic payments, rather than a loan at interest. This classification could be legitimate in some contexts, but also morally ambiguous. If a public authority sells a revenue stream, has it borrowed? Has it sold an asset? Has it created a perpetual burden on the community? Is the buyer sharing risk, or receiving secure rent? The form matters, but the substance matters more.

This is why Munro belongs in a chapter on modern scholars. He reminds us that moral theology must be historically concrete. The extrinsic titles were not floating abstractions. They were responses to damages, delays, debts, instruments, institutions, and fiscal pressures. Their meaning becomes clearer when we see the world that produced them.

For modern finance, this means that any moral audit must include institutional context. A contract does not exist in isolation. It sits inside a funding market, a legal regime, a governance system, an accounting practice, a set of power relations, and often a public policy environment. A non-extractive contract can be undermined by extractive ecosystem incentives. A mission-locked company can be pressured by investor expectations. A public subsidy can become private enrichment. A charitable fund can drift into fee dependence. An impact investment can reproduce the same extraction it claims to solve.

Munro also helps us see the importance of documentation. Actual loss, public obligation, forced contribution, repayment structure, annuity, and exchange all require records. Moral judgment depends on the ability to reconstruct what happened. In the Monti di Pietà, bookkeeping was not merely administrative. It was part of the moral safeguard. In modern non-extractive capital, accounting must perform the same role. Payments must be classified. Losses must be evidenced. Costs must be separated from returns. Related-party payments must be visible. Penalties must not become hidden yield. Public subsidies must not be captured without accountability.

This is where Munro's contribution speaks directly to the design of a modern usury metric. A metric cannot rely only on declared intent. It must ask for historical and accounting evidence. What payments actually occurred? What losses were actually incurred? What fees accumulated? What priority rules were triggered? What happened in distress? Did capital absorb losses, or did labor and mission absorb them? Did public funds reduce risk for investors without reducing their return? Did a "temporary" claim become permanent rent?

Munro also reminds us that the moral status of finance may change over time. A structure created for emergency public necessity can become a normal revenue machine. A charge introduced to cover costs can become profit. A temporary obligation can become permanent debt. A forced loan can become tradable public security. A charitable institution can drift. Historical time matters. The question is not only whether a structure was justified at the beginning, but whether it remains justified as conditions change.

This temporal dimension is crucial for non-extractive finance. Mission drift is rarely immediate. It happens when exceptions become defaults, when safeguards become formalities, when cost recovery becomes margin, when risk-sharing becomes risk-shifting, when public purpose becomes private asset, when patient capital becomes delayed extraction. Munro's economic history helps us watch these transitions.

In the architecture of this book, Munro therefore provides grounding. He keeps the doctrine from becoming too abstract, too purely theological, or too easily modernized. He shows that late medieval finance had real institutional complexity, and that the extrinsic titles developed in response to real commercial and public problems. But he also shows that those problems could generate structures of dependence, rent, and public burden.

His contribution to the moral economy project is a disciplined realism. Real losses must be recognized. Real institutions must be sustained. Real public needs must be financed. Real commerce requires credit and exchange. But real history also shows how quickly finance can convert necessity into permanent claim. That is why the extrinsic titles must remain tied to evidence, proportion, and institutional purpose.

John Munro helps us remember that usury is not merely a doctrine about contracts. It is a doctrine tested in ledgers, courts, city treasuries, merchant networks, and public obligations. It is tested wherever money claims the right to return increased from the life of another person or community. The question, then and now, is whether that increase repairs a real burden or captures a future. Munro helps us ask that question with both historical seriousness and moral precision.

sections.title: 35.9 Pierre Januard
paragraphs: Pierre Januard is important because he complicates any overly simple account of Aquinas and risk. It is easy to summarize Aquinas as the theologian who condemned interest because money is sterile, or because the use of money cannot be sold separately from the money itself. That summary is not false, but it is incomplete. Januard helps us see that Aquinas's treatment of usury, risk, and commercial exchange contains more nuance than a simple anti-interest reading allows.

This matters for the whole argument of this book. If Aquinas is treated as merely anti-finance, then later scholastic developments can appear only as compromises or corruptions. But if Aquinas already contains a more careful analysis of risk, ownership, intention, partnership, and classification, then the later tradition can be read as an extension (sometimes faithful, sometimes dangerous) of problems already present in the Thomistic framework.

Januard's work is especially valuable because he draws attention to Aquinas beyond the familiar passages in the Summa Theologiae. The standard text is ST II-II, q.78, where Aquinas argues that taking usury for money lent is sinful. But Aquinas also treats related issues in the Commentary on the Sentences, in De malo, and in his broader account of justice, restitution, and exchange. Januard's reading encourages us to examine the wider architecture rather than isolating one formula.

At the heart of Aquinas's account is the mutuum. In a loan for consumption, ownership passes to the borrower, and the borrower owes an equivalent amount. The lender cannot sell the use of what the lender no longer owns. This remains essential. But Januard helps us see that Aquinas is also attentive to cases where risk and uncertainty change the moral structure of the transaction. The question is not merely whether money was advanced, but under what form, with what allocation of risk, and with what kind of claim to return.

This becomes clearest in Aquinas's distinction between the mutuum and partnership. If one lends money, one may not demand surplus from the loan itself. But if one entrusts money to a merchant by way of partnership, one may share in the profit because one also bears the risk. Capital that remains exposed to loss is morally different from capital that demands guaranteed repayment. Januard's reading helps bring this risk-sensitive dimension to the foreground.

This is not yet the fully developed later title of periculum sortis. Aquinas does not simply say that all risk of nonpayment justifies a premium. But he does establish a crucial principle: profit-sharing can be licit where capital is genuinely at risk. The creditor and the partner are not the same. The creditor transfers ownership and receives a claim to equivalent repayment. The partner remains exposed to the fortunes of the venture. The difference is not merely legal but moral.

Januard's attention to risk helps us read this distinction more carefully. The moral legitimacy of surplus depends in part on whether capital participates in uncertainty. If the capital provider can lose, then the return may arise from shared venture. If the capital provider cannot lose, then the return begins to look like usury unless another title is present. This distinction is central for modern finance, where many instruments call themselves equity, partnership, or risk capital while contractually protecting capital from meaningful downside.

Januard also points toward what might be called the "risk of analysis." Financial transactions are not always easy to classify. A contract may appear to be one thing while functioning as another. A theologian or confessor must determine whether the transaction is truly a loan, partnership, sale, exchange, or disguised mutuum. The risk is that the analyst gets it wrong. Misclassification is not merely intellectual error; it can bless injustice.

This is a profound insight for the modern project. In complex finance, the moral danger is not always visible on the surface. A convertible note may look like debt now and equity later. Preferred equity may look like partnership but operate with debt-like priority. A revenue-based financing agreement may share upside but still impose burdens that crush the enterprise. A liquidation preference may be justified as risk protection while shifting risk onto founders and workers. A moral auditor must analyze the transaction's substance, not merely its label. Januard's reading of Aquinas helps us see that this problem is not new.

Aquinas's treatment of late repayment also benefits from Januard's wider approach. In De malo, Aquinas recognizes that late repayment can cause damage, and such damage may be compensable. This does not mean that time itself can be monetized. It means that wrongful delay can create actual loss. Januard helps us see Aquinas as more precise than the caricature: he is not blind to commercial harm, but he refuses to treat duration as an automatic title.

This matters for the distinction between damnum emergens and carentia pecuniae. Aquinas can allow compensation for actual damage caused by delay. But that is different from saying that being without money for a term is itself always compensable. The former restores a real loss. The latter risks converting time into yield. Januard's risk-sensitive Aquinas strengthens the argument that the tradition is not irrationally hostile to economic reality; it is morally resistant to abstraction.

Januard also helps reopen the question of commercial risk. Aquinas's world knew that trade involved uncertainty. Merchants could lose goods, ships, money, and reputation. A person who participates in such risk is not simply renting money. They are exposed to the real contingencies of exchange. This is why partnership matters. The moral issue is not whether capital can ever receive more than it contributed. The issue is whether the capital remains bound to the fate of the common undertaking.

This has obvious implications for venture capital. Modern venture capital often presents itself as risk-bearing capital, and in some respects it is. Startups fail. Equity can be lost. Investors may not recover their money. But the moral analysis cannot stop there. The structure may include preferred shares, liquidation preferences, anti-dilution rights, control rights, redemption rights, drag-along rights, and exit pressure. These can alter who actually bears downside. If the investor receives equity upside while also securing debt-like protections, the partnership analogy weakens. Aquinas's distinction, read through Januard, asks: is this truly societas, or a protected capital claim wearing partnership clothes?

Januard also helps us avoid using Aquinas as a blunt weapon against all financial return. Aquinas does not condemn profit as such. He permits merchant profit under morally ordered conditions. He permits partnership profit where risk is shared. He permits compensation for actual loss. His doctrine is not anti-economic life. It is anti-duplication, anti-domination, and anti-profit from the bare mutuum. This distinction is crucial if Aquinas is to inform non-extractive finance rather than merely condemn modern finance from outside.

Aquinas's broader account of justice also matters here. Usury is a problem of commutative justice because it disturbs equality between lender and borrower. But this equality is not only numerical. It is relational. The lender must not use the borrower's need, delay, or dependence to extract more than is due. If surplus restores a real loss, it can serve equality. If surplus arises from shared risk, it can reflect participation. If surplus arises from the mere fact that the borrower needed money, it becomes domination.

Januard's contribution is to show that Aquinas's own framework contains the tools for this more nuanced analysis. The later extrinsic titles are not arbitrary departures from Aquinas. Some are rooted in his own distinctions. Damnum emergens has a clear Thomistic basis in compensation for loss. Periculum sortis has a Thomistic foundation in the distinction between mutuum and partnership. Lucrum cessans remains more contested because Aquinas directly resists selling what one does not yet have, though other Thomistic principles later create openings. Carentia pecuniae remains highly dangerous because it presses against Aquinas's refusal to monetize use or time in a consumptive loan.

This map helps us read the later tradition critically. When later theologians develop risk-bearing finance, they may be extending a Thomistic insight. When they allow compensation for actual loss, they are clearly within Aquinas's logic. When they admit concrete foregone gain, they are working at the edge of Aquinas's own tensions. When they generalize liquidity deprivation or opportunity cost, they may be moving beyond Aquinas in a way that threatens the doctrine. Januard helps us distinguish these movements.

For the purposes of this book, Januard also strengthens the argument that risk analysis is not a modern import imposed on medieval theology. The tradition already had risk-sensitive distinctions. It knew that bearing downside matters. It knew that partnership differs from debt. It knew that delay can cause actual harm. It knew that misclassification can create injustice. Modern non-extractive finance can therefore draw on Aquinas not only as a critic of interest, but as a thinker of risk allocation.

This is especially important for the NEC simulator or any usury metric. A Thomistic risk analysis would not simply ask whether the nominal instrument is debt or equity. It would ask whether capital is truly at risk. It would ask whether repayment is guaranteed. It would ask whether the investor can enforce against the borrower regardless of outcome. It would ask whether downside is shared or shifted. It would ask whether the surplus comes from participation, compensation, or domination.

Januard also suggests humility. Aquinas's texts do not give an automatic answer to every modern instrument. They require interpretation. Applying them to venture capital, private equity, public debt, or revenue-based finance involves judgment. The "risk of analysis" is real. We can misread the contract. We can misread Aquinas. We can misread the economic substance. A serious moral finance framework must therefore be transparent about uncertainty.

This is not a weakness but part of the tradition's honesty. The scholastic method does not pretend that every case is easy. It distinguishes, tests, qualifies, and sometimes hesitates. That hesitation is morally important. It prevents the analyst from baptizing complexity too quickly. It also prevents the rigorist from condemning what may in fact be legitimate compensation or participation.

Januard's contribution therefore sits at a crucial point in this chapter. After Noonan's broad history, Langholm's value theory, Todeschini's Franciscan social imagination, de Roover's technical finance, Monsalve's title structure, Decock's contract analysis, Lapidus's uncertainty, and Munro's economic history, Januard brings us back to Aquinas with fresh eyes. He shows that the Thomistic foundation is not flat. It contains a living architecture of ownership, risk, compensation, partnership, delay, and classification.

This matters because Aquinas will remain one of the central authorities for any Christian reconstruction of moral finance. But the Aquinas we need is not a slogan. He is not merely "money is sterile." He is the theologian who asks what kind of thing is being transferred, who owns it, whether its use can be separated from its substance, whether loss has occurred, whether capital remains at risk, and whether the contract is truly what it claims to be.

That Aquinas is far more useful for modern finance. He does not give capital a natural right to increase. But he does give us a way to distinguish lending from participation, compensation from yield, and risk-bearing from risk-shifting. Pierre Januard helps us recover precisely that Aquinas. For a renewed moral economy, this is invaluable. It means that the tradition's greatest theologian can still speak to capital structures, not by offering a ready-made policy, but by asking the questions that capital would often prefer to avoid: who bears risk, who claims surplus, and is the contract telling the truth?

sections.title: 35.10 David Graeber
paragraphs: David Graeber belongs in this chapter for a different reason than Noonan, Langholm, de Roover, Monsalve, Decock, Lapidus, Munro, or Januard. He is not primarily a historian of scholastic usury doctrine. He does not give us the technical development of the extrinsic titles. He does not reconstruct the canon-law tradition, the Thomistic account of the mutuum, or the late scholastic debates over damnum emergens, lucrum cessans, and periculum sortis. For those purposes, he is not the safest guide.

Yet Graeber is enormously useful for the civilizational frame. His great contribution is to remind us that debt is older, stranger, more social, and more morally charged than modern financial theory often admits. Debt is not merely a neutral contract between rational market participants. It is a relationship of obligation, memory, hierarchy, violence, trust, dependence, and sometimes domination. Long before modern money markets, human communities were already organizing themselves through credit, tribute, gift, temple accounting, palace administration, household obligation, debt bondage, and periodic release.

Scholastic usury doctrine did not emerge into an empty world. It inherited Scripture, and Scripture itself stands inside a much older Near Eastern memory of debt, land, household, bondage, and release. Graeber helps recover that deep background. He reminds us that debt has always had the power to reorder social life. It can coordinate cooperation, but it can also turn persons into objects of claim. It can bind communities together, but it can also create permanent classes of creditors and debtors. It can express trust, but it can also justify violence.

Graeber's Debt: The First 5,000 Years (2011) is especially useful for challenging the modern myth that money and debt arise primarily from barter among equals. In his account, credit relations, units of account, temple and palace systems, and social obligations often precede coinage. Debt is not a late technical development added to a neutral market economy. It is part of the deep structure of civilization. This is important because it prevents us from treating usury as a narrow problem of "interest rates." The deeper problem is the moral power of obligation.

Graeber also helps us understand the link between debt and violence. A debt may begin as a promise, but under conditions of inequality it can become coercive. It can justify seizure of land, labor, children, bodies, and future harvests. In many ancient societies, debt bondage was not metaphorical. Failure to repay could mean loss of freedom, loss of household stability, or loss of ancestral land. The creditor's claim could become a claim on life itself.

This is why ancient debt release matters. Graeber's discussion of amnesties, jubilees, and debt cancellations belongs alongside the work of Michael Hudson and other scholars of the ancient Near East. The point is not that ancient rulers were economically naive, but that they often understood something modern societies forget: if debts accumulate without limit, they can destroy the social order that makes economic life possible. Periodic release was not merely charity but political and social restoration. It prevented creditor claims from consuming households, land, and the future.

This deep background illuminates the biblical material. Exodus, Leviticus, Deuteronomy, Nehemiah, the Psalms, Proverbs, and Ezekiel do not treat debt as a morally neutral instrument. Lending to the poor, taking pledges, charging increase, refusing release, and exploiting the vulnerable are spiritual matters. The debtor is not merely a counterparty but brother, neighbor, poor person, covenant member, image-bearer. Debt must be subordinated to life.

Graeber helps us see why this biblical moral grammar matters before we ever arrive at Roman law or the mutuum. The scholastic doctrine will later become technically precise. It will ask whether ownership passed, whether the good was consumable, whether the lender can sell use separately from substance, whether surplus arises from an external title. But beneath that technical structure lies an older intuition: creditor power must be restrained because debt can become domination.

This is Graeber's main value for the present book. He widens the frame. He reminds us that the scholastic analysis of usury is part of a longer human struggle to prevent obligation from becoming enslavement.
