It is one of finance's central moral facts, and the old mutuum doctrine saw this clearly: one cannot charge rent for what one has ceased to own. Modern non-extractive finance must recover the same clarity. Capital may be lent, invested, entrusted, shared, stewarded, or placed at risk. But each form carries different moral consequences. To know what surplus is just, we must first know who owns what, and for whom.

sections.title: 39.3 Third Question: Who Bears Risk?
paragraphs: The third question in a scholastic audit is: who bears risk? This question cuts through much of the ambiguity in modern finance. Every contract speaks, in one way or another, about risk. Some contracts name it explicitly: risk premium, default risk, downside protection, liquidation preference, collateral, guarantee, volatility, uncertainty, portfolio loss. Others distribute risk without announcing it, through repayment schedules, covenants, seniority, control rights, and default clauses. But every financing structure answers the question, even when it does not state the answer plainly: when things go badly, who suffers?

This question is central because periculum sortis can justify surplus only where risk to principal is real. A lender or investor may not simply invoke risk as a general atmosphere. The risk must land somewhere. It must be borne by someone. The moral question is not whether the transaction is uncertain in the abstract, but whether capital itself is genuinely exposed to loss, or whether the contract has arranged for founders, workers, borrowers, suppliers, communities, users, public institutions, or the mission to absorb the downside instead.

Modern finance often speaks from the perspective of capital. From that perspective, risk means the possibility that expected return will not materialize, that repayment may be delayed, that portfolio performance may disappoint, or that capital may be impaired. These are real concerns. But a Christian moral audit cannot look only from the investor's side. It must ask how risk is experienced by all parties inside the transaction. A five percent loss in a diversified fund is not the same as a founder losing her company, a worker losing wages, a community losing essential service, or an open-source ecosystem losing core infrastructure. The same event can be tolerable for capital and ruinous for the borrower.

The audit must therefore distinguish between statistical risk and existential risk. Statistical risk appears in models. It can be diversified, priced, discounted, and averaged. Existential risk appears in lives. It can mean insolvency, job loss, burnout, loss of housing, loss of vocation, or collapse of a community-serving enterprise. A non-extractive analysis must consider both. It is not enough to say that the investor bore risk because a spreadsheet showed default probability. One must ask what happens to each party when the adverse scenario arrives.

The first risk-bearer may be the founder or borrower. This happens when repayment remains due regardless of outcome, when personal guarantees are required, when default triggers severe penalties, or when collateral reaches essential assets. The founder may bear not only business risk but personal risk: savings, home, reputation, future income, and psychological burden. In such cases, capital may appear to be "at risk," but much of the risk has been pushed downward into the borrower's life. This is morally significant. A contract that claims to finance entrepreneurship may in fact place the entrepreneur under intensified vulnerability.

The second risk-bearer may be the worker. Workers often stand behind capital in the real economy, even if not always in the formal documents. When debt service, investor repayment, or exit pressure becomes urgent, wages may stagnate, jobs may be cut, benefits may be reduced, workloads may increase, and working conditions may deteriorate. Workers may not have signed the financing contract, but they live inside its consequences. A scholastic audit should therefore ask whether the capital structure protects work or treats labor as the shock absorber for investor claims.

The third risk-bearer may be the mission. This is especially important in mission-driven companies, steward-owned firms, social enterprises, open-source projects, and public-interest technology. A contract may not destroy the company financially, but it may force mission drift. Repayment pressure may push the enterprise toward more profitable but less faithful customers. Exit rights may force sale to an acquirer with incompatible goals. Growth targets may redirect attention from depth to scale. Governance rights may subordinate purpose to liquidity. In such cases, the mission bears risk even if the investor bears some financial uncertainty.

The fourth risk-bearer may be the community or users. A platform, cybersecurity tool, healthcare provider, school, energy cooperative, food system, or civic technology project may serve people beyond its shareholders. If financing pressures lead to higher prices, reduced access, lower quality, data extraction, surveillance, layoffs, sale, closure, or loss of trust, the affected community bears part of the downside. Conventional finance often treats these effects as externalities. Moral theology cannot. If the community is exposed while capital is protected, the contract's risk language is incomplete.

The fifth risk-bearer may be the public sector. When private capital finances essential infrastructure but exits, fails, extracts, or underinvests, public institutions may have to rescue, regulate, repair, or replace what remains. This is common in digital infrastructure, energy, housing, care, transport, and financial systems. Capital may privatize upside while socializing downside. A scholastic audit should therefore ask whether the financing structure creates hidden public risk. If so, the investor's claim to private risk premium must be morally qualified.

The sixth risk-bearer may be the investor. This is real and should not be dismissed. An investor who provides subordinated, unsecured, patient, capped, loss-absorbing capital may genuinely bear periculum sortis. If the enterprise fails, capital is lost. If revenue is delayed, repayment waits. If surplus never arises, return never comes. Such capital stands much closer to partnership than to domination. It may justly receive a proportionate return because it has accepted vulnerability within the shared undertaking.

The audit therefore asks not simply, "Is the investor taking risk?" but "What kind of risk, compared with whom, and under what contractual protections?" An investor can bear some risk while shifting deeper risk to others. A venture investor may lose money in a failed company, but may also impose exit pressure across a portfolio that reshapes the lives of founders and workers. A lender may face default probability, but may hold collateral that allows recovery by stripping the borrower's assets. A fund may have patient capital, but may still require repayment before the company has built resilience. Risk is rarely all-or-nothing. It is distributed.

This is why the documents must be read under stress. In good times, everyone appears aligned. Investors receive updates, founders build, workers are paid, customers are served, and mission language flourishes. But the moral allocation of risk becomes visible when things go wrong. What happens if revenue falls by half? What happens if a major customer does not pay? What happens if the company needs another year? What happens if the founder becomes ill? What happens if the market turns against the product? What happens if mission fidelity requires slower growth? The answer to these questions reveals who really bears risk.

Repayment obligations are one of the clearest indicators. If repayment is fixed by calendar time regardless of profitability, risk is shifted to the borrower. If repayment is linked to gross revenue, some flexibility exists, but the company may still be forced to pay while unprofitable. If repayment is linked to free cash flow after wages, taxes, operating needs, reserves, and mission commitments, capital bears more risk. If repayment occurs only from distributable surplus, the structure moves closer to participation. The source of repayment tells us where downside lands.

Seniority also reveals risk allocation. Senior capital is protected before subordinated capital, founders, and common equity. This may reduce the cost of capital, but it also means others absorb losses first. Subordinated capital, by contrast, waits. It stands behind more urgent claims. It accepts that workers, taxes, suppliers, operating needs, and resilience may come before investor repayment. Subordination is not automatically virtuous, but it is one sign that capital is willing to bear risk rather than merely price it.

Collateral and guarantees must be scrutinized carefully. A lender who holds collateral may still face risk, but the risk is mitigated by the right to seize assets. A personal guarantee shifts enterprise risk into the founder's private life. A guarantee from a foundation, parent company, or public body shifts risk to another balance sheet. Insurance may shift risk to an insurer. Each risk-transfer mechanism may be legitimate, but each weakens the claim that the original capital provider bears full periculum sortis. One cannot morally charge as though exposed while legally arranging to be protected.

Covenants and control rights also distribute risk. Financial covenants may allow a lender to intervene before default. Information rights may reduce uncertainty. Veto rights may prevent reckless action. These protections can be prudent. But they can also transfer risk by giving capital power to force decisions that protect repayment at the expense of mission or workers. If capital can control the company under stress, then capital is not simply bearing risk. It is governing risk, often in its own favor.

Exit rights are another major site of risk allocation. An investor who can force sale, redemption, IPO, or liquidity may shift time risk onto the enterprise. The company may need patient development, but capital may need realization. If the investor's liquidity timetable governs the enterprise's future, the mission bears risk. A truly patient investor accepts that liquidity may come only when compatible with the company's life. A non-extractive structure should therefore limit forced exit rights, especially in mission-critical enterprises.

The audit should also ask whether risk is double-counted. A contract may charge interest for risk, require collateral to reduce risk, impose personal guarantees to reduce risk further, add default penalties when risk materializes, require legal cost reimbursement, and include warrants or upside participation. Each item may have a plausible rationale. Together, they may compensate capital repeatedly for the same uncertainty. Double-counting is a sign that risk language has become extraction. The burden is counted many times, while the borrower bears it once in life.

A morally serious risk analysis must also consider scale and resilience. A small borrower and a large investor do not experience risk symmetrically. A foundation with diversified assets may absorb a failed investment. A founder may not absorb a failed company. A fund may survive one write-off. A community may not survive the loss of essential infrastructure. Proportionality must therefore consider the relative capacity of each party to bear loss. Justice is not blind to fragility.

This does not mean that the more vulnerable party can never bear risk. Entrepreneurship, trade, lending, investment, and mission all involve uncertainty. The goal is not to eliminate risk from life. The goal is to distribute risk truthfully and justly. Founders may bear entrepreneurial risk. Investors may bear capital risk. Workers may bear some organizational uncertainty, though not unjust precarity. Communities may accept change, but not abandonment. A good contract does not pretend risk can disappear. It orders risk according to vocation, capacity, and justice.

For non-extractive capital, this means designing for shared downside. Repayment should flex with actual capacity. Capital should be subordinated where appropriate. Returns should come from surplus rather than depletion. Personal guarantees should be avoided or strictly limited. Collateral should not strip the enterprise of its life. Grace periods and hardship clauses should be real. Governance should protect mission in bad times, not only in good times. The investor should be able to lose in the same world in which the investor hopes to receive.

Theologically, the question "who bears risk?" is a question about solidarity. To bear risk with another is to enter some form of shared fate. It does not require equality in every respect, but it does require genuine vulnerability. The investor who says, "I will benefit if you flourish, and I will also suffer if you fail," stands in a different moral position from the creditor who says, "I will benefit if you flourish, and I will enforce if you fail." The first approaches partnership. The second approaches domination when surplus is demanded.

This question also reveals whether capital is courageous or merely protected. Modern finance often honors risk-takers, but the honor is deserved only where the risk is real. There is no moral courage in charging a risk premium while standing first in line, holding collateral, demanding guarantees, and shifting loss to others. Courage requires exposure. Periculum sortis is not a decorative label. It is a claim that capital can be wounded.

The third question of the scholastic audit therefore becomes a map of downside. In each scenario (ordinary operation, delay, underperformance, default, insolvency, sale, mission conflict, and failure), who loses what? Who waits? Who is paid? Who decides? Who can enforce? Who can walk away? Who cannot? This map reveals whether the contract shares risk or merely prices risk from the safest available position.

Only after this map is drawn can the risk premium be morally assessed. If capital truly bears principal risk, receives only from surplus, waits behind more urgent human and mission claims, and cannot force extraction under stress, then a proportionate return may be justified. If capital is protected while others absorb the wound, then the appeal to risk is morally weak. The old title of periculum sortis demands more than uncertainty. It demands real participation in loss.

In the end, "who bears risk?" may be the most revealing question in the whole audit. It shows whether finance is structured as solidarity or hierarchy. It shows whether capital serves life by standing with the enterprise, or governs life by standing above it. It shows whether surplus is compensation for vulnerability accepted, or reward for vulnerability imposed on others. A non-extractive contract must be able to answer this question honestly, not only in the upside case, but in the valley where risk becomes real.

sections.title: 39.4 Fourth Question: What Justifies Surplus?
paragraphs: After identifying the contract, mapping ownership, and tracing risk, the fourth question is: what justifies surplus? This is the central question of the extrinsic-title tradition. The issue is not simply whether more than principal is paid. The issue is why more than principal is paid. What moral title supports the surplus? What burden does it correspond to? What does the payment repair, reimburse, compensate, share, or sustain? If the answer is vague, the contract has not yet been morally understood.

Modern finance often treats surplus as self-evident. Capital expects return. Loans carry interest. Equity expects upside. Funds have target rates. Investors require compensation for risk, illiquidity, time, inflation, opportunity cost, administration, and market norms. These explanations may be financially familiar, but the scholastic audit refuses to accept them as a bundle. It asks each claim to separate itself from the others and name its moral basis. Is this payment for loss? Delay? Foregone gain? Risk? Labor? Expense? Or bargaining power?

This separation matters because surplus can be overdetermined. A single contract may include interest, default interest, arrangement fees, monitoring fees, legal cost reimbursement, warrants, liquidation preferences, redemption rights, penalties, collateral, and governance rights. Each component may be justified by a different story. But unless these stories are carefully distinguished, the same burden may be charged many times. Risk may be priced in the interest rate, reduced through collateral, compensated through warrants, protected through covenants, and penalized again on default. The result is not moral precision, but cumulative extraction.

The first possible justification is loss. This is the logic of damnum emergens. If the lender or investor suffers an actual loss caused by the transaction or by the borrower's wrongful delay, compensation may be just. Legal expenses, collection costs, emergency borrowing, administrative costs, damaged collateral, or liquidity harm may fall into this category when they are real, documented, causally connected, and proportionate. The surplus is justified not because capital deserves growth, but because equality has been disturbed and must be repaired.

But loss must be actual. It cannot be merely notional, inflated, anticipated in a vague way, or invented after the fact. A lender may not label ordinary overhead as borrower-caused damage without evidence. A fund may not convert its general operating model into a damage claim against each enterprise. A contract may not impose liquidated damages so large that they cease to resemble any plausible harm. The moral test is restorative: does this payment make whole a real loss, or does it create a profit center under the name of loss?

The second possible justification is delay. This is the logic of poena conventionalis or morae poena. A borrower has failed to repay on time, missed an obligation, or breached a covenant. A penalty may be justified if the delay is culpable, the due date was realistic, the penalty is proportionate, and the lender genuinely prefers timely repayment. In this case, the surplus is not payment for the original loan, but a response to disorder introduced by default.

Yet delay is one of the easiest titles to abuse. If the repayment schedule is unrealistic, default triggers are broad, cure periods are short, or penalties are excessive, the contract may engineer the very delay it claims to punish. A lender who models default charges as expected income is not merely protecting against harm. The lender is monetizing failure. Therefore the question is not only whether delay occurred, but whether the structure made delay likely and profitable.

The third possible justification is foregone profit. This is the logic of lucrum cessans. The lender claims that the loan prevented a concrete gain that would otherwise have been obtained. This title is more dangerous than actual loss because it concerns a future that did not happen. To be morally serious, the foregone gain must be concrete, intended, licit, reasonably certain, causally prevented by the loan, and proportionately valued. It cannot be a general appeal to the fact that money might always have earned something somewhere.

This is where modern opportunity cost must be disciplined. A fund may say that capital could have earned a market return elsewhere. But which market? Which investment? Was the alternative morally licit? Was the capital actually intended for that use? Was the gain reasonably certain? Or is the lender simply importing a benchmark and demanding that the borrower compensate capital for all imagined alternatives? Lucrum cessans may justify surplus in narrow cases. It cannot justify the universal metaphysics of money-as-growth.

The fourth possible justification is risk. This is the logic of periculum sortis. If principal is genuinely exposed to loss, the investor may have a claim to proportionate participation in upside. This is especially plausible where capital is subordinated, unsecured, patient, capped, loss-absorbing, and repayable only from true surplus. The surplus is justified by vulnerability accepted. The investor has entered the uncertainty of the enterprise rather than standing above it.

But risk must be real. A risk premium is morally weak if repayment is guaranteed, collateralized, senior, insured, personally guaranteed, or enforceable against unrelated assets. It is also weakened when downside is shifted to founders, workers, suppliers, users, communities, public institutions, or the mission. The question is not whether risk exists somewhere in the system. The question is whether the party receiving the surplus bears the risk for which the surplus is charged.

The fifth possible justification is labor. This is the logic of stipendium laboris. Finance often requires real work: due diligence, mentoring, governance, accounting, reporting, structuring, stewardship, legal review, portfolio support, conflict mediation, and administration. Where this labor is necessary and actually performed, compensation may be justified. The Monti di Pietà depended on this logic. Charitable lending could not function without people to manage pledges, accounts, buildings, records, and operations.

But labor compensation too can become extraction. Management fees may detach from actual work. Monitoring fees may become routine yield. Advisory fees may be charged whether advice is useful or not. Fund managers may be paid comfortably while the enterprises they serve remain fragile. The moral test is whether the labor is real, necessary, proportionate, transparent, and ordered toward service. A fee for stewardship must actually steward.

The sixth possible justification is expense. This is related to labor and damnum emergens, but not identical. A financing structure may involve legal costs, audit costs, accounting systems, regulatory compliance, bank fees, transaction costs, reporting tools, insurance, governance infrastructure, or administrative overhead. These may be reimbursed when they are necessary, reasonable, and tied to the mission of the financing. Without cost recovery, non-extractive institutions may collapse, leaving borrowers to predatory alternatives.

Yet expense claims must be scrutinized, because institutions easily rationalize their own growth. What begins as necessary administration can become bureaucratic self-preservation. Every professional service, consultant, platform, reporting burden, and internal process may be described as necessary. The audit must ask: necessary for what? For serving the borrower? For satisfying investors? For regulatory compliance? For institutional prestige? For managerial convenience? Expense reimbursement is licit only when the expense is real, proportionate, and properly allocated.

The seventh possible justification is bargaining power. This is not a legitimate extrinsic title, but it is often the hidden reason surplus exists. The borrower pays because the lender can demand it. The founder accepts terms because no better capital is available. The community agrees because the infrastructure is needed. The entrepreneur signs because time is short. The contract is "market standard" because the market is structured by capital's preferences. Bargaining power can explain surplus sociologically, but it cannot justify it morally.

This distinction is crucial. Much of modern finance confuses enforceability with justice. If a term can be negotiated, signed, and enforced, it is treated as legitimate. But scholastic moral theology does not accept this shortcut. A price may be agreed and still unjust. A borrower may consent and still be exploited. A contract may be legal and still extractive. Bargaining power tells us who had leverage. It does not tell us whether the surplus corresponds to a real burden.

The audit should therefore require every surplus component to identify its title. Interest: what does it compensate? Default interest: what harm does it address? Arrangement fee: what cost or labor does it reimburse? Monitoring fee: what work is performed? Liquidation preference: what risk does it protect against, and is that risk already priced elsewhere? Warrant coverage: what uncertainty does it compensate? Redemption premium: what burden justifies it? Legal cost reimbursement: are the costs actual and reasonable? Management fee: what stewardship is provided? Carry: what risk or value creation supports it?

This exercise often reveals moral confusion. Some charges have no title beyond convention. Some charges duplicate others. Some charges remain after their justification has disappeared. Some charges are calculated as percentages of capital when the underlying burden is not percentage-based. Some charges are imposed automatically without regard to actual harm. Some charges are justified by risk even though the contract protects capital from loss. The audit does not assume bad faith. It asks for truthfulness.

A non-extractive structure should make this truthfulness easy. It should state why each payment exists. Administrative fees should correspond to actual administration. Stewardship fees should correspond to actual stewardship. Late penalties should be proportionate and avoidable. Risk returns should be tied to real loss absorption. Opportunity-cost claims should be concrete and mission-aligned, if used at all. Repayments should come from surplus, not depletion. Caps should prevent compensation from becoming unlimited extraction.

The source of surplus also matters. Does the surplus come from genuine value created by the enterprise? From distributable profit after obligations and reserves? From an exit? From gross revenue despite losses? From late payment? From legal enforcement? From refinancing by another lender? From cutting wages? From reducing mission commitments? The same amount may have different moral meaning depending on its source. Surplus taken from flourishing differs from surplus taken from fragility.

Timing matters as well. A payment demanded upfront may be more suspect than one paid after the burden is known. This was one of the concerns with lucrum cessans: compensation for foregone profit should not simply be deducted in advance as if the future were already certain. Similarly, risk compensation should not be structured so that capital receives premium returns before risk has actually unfolded or while the enterprise remains fragile. Payment after true surplus is morally different from payment before resilience.

Proportionality is the final discipline. Even a valid title can be exceeded. A real loss does not justify unlimited damages. A real delay does not justify punitive revenue. A real opportunity does not justify speculative benchmarks. Real risk does not justify unconstrained upside. Real labor does not justify excessive fees. Real expense does not justify institutional bloat. The title opens the door to compensation; proportionality determines how far one may walk through it.

This is where caps become important. A cap acknowledges that the purpose of surplus is not infinite growth. It limits return to a defined, transparent, morally reviewable amount. In non-extractive finance, caps can help keep compensation from becoming domination, especially when combined with repayment from surplus, loss absorption, mission locks, and hardship provisions. A cap does not automatically make a structure just, but it expresses a refusal of unlimited claim.

The fourth question of the scholastic audit is therefore a question of moral accounting. Every euro above principal must tell the truth about why it is owed. It must point to a real burden, a real service, a real risk, a real loss, or a real participation. It must not hide behind convention, complexity, or bargaining power. The older language of the extrinsic titles gives us a disciplined vocabulary for this accounting.

In the end, the question "what justifies surplus?" is the question that separates compensation from extraction. Compensation can name its burden; extraction hides behind abstraction. Compensation is limited by the reality it repairs; extraction expands as far as power allows. In a moral economy, surplus answers to truth, which is why a contract that cannot clearly explain its surplus should not be trusted merely because it is legal, standard, or financially sophisticated.

sections.title: 39.5 Fifth Question: Can the Title Be Abused?
paragraphs: The fifth question in a scholastic audit is: can the title be abused? This question must be asked even when a surplus payment appears to have a valid justification. The older tradition did not merely ask whether a lender could name an extrinsic title. It asked whether that title was real, proportionate, and free from fraud. This matters because the most dangerous forms of extraction often do not appear as open violations. They appear as plausible titles stretched beyond their proper limits.

A title can be true in principle and false in use. A late penalty can be legitimate, but also engineered. A damage claim can restore equality, but also invent loss. A foregone-profit claim can compensate a concrete interrupted use, but also universalize opportunity cost. A risk premium can reward genuine loss-bearing, but also disguise risk-shifting. A management fee can pay for real stewardship, but also become institutional rent. A legal cost reimbursement can be fair, but also become a weapon. The audit must therefore examine not only the title's name, but its pattern of use.

The danger of engineered default belongs especially to poena conventionalis. A penalty for delay is morally plausible only when delay is culpable, avoidable, and genuinely unwanted by the lender. But if the repayment schedule is unrealistic, the grace period too short, the covenants too sensitive, the reporting burden too heavy, or the penalty too profitable, the contract may be designed to fail. Default then becomes not an accident but a revenue event. The penalty is no longer a remedy for disorder. It is the business model.

The simplest diagnostic question is: does the lender prefer timely repayment? If the lender would rather be paid on time and never collect the penalty, the title may be genuine. If the lender expects, models, or welcomes the penalty as yield, suspicion is warranted. In modern contracts, this requires looking at default interest, late fees, covenant breach fees, extension fees, restructuring charges, and acceleration clauses. A humane contract helps the borrower return to order. An extractive contract profits when the borrower falls out of order.

The danger of fictive loss belongs especially to damnum emergens. Actual loss is the cleanest title because it is tied to restitution. But precisely because it is morally strong, it can be tempting to label many things as loss. General overhead becomes borrower-specific cost. Ordinary business expense becomes damage. Internal time becomes a billable injury. Conservative provisioning becomes a claim against the borrower. A vague liquidity inconvenience becomes a quantified charge. The word "loss" expands until it covers nearly everything the lender wants to recover.

To prevent this, the audit must require documentation and causality. What exactly was lost? When? By whom? Because of what act or delay? Would the cost have existed anyway? Is it already compensated by another fee? Is the amount proportionate? If a fund charges an administration fee, it should not also treat the same administration as damages. If legal costs are reimbursed, they should be actual and reasonable. If liquidity harm is claimed, it should be specific rather than merely asserted. Damnum emergens restores equality only when the damage is real.

The danger of abstract opportunity cost belongs especially to lucrum cessans. This title begins with a plausible claim: lending may prevent the lender from using capital elsewhere. But it becomes abusive when "elsewhere" is undefined. If every holder of money can say that the money could have earned something somewhere, then every loan becomes a claim for lost profit. The title ceases to be an exception and becomes the ordinary logic of interest.

A scholastic audit must therefore ask whether the foregone gain was concrete. Was there a real alternative use? Was it intended? Was it licit? Was it reasonably certain? Was it actually prevented by the loan? Was the valuation modest and proportionate? Or is the lender simply importing a market benchmark, fund hurdle, or imagined return? The abuse of lucrum cessans occurs when hypothetical yield is treated as morally possessed property. The lender does not repair a loss. The lender charges the borrower for interrupting capital's fantasy of itself.

The danger of pretended risk belongs especially to periculum sortis. Risk can justify surplus only when the principal is genuinely exposed. But modern finance is skilled at speaking the language of risk while reducing capital's vulnerability through seniority, collateral, guarantees, covenants, insurance, liquidation preferences, redemption rights, and control provisions. The investor then charges for risk while arranging for others to bear much of it. This is not risk-bearing. It is risk-shifting.

The audit must therefore ask what happens in the downside case. Can principal actually be lost? Does repayment disappear, pause, subordinate, or convert when the enterprise fails? Or does capital enforce against assets, founders, workers, or mission? Is the investor paid before the company has rebuilt resilience? Does the investor hold preference and also claim a risk premium? Are warrants, default interest, collateral, and fees all compensating the same uncertainty? A true risk title requires vulnerability. Without vulnerability, risk language becomes false description.

Double-counting is one of the most common forms of abuse. The same burden may be charged under several names. Risk appears in the interest rate, again in the liquidation preference, again in the warrants, again in the covenants, again in default penalties, and again in legal cost reimbursement. Administrative work appears in a management fee, monitoring fee, transaction fee, and expense reimbursement. Delay appears in default interest, late fees, extension fees, and accelerated repayment. Each charge may look modest alone. Together, they create an extractive stack.

A scholastic audit must therefore examine the whole structure, not isolated clauses. It should ask: what burdens have already been compensated? What protections reduce the risks being priced? What fees overlap? Which charges remain even when their justification disappears? Which payments are percentage-based even though the underlying cost is fixed? Which charges compound? Which charges are paid before the borrower has recovered? The moral unit is the transaction as a whole.

Hidden compounding is another danger. The older usury tradition was particularly concerned with the way debt could grow beyond the borrower's capacity to repay. Modern contracts can reproduce this through default interest, capitalized interest, payment-in-kind provisions, rolling fees into principal, extension charges, refinancing fees, and penalty accruals. The borrower begins with one obligation and ends with another, larger obligation generated by distress itself. This is the financial logic by which weakness becomes yield.

The audit should therefore ask whether hardship increases the lender's gain. If the borrower is late, does the claim grow? If the borrower cannot pay, are fees added? If the company needs restructuring, is the lender rewarded for granting time? If the borrower is already fragile, does the contract deepen fragility? A non-extractive structure should not turn distress into compounding advantage. It should contain mechanisms for grace, restructuring, loss-sharing, or capped recovery.

Another abuse is title migration. When one justification becomes difficult to defend, another is invoked. A lender may begin by claiming risk, then emphasize opportunity cost, then point to administrative expense, then rely on penalty, then invoke market standard. The surplus remains constant while the justification shifts. This is a sign that the title may be serving the charge, rather than the charge serving the title. In moral accounting, the title should determine the payment. In extractive accounting, the desired payment searches for a title.

There is also abuse through abstraction. The more abstract the title, the more easily it can be manipulated. Actual loss is easier to verify than foregone gain. Foregone gain is easier to discipline than generalized liquidity absence. Genuine risk-bearing can be examined, but portfolio risk language can obscure the borrower's lived downside. The audit must therefore demand more evidence as the title becomes more abstract. A vague appeal to "risk," "market," "cost of capital," or "illiquidity" is not enough.

Abuse can also arise through asymmetry of knowledge. Financial contracts are often written by sophisticated parties for less sophisticated counterparties. Founders, cooperatives, nonprofits, social enterprises, or community organizations may not understand how clauses interact under stress. They may see the headline cap but not the priority. They may see the patient term but not the default trigger. They may see the mission language but not the exit right. A title can be formally disclosed and still practically obscure.

A non-extractive contract should therefore be intelligible. It should explain what each surplus claim is for. It should show repayment scenarios. It should disclose downside consequences. It should make clear who gets paid first, what happens in default, how return is capped, how hardship is handled, and when capital can lose. Complexity is not inherently unjust, but complexity that hides moral consequences is dangerous. The scholastic instinct is toward clarity, because unclear titles easily become pretexts.

Abuse may also occur through external pressure. A fund manager may sincerely want non-extractive terms but face LP expectations, regulatory constraints, benchmark comparisons, budget needs, or reputational pressure. These pressures can push the structure toward higher fees, faster repayment, stricter protections, or more aggressive enforcement. The title may remain in the documents, but the institution's surrounding incentives may hollow it out. A serious audit should therefore examine not only the contract but the ecosystem of pressure around it.

This is where governance becomes part of the anti-abuse framework. Who decides whether a penalty is enforced? Who determines whether hardship is genuine? Who can waive repayment? Who reviews fees? Who represents the borrower, workers, mission, or community? Who audits the classification of surplus? Without governance, even well-designed titles can drift. The Monti di Pietà show this historical lesson clearly: good institutions need countermeasures, because mission can decay through routine, cost, and self-preservation.

Anti-abuse analysis also requires attention to time. A title that is justified at the beginning may cease to be justified later. Risk may decline after collateral increases or the company becomes stable. Administrative costs may fall as processes standardize. A foregone opportunity may no longer be relevant. A penalty may cease to be proportionate after partial repayment. A management fee may no longer correspond to actual work. Moral review should therefore be periodic, not merely initial. Surplus should remain accountable throughout the life of the contract.

The audit should also ask whether the borrower's flourishing is part of the design. A contract may avoid obvious abuse and still fail the deeper test if it treats the borrower merely as a payment source. Does the structure help the enterprise become more resilient? Does it allow reinvestment? Does it protect wages? Does it prevent mission drift? Does it reduce the chance of destructive default? Does it create pathways for amendment under hardship? If not, the title may be technically valid but pastorally thin.

The fifth question therefore turns the audit from classification to vigilance. It recognizes that moral categories decay when they meet incentive, fear, pressure, and power. The extrinsic titles are useful only if they remain disciplined. A title without anti-abuse safeguards is an invitation to drift. The history of usury doctrine is, in large part, the history of this drift: from compensation to convention, from exception to norm, from title to pretext.

For modern non-extractive finance, anti-abuse design should be explicit. Late penalties should be capped, proportionate, avoidable, and subject to grace. Damages should require documentation. Opportunity-cost claims should be rare, concrete, licit, and never generic. Risk premiums should be tied to actual loss absorption and reduced when protections increase. Management and stewardship fees should be transparent and reviewable. Collateral should be limited. Personal guarantees should be avoided. Returns should be capped. Repayment should come from surplus after resilience. Mission-protective governance should be able to override extractive enforcement.

The final abuse is spiritual: the desire to be justified without being changed. A lender or investor may want the language of moral finance while retaining the instincts of ordinary capital. It may want to say "stewardship" while preserving priority, "risk-sharing" while avoiding loss, "mission" while forcing exit, "compensation" while seeking yield, "non-extractive" while importing market expectations. The scholastic audit is meant to prevent this self-deception. It asks capital to tell the truth not only in words, but in structure.

The question "can the title be abused?" must therefore accompany every title from beginning to end. It is what keeps poena from becoming a trap, damnum from becoming fiction, and lucrum from becoming opportunity-cost metaphysics. It is what keeps periculum from becoming risk rhetoric, and labor and expense reimbursement from becoming institutional rent. And it is what keeps non-extractive finance from becoming conventional finance with gentler language.

In the end, this fifth question protects the moral seriousness of the whole tradition. The extrinsic titles were never meant to be passwords that unlock surplus. They were tests. And every test can be cheated. A scholastic audit must therefore examine not only whether a title exists, but whether the contract has been designed so that the title remains truthful under pressure. Only then does surplus remain compensation rather than extraction.

chapters.part: Part XII — Toward Non-Extractive Finance

chapters.title: Translating the Four Titles into a NonExtractive Capital Framework
chapters.label: Chapter 40

sections.title: 40.1 Poena Conventionalis in Modern Contracts
paragraphs: In modern contracts, poena conventionalis appears wherever a borrower, company, or counterparty must pay an additional amount because of delay, default, breach, or non-performance. The language has changed. We now speak of late fees, default interest, cure periods, grace periods, acceleration clauses, penalty rates, extension fees, break fees, covenant breaches, and liquidated damages. But the moral question remains close to the medieval one: is this charge a true penalty for disorder, or a disguised way of earning surplus from the loan?

A penalty can be legitimate. Contracts require reliability, and a lender, investor, supplier, worker, or institution may be harmed when payment is late or obligations are ignored. Delay can create administrative costs, liquidity stress, legal expenses, planning disruption, or lost trust. A penalty can therefore serve a real function: it can deter breach, compensate harm, encourage timely performance, and protect the order of the relationship. The older tradition did not deny this. It asked whether the penalty was truly penal, proportionate, and not a fraud on the usury prohibition.

Modern late fees should be read through this lens. A modest late fee may be justified if it corresponds to real administrative burden or harm caused by delay. But if the late fee is automatic, excessive, compounding, unrelated to actual damage, or imposed on borrowers who could not realistically avoid delay, it becomes morally suspect. The question is not simply whether the contract permits the fee. The question is whether the fee restores order or monetizes distress.

Default interest is even more dangerous. In many debt instruments, the interest rate increases when the borrower defaults. This may be defended as compensation for increased risk, increased monitoring, or the cost of disorder, and sometimes that may be true. But default interest can also become a way for the lender to profit precisely when the borrower is weakest. The borrower misses a payment, breaches a covenant, or faces a cash flow problem, and the cost of capital rises at the very moment when repayment capacity has fallen. Unless carefully limited, default interest can turn fragility into yield.

The scholastic diagnostic question remains powerful: does the lender genuinely prefer timely repayment? In a just structure, the lender wants the borrower to perform. The penalty is a regrettable remedy. It exists to protect order, not to create revenue. In an extractive structure, default is expected, modeled, or even privately desired. The lender may price the transaction assuming that late fees, default interest, extension fees, or restructuring charges will be collected. At that point, the penalty no longer looks like poena conventionalis. It looks like hidden interest.

Cure periods are therefore morally significant. A cure period gives the borrower time to correct a breach before harsher consequences follow. It recognizes that disorder may be temporary, accidental, or remediable. A contract with no meaningful cure period treats breach as an opportunity for immediate leverage; a contract with a fair cure period seeks restoration. This is not only a legal difference but a theological one. The first asks, "How quickly may I enforce?" The second asks, "Can the relationship be repaired?"

Grace periods carry a similar meaning. A grace period acknowledges that small delays may occur without bad faith. Payments can be missed because of bank processing, customer delays, illness, administrative error, or short-term cash flow mismatch. A morally serious contract distinguishes between culpable delay and ordinary human fragility. Grace periods prevent the lender from treating every stumble as a profit event. They make room for mercy inside justice.

Hardship exceptions are equally important, because not every delay is morally equivalent. A borrower who refuses to pay despite capacity differs from a borrower affected by illness, disaster, market collapse, theft, war, regulatory delay, or the failure of a major customer to pay. A contract that imposes the same penalty in every case may be administratively simple, but morally crude. Christian moral theology asks whether delay is culpable, unavoidable, or tragic. A non-extractive contract should include mechanisms for hardship review, payment suspension, restructuring, or negotiated cure.

Caps are necessary because penalties can grow beyond their moral basis. A late fee may begin as modest compensation and become extractive if it compounds, repeats, or accumulates without limit. Default interest can overwhelm the original obligation, extension fees can become recurring tribute, and legal fees can balloon. A cap says that the purpose of the penalty is not unlimited enrichment. It limits the penalty to a defined and reviewable relationship with the harm caused, and in moral terms a cap helps preserve the difference between discipline and domination.

Proportionality is the central test. A penalty should correspond to the seriousness of the breach, the harm caused, and the borrower's capacity to avoid it. A minor administrative delay should not trigger a severe financial burden. A technical covenant breach that causes no material harm should not become an excuse for punitive economics. A missed report should not allow the lender to impose default interest designed for payment failure. When penalties are disproportionate, they cease to repair order and begin to exploit technical power.

Modern contracts often contain multiple default triggers. Payment default is one. Breach of financial covenants is another. Failure to deliver information, maintain insurance, meet reporting deadlines, preserve ratios, obtain consent, avoid certain transactions, or comply with representations may all trigger consequences. Some of these protections are reasonable. But the more numerous and sensitive the triggers, the greater the risk of engineered default. The borrower may remain economically viable while technically exposed. The lender gains leverage not because the borrower has failed morally, but because the document creates many ways to fall.

This is especially important in venture debt, private credit, and growth finance. Covenants may allow the lender to intervene before severe deterioration, which can be prudent. But covenants can also become tripwires. If breach triggers higher interest, fees, warrants, acceleration, control rights, or renegotiation leverage, then the lender may benefit from the borrower's volatility. The contract may claim to manage risk while actually monetizing instability. A poena analysis asks whether each trigger protects a real interest or creates a pretext for surplus.

Acceleration clauses require similar scrutiny. Acceleration allows the lender to demand immediate repayment of the full outstanding amount after default. This may be necessary when the borrower is dissipating assets or acting in bad faith. But acceleration can also be destructive. A borrower who could have cured over time may be pushed into insolvency because the whole debt becomes due at once. The lender then gains enforcement power precisely when the borrower has least capacity to respond. A non-extractive structure should reserve acceleration for serious, uncured, material defaults rather than technical or temporary breaches.

Extension fees also sit near the boundary. If a borrower needs more time, the lender may incur administrative work, liquidity burden, or increased exposure, and a reasonable extension fee may compensate real cost. But if extension fees are routine, percentage-based, repeated, or expected, they can become payment for time under another name. The older warning returns: to charge merely because the borrower has money longer is morally suspect unless tied to a real external burden. Extension should be a tool of restoration, not a toll gate.

Liquidated damages clauses present another version of the same issue. Parties may agree in advance to a sum payable upon breach because actual damages would be difficult to calculate later. This can be legitimate. But the agreed amount must be a reasonable forecast of harm, not a punitive or revenue-generating charge. If liquidated damages greatly exceed plausible loss, they become a fiction. The name "damages" does not make them restorative. They must still answer to truth.

A modern poena conventionalis framework should therefore ask several questions. Was the due date realistic? Was the obligation clear? Was the breach material? Was the delay culpable? Was there a grace period? Was there a cure period? Was hardship considered? Was the penalty proportionate? Did the lender prefer timely performance? Is the penalty capped? Is the penalty calculated from actual harm or from desired yield? Is the same harm also charged elsewhere through interest, fees, costs, or risk premiums?

This last question, double-counting, is crucial. A lender may already be compensated for risk through the ordinary return, may already recover administrative costs through fees, and may already hold collateral and legal cost reimbursement. If default interest, late fees, and extension fees are added on top, what exactly remains uncompensated? Without careful accounting, poena becomes one more layer in a surplus stack, and the borrower pays repeatedly for the same disorder.

For non-extractive capital, penalties should be designed with a restorative purpose: their goal is to bring the relationship back into good order. This means the contract should distinguish between technical, temporary, material, and bad-faith defaults; allow cure and provide grace; allow communication before escalation; include hardship provisions; cap penalties and prevent their compounding; prohibit the lender from treating penalties as expected return; and make default less profitable than timely repayment.

This last design principle is decisive. The lender should be economically better off when the borrower performs on time than when the borrower defaults. If the lender earns more from default than from performance, the moral incentives are inverted, and the contract becomes a machine that feeds on disorder. A truly penal clause should be something no one wants to activate. It should protect the relationship from breach, not make breach lucrative.

In mission-driven finance, this matters even more. The goal is not simply repayment. The goal is the flourishing of the enterprise, the protection of mission, the dignity of workers, the service of users, and the responsible stewardship of capital. A harsh default regime can destroy these goods even if it protects the investor. If late penalties force layoffs, prevent reinvestment, drain reserves, or trigger mission drift, then they must be judged not only financially but theologically. They may protect capital while wounding the life capital was meant to serve.

There is also a pastoral dimension. Borrowers should not be trained to hide distress because the contract punishes early disclosure. If every warning sign triggers penalty or enforcement, founders and managers may delay communication until the crisis is worse. A non-extractive contract should encourage truth-telling. It should reward early notice, cooperative restructuring, and shared problem-solving. This is not softness. It is prudent mercy. It makes recovery more likely and domination less tempting.

A good penalty clause therefore contains both firmness and mercy. Firmness says that obligations matter, time matters, and breach has consequences. Mercy says that not every failure is bad faith, not every delay is culpable, and not every wound should be monetized. The older tradition held these together. It did not deny penalties. It denied penalties used in fraudem: in fraud of the usury prohibition, in fraud of justice, in fraud of the borrower's vulnerability.

The modern translation is clear. Late fees, default interest, cure periods, grace periods, caps, and hardship exceptions are not merely drafting details. They are moral architecture. They reveal whether the lender wants restoration or leverage, whether time is being monetized as yield, whether the borrower's weakness becomes a source of revenue, and whether the contract disciplines disorder or profits from it.

For a non-extractive capital framework, poena conventionalis should be permitted only under strict conditions. The obligation must be clear and realistic, the delay culpable or at least materially harmful, and the penalty proportionate to actual or reasonably anticipated harm. Grace and cure must be available, hardship must be considered, and penalties must be capped and non-compounding. The lender must prefer timely repayment, the penalty must not be double-counted with other charges, and the structure must be designed to restore relationship, not exploit default.

In the end, poena conventionalis teaches modern finance that default is morally dangerous for both parties. It can reveal borrower disorder, but also lender temptation. The borrower may fail to perform. The lender may discover that failure is profitable. A just contract guards against both. It calls the borrower back to fidelity without allowing the lender to turn infidelity into yield. In that discipline, the old title still serves the design of capital that serves life.

sections.title: 40.2 Damnum Emergens in Modern Accounting
paragraphs: In modern accounting, damnum emergens appears wherever a lender, investor, fund, or financing institution claims reimbursement for actual loss. The old phrase means "loss arising": not profit expected, not yield desired, not money absent, but damage actually incurred. In contemporary terms, it may include administrative costs, enforcement costs, liquidity harm, legal expenses, audit costs, restructuring costs, collection costs, or other documented losses caused by the transaction or by the borrower's failure to perform. Its moral logic is restorative. Something has been harmed, and justice permits repair.

This title is the cleanest of the four because it remains closest to commutative justice. If one party causes another to suffer real loss, the injured party may be restored. The point is not to enrich the lender, but to re-establish equality. This is why damnum emergens is so important for non-extractive finance. It allows moral seriousness without financial naivete. It recognizes that serving borrowers, enterprises, and missions can impose real burdens. Non-extractive capital still requires administration, accounting, legal work, governance, monitoring, reporting, and sometimes enforcement. These costs do not disappear because the mission is good.

But damnum emergens is also dangerous, because the language of loss is morally powerful. Once a cost is called "damage," it appears to demand reimbursement; once a fee is called "cost recovery," it appears modest; and once an institution says, "We only recover our expenses," the moral suspicion seems to fade. Yet not every cost is a borrower-caused loss, not every institutional expense should be shifted to the enterprise, not every internal burden is proportionate, and not every invoice belongs to this transaction. A non-extractive accounting system must therefore distinguish actual loss from institutional appetite.

The first category is actual administrative cost. A financing relationship requires work: onboarding, due diligence, KYC or compliance checks, contract preparation, payment administration, reporting, bookkeeping, portfolio review, and communication. These are real costs, and a fund that does not recover them may become unsustainable. The Monti di Pietà already faced this problem in another form. Charitable lending required staff, buildings, records, pledge management, and operational discipline. The question was not whether administration existed, but whether charges remained tied to administration rather than becoming profit.

Modern administrative costs should therefore be transparent, reasonable, and allocated carefully. A flat onboarding fee may be justifiable if it reflects actual setup work. A recurring administration fee may be justified if recurring work is performed. But if the fee is calculated as a percentage of principal without relation to actual labor, or if the same work is also covered by a management fee, monitoring fee, and expense reimbursement, the moral basis weakens. The accounting must show what work was done, by whom, for whose benefit, and at what reasonable cost.

The second category is enforcement cost. When a borrower refuses to pay, fails to communicate, breaches material obligations, or forces legal action, the lender may incur costs that would not otherwise exist. Legal letters, mediation, court filings, collection proceedings, restructuring counsel, valuation work, and recovery administration can all be real. If caused by culpable borrower conduct, reimbursement may fall under damnum emergens. The lender is not profiting from the loan, but recovering costs imposed by disorder.

Yet enforcement costs are morally delicate. A powerful lender can use legal machinery to overwhelm a weaker borrower, and the mere fact that a lawyer was hired does not mean the cost should be shifted without question. Was enforcement necessary? Was it proportionate? Was there an attempt to resolve the matter cooperatively? Were the legal costs reasonable relative to the amount at stake? Did the lender escalate prematurely? Did the contract encourage communication and cure before enforcement? A non-extractive framework should not allow legal cost reimbursement to become a weapon.

The third category is liquidity harm, and it is more difficult. A lender may suffer because expected repayment did not arrive when due. The lender may have obligations to others, payroll, fund commitments, planned deployments, or operational needs. If the borrower's delay forced the lender to borrow elsewhere, sell assets at a loss, miss a payment, or incur specific costs, those may be actual losses. But vague inconvenience from being without money is not enough. Liquidity harm must be concrete, not merely asserted.

This is where damnum emergens approaches carentia pecuniae and lucrum cessans. Being without money may create a real loss in some cases. But if the claim becomes simply, "I did not have my money for a period of time," then the title slides toward monetizing time. The audit must ask: what specific loss arose from the absence of liquidity? Was another obligation missed? Was emergency borrowing necessary? Was a real cost incurred? Was the harm caused by this borrower's delay? Without such specificity, liquidity harm becomes a disguised time charge.

The fourth category is documented losses. Documentation is not a bureaucratic luxury; it is a moral safeguard. Damnum emergens depends on reality, and reality must be traceable. In modern accounting, that means invoices, timesheets, bank statements, loan agreements, legal bills, accounting entries, correspondence, cash flow reports, and board minutes where relevant. A claim that cannot be documented may still be emotionally understandable, but it is not yet morally chargeable. Documentation prevents the title from becoming fiction.

Documentation also enables proportionality. If the actual legal cost was €2,000, the lender should not claim €10,000 under the name of damages. If staff time was two hours, it should not be billed as twenty. If a delay caused a small bank fee, it should not trigger a large percentage penalty. If a liquidity gap was covered from reserves without cost, the lender should not invent a market-rate loss. Accounting disciplines desire by forcing the claim to meet the ledger.

The fifth category is reimbursement. Reimbursement differs from yield because it returns the injured or burdened party to its prior position. A reimbursed cost should leave the lender whole, not better off. This is the moral center of damnum emergens. The lender may recover what was actually spent or lost. But reimbursement should not be calculated to meet a target return, internal rate of return, hurdle rate, or portfolio expectation. Once a damage claim is shaped by desired yield, it ceases to be pure reimbursement.

This creates an important rule for non-extractive capital: no target IRR should be hidden inside damnum emergens. If a fund wishes to earn a return, it must name the title supporting that return. Is it risk-bearing? Is it concrete foregone gain? Is it stewardship labor? Is it surplus-sharing? It should not inflate administrative costs or damages to reach a financial target. Damage accounting must be boring, concrete, and modest. Its credibility lies precisely in its refusal to become a return model.

The sixth category is fictional damages. These are charges that appear as losses but do not correspond to real harm. They may be formulaic, excessive, duplicative, or disconnected from the transaction. A contract might impose a fixed "damage" amount upon breach without regard to actual injury. A fund might allocate general overhead to a borrower because the borrower is available to charge. A lender might treat expected profit as a damage. A creditor might claim reputational harm, opportunity loss, or internal inconvenience without evidence. These are not damnum emergens. They are extraction in the grammar of injury.

Fictional damages often arise through percentages. Many fees are calculated as a percentage of principal because that is easy and conventional. But not every cost scales with principal. Preparing a €50,000 loan may not cost one-tenth of preparing a €500,000 loan, and monitoring a €1 million investment may not cost exactly twice as much as monitoring a €500,000 investment. Percentage-based charges are not automatically wrong, but they must be tested. Are they a reasonable proxy for cost, or a yield mechanism disguised as cost recovery?

A related danger is allocation drift. In a fund or institution, costs are often pooled: staff, rent, software, legal retainers, compliance, audits, travel, communications, governance, and reporting. Some allocation is necessary. But if cost allocation is opaque, borrowers may be charged for institutional expenses only loosely related to them. A non-extractive fund should therefore have an allocation policy. Which costs are charged directly? Which are covered by general management fees? Which are borne by the fund? Which are never passed to portfolio companies? Without such rules, damnum emergens can slip into institutional self-preservation.

Modern accounting systems can help preserve moral clarity. Each surplus payment can be classified: principal repayment, administrative cost, legal reimbursement, stewardship fee, late penalty, risk return, foregone-profit compensation, expense reimbursement, donation, grant recycling, or other. Payments that do not fit should be marked "needs review." Related-party payments should be flagged. Owner distributions should be separated from operating costs. Management fees should be distinguished from pass-through expenses. This is not merely bookkeeping. It is moral theology in ledger form. Exact Online exports, bank statements, invoices, and fund accounting can therefore become tools of discernment. They can show whether charges correspond to real costs or drift into yield, reveal double-counting, distinguish reimbursement from profit, identify whether penalties are recurring revenue, and show whether administrative fees exceed administrative work. They can help investors, founders, auditors, and mission stewards see whether the structure remains truthful.

A useful non-extractive accounting framework would classify damnum emergens claims according to several tests. Is the loss actual? Is it documented? Was it caused by this transaction or this breach? Was it reasonably avoidable by the lender? Was the response proportionate? Is the cost necessary? Is it allocated fairly? Has it already been covered by another fee? Is the amount restorative rather than profitable? Does collection of the reimbursement harm the borrower's recovery more than justice requires?

This last question matters because even real losses must be handled with prudence and mercy. Suppose a borrower causes €5,000 in legal costs but is already in serious distress. The lender may have a claim. But should it collect immediately? Should it defer? Should it waive part? Should it convert the amount into a longer-term repayment? Should it distinguish between bad faith and misfortune? The existence of damnum emergens does not abolish pastoral judgment. It identifies a possible claim; it does not command harsh enforcement in every circumstance.

In mission finance, reimbursement should also be assessed against the purpose of the relationship. If collecting every cost undermines the enterprise's mission, destroys trust, or prevents recovery, the fund must ask whether mercy better serves the common good. This does not mean absorbing all losses automatically. It means that reimbursement remains within a broader moral order. Justice repairs equality; mercy protects life; stewardship must hold them together.

Damnum emergens also requires no double-counting. If administrative costs are covered by a management fee, they should not be charged again as damages. If legal costs are reimbursed separately, they should not be embedded in a default penalty. If liquidity harm is compensated through an agreed extension fee, it should not also be claimed as lost profit. If risk is priced through a return premium, the same risk should not be relabeled as damage when it materializes. Double-counting transforms compensation into extraction.

This is particularly important for fund structures. Investors may pay management fees to cover the fund's operations. Portfolio companies may also pay arrangement fees, monitoring fees, legal fees, and expense reimbursements. The fund must be careful not to charge both sides for the same work. If LPs fund operating capacity, and companies are charged again for that capacity, the structure may over-recover costs. Non-extractive finance requires transparent accounting not only between fund and borrower, but also between fund, LPs, managers, and mission beneficiaries.

The Monti di Pietà remain instructive because their historical problem was not abstract. They had to lend cheaply enough to serve the poor, but charge enough to survive. Their moral legitimacy depended on keeping charges tied to real expense and labor, not turning charitable lending into ordinary profit. Modern non-extractive funds face a similar test. They must survive without letting survival become an idol. They must recover costs without making cost recovery a mask for extraction.

A good damnum emergens policy should therefore be written into the contract and the accounting system. It should define reimbursable costs and exclude general overhead unless specifically allocated by transparent policy, require documentation, cap certain categories, and require borrower notice where possible. It should distinguish ordinary administration from extraordinary enforcement, provide hardship review, require periodic audit, and classify every payment according to title.

This policy should also state what may not be included: no fictional damages, no generic market return, no internal target IRR, no duplicated management costs, no penalty disguised as reimbursement, no unrelated institutional expenses, no speculative liquidity harm, no legal escalation costs caused by the lender's own unnecessary aggression, and no expenses that primarily serve investor extraction rather than enterprise stewardship.

The theological importance of this discipline is profound. Damnum emergens tells the truth about harm. It says that justice does not require one party to absorb real injury caused by another. But it also says that harm must not be invented. In a fallen economy, the language of injury can be used to justify taking. The creditor can portray himself as wounded in order to wound the borrower. Accounting becomes moral when it prevents this reversal.

For non-extractive finance, damnum emergens should become the foundation of transparent cost recovery. It allows funds to be sustainable, contracts to be enforceable, and lenders to be protected from real injury. But it also limits them. It refuses to let reimbursement become yield, expenses become entitlement, or losses become fictional. It keeps capital's claim tied to reality.

In the end, damnum emergens in modern accounting asks a simple question: what actual loss arose, and what payment would restore it without exceeding it? That question is humble, concrete, and powerful. It disciplines finance by making surplus answerable to proof; it protects borrowers by preventing vague charges and protects institutions by allowing real cost recovery. And it protects the moral integrity of non-extractive capital by ensuring that compensation remains compensation, not profit wearing the face of damage.

sections.title: 40.3 Lucrum Cessans in Modern Portfolio Theory
paragraphs: In modern portfolio theory, lucrum cessans reappears under the language of opportunity cost, benchmark return, hurdle rate, cost of capital, expected return, and efficient allocation. The old phrase means "gain ceasing": a profit that would have been made, but was not made because the capital was used elsewhere. In its strict form, this title concerned a concrete foregone gain. In modern portfolio theory, however, the claim often becomes much broader: capital always has alternative uses, and therefore any use of capital must compensate the owner for the return that could have been earned somewhere else.

This is where the old doctrine becomes newly important. Modern finance often treats opportunity cost as a neutral fact. If money is invested in one enterprise, it cannot be invested in another. If a fund makes a below-market loan, it forgoes a market-rate return. If an investor accepts capped upside, it forgoes uncapped upside. If capital is locked into a patient structure, it loses liquidity and optionality. All of this may be true at the level of prudential reasoning, and a steward must know what is being sacrificed. But lucrum cessans asks a sharper question: when does a foregone alternative become a morally chargeable claim against the borrower?

The scholastic answer was never simply, "whenever capital could have earned elsewhere." That would have destroyed the usury prohibition. If every lender can say that money might have been profitably deployed in some alternative use, then every loan carries a title to surplus. The strict tradition therefore demanded concreteness. The foregone gain had to be more than possible. It had to be connected to a real, intended, licit, and reasonably certain alternative. The loan had to be the cause of the gain ceasing. The compensation had to be proportionate. The title was possible, but dangerous.

Modern portfolio theory tends to abstract away from precisely those conditions. It does not usually ask what specific alternative use the lender actually intended, but what return a rational investor could have expected from a comparable asset class; not whether the lender had a concrete transaction ready, but what the benchmark would have produced; not whether the alternative investment was morally licit, but whether the risk-adjusted return was competitive. The abstraction is analytically powerful, but morally incomplete.

A non-extractive finance framework must therefore distinguish between portfolio prudence and borrower chargeability. Portfolio prudence asks: how should entrusted capital be allocated across possible uses? What risks should be taken? What liquidity should be preserved? What return is needed to sustain the institution? What future beneficiaries must be considered? These are legitimate questions. But borrower chargeability asks something different: may this specific borrower be required to compensate the capital provider for a specific foregone gain? The answer requires more than a benchmark.

The first condition is a concrete alternative deployment. The capital provider must be able to identify a real alternative use of the funds. Not merely "the market," not merely "a diversified portfolio," not merely "our normal hurdle," but a specific kind of deployment that was actually available or sufficiently determinate. A merchant who refrains from a known trade to make a loan presents one kind of case. A fund that says it could always have invested in public equities, private credit, or venture capital presents a much more abstract claim. The more abstract the alternative, the weaker the title.

The second condition is mission alignment. For non-extractive capital, the relevant alternative cannot be merely profitable. It must be compatible with the purpose for which the capital is held. A Catholic foundation, mission fund, steward-owned vehicle, or digital sovereignty fund cannot treat every profitable opportunity as morally equivalent. If the alternative return would have come from extractive lending, predatory platform economics, speculative trading, worker exploitation, ecological damage, surveillance, or mission-destroying exits, then that foregone return is not a loss that justice must restore. One cannot claim compensation for refusing an unjust profit.

This matters because many modern benchmarks are morally mixed. A market index, private equity benchmark, venture return target, or credit spread may include gains produced by structures that a non-extractive fund explicitly rejects. To import that benchmark uncritically is to let the old economy set the moral price of the new one. The fund may need to know the benchmark for comparison, but it should not automatically treat the benchmark as a title against the borrower. The market may reveal opportunity, but it does not canonize it.

The third condition is real intention to deploy. Lucrum cessans is stronger when the capital provider actually intended to use the money in the alternative way. It is weaker when the alternative is invented afterward to justify a charge. In modern portfolio terms, this means that the fund should be able to show its allocation policy, pipeline, mandate, or investment committee record. Was this capital genuinely scheduled for another deployment? Was there an approved alternative? Was the fund forced to decline or delay it because of this investment? Or is the opportunity cost merely theoretical?

The fourth condition is probability and valuation. A foregone gain must be reasonably certain enough to value. This does not require mathematical certainty, but it does require disciplined modesty. A projected venture return, speculative upside, or best-case scenario cannot be treated as a possessed good. Expected return must be adjusted for probability, risk, time, costs, and moral uncertainty. The valuation should be conservative rather than maximal. If the supposed foregone gain is highly uncertain, the compensation claim should be weak or absent.

This is where modern expected-value reasoning must be corrected by moral humility. A spreadsheet can assign probabilities to future returns, but probability is not possession. A 20 percent expected return does not mean the investor already owns that 20 percent in moral terms. It means that, under assumptions, a distribution of outcomes has a certain average. The borrower should not be made to pay as though the expected value were a certain loss. Lucrum cessans requires an interrupted good, not merely a modeled possibility.

The fifth condition is causality. The loan or investment must truly cause the gain to cease. If the capital provider would not actually have made the alternative investment, or if the alternative was unavailable, or if the capital was idle for other reasons, then the borrower did not cause the foregone gain. Modern funds often hold reserves, dry powder, liquidity buffers, or unallocated capital. Being uninvested may be part of the fund's own strategy. A borrower should not be charged for a gain that would not in fact have occurred.

The sixth condition is proportionality. Even when a foregone gain is real, compensation must not exceed it. The claim must be limited to the value of the gain actually foregone, adjusted for uncertainty and costs. It must not become a general return target, be compounded automatically, or be stacked on top of risk premiums, management fees, administrative charges, and penalties in a way that over-recovers the same sacrifice. A legitimate lucrum cessans claim remains narrow.
