The seventh condition is no upfront deduction. Historically, one of the concerns with lost-profit compensation was that future gain should not be treated as already certain at the moment of lending. In modern terms, this cautions against deducting opportunity-cost charges upfront or embedding them automatically into principal. If compensation is justified at all, it should be tied to actual unfolding circumstances and paid in a way that does not burden the borrower before the enterprise has generated capacity. For non-extractive finance, this points toward surplus-based repayment rather than time-based yield.

Portfolio theory also introduces the concept of the hurdle rate. A fund may say it cannot make an investment unless expected return exceeds a threshold. This may be institutionally necessary. A fund with obligations to its own investors, future beneficiaries, or mission pipeline cannot deploy capital recklessly. But a hurdle rate is an internal governance tool, not automatically an extrinsic title. It may determine whether the fund invests. It does not automatically determine what the borrower morally owes.

The same applies to the cost of capital. A fund may receive capital from LPs who expect a certain return. It may have debt obligations, operating expenses, inflation exposure, or regulatory capital needs. These realities affect institutional sustainability. But they do not automatically become the borrower's moral burden. If the fund has promised extractive returns to its own capital providers, it cannot simply pass those promises down the chain and call them lucrum cessans. The moral defect may lie upstream in the fund structure itself.

This is crucial for non-extractive capital design. If the fund's own investors require high, fixed, or priority returns, then the fund will be pressured to impose those expectations on portfolio companies. The fund may speak of mission, but its capital stack will push it toward extraction. A non-extractive fund must therefore discipline opportunity cost at the LP level as well as the portfolio level. Patient capital requires patient capital providers. Otherwise, the fund becomes a conduit through which conventional return expectations enter mission enterprises.

Mission-aligned alternatives offer a better benchmark. Instead of asking, "What could this capital have earned in the market?" the fund can ask, "What other mission-aligned use could this capital have served?" A grant to an open-source project, a recoverable grant to a cooperative, a capped-return investment in a steward-owned company, a guarantee for a public-interest technology firm, or a reserve for future crisis response may all be relevant alternatives. The opportunity cost is then measured within the universe of licit and mission-consistent uses, not against the highest available yield in the broader market.

Even here, caution is required. The fact that another mission-aligned investment might have earned more does not automatically create a charge. The alternative must still be concrete, intended, probable, and causally foregone. But the moral frame changes. The question becomes one of stewardship among goods, not maximization across all available returns. This is closer to the older discipline of lucrum cessans: not every possible profit, but a particular good foregone.

Generic market return is therefore insufficient as a moral title. It may be useful for reporting, may help investors understand the financial sacrifice of non-extractive capital, and may help a fund communicate concessionary return relative to conventional markets. But it should not be treated as an automatic claim against the borrower. A borrower should not have to pay more simply because capital could have participated in a morally questionable market elsewhere. The market can be a comparison, but not a confessor.

This distinction also helps clarify below-market finance. A foundation may make a loan at 2 percent when the market would charge 8 percent. Modern finance describes the 6 percent difference as concessionary. That may be useful language. But morally, the foundation has not necessarily "lost" 6 percent in the sense relevant to lucrum cessans. It may have chosen a different good: mission, mercy, resilience, digital sovereignty, community ownership, or non-extractive enterprise. The difference from market return is not automatically damage. It may be the cost of faithfulness.

This has theological significance. Christian stewardship may require refusing certain returns. It may require accepting slower growth, lower yield, longer time horizons, and less liquidity. These are not always losses to be recovered. Sometimes they are the shape of love. If every act of patience is priced back to the borrower, then patience is emptied of gift. Non-extractive finance must be sustainable, but it must also preserve space for generosity.

At the same time, generosity should not be confused with poor management. A fund that ignores opportunity cost entirely may waste capital, fail future beneficiaries, or collapse institutionally. The right discipline is not to abolish opportunity-cost thinking, but to locate it properly. It belongs first in internal discernment: should we make this investment? Can we afford this level of patience? What future mission uses are we giving up? How do we preserve the fund's capacity to serve? Only in narrower, well-defined cases does it become a charge to the borrower.

A practical non-extractive portfolio framework might therefore classify opportunity cost in three ways. First, internal-only opportunity cost: relevant for fund allocation, not charged to the borrower. Second, mission-aligned foregone deployment: potentially relevant where a concrete alternative was actually displaced. Third, generic market opportunity cost: useful for comparison, but not a valid title for borrower payment unless translated into a concrete and licit foregone gain. This classification prevents opportunity cost from becoming an all-purpose justification for yield.

Such a framework would also prevent double-counting. If a fund charges a risk-based return because capital is loss-absorbing, it should not also charge a full opportunity-cost premium as if capital had been safely deployed elsewhere. If management fees cover the institution's operating needs, opportunity cost should not be inflated to cover them again. If the investment is repaid from surplus with a cap, the cap should include all relevant return claims rather than allowing additional opportunity-cost charges to accumulate outside it. The moral accounting must be integrated.

In portfolio reporting, the fund can still disclose financial sacrifice. It may say: compared with conventional benchmarks, this capital accepted lower liquidity, lower expected return, and capped upside in order to serve mission. But this should be framed as stewardship, not as a debt owed by the borrower. The fund's investors may choose to accept that sacrifice. They may view it as part of their vocation, philanthropy, faith, or mission. They may still receive modest return. But they should not expect every foregone market gain to be pushed onto the enterprises they claim to serve.

For the NEC simulator or similar tools, this means opportunity cost should be a visible assumption, not a hidden default. The model should allow users to distinguish generic market benchmark, mission-aligned alternative deployment, and concrete foregone gain. It should ask whether the alternative is licit, intended, and causally displaced, show when opportunity-cost assumptions drive extraction scores upward, and prevent the user from treating every missed market return as morally equivalent to actual loss.

This is where lucrum cessans becomes useful not because it validates modern opportunity cost, but because it disciplines it. It teaches that foregone gain may matter, but only under conditions, and it refuses to let capital claim all possible futures as already its own. It forces portfolio theory to become morally particular: this capital, this alternative, this intention, this probability, this borrower, this mission, this lost good.

The final question is therefore not, "What could the money have earned?" It is, "What good, if any, was truly foregone by making this financing available, and is it just to ask this borrower to compensate it?" Sometimes the answer will be yes, at least in part; often it will be no, or not directly. The foregone return may turn out to be a gift, or an internal stewardship cost, or a sign that the fund's own capital structure is too demanding, or the basis for a modest, capped, surplus-based return.

Lucrum cessans in modern portfolio theory therefore stands at a narrow and dangerous bridge. On one side is prudent stewardship: recognizing that capital has alternative uses and must be allocated responsibly. On the other side is usury: treating every imagined alternative return as a claim against the borrower. Non-extractive finance must walk the bridge carefully. It must think like a steward without charging like an extractor, and know the opportunity cost without worshipping it. It must remember that not every profit foregone is a loss, and not every market yield refused deserves to be resurrected in the borrower's repayment schedule.

sections.title: 40.4 Periculum Sortis in Investment Design
paragraphs: In investment design, periculum sortis asks whether capital is genuinely at risk. The phrase means "risk of the principal," and it is one of the most important concepts for translating the scholastic tradition into modern non-extractive finance. It does not ask merely whether an investment feels uncertain, whether a spreadsheet contains downside scenarios, or whether a fund manager describes the asset class as risky. It asks whether the capital provider can truly lose principal, and whether that loss is borne by capital rather than shifted onto the borrower, founder, worker, community, or mission.

This question is central because modern finance often justifies return through risk. Investors are compensated for risk. Interest rates include risk premiums. Venture capital seeks high returns because startups are risky. Private credit prices default risk. Preferred equity protects against downside. Revenue-based financing claims to align repayment with business performance. Yet the moral question is not simply whether risk exists. Risk always exists somewhere. The question is who bears it.

In a non-extractive investment, principal must be genuinely at risk. This does not necessarily mean that capital should be reckless, unsecured in every circumstance, or indifferent to stewardship. But it does mean that the investor may not claim partnership-like surplus while preserving creditor-like certainty. If repayment is guaranteed, fully collateralized, senior, insured, personally guaranteed, or enforceable regardless of outcome, then the appeal to periculum sortis becomes weak. Capital may face inconvenience, delay, or some residual default risk, but it is not standing fully inside the uncertainty of the enterprise.

Loss absorption is therefore the key design principle. Capital that invokes risk should be capable of absorbing loss when the enterprise cannot pay without harm. Repayment should pause, reduce, subordinate, convert, or disappear under defined conditions. The investor's return should depend on genuine surplus, not on extracting cash from a fragile company. If the company fails honestly, capital should be able to lose. Without this possibility, risk compensation becomes morally unstable.

Subordination is one practical way to embody loss absorption. Subordinated capital stands behind more urgent claims. It waits until workers are paid, taxes are met, suppliers are treated justly, operating needs are covered, and resilience reserves are built. It does not insist on being first in line. This does not automatically make the structure just, but it makes the investor's risk claim more truthful. Capital that waits behind life-serving obligations bears a different moral burden from capital that demands priority repayment before the enterprise is secure.

The absence of guarantee is another crucial feature. A guarantee shifts risk away from the investor and onto another party. A personal guarantee shifts business risk into the founder's household and future life. A parent-company guarantee shifts risk to another balance sheet. A public guarantee may shift risk to taxpayers. A foundation guarantee may shift risk to philanthropic capital. Guarantees may sometimes be useful, especially where they unlock affordable capital or protect vulnerable parties, but they must be honestly named. A guaranteed investor should not charge as though unprotected.

Collateral must be treated with similar care. A security interest in assets may reduce the lender's risk, but it does so by giving the lender power over the borrower's productive life. If the collateral consists of equipment, receivables, code, intellectual property, shares, land, or other essential assets, enforcement may destroy the enterprise's capacity to recover. Collateral can therefore be morally disproportionate, even when legally ordinary. A non-extractive structure should avoid collateral that allows capital to survive by stripping the mission of its means of life.

Risk proportionate to return is the next principle. Even where capital is genuinely exposed, the return must correspond to the degree of exposure. A small subordinated risk may justify modest surplus. A deeply loss-absorbing, patient, mission-locked investment may justify a larger but still capped return. A senior collateralized claim justifies far less. The title of periculum sortis does not say, "risk exists, therefore return is unlimited." It says that real exposure may be compensated in proportion to the vulnerability accepted.

This is where caps become morally important. A capped return acknowledges that risk-bearing does not give capital an infinite claim over the future. It allows the investor to be compensated, perhaps generously enough to sustain the fund, but prevents the investment from becoming open-ended extraction. Once the agreed return has been reached, surplus can remain with the enterprise, workers, mission, community, or future reinvestment. A cap is not sufficient by itself, but it is a powerful sign that capital has accepted limits.

Upside must be paired with downside. This principle is the heart of partnership. If capital shares in success, it must share in failure; if it participates in surplus, it must absorb loss; if it has governance influence, it must be accountable to the mission; and if it seeks return from the company's flourishing, it must not enforce claims that destroy the company during hardship. Upside without downside is extraction. Downside without upside may be charity or subsidy. Shared upside and shared downside are the moral territory of societas.

Repayment should therefore be tied to true surplus wherever possible. Gross revenue is not enough. A company can have revenue while losing money, underpaying workers, delaying maintenance, neglecting security, or failing to build reserves. Repayment from gross revenue can drain an enterprise before it is healthy. Free cash flow is better, but still requires definition. A non-extractive contract should specify that repayment begins only after operating costs, fair wages, taxes, essential reinvestment, and resilience reserves have been met. Capital should receive from fruit, not from the cutting of roots.

Resilience reserves are one of the clearest modern translations of moral priority. Before investors are repaid, the company should have enough buffer to survive ordinary shocks. This is especially important for mission-driven enterprises, open-source infrastructure, cybersecurity, care work, ecological transition, and other fields where continuity matters. A company that pays investors before building resilience may satisfy capital while remaining fragile. A non-extractive waterfall should protect resilience before return.

Hardship provisions also embody shared downside. If the company faces illness, disaster, customer nonpayment, market disruption, war, regulatory delay, or other serious hardship, repayment should not automatically become more punitive. Instead, the contract should allow suspension, extension, restructuring, waiver, or conversion. This does not mean that obligations disappear casually. It means that the contract recognizes the difference between bad faith and misfortune. Periculum sortis becomes real when capital accepts that not all negative outcomes can be pushed back onto the borrower.

No guarantee and no collateral are not always possible, but any protection given to capital should reduce the permitted risk-based return. This is a basic moral accounting rule. If the investor is protected by collateral, the risk premium should be lower. If the claim is senior, the risk premium should be lower again. If a guarantee exists, the risk premium should be lower still. And if information rights and covenants reduce uncertainty, that too should be considered. Capital should not be paid as if exposed while documented as protected.

This principle prevents double-counting. Modern instruments often compensate risk multiple times: through interest, default interest, warrants, liquidation preference, management fees, collateral, covenants, and priority. A non-extractive design should identify all risk protections and all risk compensations in one place. The contract should be able to say: here is the risk capital actually bears; here is how the return corresponds to that risk; here are the protections that reduce risk; here is why the total claim remains proportionate. Without this integrated view, risk language becomes a fog.

Investment design must also distinguish between portfolio risk and enterprise risk. A fund may diversify across many investments. A portfolio company cannot diversify across its one life. The fund may survive a failure. The company may not. This asymmetry matters. If the fund prices risk from its own portfolio perspective while enforcing repayment against the company's singular path, the structure may be non-ergodic in moral terms. What is manageable for the ensemble may be ruinous for the individual. Non-extractive finance must not treat the borrower's ruin as merely one data point in capital's distribution.

This is where ergodicity connects with periculum sortis. Averages can deceive. A portfolio may show acceptable expected returns even if several companies are destroyed by downside paths. The investor may say that risk is fairly priced because the expected value is positive. But the moral question includes the time path of each enterprise. Does the structure allow companies to survive bad sequences, or does it extract fixed payments until one shock becomes fatal? A risk-sharing investment should be designed to reduce ruin risk, not merely price it.

Loss absorption can be designed in several ways. Return can be paid only from distributable surplus, and repayment can pause when cash flow falls below a threshold. Principal can be written down after defined mission-preserving triggers, or debt can convert into non-controlling, capped, loss-absorbing equity. Repayment terms can extend automatically without penalty under hardship. Investors can stand behind workers and essential suppliers, reserves can be filled before distributions, and exit rights can be limited. Each of these mechanisms makes risk-bearing more real.

A non-extractive investment should also limit control rights that protect capital at the expense of mission. Some governance rights are appropriate: information, consent over major changes, protection against fraud, and safeguards against mission betrayal. But control rights become extractive when they allow investors to force sale, block mission-preserving decisions, demand growth beyond capacity, replace leadership for liquidity reasons, or redirect the enterprise toward investor return. If capital claims return for risk while also controlling the response to risk in its own favor, then risk-sharing is compromised.

Mission locks and steward ownership can help here. They can ensure that capital does not gain absolute control simply because it provides funds, separate economic rights from purpose governance, and prevent forced exits, asset stripping, or mission drift. They can protect the company's vocation through golden shares, foundation ownership, steward boards, asset locks, or capped investor instruments. These structures make it easier for capital to participate without dominating.

However, mission locks must be integrated with financial design. A company may have steward ownership but still accept capital with rigid repayment claims. A foundation may hold voting control but still owe investors priority distributions. A mission lock may prevent sale but not prevent cash extraction. Therefore the audit must examine the whole structure: governance, repayment, downside, caps, collateral, guarantees, and reserves. Periculum sortis lives in the combined architecture, not in one clause.

The timing of return also matters. Early repayment may reduce investor risk before the enterprise has stabilized. This can make the structure look safer for capital while leaving the company under-resourced. A better design may delay investor repayment until the company has achieved defined resilience: positive operating cash flow, adequate reserves, fair wage levels, tax compliance, maintenance budgets, and mission-critical reinvestment. The investor waits not arbitrarily, but because the enterprise's life comes before capital's return.

A non-extractive investment can also use capped surplus-sharing. Instead of fixed interest, the investor receives a percentage of distributable surplus until a capped multiple is reached. If surplus is low, repayment is low or zero. If surplus is high, repayment accelerates, but stops at the cap. This structure ties return to actual flourishing. It is not automatically perfect, because definitions of surplus and priority still matter. But it moves closer to societas than a fixed claim does.

Revenue-based financing can be adapted in this direction, but only carefully. Revenue-sharing is often presented as founder-friendly because payments fluctuate with revenue. Yet revenue is not profit. A business may owe payments while still unable to pay wages properly or invest in survival. A non-extractive revenue share should therefore include profitability thresholds, reserve requirements, hardship pauses, total caps, and mission protections. Otherwise it may simply become a smoother-looking form of senior debt.

Equity can also be designed non-extractively. Investors may receive non-voting or limited-voting shares, capped redemption rights, dividends only from surplus, no forced exit, no participating liquidation preferences, and no mission override. If the company fails, capital can be lost. If it succeeds, investors receive a fair return and then step back economically. Such equity more closely resembles partnership because it shares fate without claiming dominion.

Convertible instruments require particular caution. A convertible note may begin as debt and later become equity. During the debt phase, it may accrue interest, have a maturity date, and require repayment. During conversion, it may receive discounts or valuation caps. After conversion, it may become preferred equity. The moral question is whether the instrument accumulates protections at each stage. If it receives debt protection, conversion upside, preference, and control, it may overcompensate capital. A non-extractive convertible should be simple, capped, transparent, and designed to move toward genuine loss-sharing rather than layered advantage.

A practical periculum sortis checklist for investment design would ask: Can principal actually be lost? Is repayment guaranteed? Is the claim senior or subordinated? Is there collateral? Is there a personal guarantee? Does repayment come from revenue, profit, free cash flow, or distributable surplus? Are wages, taxes, suppliers, reserves, and mission commitments protected first? Is return capped? Can the investor force exit? Can default make the investor better off? Is risk already compensated elsewhere? What happens in the downside scenario?

The answer should be visible not only in legal drafting but also in accounting and reporting. The dashboard should show whether payments to capital came from true surplus or from fragile cash flow, whether reserves were full before repayment, and whether any hardship provisions were triggered. It should show principal at risk, write-downs, deferred payments, and losses accepted by investors. A fund that claims non-extractive risk-sharing should be able to prove it.

There is a spiritual discipline here. Capital often wants to be called brave while remaining safe. It wants the dignity of risk without the wound of loss. Periculum sortis refuses this self-deception. It says that risk is not a costume. To bear risk is to accept the possibility of not being repaid; to share upside is to share downside; to accompany an enterprise is to stand with it under uncertainty, not merely above it with documents.

This does not mean investors should be careless. Stewardship requires diligence, diversification, prudence, and governance. Capital entrusted for mission should not be wasted. But prudence must not be confused with insulation from every loss. If capital is structured so that everyone else can be wounded while it remains whole, then the return it receives is not truly compensation for periculum sortis. It is payment extracted from risk borne by others.

In the end, periculum sortis in investment design asks capital to become honest about vulnerability. If capital wants return for risk, it must bear risk in the legal documents, the repayment waterfall, the governance structure, the downside scenario, and the accounting. It must accept loss absorption, subordination where appropriate, no guarantee, no excessive collateral, proportional return, and upside paired with downside. Only then can risk premium become compensation rather than extraction.

A non-extractive investment is therefore not defined merely by good intentions or mission language. It is defined by where the pain goes when things go wrong. If capital shares the pain, it may share the fruit. If capital shifts the pain, its claim to the fruit becomes suspect. That is the enduring moral force of periculum sortis.

sections.title: 40.5 Stipendium Laboris and Operational Sustainability
paragraphs: Not every legitimate payment above principal concerns delay, damage, foregone profit, or risk. Some payments compensate labor. The older phrase stipendium laboris names this reality: a stipend or payment for work performed. In modern non-extractive finance, this category is essential because moral capital still requires human labor. Someone must find enterprises, evaluate them, structure agreements, perform due diligence, manage accounts, support founders, monitor mission, prepare reports, handle governance, mediate problems, and preserve the integrity of the institution. If this work is real, necessary, and proportionate, it may justly be compensated.

This point prevents a false opposition between morality and sustainability. A fund, cooperative lender, recoverable-grant vehicle, steward-ownership transition office, or mission-locked investment institution cannot serve anyone for long if it cannot pay for the work required to operate. The Monti di Pietà faced this problem centuries ago. Lending to the poor required buildings, pledge storage, records, staff, accounting, supervision, and administration. If no charge could ever be made for these costs and labors, the institution would collapse or become dependent on endless subsidy. If charges became profit-seeking, the institution would betray its purpose. The moral path lay between these extremes: cost and labor could be covered, but not converted into extraction.

Modern funds face the same tension. Due diligence takes time. Legal structuring costs money. Accounting systems must be maintained. Portfolio companies may need mentoring, governance support, conflict mediation, financial review, and mission-protection work. A non-extractive fund may require more work than a conventional fund, not less, because it refuses simple extractive defaults. It may need to design custom instruments, monitor surplus definitions, review hardship cases, audit payments by moral title, and ensure that governance remains faithful to mission. This labor is not incidental. It is part of the stewardship.

Management fees are therefore not automatically extractive. A modest, transparent fee that pays for real fund administration can be morally legitimate. The question is whether the fee corresponds to actual labor and necessary institutional capacity. If a fund manager spends time sourcing aligned enterprises, supporting founders, preparing reports, maintaining accounts, and protecting mission, compensation may be just. The worker is worthy of wages. Moral finance should not depend on invisible unpaid labor, burnout, or romanticized self-sacrifice.

But management fees are also dangerous. They can detach from work performed and become an automatic claim on capital under management. They can reward asset accumulation rather than mission service, make the institution more interested in growing the fund than serving enterprises, and be charged regardless of whether stewardship is good, useful, or proportionate. Once this happens, stipendium laboris becomes institutional rent. The fee is no longer primarily payment for labor. It becomes payment for occupying a financial position.

This danger is especially acute when fees are calculated as a percentage of committed or invested capital. Percentage-based fees may be administratively convenient, but labor does not always scale linearly with capital. A larger fund may require more work, but not necessarily in exact proportion to assets under management. A €10 million fund charging 2 percent receives €200,000 per year. A €100 million fund charging the same percentage receives €2 million per year. The work may be greater, but is it ten times greater? A non-extractive fund must ask whether its fee formula reflects actual labor or merely imitates market convention.

Fund administration is another legitimate but limited category. Bookkeeping, audits, tax filings, regulatory compliance, bank administration, payment processing, investor reporting, portfolio tracking, document storage, and governance logistics all create costs. These may be covered. But they should be distinguished from return on capital, risk premium, damages, and penalties. Administrative cost recovery should not be hidden in interest rates or repayment multiples if it can be named directly. Moral clarity improves when each payment says what it is for.

Due diligence likewise requires care. It is real labor: reviewing business models, governance structures, ownership forms, financial statements, mission alignment, legal documents, technology, cybersecurity, market context, team capacity, and downside scenarios. In a non-extractive fund, due diligence may also include theological, ethical, or mission analysis. This work protects both capital and enterprise. It can prevent harmful investments, badly designed repayment structures, mission drift, and avoidable failure.

But due diligence costs must be allocated justly. If a fund charges large due-diligence fees to applicants regardless of whether they receive capital, it may burden precisely the enterprises it claims to support. If fees are imposed upfront on fragile organizations, the process can become exclusionary. If due diligence primarily protects investors rather than serving the enterprise, it is not obvious that the portfolio company should bear the full cost. A non-extractive framework should ask who benefits from the labor and allocate the cost accordingly.

Accounting is not merely technical. In moral finance, accounting becomes a safeguard against drift. Someone must classify payments: principal, reimbursement, labor, expense, penalty, risk return, surplus share, donation, reserve allocation, owner distribution, or item needing review. Someone must ensure that the same burden is not counted twice, verify that investor repayment begins only after agreed thresholds are met, and check whether fees correspond to actual work. This is stewardship labor. It is worthy of compensation because it protects the truthfulness of the structure.

Legal work is also often necessary. Non-extractive contracts may require more careful drafting than standard templates. They must define surplus, caps, mission locks, hardship provisions, repayment waterfalls, governance rights, stewardship obligations, investor limits, and anti-abuse mechanisms. They may involve foundations, cooperatives, steward-owned companies, golden shares, asset locks, or capped-return instruments. Lawyers must be paid. But legal costs must remain proportionate and mission-serving. If legal complexity becomes so expensive that only well-funded enterprises can access non-extractive structures, the form begins to undermine its own purpose.

Stewardship work deserves particular attention. A conventional investor may monitor performance mainly to protect return. A steward investor must do more. It must ask whether the company remains faithful to its mission, whether workers are treated justly, whether governance remains healthy, whether repayment burdens are sustainable, whether investor pressure is distorting purpose, and whether the enterprise is growing in a way compatible with life. This work is relational, qualitative, and often invisible. It may involve accompaniment rather than control. It may require patience, listening, and discernment. Such labor should not be treated as costless.

Yet stewardship fees can also become performative. A fund may charge for stewardship while providing little more than occasional calls, generic advice, or investor reporting. It may use the language of accompaniment while functioning like ordinary portfolio management. It may charge companies for support they did not request or do not need. Therefore stewardship labor must be accountable. What work was promised? What work was performed? Did it serve the enterprise? Was it useful? Was it proportionate? Was it welcomed? Did it protect mission, or merely monitor capital?

Operational sustainability is the broader category that gathers all these concerns. A non-extractive institution must endure if it is to serve across time. It cannot depend entirely on heroic founders, unpaid volunteers, emergency grants, or hidden subsidies. It needs a sustainable operating model. But sustainability must be defined carefully. The institution exists to serve the mission, not the mission to serve the institution. The fund must be sustainable enough to continue its work, but not so self-protective that it extracts from the enterprises it was created to nourish.

This is one of the recurring dangers of moral institutions. They begin with service, and service develops administrative needs. Those needs become budgets, budgets become fixed expectations, expectations become claims, claims become pressure, and the pressure is passed down to the vulnerable. At the end of this chain, the institution still speaks the language of mission, but its practical energy has shifted toward its own preservation. The old concern about the Monti di Pietà remains relevant: cost recovery can drift into self-serving finance unless disciplined by governance, transparency, and limits.

A non-extractive fund should therefore distinguish between several kinds of operational payment. First, direct reimbursement for specific expenses. Second, labor compensation for specific work performed. Third, general management fees for maintaining institutional capacity. Fourth, stewardship fees for ongoing mission support. Fifth, success-based or surplus-based compensation, where appropriate, for value actually created. Each category should be named separately. The more clearly payments are classified, the less likely they are to become disguised yield.

The source of these payments also matters. Should they be paid by portfolio companies, by investors, by philanthropic subsidy, by the fund itself, by a shared pool, or from surplus after resilience? The answer may differ by category. Investor reporting arguably benefits investors and should not automatically be charged to companies. Mission stewardship may benefit both the fund and enterprise. Legal structuring may benefit the specific transaction. General fundraising costs may belong to the fund manager. A moral accounting system should allocate costs according to benefit, causality, and capacity.

Timing matters as well. Upfront fees can burden fragile enterprises before they have received any benefit. Recurring fees can drain cash flow during difficult periods. Success-based fees can align payment with capacity, but may also create incentives to push for growth or exit. Fees paid from distributable surplus after reserves may be more compatible with non-extractive logic. A fee is not only an amount. It is a claim on time, cash flow, and priority.

Caps and review mechanisms help preserve proportionality. Management fees can be capped relative to budgeted work. Direct expenses can require approval above thresholds. Legal costs can require estimates and borrower notice. Stewardship fees can be reviewed annually. Related-party payments can be disclosed. Success fees can be limited or avoided. Total cost recovery from portfolio companies can be capped so that the fund does not shift its entire institutional burden onto them unnoticed. These mechanisms keep stipendium laboris tied to labor.

Transparency is essential. A borrower should know what fees exist, what they pay for, when they are charged, whether they are optional, whether they are capped, and whether they duplicate other charges. Investors should know how much of their capital goes to enterprises and how much to fund operations. Mission stewards should know whether the institution's cost structure pressures portfolio companies. Transparency does not eliminate every moral problem, but secrecy almost guarantees drift.

A non-extractive fee policy might include several rules. Fees must correspond to real labor or necessary expense, be proportionate, and be disclosed. They must not duplicate other charges, must not be calculated primarily to reach a target return, and must not be imposed in ways that endanger wages, reserves, or mission. They must be reviewable, they must distinguish investor-serving work from enterprise-serving work, and they must be reducible or waivable under hardship.

This has implications for the design of the NEC simulator or any moral-finance dashboard. Operational costs should be visible, not hidden inside return assumptions. Users should be able to see how management fees, stewardship costs, legal costs, accounting costs, and administration affect both fund sustainability and enterprise burden. The model should distinguish cost recovery from yield, show when fees are paid from capital, revenue, free cash flow, or surplus, and flag double-counting and related-party payments. It should ask whether operational sustainability is being achieved by serving the mission or by burdening it.

Theologically, stipendium laboris honors work. This is important. A moral economy should not pretend that the labor of stewardship is worthless. It should not rely on hidden female labor, unpaid religious labor, founder self-exploitation, or activist burnout. Justice includes paying people for real work. In this sense, operational sustainability is part of moral finance, not a concession to it. Institutions that cannot pay for necessary labor may become fragile, chaotic, or dependent on charismatic sacrifice.

But the same theology also disciplines institutional desire. Work deserves wages, but not every institutional desire is work; stewardship deserves support, but not every managerial activity is stewardship; administration deserves reimbursement, but not every bureaucratic expansion serves the poor, the borrower, or the mission. The fact that an institution has costs does not mean every cost should be passed on. The fact that a manager works hard does not mean every fee is proportionate. Labor must be real, useful, necessary, and ordered toward service.

This distinction becomes especially important when finance borrows the language of vocation. A fund may say it is accompanying entrepreneurs, stewarding mission, serving digital sovereignty, or building a moral economy. These may be true. But vocation is not a blank check. Precisely because the language is sacred, the accounting must be more honest. A ministry can exploit, a mission can overcharge, and a steward can become a rentier. The antidote is not cynicism, but disciplined transparency.

The Monti di Pietà again offer the warning and the hope. They showed that institutions can charge small amounts for labor and expense without becoming usurious, if those charges are necessary for service and constrained by mission. They also remind us that institutions drift: what begins as sustainability can become self-protection, what begins as cost recovery can become profit, and what begins as service to the poor can become service to the institution. Modern non-extractive finance must build countermeasures from the start.

In the end, stipendium laboris and operational sustainability teach that capital serving life must also support the labor required to serve life. The question is not whether fund managers, administrators, accountants, lawyers, and stewards may be paid. They may. The question is whether their payment remains truthful: tied to real work, proportionate to the burden, transparent in the accounts, and subordinate to the mission. When this discipline holds, operational payments sustain non-extractive finance. When it fails, they become extraction under the name of service.

sections.title: 40.6 Carentia Pecuniae and Lock-Up
paragraphs: Carentia pecuniae is one of the most dangerous adjacent titles because it stands so close to the modern idea of the time value of money. The phrase means lack, absence, or deprivation of money. It asks whether a lender may be compensated simply because the lender is without the money for a period of time. In modern finance, this appears under the language of lock-up, liquidity premium, commitment period, duration, illiquidity, and time-based return. The moral danger is obvious: if being without money is itself enough to justify surplus, then almost every loan becomes interest-bearing by nature.

This is why carentia pecuniae must be handled with caution. It is related to lucrum cessans, but it is not identical. Lucrum cessans concerns a concrete profit foregone. The lender would have used the money in a specific, licit, intended, and reasonably certain way, and the loan prevented that gain. Carentia pecuniae is more abstract. It concerns the lender's deprivation of money as such. The lender cannot use the money because it has been committed to another. In modern terms, the lender has lost liquidity and optionality.

This can be real. To commit capital for a term is not nothing. A fund that locks money into a five-year instrument cannot use that money elsewhere. A foundation that provides patient capital may have less liquidity for emergencies. An investor who accepts a long repayment horizon loses flexibility. A lender whose funds are tied up may be unable to respond to new opportunities or obligations. Time, illiquidity, and commitment do create burdens. The question is whether those burdens are concrete enough to charge to the borrower, or whether they simply become a new vocabulary for ordinary interest.

The older suspicion is important because carentia pecuniae can dissolve the whole doctrine. If every lender may say, "I lacked my money during the term of the loan," then every lender has an extrinsic title. The title is no longer extrinsic in any meaningful sense. It becomes intrinsic to lending. Every loan involves the lender being without the money. Therefore, if absence alone is compensable, the prohibition on profit from the loan itself has effectively vanished.

Modern liquidity premiums face the same danger. A liquidity premium compensates an investor for holding an illiquid asset rather than a liquid one. In financial markets, this may be a standard valuation concept. Illiquid investments are less flexible and may require higher expected return. As a prudential tool, this is understandable. But as a moral title charged to the borrower, it requires discipline. The borrower should not automatically owe surplus merely because the investor chose an illiquid form. Otherwise, every patient commitment becomes a claim for yield.

The same is true of lock-up. In investment funds, a lock-up period prevents investors from withdrawing capital for a defined time. This can be necessary. Long-term mission finance cannot function if investors can demand liquidity at every moment. Patient capital requires patient funding. But if the fund then charges portfolio companies simply because its own investors are locked up, the moral question shifts upstream. Who created the liquidity expectation? Who benefits from the structure? Why should the enterprise bear the cost of the fund's promise to its capital providers?

A commitment period can also be morally ambiguous. Investors may commit capital that is drawn down over time. The fund may charge fees on committed capital, invested capital, or undrawn commitments. The investor may claim that the capital must remain available and therefore cannot be used elsewhere. This is a real institutional constraint. But again, the question is allocation. Is the burden caused by a specific borrower? Or is it part of the fund's general operating model? A specific enterprise should not automatically bear the cost of capital being reserved for the fund's broader strategy.

The time value of money is the broadest modern expression of the problem. Finance commonly assumes that money today is worth more than money tomorrow. This assumption underlies discounting, valuation, interest rates, net present value, bond pricing, and investment decisions. As a descriptive or prudential tool, it can be useful. Future payments are uncertain. Liquidity matters. Inflation may erode purchasing power. Delay may create real burdens. But as a moral claim, the time value of money must not be absolutized. Time itself does not belong to capital.

This is the theological heart of the matter. If time is treated as capital's private property, then the passage of time becomes a rent-producing asset. The borrower must pay because time has passed while the money was absent from the lender. But the older tradition resists exactly this move. Time is not a thing the lender owns and leases. Money transferred in a mutuum is not an asset whose use remains with the lender. The lender may be compensated for real burdens that occur during time, but not simply for time itself.

This distinction is subtle but decisive. Delay may cause damnum emergens. A concrete alternative use may support lucrum cessans. Genuine exposure over time may support periculum sortis. Administrative work over time may support stipendium laboris. But time alone is not any of these. The fact that a contract lasts twelve months does not automatically create twelve months of moral entitlement. The passage of time must be connected to a real burden, not treated as a burden in itself.

Inflation complicates the analysis. If repayment of nominal principal after a long period leaves the lender with substantially reduced purchasing power, one might argue that the lender has not truly received the equivalent. This is not exactly carentia pecuniae, but it is adjacent. The moral issue becomes whether preserving real value is repayment of the tantundem in economic substance, or whether inflation adjustment becomes an interest-like surplus. A careful non-extractive framework may allow transparent inflation adjustment in some cases, especially for long-term instruments, but should distinguish it from profit. Preserving principal is not the same as extracting yield.

Currency risk raises a similar issue. If capital is advanced in one currency and repaid in another, exchange-rate movements can create real loss. Compensation for such loss may be closer to damnum emergens or exchange-contract logic than to carentia pecuniae. But it must be documented and proportionate. The fact that money was locked up is not enough. The specific loss must be shown.

In modern fund design, carentia pecuniae should therefore be treated primarily as an internal stewardship consideration, not a default borrower charge. A fund may ask: how much illiquidity can we accept? How long can capital be patient? What reserves do we need? What commitments have we made to our own investors? What future mission opportunities might we be unable to support if capital is locked? These are legitimate questions. But they do not automatically become reasons to charge a portfolio company a liquidity premium.

When a liquidity premium is considered, it should satisfy strict tests. Is the lock-up real? Is it caused by this specific investment? Does it impose a concrete burden beyond the general nature of patient capital? Is the burden necessary for the borrower's benefit or primarily for the fund's structure? Is the premium proportionate? Is it already captured in another return component? Does it burden the enterprise before it has generated surplus? Is the premium capped? Is the investor also receiving risk compensation, administrative fees, or opportunity-cost compensation for the same commitment?

This last point is crucial because liquidity, opportunity, and risk are easily double-counted. The investor may charge for being without money, for the opportunity foregone because of being without money, and for the risk borne while being without money. These are related but not identical. Without careful classification, they merge into a single justification for return. The language changes, but the result is ordinary interest: payment because capital was absent from the lender and present with the borrower for a time.

A non-extractive contract should therefore avoid generic lock-up premiums unless they are clearly tied to real burden and paid from surplus. If an investor accepts a long-term commitment for mission reasons, part of that patience may be gift, vocation, or stewardship. It should not automatically be monetized. The investor may receive a capped surplus share for genuine risk-bearing. The fund may recover actual costs. It may adjust for inflation if necessary to preserve principal. But it should resist turning illiquidity itself into an open-ended claim.

There is also a deeper spiritual danger in carentia pecuniae. It can train capital to experience every act of patience as deprivation. The money is not immediately available, so capital feels wronged; the fund cannot redeploy instantly, so it demands compensation; the investor waits, so the borrower owes. This psychology is the opposite of stewardship. Stewardship may require waiting without full compensation. It may require accepting that mission capital is not always maximally liquid, and patience as a form of love.

Yet patience should not be romanticized in a way that destroys institutions. A fund that ignores liquidity entirely may fail its future beneficiaries. A foundation that locks too much capital may be unable to respond to crisis. A mission investor that accepts indefinite illiquidity without planning may become irresponsible. The question is not whether liquidity matters. It does. The question is whether the cost of illiquidity is borne justly, transparently, and proportionately, rather than passed automatically to the borrower.

One practical solution is to hold liquidity reserves at the fund level. Instead of charging each enterprise a liquidity premium, the fund can manage its own liquidity through reserves, staggered maturities, diversified instruments, patient LP agreements, and clear redemption policies. The burden of patience is then structured into the fund honestly. Portfolio companies are not forced to pay for liquidity promises that the fund made elsewhere. This is especially important for non-extractive funds that claim to protect enterprises from conventional capital pressure.

Another solution is to align LP expectations from the beginning. If investors in the fund demand liquidity, the fund will be tempted to demand liquidity from portfolio companies. If investors accept lock-up as part of their mission, the fund can offer patient capital downstream. Non-extractive capital therefore begins upstream. The fund cannot be more patient with borrowers than its own capital providers allow, unless it absorbs the tension internally. A moral capital stack must be coherent from LP to portfolio company.

A third solution is to use surplus-based liquidity. Instead of fixed time-based compensation, capital may receive repayment when the enterprise has true distributable surplus. The investor accepts that liquidity depends on the company's health. This makes the lock-up part of risk-sharing rather than a separate entitlement. If the company flourishes, capital returns. If it struggles, capital waits. The absence of money is not charged as such; it is embedded in shared fate.

A fourth solution is to distinguish principal preservation from return. Inflation adjustment, currency protection, and cost reimbursement may sometimes preserve the real value of what was entrusted. But these should be explicitly classified as preservation or reimbursement, not profit. If a fund wants an additional return, it should name the title: risk, surplus participation, concrete foregone gain, or labor. Clarity prevents carentia pecuniae from smuggling yield into the contract.

In the NEC simulator or similar tools, carentia pecuniae should be visible as a high-risk moral assumption. Users should be able to toggle whether lock-up or liquidity premium is treated as chargeable. Strict Franciscan mode might reject it almost entirely, except where it becomes actual loss or principal preservation. Thomistic moderate mode might allow only concrete burdens tied to delay, loss, or foregone use. Salamanca or late-scholastic mode might explore more permissive interpretations, while clearly flagging the danger of doctrine collapse. Modern finance comparison mode might include ordinary liquidity premiums to show how far they move from the stricter tradition.

This makes carentia pecuniae useful pedagogically. It exposes the hidden assumption behind much of modern finance: that capital must be compensated for not being elsewhere. The simulator could ask: is this liquidity premium tied to actual loss? Is it a generic time charge? Is it already included in opportunity cost? Is it already compensated through risk return? Is it paid before surplus? Does it burden the enterprise's resilience? These questions would show whether the lock-up is being treated as stewardship or entitlement.

For modern contracts, a cautious rule would be: do not charge for lock-up as such unless the burden is concrete, necessary, proportionate, not double-counted, and compatible with the borrower's flourishing. Prefer internal fund-level liquidity management, patient LP capital, repayment from surplus, caps, and transparency. Treat generic liquidity premium as morally suspect, and treat the time value of money as a prudential tool, not a theological truth.

The broader lesson is that not every absence is an injury. The lender's money is absent because the lender chose to deploy it. That deployment may be prudent, generous, risky, or foolish. It may create real burdens. But the mere fact of absence does not automatically make the borrower a debtor for more than principal. If absence causes actual loss, document it. If it prevents concrete gain, examine it under lucrum cessans. If it exposes principal to loss, examine it under periculum sortis. If it requires labor, examine stipendium laboris. Do not let absence itself become a universal title.

In the end, carentia pecuniae marks the edge of modern interest. It is the point where compensation shades into payment for time, and payment for time settles into the assumption that money naturally deserves growth. A non-extractive moral economy must resist that slide. It must acknowledge liquidity without worshipping it, plan for lock-up without charging every burden downstream, and honor patience as a real form of stewardship. Capital may sometimes be compensated for what it truly bears. But it may not claim the borrower's future simply because it was willing to wait.

chapters.part: Part XII — Toward Non-Extractive Finance

chapters.title: The NEC Simulator as Moral Theology in Code
chapters.label: Chapter 41

sections.title: 41.1 Why Model the Titles
paragraphs: At first glance, it may seem strange to translate scholastic moral theology into code. The extrinsic titles were born in Scripture, Roman law, canon law, confession manuals, university disputations, merchant practice, and pastoral anxiety. They were not mathematical formulas. They were moral distinctions. They asked whether a surplus payment was truly compensation for something external to the loan, or whether it was simply gain extracted from the loan itself. To place such distinctions inside a simulator can feel like a category mistake.

But in a modern financial world, many of the most important moral assumptions are already hidden inside models. Every spreadsheet contains a theology of capital, even when it denies having one. Every discounted cash-flow model, fund-return projection, repayment waterfall, sensitivity analysis, IRR target, and valuation model answers moral questions without announcing them. It decides what counts as value, whose claims are senior, and which losses matter. It decides whether the borrower's survival is a constraint or merely a variable, and whether capital should be restored, compensated, protected, multiplied, or privileged. It decides whether downside is shared or shifted, and whether the human enterprise exists to serve life, or whether life must be reorganized to serve capital.
The purpose of the NEC Simulator is not to replace moral judgment with code but to make moral judgment more explicit.

The old scholastic tradition did not ask only whether money had increased. It asked why it had increased. Was the increase tied to culpable delay? Then the relevant title might be poena conventionalis or morae poena. Was it tied to an actual loss incurred by the lender? Then the relevant title might be damnum emergens. Was it tied to a concrete foregone gain that the lender really would have earned? Then the relevant title might be lucrum cessans, though always under suspicion because of its tendency to become abstract opportunity cost. Was the surplus tied to genuine risk of losing principal? Then the relevant title might be periculum sortis, though only if the capital was truly exposed to loss and not protected by guarantees, seniority, collateral, or enforcement against the borrower's unrelated assets.

The NEC Simulator takes this old moral diagnostic system and asks whether it can be made visible inside modern investment design. Instead of treating a return as a single undifferentiated number, the simulator decomposes it. It asks what portion of the surplus, if any, is justified by actual cost recovery, what portion by documented loss, what portion by real risk-bearing, and what portion by stewardship labor; what portion is merely yield, what portion depends on the borrower's weakness, and what portion would disappear if the contract were truly solidaristic.

This is why modeling matters. Without modeling, extraction often hides behind language. A penalty can be described as discipline while functioning as revenue, a management fee as sustainability while drifting into institutional self-interest, a risk premium as compensation while the actual risk has been shifted elsewhere. An opportunity cost can be described as sacrifice while functioning as a universal claim that money deserves growth simply because it could have been deployed elsewhere. A repayment cap can be described as non-extractive while still producing pressure, fragility, or mission drift if the repayment waterfall privileges capital too strongly over workers, founders, community, or mission.
The simulator is therefore a tool of moral exposure. It does not merely calculate returns; it reveals the moral geometry of the contract.

This is especially important because modern finance often collapses all surplus into yield. Once a fund has a target return, every contractual feature tends to be interpreted in relation to that return. Fees, penalties, repayment schedules, preferred returns, liquidation preferences, warrants, convertibles, collateral, guarantees, and redemption rights can all become instruments for protecting capital's expected growth. The language may vary, but the gravitational pull is the same: capital must be compensated for time, protected from loss, and positioned to capture upside.

Scholastic moral theology interrupts that gravitational pull. It refuses to begin with the premise that capital is entitled to growth. It asks first whether there has been a real burden, and then who bears that burden. It asks whether the burden is documented, proportionate, and external to the mutuum, and whether the surplus restores equality or creates domination. It asks whether the creditor would have preferred repayment on time, whether the lender actually lost something, whether the alleged foregone gain was concrete rather than speculative, and whether the investor truly stood to lose principal.

These questions are difficult to hold in ordinary financial conversation because modern finance has trained us to treat them as secondary. The simulator makes them primary again. It forces the user to name the title, to classify the surplus, and to distinguish compensation from yield.

This does not mean the model can determine sin. A simulator cannot know the full interior intention of the parties. It cannot replace prudence, conscience, pastoral judgment, legal analysis, or theological discernment. It cannot know whether a borrower felt pressured, whether a founder signed under desperation, whether an investor secretly preferred default, or whether a board gradually drifted from stewardship into extraction. But it can make visible the structures in which those intentions operate. It can show whether the contract invites solidarity or domination, whether the downside is actually shared, and whether the same harm has been counted twice. It can show whether the lender's return depends on real loss absorption or on protected seniority, and whether a supposedly non-extractive instrument still behaves like extractive finance under stress.

This is why the NEC Simulator is not just a financial tool. It is moral theology in code. It is also a form of practical casuistry. The medieval and early modern theologians did not apply the usury doctrine only in abstraction. They examined concrete cases: delayed repayment, damaged merchants, foregone trade, risky voyages, public debt, charitable pawn banks, bills of exchange, penalties, partnerships, and disguised loans. They asked what the transaction really was, not merely what it called itself. The simulator continues this casuistic method in a modern environment. It asks about repayment priority, loss allocation, risk-bearing, administrative costs, late penalties, stewardship labor, collateral, guarantees, caps, and upside participation. It tries to classify the moral substance beneath the legal form.

The deepest purpose of the model is to prevent self-deception. Good people can build extractive systems without intending to. Mission-driven funds can drift, charitable institutions can become self-protective, stewardship language can be used to justify capital privilege, risk-sharing language can conceal risk-shifting, and cost recovery can slowly become profit extraction. A model that makes moral assumptions explicit creates friction against this drift. It slows the user down. It asks: what are you really charging for? What loss are you restoring? What risk are you actually bearing? What cost are you recovering? What burden are you placing on the borrower? What happens if the enterprise fails? Who can lose everything? Who remains protected?

In this sense, the simulator is not an answer machine but an examination of conscience. The scholastic doctors developed the extrinsic titles because they knew that surplus could be either restorative or predatory. They knew that a payment above principal was not automatically usury, but they also knew that nearly any title could become a pretext. The task, then and now, is not merely to prohibit all surplus. It is to discipline surplus: to ask whether capital is being compensated for a real burden, or whether it is using law, contract, time, and bargaining power to secure its own increase at the expense of another's vulnerability.

The NEC Simulator models the titles because modern finance needs this discipline again. It needs tools that can distinguish actual loss from fictive damages, concrete foregone gain from abstract opportunity cost, real risk-bearing from risk premiums without downside, and stewardship labor from fee extraction. It needs models that do not simply optimize return but interrogate the moral basis of return.

To model the titles, then, is not to reduce theology to mathematics. It is to refuse the false neutrality of mathematics: to insist that every model of capital already carries a moral vision, and that a Christian moral economy must make that vision visible, testable, and accountable. The old question remains, whether a surplus is restorative, participatory, or extractive. The simulator exists to make that question impossible to ignore.

sections.title: 41.2 Inputs and Classifications
paragraphs: A moral model begins with the humility to ask what it is actually measuring. Modern financial models usually begin with familiar inputs: principal, valuation, revenue, growth rate, repayment rate, discount rate, default probability, exit multiple, IRR, cash-flow timing, and expected return. These are useful, but they are not morally neutral. They often assume that the central question is how capital can be repaid, preserved, or multiplied. They rarely begin by asking whether the surplus claimed by capital is restorative, participatory, or extractive.

The NEC Simulator therefore needs a different kind of input architecture. It must still speak the language of finance, because it must analyze real contracts and real capital structures. But it must also speak the language of moral theology. It must ask not only what the contract pays, but why it pays; not only how much surplus is generated, but what title justifies that surplus; not only whether a return is possible, but who bears the loss if reality disappoints the projection.

The first input is principal. In ordinary finance, principal is the amount invested, loaned, or advanced. In scholastic moral analysis, however, principal has a more specific significance. It is the sors: the underlying sum, the thing placed at stake, the amount whose repayment may be demanded or whose loss may be borne. The moral meaning of principal depends on the structure of the transaction. If the principal is owed back absolutely, regardless of the borrower's success or failure, then the contract resembles a mutuum. If the principal is genuinely exposed to loss, then the contract moves closer to societas, commenda, risk-bearing capital, or another participatory structure. The same numerical input therefore has different moral meaning depending on whether it is protected or exposed.

The second input is repayment priority. This is one of the most morally revealing features of a capital structure. Who gets paid first? Does capital stand ahead of labor, suppliers, founders, mission, community, taxes, reserves, or reinvestment? Does the investor receive repayment before the enterprise has achieved resilience? Does the contract require repayment while the organization is still fragile? Does the repayment waterfall protect the investor from losses that are pushed onto others?

A repayment schedule may look moderate in isolation, but become morally troubling when viewed in priority order. A capped return may seem non-extractive compared to venture capital, but if capital is still repaid before wages, reserves, or mission-critical reinvestment, it may retain a structure of privilege. This is why the simulator cannot simply ask whether the return is capped. It must ask where the capital claim sits in the moral order of payment.

The third input is risk-bearing. This is central to periculum sortis. The simulator must distinguish between risk language and actual risk exposure. Many contracts speak as if investors bear risk because repayment is uncertain in practice. But the real question is whether the contract legally and economically permits the principal to be lost. If the investor has collateral, guarantees, seniority, enforcement rights, default penalties, or recourse to unrelated borrower assets, then the investor may be protected even when the entrepreneur, workers, or mission bear the effective downside.

Risk-bearing therefore cannot be entered as a vague impression. It must be classified. Is principal fully guaranteed? Partially guaranteed? Unsecured but still legally owed? Subordinated? Repayable only from surplus? Written down if the enterprise fails? Converted into a non-economic participation right after a cap? Does the investor share losses proportionally, or only share upside after downside has been absorbed by others? The moral classification depends on these details.

The fourth input is loss allocation. Risk-bearing asks whether capital can lose. Loss allocation asks who actually loses when things go wrong. This is often the place where the moral reality of a contract becomes visible. A financing structure may appear fair during success, but reveal its extractive character under stress. If revenue falls, who absorbs the shock? Are founders pressured into unpaid labor? Are workers laid off to service capital? Are suppliers stretched? Is mission postponed? Are communities abandoned? Are public subsidies used to protect private return? Does the investor's claim remain intact while everyone else is forced to adjust?

The simulator should therefore classify losses across parties: investor, founder, worker, enterprise, mission, community, supplier, public sector, and future beneficiaries. A non-extractive capital structure should not merely cap upside. It should also discipline downside. It should prevent the investor from enjoying theological language of risk-sharing while contractually pushing the wounds of failure onto weaker parties.

The fifth input is foregone opportunity. This corresponds to lucrum cessans. Here the simulator must be especially strict, because this title is morally dangerous. In modern finance, opportunity cost is often treated as universal. Money could have earned elsewhere; therefore money must be compensated here. But scholastic theology does not begin from the assumption that money deserves growth. It asks whether a concrete, licit, intended, and reasonably certain gain was actually interrupted by the loan or investment.

The simulator must therefore refuse vague opportunity-cost claims. It should ask: what alternative deployment was actually available? Was it mission-aligned? Was the investor genuinely intending to use the funds in that way? Was the expected gain reasonably determinate? What probability attaches to it? What costs and risks would have accompanied it? Did this specific transaction truly cause the gain to cease? Or is the investor merely invoking a general market return as if abstract capital always has a moral claim to growth?

In a non-extractive framework, foregone opportunity can sometimes matter. A small mission-driven fund may truly sacrifice one concrete deployment to make another. A patient capital provider may forgo a specific licit use of capital in order to support an enterprise. But the simulator must distinguish this from the metaphysical claim that all money, by nature, should grow over time. Lucrum cessans is admissible only when disciplined. Undisciplined, it becomes the bridge from compensation to ordinary interest.

The sixth input is actual damages. This corresponds to damnum emergens. It is the cleanest of the titles because it concerns real loss rather than hypothetical gain. But even here, classification matters. The simulator must ask whether the loss is actual, documented, caused by the transaction, proportionate, and not double-counted elsewhere. Administrative inconvenience is not necessarily damage. A desired return is not damage. A disappointment in portfolio performance is not damage. A modeled shortfall from target IRR is not damage. Actual damages must be tied to something the lender or investor truly lost.

Examples may include legal costs caused by default, collection costs, documented liquidity harm, emergency replacement financing, actual operational disruption, or specific expenses caused by the borrower's wrongful delay. But even these must be handled carefully. If the same harm is already covered by a late penalty, administrative fee, reserve requirement, or management charge, it cannot be counted again as damnum emergens. Restorative accounting requires moral anti-duplication.

The seventh input is late penalties. This corresponds to poena conventionalis or morae poena. Late penalties can be legitimate when they discipline culpable delay and compensate for real inconvenience or harm. But they are also among the easiest devices to abuse. A contract can create an unrealistically short repayment date, expect default, and then harvest penalties as hidden yield. The simulator must therefore ask whether the due date is realistic, whether delay is culpable, whether the penalty is proportionate, whether there is a grace period, whether hardship is considered, and whether the lender would genuinely prefer repayment on time.

This last question is morally crucial. If the lender benefits more from default than from timely repayment, the penalty has begun to function as revenue. Scotus's diagnostic insight becomes programmable here: the structure of incentives reveals the truth of the title. A penalty that the lender hopes to collect is no longer simply a penalty. It is a disguised price of time.

The eighth input is administrative costs. These belong near damnum emergens and stipendium laboris, but they require their own classification. Non-extractive finance still needs administration. The Monti di Pietà faced this problem directly. Lending to the poor required staff, accounting, buildings, record-keeping, pledge management, and institutional continuity. A charitable institution that refuses all cost recovery may collapse. But an institution that converts cost recovery into profit extraction may betray its purpose.

The simulator should therefore distinguish between actual cost recovery, allocated overhead, management fees, surplus-generating fee structures, related-party payments, and unexplained institutional leakage. Administrative cost should not become a cloak for return. A management fee should be tested against actual work performed, actual expenses incurred, proportionality, transparency, and mission necessity.

The ninth input is labor and stewardship costs. This is related to stipendium laboris, but in a modern fund it has special importance. Stewardship work is real work. Due diligence, accompaniment, governance support, mentoring, financial structuring, impact reporting, mission protection, legal design, and conflict mediation all require time and skill. A non-extractive capital structure should not romanticize unpaid labor or pretend that good finance sustains itself without human effort.

But stewardship compensation must remain compensation for labor, not a disguised claim on capital. The simulator must ask whether the payment corresponds to actual work, whether the work serves the enterprise, whether the fee is proportionate, whether it scales reasonably, and whether it remains necessary after the fund or institution grows. A fee that begins as stipendium laboris can drift into institutional self-interest. Classification must therefore be revisited over time.

These inputs are not merely data fields. They are moral questions translated into model structure. A conventional simulator might ask what return the investor receives. The NEC Simulator asks: what kind of return is this? Is it repayment of principal? Is it restoration of actual loss? Is it compensation for culpable delay? Is it recovery of administrative cost? Is it payment for stewardship labor? Is it compensation for concrete foregone gain? Is it reward for genuine risk-bearing? Is it partnership profit? Or is it simply yield extracted from the borrower's need?

The classifications should therefore include at least the following categories: principal repayment, actual damages, late penalty, administrative cost, stewardship labor, foregone opportunity, capital risk, partnership participation, mission reinvestment, investor distribution, related-party payment, owner distribution, borrower protection, reserve-building, and needs review. The "needs review" category is essential. A moral simulator should not force false certainty. Some payments will be ambiguous, some contracts will mix titles, and some classifications will require human judgment, documentary proof, or theological review. The point is not to automate conscience but to structure attention.

Once the inputs are classified, the simulator can begin to show moral patterns. It can reveal when a contract relies heavily on opportunity cost, when risk premiums are charged despite strong downside protection, and when penalties become a major source of return. It can show when administrative fees exceed plausible cost recovery, when capital is repaid before the enterprise has resilience, when the same burden is used to justify multiple payments, and when a supposedly patient structure becomes aggressive under stress.

The simulator should also preserve the distinction between legal form and moral form. A payment may be legally permitted but morally suspicious. A contract may call itself equity while functioning like senior debt. A revenue-based financing instrument may appear flexible but still pressure the enterprise if repayment begins too early. A redeemable instrument may appear capped but still dominate cash flow. A foundation-owned or steward-owned structure may still contain extractive financial terms. Moral classification must therefore pierce labels.

In this sense, the input layer of the simulator performs the first task of scholastic analysis: it asks what the thing really is. The old theologians knew that usury often hides in classification errors. A loan is called a partnership. A penalty is called discipline. A profit claim is called compensation.
