It can reject profit maximization while allowing administrative structures to become too comfortable. It can begin as a work of charity and gradually become a machine for its own preservation.

This is why the Monti are so important for the NEC Simulator. The simulator cannot merely ask whether a financing structure was founded with good intentions. It must ask whether those intentions remain visible in the flows of money. Are administrative charges still tied to actual cost? Are stewardship fees tied to actual labor? Are reserves proportionate to real risk? Are penalties rare and corrective, or common and revenue-producing? Are hardship cases treated with mercy? Are related-party payments monitored? Are borrowers protected from institutional self-interest? Does the financing structure still serve the people it was created to serve?

The third principle is that drift often appears through accounting categories. A charge originally classified as reimbursement may become a revenue stream. A labor stipend may become a management fee. A management fee may become a percentage of assets under management, detached from actual work. A reserve may become a pool for institutional expansion. A penalty may become expected income. A pledge sale may become routine. A "small charge" may remain small in percentage terms but large relative to the borrower's fragility. The moral problem appears not only in the contract, but in the books.

This is why accounting is not spiritually neutral. The ledger records the institution's actual priorities. It shows whether the poor are being served or monetized, and whether capital is being restored or privileged. It shows whether labor is being compensated or inflated, whether mission is receiving surplus or merely legitimating it, and whether the institution's original theology still governs its economic life.

The Monti also reveal the need for governance. A charitable purpose must be protected by institutional countermeasures. There must be oversight, rules against excess, and scrutiny of fees. There must be limits on penalties and review of hardship cases. There must be clarity about who benefits from surplus, and there must be mechanisms to prevent the managers of mercy from becoming beneficiaries of the system they administer.

Modern non-extractive finance needs the same safeguards. Asset locks, steward ownership, mission locks, caps, independent audit, borrower protections, grace periods, hardship clauses, transparent accounting, related-party review, and loss-sharing rules are not bureaucratic decorations. They are the modern equivalents of anti-drift disciplines. They exist because institutions cannot rely indefinitely on founding charisma, personal virtue, or good intentions. The structure must help preserve the conscience of the institution after the first generation of founders is gone.

The Monti di Pietà therefore stand at the beginning of this chapter not as a romantic model to imitate uncritically, but as a case study in the fragility of moral finance. They show that a Christian financial institution must answer three questions continuously. First, what problem was this institution created to solve? Second, what payments does it require in order to survive? Third, at what point do those payments begin to recreate the problem it was created to solve?

This third question is the heart of mission drift. The Monti were created to protect vulnerable borrowers from usury. But if their charges, penalties, procedures, or institutional needs became burdensome, they risked moving toward the moral territory they were meant to resist. The same is true today. A non-extractive fund is created to protect mission-driven enterprises from extractive capital. But if its fees, repayment priorities, control rights, risk premiums, administrative layers, or investor expectations begin to burden those enterprises, it too risks becoming a softer version of the thing it opposes.

This does not mean the Monti failed. Nor does it mean modern non-extractive finance is impossible. It means moral finance requires vigilance. The Monti teach that mercy must become institutional, but institutional mercy must remain accountable. They teach that cost recovery can be just, but only when it remains cost recovery. They teach that labor should be compensated, but not allowed to become a concealed profit claim. They teach that sustainability is necessary, but never absolute. They teach that serving the vulnerable requires systems, and that systems can begin to serve themselves.

Their warning is therefore deeply relevant for the NEC Simulator. A simulator that models only the original contract will miss the danger. It must also model actual behavior over time. It must track drift. It must compare stated purpose with financial flows. It must ask whether fees remain proportionate, whether penalties remain exceptional, whether risk is still shared, whether mission remains protected, and whether the vulnerable party remains at the center.

The lesson of the Monti is not that moral finance should avoid institutions. Without institutions, mercy remains episodic, fragile, and dependent on personal charity. The lesson is that institutions of mercy must be designed for self-examination.

The Monti di Pietà began with a beautiful intuition: credit could be reordered toward the poor. Lending could be removed from the logic of predation and placed under the discipline of charity. Money could serve life rather than feed on need. But that intuition had to be defended in practice, transaction by transaction, fee by fee, ledger by ledger, year by year.

So too with non-extractive capital. The danger is not only that hostile finance will remain extractive. That is obvious. The deeper danger is that friendly finance, moral finance, Christian finance, steward-owned finance, mission-driven finance, and non-extractive finance will slowly forget what they were created to do. They will keep the language while losing the discipline. They will keep the brand while losing the burden of proof. They will keep the mission statement while allowing the flows of money to tell another story. The Monti di Pietà warn us that this can happen. They also show us what must be built to resist it.

sections.title: 42.2 Drift in Modern Funds
paragraphs: Mission drift rarely begins as betrayal. It usually begins as adjustment. A fund faces pressure. A portfolio company underperforms. Legal costs rise. Staff capacity is stretched. Investors ask harder questions. A promising deal requires faster execution. A reporting requirement becomes more complex. A manager wants to professionalize. A co-founder leaves. A new partner joins. A side letter is granted. A fee is increased slightly. A repayment schedule is tightened. A hardship clause is interpreted narrowly. A cap remains in place, but the path toward the cap becomes more aggressive.

Nothing dramatic has happened. No one has announced that the mission is being abandoned. No one has declared a preference for extraction. The language remains intact. The website still speaks of stewardship, patient capital, mission alignment, impact, dignity, resilience, and service. But the internal gravitational field begins to shift. The fund starts to protect itself. The investor's position becomes a little more secure. The entrepreneur's burden becomes a little heavier. The mission's flexibility becomes a little smaller. The vulnerable party becomes a little less central. This is drift.

Modern funds are especially vulnerable to drift because they sit inside a financial ecosystem that assumes capital should grow. Even when a fund begins with a non-extractive intention, it must still relate to lawyers, accountants, regulators, investors, banks, consultants, auditors, portfolio companies, tax structures, market conventions, and expectations about professionalism. These surrounding systems carry their own anthropology. They assume that capital has rights, that risk deserves return, that time has a price, that investors require incentives, that fund managers require fees, that growth is proof of success, and that survival justifies many compromises. A mission-driven fund may resist these assumptions explicitly, but still absorb them implicitly.

The first form of drift is fee drift. Every fund needs administration. Legal structuring, compliance, due diligence, accounting, reporting, governance support, investor relations, audit, and portfolio accompaniment all cost money. These costs are real. A fund that refuses to pay for them may become fragile or dependent on hidden sacrifice. But fee drift begins when cost recovery becomes fund economics. A fee that once reimbursed necessary labor becomes a stable revenue line. A management charge becomes detached from actual work. A percentage-of-assets model continues even when marginal costs decline. Related-party service companies appear. Consulting arrangements accumulate. "Stewardship" becomes a billable category broad enough to absorb almost anything.

Fee drift is morally dangerous because it often hides behind necessity. The institution says, truthfully, that it needs money to operate. But the relevant question is not whether costs exist. The question is whether the charges remain proportionate, transparent, documented, and ordered toward the mission. If fees grow while service quality does not, if fees rise while portfolio companies struggle, if fees continue after active stewardship has ended, or if fees become a way to extract value despite return caps, then the fund has begun to move away from cost recovery toward institutional self-interest.

The second form of drift is return drift. A fund may begin with a modest return expectation. It may promise capped returns, patient repayment, or non-extractive upside participation. But over time, the target can harden. What began as a ceiling becomes a goal. What began as a possible return becomes an expected return. What began as compensation for risk becomes a portfolio requirement. The cap stops functioning as a restraint and starts functioning as a target.

Return drift is subtle because capped capital often looks morally cleaner than uncapped capital. And indeed, it may be cleaner. But a cap alone does not determine whether a structure is non-extractive. If the fund designs repayment schedules, covenants, fees, penalties, and priorities to make the cap highly likely, then the cap may simply define the desired yield. A 1.5x cap can still be extractive if the path to 1.5x burdens the enterprise, suppresses wages, weakens reserves, or forces mission compromise. A non-extractive fund must therefore ask not only how much capital can receive, but how capital receives it, when it receives it, and what the enterprise must sacrifice along the way.

The third form of drift is mission drift. Mission drift occurs when the fund's stated purpose remains the same, but its practical decision criteria change. At first, the fund may exist to serve mission-driven enterprises, steward-owned companies, open-source infrastructure, digital sovereignty, ecological repair, community resilience, or economic justice. But as pressures mount, it may begin selecting companies that are easier to finance, easier to measure, easier to explain to investors, easier to exit, or more likely to repay. The mission language remains, but the portfolio edges toward safer, more conventional, more investor-legible opportunities.

This form of drift is especially dangerous because it can be rationalized as prudence. A fund does need viable companies. It does need repayment. It does need discipline. But mission drift begins when financial legibility replaces mission fidelity. The fund starts asking: which enterprises make our model look successful? Rather than: which enterprises are we called to serve? It may still fund good companies, but it gradually stops reaching the more difficult edges of its original vocation.

The fourth form of drift is investor-pressure drift. Even patient, mission-aligned investors can become anxious. They may support non-extractive finance in principle, but still expect updates, benchmarks, repayment, comparables, risk controls, liquidity, and evidence that the model "works." A fund manager may begin adapting to these expectations. Reporting becomes more investor-facing. Metrics emphasize financial performance. Portfolio support is reframed around de-risking capital. Terms become slightly more protective. The fund begins to anticipate investor discomfort before investors explicitly demand change.

Investor-pressure drift is powerful because it does not require coercion. It works through imagination. The manager imagines what investors will tolerate. The manager imagines what will help the next fundraise. The manager imagines what will look credible to the market. Over time, the fund's internal audience changes. It no longer thinks first from the perspective of the enterprise, worker, mission, or community. It thinks first from the perspective of capital confidence.

The fifth form of drift is governance drift. A fund may begin with strong mission protections, independent stewards, asset locks, advisory boards, borrower protections, or community voice. But governance structures can weaken over time. Independent reviewers become passive. Mission boards become ceremonial. Conflicts of interest are disclosed but not challenged. Founders retain informal power. Investors gain influence through side agreements or repeated fundraising dependence. Legal documents remain intact, but the living practice of governance becomes thinner.

Governance drift matters because moral finance cannot depend only on documents. The real question is who can say no. Who can challenge a fee increase? Who can protect a borrower in distress? Who can stop a related-party arrangement? Who can insist that a hardship clause be honored? Who can prevent investor expectations from reshaping the mission? Who can defend the purpose of the fund when financial pressure rises? If no one has real authority to protect the mission, the mission is not truly locked. It is merely stated.

The sixth form of drift is measurement drift. What gets measured becomes what gets managed. A fund may begin with a rich understanding of mission: human dignity, worker flourishing, community resilience, digital sovereignty, ecological repair, open infrastructure, ownership justice, spiritual purpose, and non-extraction. But over time, these may be compressed into measurable proxies: revenue growth, jobs created, tons of carbon avoided, users served, diversity statistics, governance documents signed, or impact scores. Some of these metrics may be useful. But they can gradually replace the deeper moral reality they were meant to approximate.

Measurement drift is not the same as using metrics. Moral finance needs measurement. The problem arises when measurement becomes a substitute for discernment. A dashboard can show that a company is growing while hiding that its workers are exhausted. It can show that an impact target was met while hiding that mission governance is weak. It can show that capital is being repaid while hiding that resilience is declining. It can show that a fund is "impactful" while leaving its extraction geometry untouched.

The seventh form of drift is legal-form drift. Steward ownership, asset locks, foundations, cooperatives, trusts, golden shares, capped returns, and mission locks can all help protect purpose. But legal form is not moral substance. A steward-owned company can still accept extractive capital. A foundation-owned structure can still create related-party leakage. A capped-return instrument can still pressure cash flow. A cooperative can still exploit suppliers. A nonprofit can still underpay workers. A mission lock can still be interpreted narrowly.

Legal-form drift occurs when the institution begins to trust the form more than the underlying moral analysis. It assumes that because the structure is steward-owned, foundation-owned, or mission-locked, the finance is clean. But the scholastic tradition warns against exactly this kind of formalism. The name of the contract is not enough. One must ask what the contract does. Is it a loan, a partnership, a sale, an insurance contract, a disguised mutuum, or a hybrid? Who bears risk? Who receives surplus? Who can enforce? Who is vulnerable?

The eighth form of drift is solidarity drift. This may be the deepest of all. A non-extractive fund begins with a desire to accompany enterprises. It wants to be patient, human, relational, and mission-aligned. But as the portfolio grows, relationships become standardized. Templates replace conversation. Exceptions become inconvenient. Covenant monitoring replaces trust. Distress cases become "portfolio management." The fund still speaks of accompaniment, but its practices become increasingly procedural.

Solidarity drift occurs when the fund stops suffering with the enterprise. It may still be polite, flexible, and mission-oriented. But when stress arrives, the fund's first instinct is self-protection. It asks how to preserve the model, not how to share the burden. It asks how to prevent loss, not how to remain faithful. It asks how to maintain investor trust, not how to protect the vulnerable party from ruin. A genuinely non-extractive fund must therefore design for solidarity under stress, not only alignment under success.

This is where ergodicity becomes important again. Many drift patterns are invisible in the average case. The fund appears patient when companies perform well. It appears mission-aligned when repayments arrive on schedule. It appears non-extractive when the cap is respected. But under stress, the hidden hierarchy appears. Does the fund wait? Does it forgive? Does it subordinate? Does it reduce its claim? Does it help the enterprise rebuild? Or does it enforce the terms that were always present but rarely noticed? Stress reveals whether the fund is truly non-extractive.

Modern funds also face a particular danger that the Monti did not face in the same way: comparison pressure. A non-extractive fund will often be compared to venture capital, private equity, private credit, philanthropy, impact investing, ESG funds, catalytic capital, recoverable grants, or bank loans. Each comparison can pull the fund in a different direction. Compared to venture capital, the fund may look gentle. Compared to philanthropy, it may look demanding. Compared to bank debt, it may look risky. Compared to private equity, it may look underpowered. Compared to impact investing, it may look insufficiently standardized. These comparisons can distort the fund's self-understanding.

The danger is that the fund begins to define itself by being "less extractive than venture capital" rather than by being positively ordered toward justice. But non-extraction is not merely a relative improvement. It is not enough to be kinder than the market. The question is whether the structure itself is morally coherent. Does it restore rather than dominate? Does it share risk rather than shift it? Does it discipline surplus rather than normalize yield? Does it serve life rather than merely soften extraction?

This is why the extrinsic titles are useful. They give the fund a vocabulary that is not borrowed from modern finance. Instead of asking only whether a term is market-standard, below-market, concessionary, catalytic, or impact-aligned, the fund can ask: what title justifies this payment? Is there actual loss? Is there culpable delay? Is there concrete foregone gain? Is there real risk to principal? Is there labor? Is there expense? Or is this simply a return claim looking for moral language?

Drift often occurs when this vocabulary is lost. A fund begins with strong moral language but gradually reverts to market categories. It starts talking less about loss, risk-sharing, proportionality, and vulnerability, and more about portfolio performance, target returns, de-risking, investor appetite, liquidity, scalability, and fund economics. These terms are not automatically wrong. But if they become the dominant language, they reshape the imagination. The moral grammar weakens. The fund begins to think like the system it hoped to reform.

The antidote is not to reject all financial discipline. A non-extractive fund must be financially competent. It must understand risk, repayment, operations, governance, regulation, and sustainability. But it must discipline financial competence under a stronger moral grammar. It must know modern finance without being converted by it.

This is why the NEC Simulator should include drift monitoring as a core function, not an optional add-on. It should track fee drift by comparing fees to actual labor and expenses. It should track return drift by comparing realized and targeted returns to the original moral justification. It should track mission drift by comparing portfolio selection against mission criteria. It should track investor-pressure drift by monitoring changes in reporting, terms, side letters, and fundraising language. It should track governance drift by reviewing who actually exercises oversight. It should track measurement drift by identifying whether proxies are replacing substantive mission judgment. It should track legal-form drift by testing whether the moral substance matches the legal form. It should track solidarity drift by analyzing distress behavior, hardship treatment, restructuring decisions, and loss-sharing.

A modern fund also needs drift thresholds. Some change is legitimate. Institutions learn. Costs change. Regulations evolve. Portfolio realities differ from projections. A moral finance institution should not be frozen in its founding assumptions. But changes should be named, justified, and reviewed. A fee increase should require evidence. A new investor protection should require moral analysis. A repayment acceleration should require vulnerability review. A related-party arrangement should require independent approval. A change in mission criteria should require governance consent. A repeated exception should trigger rule revision.

Drift becomes dangerous when change becomes invisible, and this is why the simulator should create an institutional memory. It should preserve the original moral theory of the fund. It should record why certain terms were chosen, which titles justified them, what risks were accepted, what protections were promised, and what assumptions were made. Later decisions can then be compared against this original moral charter. The question becomes: are we still doing what we said we were doing? If not, why? Is the change faithful development, or drift?

This distinction matters. Not all change is drift. Some change is maturation. A fund may discover that its original fee model underpaid necessary labor. It may learn that its hardship clauses need to be stronger. It may find that a repayment schedule is too aggressive and must be softened. It may realize that a return cap is still too high. It may decide to subordinate capital more deeply. It may add stronger related-party safeguards. These changes are not drift away from mission. They are conversion toward mission. Drift, by contrast, moves toward self-protection, capital privilege, abstraction, and hidden extraction.

The moral direction of change is therefore essential. Is the fund becoming more patient, more transparent, more solidaristic, more accountable, more protective of mission, and more honest about surplus? Or is it becoming more return-oriented, more fee-dependent, more investor-facing, more legally defensive, more abstract in its justifications, and more willing to burden the enterprise? This is the question every modern fund must ask.

The Monti di Pietà remind us that institutions created to resist usury can themselves become morally ambiguous. Modern funds deepen the warning because the surrounding financial ecosystem is far more sophisticated, far more globalized, and far more capable of absorbing moral language into market logic. A fund can call itself non-extractive while using familiar return expectations. It can speak of stewardship while privileging capital. It can reject exits while demanding redemption. It can cap upside while protecting downside. It can serve mission while making mission pay for the privilege. Drift is therefore not a peripheral risk. It is one of the central moral problems of non-extractive finance.

A fund that wants to remain faithful must build countermeasures from the beginning. It must assume that drift will happen unless resisted. It must design accounting systems, governance structures, dashboards, audits, borrower protections, mission locks, fee reviews, hardship processes, and theological reflection into the institution itself. It must make it easier to remain faithful than to slide back into extraction.

Modern funds need this discipline because capital is patient in its own way. It waits for language to weaken. It waits for pressure to rise. It waits for exceptions to become precedents. It waits for founders to tire. It waits for governance to become ceremonial. It waits for mission to become brand. It waits for the dashboard to celebrate repayment more than justice. Non-extractive finance must be more patient still. It must return again and again to the old questions: What is this payment for? What title justifies it? Who bears risk? Who receives surplus? Who is protected? Who is burdened? What happens under stress? Are we restoring equality, sharing risk, sustaining mission, and serving life? The drift of modern funds is real. So the countermeasures must be real too.

sections.title: 42.3 Scholastic Countermeasures
paragraphs: The scholastic tradition did not trust moral language by itself. This is one of its great strengths. The theologians, canonists, confessors, and jurists who developed the usury doctrine knew that people could describe an extractive arrangement in respectable terms. A lender could call gain "compensation." A penalty could be called "discipline." A speculative expectation could be called "loss." A disguised loan could be called a partnership. A payment for time could be hidden inside a sale price. A risk premium could be charged even when the lender had protected himself against the risk. The form of the contract could be made to say one thing while the moral substance said another.

The scholastic response was not simply to condemn everything. It was to develop countermeasures. These countermeasures were intellectual, legal, pastoral, and institutional. They did not eliminate ambiguity, but they disciplined it. They asked specific questions. They required specific titles. They demanded proof. They tested proportionality. They examined intention. They looked for fraud. They distinguished legal form from moral substance. They placed the transaction under the scrutiny of conscience. Modern non-extractive finance needs these countermeasures again.

The first scholastic countermeasure is the requirement of a specific title. A surplus payment cannot justify itself. It must be named. Is it a penalty for culpable delay? Is it reimbursement for actual loss? Is it compensation for a concrete foregone gain? Is it payment for genuine risk to principal? Is it recovery of expense? Is it compensation for labor? Is it profit from partnership? Or is it simply gain from the loan itself?

This requirement sounds simple, but it is powerful. It prevents vague moral blending. Modern finance often uses broad terms like "return," "premium," "yield," "risk-adjusted compensation," "cost of capital," or "market rate." These terms are not precise enough for moral theology. The scholastic tradition forces the question: what exactly is being paid for? If the answer is "time," the claim is dangerous. If the answer is "money could have earned elsewhere," the claim must be tested under lucrum cessans and disciplined severely. If the answer is "risk," the simulator must ask whether capital is actually at risk. If the answer is "cost," the cost must be documented. If the answer is "labor," the labor must be shown. If the answer is "delay," the delay must be culpable, not engineered. The specific-title requirement prevents surplus from dissolving into abstraction.

The second scholastic countermeasure is specific proof. A title is not enough. It must be substantiated. Damnum emergens requires actual loss. Lucrum cessans requires a concrete foregone opportunity. Periculum sortis requires real exposure of principal. Poena conventionalis requires culpable delay and an anti-fraud structure. Stipendium laboris requires work. Expense reimbursement requires expense. Partnership profit requires genuine sharing of gains and losses.

This proof requirement is the difference between moral analysis and moral branding. Without proof, every fund can claim to be compensating risk, covering costs, supporting stewardship, or recovering losses. The scholastic method asks: show me. Show the invoice. Show the missed payment. Show the actual liquidity harm. Show the alternative opportunity. Show the subordination. Show the loss-absorption mechanism. Show the grace period. Show that the lender preferred timely repayment. Show that the fee corresponds to labor. Show that the investor can lose before the founder, worker, mission, or community is harmed. This is why bookkeeping and audit are not external to moral finance. They are the practical form of proof. A non-extractive fund should not only assert that its payments are justified. It should be able to document the title behind each significant claim.

The third scholastic countermeasure is proportionality. Even when a title is real, the amount must fit the burden. A real loss justifies restoration, not enrichment. A real delay may justify a penalty, but not a revenue strategy. A real administrative cost may justify reimbursement, but not institutional extraction. A real risk may justify participation in upside, but not unlimited protection plus return. A real foregone gain may justify compensation, but not an abstract market yield detached from the actual opportunity lost. Proportionality is the discipline that keeps compensation from becoming profit.

This is especially important because modern finance often scales payments according to capital, not burden. A management fee may be charged as a percentage of assets even when the actual work does not rise proportionally. A risk premium may increase with principal even when the real risk has been mitigated. A late penalty may compound beyond any actual harm. A return cap may be set according to investor appetite rather than mission resilience. Scholastic proportionality pushes back. It asks whether the claimed payment corresponds to a real burden. It asks whether the payment restores equality or creates a new inequality. It asks whether the surplus exceeds the title that justifies it.

The fourth scholastic countermeasure is anti-fraud analysis. The tradition repeatedly worried about transactions in fraudem usurarum: arrangements designed to evade the usury prohibition while preserving the economic substance of usury. A penalty clause might be formally valid, but if default was expected or engineered, the penalty became disguised interest. A sale on credit might be legitimate, but if the higher price simply concealed a charge for waiting, it could become a loan in disguise. A partnership might be licit, but if the capital provider was guaranteed repayment while also taking profit, the partnership label might conceal a mutuum. An exchange contract might be real, or it might be a way to hide lending for gain. Anti-fraud analysis is therefore the discipline of looking beneath labels.

Modern finance needs this urgently. A revenue-based financing agreement may be more flexible than a loan, but it can still function like debt if repayment is mandatory and aggressive. Preferred equity may be called equity, but function like senior secured debt if redemption rights, liquidation preferences, and control provisions protect the investor too strongly. Convertible instruments may appear founder-friendly while preserving investor optionality behind the scenes. Stewardship fees may appear mission-oriented while extracting value through related-party structures. Impact terms may appear ethical while leaving the extraction geometry untouched.

The scholastic question is always: what is this really? Is it truly partnership, or a disguised loan? Is it truly risk-sharing, or risk-shifting? Is it truly cost recovery, or revenue? Is it truly a penalty, or expected yield? Is it truly foregone profit, or universalized opportunity cost? Is it truly mission protection, or investor control in moral language? Anti-fraud analysis protects moral substance against contractual cleverness.

The fifth scholastic countermeasure is scrutiny of intention and incentive. The tradition did not reduce morality to subjective intention, but it also did not ignore intention. Scotus's concern about the lender who prefers late repayment because of the penalty is a powerful example. The external structure reveals the internal temptation. If the lender benefits from default, the penalty is morally suspect. If the investor benefits from distress, the structure is dangerous. If capital receives more when the enterprise is weak, the incentive is disordered.

Modern contracts create many such temptations. Default interest may make delay profitable. Warrants may make distress financing attractive. Liquidation preferences may make an early sale better for investors than long-term mission preservation. Control rights may allow capital to force an exit. Fees may create incentives to raise more capital than the mission needs. Related-party arrangements may reward complexity. Short repayment schedules may pressure companies into growth strategies they would otherwise reject.

Scholastic scrutiny of intention asks not only what the contract says, but what it trains the parties to desire. Would the investor prefer the enterprise to flourish slowly, or to repay quickly? Would the lender prefer timely repayment, or default penalties? Would the fund manager prefer mission fidelity, or larger assets under management? Would the governance structure reward patience, or exits? Would the investor share pain in downturns, or benefit from enforcement? Would the institution choose mercy when mercy reduces its own revenue? A moral finance structure must be designed so that incentives support the stated mission.

The sixth scholastic countermeasure is confessional scrutiny. In the medieval and early modern world, usury was not merely a legal matter. It was a matter for conscience, confession, restitution, and pastoral guidance. This matters because many usury-like structures cannot be judged by formal legality alone. They require examination of intention, pressure, vulnerability, bargaining power, knowledge, and actual effects. A confessor or moral theologian might ask questions that a lawyer would not ask. Did you exploit need? Did you intend gain from the loan itself? Did you disguise interest as something else? Did you impose terms the borrower could not realistically avoid? Did you restore what was unjustly taken?

Modern finance does not have a direct equivalent of the confessional forum. But non-extractive finance needs some analogous space of moral review. This could take the form of an ethics committee, theological advisory board, independent steward council, borrower ombuds process, annual moral audit, or structured examination of conscience for the fund. The point is not to recreate medieval confession in corporate form. The point is to create a place where the institution is asked questions deeper than compliance. Legal review asks: is this permitted? Financial review asks: is this viable? Impact review asks: does this produce measurable good? Scholastic moral review asks: is this just? That question must have an institutional home.

The seventh scholastic countermeasure is the distinction between legal form and moral substance. The tradition repeatedly refused to let names settle the matter. A contract could be called a sale, but morally function as a loan. A partnership could be called societas, but morally function as guaranteed debt. A gift could be called voluntary, but morally expected from the beginning. A penalty could be called punishment, but morally function as a price for time. A payment could be legally valid but spiritually dangerous.

This distinction is essential for modern non-extractive capital. Steward ownership, asset locks, foundations, capped returns, recoverable grants, revenue-based financing, redeemable equity, preferred shares, convertible notes, and mission locks all need moral analysis. None is automatically clean. Each must be examined according to ownership, risk, priority, control, surplus, enforceability, vulnerability, and mission effects. The legal form matters, but it is not enough. A steward-owned company can still be financed extractively. A capped-return investor can still dominate cash flow. A nonprofit can still exploit workers. A foundation can still preserve capital privilege. A cooperative can still shift risk to suppliers. A mission-locked fund can still design investor-friendly terms. A contract called patient can still be impatient under stress. The scholastic method teaches us to ask what the thing is, not merely what it is called.

The eighth scholastic countermeasure is restitution. The usury doctrine did not end with classification. If usury was taken, restitution was owed. This is morally important because it means that unjust surplus cannot be made clean by later explanation. If a payment was extracted without legitimate title, the problem is not merely reputational. It creates a duty of repair.

Modern non-extractive finance should recover this logic. If a fund discovers that a fee exceeded actual cost, the excess should be corrected. If a penalty was wrongly charged, it should be refunded or credited. If a risk premium was taken while risk was shifted to the borrower, the structure should be revised. If related-party payments created hidden extraction, the value should be restored. If repayment pressure damaged the mission, the fund should not merely note the harm; it should participate in repair.

Restitution turns moral audit into moral action. Without restitution, the simulator becomes a diagnostic toy. With restitution, it becomes part of conversion. It allows the institution to say: we have discovered that this payment was not justified; we will correct it. This is not weakness. It is fidelity. A fund that can repair itself is morally stronger than a fund that must pretend it never errs.

The ninth scholastic countermeasure is suspicion toward universalized titles. The tradition understood that extrinsic titles could swallow the usury prohibition if generalized too broadly. If every delay justifies penalty, penalty becomes interest. If every inconvenience justifies damages, damages become yield. If every possible alternative use justifies lost profit, lucrum cessans becomes opportunity cost. If every uncertainty justifies risk premium, periculum sortis becomes ordinary interest. If every period without money justifies compensation, carentia pecuniae becomes the time value of money.

Modern finance lives largely inside these universalized titles. It assumes opportunity cost. It assumes liquidity premiums. It assumes risk pricing. It assumes time value. It assumes return expectations. The scholastic countermeasure is to resist universalization. A title must remain tied to a specific burden in a specific case. The more abstract the title becomes, the more dangerous it becomes. This is why the NEC Simulator should flag generalized claims. "Market return" should not automatically count as lucrum cessans. "Default risk" should not automatically count as periculum sortis. "Lock-up" should not automatically count as carentia pecuniae. "Administrative burden" should not automatically justify fees. "Investor expectation" should not become a moral title. Each claim must be brought back to concrete proof.

The tenth scholastic countermeasure is care for the vulnerable borrower. This is the biblical root beneath the whole tradition. The poor neighbor, the brother in need, the debtor whose cloak must be returned at night, the covenant community tempted to harden its heart, the people restored through release: all of this remains beneath the later legal and theological distinctions. The scholastic titles are not merely technical devices. They are meant to prevent finance from turning need into revenue.

Modern non-extractive finance must therefore ask not only whether a title is analytically valid, but whether the vulnerable party is protected. A payment may be technically defensible and still pastorally harsh. A repayment schedule may be legal and still fragile. A fee may be documented and still excessive relative to the borrower's condition. A risk-sharing structure may be real and still misaligned with the enterprise's capacity. A fund may be morally better than venture capital and still not merciful enough. The scholastic tradition, at its best, disciplines both greed and abstraction. It keeps returning moral analysis to the concrete person before us.

For the NEC Simulator, these scholastic countermeasures can become operational rules. Every surplus payment must name a title. Every title must have evidence. Every amount must be proportionate. Every structure must be tested for disguised usury. Every incentive must be examined. Every legal form must be tested against moral substance. Every ambiguity must be marked for review. Every unjust surplus must be repairable. Every generalized title must be treated with suspicion. Every judgment must ask how the vulnerable party is affected. This is not a replacement for conscience. It is a training in conscience.

The scholastic tradition developed these countermeasures because it understood both the necessity and the danger of finance. Credit can help households survive, merchants trade, institutions endure, and communities flourish. But credit can also dominate, extract, enslave, and disguise exploitation as contract. The extrinsic titles were created to preserve a narrow path between naive prohibition and permissive collapse. They allowed genuine compensation without surrendering to the idea that capital deserves growth simply because it is capital.

Modern non-extractive finance walks the same narrow path. It must compensate real labor without creating fee extraction. It must preserve institutional sustainability without becoming self-serving. It must recognize risk without monetizing every uncertainty. It must allow participation without domination. It must recover costs without burdening the vulnerable. It must model opportunity without baptizing opportunity cost. It must use contracts without trusting labels. It must build institutions without losing mercy.

The scholastic countermeasures are therefore not antiquarian details. They are design principles. They teach a fund how to remain honest. They teach a dashboard what to display. They teach an audit what to ask. They teach a governance board when to intervene. They teach investors how to examine their claims. They teach entrepreneurs how to recognize disguised extraction. They teach moral finance that its own language is not enough. The old theologians knew that usury could hide in cleverness. Our task is to build institutions where it has fewer places to hide.

sections.title: 42.4 Modern Countermeasures
paragraphs: If drift is predictable, countermeasures must be designed in from the beginning. This is the first practical rule of modern moral finance. A fund should not assume that good intentions, shared values, charismatic founders, or a beautiful mission statement will preserve its purpose. These things matter, but they are not enough. Institutions grow tired. People change. Investors become anxious. Legal structures are tested. Market pressure returns. Exceptions accumulate. The language of mission remains, but the flows of money begin to tell another story.

The question, then, is not whether a non-extractive fund can avoid pressure. It cannot. The question is whether the fund is built so that pressure reveals and strengthens its mission, rather than slowly converting it back into ordinary finance.

Modern countermeasures are the institutional descendants of the scholastic disciplines. Where the scholastics required a title, modern finance needs explicit classification. Where they required proof, modern finance needs documentation and audit. Where they tested proportionality, modern finance needs caps and review processes. Where they looked for fraud, modern finance needs substance-over-form analysis. Where they examined conscience, modern finance needs governance capable of moral scrutiny. Where they required restitution, modern finance needs mechanisms for correction.

The first modern countermeasure is the asset lock. An asset lock prevents the underlying mission-bearing assets from being freely converted into private gain. It changes the background assumption of ownership. Instead of treating the company or fund as property that can be sold, stripped, or redirected for the benefit of capital, the asset lock places limits on extraction. It says that the organization's value is not simply available for appropriation. Its assets are held for a purpose.

For non-extractive finance, this matters because many forms of extraction occur not through ordinary interest, but through capital gains, exits, liquidation preferences, asset sales, recapitalizations, and control rights. A company may avoid formal debt and still be structured so that mission-built value can later be captured by investors or insiders. An asset lock creates a structural barrier against this. It protects the accumulated value of the enterprise from being treated as a prize.

But an asset lock is not enough. It can protect residual value while leaving operating cash flows exposed. A company may be asset-locked and still pay excessive fees, burdensome redemptions, inflated management charges, or related-party payments. The lock protects one door, but extraction can enter through another. The simulator should therefore treat asset locks as important but incomplete. They are a countermeasure against ownership extraction, not a substitute for contract-level moral analysis.

The second countermeasure is steward ownership. Steward ownership separates governance rights from pure economic rights. It aims to ensure that those who control the enterprise are bound to its mission rather than to the maximization of shareholder value. In a steward-owned structure, voting control is held by stewards, trusts, foundations, golden-share bodies, or mission-guarding mechanisms, while economic claims are limited. The enterprise is not simply for sale to the highest bidder. It is governed for purpose.

This is deeply relevant to the usury tradition because ownership and lending are both ways of structuring claims on future value. The old mutuum problem asked whether a lender could demand more than principal from the borrower. Modern corporate finance adds another question: can owners demand that the entire enterprise be governed for their financial upside? Steward ownership answers by limiting the dominion of capital over governance.

Yet steward ownership too can drift. Stewards may become passive. Mission guardians may lack power. Economic instruments may still create pressure. Investors may receive capped returns that are modest in theory but aggressive in timing. Founders may retain informal control. Related-party structures may leak value. Therefore steward ownership must be paired with financial design. Purpose governance and non-extractive capital are complementary; neither replaces the other.

The third countermeasure is the mission lock. A mission lock states what the institution exists to do and restricts decisions that would undermine that purpose. It may appear in articles of association, foundation statutes, investment documents, shareholder agreements, governance charters, side letters, or ethical covenants. It gives the institution a formal memory. It says: this is the purpose that capital must serve.

But a mission lock must be specific enough to govern. Vague language about impact, sustainability, innovation, or social good is too easy to absorb into ordinary finance. A strong mission lock should identify the protected purpose, the prohibited forms of extraction, the hierarchy of stakeholders, the treatment of surplus, the role of capital, and the conditions under which the mission cannot be subordinated to repayment or return.

A mission lock should also have an enforcer. A mission that no one can defend is not locked. It is ornamental. Modern countermeasures therefore require a person or body with standing to object when mission is threatened: a steward board, golden-share holder, foundation, ethics council, borrower representative, community guardian, or independent trustee. Someone must be able to say no when financial pressure rises.

The fourth countermeasure is caps. Caps are among the simplest and most visible restraints on extraction. A capped return says that capital cannot take unlimited upside. A capped management fee says that administration cannot grow without limit. A capped penalty says that delay cannot become a revenue engine. A capped repayment burden says that the enterprise cannot be drained beyond a certain threshold. Caps discipline appetite.

But caps must be designed carefully. A return cap can become a target. A fee cap can still be too high. A penalty cap can still be punitive. A revenue-share cap can still burden the company if repayment begins too early or takes too much from fragile cash flow. A cap limits the maximum, but it does not by itself prove that the claim is justified. The simulator should therefore distinguish between "capped" and "clean." A capped surplus still needs a title. It still needs proof. It still needs proportionality. It still needs downside analysis. A cap is a guardrail, not a sacrament; it restrains extraction, but it does not automatically convert extraction into justice.

The fifth countermeasure is transparency. Hidden finance is dangerous finance. Non-extractive capital must be legible to the people it affects. Entrepreneurs should understand what capital can claim, when it can claim it, what happens under stress, what fees may be charged, what penalties may arise, what rights investors hold, what protections the mission has, and how disputes are handled. Investors should understand that their return is limited by moral commitments. Governance bodies should understand the flow of money. Auditors should be able to trace classifications. Communities should be able to trust that the mission is not being used as a cover for extraction.

Transparency should include clear term sheets, plain-language summaries, repayment waterfall diagrams, downside scenarios, fee schedules, related-party disclosures, moral-title classifications, hardship procedures, and annual reporting. It should also include transparency about uncertainty. If a payment category is ambiguous, the institution should say so. If a title is contested, it should be reviewed. If a moral regime produces different judgments, the dashboard should show the disagreement. Transparency is not merely communication. It is a discipline against self-deception.

The sixth countermeasure is independent audit. A fund should not be the sole judge of its own non-extraction. Independent audit brings an external eye to the institution's claims. Financial audit verifies that the numbers are accurate. Moral-finance audit asks whether the classifications are true. Are fees tied to actual cost and labor? Are penalties rare and proportionate? Are related-party payments fair and approved? Are risk premiums supported by real loss absorption? Are opportunity-cost claims concrete? Are hardship protections used? Are mission reserves protected? Are capital claims consistent with the stated theology of the fund?

This kind of audit will require new methods. Ordinary auditors may not be trained to evaluate extrinsic titles. Theological advisors may not be trained to read ledgers. Lawyers may understand enforceability but not moral substance. Economists may understand risk but not usury. Non-extractive finance therefore needs interdisciplinary audit: accounting, law, finance, theology, governance, and borrower experience. The audit should not only produce a pass or fail. It should produce findings, recommendations, and required corrections. If fees exceed documented cost, adjust them. If a penalty structure creates perverse incentives, rewrite it. If related-party payments create leakage, restructure governance. If capital is insufficiently at risk for a periculum sortis claim, reduce the return or increase loss absorption. If repayment priority weakens the mission, revise the waterfall. Audit should lead to conversion.

The seventh countermeasure is borrower protection. This is where the biblical root of the doctrine returns most directly. The borrower must not be treated merely as a counterparty. The borrower is a person, a community, a mission-bearing institution, a neighbor. Modern borrower protections can include grace periods, cure periods, hardship clauses, payment holidays, restructuring rights, caps on repayment burden, limits on penalties, no personal guarantees, no aggressive collateral, no compounding default charges, no acceleration without review, and access to mediation.

Borrower protections should not be hidden as emergency exceptions that no one dares to use. They should be part of the moral architecture. The entrepreneur should know that distress does not automatically turn the fund into an adversary. If the capital is truly non-extractive, then hardship must be a moment of discernment, not merely enforcement. This does not mean borrowers have no obligations. Non-extractive finance is not irresponsibility. It can require reporting, honesty, effort, transparency, and repayment when possible. But it must distinguish inability from bad faith, fragility from exploitation, and human difficulty from moral failure. A structure without mercy will eventually become extractive, even if its financial terms are modest.

The eighth countermeasure is grace and hardship clauses. Grace is not sentimentality. It is a structural recognition that human beings and enterprises live through time, uncertainty, illness, delay, error, and shock. A hardship clause gives the contract a way to remain human when reality departs from projections. It can allow temporary deferral, reduced payments, restructuring, forgiveness, conversion, standstill periods, or mission-protective renegotiation.

The old tradition worried about delay because delay could harm the lender. But modern moral finance must also worry about enforcement because enforcement can destroy the borrower. Grace and hardship clauses help preserve the distinction. They prevent late payment from automatically becoming revenue. They prevent stress from immediately triggering extraction. They create space for discernment before penalty. The NEC Simulator should track whether hardship clauses exist and whether they are actually used. A clause that is never invoked may indicate that borrowers are afraid, unaware, or discouraged. A fund should be evaluated not only by the kindness of its documents, but by the mercy of its practice.

The ninth countermeasure is loss-sharing rules. This is central to periculum sortis. If capital claims a return for risk, it must share loss. Loss-sharing rules specify when investor claims are reduced, deferred, subordinated, converted, forgiven, or written down. They make risk-bearing real. Without such rules, risk language remains vague.

Loss-sharing can take many forms. Repayment may be contingent on surplus. Investor return may pause when wages or mission reserves fall below thresholds. Capital may be subordinated to workers, suppliers, or mission-critical obligations. Principal may be written down if the enterprise fails despite good-faith effort. Returns may be reduced in low-revenue years. Upside participation may be paired with downside absorption. Public or philanthropic first-loss layers may protect vulnerable enterprises, though care must be taken that private investors do not use public loss absorption to justify private gain. The key principle is that the party claiming risk compensation must actually be wounded by the risk. If the investor is protected while others absorb the loss, the return is morally suspect.

The tenth countermeasure is repayment waterfall design. A repayment waterfall reveals the moral order of the institution. Who is paid first? What must be protected before capital receives return? Are workers paid living wages before investors receive surplus? Are taxes, suppliers, reserves, maintenance, and mission-critical reinvestment protected? Does the enterprise build resilience before capital is repaid? Does investor return begin only after the company reaches stability? Are there thresholds for liquidity, solvency, and mission health? A non-extractive waterfall should not simply place capital at the top. It should reflect the purpose of the enterprise. Capital may be repaid, but not in a way that hollows out the living body of the organization. The waterfall should be designed so that the enterprise can breathe.

The eleventh countermeasure is related-party governance. Hidden extraction often occurs through side channels. Management companies, advisory firms, consulting contracts, licensing fees, service agreements, real estate leases, software charges, and affiliate transactions can move value out of a mission structure without appearing as investor return. A fund may cap investor upside while allowing insiders to receive uncapped value through related parties. Modern moral finance must therefore treat related-party payments as high-risk. They should require disclosure, independent approval, market comparison, mission necessity, proportionality review, and periodic audit. The simulator should flag them automatically. A related-party payment is not wrong by default, but it should never be invisible.

The twelfth countermeasure is stakeholder voice. The people who bear risk should have some way to speak into the structure. Workers, founders, borrowers, mission guardians, community representatives, or beneficiaries may see forms of pressure that investors and managers miss. They may know when repayment is harming resilience, when fees are burdensome, when reporting requirements are excessive, when hardship clauses are not accessible, or when mission is being compromised. Stakeholder voice can be built through advisory councils, borrower feedback, worker representation, grievance mechanisms, periodic interviews, governance seats, ombuds processes, or mission review forums. The key is that voice must be safe and consequential. If borrowers fear retaliation, voice will be formal but empty. If feedback produces no change, voice becomes performance.

The thirteenth countermeasure is a moral dashboard. The dashboard should not celebrate capital repayment in isolation. It should show justified surplus, title documentation, double-counting risk, downside allocation, upside distribution, mission reserves, fee drift, penalty frequency, hardship use, related-party payments, risk-bearing reality, and regime comparison. It should ask whether the structure serves life. A moral dashboard turns the scholastic examination into institutional practice. It makes visible what otherwise drifts into the background. It reminds the fund that its task is not merely to perform financially, but to remain faithful.

The fourteenth countermeasure is periodic re-underwriting of moral assumptions. A contract may be morally acceptable at signing and become problematic later because circumstances change. Risk may decline. Costs may fall. The enterprise may become fragile. The fund may become larger. Fees may no longer match labor. The investor may have recovered principal but still retain upside. A once-justified title may expire. The fund should therefore periodically re-underwrite the moral basis of its claims. Does this fee still correspond to labor? Does this risk premium still correspond to risk? Does this repayment schedule still fit the enterprise's capacity? Does this penalty still discipline culpable delay, or has it become expected income? Does this return still reflect participation, or has capital already been made whole? This is the temporal form of justice. A moral claim must remain true through time.

The fifteenth countermeasure is restitution and correction. No structure will be perfect. Errors will occur. Classifications will be mistaken. Fees may drift. Penalties may be overcharged. A hardship case may be mishandled. A related-party payment may prove excessive. A risk premium may no longer be justified. The question is whether the institution can repent. Restitution mechanisms should be built in. Overcharges can be refunded or credited. Terms can be amended. Fees can be reduced. Penalties can be waived. Investor return can be lowered. Capital can be subordinated. A borrower can be compensated for harm. A governance rule can be tightened. The institution can acknowledge error without collapsing. This is perhaps the most Christian countermeasure of all. It recognizes that moral finance is not a claim to purity. It is a practice of ongoing conversion.

Modern countermeasures should therefore be integrated into a coherent system. An asset lock protects accumulated value, and steward ownership protects governance from capital domination. A mission lock preserves purpose. Caps restrain appetite. Transparency makes claims visible, and independent audit tests whether the claims are true. Borrower protections guard the vulnerable. Grace and hardship clauses humanize enforcement. Loss-sharing rules make risk-bearing real. Repayment waterfalls reveal and discipline priority. Related-party governance blocks hidden leakage. Stakeholder voice brings the burdened party into the conversation. A moral dashboard keeps the institution attentive. Periodic re-underwriting tests whether titles remain valid. Restitution repairs unjust surplus.

Together, these countermeasures do what the scholastic tradition tried to do in its own time: distinguish compensation from extraction, preserve justice in exchange, and prevent financial cleverness from overwhelming moral truth. They also make non-extractive finance credible. Without countermeasures, non-extractive finance is only an aspiration. With countermeasures, it becomes a discipline. It can be examined, audited, corrected, and trusted. It can tell investors what they may justly receive. It can tell entrepreneurs what they are protected from. It can tell communities that mission is not merely decorative. It can tell the Church, civil society, and the market that another kind of capital structure is possible.

The deepest purpose of these countermeasures is not compliance. It is conversion of capital. Capital must be trained to wait, to serve, to accept limits, to bear risk, to tell the truth, to share loss, to receive only what is just, and to relinquish what it cannot morally claim. Institutions do not learn these habits automatically. They must be built into governance, accounting, contracts, dashboards, audits, and daily practice.

The Monti di Pietà teach us that even charitable finance can drift. The scholastics teach us how to ask the right questions. Modern countermeasures teach us how to build the answers into institutions. If non-extractive finance is to remain faithful, it must not only begin with a moral vision. It must become an architecture of accountability.

chapters.part: Part XII — Toward Non-Extractive Finance

chapters.title: What the Tradition Actually Teaches
chapters.label: Chapter 43

sections.title: 43.1 Not All Surplus Is Usury
paragraphs: The tradition does not teach that every payment above principal is usury. This is one of the most important conclusions of the whole book, because it corrects two opposite misunderstandings at once. The first is the modern assumption that the old usury doctrine was economically naive, hostile to finance, and incapable of distinguishing predatory lending from legitimate compensation. The second is the rigorist temptation to treat every surplus payment as morally corrupt simply because it exceeds the original principal. The scholastic tradition is more demanding than the modern world, but also more subtle than its critics imagine. It did not say that all surplus is evil; it said that surplus must be morally explained.

The central prohibition was narrow but profound: one may not demand gain from the mutuum itself. In a loan for consumption, ownership passes to the borrower, who owes back the equivalent (the tantundem), not a payment for the "use" of what has already become the borrower's own. To demand more simply because time has passed, or because money as money has been lent, is to sell what one does not own, or to sell the same thing twice. It is to convert the borrower's need into the lender's increase.

But this prohibition does not eliminate every possible payment above principal. It requires that such payment arise from something external to the loan itself. If the lender has suffered actual loss, compensation may be just. If the borrower has culpably delayed repayment and thereby caused injury or disorder, a proportionate penalty may be just. If the lender has truly foregone a concrete and licit gain because of the transaction, some later theologians allowed compensation under strict conditions. If capital is genuinely exposed to loss in a partnership-like or risk-bearing structure, profit-sharing may be just. If an institution must recover real administrative costs in order to serve the poor or sustain its mission, reimbursement may be just. And if stewardship labor is actually performed, compensation for that work may be just. The moral problem, in short, is not surplus as such but unjust surplus.

This distinction matters for modern finance because much contemporary criticism of capitalism becomes imprecise at exactly this point. It notices extraction, but cannot always distinguish extraction from compensation, participation, sustainability, or service, and it can therefore collapse into slogans: profit is bad, interest is bad, investment is bad, return is bad, finance is bad. The scholastic tradition offers something sharper. It asks what the surplus is for: whether it restores equality, compensates a real burden, rewards genuine participation, sustains necessary labor, or instead arises from power over another's need.

The distinction matters in the other direction too, because modern defenders of finance often use the complexity of legitimate surplus to defend illegitimate surplus. They point to risk, labor, opportunity, inflation, administration, default, liquidity, or market norms, and conclude that capital must be paid. But the tradition refuses that leap. Risk must be real, labor must be performed, opportunity must be concrete, loss must be actual, and costs must be documented; penalties must be proportionate and not desired; partnership must share downside. A title that is possible in principle may still be false in the particular case. The extrinsic titles therefore form neither a blanket prohibition nor a blanket permission. They form a moral diagnostic system.

Compensation can be just. This is clearest in damnum emergens, actual loss incurred. If the lender or investor suffers a real loss because of the transaction or because of wrongful delay, justice may require restoration. Aquinas himself allows compensation for loss incurred, precisely because this does not sell the use of money. It repairs a harm: the lender is not enriched by the loan but restored from damage. The logic is restorative, not extractive. Modern finance needs this distinction. A non-extractive fund may incur legal costs, administrative costs, restructuring costs, or liquidity harm; a borrower's default may create real expenses; a mission-driven institution may need reimbursement in order to remain viable. To deny all compensation would not be justice. It could simply shift the burden unfairly onto the capital provider, administrator, steward, or worker. But the compensation must remain tied to the harm. It cannot become a target return disguised as damages.

Risk-bearing can be just. This is the core insight behind partnership, societas, commenda, and the later title of periculum sortis. When capital is truly placed at risk, and when the provider of capital shares the fate of the enterprise, participation in upside can be morally intelligible. Aquinas's distinction between lending and partnership remains decisive: if money is entrusted to a merchant as capital that remains exposed to the risks of the venture, then the provider of capital may share in profit because the provider also shares in loss. This is not gain from a mutuum. It is participation in a common undertaking. Modern finance often invokes risk, but does not always bear it, and here the tradition is a necessary discipline. A risk premium is not justified merely because the future is uncertain. The question is whether principal can actually be lost. Is capital subordinated? Is repayment contingent on success? Are guarantees absent? Is collateral limited? Does the investor lose before workers, mission, or community are sacrificed? Is upside paired with downside? If so, surplus may have the character of participation. If not, the language of risk may be a cloak for protected yield.

Labor and expense can be just. The Monti di Pietà forced this question into institutional form. Lending to the poor required buildings, clerks, records, pledge storage, valuation, administration, and governance, and if no one paid for these things the institution could not survive. A modest charge for real expense and labor could therefore be defended as something other than usury. It was not a price for the use of money; it was a way of sustaining the work required to make charitable lending possible. This is directly relevant to non-extractive funds, steward-owned institutions, open-source infrastructure, and mission-driven capital. Moral finance still needs administration. Stewardship is not weightless. Due diligence, accompaniment, legal structuring, audit, accounting, governance support, and mission protection all require skilled labor, and to pretend otherwise often results in hidden exploitation of the people doing the work. But the charge must remain proportionate to the work and expense. It must not become a private profit channel under the language of stewardship.

Partnership profit can be just. The Christian tradition does not condemn all profit. Aquinas allows merchant gain when it is ordered to household support, public utility, or necessary service rather than greed, and partnership profit can be legitimate when parties join in a common venture, share uncertainty, contribute different forms of value, and divide surplus according to a just agreement. The moral danger appears when partnership language is used without partnership reality, when one party receives protected return while the other bears the enterprise's vulnerability. Modern equity is therefore ambiguous. It may resemble societas when it shares upside and downside. But it may also become extractive through liquidation preferences, redemption rights, control rights, drag-along provisions, anti-dilution clauses, exit pressure, and governance domination. Legal equity is not automatically moral partnership. The tradition again requires substance over form.

This is the conclusion toward which the extrinsic titles have been leading: surplus must be judged by its source, structure, proportionality, and effect. A payment above principal may be just when it restores what was lost, when it compensates work actually performed, when it reimburses expense actually incurred, when it shares the fruits of a venture whose losses are also shared, when it sustains an institution genuinely ordered toward service, or when it reflects concrete, documented, and proportionate burdens external to the loan itself. But surplus becomes usurious or usury-like when it arises from the mere fact of lending, the mere passage of time, the borrower's need, the lender's bargaining power, a fictive loss, an abstract opportunity cost, a penalty designed as revenue, a risk premium without real risk, or a partnership form without shared downside.

This is why the tradition remains so powerful: it refuses both naive anti-finance and naive pro-finance. It does not ask, "Is there surplus?" and stop there. It asks, "What kind of surplus is this?" It asks, "Does this payment heal a rupture in justice, or create one?" It asks, "Does this structure restore equality, or dominate the vulnerable?" It asks, "Does capital participate in a shared venture, or demand growth while shielding itself from loss?" It asks, "Does the institution recover the cost of service, or turn service into a revenue model?"

For anyone hoping to recover moral economy in an age of extraction, this distinction is essential, because either collapse is fatal. If all surplus is condemned, then we cannot build durable institutions. We cannot compensate stewardship labor, sustain charitable lending, share risk in common enterprise, design patient capital, or distinguish exploitative debt from legitimate partnership. We collapse the complexity of economic life into a purity that may be spiritually attractive but institutionally fragile. And if all surplus is accepted, then usury doctrine disappears and capital's claim becomes unlimited in principle. Every burden becomes priceable, every risk becomes yield, every delay becomes revenue, every foregone possibility becomes opportunity cost, and every institution learns to justify itself. The vulnerable are once again told that their need is a market opportunity.

The extrinsic titles preserve the narrow path between those two cliffs. They teach that surplus may be permitted, but never presumed. It must be named, proven, and proportionate; it must be external to the loan itself, tested against fraud, and examined under conditions of stress; it must be accountable to the vulnerable party, and it must remain ordered toward restoration, participation, service, or sustainability, not domination. This is the theological discipline that modern capital has largely forgotten. Modern finance tends to treat return as normal and asks only how much risk adjusts it. The older tradition treats return as morally questionable until its title is known. That shift in default assumption changes everything. It does not make finance impossible; it makes finance answerable. Not all surplus is usury, but all surplus must be judged, and the rest of this chapter is about how the judging is done.

sections.title: 43.2 Not All Legal Interest Is Morally Clean
paragraphs: If not all surplus is usury, it is equally true that not all legal interest is morally clean. Modern law permits many things that Christian moral theology may still question, and this should not surprise us. Law and morality overlap, but they are not identical. Civil law must govern public order, enforce contracts, regulate markets, protect property, and create predictable rules for social life. Moral theology asks a deeper question: whether an act, structure, or claim is rightly ordered before God, neighbor, justice, and the common good.

A contract can be enforceable and still be unjust. A payment can be legal and still be extractive. A lender can comply with regulation and still exploit vulnerability; an investor can disclose every term and still structure domination; a fund can satisfy its documents and still betray its mission. A return can be permitted by law and still lack a legitimate moral title.

The distinction is crucial because modern finance often treats legality as moral absolution. If the borrower signed, if the terms were disclosed, if the interest rate is permitted, if the investor complied with regulation, if the fund documents authorize the fee, then the transaction is presumed clean. The moral question is reduced to consent and compliance. The usury tradition refuses that reduction. Consent is important, but consent alone does not make a transaction just. The desperate can consent. The uninformed can consent. The weaker party can consent because no better option is available. A founder can sign terms that threaten the mission because payroll is due; a borrower can accept punitive interest because the alternative is eviction, hunger, or bankruptcy; a community can accept extractive infrastructure because no patient capital exists. Consent under pressure may create legal enforceability. It does not necessarily create moral equality.

This is why the biblical background remains so important. The commands against usury are not built around abstract market exchange between equally situated parties. They arise in scenes of vulnerability: the poor neighbor, the cloak taken in pledge, the brother who has fallen into need, the debtor whose cry reaches God, the community tempted to harden its heart as the year of release approaches. The moral concern is not merely whether the borrower agreed but whether the lender has turned need into revenue.

Legal interest often does exactly that. It may be priced according to market norms, but market norms can normalize extraction. It may compensate risk, but the risk may be shifted to the borrower. It may reflect opportunity cost, but the opportunity may be abstract. It may include default penalties, but default may be structurally likely. It may be disclosed, but not understood; affordable in the base case, but destructive under stress; ordinary, but ordinary does not mean just.

The old doctrine is therefore not refuted by the fact that modern law permits interest. The law may permit what theology still judges dangerous. The law may treat money as capital entitled to return, while theology asks whether the specific return restores loss, compensates labor, shares risk, or merely monetizes time. The law may enforce repayment, while theology asks whether repayment destroys the borrower's household, mission, or dignity. The law may recognize a creditor's right, while theology asks whether that right has become domination. Formal legality is not moral sufficiency.

This matters most in modern corporate finance, where extraction often appears through highly sophisticated legal forms. A venture capital term sheet may be entirely legal. Its liquidation preferences, anti-dilution provisions, drag-along rights, protective provisions, redemption rights, board controls, and exit expectations may all be standard. Yet the structure may still subordinate founders, workers, mission, and community to capital's preferred path. Legal equity may become morally debt-like when capital is protected too strongly; partnership language may conceal asymmetry; upside may be shared only after downside has been shifted.

Private equity raises similar questions. Debt-financed acquisition, dividend recapitalization, management fees, monitoring fees, related-party transactions, aggressive cost-cutting, and exit-driven governance may all be legal.
