But legality does not answer whether the structure serves the acquired company, its workers, its customers, its suppliers, or its community. The law may allow capital to extract value from the enterprise. Moral theology asks whether capital has a just title to that value.

Private credit, revenue-based financing, convertible notes, and venture debt raise still other questions. A revenue share may look gentle because it flexes with revenue, but it may still drain cash flow if the percentage is too high or begins too early. A convertible note may look efficient, but it may give investors optionality without real solidarity. Venture debt may look less dilutive than equity, but it can increase fragility through fixed repayment, warrants, covenants, and default provisions. Legal innovation does not automatically produce moral innovation.

This is why contract form can hide extraction, and why the scholastic tradition repeatedly insisted that one must ask what the transaction really is. Is this a true sale, or a disguised loan? Is this a partnership, or a mutuum with profit attached? Is this a penalty, or expected yield? Is this compensation, or a price for time? Is this exchange, or evasion? Is this risk-sharing, or protected capital claiming risk language? Modern law often emphasizes form, disclosure, and enforceability. Moral theology must examine substance, burden, vulnerability, and effect.

A legally valid interest charge may still be morally unclean if it lacks a real extrinsic title. If the lender has suffered no actual loss, borne no genuine risk, performed no additional labor, incurred no expense, and sacrificed no concrete opportunity, then the charge may be little more than payment for the use of money. Modern law may call it interest. The older tradition may call it usury.

A legally valid default penalty may still be morally unclean if it is excessive, expected, engineered, or desired as revenue. A penalty is supposed to discipline wrongful delay or compensate harm. If the lender structures the contract so that default is likely, or profits more from late repayment than timely repayment, then the penalty has become a disguised yield. Legal enforceability does not cleanse the incentive.

A legally valid risk premium may still be morally unclean if capital is not truly at risk. If the lender has collateral, guarantees, seniority, covenants, insurance, public guarantees, or enforcement against unrelated borrower assets, then the investor may be protected while still charging for risk. In such a case, periculum sortis is invoked without its moral substance. The law may permit the pricing. Theology asks who actually bears the wound.

A legally valid opportunity-cost claim may still be morally unclean if the foregone opportunity is abstract. Modern finance routinely assumes that capital could have earned elsewhere, and therefore must be compensated for being deployed here. But the older tradition asks whether the foregone gain was concrete, intended, licit, probable, and caused by the transaction. "Money could always have earned something somewhere" is not a moral title. It is a metaphysics of capital growth.

A legally valid management fee may still be morally unclean if it exceeds real labor and expense. A fund manager may be entitled by contract to a fee, but the moral question is whether the fee corresponds to actual stewardship work, administration, due diligence, governance support, and mission protection. If the fee scales with assets while labor does not, if related-party structures absorb value, or if fees continue after work declines, then cost recovery has drifted toward extraction.

A legally valid repayment priority may still be morally unclean if it protects capital while making everyone else absorb loss. Seniority, collateral, and guarantees are legal tools, but they reveal a moral order. If workers, suppliers, founders, mission reserves, and community obligations are sacrificed before capital takes loss, then the structure may be legally safe and morally inverted. Capital has placed itself above the living body of the enterprise. The legal system may not see this as a problem. Moral theology must.

None of this means that civil law is irrelevant or that all legal interest is sinful. Law can restrain abuse, provide clarity, enforce promises, protect parties, and create institutions in which trust is possible, and many legal interest charges may correspond to real cost, risk, inflation, administration, or other legitimate burdens. The point is not to dismiss law but to refuse to let it have the final word.

Christian moral economy requires a second reading of the contract. Where the first reading asks what the law says, the second asks what justice says. The first identifies rights; the second identifies burdens. The first asks whether the contract can be enforced; the second asks whether it should be. The first asks whether consent was obtained; the second asks whether vulnerability was exploited. The first asks whether a payment is authorized; the second asks whether the payment is morally titled. The first asks whether capital is protected; the second asks whether life is served.

The second reading is especially necessary because modern financial systems are very good at making extraction look normal. Extraction does not usually appear as crude oppression. It appears as discount rates, risk premiums, preferred returns, liquidity preferences, covenants, default interest, commitment fees, origination fees, monitoring fees, transaction costs, redemption rights, and market terms. Each instrument has a rationale; each can be defended; each may be legal. But taken together, they can create a system in which capital's growth is treated as natural and the borrower's vulnerability as priceable.

The scholastic tradition interrupts that normalization. Do not begin with the market, it says; begin with justice. Do not ask only whether the term is standard; ask whether it is rightly ordered. Do not ask only whether the borrower signed; ask whether the borrower was treated as neighbor. Do not ask only whether capital was compensated; ask whether capital had a title to compensation. Do not ask only whether the return was legal; ask whether the return was clean.

This has demanding implications for non-extractive finance. A non-extractive fund cannot simply rely on legal documents to prove its morality. It must design beyond compliance, and that means asking whether its terms remain just under stress, whether its fees are proportionate, whether its caps are meaningful, whether its risk-sharing is real, whether its investors can lose, whether its repayment priority protects or weakens the enterprise, and whether its governance preserves mission or merely decorates capital. A fund may be legally innovative and still morally ordinary. It may use a foundation, a steward-owned form, a capped return, a revenue share, a mission lock, or a new corporate vehicle, and yet still reproduce the deeper logic of capital priority. Moral cleanliness requires more than novelty. It requires conversion of the claim itself.

The same is true for public policy. Legislators may create new legal forms, tax incentives, investment categories, social enterprise structures, or digital sovereignty funds, and these can be useful. But unless they address risk allocation, surplus claims, governance rights, asset locks, mission drift, related-party leakage, and borrower protections, they may simply create new legal containers for old extraction. A moral economy cannot be legislated by label alone. The deeper question is whether the law helps capital serve life.

This is where Christian moral theology can make a distinctive contribution. It does not merely denounce finance; it brings a grammar that can examine finance from within. It can distinguish loan from partnership, compensation from yield, loss from opportunity, risk-bearing from risk-shifting, labor from fee extraction, legality from justice, and ownership from stewardship. It can honor law while refusing legalism. It can build institutions while judging their moral substance.

The conclusion, then, is not that legal interest is always immoral, nor that the Church should ignore modern financial complexity. The conclusion is more exacting: every legal interest claim must still answer the moral question. What is the title, and what is the proof? What is the burden? Who bears downside, and who receives upside? Who is vulnerable, and who is protected? What happens under stress? Does the payment restore equality, compensate real loss, share genuine risk, support labor, sustain mission, or merely secure capital's growth? These questions remain necessary even when the law has already spoken.

The old usury doctrine survives wherever legality is not mistaken for justice. It reminds us that a permitted contract can still wound the soul of an economy. It reminds us that disclosure is not mercy, consent is not equality, and enforceability is not righteousness; that capital can obey the law while failing love of neighbor. Not all surplus is usury, and not all legal interest is clean. Between those two truths lies the work of moral economy, and the next section names the distinction that organizes it.

sections.title: 43.3 The Central Distinction
paragraphs: The central distinction is not between finance and no finance. Nor is it between money and holiness, business and charity, profit and purity, or markets and morality. These oppositions are too crude to carry the weight of the tradition. They cannot explain Aquinas's distinction between mutuum and partnership. They cannot explain the Monti di Pietà. They cannot explain damnum emergens, lucrum cessans, poena conventionalis, periculum sortis, stipendium laboris, or the long debates over exchange, risk, merchant life, public debt, and institutional sustainability. The central distinction is between surplus from restoration, surplus from participation, and surplus from domination. That threefold distinction gathers the entire usury tradition into a form that can speak to modern finance.

Surplus from restoration is the cleanest. It arises when a payment above principal repairs a real burden: the lender or institution has suffered actual loss; a borrower has culpably delayed repayment and caused damage; a steward has performed real labor; an institution has incurred real administrative costs; a creditor has spent money on collection, legal process, or replacement financing; a charitable lending institution must recover expenses in order to continue serving the poor. In such cases the surplus does not arise from the mere use of money; it arises from the need to restore equality after some external burden has disturbed it.

Restorative surplus has a moral shape. It points backward, to a loss, burden, cost, or injury. It asks what was actually harmed, and it seeks proportional repair. It does not aim to enrich the capital provider, does not convert the borrower's need into yield, does not demand growth because time has passed. It says: something has been lost, spent, damaged, or labored; justice permits compensation. Damnum emergens belongs most clearly here, along with many legitimate administrative costs and some forms of stipendium laboris. Poena conventionalis may also belong here, but only when it remains truly penal and restorative: a proportionate response to culpable delay, not a revenue strategy. And restoration is morally bounded by the burden it restores. If the payment exceeds the loss, it begins to move away from restoration. If the loss is invented, it becomes fiction; if the same loss is counted twice, it becomes extraction; if cost recovery becomes institutional surplus, it drifts; if penalty becomes expected income, it no longer restores justice but profits from disorder.

The second kind is surplus from participation. This arises when capital, labor, skill, governance, and risk are joined in a common undertaking. Here surplus is not merely compensation for a past loss; it is a share in the fruits of a venture, and the key moral question is whether the sharing is real. Does the capital provider participate in downside as well as upside? Does the investor share the fate of the enterprise? Is repayment contingent on success? Is the investor's claim subordinated to the life of the organization? Does the structure resemble partnership rather than guaranteed debt?

This is the logic of societas. It is also the moral intuition behind commenda, genuine equity, and some forms of risk-bearing capital. Aquinas's distinction is decisive: if money is transferred in a mutuum, the borrower owes back the equivalent principal, and the lender cannot charge for the use of what now belongs to the borrower; but if capital is entrusted by way of partnership, remaining at the risk of the capital provider, then profit-sharing can be legitimate. The surplus arises not from lending as such, but from participation in a common venture. Participation is therefore not a mere label; it is a structure of shared fate.

Modern finance often uses the language of participation while preserving capital privilege. Equity may claim to share risk, but liquidation preferences, redemption rights, anti-dilution protection, control rights, and exit pressure may shield capital from true downside. Revenue-based financing may claim to flex with performance, but if the repayment burden is too heavy or begins too early, it can still function extractively. A capped-return investment may appear modest, but if capital is repaid before the enterprise builds resilience, participation remains shallow. Surplus from participation is morally clean only when upside is joined to downside. If the investor receives upside while the founder, worker, mission, or community bears the real loss, the structure is not participation but domination in participatory language. Periculum sortis becomes legitimate only when the sors is genuinely exposed. Risk must be borne, not merely priced; loss must be possible, not merely statistical; capital must be able to suffer with the enterprise.

The third kind is surplus from domination. This is the surplus the usury tradition condemns most deeply. It arises when one party uses another's need, weakness, delay, dependence, lack of alternatives, or legal obligation as an opportunity for gain. It may appear as interest on a mutuum. It may appear as excessive penalty, as fictive damages, as abstract opportunity cost, as a risk premium without real risk, as fees detached from labor, as priority repayment that hollows out the enterprise, as legal equity that functions like protected debt, as a contract that is formally consensual but materially coercive.

Domination does not always look cruel. Often it looks normal. It uses ordinary financial language: yield, market rate, preferred return, time value, default interest, liquidity premium, cost of capital, investor protection, risk adjustment, downside control. Each phrase can be legitimate in some contexts. But when these claims are detached from real burden, shared risk, or service, they become a language of domination.

Surplus from domination has a different moral shape. It is not bounded by restoration, and it is not disciplined by shared fate. It seeks protected increase: upside without wound, compensation without loss, penalty without mercy, opportunity without concreteness, risk premium without exposure, ownership without stewardship, governance without accountability. It converts the other party's need into capital's growth.

This is why the old doctrine remains radical. It says that capital may not grow simply because another lacks money, may not demand increase merely because time passes, may not monetize vulnerability, and may not hide behind contract form. It may not call domination "risk" when the wounds fall elsewhere. It may not call speculation "loss," or institutional appetite "expense," or control "partnership," or legality "justice."

The difference between these three forms of surplus can be stated simply. Restoration asks: what real burden is being repaired? Participation asks: what common venture and shared risk is being joined? Domination asks: what vulnerability is being used?

The triad also translates the extrinsic titles into modern language, one by one. Poena conventionalis is clean only when it restores order after culpable delay; it becomes domination when delay is engineered or desired. Damnum emergens is clean when it restores actual loss; it becomes domination when losses are fictive, inflated, or double-counted. Lucrum cessans may be defensible when a concrete, licit, intended, and reasonably certain gain has truly been interrupted; it becomes domination when it universalizes opportunity cost and treats all money as entitled to growth. Periculum sortis is clean when capital genuinely bears loss; it becomes domination when risk is priced but shifted to others. Stipendium laboris is clean when labor is real, necessary, and proportionately compensated; it becomes domination when fees detach from work. Administrative reimbursement is clean when it sustains service; it becomes domination when the institution's own appetite becomes the hidden beneficiary. And carentia pecuniae is dangerous precisely because it sits so close to domination: the mere absence of money for a time can easily become a claim that time itself must pay tribute to capital.

This distinction also clarifies modern debates over debt and equity. Debt often tends toward domination when repayment is fixed, senior, collateralized, guaranteed, and enforceable regardless of outcome. But not every debt instrument is equally extractive. Some may include grace, forgiveness, hardship protections, proportional cost recovery, and genuine concern for the borrower; the moral question is whether the lender's claim remains bounded by justice or becomes protected growth from another's vulnerability. Equity often tends toward participation because it shares upside and downside. But not all equity is morally clean. If equity includes strong preferences, control rights, exit pressure, and downside protections that leave workers, founders, and mission exposed first, then it can become domination in the form of ownership. Legal equity can still violate the spirit of societas.

Non-extractive finance must therefore avoid both formalism and sentimentality. It cannot say "debt bad, equity good." It cannot say "capped return good, uncapped return bad." It cannot say "steward ownership solves everything." It cannot say "impact language makes finance clean." It must ask the deeper questions: restoration, participation, or domination?

This is also the difference between capital serving life and capital extracting from life. Capital serves life when it repairs what was lost, supports work, shares risk, strengthens mission, builds resilience, and accepts limits; when it enters the enterprise as servant, partner, steward, or helper; when it can wait; when it can lose; when it does not confuse its own preservation with the common good; when it receives only what it can morally claim. Capital extracts from life when it demands priority over the living body of the enterprise; when it turns time into automatic yield; when it prices risk while shifting loss; when it treats the borrower's lack of alternatives as a business model; when it uses law to enforce what justice would temper; when it forces mission, labor, community, and vocation to bend around its repayment schedule; when it becomes impatient with life's actual rhythm.

The moral economy this book proposes is not hostile to capital. It is hostile to capital's false sovereignty. Capital is not evil. It is a tool, a claim, a stored capacity, a form of social power, and sometimes a gift. It can help households survive, enterprises begin, workers flourish, communities build, technologies serve, and missions endure. But capital becomes dangerous when it forgets that it is subordinate to life, and the usury tradition is one long attempt to keep capital in its proper place. That proper place is not absolute ownership but stewardship, which means that capital is held under a purpose beyond itself. It may be preserved, but not idolized. It may be restored, but not guaranteed at all costs. It may be compensated, but only for real burdens. It may participate, but only with shared risk. It may sustain institutions, but not become their hidden master. It may serve enterprise, but not devour it.

The distinction between restoration, participation, and domination gives us a practical way to examine whether the stewardship is real. When a fund receives surplus, ask: did this restore a real burden? When an investor receives upside, ask: did this investor share downside? When a lender charges for delay, ask: was delay culpable, or profitable? When an institution charges fees, ask: do these correspond to labor and expense, or to self-preservation? When capital claims opportunity cost, ask: what opportunity was truly lost? When risk is priced, ask: who was actually wounded by failure? When governance rights are exercised, ask: do they protect mission or protect capital? When a structure is called non-extractive, ask: where does the money go under stress? The answer to these questions reveals the moral form of the transaction.

This is the central distinction that modern finance needs to recover. Finance is not morally judged by complexity, legality, sophistication, innovation, or market acceptance. It is judged by whether its surplus arises from restoration, participation, or domination; the first can be just, the second can be fruitful, and the third is the old sin in new clothes. What remains is to state the test itself.

sections.title: 43.4 The Final Moral Test
paragraphs: The tradition's teaching can now be put in final form. A book's worth of distinctions, titles, cases, and controversies comes down, in the end, to five questions, and they can be asked of any financing arrangement in any century, under any legal wrapper. Does finance restore equality? Does it share risk? Does it protect the vulnerable? Does it support work, mission, and community? Or does it transform another's need into capital's guaranteed growth?
The first four questions share a grammar: they ask what the surplus is doing. The fifth asks what the surplus is hiding. Take them in order.

Does finance restore equality? This is the restorative test, and it points backward. Commutative justice treats exchange between neighbors as something that should leave both parties whole, so a burden that falls on one of them (a loss suffered, an expense incurred, labor performed, a culpable delay endured) may justly be repaired by the other. Surplus that restores equality can name the burden it repairs, show that the burden was real, and stop when the repair is complete. If nothing was actually lost, spent, or labored over, restoration cannot be the explanation. If the payment continues after the burden has been made whole, it has stopped restoring and started collecting.

Does it share risk? Not price risk: share it. The word "risk" appears in nearly every modern financing document, so the question has to be asked of the structure rather than the vocabulary. Can this capital be wounded? Does the provider stand to lose alongside the enterprise, or has it arranged, through seniority, collateral, guarantees, preferences, and covenants, to be paid in every state of the world while others absorb the states that hurt? Where capital genuinely shares the fate of the venture, its portion of the fruits is participation, and the tradition blesses it. Where capital charges a premium for danger that has been shifted onto someone else, the premium is tribute, whatever the term sheet calls it.

Does it protect the vulnerable? The usury commands were not written for negotiations between equals; they were written for the poor neighbor, the cloak in pledge, the brother fallen into need. So the test asks where a structure's weight lands when things go wrong, because that is when protection costs something, and because a structure shows what it believes at exactly the point where the weaker party has run out of choices. At default, at renegotiation, at collection, does the arrangement remember that the borrower is a neighbor, with grace where hardship is honest, penalties that discipline rather than profit, collection that stops short of the household's survival? Or does it treat the borrower's weakness as the moment of maximum leverage?

Does it support work, mission, and community? Capital is one contributor to an enterprise among several, and not the first. There are also the people who do the work, the mission that justifies it, and the community that depends on it, and a financing arrangement either strengthens that living order or bends it around the repayment schedule. The question sounds soft and is not, because it can be asked of budgets and board minutes. What was cut when the payment came due? Who absorbed the sacrifice so the covenant could be met? Did the mission adjust to the financing, or the financing to the mission? An arrangement can pass the first three questions and still fail this one, by making the enterprise exist for the capital rather than the capital for the enterprise.

Or does it transform another's need into capital's guaranteed growth? This fifth question gathers the other four, because every failure they detect has the same shape at the bottom: need converted into yield, time converted into tribute, vulnerability converted into a revenue model. It is the old definition of usury restated for an age that has retired the word. The tradition never objected to money changing hands above principal; it objected to increase that grows from the mere fact that someone else lacked money and could not wait. Whenever the honest answer to the first four questions is no, because nothing was restored, no loss was possible, no one was protected, and nothing was served, the answer to the fifth is almost always yes, and the arrangement has earned the old name, whatever the new one is.

A word about how the questions work together. The first four are not four hurdles to be cleared at once; they are four distinct sources of a just claim, and one honest yes can be enough. A lender genuinely made whole for a genuine loss owes no apology for not also sharing risk. But each yes has to be honest in the tradition's sense, which this chapter has already spelled out: named, proven, proportionate, and still true when the enterprise is struggling. And no yes to any of the first four can be manufactured after the fact to dress up a structure that was built for the fifth.

Five questions, then. They are not a scoring rubric, and they cannot be answered once at signing and filed away. They have to be asked the way the scholastics asked their own versions: of the substance rather than the form, under stress rather than in the base case, and on behalf of the party least able to ask them. The audit questions posed earlier to a financing contract examined its anatomy; these examine its fruit. A contract can answer them well and still fail in practice, and a contract that answers them badly will usually come with excellent documentation.

Nor are the questions new. The first is the logic of restoration that runs from commutative justice through damnum emergens. The second is the soul of societas and periculum sortis. The third is what the Scriptures command and what the Monti di Pietà tried to build in brick and ledger. The fourth is stipendium laboris widened to the whole living enterprise. The fifth is the ancient prohibition itself, the refusal to let a mutuum breed. The final moral test is simply the tradition, folded small enough to carry into a meeting.

The tradition does not hand us a rate cap or a model statute. It hands us these five questions and the nerve to keep asking them. What it would take for a modern economy to answer them well, and what could be rebuilt from a doctrine so long forgotten, is the business of the final chapter.

chapters.part: Part XIII — Conclusion: Capital That Serves Life

chapters.title: Recovering Moral Economy in an Age of Extraction
chapters.label: Chapter 44

sections.title: 44.1 Why the Old Doctrine Is Newly Relevant
paragraphs: Chapter 43 ended with five questions and a promise: that what it would take for a modern economy to answer them well would be the business of this final chapter. The preface made a claim at the very beginning of the book, that the old question of usury has not disappeared but has only changed its clothing. Forty-three chapters later, that claim can be stated more exactly. The question is wearing more clothing than it has ever worn, cut by better tailors, and the wardrobe is still growing.

A reasonable reader might still ask why a doctrine worked out by confessors, canonists, and friars should matter to an economy they could not have imagined. The question deserves an answer built from the present rather than the past, so consider where the old questions have turned up lately.

Somewhere tonight a founder is reading a term sheet at a kitchen table, trying to work out what the liquidation preference on page nine will mean for the people down the hall if the next two years go wrong. Somewhere a finance official is discovering that the repayment schedule, not the budget debate, will decide what her country builds next. Somewhere a volunteer maintainer is weighing an acquisition offer for a piece of code that other systems everywhere depend on. None of them is likely to reach for a treatise on the extrinsic titles. All of them are standing exactly where the tradition stood for seven centuries: at the point where capital meets need and must explain its claim.

Begin with debt itself. Debt is now the ordinary weather of economic life. Households borrow for housing, schooling, medical care, and the gap between paychecks; enterprises borrow to smooth revenue, to bridge receivables, and to grow; nations borrow on a scale that makes their repayment schedules a more binding constraint on public life than most legislation. Each of the three cases raises the tradition's questions in its own register. The tradition's core case, remember, was the consumption loan: the mutuum extended not to launch a venture but to survive until harvest. A great deal of modern household borrowing is closer to that case than to venture finance, which means the tradition's central concerns apply to it directly, and they are not gentle concerns. Is the surplus titled, or is it payment for time and need? Does repayment leave the debtor's household standing? Is default priced as a risk or farmed as a revenue stream, with penalties and fees designed into the expected return? Enterprise debt raises the participation questions: whether the lender shares any of the fate of the business it feeds, or has arranged to be whole in every state of the world. Sovereign debt raises the protection questions, since the parties who ultimately carry a nation's repayment schedule, in reduced services and foreclosed futures, are rarely the parties who signed it. When these questions are answered badly for one borrower, the tradition called it usury. When they are answered badly for whole populations at once, the modern world calls it a debt crisis. The two names describe the same failure at different scales: need transformed into capital's guaranteed growth until something breaks.

Venture capital presents the opposite face, and claims the noblest scholastic pedigree. On paper it is societas: capital joining labor, skill, and vision in a common venture and sharing its fate. Sometimes it is exactly that, and when it is, the tradition has nothing against it and much to say for it; a fund that puts capital genuinely at risk beside a founder's years of work is practicing what the scholastics spent centuries defending against rigorists who wanted every return condemned. But the instruments that have grown up around the form tell a more complicated story. Liquidation preferences, redemption rights, anti-dilution protection, drag-along provisions, and protective covenants can arrange matters so that one partner's downside is cushioned at every point where the other partners remain exposed. A founder can discover, usually at the worst possible moment, that the partnership was structured to be a partnership only on the way up. The question this book has taught the reader to ask of a term sheet is the one Aquinas asked of merchants and Olivi asked of capital: is this a partnership, or a mutuum wearing partnership's clothing? Where upside is shared and downside is shifted, the answer is already in.

Private equity raises the ownership questions in their sharpest modern form. A company can be bought with borrowed money and then made to carry the debt that bought it, while paying management fees to the buyer for the privilege of being owned. The scholastics never saw a dividend recapitalization, but they would have recognized the shape of the problem at once, because it is their problem: what is really being sold here, who really owns this enterprise, and who really bears its risk? The last question has the most uncomfortable answer, because when a leveraged acquisition fails, the loss rarely lands on the acquirer alone. It lands on workers whose jobs are restructured away, on suppliers whose invoices go unpaid, on pension obligations, on the towns built around the enterprise. Chapter 39 traced these hidden risk-bearers one by one, and the tracing turns the risk-premium argument inside out: capital claims payment for a danger that has been arranged to fall on someone else. An acquisition structure can be brilliant as engineering and still fail every one of these questions. Cleverness was never the issue; the title of the surplus was.

Digital infrastructure adds a dimension the tradition did not have to face but supplies the grammar for. Code, data, platforms, and protocols now run beneath commerce, speech, government, and daily life, and much of that layer depends on open-source work maintained by communities rather than companies. Legal ownership of such infrastructure tends to concentrate; moral ownership, as chapter 39 argued, is distributed among maintainers, users, communities, and the future. When capital takes control of an infrastructure layer and begins pricing access to what has become a common utility, the old questions of ownership and just price return with force, and they are joined by the familiar asymmetry: when such systems fail or are abandoned, the public rescues, regulates, or replaces them, so downside is socialized while upside remains private. Digital sovereignty, on this reading, is not a technology policy so much as an ownership question, and the tradition has been asking ownership questions for seven centuries.

The newest clothing of all is artificial intelligence, and it dresses the old question twice. The first direction is the financing of AI itself. Capital is gathering around a general-purpose capability at a scale with few precedents, and whatever that capability becomes, whoever owns and governs it will hold something closer to infrastructure than to product: a layer that other enterprises, and eventually other institutions, must build upon and pay for. None of the book's questions (who owns, who governs, who bears the risk, who is protected) can wait here until the building is finished; ownership and governance structures harden early, and they are being drafted now, mostly with the same instruments and assumptions this book has spent five chapters auditing. The second direction is AI inside finance: underwriting, pricing, servicing, and collection are increasingly done by models, and here the tradition's oldest worry acquires a new precision. A system that can estimate what each borrower will bear can price need itself, borrower by borrower, at scale; what the tradition condemned as turning another's necessity into revenue can become, without anyone deciding it in so many words, an optimization target. Extraction can now be automated and refined, with the confessor's questions nowhere in the loop. Condemning the tools is not the doctrine's move here, since the same computational machinery can as easily be turned to protection as to extraction (chapter 41 showed it running in the opposite direction, as a conscience rather than a collector); refusing the delegation is: a claim on another person's need must answer to a person, and conscience cannot be outsourced to a model any more than it could be outsourced to a contract form. The scholastics pierced clever paperwork to reach the substance of transactions; their heirs will have to pierce cleverer software, and the questions to carry through are the same ones.

Mission drift, the subject of chapter 42, completes the survey from the inside. There has never been more mission-language in finance than there is now: impact, purpose, ESG, stewardship, regeneration. Some of it is sincere, some of it is marketing, and the two are not always distinguishable from the outside, or, after enough years, from the inside. The Monti di Pietà teach that even finance founded in charity drifts unless its governance, accounting, and incentives are disciplined, and the more mission-language an era produces, the more territory that lesson has to police. A label is a promise, not a structure, and promises drift; the countermeasures of chapter 42 exist because the drift is predictable enough to plan for.

And yet the survey should end on the encouraging item, because there is one. Ownership and governance have become live questions again. Steward ownership, foundation ownership, cooperatives, mission locks, and golden shares are being debated, drafted, and adopted by people who have mostly never heard of the extrinsic titles, and who are nonetheless asking the scholastic questions in modern dress: who should own the firm, who should govern it, and what may capital claim from it? A generation of founders is discovering that it does not want to sell its life's work into extraction, and a smaller but growing pool of capital is discovering that it does not want to demand extraction, and between them they are improvising structures that the tradition would have recognized and, in most cases, blessed. This book has tried to supply that movement with its missing pedigree. The questions being asked at law firms and founder retreats are older than the corporation, and the tradition that asked them first also worked out, in exacting detail, how to tell good answers from bad ones.

Relevance, however, is not recovery. A doctrine can be newly relevant and stay buried. Recovery begins when its questions are actually asked of actual structures, and for that the questions must be put in their modern form.

sections.title: 44.2 The Old Question in New Form
paragraphs: The scholastics asked one question of every payment above principal: by what title? Everything else in the tradition is commentary on that question, apparatus for testing the answers, and case law from applying it. Asked of modern structures, the one question unfolds into six. The preface posed them at the beginning of this book; they can now be asked with the whole tradition behind them.

Who owns? The mutuum's central insight was about ownership: in a loan for consumption, the thing lent becomes the borrower's, so charging the borrower for the use of what is now the borrower's own is selling what one does not own. Ownership analysis is where the tradition always began, and it is where modern analysis should begin too. Legal title can be read off the documents; moral ownership takes more work. Who owns the acquired company that carries its own purchase debt? Who owns the platform built on open-source labor and its users' data? Who owns the enterprise: its shareholders alone, or in some morally relevant sense its workers, its mission, and the community that depends on it? The tradition never treated ownership as a mere fact to be looked up; it treated ownership as a claim to be justified, bounded by purpose and by the common good. That habit of asking what has actually been transferred, and to whom, and under what obligations, cuts through a remarkable amount of modern paper, and it is the doctrinal root of every steward-ownership structure described in this book.

Who governs? Governance is where domination goes when yield is being watched. A capital provider whose return is capped or scrutinized can still take structurally what it may no longer take openly: board control, veto rights over hiring and strategy, covenant packages that steer the enterprise from a distance, consent rights over every consequential decision. The canonists' phrase in fraudem usurarum named contract forms designed to evade the usury prohibition while reproducing its substance, and the modern equivalent is often governance design. What interest can no longer take as price, control can take as power. A moral audit therefore reads the governance terms as carefully as the economic ones, and the test is the one this book has used throughout: ask whom the structure obeys when interests conflict, and especially whom it obeys under stress, because a board that serves mission in good years and capital in bad years is governed by capital.

Who bears risk? Periculum sortis earned its place among the titles only when the risk was real, and chapter 43 put the modern test plainly: risk must be borne, not merely priced. The place to look is the seniority stack, together with everything that reorders it behind the scenes: collateral, personal guarantees, security interests, covenants that accelerate repayment at the first sign of trouble. The order in which parties absorb loss is the structure's honest confession of whom it protects, however the marketing reads. Chapter 41 added the time dimension: a structure can look fair on average and still be ruinous in sequence, because an enterprise lives through one path of events, not an average of them, and a repayment schedule that ignores bad sequences shifts their whole weight onto the borrower. Where capital stands last in line for loss and first in line for payment, its claim to a risk premium deserves scholastic suspicion; where capital genuinely stands to be wounded, its share in the fruits has the shape of participation.

Who receives surplus? This is the triad of chapter 43 doing its daily work. Every fee, preference, premium, and penalty in a structure can be assigned to one of three families: restoration, compensating a real and provable burden; participation, sharing fruits where fate is shared; or domination, collecting on need, time, or bargaining power. A late-payment charge that tracks actual collection costs is restoration; the same charge tripled and built into the expected return is domination with a restorative name. A management fee that pays for real stewardship labor is restoration; the same fee scaling with assets while the labor does not is extraction on autopilot. A share of profits where losses are also shared is participation; a preferred return that must be paid whether or not there are profits is a mutuum's yield in equity costume. The assignment is not always easy, which is why the tradition built tests, but the sorting question itself is simple, and merely asking it changes what a fund document looks like from the inside.

Who can lose everything? The tradition asked this question on behalf of the neighbor whose cloak was taken in pledge, and it remains the most revealing question on the list. In any financing arrangement, someone's whole position can be at stake: the founder who signed the personal guarantee and can lose home and livelihood together, the workers whose livelihoods travel with the payroll, the community whose utility or care infrastructure is the collateral, the small supplier for whom one unpaid invoice is the difference between solvency and ruin. Total loss is a different kind of thing from partial loss, and the tradition treated it differently: this is why collection was to stop short of the household's survival, and why the pledge laws protected the cloak. A structure that concentrates survivable losses on capital and unsurvivable losses on everyone else has inverted that order. Capital that has arranged to lose nothing, in a structure where others can lose everything, has already answered the moral question about itself, whatever its documents say.

Who is protected? Chapter 43's final test asked where a structure's weight lands when things go wrong, because protection only costs something at default, renegotiation, and collection. Modern law protects capital by default: collateral, seniority, enforcement, and the presumption that contracts mean what they say. The older tradition protected the borrower by default: proof before surplus, proportion in penalty, mercy in collection, grace where hardship is honest, and the standing suspicion that need was being farmed. The difference shows up in drafting. A hardship clause that pauses repayment during honest distress is protection written down; a default-interest clause that raises the rate at the borrower's weakest moment is the opposite, and both are perfectly legal. A renewed moral economy does not need law to abandon capital; it needs law, contract, and custom to remember both parties, and to remember them especially at the moments when one of them has run out of choices.

Six questions, and they are one question. Each asks, from a different side, whether surplus has a title. The preface asked them with a seventh that gathers the rest: who is served? Any financing arrangement, in any century, answers these questions somehow. The only real choice is whether they are answered by design or by default, and structures that answer them well by design now exist, which is what the next section is about.

sections.title: 44.3 Toward Non-Extractive Capital
paragraphs: If the six questions are the diagnostic, what does treatment look like? Part XII assembled the toolkit piece by piece: the audit of chapter 39, the translations of chapter 40, the simulator of chapter 41, the countermeasures of chapter 42. Gathered into one place, the program has six elements. None of them is utopian. Each is a structure that exists, has been drafted, and can be audited, and each has both a doctrinal ancestor and a known failure mode.

Steward ownership holds the firm's ownership under its purpose. Control goes to people bound to the mission rather than to the highest bidder; the company cannot be sold out from under its work; capital's claims are limited by design rather than by goodwill. The change is not cosmetic, because it alters what the enterprise can be made to do. An extractive acquirer has nothing to acquire, an exit-driven investor has no exit to force, and the pressure that bends so many enterprises around their financing simply has less to push against. Its ancestor is the tradition's deepest teaching about property: that ownership is stewardship, held under a purpose beyond itself, rather than absolute dominion. Its failure mode is the label without the structure. Chapter 43 already issued the warning that steward ownership cannot be said to solve everything; a steward-owned shell with extractive financing on top of it drifts like anything else.

Mission-locked governance binds the purpose into the legal skeleton: asset locks that keep value from being converted into private gain, golden shares or mission veto rights that can block a betraying transaction, governance documents that protect the mission from capital and from management fashion alike, and stakeholder voice that brings the burdened parties into the room where decisions are made. The ancestors here are the statutes of the Monti di Pietà, which did not trust their own founders' piety and wrote the discipline into the institution: audits, caps, records, accountability. The failure mode is decoration: locks that exist on paper but are never tested, veto rights held by parties with no will to use them, a mission clause admired at the annual meeting and ignored in the term sheet. A lock is only as good as the last time someone tried it.

Patient capital restores time to its proper place. The tradition refused to let the mere passage of time generate a claim, and refused to treat carentia pecuniae, the bare absence of one's money for a while, as a wound requiring tribute. Patient capital operationalizes that refusal: repayment schedules that follow the enterprise's actual rhythm rather than the calendar's, grace built in for honest hardship, capital that can wait without charging for waiting. This is not softness; it is accuracy. An enterprise's cash flows are seasonal, lumpy, and mortal, and financing that pretends otherwise is not neutral, it is a transfer of risk dressed as a schedule. The failure mode is patience priced as a premium. A lock-up charge, a liquidity premium, or a time-based return converts waiting back into yield, and chapter 40 showed how quickly that door swings open once absence alone is allowed to count as a burden.

Loss-sharing finance makes societas contractual rather than rhetorical. Repayment rises and falls with actual performance; downside is genuinely shared; capital's claim is subordinated to the survival of the living enterprise; revenue shares carry honest caps and honest pauses, and equity comes without the protective moats that would turn it into debt in costume. The ancestor is the whole partnership tradition, commenda to societas, with periculum sortis as its test: profit-sharing was blessed exactly where loss-sharing was real. The failure mode was named in chapter 43: participation language with domination mechanics, upside shared in the brochure and downside shifted in the definitions section.

Non-extractive returns are returns that can answer the title question in the tradition's own terms: named, proven, proportionate, and still just under stress. Chapter 40 translated each title into modern accounting, damnum emergens into documented cost, lucrum cessans into a discipline on opportunity-cost claims, poena conventionalis into penalties that discipline rather than earn, stipendium laboris into fees that track real labor. Chapter 41 built the working conscience for it: the NEC Simulator's whole purpose is to make a return show its title, to ask not how much capital can receive but what capital may receive justly, and to keep asking under simulated bad years, not just good ones. And chapter 42 supplied the maintenance schedule: periodic re-underwriting to test whether titles remain valid as circumstances change, disclosure that lets the burdened party see the structure, restitution where surplus turns out to have been unjust. The failure mode is the dashboard as rationalization, a tool that computes what capital wants to hear, which is why chapter 41 spent a section on dashboard ethics.

Capital that serves life is the sixth element and not really an element at all; it is the standard the other five serve, and the title of this part of the book. A structure earns the description not by declaring it but by passing the tests: the audit questions of chapter 39 on its anatomy, the five questions of chapter 43 on its fruit. Serving life is what the whole apparatus is for; the apparatus is what keeps the phrase from being a slogan.

The elements are listed separately but work as one structure, each guarding the others' backs. Steward ownership settles who cannot buy the enterprise; mission-locked governance settles who cannot bend it; patient and loss-sharing capital shape what its financing may demand; non-extractive returns discipline what its capital may receive. Remove one and the pressure finds the gap. Locked governance funded by impatient capital lives under siege; patient capital without locks is a takeover waiting for a bid; titled returns without stakeholder voice drift in exactly the way chapter 42 described, one reasonable exception at a time. The Monti survived, where they survived, on the whole kit, statutes and audits and caps together, and there is no reason to expect less of their successors.

None of this is self-executing. Every element on the list can be adopted as fashion, drained by exceptions, or outgrown by an institution that has learned to love its own surplus; chapter 42 exists because drift is the normal weather of mission-driven finance. So the elements come with the countermeasures attached: audit, disclosure, stress-testing, re-underwriting, restitution, and governance that lets the burdened parties speak. It is worth remembering that the tradition's own institutions were structures rather than sentiments. The Monti di Pietà ran on statutes, auditors, ledgers, and pledge shelves, and needed every one of them, because they had discovered what every mission-driven fund eventually discovers: good intentions do not survive contact with compound arithmetic unless something in the constitution is watching.

An architecture like this will not assemble itself. Markets do not spontaneously build limits on their own claims, and never have. The Monti were built by people who thought theology was worth institutionalizing, and worth defending clause by clause against clever drafting; the structures in this section are being built, right now, by their heirs, whether or not the heirs know the family history. That work is where the book has been heading all along, and it brings us to the final thesis.

sections.title: 44.4 Final Thesis
paragraphs: The thesis of this book can now be stated in four sentences. The extrinsic titles were not loopholes. They were moral tests. Their purpose was to distinguish restoration from extraction. A renewed moral economy must do the same.

The familiar story says otherwise, and saying why it is wrong is a fair summary of everything the preceding chapters have shown. In the familiar story, the usury prohibition was an economic naivete that could not survive contact with real commerce, and the titles were the slow, embarrassed retreat: exception after exception wrung from the theologians until interest slipped in through the back door and the doctrine died of its own qualifications. But titles designed as loopholes would not have come with tests attached. Each title named a burden external to the loan itself; each demanded proof in the particular case; each was bounded by proportion, policed against fraud, and capable of failing. Damnum emergens required an actual loss. Lucrum cessans, the most contested of the four, was hedged with conditions precisely because the theologians saw where an abstract version would lead. Poena conventionalis was disqualified the moment delay became desirable. Periculum sortis demanded that capital could bleed. This is not the architecture of surrender. It is the architecture of discrimination, in the old and honorable sense of that word: the patient work of telling one thing from another, compensation from extraction, partnership from predation, a titled surplus from a tribute collected on need.

The distinction the titles enforced is the one chapter 43 placed at the center: surplus from restoration and surplus from participation can be just, and surplus from domination cannot, however it is papered. Every title is clean in its restorative or participatory use and corrupt in its dominating use, which is why the tradition never tired of asking which use was actually before it, case by case, contract by contract, and why its heirs cannot retire the question either.

To say that a renewed moral economy must do the same is not to import medieval conclusions. It is to recover the medieval question, and the discipline that made it answerable: titles named, proof required, proportion enforced, structures examined under stress, and the weaker party protected at the moments that reveal what a structure believes. Section 44.3 described what that discipline looks like when it is drafted into modern instruments, and the demand it makes falls on everyone who touches capital. It asks investors to know the title of every return they accept, and founders and enterprises to refuse structures that price their need. From the drafters of contracts, the designers of funds, and the builders of models it asks something more: to treat their instruments as moral architecture, because that is what they are. And of the Church, civil society, and the market together it asks what chapter 42 asked, that legality stop being mistaken for justice. The tradition never promised that just finance would be effortless, only that it was possible, and that the difference between just and unjust finance could be known.

The preface opened with the suspicion that a civilization equipped with derivatives and discount models had left the medieval question behind, and answered that the old question had only changed its clothing. The question was this: when may capital receive more than it gives? After forty-four chapters, the book's answer can be given in final form. Capital may receive more than it gives when the surplus can name its title and prove it: when it restores a real and documented burden, or shares the fate of the venture it feeds, or sustains real work and real service in proportion to their cost. It may never receive more than it gives merely because time has passed, because money was scarce, or because the other party's need left no better option. That is the whole doctrine. The rest, from Deuteronomy to the dashboard, is the working out.

For seven centuries the tradition retranslated that doctrine into whatever instruments its economy had just invented: the confessors carried it in their questions, the canonists in their tests, the preachers in their sermons, the Monti in their statutes and pledge shelves, Salamanca in its lecture notes on exchange, the manualists in their cases. Every generation received the questions, found them slightly too small for its new instruments, and did the tailoring. The instruments in front of us are term sheets, fund documents, covenant packages, dashboards, and models, and the tailoring has begun again, some of it described in Part XII of this book. The tradition hands over its questions in working order. Where they go next is not written in any of its books.

chapters.part: Part XIII — Conclusion: Capital That Serves Life

chapters.title: Glossary of Latin Terms
chapters.label: Appendix A

paragraphs: The terms gathered here are the book's working vocabulary, defined as the book uses them and given in the order the table of contents gives them, which is a logic of families rather than an alphabet. First come the contracts of Roman law, the taxonomy that let jurists and theologians tell one transfer of money or goods from another. Then the two words modern readers are most tempted to merge, usura and interesse, whose difference carries most of the book's argument. Then the penalty family, the earliest gate through which compensation entered the loan, including the first of the four extrinsic titles. Then the titles themselves and their two neighbors: carentia pecuniae, which the main tradition refused as a freestanding title, and stipendium laboris, which stands beside the titles rather than among them. Two terms of art from the canonists and the schools close the list. Each entry names the chapters where the full treatment lives; the definitions are deliberately short, since a glossary is a place to check a term, not to learn the doctrine.

Mutuum (loan for consumption). The Roman contract at the center of this book. Ownership of money or other fungible goods passes to the borrower, who consumes what he receives and owes back not the same coins but their equivalent, the tantundem. Because the thing lent is consumed and only its equivalent returns, the scholastics reasoned that money's use cannot be sold separately from its substance, and that a lender who demands more than the principal on the strength of the loan itself is charging for something that does not exist. Chapter 4 gives the contract's anatomy; the argument built on that anatomy runs through every chapter that follows.

Tantundem (equivalent amount of same kind and quality). What the mutuum borrower owes back: the same in quantity and quality, though never the same physical coins or grain. The term fixes the loan's moral baseline. Repayment of the tantundem settles the debt exactly, and anything demanded above it needs a justification that is not the loan itself. Chapter 4 introduces it alongside the mutuum.

Commodatum (loan for use). The loan in which the very thing comes back: a plow, a horse, a book lent and returned. Ownership stays with the lender, the borrower has the use of the thing for a time without payment, and the thing itself is restored. It matters to the argument as the mutuum's foil, the case where use and substance really are separable. Chapter 4 sets out the contrast.

Depositum (deposit). A thing handed over to be kept safe and returned. The keeper takes custody, not use; safekeeping is the whole point of the arrangement. Chapter 4 places it among the contracts Roman law formed by delivery of the thing itself, and it reappears wherever the book rehearses the contract taxonomy, a reminder that holding another's property and having it for one's own are different acts.

Locatio-conductio (hire or lease). The contract of letting for payment: a house, a field, a horse, a laborer's services, hired out for a price. The thing endures, the owner keeps ownership and its risks, and the rent pays for a use genuinely distinct from the thing's substance. In the book it usually appears as the standard against which money fails: a house can be leased because it survives its use, while money is spent in the using. The book's short form is locatio, following its own chapter 4 usage.

Societas (partnership). Resources joined in a common enterprise, with gain and loss shared and each partner's capital remaining at its owner's risk. That last clause does the moral work: because the investor's money continues to share the venture's fate, a partnership return is earned by risk actually borne rather than by the mere passage of a loan's term. Whether an arrangement is truly a societas or a mutuum dressed as one is among the book's most repeated tests. Chapters 4 and 25 give the anatomy, chapter 36 sets it formally against the mutuum, and the modern chapters of Part XII lean on it throughout.

Commenda (merchant investment partnership). The medieval instrument of long-distance trade: a silent investor entrusts capital to a traveling merchant, profits divide by agreement when the venture ends, and the capital's risk stays with the investor throughout. It mattered to the moralists as proof that capital could seek gain honestly by genuinely sharing a venture's fate, and it matters to this book as an honest partnership to point to. Chapter 25 tells its story.

Foenus nauticum (sea loan). The Roman maritime loan, under which repayment is owed only if ship or cargo arrives safely. The premium prices a real peril, the sea's, and the lender genuinely stands to lose the principal, which is why the book reads it as prehistory of periculum sortis rather than as an ordinary loan. Chapters 4 and 25 treat it, and the medieval argument over pricing such risk runs through Part VIII.

Usura (usury or interest). In Roman speech usurae were the sums paid for the use of borrowed money, and foenus was lending at interest as a business. In the moral tradition this book follows, the word is narrower and sharper: usury is whatever is demanded on the mutuum beyond the principal, on the strength of the loan itself. The scholastic case against it does not turn on the rate being high or the borrower being poor; it is an argument about unjust gain as such. Chapters 5 through 8 build the doctrine, and chapter 43 states what it does and does not condemn.

Interesse (compensation for loss). "That which stands between": the gap between the position a person should have occupied and the one he occupies, measured so that it can be made good. In its older legal sense the word named compensation for damage, delay, or deprivation, not a return on capital; it was a measure of harm before it was a financial term. The modern word "interest" descends from it without preserving that sense, and chapters 9 and 14 are largely about what changed in the descent.

Poena conventionalis (agreed penalty). A penalty stipulated in the contract itself: if the debtor fails to perform, an additional sum falls due. Its legitimacy rests on its shape. A true penalty is conditional, avoidable, attached to breach, and never the outcome the lender hopes for; the moment the surplus becomes the expected point of the bargain, the clause has changed character, whatever the contract calls it. It is the first of the four extrinsic titles the book examines, and the whole of Part V, chapters 10 through 13, tests where its line runs.

Morae poena (penalty for delay). The penalty family's specific case: the borrower is late, retains what should have been repaid, and owes something for the culpable delay. Its focus is narrower than the poena conventionalis, not breach in general but time wrongfully taken, and the same discipline governs it: the penalty must mark a wrong, not manufacture a yield. Chapter 10 draws the distinction, and chapter 36's comparison of the titles returns to it.

Poena nec in fraudem (penalty not in fraud of usury prohibition). The canonists' proviso attached to every permitted penalty: it must not operate in fraudem usurarum, in fraud of the usury prohibition. A penalty could be enforceable, attached to genuine breach, and still condemned if it existed to sell time under another name. The phrase is the book's compact form of the canonical warning. Chapter 10 introduces it, and the fraud test it names recurs in nearly every later chapter.

Damnum emergens (actual loss arising). Loss the lender actually incurs because of the loan or its delay: a payment missed, a debt contracted elsewhere, a real hole in the accounts. Compensation under this title restores; it does not enrich. Backward-looking and provable, it was the most readily accepted of the four titles, with a classic formula in Aquinas that the later tradition refined for centuries. Part VI, chapters 14 through 17, follows it from interesse to what chapter 17 calls the master title.

Lucrum cessans (gain ceasing or profit foregone). The claim that lending prevented a gain: the lender points not to a loss suffered but to a profit that never arrived because the money was out on loan. Forward-looking and therefore contested, since expected profits are easy to assert and hard to bound, it was admitted late, reluctantly, and under strict conditions: the foregone gain concrete, probable, and causally connected, never the bare fact that money can always earn something. Part VII, chapters 18 through 23, tells that story, controversy included.

Periculum sortis (risk of principal). The risk that the sors, the principal sum itself, may be lost. The title asks the structural question: does the capital provider genuinely stand to lose the capital? Where the answer is yes, as in partnership, return can be earned by the risk borne; where the contract arranges matters so the provider cannot lose, a risk premium is being charged for a danger that has moved onto other shoulders. The book calls it the most structurally disruptive of the four, and Part VIII, chapters 24 through 30, is its territory.

Carentia pecuniae (lack or absence of money). Literally the lender's being without his money for the term of the loan. It sounds like the most natural title of all, which is exactly its danger: taken alone it would justify charging for time and liquidity as such, and the prohibition would dissolve. The main tradition therefore refused it as a freestanding title; deprivation counts only when it shows up as demonstrable loss or as qualifying foregone gain. Chapter 32 examines it as the edge where scholastic doctrine and modern interest face each other.

Stipendium laboris (compensation for labor). The wages of real work: appraising pledges, keeping books, staffing the loan desk. Not one of the four titles but their near neighbor, invoked above all around the Monti di Pietà, whose small charges kept charitable lending staffed and solvent without pretending the loan itself had earned anything. The family's usual test applies: the payment must answer to work and cost actually incurred. Chapter 31 gives it its fullest treatment.

Lex commissoria (forfeiture or penalty clause). A clause by which failure to perform triggers a consequence: forfeiture of a pledge, cancellation of an arrangement, transfer of a right. It appears in several legal contexts, and in the canonists' compressed lists of modes by which more than the principal might lawfully be received it stands beside the permitted penalty, the free gift, and partnership. It faces the family's standard question, whether it secures performance or disguises yield. Chapters 11 and 33 handle it.

Sententia communis (common teaching). The shared judgment of the school: the position that, once enough weighty authorities converge on it, a confessor or adviser could reasonably follow in practice. Not a decree and not unanimity, but the settled center of gravity of a long argument. The book's chief example is the late scholastic settlement, organized around Lugo's position, that admitted lucrum cessans under strict conditions; chapter 23 shows the term at work.

chapters.part: Appendices

chapters.title: Key Scriptural Texts
chapters.label: Appendix B

paragraphs: The nine texts gathered here are the scriptural backbone of the book's argument, listed in the order the table of contents gives them, which is also the order of the canon: the three great law texts first, then a scene from Israel's history, a psalm, a proverb, and the prophet who presses the matter hardest. A note on handling. This book renders Scripture in its own words, closely and at the texts' own scale, and it does not quote at length; the entries below do the same, carrying the book's renderings and nothing else. Each entry says what the text says, what it contributes to the moral grammar of debt that chapter 2 assembles, and where in the book the full treatment lives. The appendix is a place to check a reference. The texts themselves, and the argument they carry, live in chapter 2.

Exodus 22:25–27. The law the whole tradition keeps returning to. If one lends money to the poor among God's people, one must not act toward him as a creditor who exacts interest; and if the borrower's cloak is taken in pledge, it must be returned before sunset, because it may be his only covering, the garment in which he sleeps, and if he cries out, God will hear, because God is compassionate. The text sets the prohibition beside the image of a poor body at night, and that placement is its contribution: a creditor's right is real and is limited by the debtor's vulnerability, and the more the loan arises from vulnerability, the more strictly the creditor's surplus claim must be judged. Chapter 2 opens its account of Scripture with this scene, and the cloak returns in chapter 31, when pledge-taking becomes a working institution at the Monti di Pietà and the old command about security and mercy has to be honored in procedure.

Leviticus 25:35–37. When a brother becomes poor and cannot maintain himself, the community must uphold him; no interest or profit is to be taken from him; the lender is to fear God, and the purpose is stated in the gentlest phrase in the legal material and the most demanding: "so that he may live," and live "beside you," not beneath you. The chapter of the Sabbath year and the Jubilee surrounds the command: land returns, households are restored, and the land itself belongs to God, so that one generation's misfortune cannot become a permanent dispossession. The contribution is the purpose clause. Lending exists for the neighbor's life, not for the growth of the lender's claim, and poverty is not an opportunity for yield. Chapter 2 develops the command and its Jubilee frame; chapter 1 sets out the older ancient Near Eastern world of debt release and restoration that the Jubilee tradition stands within.

Deuteronomy 15:1–11. Every seventh year, debts are to be released, and the creditor is not to press his brother, because the Lord's release "has been proclaimed." Then the law turns from the ledger to the lender: as the release year approaches and lending becomes unattractive by design, the heart may grow hard and the hand may close, and the text commands the opposite, an open hand toward the poor brother even when repayment is visibly uncertain. The contribution is double. Time itself interrupts the creditor's claim, so that the past cannot own the future forever; and the law disciplines the lender's heart, not only the debtor's conduct, treating the fear of loss as a moral danger in its own right. Chapter 2 gives the full treatment; chapter 8 returns to the hardened-heart command when Aquinas asks what waiting may and may not cost the borrower.

Deuteronomy 23:19–20. Interest to the brother is forbidden outright: not on money, not on food, not on anything that may be lent at interest. Interest to the foreigner is permitted, and the prohibition closes with a promise, "so that the Lord may bless you." Two things make the text load-bearing. First, it judges interest by relationship: the borrower within the covenant is a brother before he is a debtor, and brotherhood and extraction cannot be reconciled, while the foreigner clause, difficult then and difficult now, has often been read as marking off external commercial dealings rather than licensing exploitation of the vulnerable, and the Gospel's widening of neighbor-love presses the prohibition outward rather than relaxing it. Second, its inventory of money, food, and anything lent already senses the special danger in goods that are consumed in the use, the material intuition that the Roman mutuum and the scholastic analysis will later make precise. Chapter 2 handles the text and its difficulties; chapters 4 and 8 supply the legal anatomy the intuition anticipates.

Nehemiah 5. The only entry in this list with a plot, and a painful one. In the restored community after the exile, families are mortgaging fields, vineyards, and houses to buy grain; some have borrowed to pay the king's tax; some are watching their sons and daughters go into servitude. The crisis is internal exploitation, wealthier Jews lending to their own brothers on terms that produce dispossession, and Nehemiah confronts the creditors publicly, demanding that the fields, the vineyards, the olive orchards, the houses, and the interest be restored. The creditors agree to give everything back and to require nothing more. What Nehemiah adds is scale, and a remedy. Debt appears as a communal crisis, capable of reorganizing a whole people into creditors and dependents, and repentance costs restitution, repair that reaches the ledger and not only the conscience. Chapter 2 tells the story; chapter 6 cites it to show that usury is never merely an issue between individual lenders and borrowers.

Psalm 15. The psalm asks who may dwell on the Lord's holy hill, and answers with a portrait: the one who walks blamelessly, speaks truth, does no evil to his neighbor, keeps his word, takes no bribe, and does not lend money at interest. The refusal of usury stands in the list of marks of the person fit to stand before God, beside truthfulness and neighbor-love, with no financial analysis attached. That is the contribution: the way one handles money belongs to holiness, and the later tradition's insistence that usury is spiritually serious, a matter of the worshipper and not only of the contract, stands on this psalm. Chapter 2 places it at the head of the wisdom and prophetic material.

Proverbs 28:8. One who increases wealth by interest and profit, says the proverb, "gathers it for one who is kind to the poor." A single verse carries the whole idea of moral order: wealth gathered through exploitative increase is unstable before divine justice, marked within itself as belonging finally to the merciful, however long it sits on the gatherer's ledger. Chapter 2 reads the proverb as the wisdom literature's contribution to the grammar of debt.

Ezekiel 18. In the prophet's portrait, the righteous man does not lend at interest or take profit, withholds his hand from injustice, and executes true justice between persons; the wicked man lends at interest and takes increase. Usury here is not imprudence and not a shortfall in generosity. It is serious injustice, one of the acts by which a person's standing before God is described. This is individual accountability at full prophetic strength, and chapter 2 sets the portrait beside the psalm's as the personal dimension of the biblical witness.

Ezekiel 22. Among the sins of Jerusalem the prophet indicts, taking interest and profit stands beside bloodshed, oppression, extortion, and forgetting God. The placement is deliberate and severe. Economic extraction belongs to a constellation of violence, whether or not a sword is drawn, and its spiritual root is named in the same breath: when God is forgotten, the neighbor becomes available for use. It is at exactly this point that chapter 2 gathers the four dimensions of the non-legal material: the psalm gives personal righteousness, the proverb moral order, Nehemiah communal restoration, and Ezekiel prophetic judgment.

chapters.part: Appendices

chapters.title: Key Texts in Aquinas
chapters.label: Appendix C

paragraphs: The ten texts gathered here are the passages of Aquinas on which the book's argument rests, listed in the order the table of contents gives them, which is also the order of the Summa's own architecture: the opening questions of the treatise on justice and its parts first, then restitution, then buying and selling, then usury, then the three replies within the usury question from which the extrinsic titles grow, and finally two witnesses from beyond the Summa. Citations appear as the book carries them: II-II names the Secunda Secundae, the Second Part of the Second Part of the Summa; q. marks the question, a. the article within it, and ad the reply to a numbered objection. It is worth noticing that four of the ten entries are replies to objections, three of them inside a single article; the tradition this book recovers was built in large part from lines Aquinas wrote in answering objections. Each entry says what the text holds, in the book's own rendering, what it contributes to the argument, and where the full treatment lives. An appendix of texts is a place to check a reference. The reading itself happens in chapters 6 through 8 and the chapters named below.

Summa Theologiae II-II, q.57–q.60 (justice and judgment). The four questions that open Aquinas's treatise on justice in the Secunda Secundae: on right, or ius (q.57), on justice (q.58), on injustice (q.59), and on judgment (q.60). Aquinas does not begin his treatment of usury with money. He begins a long way back, with what is due between persons, and the questions on price, sale, and lending arrive only after he has established what justice is, how it divides, and how broken equality is repaired. The book reads that placement as itself the teaching: usury is a violation of justice before it is anything financial, and the argument of the titles presupposes these questions throughout.
