Chapter 6 opens Part III by walking this sequence and drawing out its consequences.

II-II, q.61 (distributive and commutative justice). "Of the Parts of Justice," the question the book calls the most important for commutative justice. Following Aristotle, Aquinas distinguishes two species of justice in the first article: distributive justice, which orders the community's relation to persons in the distribution of common goods, and commutative justice, which orders the relation of one person to another in exchanges and dealings. In the second article he assigns each its own equality: distribution follows proportion, a geometrical equality answering to office, need, merit, role, or contribution; exchange follows arithmetic equality, measured by what one person gives, takes, owes, damages, restores, or pays in relation to another. Usury belongs to the second species. The usurer disturbs equality in exchange by receiving what is not due, which is why the book's recurring test of every surplus claim is commutative: has equality between these parties been preserved? Chapter 6 develops the distinction and what it protects.

II-II, q.62 (restitution). What restitution is, whether it is necessary for salvation, whether one must restore what one has not taken, whether one must restore exactly what was taken, and to whom. This question supplies the doctrine's teeth. If usury is unjust gain rather than mere greed, then something has moved from borrower to lender that should not have moved, and keeping it continues the injustice: the surplus must be returned, and charity cannot substitute for restitution. The book returns to the question twice more. Its fourth article, which asks whether restitution extends to what someone was prevented from obtaining, becomes the latent opening through which later Thomists argue that a concrete foregone gain can matter in justice; and its logic makes every title auditable after the fact, since a payment collected under a false title was never due and remains subject to repair. Chapter 6 gives the base treatment, chapter 8 notes the openings, and chapter 20 reads the fourth article closely against the usury question.

II-II, q.77 (buying, selling, and just price). The question on cheating in buying and selling, treated immediately before usury, in four articles: whether it is lawful to sell a thing for more than it is worth (the classic just-price article), whether a defect in the thing renders a sale unjust, whether the seller is bound to reveal defects, and whether it is lawful in trading to sell a thing for more than one paid (merchant profit). Two of its holdings carry far. The first article recognizes that a thing may have special value to its owner, so that parting with it imposes a particular loss; the fourth allows moderate merchant profit where trade is ordered toward household support or public utility. Together they hand the later tradition materials for lucrum cessans: commerce can be licit, and interrupting a concrete commercial use of capital may be a real detriment. Chapter 7 gives the anatomy of the question; chapter 20 reads the first article's special-value principle beside the usury prohibition.

II-II, q.78 (usury). "On the sin of usury," the book's central text, in four articles. Whether it is a sin to take usury for money lent: yes, by the consumable-good argument, for money, like wine or wheat, is consumed in its use, so charging for the money and again for its use is selling what does not exist as a separate thing, or selling the same thing twice. Whether any other kind of consideration may be demanded: the article on which the whole extrinsic-title tradition hangs, since it refuses payment for the loan itself while allowing compensation for loss incurred, repayment of expenses, a genuinely free gift, and profit-sharing where money is entrusted by way of partnership rather than lent. Whether usurious gain must be restored: it must. Whether one may borrow under usury: the needy borrower who submits to unjust terms is not the moral equal of the lender who imposes them. The book reads the four as definition, sorting, remedy, and pastoral realism. Chapter 8 is given to the whole question; chapter 9 turns its second article's openings into the doctrine of the titles, and Parts V through VIII develop them one by one.

II-II, q.78, a.2, ad 1 (actual loss versus profit not made). The classic formula, and the root of the contrast between damnum emergens and lucrum cessans. A lender may contract for compensation for a loss he incurs, because that is not selling the use of money but avoiding a loss; he may not contract for compensation for the profit he might have made, because he must not sell what he does not yet possess and might never obtain. Actual loss may be repaired; expected gain may not be sold. Without that line, the book argues, the prohibition collapses, for if every profit not made were compensable, every loan would bear a return. Chapter 8 states the distinction, chapter 15 gives the formula a chapter of its own, and the controversy that fills Part VII is the history of how far the second half of the line could be stretched without breaking the first.

II-II, q.78, a.2, ad 5 (partnership and risk). The distinction between loan and partnership, mutuum and societas. Money entrusted to a merchant or craftsman by way of partnership remains in some sense at the investor's risk, and a share of the resulting profit is licit; money given as a loan becomes the borrower's own, an equivalent is owed back, and any demand beyond the principal is usury. The reply is the architecture of periculum sortis before the title exists: it ties capital's claim on gain to capital's exposure to loss, and it blocks two errors at once, the rigorist error that treats all profit from capital as usury and the permissive error that treats all provision of capital as investment. Chapter 8 introduces the reply, chapter 26 gives it a chapter of its own, and the modern chapters of Part XII test present-day financing structures against it.

II-II, q.78, a.2, ad 7 (delayed payment and hidden usury). Usury without a loan. If a seller charges more simply because he allows the buyer to pay later, the arrangement has the character of usury: the sale contains a hidden loan, and the surplus attaches to time rather than to the worth of the thing sold. The reply bridges just price and usury, and the tradition's whole analysis of disguised usury descends from it, having learned here to ask not what a contract is called but what role time plays in the surplus. Delay may still matter morally, but only through something real that happens in time: an actual loss, a culpable default, an expense, a risk genuinely borne. Chapter 8 develops the warning, chapter 12 states its anti-abuse logic, and chapter 33 follows its descendants through bills of exchange, public debt, and disguised loans.

De malo, q.13, a.4, ad 14 (late repayment). The text that shows Aquinas is not blind to the real effects of time. Beyond the Summa, he recognizes that a creditor may suffer harm when repayment is wrongfully delayed, and that such damage may be compensable: payment not for the passage of time but for the injury the delay caused, the logic of restitution rather than the logic of usury. The book reads the text as the seed of morae poena and of the whole late-payment analysis, legitimate where delay is culpable and harmful, corrupt where the penalty is expected from the beginning as a revenue model. Chapter 8 places it among Aquinas's writings beyond the Summa, and chapter 12 sets it beside the seventh reply as the two halves of one balance: damage from delay acknowledged, payment for time refused.

Commentary on the Sentences (early usury treatment and Januard's analysis). The reminder that Aquinas's thought on usury has a developmental character. He wrote across contexts, genres, and stages, and the Commentary on the Sentences carries his early usury treatment, before the Summa's mature formulation; the texts do not always function identically, and the book follows modern scholarship, above all Pierre Januard's analysis, in reading the wider architecture rather than isolating one formula. Januard's Aquinas is risk-sensitive: alert to commercial uncertainty, to the moral difference between exposed and protected capital, and to the "risk of analysis," the danger that a contract is misclassified and an injustice thereby blessed. None of this makes Aquinas a proto-modern economist; it means his analysis of risk, uncertainty, and contract form is more nuanced than the textbook account allows. Chapter 8 closes on this developmental reading, and chapter 35 gives Januard's contribution a section of its own among the modern scholars.

chapters.part: Appendices

chapters.title: Historical Figures
chapters.label: Appendix D

paragraphs: The figures gathered here are the book's cast: the lawgivers, philosophers, jurists, popes, canonists, and theologians who built, tested, guarded, and occasionally endangered the doctrine of the extrinsic titles. They are listed in the order the table of contents gives them, which runs in rough chronology from Babylon to the eighteenth century, and each entry says who the person was, what he gave the argument, and where his full treatment lives. Dates appear exactly as the chapters carry them, and a few entries run without any, since a reference apparatus should add nothing the book has not already verified. Reading the list straight through gives a compressed history of the doctrine itself: regulation first, then philosophy, then legal anatomy, then the long scholastic discipline of exceptions, then backlash. An appendix of persons is a place to check a name. The acquaintance itself happens in the chapters named below.

Hammurabi. Ruler of Babylon from about 1792 to 1750 BC, whose Code, cut into polished black stone in the eighteenth century before Christ and standing today in the Louvre, shows lending, pledging, interest, crop failure, and creditor misconduct already central enough to require legal attention. Two provisions carry particular weight in this book: Law 48, under which crop failure suspends payment, and Law 117, which caps the debt-service of a wife, son, or daughter at three years. He stands at one end of the book's very long arc as the ancient architecture of one conviction: creditor power cannot be unlimited. Chapter 1 gives his world.

Ammisaduqa. Hammurabi's great-great-grandson, who on taking the throne of Babylon around 1646 BC proclaimed a mīšarum, the royal release of debts, the only such edict whose text survives nearly complete. The release did not abolish economic life; commercial debts between merchants might be treated differently from subsistence debts. Its point was to restore social balance where debt had become destructive, and the book reads him as the clearest witness to the ancient release tradition. Chapter 1 places his edict in that long Mesopotamian practice.

Aristotle. Born in 384 BC at Stagira, twenty years in Plato's Academy, tutor to the boy who became Alexander the Great, and from about 335 BC master of his own school at Athens, the Lyceum. Near the close of Book I of the Politics (1258b) he calls usury the most contrary to nature of all ways of getting wealth, because it makes money breed from money when money exists for exchange. The book takes from him the sterility argument, the language of natural limit, and the question every title must eventually answer: what is the real source of the increase? Chapter 3 is his, and his categories return with Aquinas in Part III.

Gaius. The Roman jurist who wrote the Institutes, a teaching manual of Roman law, around AD 161, and about whom almost nothing else is known; not even his full name survives. His book vanished for centuries until 1816, when Niebuhr found it at Verona beneath a copied text of St. Jerome, nearly whole. It supplies the tradition's legal anatomy: the mutuum as the paradigm obligation arising by delivery of the thing itself (Institutes 3.90). Chapter 4 tells his story.

Justinian. The emperor whose Corpus Iuris Civilis, issued between 529 and 534, became, once recovered and taught, one of the great foundations of the medieval ius commune; its Institutes of 533, a first-year textbook with the force of law, explains the loan for consumption in the terms the whole tradition inherited: what is mine becomes yours, meum fit tuum. His texts count the four real contracts, and his sliding interest caps price the sea loan's peril above the ordinary lender's rate, the law's own admission that money afloat is not money ashore. Chapter 4 gives his legacy.

Gregory IX. The pope who gave canon law its book. He set Raymond of Peñafort to organizing the Church's scattered legislation, and the resulting collection of decretals, the Liber Extra, promulgated on 5 September 1234, remained the core of canon law until 1917. In it travels the decretal Naviganti, which opened canonical space for valuing goods with reference to future circumstances. Chapter 11 records the commission; chapter 19 reads the opening.

Raymond of Peñafort. Catalan, born around 1175, holder of the chair of canon law at Bologna before joining the Dominicans in 1222. He wrote the age's standard confessors' manual, the Summa de casibus poenitentiae, compiled the Decretals of 1234 at Gregory IX's commission, led his order as Master General from 1238 (he resigned after two years), and lived to January 1275. Canonized in 1601, he is the patron saint of canon lawyers. The book's debt to him is a formula: interesse means "not gain, but avoidance of loss," the line between a creditor restored and a creditor enriched. Chapters 11, 12, and 19 show it at work.

Robert of Courçon. An Englishman, born about 1160 in Derbyshire, who studied under Peter the Chanter at Paris, stayed to teach theology, and treated usury at length in his Summa; cardinal in 1212, papal legate from 1213, author of the University of Paris statutes of 1215, dead at the siege of Damietta in February 1219. In him the book finds the penalty problem while it is still raw: the tradition began not by asking how more payment might be permitted but by asking how a legal penalty could be kept from becoming a spiritual fraud. Chapter 11 gives his portrait; chapter 12 counts him among the convergent early voices.

Hostiensis / Henry of Susa. Born around 1200 at Susa in Piedmont, degreed in both laws at Bologna, teacher of canon law at Paris, bishop of Sisteron in 1244, archbishop of Embrun in 1250, cardinal-bishop of Ostia from 1262, dead at Lyon in 1271, and by common consent the most brilliant canonist of his century. His Summa aurea gave the penalty title its lasting formula, poena nec in fraudem, a penalty but not in fraud of the usury prohibition, and his name anchors the canonists' verse-list of licit exceptions. Chapter 11 gives the portrait and the formula; chapter 19 shows him opening a first, narrow door toward lucrum cessans for the lender not in the business of lending.

Thomas Aquinas. The book's central authority, treated not as the sole inventor of the extrinsic titles but as the source of the principles they grow from: the consumable-good analysis of the mutuum, the justice architecture of the Secunda Secundae, and the three replies within the usury question from which the titles descend. The book's own rule for him is "central, not solitary." Chapters 6 through 8 walk his treatise on justice, buying and selling, and usury; chapter 20 reads his latent openings; his key texts are gathered in Appendix C.

Duns Scotus. The tradition's sharpest questioner of incentives. His test for the penalty clause cuts past wording to what the contract makes the lender want: does he actually prefer repayment on time, or is he disappointed when punctuality costs him the penalty? The book treats the question as morally simple and economically devastating, and applies it from medieval penalty clauses to modern business models built on late fees and default. Chapters 10 and 12 develop his test.

Peter John Olivi. Franciscan, born around 1247 at Sérignan in the Languedoc, in the order at Béziers from about age twelve, censured by a commission of seven masters in 1283, cleared at Montpellier in 1287, dead at Narbonne in March 1298, his tomb later destroyed and his books banned. In the mid-1290s he wrote the Tractatus de contractibus, observing that money committed by its owner's intention to a probable profit carries "a certain seminal character of profit, which we commonly call capital" (quod communiter capitale vocamus); he was the first theologian to take the merchants' word capitale seriously enough to define it. Chapter 21 tells his story, including how his banned analysis reached all of Italy through Bernardino of Siena, verbatim and unattributed.

Alexander of Alexandria / Alexander Lombard. A Franciscan of Alessandria in Piedmont, born around 1270 (the town supplied the name), who taught theology at Paris, was elected minister general of the order in 1313, and died in office the next year. His Tractatus de Usuris, composed around 1307 and left in manuscript for six centuries, carries one of the earliest explicit formulations of periculum sortis as a possible title. The book marks him as a conceptual threshold: with him it becomes thinkable that danger to the principal is morally relevant to the judgment of surplus. Chapter 27 is his.

Bernardino of Siena. Franciscan missionary preacher, 1380–1444, canonized only six years after his death. He does three kinds of work in this book: he standardizes the exception categories into teachable form while keeping them morally dangerous; he stands at the center of the moral defense of the Monti di Pietà; and he transmits Olivi's banned analysis of capital from a manuscript in his own library, verbatim and unattributed, to all of Italy. Chapters 13 and 22 give his doctrine, chapter 31 his defense of the Monti, and chapter 21 the borrowed theory of capital.

Sant'Antonino of Florence. Dominican, archbishop of Florence, 1389–1459, working where confession, commerce, and civic life met in Europe's great banking city. His Summa moralis, an early landmark in the moral analysis of commerce and banking, preserves and organizes the exception-list tradition for later inheritance, and his gift to the doctrine is synthesis under discipline, and his gradient of suspicion still holds: the more predictable the penalty, the more suspect. Chapters 13 and 22 treat his moral theology, and chapter 31 his defense of the Monti alongside Bernardino.

Conrad Summenhart. Born at Calw in the Black Forest around 1455, professor of theology at Tübingen until his death in 1502. His seven-part treatise on contracts, the Opus septipartitum de contractibus, printed at Hagenau in 1500, gathers the scattered cases, titles, and distinctions into a comprehensive moral anatomy of exchange, and so marks the transition from medieval case discussion to the systematic contract analysis that Salamanca and the Jesuits would complete. Chapter 22 places him at that hinge.

Cajetan / Tommaso de Vio. Jacopo de Vio of Gaeta, born 1469, a Dominican at fifteen over his parents' objections, Master General of the order in 1508, cardinal in 1517, and between 1507 and 1522 author of the commentary that became the standard companion to the Summa Theologiae. (He is also the legate who spent three days at Augsburg in 1518 failing to persuade Luther to recant.) The book stations him at the hardest line in the doctrine, the boundary between actual loss and foregone profit. Chapter 16 gives him the boundary problem; chapter 22 shows him arguing that a properly bounded lucrum cessans can be reconciled with Aquinas himself.

Domingo de Soto. Dominican, 1494–1560, whose De iustitia et iure of 1553 became one of the foundations of the School of Salamanca. He is the tradition's cautious voice: determined to preserve the gratuity of the mutuum, alert to how easily "damage" could be claimed by any inconvenienced lender, and willing to admit compensation only where the injury stands genuinely outside the loan. Chapters 13 and 17 carry his caution.

Martín de Azpilcueta / Navarrus. The Navarrese canonist, 1492–1586, whose Manual de confesores y penitentes of 1549 became one of the most consulted confessors' guides of the age. His work is mostly remembered now for its sophistication about money, exchange, and the changing value of currency; his importance for the titles lies in the movement he shared with the whole Salamanca world, making moral theology concrete about merchant practice without letting the market set its own terms. Chapters 13 and 17 place him.

Medina. Juan de Medina, 1490–1546, professor of theology at Alcalá, whose Codex de restitutione et contractibus was printed there in 1546. In the book he marks the early movement toward risk pricing: the recognition that the reliability of repayment affects the practical value of a claim, together with the immediate corollary that the debtor's fragility must not thereby become a revenue stream. Chapter 28 gives him the transition.

Juan de la Peña. Dominican, born around 1513, trained under Melchor Cano, holder of the Vísperas chair of theology at Salamanca from 1561 until his death in 1565. He carried the periculum sortis debate into its precise later form, a question about price and probability: if repayment is uncertain, does the uncertainty affect the just value of the claim? Chapter 28 sets his question beside Medina's.

Luis de Molina. Jesuit, 1535–1600, author of a massive De iustitia et iure. With him the inherited doctrine is brought into close contact with the practical legal and commercial world of early modern Europe: how compensation is identified, measured, limited, and written into actual contracts without becoming a disguised price for the loan; he also sharpens the delicate question of whether compensation may be fixed in advance. Chapters 13 and 17 give his integration, and chapter 30 records the backlash his subtlety helped provoke.

Leonardus Lessius. The moral theologian whom the merchants of Antwerp consulted about their dealings; he is said to have gone regularly to the exchange himself to question the most experienced of them, field work for a doctrine. In the book he stands for systematization: the move from prohibition alone to contract diagnosis, asking of every surplus what it is attached to. He also carries the developed treatment of carentia pecuniae, the deprivation claim the main tradition refused as a freestanding title. Chapter 17 gives him at length; chapter 30 the reaction he drew.

Juan de Lugo. Born in Madrid in 1583, in the Society of Jesus from 1603, professor of theology at the Roman College for over two decades. His De iustitia et iure of 1642 so impressed Urban VIII that the pope made its author a cardinal the following year, over his attempted refusal; he died in Rome in 1660. In him the scattered logic of the titles gathers into a comprehensive theory of detriment, and the late scholastic settlement the book calls the sententia communis organizes itself around his position. Chapter 17 gives the synthesis; chapter 23 the settlement.

Benedict XIV. Prospero Lambertini, one of the most learned men ever to hold the papal office. His Vix pervenit, issued on November 1, 1745, and addressed to the bishops of Italy, restated the prohibition's core while leaving the extrinsic titles standing: surplus demanded on the loan itself is condemned, compensation under real titles is not, and naming a loss does not create one. The book reads the document as the tradition's warning against its own abuse, and as more radical than it first appears. Chapter 17 reads it closely.

St. Alphonsus Liguori. Founder of the Redemptorists, 1696–1787, later a Doctor of the Church, whose Theologia moralis, first published in 1748, guided generations of confessors between rigorism and laxity. He ranked Lugo "easily first after St. Thomas" among theologians, then spent much of his career disciplining the expansions Lugo had made possible. His mature rule for the penalty title fits in one sentence: a penalty may be licit when it is truly penal, proportionate, avoidable, and not desired as revenue. Chapters 13 and 17 give his settlement.

Daniele Concina. Dominican, 1687–1756, with twelve volumes of moral theology to his name, the voice of the eighteenth-century backlash. His fear was that the doctrine could be emptied from within, never formally denied, simply surrounded by exceptions until almost nothing remained, and his particular target was the elasticity of the risk-title. The book calls him the conscience of the doctrine: the reminder that moral theology is also a discipline of suspicion toward sin. Chapter 30 gives him the argument.

chapters.part: Appendices

chapters.title: Modern Scholars to Engage
chapters.label: Appendix E

paragraphs: The scholars gathered here are the book's modern interlocutors: the historians, economists, and anthropologists through whom chapter 35 reads the usury tradition and against whom this book's own project is tested. They are listed in the order the table of contents gives them, which leads with the two scholars of debt's deep past (chapter 35 saves them for last) and then follows that chapter's own survey of the doctrine: its grand history, its economic thought, its social meaning, its banking practice, the structure of its titles, its contract law, its information problems, its institutional history, and finally the book's central theologian read afresh. Dates and book titles appear exactly as the chapters carry them, and most entries run without either, since a reference apparatus should add nothing the book has not already verified. An appendix of scholars is a place to check a name. The engagement itself happens in chapter 35, and for the first two also in chapter 1.

David Graeber. The book's widest lens. His Debt: The First 5,000 Years (2011) recovers the anthropological strangeness of debt: credit, obligation, account, and social memory organized economic life long before coinage, and the familiar barter-first story of money runs largely backward. Debt in his telling is a moral relation before it is a financial instrument, entangled with honor, hierarchy, religion, and enforcement; behind many debt systems stands the possibility of seizure, bondage, or force, and societies moralize the debtor's repayment while leaving the justice of the creditor's claim unexamined. The book says plainly that he is not the safest guide to technical scholastic doctrine; his gift is the civilizational frame that shows why the doctrine matters. Chapter 1 opens the book with his provocation, and section 35.10 closes the survey of scholars with it.

Michael Hudson. The economist of the ancient release. The book's account of Mesopotamian debt cancellation rests on his decades of argument, made most fully in ...and forgive them their debts (2018), that the earliest economies cannot be understood through modern market individualism: debt was embedded in land tenure, temple and palace administration, taxation, and household survival, and periodic royal release was not an accidental kindness but a structural feature of early statecraft. Rulers annulled subsistence debts because unlimited creditor claims would devour the cultivators, households, and soldiers their own power rested on. The biblical Jubilee stands inside that world and transforms it covenantally. Chapter 1 gives his argument in full; section 35.10 sets him beside Graeber among the scholars of the ancient Near East.

John T. Noonan. Any modern reconstruction of scholastic usury doctrine must begin with Noonan. His classic study, The Scholastic Analysis of Usury (1957), shows the teaching as a living and contested tradition of moral reasoning rather than a flat prohibition mechanically repeated, and traces how the extrinsic titles gradually alter the prohibition's practical force: actual loss accepted most readily, foregone profit far more dangerous, risk of principal close behind. The book takes from him the difference between usury doctrine as slogan and as analytical tradition, the recognition that the doctrine became a moral diagnostic system, and the warning that mission drift often happens through reasonable exceptions. His one limit: read alone, his arc can make the movement toward interest look inevitable. Section 35.1 reads him at length, and its roster of correctives is this appendix in miniature.

Odd Langholm. The intellectual machinery. Langholm reconstructs scholastic thought on value, price, and exchange, reading the scholastics as serious analysts rather than as a crude early economics awaiting correction. From him the book takes the placement of usury inside commutative justice: the just price formed by common estimation, need, scarcity, and judgment rather than by bargaining power alone; the insistence that consent given under desperation does not settle the moral question; and the demonstration that between technical permissiveness and mere exhortation lies a third possibility, technical moral reasoning. He also supplies the translation caution this book works under: the Latin tools were formed inside a moral universe, and they must be translated, not copied. Section 35.2 gives his framework.

Giacomo Todeschini. The scholar of belonging. His work on Franciscan economics, poverty, and credit shows that the medieval argument over money was also an argument over who counted as trustworthy and who was pushed outside the circle of economic life. In his light the Monti di Pietà appear as attempts to keep the poor inside that circle, and creditworthiness as a moralized category that ranks persons. The book takes from him its social diagnostic: it is not enough to ask whether a title exists; one must also ask what the title does to belonging, since inclusion itself can happen on extractive terms. Finance distributes not only money, but credibility. Section 35.3 develops the point from the payday storefront to the term sheet.

Raymond de Roover. Belgian historian of medieval and Renaissance banking, 1904–1972, whose The Rise and Decline of the Medici Bank appeared in 1963. He brings the reader into the counting house: bills of exchange, exchange fairs, merchant banks, and the working instruments the theologians actually had to judge. His double corrective: the theologians were not economically ignorant, and the bankers were not simply modern financiers under inconvenient rules; the two sides were negotiating over real instruments, real services, and real evasions. Dry exchange, the loan carried under an exchange contract's name, is his classic case of substance judged beneath surface, and his standing lesson is that complexity is not proof of legitimacy. Section 35.4 gives him at length; Sant'Antonino, whose world he illuminates, lives in chapters 13 and 31.

Fabio Monsalve. The structure of the titles. Monsalve organizes the late Spanish scholastic treatment of the extrinsic titles as a family with an inner logic and a chronology: damnum emergens accepted earliest because actual loss sits closest to restitution, lucrum cessans admitted later and under discipline, periculum sortis increasingly central as the valuation of uncertain claims became thinkable. The sequence runs from the concrete toward the abstract, and the danger grows with the abstraction. His reading of Lugo shows the titles gathered into a broader theory of detriment, each a "loss of a sort," a synthesis that brings coherence and, read carelessly, overextension. The book's habit of disaggregating stacked modern claims, asking each fee, preference, and premium for its title, follows his lead. Section 35.5 gives the anatomy; Lugo's synthesis itself lives in chapter 17.

Wim Decock. The bridge to contract law. Through his work on Lessius, the extrinsic titles come into focus as contract concepts: questions about what was promised, who owns the capital, who bears risk, and what the document actually does. His Lessius is both expansive and disciplined, taking commercial practice seriously without letting enforceability settle justice. From him the book takes the rule that risk must be located in the contract rather than invoked over it, since collateral, guarantees, and seniority can move downside while the word stays put; the account of carentia pecuniae as a term commitment whose generalization would dissolve the doctrine; and the warning that once a burden can be named it can be priced. A title can become a product. Section 35.6 carries the full argument; Lessius himself is chapter 17's.

André Lapidus. The epistemics of the doctrine. Lapidus reads the usury tradition as moral reasoning about knowledge under time: between advance and repayment stand uncertainty, delay, failure, and interpretation, and each title is an information-sensitive moral test. Poena asks whether delay was culpable and not engineered; Damnum whether loss was real and caused; Lucrum whether the lost gain was concrete and probable rather than an invisible alternative world; Periculum whether principal was genuinely at risk; Carentia pecuniae whether absence of money produced a real burden or merely monetized time. Risk merely asserted stays rhetoric until it is analyzed, because risk is not a label. It is a structure. A moral audit, he teaches, should produce not only a verdict, but a map of uncertainty. Section 35.7 builds the case.

John Munro. The grounding in economic history. Munro supplies the financial world the doctrine had to answer: merchant credit chains, fairs, forced loans, annuities, and municipal debt. He is the book's best witness for damnum emergens in concrete commercial cases, where one delayed payment cascades through a network of obligations, and for the limit that keeps the title honest: loss must be actual, caused, and documented, since a damage charge collected in advance on every loan is simply yield under another name. His public-finance work shows classification under political pressure: who pays, who receives, and whether public necessity has become private income. And he watches moral status change over time, as cost recovery becomes margin and temporary claims become permanent rent. Section 35.8 gives his casebook.

Pierre Januard. Aquinas with fresh eyes. Januard complicates any simple anti-interest reading of Aquinas, drawing on the Commentary on the Sentences and De malo alongside the Summa Theologiae. He foregrounds the risk-sensitive architecture already inside the doctrine: the mutuum transfers ownership and forbids surplus, while societas leaves capital exposed to the venture and permits shared profit, so the moral line runs along who bears loss. From him the book takes the "risk of analysis": transactions resist classification, and a misclassified contract can bless injustice. His Aquinas allows compensation for the damage wrongful delay causes while refusing to let duration itself be monetized, and gives each later title its Thomistic coordinates. Section 35.9 gives the reading; the texts themselves are gathered in Appendix C.

chapters.part: Appendices

chapters.title: Four Extrinsic Titles Diagnostic Matrix
chapters.label: Appendix F

paragraphs: This appendix turns the book's per-title anti-abuse logic into a working checklist: six questions for each of the four extrinsic titles, in the order the table of contents gives them. The questions are the ones the tradition itself learned to ask, and the whole matrix rests on the one-sentence grammar chapter 36 distills from the doctrine: "no surplus is justified unless it is tied to a real external burden that is documented, causally connected, proportionate, and not merely a disguised price for the use of money." Each gloss below states which answer a sound title needs. Two cautions govern the use of the matrix. First, a title survives only if its whole battery lands right; a single failed answer is enough to reclassify the payment. Second, titles are routinely layered in modern contracts, so after auditing each claim alone, ask chapter 36's closing question: whether many partial justifications combine into an extractive whole. Carentia pecuniae has no block here because the tradition refused it as a freestanding title (chapter 32); a liquidity claim counts only where it can qualify as actual loss or as disciplined foregone gain. An appendix of questions is a place to check a claim. The doctrine lives in Parts V through VIII, the comparison in chapter 36, and the contract-level audit in chapter 39; Appendix G turns the same discipline into a review template.

Poena conventionalis (agreed penalty). Moral basis: culpable delay; it compensates the harm of late repayment or breach. Its characteristic deformation, in chapter 36's table, is engineered default. Part V (chapters 10 through 13) develops the doctrine.

1. Is the due date realistic? A sound title needs yes. The date must be one the borrower's actual circumstances can meet; an impossible schedule manufactures the breach it then punishes.
  2. Is delay culpable? Yes is required: the delay must be wrongful and avoidable, "culpable rather than unavoidable," not misfortune. A penalty on unavoidable hardship is not answering a wrong.
  3. Is the penalty proportionate? Yes: scaled to the harm the breach causes, not to what default can be made to earn.
  4. Does the lender prefer repayment on time? The classic test, Scotus's (chapters 10 and 12), and the sound answer is an unhesitating yes. A lender for whom punctuality is bad news is collecting rent on the calendar.
  5. Is default engineered? No is required. Short windows, schedules mismatched to cashflow, cure provisions designed to fail: where these appear, the penalty has begun to imitate usury.
  6. Is the penalty booked as yield? No. A penalty the business model counts on is income, not discipline; where the forecast already includes it, the clause has changed its function.

Damnum emergens (actual loss arising). Moral basis: restitution; it compensates real loss the lender has suffered. Its characteristic deformation is fictive damages. Part VI (chapters 14 through 17) develops the doctrine.

1. Is the loss actual? Yes: a loss that has arisen, not a projected, standardized, or advance-priced one. A damage charge collected on every loan before any damage occurs is pricing, not restitution.
  2. Is it documented? Yes. This title lives on evidence: the invoice, the ledger entry, the replacement borrowing. An undocumented loss is an asserted one.
  3. Is it caused by the loan? Yes: the causal chain must run from this transaction to this harm; without causality, the title becomes opportunism.
  4. Is it proportionate? Yes: compensation measures the gap the harm opened, and stops there.
  5. Is it restorative? Yes: the payment returns the lender to the position the breach disturbed. Anything beyond restoration is gain from the loan as such.
  6. Is it double-counted? No. The lender cannot charge for the same harm twice: once as a fee, again as a penalty, again as a premium, again as return. Sum the claims before judging any of them.

Lucrum cessans (gain ceasing). Moral basis: interrupted gain; it compensates concrete profit foregone because of the loan. Its characteristic deformation is abstract opportunity cost. Part VII (chapters 18 through 23) tells its long, controversial story.

1. Was there a concrete alternative use? Yes is required: a named, real employment of the money, not the whole field of possible investments claimed as if already owned.
  2. Was the use licit? Yes. Only an honest employment can ground the claim; chapter 22 holds the point that an illicit one cannot.
  3. Was the gain reasonably certain? Yes: probable at the level commerce can actually show, before the fact, not imagined after it.
  4. Did the loan truly cause the gain to cease? Yes: the lending itself, not the market, the season, or the lender's own choices, must have interrupted the use.
  5. Is the valuation reasonable? Yes: bounded by the value of the interrupted gain itself, not a generic market alternative.
  6. Is this more than abstract opportunity cost? The collapse test, and yes is required. If the claim would be equally true of any loan whatever, it is opportunity-cost pricing, and the title has dissolved into ordinary interest.

Periculum sortis (risk of principal). Moral basis: risk-bearing; it compensates genuine exposure of capital to loss. Its characteristic deformation is the universalized risk premium. Part VIII (chapters 24 through 30) is its territory.

1. Can principal actually be lost? Yes is required, and in the structure, not the mood: a return claimed for risk "does not put capital at risk if legal documents guarantee repayment."
  2. Who bears downside? The capital provider must hold a real share of it. Where the borrower bears the operational risk, the workers the wage risk, the community the social risk, and the founder the personal risk, while capital holds only delayed upside, the answer disqualifies the title.
  3. Is repayment guaranteed? No is required. A guaranteed claim is a loan whatever the term sheet calls itself, and its surplus must find another title.
  4. Is there collateral or seniority? Each protection is not disqualifying by itself, but each one subtracts from the risk actually borne: collateral, seniority, guarantees, covenants, and enforcement rights move downside onto other shoulders, and the claimed premium must shrink accordingly.
  5. Is the return proportionate to risk? Yes: proportionate to the actual risk borne, after the protections are netted out, not to the risk recited.
  6. Is risk shared or shifted? Shared is required. This is the mutuum and societas line: "Profit-sharing is easier to justify where loss-sharing is real," and capital that has arranged its own safety is not a partner but a protected creditor.

chapters.part: Appendices

chapters.title: A Non-Extractive Capital Contract Review Template
chapters.label: Appendix G

paragraphs: This appendix turns the book's audit practice into a working template: ten steps, in the order the table of contents gives them, moving from what the contract is (steps 1 and 2), to what it charges and why (steps 3 through 6), to what it does to persons and purpose (steps 7 through 10). It is Appendix F's companion piece. The matrix there tests a single title; this template reviews a whole contract, and it leans on chapter 39 throughout, where the five audit questions walk through a live financing structure at full length. Use the template before signing, and then periodically, since moral review should be periodic, not merely initial: a title justified at signing can cease to be justified later. An appendix of steps is a place to check a contract. The reasoning lives in chapter 39, the per-title batteries in Appendix F, and the design patterns of Part XII in chapters 39 through 42.

1. Identify the legal form. Read the actual instruments, not the pitch. What do the documents say this is: loan, sale, partnership, investment, insurance, exchange, lease, license, guarantee, or a hybrid? List the clauses that carry force: repayment terms, security, priority, covenants, governance rights, exit rights. Hybrids must be audited clause by clause, because each clause can shift the form.

1. Identify the moral form. Now ask what the contract does. Where does ownership pass? What must be returned, and is repayment fixed or contingent? Run the two tests chapter 39 begins with: the downside scenario often reveals the real contract, and recourse is one of the clearest signs of moral form. The central danger is the disguised mutuum: capital advanced, principal protected, surplus enforced, whatever the label. Where legal and moral form disagree, the moral form governs the rest of the review.

1. Classify the surplus claim. Take every payment above principal and make each component separate itself from the others and name its moral basis: loss, delay, foregone gain, risk, labor, or expense. Bargaining power is the seventh explanation, and it explains a charge without ever justifying one. Inventory the whole stack: interest, default interest, arrangement and monitoring and management fees, warrants, preferences, redemption premiums, reimbursements, carry.

1. Name the extrinsic title, if any. Match each classified claim to its title: culpable delay to poena conventionalis, actual loss to damnum emergens, interrupted gain to lucrum cessans, genuine exposure to periculum sortis, real work to stipendium laboris, real costs to expense recovery. Then run each named title through its Appendix F battery. A charge with no title beyond convention is unjustified surplus, and watch for title migration: when the justification shifts but the charge stands, the desired payment searches for a title.

1. Document the title. A title is only as strong as its paper. Require the loss invoiced, the alternative use named before the fact, the risk located in the signed waterfall, the repayment schedule shown to be realistic, the labor actually performed and priced. Demand more evidence as the title becomes more abstract: a documented cost needs a receipt, a risk claim needs the whole priority stack.

1. Test proportionality. The title opens the door to compensation; proportionality determines how far one may walk through it. Measure each payment against its burden, then sum the stack and measure the whole against the whole: the commonest abuse is the same burden charged under several names, as fee, penalty, premium, and preference at once. A cap expresses a refusal of unlimited claim; its absence is worth noticing.

1. Test risk allocation. Run the periculum sortis battery from Appendix F across the structure, then watch the contract under stress. When the enterprise fails, does repayment pause, subordinate, or convert, or do enforcement rights appear? Does distress enlarge the claim through default interest, capitalized interest, fees rolled into principal, extension charges, and penalty accruals? That compounding is the logic by which weakness becomes yield, and a non-extractive structure refuses it: grace, restructuring, loss-sharing, or capped recovery belong in the documents, not in the lender's discretion.

1. Test borrower vulnerability. Ask who had power when the terms were set. A borrower may consent and still be exploited; market-standard terms tell you who structured the market. Then test intelligibility: could the weaker party see how the clauses interact under stress, or only the headline cap and not the priority, the patient term and not the default trigger, the mission language and not the exit right? A structure that draws its surplus from the borrower's need, or deepens that need, fails the review whatever its titles.

1. Test mission alignment. For a mission-bearing enterprise, ask whether the structure protects the purpose or subordinates it to capital's exit. Look for the pressures: growth requirements the enterprise cannot bear, forced-sale rights, redemption clocks. Look for the protections: mission locks, hardship provisions, and governance able to override extractive enforcement. Chapters 40 through 42 supply the design vocabulary; the review asks whether this contract uses it or merely quotes it.

1. Test whether capital serves life. Last, step back from the clauses and ask what the whole arrangement makes more likely over its life: an enterprise that can reinvest, pay its people, keep its purpose, and survive hardship, or one in which capital collects for risks that others actually bear. The ten steps end where the book ends: every euro above principal must tell the truth about why it is owed, and chapter 43 states the final moral test.
